25 wild, brilliant and tragic events from the past 25 years that defined culture in Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/25-wild-brilliant-tragic-events-past-years-that/docview/3159623210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=25+wild%2C+brilliant+and+tragic+events+from+the+past+25+years+that+defined+culture+in+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It began with the Y2K scare and ended with our communities redefining "normal" following a global pandemic. The past quarter-century packed in a lot. New trends emerged. Some took root, while others proved to be fads.
Here are 25 wild, brilliant and tragic events from the past 25 years that shaped culture in Toronto.
2024Taylor Swift takes over Toronto
For two magical weeks in November, Taylor Swift cast a spell over Toronto. Thousands of Swifties from around the world made the pilgrimage to experience the pop superstar's penultimate stop of her record-breaking Eras Tour. Bedazzled in sparkles and sequins, they flooded our streets, restaurants and bars, turning Toronto into Taylor's Town. Inside the Rogers Centre, the pop icon performed a marathon three-and-a-half hour set, playing through the most popular hits of her career. Those six concerts, months in the making, marked not only one of the largest cultural events of the century, but also in the city's history. - Joshua Chong
2023Toronto tests out booze in parks
Though it was technically illegal at the time, many Torontonians drank and socialized in parks with friends during the COVID-19 pandemic due to restrictions on indoor social gatherings. In the summer of 2023, Toronto finally tested a pilot program that officially allowed residents to crack open a cold one at certain parks across the city. Despite initial concerns that the initiative would lead to increased public disobedience and littering, the program was overwhelmingly well-received and was adopted permanently the following year. - Joshua Chong
2022NOW Magazine ends as we know it
NOW Magazine's newsstands were once ubiquitous in Toronto. Launched in 1981 by Michael Hollett and Alice Klein, the free, alt-weekly publication had a circulation of more than 60,000 copies at its peak. It was especially known for its progressive politics and near-exhaustive coverage of the city's indie arts scene. But the media organization long struggled with a decline in advertising revenue - a decline, some say, that echoes the struggles of Toronto's arts industry as a whole. Since 2019, the paper has changed ownership twice and currently operates as a digital-only publication. But with its former staff entirely gone, NOW is largely a shell of its former self. - Joshua Chong
2021 Ford announces controversial Ontario Place redevelopment
Premier Doug Ford was met with controversy when he announced plans to redevelop Ontario Place, once a booming cultural hub and urban park on Toronto's waterfront that's been closed since 2012 due to mounting financial losses. The provincial government's proposal included the preservation of Ontario Place's Cinesphere and iconic pods, along with the construction of a private spa, to be operated by the Austrian company Therme, and the relocation of the Ontario Science Centre to the site. Ford's government initially pegged the project to cost as little as $335 million, but that figure has since ballooned to more than $2.2 billion. In 2024, the province's auditor general went on to lambast the redevelopment scheme, saying it was "not fair, transparent or accountable." - Joshua Chong
2020The COVID-19 pandemic brings the curtain down
No single event has had more of a lasting impact on the city's cultural sector than the COVID-19 pandemic. Over a single week in mid-March, nearly every arts organization in the city was forced to close its doors, as the number of confirmed COVID-19 cases and deaths exponentially climbed. What was initially intended to be a closure of several weeks turned into a months-long lockdown, followed by the easing of restrictions in fits and starts. Many companies that closed their doors in 2020 never reopened. Those that did faced a long road to recovery amid changing consumer habits and declining revenue streams. - Joshua Chong
2019 Toronto basks in the glory of the Raptors championship
Few Torontonians - in fact, few Canadians - will ever forget where they were on June 13, 2019, when the scrappy Raptors, led by a truly superhuman effort from star Kawhi Leonard, defeated the juggernaut Golden State Warriors to win their first NBA Championship. As the final buzzer sounded, the city erupted into a cacophony of cheers, blaring horns and screams of delight, as thousands poured into the streets to celebrate what felt like, at least for embattled Toronto sports fans, the fulfilment of destiny. Of course, the celebrations didn't stop that evening, but continued for several delirious days, culminating in a victory parade that drew an estimated two million fans into the city streets to catch a glimpse of the hometown heroes. Taking place just months before COVID-19 forced the city and much of the world to shut down, the championship spring was a special and rare moment of collective joy and triumph for a city that is too often hindered by cynicism or impostor syndrome. For a few jubilant weeks, as the warm spring turned to another hot summer, it felt like there was no better place on earth to be. - Richie Assaly
2018Theatre company shaken by sexual assault allegations
Soulpepper, one of the most prominent not-for-profit theatres in Toronto, was shaken by a series of sexual assault and harassment allegations levelled against Albert Schultz, its co-founder and artistic director. Four female actors each filed separate civil claims in early January, seeking $4.25 million from Soulpepper and $3.6 million from Schultz for what they described as a pattern of sexual harassment. Schultz resigned two days after the lawsuits were filed and the case was settled by the end of the summer. But it resulted in a broader, years-long reckoning and cultural renewal at Soulpepper, ultimately leading to the appointment of Weyni Mengesha as its artistic director. - Joshua Chong
2017A new era for Canadian musical theatre
When "Come From Away" officially opened on Broadway in 2017, it ushered in a new, golden age of Canadian musical theatre. It's easy to forget that David Hein and Irene Sankoff's stirring masterpiece, which ran in New York City for five years and spawned countless productions around the globe, was developed out of a Sheridan College classroom in Oakville, Ont., and funded, in part, by a government arts grant that allowed the married writing team to travel to Newfoundland on the 10th anniversary of the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks. "Come From Away" may be the most successful musical in this country's history but, more than that, it should also be remembered for paving a path for emerging writers in this country, helping establish a developmental pipeline for new works and pushing playwrights to dream even bigger. - Joshua Chong
2016 "Kim's Convenience" jumps from stage to screen
Canadians hadn't seen a TV family like the Kims before when "Kim's Convenience" premiered in October 2016. Spun off from Ins Choi's hit play, the sitcom featured Korean Canadian actors (Paul Sun-Hyung Lee and Jean Yoon) playing Korean immigrants who run a Toronto convenience store, fending in comic ways with their Canadianized children and the larger community. It wasn't devoid of stereotypes and we now know about the lack of diversity behind the scenes, as flagged by star Simu Liu and others, but it was beloved not only by viewers here but millions worldwide after Netflix picked it up. The recent cancellations of Canadian TV comedies with diverse characters make it feel like "Kim's" was perhaps more an outlier than a vanguard, but it still had value to those who hadn't seen themselves on homegrown TV before. As Liu said in a 2021 interview, it was important for "Canadians that quite honestly have not seen themselves onscreen ... to know that you are a part of the fabric of Canadian society and culture and identity." - Debra Yeo
2015 "Schitt's Creek" begins journey to worldwide acclaim
When it debuted in January 2015, there was a lot riding on "Schitt's Creek." National broadcaster CBC was in desperate need of a hit; and having "SCTV" legends Eugene Levy and Catherine O'Hara in the cast meant higher expectations than for your garden variety sitcom. Although we couldn't have known then what a sensation "Schitt's" would become - migrating first to Pop TV in the U.S. and then Netflix on its way to a record Emmy Awards haul in 2020 - the makings of its greatness were present in that first season. This Ontario-made show about a spoiled rich family who loses everything, but finds themselves in the process, was the first of several TV series that brought Canada international acclaim. - Debra Yeo
2014Jian Ghomeshi allegations spark a national discourse
In the fall of 2014, Canada's public broadcaster fired its star radio host, Jian Ghomeshi, in the wake of a series of sexual assault and harassment allegations. Reporting by the Star revealed that three young women, all about 20 years his junior, alleged that Ghomeshi was physically violent with them without their consent during sexual encounters or in the lead-up to sexual encounters. Ghomeshi was charged with four counts of sexual assault and one count of overcoming resistance by choking. In 2015, he faced an additional three charges of sexual assault. And though he was never convicted of sexual assault, the disturbing allegations against one of Canada's best known media personalities sent shock waves through the country, and sparked a national discussion about sexual assault and the cult of personality nearly three years before the #MeToo movement grew to prominence in Hollywood and beyond. - Richie Assaly
2013NXNE music and arts festival soars to new heights
Writing in 2025, it's hard to imagine a world where you might be able to see some of your favourite artists perform live without spending hours on Ticketmaster or forking over a small fortune. But what sounds like a luxury was actually the norm during the early 2010s heyday of North by Northeast (NXNE) - an annual music and arts festival founded in 1995 that curates live music in independent venues across the city - when Yonge-Dundas Square in downtown Toronto was transformed into a massive concert venue hosting dozens of shows from local and international artists. In the summer of 2013, throngs of Toronto music fans jammed into the square to check out free shows from the National, Billy Talent, Social Distortion and Ludacris. It was also the year that NXNE expanded its scope beyond music, film and interactive installation, adding comedy and art showcases across the city. NXNE remains a vital Toronto arts institution today, but cash-strapped music fans will be forgiven for pining for the outdoor mayhem of yesteryear. - Richie Assaly
2012 A year of growth for Toronto's cultural festivals
The year 2012 was a big one for big arts festivals in Toronto. Hot Docs bought the shuttered Bloor Cinema and turned it into the Bloor Hot Docs Cinema, making it their official home base. It was also the year that Just for Laughs launched a Toronto version of its legendary comedy festival - originally dubbed JFL42 - hosting hundreds of the industry's best comics. Both Hot Docs and JFL42, now Just for Laughs Toronto, survived the pandemic but, 12 years later, their destinies remained strangely twinned and slightly precarious: in 2024, the Hot Docs theatre shuttered for months to recoup costs, while JFL skipped its 2024 fest altogether after losing a huge pile of cash (in an email scam, of all things). - Briony Smith
2011Drake's OVO Fest spotlights burgeoning music scene
The inaugural OVO Fest was in 2010, but it was the 2011 edition of the annual summer music festival that acted as both a showcase of Toronto's exploding R&B and hip-hop scene, and a chance for Drake to flex his rapidly rising cultural cachet. Held at the Molson Amphitheatre (today known as the Budweiser Stage) just a few months before the release of Drake's seminal sophomore album "Take Care," the single-day extravaganza featured an extraordinary cohort of surprise guests, including hip-hop luminaries Lil Wayne, Nas, Rick Ross and J. Cole, plus a surprise 30-minute set from the legendary Stevie Wonder. But looking back, perhaps the most significant moment of the event took place earlier in the evening, when the Weeknd took the stage for one of his very first live performances. Who could have predicted that this mysterious singer from Scarborough, whose moody and atmospheric approach to R&B had lit the internet on fire earlier in the year with the release of the "House of Balloons" mixtape, would within years transform into a Super Bowl-calibre megastar? The 2011 edition of OVO Fest not only put Toronto's music scene on the map, it established Drake as one of pop culture's premiere tastemakers. - Richie Assaly
2010Toronto International Film Festival finds its new home
At the start of the 2010 Toronto International Film Festival, the arts organization inaugurated its new home, the Lightbox, with a block party that shut down King Street West and drew about 1,000 revellers to the entertainment district. Seven years in the making, the venue replaced TIFF's old digs at the Art Gallery of Ontario and now hosts the company's year-round programming. Located in the five-level podium of a 46-storey residential tower, the state-of-the-art development features five cinemas, gallery spaces and two restaurants. - Joshua Chong
2009 Toronto theatre titan convicted of fraud and forgery
Garth Drabinsky was one of the theatre industry's most prominent producers in the 1990s. His company, Livent, was behind the long-running Canadian production of "Phantom of the Opera," and also helped usher musicals such as "Ragtime" and "Kiss of the Spider Woman" to Broadway. But by the turn of the century, Drabinsky's business began to unravel as he fought criminal charges in both Canada and the U.S. Drabinsky and his business partner, Myron Gottlieb, were ultimately convicted of fraud and forgery in 2009 in Toronto, marking the end of the decade-long saga and indelibly changing the face of commercial theatre in the city. - Joshua Chong
2008The AGO unveils Frank Gehry-designed wing
When work began on the Art Gallery of Ontario's $276-million renovation in 2004, expectations were high. The man tapped to lead the project was none other than Frank Gehry, the renowned Canadian-American architect who grew up not far from the museum. It also marked Gehry's first commission in his hometown. Four years later, when Gehry's work was unveiled, it received critical acclaim. The AGO's massive new facade, composed of glass and wooden beams, was praised for its modest beauty. Now it has cemented itself as an iconic part of Toronto's growing skyline. - Joshua Chong
2007ROM opens the Michael Lee-Chin Crystal
No structure in Toronto is more controversial than the Royal Ontario Museum's Michael Lee-Chin Crystal, which opened in 2007 with a eye-watering price tag of $416 million. Critical reaction at the time was divisive, echoing the sentiments of the broader public. The Toronto Star's former architecture critic, Christopher Hume, lauded the museum's new wing as "21st-century architecture at its most brilliant." Meanwhile, the Globe and Mail's former architecture critic, Lisa Rochon, was already waiting for the day the "tin pinata" would be torn down and replaced. Nearly two decades later, the Crystal still hangs over Bloor Street, though an ongoing renovation will see it receive a significant facelift. - Joshua Chong
2006Toronto gets a dedicated opera and ballet house
The campaign to build a dedicated venue in Toronto for opera and ballet took so long, hampered by a litany of political and financial challenges, that the Canadian Opera Company's former general director, Richard Bradshaw, called it his "30-year war." When the Four Seasons Centre for the Performing Arts did finally open in 2006, it marked a culmination of that long struggle. The new development on University Avenue was meant to succeed the unwieldy Meridian Hall, formerly known as the O'Keefe Centre and Hummingbird Centre. Though the building initially was greeted with mixed reviews (its rather bland exterior received much of the criticism), it's now widely regarded as one of the best venues acoustically on the continent. - Joshua Chong
2005 A cutting-edge new catwalk debuts
The Six joined the international roster of catwalk cities in 1999 with the launch of Toronto Fashion Week. But designer Vanja Vasic still felt like something was missing in the city's sartorial landscape. Where was the inclusivity? The diversity? The accessibility? She wanted to create a more boundary-pushing event that would welcome all designers, no matter how wild their designs, so she started Fashion Art Toronto (FAT) to promote our city's freakier fashion visionaries. Two decades later, Fashion Week Toronto is no more, but the underdog FAT continues unabated, celebrating 20 years of cutting-edge catwalks. - Briony Smith
2004The Drake Hotel is Toronto's new chic clubhouse
The Drake Hotel helped cement West Queen West as the hot neighbourhood of the era, acting as an honorary clubhouse for everything mid-aughts cool. Lines snaking around the block - even in the dead of winter - were a common sight as the guys, girlies and gays jostled to get into the hottest parties in town. Indie sleaze and bumping dance parties were in full swing at the Drake Underground music space, while folks crowded onto the rooftop Sky Yard for fancy cocktails and packed the patio for brunch (despite the hotel eateries' notoriously terrible service). The Drake would spawn many cottage industries, from the Drake General Store to various outposts both in Toronto (we still mourn the Drake Commissary up on Sterling) and further afield (out in happening Prince Edward County), but the old days at the OG hotel will always hold a special place in many millennial hipsters' hearts. - Briony Smith
2003The Rolling Stones help Toronto recover from SARS
An outdoor concert featuring the Stones, Rush, AC/DC and Justin Timberlake. Appearances by Dan Aykroyd and Catherine O'Hara. Premier Ralph Klein serving up Alberta beef sandwiches. Nearly 500,000 fans braving the sweltering summer heat. It's a miracle to think that "Molson Canadian Rocks for Toronto" - the official name of the massive concert benefit at Downsview Park better known by the moniker "SARS-Stock" - was pulled together in less than two months. Organized in the wake of a SARS outbreak that killed 44 Ontarians and devastated Toronto's tourism sector, the benefit attracted legions of fans from across the country and from the U.S. to become the largest outdoor ticketed event in Canadian history. More than that, SARS-Stock was a display of immense resiliency, showing the world that Toronto will always get back on its feet. - Richie Assaly
2002Roy Thomson Hall tries to fix an acoustical mess
The opening of Roy Thomson Hall in 1982 offers important lessons about how not to advertise a new venue. In the run-up to the unveiling, press releases promised the new home of the Toronto Symphony Orchestra would not just be a fine hall, but the finest hall in the world. Talk about overhyping something to the extreme. In reality, the hall probably offered one of the worst acoustical experiences, described by critics and musicians as exceedingly cold and with dead spots throughout the auditorium. In 2002, after years of complaints, the hall finally closed for several months to facilitate a $20-million renovation, which saw the addition of a wooden canopy above the stage to help improve acoustics. It by no means turned Roy Thomson Hall into "the finest hall in the world," but the changes were noticeable and largely for the better. - Joshua Chong
2001A "Degrassi" reboot introduces us to a fresh-faced Drake
"Degrassi: The Next Generation," which first hit the airwaves in the fall of 2001, was the fourth series in the Canadian-made "Degrassi" franchise and would go on to be the longest, running for 14 seasons through to 2015. During that time, it became one of the most watched youth dramas in North America. And not only did it put Toronto on the map (the series was filmed across the city, including on its namesake De Grassi Street, near the Riverdale neighbourhood), it also introduced a cadre of fresh-faced actors who would go on to be household names. Drake, anyone? - Joshua Chong
2000"Mamma Mia!" makes a splash in Toronto
"Mamma Mia," the ABBA-inspired jukebox romp, marked its North American premiere at Mirvish's Royal Alexandra Theatre in 2000. By the time it closed five years later, the production cemented itself as one of the most successful theatre shows in the city's history. It brought in more than $200 million in ticket sales and was seen by more than 2.8 million people, equivalent to about half the population of Toronto at the time. The show also ushered in a new era of jukebox musicals in the city and propelled Canadian actor Louise Pitre to a Tony nomination in 2002 when she reprised her role on Broadway. - Joshua Chong

Document 2 of 500

Capital gains tax hike and expansion of dental care hang in the balance as minority Liberal government faces near-certain election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/capital-gains-tax-hike-expansion-dental-care-hang/docview/3159702680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Capital+gains+tax+hike+and+expansion+of+dental+care+hang+in+the+balance+as+minority+Liberal+government+faces+near-certain+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Parliament is suspended. The country's governing party is absorbed with a race to crown a new leader. An early election may be in the offing. And Canada is staring down a potentially punishing trade war.
While a prorogued Parliament only puts the brakes on the legislative business of governing, there are a multitude of distractions - and uncertain outcomes - that could jeopardize a number of existing and planned programs and policies.
Here's the status of some of those initiatives, and whether they're at risk.
Capital gains tax increase
The Liberal government's capital gains tax hike is going ahead - for now.
First announced in last year's federal budget, the changes mean that for individuals, the inclusion rate - the amount of capital gains subject to income tax - have jumped from 50 per cent to 66.7 per cent on capital gains earnings over $250,000. Now, two thirds of the profits made from an asset's sale, as long as those profits exceed $250,000, will be taxed as income. The same inclusion rate would also apply to corporations and trusts for all capital gains, not just those exceeding $250,000.
But the Liberals hived off the tax changes from its budget bill, and instead tabled the changes in a notice of ways and means motion. The proposal was never actually enshrined in law because the Conservatives gridlocked most legislative business last fall. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's decision to prorogue Parliament until late March has further put the proposed changes on ice.
But the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) says it will still administer the proposed legislation regardless of Parliament's suspension, because "parliamentary convention dictates that taxation proposals are effective as soon as the government tables a notice of ways and means motion."
The CRA told the Star that taxpayers will be able to fill out the relevant forms by Jan. 31. The agency said that if the government announces post-suspension that it won't proceed with the changes, the CRA would stop administering the measure and help process "corrective reassessments of implicated returns."
Could that happen? Maybe.
Parliament will return with a new Liberal leader and prime minister. That person could be former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, who, despite backing the changes when she delivered her 2024 budget, said Thursday she would repeal them due to U.S. President Donald Trump's return to the White House.
The tax hike could also face further challenges if an early spring election is called: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has said that if he were to form government, he would roll back the changes.
Canada Disability Benefit
The 2024 budget also promised to launch the country's long-anticipated federal disability benefit in July of this year.
But eligible Canadians cannot yet apply for the aid because regulations to cement the details have not been finalized.
Waleed Saleem, a spokesperson for Persons with Disabilities Minister Kamal Khera, said prorogation is not expected to hamper the regulatory process and the benefit's rollout.
An early election call could, however, scupper the planned July 1 start date for a benefit that has already faced its fair share of setbacks and criticisms.
Rabia Khedr, the national director of Disability Without Poverty, said delivering the benefit in July must "be an administrative priority," even if Trump's tariff threats are presently seizing government officials.
The Conservatives have not said what they would do with the benefit. The party voted in favour of the legislation when it was before Parliament.
Dental care expansion and pharmacare rollout
Two big-ticket health-care items are top of Health Minister Mark Holland's agenda in the weeks ahead: the final expansion of Canada's dental care program and getting the first phase of a pharmacare program out the door.
The Liberals' dental care plan is currently available for eligible seniors aged 65 and older, youth under the age of 18, and adults with a valid disability tax credit certificate. This year, the plan is meant to open up to millions of other eligible Canadians, but no date for that expansion has been set.
Health Canada told the Star that prorogation does not impact the existing implementation of the program, but said details on the program's expansion are coming soon.
The pharmacare program, meanwhile, could be facing an uphill battle.
Holland has been tasked with signing deals with all provinces and territories, something he hoped would happen this spring. Those agreements would outline a plan to cover diabetes medication and birth control drugs and some devices. So far, only a memorandum of understanding has been signed with British Columbia, and no actual deals have been inked.
The runway to hit that goal has narrowed significantly with a possible spring election call and a federal government preoccupied with U.S. tariff threats and a leadership race.
Holland told the Star earlier this month he hopes to land some of those agreements "very soon," particularly before a potential Conservative-led government could undo them.
National School Food Program
Also hustling behind the scenes is Families, Children and Social Development Minister Jenna Sudds, who is tasked with rolling out the Liberals' National School Food Program.
Deals to launch the program have been struck with Ontario, Manitoba, Newfoundland and Labrador, and Prince Edward Island. Those provinces have received funding to deliver meals this school year.
A spokesperson for Sudds' office said the future of the program "is at stake" should a Poilievre-led government form and that other provinces and territories should sign on as quickly as possible. The Conservative leader has previously criticized the program.
Carbon tax revenues for First Nations
A question mark is also dangling over hundreds of millions of dollars First Nations are owed in carbon pricing proceeds.
The federal government's carbon pricing regime is intended to return some of the proceeds it collects to Indigenous communities across Canada, but they have yet to receive the more than half a billion in cash they have been owed for years, prompting court challenges from First Nations groups who have called the regime discriminatory.
Amid the uncertainty of a potential early election, those challenging Ottawa's carbon levy in court have called on the government to immediately dole out the money or guarantee that it will start to flow even if there is a change at the top.
Kate Kempton, legal counsel for Chiefs of Ontario, said Ottawa has since come up with a distribution formula that lays out what each First Nation is set to receive, and that she is reviewing those decisions now.
To access the money, Kempton said First Nations must sign a funding agreement with the federal government, and said she had been told that Parliament's suspension would not affect that process.
But Kempton told the Star she's received no assurances that an early election would not affect the doling out of the money.
While Poilievre has campaigned heavily to "axe" Ottawa's consumer carbon pricing scheme, two Liberal leadership front-runners, Freeland and Mark Carney, have also indicated they would drop the federal policy.
Environment and Climate Change Canada did not respond to the Star's request for comment by deadline.
CAPTION: No date has been set for the expansion of Canada's dental care program.

Subject: Conservatism; Federal budget; Food programs; Carbon; Native North Americans; Tariffs; Leadership; Dental care; Parliaments; Disability; Prime ministers; Capital gains; Tax increases

Document 3 of 500

Gould, Freeland or Carney? Liberals wait for their leadership front-runners to set themselves apart

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gould-freeland-carney-liberals-wait-their/docview/3159702554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gould%2C+Freeland+or+Carney%3F+Liberals+wait+for+their+leadership+front-runners+to+set+themselves+apart&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The race to replace Justin Trudeau is a sprint to March 9 that's narrowed to two leading contenders, a third aspiring to come from behind, and a quartet of also-running names that most Canadians don't know.
In that tight field, there are few distinctions at this stage between the front-runners: Mark Carney, the brainy outsider; Chrystia Freeland, the brainy insider; and Karina Gould, the young insider aiming high.
Since Trudeau announced three weeks ago that he intended to step down, those vying to replace him as leader of the Liberal Party of Canada and prime minister will undoubtedly put forward a different face to Canadians.
What that would look like is, so far, unclear.
While there are some differences between the three leading contenders, insider/outsider-wise, there's not much differentiation when it comes to where they stand on big policy questions. Yet.
Carney - the outsider - has never run for political office, having turned down several opportunities in the past, but has top insider connections. Courted by Trudeau, he is godfather to Freeland's son, and served as a central banker in Canada and the U.K. when Conservative governments were in power.
Freeland - the insider - ran for the Trudeau Liberals back in 2013, when it wasn't cool to do so and the party was in third place. She rose to become deputy prime minister and Canada's first female finance minister, and brought with her a global Rolodex from her previous life as a journalist.
Gould rode the Trudeau wave in 2015 and, at age 29, became the youngest woman to serve as a Canadian cabinet minister in 2017. She is also the first woman to give birth while serving in cabinet.
Most believe the horse race is really between Carney or Freeland, with Gould well behind, positioning herself for the future.
Carney and Freeland both struggled to communicate effectively at their campaign launches, their retail political weaknesses on view.
And in the short period of time that remains, their challenges are different, says one senior Liberal who is remaining neutral in the race: Carney has to prove to Liberals he can do it, while Freeland has to disprove to people she can't do it.
If either Carney or Freeland get the crown, there's little doubt they'll swing the Liberal party's economic compass more to the centre, if not the centre-right - at least based on their hints to date, if not their track records.
But if you're suddenly intrigued by what Trudeau's successor might stand for, or are casting an eye at the Liberals for the first time, the first few weeks of the race haven't been very revealing.
There have been no candidate debates, although Freeland called Friday for four of them. In an open letter, she said there should be two in each official language - and as soon as possible. She also said all candidates should publicly pledge to run for a seat in the House of Commons in the next election "no matter the outcome of the leadership race."
Freeland's demands are an attempt to highlight the difference between her and Carney, who has offered only oddly vague assurances he would run to become a member of Parliament.
None of the leading candidates has rolled out much in the way of substantive policy, although they hint that's to come.
What Carney and Freeland have said, in all-too-brief and controlled encounters with reporters ("one question, no followups") on dealing with Donald Trump's tariffs or on ditching the consumer price on carbon doesn't draw any contrast between them.
Instead, it draws contrasts between the candidates and Trudeau.
Both Carney and Freeland say Canada should meet the challenge of Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs with robust, dollar-for-dollar counter-tariffs. Trudeau has said he supports "dollar-for-dollar" reciprocal tariffs in principle but intends to levy graduated, escalating countermeasures.
Both Carney and Freeland reject the Trudeau Liberals' signature climate change policy, a consumer price on carbon (Gould would freeze the planned increases), but offer no details on a climate action plan to replace it.
Freeland has abandoned her 2024 budget plan to expand the inclusion rate for capital gains taxes in the face of Trump's "radical" plans to draw investment south of the border. Gould says it should be revisited, and she would make permanent Freeland's temporary GST holiday on children's clothing and diapers.
It is possible Carney would also drop the capital gains tax changes as part of his pledge to build "the strongest economy in the G7," but hard to say. He never delivered the economic growth report Trudeau asked him for, with the last discussions on it held in November, according to a source. Carney has held only one news conference since launching his leadership bid, and was not asked the question directly.
Carney, Freeland and Gould all rebuff the American president's "51st state" talk, with Freeland talking the toughest.
Beyond that, Freeland and Gould are pledging to make the Liberal party more democratically responsive to its members. Both want more regular party policy conventions, and have taken shots at Trudeau.
"We can never again be in a position where the leader is the only person who decides who the leader is," Freeland said Thursday.
She proposes automatic leadership reviews at regular biennial conventions, or an ability for caucus to trigger a vote by the national membership. Gould says Liberal MPs could adopt powers offered by a law called the Reform Act, which gives caucus power to trigger a leadership review, but would leave it to caucus members to decide that.
The Conservatives say there is no difference in any of them.
But there are six weeks to go for the candidates to distinguish themselves, not just from Trudeau or Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, but from each other.
It's a bit of a lopsided race so far.
Carney has gathered more caucus and cabinet endorsements. Freeland has got a handful of cabinet minsters rooting for her, and has gathered good grassroots organizers and the support of the Ontario and Quebec caucus chairs. Gould is pitching to bring a generational shift within the party, and a focus on young Liberals.
The others - Ottawa MP Chandra Arya, Nova Scotia MP Jaime Battiste and former MPs Frank Baylis and Ruby Dhalla - are running with little chance of success.
At this stage, each camp is very much focused on signing up members to vote before Monday's registration deadline.
The debates over policy will come. But the leadership campaigns are also aware of the need for the party to emerge united after the race, with little to no time remaining to smooth over any bitter divisions before the Liberals must plunge into an almost-certain election campaign this spring.
CAPTION: Karina Gould speaks to reporters after filing her paperwork to run for the Liberal leadership, in Ottawa on Jan. 23, 2025.Liberal MP Chrystia Freeland, candidate for the leadership of the Liberal Party of Canada, answers questions from journalists as she makes her way to a meeting of the Liberal caucus on Parliament Hill on Jan. 23, 2025.Mark Carney, candidate for the leadership of the Liberal Party of Canada, participates in a scrum with journalists on the sidewalk outside the venue of his campaign event in Ottawa on Jan. 23, 2025.

Document 4 of 500

Doug Ford insists he's not rushing into snap Feb. 27 Ontario election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-insists-hes-not-rushing-into-snap-feb/docview/3159427398/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+insists+he%27s+not+rushing+into+snap+Feb.+27+Ontario+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford insists he's not rushing into a snap Feb. 27 Ontario election to get in front of a federal vote expected this spring.
Ontarians traditionally prefer different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, so some Progressive Conservative strategists have privately expressed concern about their provincial electoral prospects if, as polls suggest, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is triumphant.
Asked Friday if he were "scared" of a Poilievre victory, Ford said: "No, not whatsoever."
Pressed on whether he was scrambling to get ahead of potential federal spending cuts should the Conservatives succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals, the Ontario Tory leader shook his head.
"My job - when we hopefully get a big mandate - is going to be standing up to any federal government that wants to cut health care from Ontario, cut jobs from Ontario, to tax people from Ontario," Ford said in Brampton where he announced the downtown section of the still-unfinished Hazel McCallion Line LRT would be tunnelled at an undisclosed cost.
Last weekend in Mississauga, Poilievre bumped into provincial Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at a community event where they had a constructive conversation after the federal Tory chief warmly greeted her.
"Pierre mentioned to Bonnie that he is a fan of her housing plan," said a senior Liberal strategist, speaking confidentially in order to recount details of the impromptu chat.
That's a reference to Crombie's plan to make housing more affordable by scrapping the land-transfer tax for first-time homebuyers and seniors who are downsizing, eliminating development charges on new homes, and interest-free loans for renters facing financial emergencies that could result in evictions.
"Look, these are two politicians who are focusing their plans on what people need - they couldn't be more different than Ford and Trudeau. Makes sense that they would be talking about it," said the provincial Liberal.
The federal Tory leader's office would not comment on the discussion. There is no love lost between the national Conservatives and the PCs, who work closely with Trudeau's Liberals.
Poilievre's "axe the tax" mantra is one reason he is leading in polls against Trudeau.
Crombie was one of the first prominent Liberals to distance herself from the consumer price on carbon, a stance now adopted by would-be federal Grit leaders like Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland.
Ford stressed the main rationale for the early vote is U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent levies on Canadian products as of Feb. 1.
"I'll be seeing the lieutenant governor on Tuesday (for the issuing of the writs Wednesday). We need a mandate from the people to fight against Donald Trump's tariffs, the attack he's coming against our families, our businesses, our communities," he said.
The Tories already have a majority at Queen's Park and an election, which will cost between $150 million and $175 million, was not supposed to be held until June 2026.
"With a strong mandate, we will be able to fight with Donald Trump to make sure we stop the tariffs and make sure that we give certainty to the people of Ontario," said Ford, adding it may take "tens of billions of dollars" in stimulus funding to keep the province's economy afloat.
Aside from the Trump tariffs and a Poilievre victory, the Tories are worried about the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal and would like to strike the Liberals when they may be too busy with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie.
Even though he was at a government announcement in Brampton, Ford struck a fiercely partisan tone, repeatedly attacking Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
"Imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal, it would be an absolute disaster, and God help us if that ever happens," he said dismissively, noting he will go to Washington twice during the campaign on previously planned trips to lobby U.S. officials.
No meeting between Ford and Trump is expected and the PC campaign will have to foot the tab for his February jaunts stateside.
Rival leaders accused him of being a political opportunist by triggering an election at a time of uncertainty.
"He thinks he can con Ontarians with an early election to escape his record - but we won't let him," said Stiles.
"Doug Ford has been on the side of wealthy insiders while life for working people has gotten harder and more expensive. How can we trust him to stand up against Donald Trump and his billionaires when he can't deliver on making life more affordable, getting people a family doctor, or building more homes?" said the New Democratic leader.
Crombie, who has yet to disclose which Mississauga riding she will run in, said "with the looming threat of tariffs and the challenges facing our province, Ford has chosen to prioritize his own political survival over the people he was elected to serve."
"He's not a leader - he's a coward," said the Liberal leader, a former Mississauga mayor.
"Doug Ford can attack me all he wants. But as premier, I wouldn't run from my responsibilities. This snap election isn't about Trump or tariffs, it's about Doug Ford scrambling to outrun the RCMP investigation into his Greenbelt scandal," she added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "Doug Ford is putting his job before your job" when Trump tariffs could throw 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
"All the things that a provincial government should do - build affordable homes, make sure we have doctors and nurses, safe schools - Doug Ford has walked away from," said Schreiner.
Ford bristled when reporters raised parallels to former Liberal premier David Peterson's ill-advised 1990 snap election that led to a surprise NDP victory at a time of angst over national unity due to the collapse of the Meech Lake Accord that, if passed, would have seen Quebec sign the Constitution.
"I love you media. You guys are classic. But 35 years ago, he woke up and decided to call election. He wasn't facing the largest attack in the history of our country, attack against our families, against what they do."
Peterson is currently chair of Torstar, the Star's parent company.
But there is some other Ontario political history Ford may be abiding by.
According to the "Underhill balance theory," Ontarians tend to opt for different parties in office at the same time provincially and federally. The phenomenon has held true for about 71 of the past 82 years.
Upon dissolution next Wednesday, there are 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.
Ford will head into the campaign without his nephew, Citizenship and Multiculturalism Minister Michael Ford, as the candidate in York-South Weston.
The younger Ford, a former school trustee and city councillor who took a leave of absence from his duties last year, said he was leaving provincial politics to start "a new chapter in my life."
CAPTION: Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie explains why it might be in Doug Ford's favour to call an election very soonOntario Premier Doug Ford attends a news conference at Queen's Park Legislature in Toronto on December 12, 2024.Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre chats with Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at a community event last weekend in Mississauga.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Liberal+leadership+hopefuls+flop+out+of+the+gate%2C+Opinion%2C+Jan.+21+Elon+Musk+responds+to+backlash+after+his+%27odd-looking+salute%27+at+Donald+Trump%27s+inauguration%2C+Jan.+21+Most+provinces+have+dropped+daycare+costs+to+%2410%2Fday.+Why+are+some+Toronto+parents+still+paying+full+fees%3F+Jan.+19+Desperately+seeking+a+doctor%2C+Jan.+21+Personal+information+of+Ontario+students+going+back+decades+may+have+been+breached+in+cyberattack%2C+Jan.+20&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada needs a statesperson, not Tory slogans
Liberal leadership hopefuls flop out of the gate,
Opinion, Jan. 21
Éric Blais writes that the Liberal leadership race is devoid of sizzle and slogans. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is great at giving us slogans and nothing more. I am not looking for these in a prime minister. In these complex times, I am looking for a statesperson. I am just finishing Mark Carney's book "Value(s): Building a Better World for All." In it, he lays out a progressive vision of how he would deal with the pressing issues of climate change, world hunger, the appalling and rising gap between the rich and the poor, and much more. He is truly a man of intelligence, inspiration and integrity - there's a slogan for you!
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
What happened to the world's moral compass?
Elon Musk responds to
backlash after his 'odd-looking salute' at Donald Trump's inauguration, Jan. 21
As the child of Holocaust survivors, I am horrified by what appeared to be a Nazi salute by Elon Musk during Trump's inauguration. These are the kinds of people Trump surrounds himself with, the people he takes advice from, the people who will ultimately shape the political and social direction the U.S. will take for the next four years. But having taken root, this contagion will likely continue to spread unless it's stopped in its tracks. What has happened to the world's moral compass?
Michael Bines, Toronto
Ontario should cancel Starlink deal with Musk
It is time for the Ontario government to rethink and cancel its deal with Elon Musk's Starlink service. Musk has proven himself to be a despicable human being who, during his "performance" at Donald Trump's inauguration ceremony, seemed to enthusiastically raise his arm in the Nazi salute. Premier Doug Ford appears to have taken on the role of Captain Canada and has a large part to play in preparing a Canadian response to potential threats from the Trump presidency. Musk has an outsized role the Trump administration. I am sure that there are millions of Ontarians like me who don't want to see our hard-earned tax dollars flowing to Musk. There is no room in business conducted with our tax dollars for anyone like Musk.
Volker Triebe, Midland, Ont.
Cost for a family
living in Toronto
is not sustainable
Most provinces have dropped daycare costs to $10/day. Why are some Toronto parents still paying full fees? Jan. 19
Quebec, where my wife and I raised our daughter, does not participate in the Canada-wide Early Learning and Child Care program. Instead, Quebec has had its own daycare subsidy for more than 30 years. And thank God. It was hard enough then to pay for pre-school, full-day care for our daughter. I can't imagine what it would be like now. Actually, I can imagine it. And so can Premier Doug Ford and Education Minister Jill Dunlop. If Toronto and Ontario wish to keep hard-working, taxpaying people here to raise families and buy homes, subsidizing daycare has to be part of the package.
The woman featured in Brendan Kennedy's story leaves her preschooler in a daycare that charges market rate. That's $2,090 per month. The woman also has an infant she was hoping would be starting in the same daycare. The market rate for that child is $2,565. That's almost $4,700 a month. Let's assume the couple have a million-dollar house with a 25-year amortization period and a five per cent fixed mortgage rate. They're paying more than $5,000 a month for their house. Add insurance, utilities, the ever-rising cost of groceries, transit or a car and, to paraphrase Ford, you'd have to be a few fries short of a Happy Meal to see this is not a life. This is not sustainable.
Raymond Beauchemin, Hamilton
Harris 'common sense' kicked off doctor shortages
Desperately seeking a doctor, Jan. 21
This article sounded as though people "desperately seeking a doctor" is a new phenomenon. We had to do the same thing in 1999 in Sudbury during the infamous Mike Harris PC Common Sense Revolution from 1995 to 2002. We were lucky, as we lined up in a warm bowling alley to be interviewed and finally accepted by the new doctor.
For the past 18 months, the federal Conservative party has been lobbying Canadians with the same "common sense" stuff and with promises to "axe the tax" and balance the budget. I shudder to think about the consequences. If the Conservatives do win the next election, would they cut the $10-a-day child care? Or the new dental care program, or the new pharmacare deal, or get rid of any progress we have made in the area of climate change or protection of our environment? Or maybe all of them? We all have one vote and hopefully people will think very carefully about this when marking their X.
Sharon Mason,
Wasaga Beach, Ont.
Federal Tories' claims based on hate, not facts
Canada's Conservative leader started out claiming "everything is broken" (which was not true) and that our very real problems with inflation, housing prices, budget deficits and crime on our subway could all be blamed on one man (and now Liberals in general). Was Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also responsible for the same problems being experienced in the U.S. and other G7 countries? The absurdity of these claims demonstrates that all this political name calling, lying and posturing has nothing to do with common sense: it is U.S.-style, hate-driven politics at their worst. Hate has never solved any human problems - it has only made them worse.
David Crowley, Richmond Hill
School breach
is despicable
Personal information of Ontario students going back decades may have been breached in cyberattack, Jan. 20
Why are school boards outsourcing children's and teachers' private information to a U.S. company? To have children's personal information compromised is despicable. What's wrong with the minister of education to approve personal info collection and not ensure it is looked after? No wonder people resent handing over and disclosing information.
Sheldon Shepherd, Toronto

Document 6 of 500

Post-secondary funding crisis isn't a big issue to Ford or voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/post-secondary-funding-crisis-isnt-big-issue-ford/docview/3159093699/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Post-secondary+funding+crisis+isn%27t+a+big+issue+to+Ford+or+voters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's one issue you'll hardly hear about on the campaign trail ahead:
The crisis in post-secondary education.
Colleges and universities are core to the knowledge economy of tomorrow, but you wouldn't know it today. Most days, you'll hear Doug Ford talk about our highly skilled workforce as Ontario's biggest draw, yet nothing ranks lower than higher education in our political discourse.
There's a reason campuses across Ontario are forever underfunded by the premier: Ford's government sees little political payoff from investing more funds and, more importantly, senses little electoral risk from putting less money down.
So why don't political parties care more about Ontario's 24 colleges and 23 universities?
Here's a better question: Why don't more voters care more about the crisis on campus?
Yes, students surely worry, but they don't vote in large enough numbers to count. True, parents certainly care, but you'll never see them rise up and rally for their grown children as they do for younger kids in kindergarten or high school.
On the eve of an election, the results are already in for higher education: It doesn't rate as a vote-determining issue, which is why politicians don't put it on the agenda.
And so when colleges lay off staff, as they did again this month, it barely makes news. When professors threaten to strike, as they did again the other day, it's old news.
When universities cut their courses, as they did this semester, it's more of the same retrenchment. When our institutions of higher learning go deeper in debt, as they have in the past year, there's no one to dig them out.
How did we get here? Perennial funding shortfalls are nothing new, but Ford's Progressive Conservatives did what no other government had done before.
Shortly after taking power in 2018, with colleges and universities starved for money, the premier further reduced their cash flow by ordering every campus to cut tuition by 10 per cent. It was a classic populist gesture from a premier who plays pocketbook politics, knowing that affordability matters most to voters.
But those tuition dollars weren't his to cut - the money was remitted by students.
More to the point, his government didn't consider making up the difference to keep universities and colleges whole, leaving them in a deeper fiscal hole.
It only got worse. In the six years since, the premier has compelled post-secondary institutions to keep tuition fees frozen at that same reduced level, while inflation rose steadily year after year.
That's no way to run an institution, as even the minister of post-secondary institutions, Nolan Quinn, surely understands. After all, Quinn is a college graduate and the proud owner of a Dairy Queen in Eastern Ontario.
If you'd told Quinn that headquarters had just dictated a 10 per cent price decrease in chocolate-dipped cones from afar and expected local Dairy Queen outlets to make up the difference, he'd swallow hard. If you told Quinn that DQ HQ had also demanded a perpetual price freeze for the Misty Freeze slushy, he'd wonder how his own franchise would stay solvent.
Yet Quinn is just following orders from head office at Queen's Park. He is the fourth minister in six years, unable to push back against a premier determined to cut higher education down to size.
Even a government-appointed panel of experts, hand-picked to avoid embarrassing the Tories, dissented from the party line: A blue-ribbon panel recommended a major tuition increase, but the government balked last year, offering only a short-term bailout that ignored warnings of a long-term flame-out.
Instead, Ford put forward what amounts to a perverse Ponzi scheme: Underfunding (and undercharging) for domestic students, cross-subsidized by overcharging (and overcrowding) for a seemingly unlimited number of foreign students whose endless pool of revenues would keep campuses afloat.
It was never sustainable, nor desirable. As the money rolled in, colleges recruited so many international students that they emerged as the majority on many campuses - shifting the demographic balance in the classroom.
Colleges (unlike universities) are on the provincial balance sheet, so red ink on campuses dragged down the overall Ontario deficit. By turning public colleges into profit centres, the Tories buffed up their own annual budget.
But a sudden surge in foreign students overwhelmed inner-city housing supplies. Recurring visa abuses sparked a public clamour about a distorted agenda.
When the federal government belatedly cracked down on a student visa program out of control, reducing the allocations for each province, the fiscal fallout was inevitable. The sudden declines in foreign enrolments amounted to shock treatment for campuses that were already financially stressed, and are now doubly distressed.
Ultimately, it all came crashing down - leaving colleges and universities in the lurch. And yet the premier has never paid a political price.
Today, with a campaign perhaps just days away, Ford's Tories are riding high in public opinion polls - not least because they have kept tuition fees unsustainably low.
(Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at Toronto Metropolitan University and also at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy).
CAPTION: When colleges and universities lay off staff and cancel programs, as Sheridan did last fall, it barely registers with most voters, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo

Document 7 of 500

With first fee paid, candidates rush for endorsements: Freeland, Carney have gathered support from top members, Gould goes grassroots

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/with-first-fee-paid-candidates-rush-endorsements/docview/3159093681/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+first+fee+paid%2C+candidates+rush+for+endorsements%3A+Freeland%2C+Carney+have+gathered+support+from+top+members%2C+Gould+goes+grassroots&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal leadership contenders cleared the first hurdle with their initial entry fee deposit due Thursday. And now the rush is on to line up caucus endorsements and organizers as they push through a short timeline in their bids to become party leader.
Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney and former government House leader Karina Gould have all paid their $50,000 deposits, but the pressure to raise cash doesn't stop there, with another $50,000 due Monday and the balance of their $350,000 entry fees due in a matter of weeks.
Ottawa MP Chandra Arya, Nova Scotia MP Jaime Battiste and former MPs Frank Baylis and Ruby Dhalla have also said they submitted the necessary applications to run for leader.
The party has 10 days to formally approve of leadership contenders.
Here's a look at who the top contenders have attracted so far, with just 46 days before the party's new leader will be announced on March 9.
Mark Carney
Despite never having been a member of Liberal caucus, the former central bank governor has secured the endorsements of more than a dozen cabinet ministers, including big names like Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, Emergency Preparedness Minister Harjit Sajjan and Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree, three of the Liberals' significant regional organizers.
Joly started her own political party in Montreal when she ran to be that city's mayor more than a decade ago. She said Thursday she is focusing on her job as foreign affairs minister and the threat of U.S. tariffs, but is endorsing Carney in the hopes her organization will also come to his cause.
"When it comes to Mr. Carney, I have a lot of respect for him and I've endorsed him," she said. "And I sent a clear message to my base and to my organizers and to my volunteers to send the signal."
Anandasangaree has a significant volunteer base in the Greater Toronto Area that he has used to help other candidates in past campaigns, and broad connections in the Tamil community across the county. Sajjan routinely headlines fundraisers for the Liberal party in British Columbia where he holds significant influence, especially within the South Asian community. MP Sukh Dhaliwal is also endorsing Carney and is a big organizer in the Sikh community.
Carney also has the endorsement of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's former principal secretary, Gerald Butts, who stressed he is not a formal part of the campaign. Most of the prime minister's current staff members, including chief of staff Katie Telford, are staying on the sidelines.
Chrystia Freeland
The former finance minister, whose dramatic resignation accelerated the caucus revolt that prompted Trudeau's decision to resign, has attracted the support of almost two dozen MPs and cabinet ministers.
Flanked by Justice Minister Arif Virani and International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen ahead of a Liberal caucus meeting Thursday, Freeland highlighted the support she has from two of the party's regional caucus chairs, Ontario's Michael Coteau and Quebec's Stéphane Lauzon.
"I'm especially grateful to the people who bring the caucus together, do the hard work in our caucus," she said.
Hussen is anticipated to deliver votes for Freeland in Toronto, Winnipeg and Edmonton among their Somali, Sudanese, Nigerian and Muslim communities. Freeland is counting on MP Lena Metlege Diab to help deliver Lebanese community votes.
Freeland has also attracted the support of cabinet ministers Mark Holland, Terry Beech and Diane Lebouthillier, as well as former ministers Dan Vandal and Marie Claude Bibeau.
Most cabinet ministers from Quebec have thrown their support to Carney so far, leaving Freeland needing to make up ground in that province.
Freeland pledged major party reforms on Thursday, including giving caucus the authority to initiate a leadership review. Despite unrest growing in the Liberal caucus about Trudeau's performance over the past year, there was no mechanism to replace him until he decided to resign. Freeland said that can't be the case going forward.
Karina Gould
Gould's entry into the race was the most unexpected. Speaking to reporters Thursday, she admitted she had only been organizing for the past two weeks.
So far, her caucus support is also limited, with only Hamilton MP Lisa Hepfner and Oakville MP Pam Damoff standing up with her.
Gould said that ultimately it won't be her caucus colleagues who decide the next Liberal leader.
"The leadership of the party is decided by the membership, and what I am hearing from the membership across the country is how excited they are by my candidacy," she said.
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star

Document 8 of 500

Why doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Because you don't care either

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doesnt-doug-ford-care-about-funding-colleges/docview/3159093499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+doesn%27t+Doug+Ford+care+about+funding+colleges+and+universities%3F+Because+you+don%27t+care+either&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's one issue you'll hardly hear about on the campaign trail ahead:
The crisis in post-secondary education.
Colleges and universities are core to the knowledge economy of tomorrow, but you wouldn't know it today. Most days, you'll hear Doug Ford talk about our highly skilled workforce as Ontario's biggest draw, yet nothing ranks lower than higher education in our political discourse.
There's a reason campuses across Ontario are forever underfunded by the premier: Ford's government sees little political payoff from investing more funds and, more importantly, senses little electoral risk from putting less money down.
So why don't political parties care more about Ontario's 24 colleges and 23 universities?
Here's a better question: Why don't more voters care more about the crisis on campus?
Yes, students surely worry, but they don't vote in large enough numbers to count. True, parents certainly care, but you'll never see them rise up and rally for their grown children as they do for younger kids in kindergarten or high school.
On the eve of an election, the results are already in for higher education: It doesn't rate as a vote-determining issue, which is why politicians don't put it on the agenda.
And so when colleges lay off staff, as they did again this month, it barely makes news. When professors threaten to strike, as they did again the other day, it's old news.
When universities cut their courses, as they did this semester, it's more of the same retrenchment. When our institutions of higher learning go deeper in debt, as they have in the last year, there's no one to dig them out.
How did we get here? Perennial funding shortfalls are nothing new, but Ford's Progressive Conservatives did what no other government had done before.
Shortly after taking power in 2018, with colleges and universities starved for money, the premier further reduced their cash flow by ordering every campus to cut tuition by 10 per cent. It was a classic populist gesture from a premier who plays pocketbook politics, knowing that affordability matters most to voters.
But those tuition dollars weren't his to cut - the money was remitted by students. More to the point, his government didn't consider making up the difference to keep universities and colleges whole, leaving them in a deeper fiscal hole.
It only got worse. In the six years since, the premier has compelled post-secondary institutions to keep tuition fees frozen at that same reduced level, while inflation rose steadily year after year.
That's no way to run an institution, as even the minister of post-secondary institutions, Nolan Quinn, surely understands. After all, Quinn is a college graduate and the proud owner of a Dairy Queen in Eastern Ontario.
If you'd told Quinn that headquarters had just dictated a 10 per cent price decrease in chocolate dipped cones from afar and expected local Dairy Queen outlets to make up the difference, he'd swallow hard. If you told Quinn that DQ HQ had also demanded a perpetual price freeze for the Misty Freeze slushy, he'd wonder how his own franchise would stay solvent.
Yet Quinn is just following orders from head office at Queen's Park. He is the fourth minister in six years, unable to push back against a premier determined to cut higher education down to size.
Even a government-appointed panel of experts, hand-picked to avoid embarrassing the Tories, dissented from the party line: A blue-ribbon panel recommended a major tuition increase but the government balked last year, offering only a short-term bailout that ignored warnings of a long-term flame-out.
Instead, Ford put forward what amounts to a perverse Ponzi scheme: Underfunding (and undercharging) for domestic students, cross-subsidized by overcharging (and overcrowding) for a seemingly unlimited number of foreign students whose endless pool of revenues would keep campuses afloat.
It was never sustainable, nor desirable. As the money rolled in, colleges recruited so many international students that they emerged as the majority on many campuses - shifting the demographic balance in the classroom.
Colleges (unlike universities) are on the provincial balance sheet, so red ink on campuses dragged down the overall Ontario deficit. By turning public colleges into profit centres, the Tories buffed up their own annual budget.
But a sudden surge in foreign students overwhelmed inner city housing supplies. Recurring visa abuses sparked a public clamour about a distorted agenda.
When the federal government belatedly cracked down on a student visa program out of control, reducing the allocations for each province, the fiscal fallout was inevitable. The sudden declines in foreign enrolments amounted to shock treatment for campuses that were already financially stressed, and are now doubly distressed.
Ultimately, it all came crashing down - leaving colleges and universities in the lurch. And yet the premier has never paid a political price .
Today, with a campaign perhaps just days away, Ford's Tories are riding high in public opinion polls - not least because they have kept tuition fees unsustainably low.
(Disclosure: I'm a senior fellow at the Dais at Toronto Metropolitan University and also at the University of Toronto's Munk School of Global Affairs and Public Policy).
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement in Toronto on Jan. 13 2025.

Title: Why doesn't Doug Ford care about funding colleges and universities? Because you don't care either

Document 9 of 500

Instead of a show of strength, Canada is cracking under threats from Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/instead-show-strength-canada-is-cracking-under/docview/3159093431/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Instead+of+a+show+of+strength%2C+Canada+is+cracking+under+threats+from+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "You never know how strong you are," said the late, great Bob Marley, "until being strong is the only choice you have." In the face of the unprecedented threat from Donald Trump, this is Canada's moment to be strong. It's not yet clear how we're going to measure up, but at this point the news frankly isn't great.
You only have to look at how Canada's first ministers are meeting the challenge - or failing to meet it. They got together virtually again this week a couple of days after Trump's latest threat to impose punishing tariffs as soon as Feb. 1 and the headline in the Star summed it up: "Leaders split on response to tariffs."
There was plenty of talk about unity and Team Canada and standing strong. But the reality is they're getting more divided, not less, as Trump ratchets up the pressure. Alberta's Danielle Smith keeps insisting Canada should not even talk about possible retaliation, as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario's Doug Ford are doing. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe seemed to be lining up with the Trudeau-Ford majority last week, but now he's switched and is singing from Smith's songbook.
Maybe they'll eventually get on the same page, or at least pretend to in public. But what a sad display. If just the threat from Trump has them running off in different directions, imagine how bad it may get once tariffs actually hit and hard decisions must be made.
But don't worry - now the premiers are talking again about tearing down those internal barriers to trade you may have heard about. We've really got to get on that, offered Nova Scotia's Tim Houston. And Anita Anand, who's been our minister of internal trade for just over a month and can expect maybe three more months in office, says it's her "top priority."
This is unquestionably a good idea but it's almost embarrassing to have to talk about it at this point. On the good idea front, consider this: a 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian imports to the U.S., as threatened by Trump, would cut Canada's GDP by an estimated 2.6 per cent - enough to drive this country into recession.
But fully tearing down those internal barriers to trade, according to Scotiabank, would increase Canada's GDP by between 3 and 7 per cent. So even at the lowest end of that range, the benefits from removing the obstacles we put up between provinces would be greater than the Trump threat that we're treating as an approaching economic apocalypse.
This could have been addressed years ago. In fact, it was - on paper. In 1994, three decades ago, all the first ministers signed onto an Agreement on Internal Trade that pledged to remove the barriers. As recently as 2017 they signed the grandly named Canadian Free Trade Agreement, aimed at doing the same thing.
The result in the real world: not much. The problem is that like all such agreements, the benefits would be widely spread but the pain would be felt by specific groups like B.C. wineries and Quebec construction workers, who would lose local protection - and would squeal loudly. It's much easier for the premiers to pay lip service to internal free trade while guarding the status quo and the votes that come with it.
Maybe the Trump tariffs will bite deeply and force change. But there's no guarantee. The worrisome truth is that the threat from the south has shone a bright light on Canada's weaknesses, both economic and political.
This country isn't as strong as it should be or united as it could be. We're being forced to take a hard look at ourselves, and we're discovering that we've let a lot of things slide over the past decade.
The good news is there's an upsurge of patriotism among ordinary Canadians angry at the disrespect shown to this country by the new president. Our political leaders should honour that by showing a truly united front against Trump in the short run and making sure that in the longer run we aren't left as vulnerable to outside threats as we are now.
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith posted these photos to X after meeting with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his Mar-a-Lago club in Florida. She was joined by Canadian businessman Kevin O'Leary, right. Smith does not think Canada should retaliate against the U.S. while other premiers and the prime minister disagree.

Subject: Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Free trade; Prime ministers; Tariffs

People: Trump, Donald J
