Document 10 of 500

Donald Trump is exploiting Canada's reliance on trade with America. Why don't we trade with more countries?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-is-exploiting-canadas-reliance-on/docview/3159093278/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+is+exploiting+Canada%27s+reliance+on+trade+with+America.+Why+don%27t+we+trade+with+more+countries%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada is a laggard when it comes to trade diversity despite all the years of political pledges to do better, and all the free trade agreements signed.
At a quick glance, the facts are promising.
Canada is the only G7 nation with a free-trade agreement with every other member. And with 15 trade deals covering 49 countries and more on the way, we can boast to ourselves about our preferential access to a world eager for the True North's rich resources.
But behind that rosy picture, our track record of reliance on the U.S. for nearly 80 per cent of our exports means that if U.S. President Donald Trump moves forward on his tariff threat, it will pummel our economy.
Canadian political and business leaders are now grappling with the fact that despite decades of signing deals in an attempt to diversify our trade, the Canadian economy remains deeply dependent on trade with America. That's forced Canadians to make an existential pitch to their American counterparts: Our economies are so connected that our fates are intertwined - basically, hurt me and you'll feel it too.
International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen, who has long advocated for Canada to do more business with emerging markets in Africa, said it's "understandable" Canadian businesses turn to American customers.
"There's two things at play. One is that we have access to the largest market in the world. The U.S. market is very attractive - it's very easy to work in. We have the same language. We have similar ways of doing business. It's easier to work and grow in the U.S.," he said in an interview.
"The second part of the equation is Canadian businesses have been extremely risk averse when it comes to emerging markets," he said.
"However, that reliance (on American markets) comes with risk," he said.
He believes new trade agreements and government supports like trade commissioner and consular offices have helped. "We're heading in the right direction, just not fast enough. I think the challenge for governments is that ... you can build a bridge, but unless people walk over the bridge or drive across the bridge, it's not very useful."
Meredith Lilly, a professor at Carleton University and a former international trade adviser to prime minister Stephen Harper, underlined the significance of a diversified export market: "It sends an important signal to the United States, when we have other partners, that we have other options."
But right now, she said, "the statistics just don't support that."
"We have actually become more dependent on the United States," Lilly said in an interview with the Star.
It's a tough geopolitical situation that is forcing Canadians to reckon with why we haven't positioned ourselves to be less reliant on the U.S., and has reignited decades-old calls for a transformation in how we do business abroad.
Hussen points to the example of Turkey, a country of 85 million and a "gateway" for Canadian businesses not just to that market but into western Asia, as well as to Africa. He spoke last week to his counterpart in Turkey, where the government has aggressively moved to build embassies in 51 of 54 African countries, and has ramped up trade on that continent to the point where it has aided Canadian contractors who are partnering with Turkish businesses to do work in places like Senegal and Tanzania on high-speed rail. "Canada benefitted from that hustle by Turkey," he said.
Hussen gave another example: Nigeria, where he said 70 per cent of the population doesn't have adequate electricity. "That's a huge inroad for Canadian power companies to go in there and build all forms of energy-producing projects. The opportunities are there, whether it's food security, whether it's health care, transport, logistics, education. Again, these middle powers are going all in," he said. So are big powers like China and India.
In the last week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, whose province does about $500 billion in two-way trade annually with the U.S., acknowledged "we need to move forward as quickly as possible" with diversifying our trade.
So, too, did Liberal leadership contender Mark Carney, who declared that "if we can no longer rely on American neighbours, we must diversify our trading relationships and build new sources of jobs and growth."
But for now, the stats are grim. Over the last two decades at least, between 75 and 80 per cent of Canadian exports have gone to our neighbours south of the border. No other country has surpassed five per cent in that same time period.
Meanwhile, less than 20 per cent of U.S. exports go to Canada, even though we're America's biggest trading partner.
"We've been talking about it forever," said Catherine Fortin LeFaivre, the vice president of International Policy and Global Partnerships at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce. "There's nothing like an emergency, though, or urgency like the type of crisis that we're facing now, to really force people to really innovate ... perhaps this will be the catalyst for that."
The task ahead is much easier said than done. Part of it is complacency. But despite booming efforts from successive governments to diversify our trade with new agreements, Canadian companies face regulatory burdens and the country lacks the infrastructure required to expand its overseas exports.
While trade experts who spoke to the Star say there is no magic number to aim for in a quest to reduce our reliance on the United States, moving the needle by just a few percentage points would come at a steep cost.
That leads to a situation where it may always be easiest and most natural to trade with the U.S. Lilly said that can also be attributed to a trade theory called the "gravity model," which says that "the closer a country is to you, and the larger it is in GDP, the more likely you'll trade with it."
But there are other challenges, too. The Canadian economy has a high concentration of small- to medium-sized businesses that don't have the capacity to trade at all. Of the fraction who are able to export their products, 65 per cent only export to the U.S.
"It's not like you can just flip a switch and suddenly be exporting to a very different market on the other side of the world," Lilly said.
Canada is set to have several pipelines opening in the coming years, including B.C.'s Coastal Gaslink, and in the face of the Trump threats, there is renewed talk of breaking down interprovincial trade barriers.
Indeed, on Wednesday, Ford said it "it's unacceptable that we don't have (the) Energy East pipeline coming out to Ontario and out to New Brunswick, and again, the Northern Gateway going out through B.C. (so) we can ship our crude oil around the world."
Duncan Munn, the CEO of Elevate Finance, a company that supports Canadian exporters, said his phone has been buzzing with businesses now ready to explore trading beyond the U.S..
"We've always taken the assumption that we're going to have, you know, largely free and unfettered access to the American market. I do think that continues, but it may not be as free, and it may come with new costs," Munn told the Star. "I think this is an inflection point for Canadian business, and the risk posed is very real."
Fortin LeFaivre said that while businesses are starting to think more about the long-term risks due to the challenge with the U.S. right now, there is still uncertainty about what happens next.
She said the conversation a few months ago "was all about doubling down on working with the U.S., and decoupling from China and such states."
"The context is shifting quickly, and it's not to say that if we diversify the other markets, there aren't other risks," she said.
With files from Robert Benzie

Title: Donald Trump is exploiting Canada's reliance on trade with America. Why don't we trade with more countries?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Document 11 of 500

Why not trade with more countries?: U.S. tariff threat has Canadian leaders looking to diversify

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-not-trade-with-more-countries/docview/3159091737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+not+trade+with+more+countries%3F%3A+U.S.+tariff+threat+has+Canadian+leaders+looking+to+diversify&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is a laggard when it comes to trade diversity despite all the years of political pledges to do better, and all the free trade agreements signed.
At a quick glance, the facts are promising.
Canada is the only G7 nation with a free-trade agreement with every other member. And with 15 trade deals covering 49 countries and more on the way, we can boast to ourselves about our preferential access to a world eager for the True North's rich resources.
But behind that rosy picture, our track record of reliance on the U.S. for nearly 80 per cent of our exports means that if U.S. President Donald Trump moves forward on his tariff threat, it will pummel our economy.
Canadian political and business leaders are now grappling with the fact that despite decades of signing deals in an attempt to diversify our trade, the Canadian economy remains deeply dependent on trade with America. That's forced Canadians to make an existential pitch to their American counterparts: Our economies are so connected that our fates are intertwined - basically, hurt me and you'll feel it too.
International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen, who has long advocated for Canada to do more business with emerging markets in Africa, said it's "understandable" Canadian businesses turn to American customers.
"There's two things at play. One is that we have access to the largest market in the world. The U.S. market is very attractive - it's very easy to work in. We have the same language. We have similar ways of doing business. It's easier to work and grow in the U.S.," he said.
"The second part of the equation is Canadian businesses have been extremely risk averse when it comes to emerging markets," he said.
"However, that reliance (on American markets) comes with risk."
He believes new trade agreements and government supports like trade commissioner and consular offices have helped. "We're heading in the right direction, just not fast enough. I think the challenge for governments is that ... you can build a bridge, but unless people walk over the bridge or drive across the bridge, it's not very useful."
Meredith Lilly, a professor at Carleton University and a former international trade adviser to prime minister Stephen Harper, underlined the significance of a diversified export market: "It sends an important signal to the United States, when we have other partners, that we have other options."
But right now, she said, "the statistics just don't support that."
"We have actually become more dependent on the United States," Lilly told the Star.
It's a tough geopolitical situation that is forcing Canadians to reckon with why we haven't positioned ourselves to be less reliant on the U.S., and has reignited decades-old calls for a transformation in how we do business abroad.
Hussen points to the example of Turkey, a country of 85 million and a "gateway" for Canadian businesses not just to that market but into western Asia, as well as to Africa. He spoke last week to his counterpart in Turkey, where the government has aggressively moved to build embassies in 51 of 54 African countries, and has ramped up trade on that continent to the point where it has aided Canadian contractors who are partnering with Turkish businesses to do work in places like Senegal and Tanzania on high-speed rail.
"Canada benefitted from that hustle by Turkey," he said.
Hussen gave another example: Nigeria, where he said 70 per cent of the population doesn't have adequate electricity.
"That's a huge inroad for Canadian power companies to go in there and build all forms of energy-producing projects. The opportunities are there, whether it's food security, whether it's health care, transport, logistics, education. Again, these middle powers are going all in," he said. So are big powers like China and India.
In the last week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, whose province does about $500 billion in two-way trade annually with the U.S., acknowledged "we need to move forward as quickly as possible" with diversifying our trade.
So, too, did federal Liberal leadership contender Mark Carney, who declared that "if we can no longer rely on American neighbours, we must diversify our trading relationships and build new sources of jobs and growth."
But for now, the stats are grim. Over the last two decades at least, between 75 and 80 per cent of Canadian exports have gone to our neighbours south of the border. No other country has surpassed five per cent in that same time period.
Meanwhile, less than 20 per cent of U.S. exports go to Canada, even though we're America's biggest trading partner.
"We've been talking about it forever," said Catherine Fortin LeFaivre, the vice-president of International Policy and Global Partnerships at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce. "There's nothing like an emergency, though, or urgency like the type of crisis that we're facing now, to really force people to really innovate ... perhaps this will be the catalyst for that."
The task ahead is much easier said than done. Part of it is complacency. But despite booming efforts from successive governments to diversify our trade with new agreements, Canadian companies face regulatory burdens and the country lacks the infrastructure required to expand its overseas exports.
While trade experts who spoke to the Star say there is no magic number to aim for in a quest to reduce our reliance on the United States, moving the needle by just a few percentage points would come at a steep cost.
That leads to a situation where it may always be easiest and most natural to trade with the U.S. Lilly said that can also be attributed to a trade theory called the "gravity model," which says that "the closer a country is to you, and the larger it is in GDP, the more likely you'll trade with it."
But there are other challenges, too. The Canadian economy has a high concentration of small- to medium-sized businesses that don't have the capacity to trade at all. Of the fraction who are able to export their products, 65 per cent only export to the U.S.
"It's not like you can just flip a switch and suddenly be exporting to a very different market on the other side of the world," Lilly said.
Canada is set to have several pipelines opening in the coming years, including B.C.'s Coastal Gaslink, and in the face of the Trump threats, there is renewed talk of breaking down interprovincial trade barriers.
Indeed, on Wednesday, Ford said it "it's unacceptable that we don't have (the) Energy East pipeline coming out to Ontario and out to New Brunswick, and again, the Northern Gateway going out through B.C. (so) we can ship our crude oil around the world."
Duncan Munn, the CEO of Elevate Finance, a company that supports Canadian exporters, said his phone has been buzzing with businesses now ready to explore trading beyond the U.S.
"We've always taken the assumption that we're going to have, you know, largely free and unfettered access to the American market. I do think that continues, but it may not be as free, and it may come with new costs," Munn told the Star. "I think this is an inflection point for Canadian business, and the risk posed is very real."
Fortin LeFaivre said that while businesses are starting to think more about the long-term risks due to the challenge with the U.S. right now, there is still uncertainty about what happens next.
She said the conversation a few months ago "was all about doubling down on working with the U.S., and decoupling from China and such states."
"The context is shifting quickly, and it's not to say that if we diversify the other markets, there aren't other risks," she said.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

We need a statesperson, not a slogan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-need-statesperson-not-slogan/docview/3159091379/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+a+statesperson%2C+not+a+slogan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal leadership hopefuls flop out of the gate, Jan. 21
Eric Blais writes that the Liberal leadership race is devoid of sizzle and slogans. Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre is great at giving us slogans and nothing more. I am not looking for these in a Prime Minister. In these complex times, I am looking for a statesperson. I am just finishing Mark Carney's book "Value(s): Building a Better World for All." In it he lays out a progressive vision of how he would deal with the pressing issues of climate change, world hunger, the appalling and rising gap between the rich and the poor, and much more. He is truly a man of intelligence, inspiration, and integrity - there's a slogan for you!
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
What has happened to the world's moral compass?
Elon Musk responds to backlash after his 'odd-looking salute' at Donald Trump's inauguration, Jan. 21
As the child of Holocaust survivors, I am horrified by what appeared to be a Nazi salute made by Elon Musk during Trump's inauguration. These are the kinds of people Trump surrounds himself with, the people he takes advice from, the people who will ultimately shape the political and social direction the U.S. will take for the next four years. But having taken root, this contagion will likely continue to spread unless it's stopped in its tracks. What has happened to the world's moral compass?
Michael Bines, Toronto
Ontario shouldn't do business with Musk
It is time for the Ontario government to rethink and cancel its deal with Elon Musk's Starlink service. Musk has proven himself to be a despicable human being who, during his "performance" at Donald Trump's inauguration ceremony seemed to enthusiastically raise his arm in the Nazi salute. Premier Doug Ford appears to have taken on the role of Captain Canada and has a large part to play in preparing a Canadian response to potential threats from the Trump presidency. Musk has an outsized role the Trump administration. I am sure that there are millions of Ontarians like me who don't want to see our hard-earned tax dollars flowing to Musk. There is no room in business conducted with our tax dollars, for anyone like Musk.
Volker Triebe, Midland
Cost of family life in Toronto is not sustainable
Most provinces have dropped daycare costs to $10/day. Why are some Toronto parents still paying full fees? Jan. 19
Quebec, where my wife and I raised our daughter, does not participate in the Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care program. Instead, Quebec has its own day-care subsidy program and has had for more than 30 years. And thank God. It was hard enough then to pay for pre-school full-day care for our daughter. I can't imagine what it would be like now. Actually, I can imagine it. And so can Premier Doug Ford and his education minister, Jill Dunlop. If Toronto and Ontario wish to keep hard-working, tax-paying people here to raise families and buy homes, subsidizing daycare has to be part of the package.
The woman featured in Brendan Kennedy's story leaves her preschooler in a daycare that charges market rate. That's $2,090. The woman also has an infant who she was hoping would be starting in the same daycare. The market rate for that child, however, is $2,565. That's almost $4,700 a month, as Kennedy's story points out. Let's assume the couple have a million-dollar house with a 25-year amortization period and a five-per-cent fixed mortgage rate. They're paying more than $5,000 a month for their house. Add insurance, utilities, the ever-rising cost of groceries, transit cost or a car and to paraphrase Ford, you'd have to be a few fries short of a Happy Meal to see this is not a life. This is not sustainable.
Raymond Beauchemin, Hamilton
Harris 'common sense' kicked off doctor shortages
Desperately seeking a doctor, Jan. 21
This article sounded as though people "desperately seeking a doctor" is a new phenomenon. Well, we had to do the same thing in 1999 in Sudbury during the infamous Mike Harris PC Common Sense Revolution from 1995 to 2002.We were lucky as we lined up in a warm bowling alley to be interviewed and finally accepted by the new doctor.
For the past 18 months the federal Conservative party has been lobbying Canadians with the same "common sense" stuff and with promises to "Axe the tax" and balance the budget. I shudder to think about the consequences. If the Conservatives do win the next Federal election would they cut the $10 a day child care? Or the new dental care program or the new pharmacare deal or get rid of any progress we have made in the area of climate change or protection of our environment? Or maybe all of them? We all have one vote and hopefully people will think very carefully about this when marking their X.
Sharon Mason, Wasaga Beach
No common sense found in Conservative claims
Common Sense Conservatives? Whenever politicians talk about "common sense" you know that they are not thinking of the dictionary definition of the term (i.e. good sense and sound judgment in practical matters according to Oxford). Common sense must be based on known facts and logical causal relationships. To apply "common sense" one must always consider the source of any facts and conclusions that are presented and evaluate whether related statements and conclusions make any sense at all.
The CP leader started out claiming "everything is broken" (which was not true) and that our very real problems with inflation, housing prices, budget deficits and crime on our subway could all be blamed on one man (and now Liberals in general). Was Prime Minister Justin Trudeau also responsible for the same problems being experienced in the U.S.A. and other G7 countries? The absurdity of these claims demonstrates that all this political name calling, lying and posturing has nothing to do with Common Sense: it is U.S. style hate-driven politics at their worst. Hate has never solved any human problems - it has only made them worse.
David Crowley, Richmond Hill
Allowing children's personal information to be compromised is despicablePersonal information of Ontario students going back decades may have been breached in cyberattack, Jan. 20
Why are school boards outsourcing children's and teachers' private information and why is this information going to a U.S. company? Why were parents and teachers not notified as soon as it happened? Instead, we found out in the news a month later. To have children's personal information compromised is despicable. What's wrong with the minister of education to approve personal info collection and not ensure it is looked after? Teachers are always teaching students to be careful with their personal information. Now look at the example set by the school boards and ministry of education. No wonder people resent handing over and disclosing information.

Document 13 of 500

Canada is cracking under threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-is-cracking-under-threats/docview/3159091152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+is+cracking+under+threats&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "You never know how strong you are," said the late, great Bob Marley, "until being strong is the only choice you have." In the face of the unprecedented threat from Donald Trump, this is Canada's moment to be strong. It's not yet clear how we're going to measure up, but at this point the news frankly isn't great.
You only have to look at how Canada's first ministers are meeting the challenge - or failing to meet it. They got together virtually again this week a couple of days after Trump's latest threat to impose punishing tariffs as soon as Feb. 1 and the headline in the Star summed it up: "Leaders split on response to tariffs."
There was plenty of talk about unity and Team Canada and standing strong. But the reality is they're getting more divided, not less, as Trump ratchets up the pressure. Alberta's Danielle Smith keeps insisting Canada should not even talk about possible retaliation, as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario's Doug Ford are doing. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe seemed to be lining up with the Trudeau-Ford majority last week, but now he's switched and is singing from Smith's songbook.
Maybe they'll eventually get on the same page, or at least pretend to in public. But what a sad display. If just the threat from Trump has them running off in different directions, imagine how bad it may get once tariffs actually hit and hard decisions must be made.
But don't worry - now the premiers are talking again about tearing down those internal barriers to trade you may have heard about. We've really got to get on that, offered Nova Scotia's Tim Houston. And Anita Anand, who's been our minister of internal trade for just over a month and can expect maybe three more months in office, says it's her "top priority."
This is unquestionably a good idea but it's almost embarrassing to have to talk about it at this point. On the good idea front, consider this: a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian imports to the U.S., as threatened by Trump, would cut Canada's GDP by an estimated 2.6 per cent - enough to drive this country into recession.
But fully tearing down those internal barriers to trade, according to Scotiabank, would increase Canada's GDP by between three and seven per cent. So even at the lowest end of that range, the benefits from removing the obstacles we put up between provinces would be greater than the Trump threat that we're treating as an approaching economic apocalypse.
This could have been addressed years ago. In fact, it was - on paper. In 1994, three decades ago, all the first ministers signed onto an Agreement on Internal Trade that pledged to remove the barriers. As recently as 2017 they signed the grandly named Canadian Free Trade Agreement, aimed at doing the same thing.
The result in the real world: not much. The problem is that like all such agreements, the benefits would be widely spread but the pain would be felt by specific groups like B.C. wineries and Quebec construction workers, who would lose local protection - and would squeal loudly. It's much easier for the premiers to pay lip service to internal free trade while guarding the status quo and the votes that come with it.
Maybe the Trump tariffs will bite deeply and force change. But there's no guarantee. The worrisome truth is that the threat from the south has shone a bright light on Canada's weaknesses, both economic and political.
This country isn't as strong as it should be or united as it could be. We're being forced to take a hard look at ourselves, and we're discovering that we've let a lot of things slide over the past decade.
The good news is there's an upsurge of patriotism among ordinary Canadians angry at the disrespect shown to this country by the new president. Our political leaders should honour that by showing a truly united front against Trump in the short run and making sure that in the longer run we aren't left as vulnerable to outside threats as we are now.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, shown with Donald Trump recently in Florida, doesn't think Canada should retaliate against potential U.S. tariffs. The prime minister and other premiers disagree.Danielle Smith via X
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Document 14 of 500

Ford's team set for Feb. 27 election: Ministers scramble to tout projects ahead of campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-team-set-feb-27-election/docview/3159091069/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+team+set+for+Feb.+27+election%3A+Ministers+scramble+to+tout+projects+ahead+of+campaign&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has ordered his cabinet ministers to get government announcements out by Tuesday to clear the decks for an election call the following day.
The election will be held Feb. 27, making it the first winter vote in Ontario since 1981.
Ford will be in Brampton Friday morning with Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria to tout the tunnelling of a section of the next phase of the Hazel McCallion Line LRT, which runs along Hurontario Street from the Port Credit GO station to the Gateway Terminal.
The delayed 18-kilometre project, named for the late Mississauga mayor, was once supposed to be up and running last month, but no completion date has yet been announced.
A tunnel through the narrow historic downtown stretch near city hall would fulfil Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown's 2018 pledge.
Ford's edict to cabinet has increased the tempo of news conferences as the Progressive Conservatives aim to garner maximum publicity heading into the campaign, with more held on Thursday.
Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce made a minor announcement about the planned refurbishment of the aging Pickering B nuclear plant that was confirmed a year ago.
Lecce said the province has authorized Crown-owned Ontario Power Generation to sign a $2.1-billion contract with a supplier for "early engineering and procurement" work to prepare the plant to produce enough electricity to power two million homes for an additional 30 years.
There was a clear election bent to the minister's words at a Mississauga nuclear supplier.
"As President Ford leads the fight against U.S. tariffs ... we're planning for affordable and reliable energy we're going to need because our province is growing," Lecce said, reading from a teleprompter but giving the wrong title for the premier.
Asked about the slip, Lecce, who recently returned from President Donald Trump's inauguration in Washington, quipped, "OK, I'm not going to stand when he (Ford) walks in a room. That's a little too West Wing."
As ministers scramble with a flurry of more than a dozen government announcements, Tory MPPs, candidates and staff have been summoned to a Saturday campaign readiness meeting at an airport hotel.
"I'm asking for a mandate ... from the people of Ontario to make sure that we protect them," Ford said Thursday of an election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million.
"The 'super caucus' event, we're having everyone by and having a good chat, telling them what's going on regarding the tariffs, what's going on here in Ontario," the Tory leader said of the pre-campaign confab.
With the election imminent, Ford was asked about juggling the roles of premier, chair of the Council of the Federation and the re-election campaign all at the same time as Trump is threatening tariffs.
"We can call back cabinet anytime," the premier said in Mississauga, where pharmaceutical giant AstraZeneca announced an $820-million expansion of its research and development facilities and a move to a larger office.
"I'm going to continue being chair of the COF. I'll be in Washington as well," he added, noting he will be there twice in February lobbying American politicians and officials "to make sure we set our case for Canada and Ontario."
As the Star reported, the premier told supporters at a Tuesday night fundraiser he needs a "very strong mandate" in the upcoming snap election to send Trump a message that Ontarians won't tolerate his threatened 25 per cent tariffs.
The next Ontario election is not scheduled until June 2026, but Ford wants to have one sooner because of the Trump tariff threat. His government is also facing the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
He also hopes to gain an advantage because Grit activists may be too busy with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie with her campaign.
Tory sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal strategy, insist that while the Trump threat is their biggest concern, they are also wary about a softening economy even if tariffs aren't implemented.
Crombie took a shot at the "Canada is not for sale" cap that Ford wore at a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa last week in arguing against an "unnecessary, very expensive election" when he already has a mandate to govern for another 17 months.
"What we need is leadership, not showmanship. You just can't slap a label on a hat and call that a plan," she told host Vassy Kapelos on CTV's Power Play.
"Let's be honest. He's a small player and will have a very small role in what will be the federal government response to the tariff threat," Crombie added.
"It's a very selfish ploy."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson, Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 15 of 500

Doug Ford tells his ministers to fast-track any announcements with election call just days away

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-tells-his-ministers-fast-track-any/docview/3159511090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+tells+his+ministers+to+fast-track+any+announcements+with+election+call+just+days+away&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has ordered his cabinet ministers to get government announcements out by Tuesday to clear the decks for an election call the following day.
Ford will be in Brampton Friday morning with Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria to tout the tunnelling of a section of the next phase of the Hazel McCallion Line LRT, which runs along Hurontario Street from the Port Credit GO station to the Gateway Terminal.
The delayed 18-km project, named for the late Mississauga mayor, was once supposed to be up and running last month but no completion date has yet been announced.
A tunnel through the narrow historic downtown stretch near city hall would fulfil Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown's 2018 pledge.
Ford's edict to cabinet has increased the tempo of news conferences as the Progressive Conservatives aim to garner maximum publicity heading into the campaign, with more held on Thursday.
Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce made an minor announcement about the planned refurbishment of the aging Pickering B nuclear plant that was confirmed a year ago.
Lecce said the province has authorized Crown-owned Ontario Power Generation to sign a $2.1-billion contract with a supplier for "early engineering and procurement" work to prepare the plant to produce enough to power two million homes for an additional 30 years.
There was a clear election bent to the minister's words at a Mississauga nuclear supplier.
"As President Ford leads the fight against U.S. tariffs ... we're planning for affordable and reliable energy we're going to need because our province is growing," Lecce said, reading from a teleprompter but giving the wrong title for the premier.
Asked about the slip, Lecce, who recently returned from President Donald Trump's inauguration in Washington, quipped, "OK, I'm not going to stand when he (Ford) walks in a room. That's a little too West Wing."
As ministers scramble with a flurry of more than a dozen government announcements, Tory MPPs, candidates and staff have been summoned to a Saturday campaign readiness meeting at an airport hotel.
"I'm asking for a mandate ... from the people of Ontario to make sure that we protect them," Ford said Thursday of his expected Feb. 27 election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million.
"The 'super caucus' event, we're having everyone by and having a good chat, telling them what's going on regarding the tariffs, what's going on here in Ontario," the Tory leader said of the pre-campaign confab.
With the election imminent, Ford was asked about juggling the roles of premier, chair of the Council of the Federation and the re-election campaign all at the same time as Trump is threatening tariffs.
"We can call back cabinet anytime," the premier said in Mississauga, where pharmaceutical giant AstraZeneca announced an $820-million expansion of its research and development facilities and a move to a larger office.
"I'm going to continue being chair of the COF. I'll be in Washington as well," he added, noting he will be there twice in February lobbying American politicians and officials "to make sure we set our case for Canada and Ontario."
As the Star reported, the premier told supporters at a Tuesday night fundraiser he needs a "very strong mandate" in the upcoming snap election to send Trump a message that Ontarians won't tolerate his threatened 25 per cent tariffs.
The next Ontario election is not scheduled until June 2026, but Ford wants to have one sooner because of the Trump tariff threat and the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
He also hopes to gain an advantage because Grit activists may be too busy with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie with her campaign.
Another factor is his desire to face voters before a federal election expected this spring that polls suggest could be won by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Because Ontario voters historically opt for different parties in power at Queen's Park and Ottawa at the same time, a Conservative federal victory could be bad for the Ontario Tories.
Tory sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal strategic discussion, insist while the Trump threat is their biggest concern, they are also wary about a softening economy even if tariffs aren't implemented.
Crombie took a shot at the "Ontario is not for sale" cap Ford wore at a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa last week in arguing against an "unnecessary, very expensive election" when he already has a mandate to govern for another 17 months.
"What we need is leadership, not showmanship. You just can't slap a label on a hat and call that a plan," she told host Vassy Kapelos on CTV's Power Play.
"Let's be honest. He's a small player and will have a very small role in what will be the federal government response to the tariff threat," Crombie added.
She said an early election would be "a distraction because of the chaos here in Ontario," referring to a rising unemployment rate, a physician shortage that has left 2.5 million residents without a family doctor and overcrowded schools with repair backlogs.
"It's a very selfish ploy."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who has questioned the need for an early vote, has warned the campaign will be about more than the 500,000 jobs threatened by Trump tariffs.
"(Ontarians) don't trust Mr. Ford to protect their jobs because they can't trust Mr. Ford to deliver them a family doctor ... people have seen housing costs go through the roof," Stiles said Wednesday.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at an announcement in Toronto on Jan. 13 2025.

Document 16 of 500

Liberal leadership race: Here are the big names endorsing front-runners Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-leadership-race-here-are-big-names/docview/3159511060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+leadership+race%3A+Here+are+the+big+names+endorsing+front-runners+Mark+Carney+and+Chrystia+Freeland&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Liberal leadership contenders have put down the first $50,000 of their entry fees, and continue lining up caucus endorsements and organizers as they push through a rushed timeline in their bids to become party leader.
Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney and former government House leader Karina Gould have all paid their $50,000 deposits, but the pressure to raise cash doesn't stop there, with another $50,000 due Monday and the balance of their $350,000 entry fees due in a matter of weeks.
Ottawa MP Chandra Arya, Nova Scotia MP Jaime Battiste and former MPs Frank Baylis and Ruby Dhalla have also said they submitted the necessary applications to run for leader.
The party has 10 days to formally approve of leadership contenders.
Candidates who have cleared the first hurdle are now trying to line up the endorsements of MPs and cabinet ministers. Here's a look at who the top contenders have attracted so far, with just 46 days before the party's new leader will be announced on March 9.
Mark Carney
Despite never having been a member of Liberal caucus, the former central bank governor has secured the endorsements of more than a dozen cabinet ministers, including big names like Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, Emergency Preparedness Minister Harjit Sajjan and Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree, three of the Liberals' significant regional organizers .
Joly started her own political party in Montreal when she ran to be that city's mayor more than a decade ago. She said Thursday that she is focusing on her job as foreign affairs minister and the threat of U.S. tariffs, but is endorsing Carney in the hopes her organization will also come to his cause.
"When it comes to Mr. Carney, I have a lot of respect for him and I've endorsed him," she said. "And I sent a clear message to my base and to my organizers and to my volunteers to send the signal."
Anandasangaree has a significant volunteer base in the Greater Toronto Area that he has used to help other candidates in past campaigns, and broad connections in the Tamil community across the county. Sajjan routinely headlines fundraisers for the Liberal party in British Columbia where he holds significant influence, especially within the South Asian community. MP Sukh Dhaliwal is also endorsing Carney and is a big organizer in the Sikh community.
Carney also has the endorsement of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's former principal secretary, Gerald Butts, who stressed he is not a formal part of the campaign. Most of the prime minister's current staff members, including chief of staff Katie Telford, are staying on the sidelines.
Carney's campaign has two official co-directors: Braeden Caley, who is the president and CEO of the Liberal-aligned think tank Canada 2020, and Marie-Pascale Des Rosiers, who has worked for the prime minister and managed the campaign of Montreal MP Anna Gainey, who is also supporting Carney.
Chrystia Freeland
The former finance minister, whose dramatic resignation accelerated the caucus revolt that prompted Trudeau's decision to resign, has attracted the support of almost two dozen MPs and cabinet ministers.
Flanked by Justice Minister Arif Virani and International Development Minister Ahmed Hussen ahead of a Liberal caucus meeting Thursday, Freeland highlighted the support she has from two of the party's regional caucus chairs, Ontario's Michael Coteau and Quebec's Stéphane Lauzon.
"I'm especially grateful to the people who bring the caucus together, do the hard work in our caucus," she said.
Hussen is anticipated to deliver votes for Freeland in Toronto, Winnipeg and Edmonton among their Somali, Sudanese, Nigerian and Muslim communities. Freeland is counting on MP Lena Metlege Diab to help deliver Lebanese community votes.
Freeland has also attracted the support of cabinet ministers Mark Holland, Terry Beech and Diane Lebouthillier, as well as former ministers Dan Vandal and Marie Claude Bibeau.
Most cabinet ministers from Quebec have thrown their support to Carney so far, leaving Freeland needing to make up ground in that province.
Freeland pledged major party reforms on Thursday, including giving caucus the authority to initiate a leadership review. Despite unrest growing in the Liberal caucus about Trudeau's performance over the past year, there was no mechanism to replace him until he decided to resign. Freeland said that can't be the case going forward.
"We can never again be in a position where the leader is the only person who decides who the leader is," she said.
Freeland's campaign is being managed by Tom Allison, who served as campaign manager for former Ontario Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne and co-chaired Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's leadership campaign, among many other roles.
Karina Gould
Gould's entry into the race was the most unexpected. Speaking to reporters Thursday, she admitted she had only been organizing for the past two weeks.
So far, her caucus support is also limited, with only Hamilton MP Lisa Hepfner and Oakville MP Pam Damoff standing up with her.
Gould said that ultimately it won't be her caucus colleagues who decide the next Liberal leader.
"The leadership of the party is decided by the membership, and what I am hearing from the membership across the country is how excited they are by my candidacy," she said.
She said she was raising the rest of the $350,000 entry fee through small donations from supporters.
"This is a grassroots, people-powered campaign, and I am so excited to have so many people across this country on board," she said.
Gould's campaign is being managed by Elliott Lockington, a longtime Liberal staffer who previously served as chief of staff to then-minister Randy Boissonnault.
The Liberal race will be decided on a one-member, one-vote basis, with party supporters voting on a ranked ballot.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership contenders, left to right, Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland and Karina Gould.

Full text: Guest: Robert Benzie
It's likely Doug Ford will call a snap election nearly a year and a half before the province is due. Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie explains why now is an ideal time for Ford to call this election: what's at stake, what issues are most pressing, what his opposition thinks, and what is the historical precedence in Ontario for snap elections.

Full text: Doug Ford may call Ontario election as early as next week: sources, Jan. 21
It appalls me that Doug Ford thinks we the voters will fall for his fantasies. Doesn't anyone remember the last election when all the talk was houses, houses, houses? We are going to build so many affordable houses. Where are the houses? Did any get built? How many more people are living on the streets now than there were at the last election?
Think of who has benefited from this Ford government - Staples, fancy spa, Beer Store - meanwhile, the waiting time at hospitals and for doctors is increasing and the cost of living rising. Now Ford has switched gears and wants an election immediately so he can save us from U.S. President Donald Trump. But who will save us from Ford? Maybe the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt deal if it proceeds soon enough. Let's think before we vote. And that $200 cheque? It's being added on to our provincial debt .
Doris Moxam, Toronto
Who's paying for this election?
Premier Doug Ford can call the election. I really don't care as long as the Progressive Conservative party of Ontario pays the full cost of somewhere between $150 and $200 million.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
Snap election is a big mistakeDoug Ford is acting admirably as Captain Canada. He shouldn't ruin it by calling an early election, Jan. 21
I agree with Laura Babcock that the worst thing Premier Doug Ford could do right now - for Canada and himself - is call a snap election. And I suspect I'm just one of many Ontario voters who's happy he's leading the charge against U.S. President Donald Trump but would never vote for him.
Sylvia Barrett, Kingston
Expensive and self-serving
Premier Doug Ford has so far come out swinging against the threat from U.S. President Donald Trump. Ford is the Premier of Ontario and by defending this province against Trump's threatened tariffs, he is doing a job for which he is well paid. It is obvious that he is also an opportunist. He is creating excuses for calling an early election which is expensive and self-serving. He has the backing of the opposition parties, showing a level of solidarity within our political system that is a rarity in politics. This is a clear indication to Trump that all political parties representing all Ontario voters are united in this particular cause. What better way to say, "Don't mess with us. We're united in this cause." Ford doesn't need an early election; he just wants it. Folks, there is a huge difference.
Patricia Steward, East York
Musk endorses PoilievreElon Musk has Donald Trump's ear and a new-found interest in Canada. How much should we worry? Jan. 18
Considering Elon Musk appeared to enthusiastically give the Nazi fascist salute - not just once but twice - at Donald Trump's inauguration ceremonies on Monday, and that he has endorsed Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre as his preferred candidate for Canada's next prime minister, I would like to ask Poilievre "Do you accept the endorsement of Elon Musk?"
Teresa Ganna Porter, Newmarket
We might just see history repeat itselfDonald Trump promotes a stark vision of America as he returns to the White House, Jan. 21
The Russian Emperor, Nicholas II, and his family had Grigori Rasputin, healer or opportunist, but the man who ultimately caused the Romanov dynasty downfall. U.S. President Donald Trump has Elon Musk whispering in his ear, the billionaire man with very large ambitions. Is it possible that history could perhaps repeat itself?
Patricia Steward, East York
I'll miss the carbon rebateTrudeau government's carbon price has had 'minimal' effect on inflation and food costs, study concludes, Jan. 16
Why aren't the candidates for the Liberal leadership race yelling from the rooftops about the carbon tax rebate that goes into most householders' accounts? For me it makes my life much easier, covering the gas usage and a major part of my groceries. I will miss it.
Ann Boyd, Woodville
Many can't afford to eat let alone pay the carbon tax
I find it interesting that there actually are people who like the carbon tax. They see it as a beneficial thing. They believe it helps the environment and some even say they are better off with it than without. I've never met any of these people though. Everyone I know without exception says it hurts them financially. I've yet to see how taking money from me does the environment any good at all. Does it curtail anybody from driving as much as the used to? Maybe a few but I bet it's not enough to make a difference.
There's a point. Make a difference. We know that China and India are the biggest polluters. We know they don't have a carbon tax. The amount that Canada produces totals about one per cent of the world's carbon dioxide. We also have an abundance of forests that live on the carbon dioxide. What I'm saying is that our total pollution amounts to less than a drop in the lake compared to other countries. Even if we completely stopped, it wouldn't matter one bit. It doesn't make a difference.
I hope people who support the carbon tax are also donating generously to the food banks because not all of us can afford the carbon tax and we certainly don't want to pay it. In fact, a lot of us are debating on whether we keep a roof over our heads or eat. The numerous tent cities across the country tell you that people decided to eat and forego the roof.
Jeremy Thornton, Stouffville
Yorkville gave rise to feelings of being in on something lastingMemories of Bob Dylan, and of a forgotten Toronto, Jan. 15
Like Rosie DiManno, I have my own wistful memories of a Toronto past. First, a bit of background. I started practicing architecture and urban design at age 23 in Sudbury in 1954. A couple of buddies and I frequently hopped on the CPR Friday overnight train to Toronto to experience Yorkville, the "Yonge Street Strip," and later, the great Peter Appleyard featured with the Cal Jackson Quartet in the Plaza Room just across Avenue Road from Yorkville. I particularly remember seeing Ian and Sylvia. Beyond that, I simply recall the ambience of the neighbourhood at night and the exciting feelings of being in on something lasting. Of course nothing lasts forever, except maybe the memories of old guys like me. I was one of the first in line at the Cineplex in Sudbury for the first showing of the movie "A Complete Unknown" with Timothee Chalamet as Bob Dylan.
Arthur Peach, Sudbury

Document 19 of 500

Mark Carney supported by three of Justin Trudeau's environment ministers despite his plan to kill the consumer carbon price

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mark-carney-supported-three-justin-trudeaus/docview/3158722429/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mark+Carney+supported+by+three+of+Justin+Trudeau%27s+environment+ministers+despite+his+plan+to+kill+the+consumer+carbon+price&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The three environment ministers who have served in Justin Trudeau's Liberal government since 2015 are supporting a candidate to replace him who favours ditching their signature climate policy: the consumer carbon price.
Jonathan Wilkinson, the Natural Resources minister who held the environment portfolio from 2019 to 2021, told the Star that he will endorse former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney for the party leadership. His endorsement follows that of current Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, as well as expressions of support from Trudeau's first environment minister, Catherine McKenna.
The Star reported last week that Carney has told MPs he intends to walk away from the consumer carbon price. Other candidates for the leadership also plan to drop the policy, including former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, while former government house leader Karina Gould has said she would freeze the consumer price at current levels.
In an interview, Wilkinson said he is disappointed that the days of the consumer carbon price seem numbered, describing it as the cheapest and most efficient way to reduce greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change.
But Wilkinson said it is more important to focus on outcomes, and that he expects Carney - whom he said has the best economic credentials to lead the Liberals - to present a climate plan that makes up for the lost emissions reductions that come from scrapping the consumer carbon price.
"It's unfortunate we've gotten to this place," Wilkinson said, blaming the opposition Conservatives for distorting the facts of the policy.
Since taking over as Tory leader in 2022, Pierre Poilievre has constantly attacked the consumer carbon price and vowed to "axe the tax." He has said it drives up the cost of food and gasoline, for instance, though routinely omits the existence of carbon price rebate payments that go out to people who live in provinces where the policy applies. In Ontario cities, a family of four received $1,120 in the current fiscal year.
Those payments are set to increase along with the consumer carbon price itself, which adds 17.6 cents per litre to the cost of gasoline at its current level, and will add 37.4 cents per litre by 2030, according to government statistics.
The independent Parliamentary Budget Officer has reported that these rebates cover the cash costs of carbon pricing for most households, but that most end up losing out when the broader economic drag of the policy is considered.
A study published in December, meanwhile, concluded carbon pricing has had a minimal impact on inflation since the Liberal scheme was introduced in 2019.
"Mr. Poilievre has spent the last three years polarizing this issue, lying about the facts on this issue," Wilkinson said.
"In that context, it may well be time for us to adopt a policy that may cost us a little bit more money overall as a society, in order not to have that very divisive debate."
Wilkinson's comments echoed what Guilbeault told reporters earlier this week, before he announced he is also endorsing Carney for the leadership. Guilbeault said that, while carbon pricing is "very effective," there are many other ways to reduce greenhouse gas pollution.
For instance, he noted that industrial carbon pricing was responsible for three times the emissions reductions of the consumer levy.
The federal government's carbon pricing system includes a national requirement for provinces and territories to have a minimum carbon pricing system for industrial emitters and another for consumers. The minimum price is set at $80 per tonne of emissions this year and is scheduled to climb annually until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030.
The Canadian Climate Institute, a policy think tank with financial support from the federal government, estimated last year that industrial pricing will be responsible for 20 to 48 per cent of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030. The consumer levy, meanwhile, would be responsible for eight to 14 per cent.
McKenna, Trudeau's first environment minister, has also publicly expressed support for Carney. She did not respond to an interview request this week.
Carney isn't the only leadership contender turning away from the consumer carbon price. Freeland, the former finance minister who resigned from Trudeau's cabinet in December, also plans to drop the policy if she becomes prime minister.
"Democracy means when people tell you something, you have to listen," Freeland said at her campaign launch event on Sunday.
Gould, the former Liberal House Leader who is also running for the Liberal leadership, has said she would stop the mandatory minimum carbon price from increasing as scheduled on April 1.
Chandra Arya, a backbench Liberal MP running for the leadership, has said he would get rid of the consumer price, while former Liberal MP Frank Baylis has said the policy deserves examination.
CAPTION: Liberal leadership candidate Mark Carney has told MPs he plans to walk away from the consumer carbon price.

Subject: Leadership; Greenhouse gases; Emissions; Carbon; Climate change

Full text: But who will
save us from Doug Ford?
Doug Ford may call Ontario election as early as next week: sources, Jan. 21
It appalls me that Doug Ford thinks we the voters will fall for his fantasies. Doesn't anyone remember the last election when all the talk was houses, houses, houses? We are going to build so many affordable houses. Where are the houses? Did any get built? How many more people are living on the streets now than there were at the last election?
Think of who has benefited from this Ford government - Staples, a fancy spa, the Beer Store. Meanwhile, the waiting time at hospitals and for doctors is increasing and the cost of living is rising.
Now Ford has switched gears and wants an election immediately so he can save us from U.S. President Donald Trump. But who will save us from Ford? Maybe the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt deal if it proceeds soon enough. Let's think before we vote. And that $200 cheque? It's being added on to our provincial debt.
Doris Moxam, Toronto
Premier Doug Ford can call the election. I really don't care, as long as the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario pays the full cost of $150 million to $200 million.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
Snap election would be bad for Canada and Ford
Doug Ford is acting admirably as Captain Canada. He shouldn't ruin it by calling an early election, Opinion, Jan. 21
I agree with Laura Babcock that the worst thing Premier Doug Ford could do right now - for Canada and himself - is call a snap election. And I suspect I'm just one of many Ontario voters who's happy he's leading the charge against U.S. President Donald Trump, but would never vote for him.
Sylvia Barrett, Kingston, Ont.
Premier Doug Ford has so far come out swinging against the threat from U.S. President Donald Trump. Ford is the premier of Ontario and by defending this province against Trump's threatened tariffs, he is doing a job for which he is well paid.
It is obvious that he is also an opportunist. He is creating excuses for calling an early election that will be expensive and self-serving. He has the backing of the opposition parties, showing a level of solidarity within our political system that is a rarity in politics. This is a clear indication to Trump that all political parties representing Ontario voters are united in this particular cause. What better way to say, "Don't mess with us. We're united in this cause."
Ford doesn't need an early election; he just wants it. There is a huge difference.
Patricia Steward, East York
Will Poilievre accept Musk's endorsement?
Elon Musk has Donald Trump's ear and a new-found interest in Canada. How much should we worry? Jan. 18
Considering Elon Musk appeared to enthusiastically give the Nazi fascist salute - not just once but twice - at Donald Trump's inauguration ceremonies on Monday, and that he has endorsed Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as his preferred candidate for Canada's next prime minister, I would like to ask Poilievre "Do you accept the endorsement of Elon Musk?"
Teresa Ganna Porter,
Newmarket
I'll miss the carbon rebate
Trudeau government's carbon price has had 'minimal' effect on inflation and food costs, study concludes, Jan. 16
Why aren't the candidates for the Liberal leadership race yelling from the rooftops about the carbon tax rebate that goes into most householders' accounts? For me, it makes my life much easier, covering the gas usage and a major part of my groceries. I will miss it.
Ann Boyd, Woodville, Ont.
Many can't afford to eat, let alone pay carbon tax
I find it interesting that there are people who actually like the carbon tax. They see it as a beneficial thing. They believe it helps the environment and some even say they are better off with it than without.
I've never met any of these people though. Everyone I know, without exception, says it hurts them financially. I've yet to see how taking money from me does the environment any good at all. Does it curtail anybody from driving as much as they used to? Maybe a few, but I bet it's not enough to make a difference.
We know that China and India are the biggest polluters. We know they don't have a carbon tax. The amount that Canada produces totals about one per cent of the world's carbon dioxide.
We also have an abundance of forests that live on carbon dioxide. Our total pollution amounts to less than a drop in the lake compared to other countries. Even if we completely stopped, it wouldn't matter one bit. It doesn't make a difference.
I hope people who support the carbon tax are also donating generously to the food banks, because not all of us can afford the carbon tax and we certainly don't want to pay it. In fact, a lot of us are debating on whether we keep a roof over our heads or eat.
The numerous tent cities across the country tell you that people decided to eat and forego the roof.
Jeremy Thornton, Stouffville
Fond memories
of being in on something lasting
Memories of Bob Dylan, and of a forgotten Toronto, DiManno, Jan. 15
Like Rosie DiManno, I have my own wistful memories of a Toronto past.
First, a bit of background. I started practising architecture and urban design at age 23 in Sudbury in 1954. A couple of buddies and I frequently hopped on the CPR Friday overnight train to Toronto to experience Yorkville, the "Yonge Street Strip," and later, the great Peter Appleyard, featured with the Cal Jackson Quartet in the Plaza Room just across Avenue Road from Yorkville.
I particularly remember seeing Ian and Sylvia. Beyond that, I simply recall the ambience of the neighbourhood at night and the exciting feelings of being in on something lasting.
Of course, nothing lasts forever, except maybe the memories of old guys like me. I was one of the first in line at the Cineplex in Sudbury for the first showing of the movie "A Complete Unknown" with Timothée Chalamet as Bob Dylan.
Arthur Peach, Sudbury, Ont.

Document 21 of 500

Leaders split on response to tariffs: Premiers call for wider range of retaliatory measures against Trump after meeting with PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaders-split-on-response-tariffs/docview/3158414387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaders+split+on+response+to+tariffs%3A+Premiers+call+for+wider+range+of+retaliatory+measures+against+Trump+after+meeting+with+PM&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More cracks are showing in the national response to Donald Trump's tariff threat as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau tries to rally support for a co-ordinated approach.
Several premiers, after meeting virtually with the prime minister and his team on Wednesday, suggested a consensus is building. But in reality, the splits are clearer than before.
It is not only Alberta Premier Danielle Smith who objects to any federal plan to use export tariffs on energy or restrictions on shipments of oil and gas as a response to the U.S. president's threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian imports.
Now, Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe says he is flatly opposed to any broad-based "dollar-for-dollar" counter-tariffs, as well as to export tariffs on energy or any other products from his province, which is a major producer of potash, uranium, grains and oil.
Instead, Moe said his province only supports "very targeted" and "very small" counter-tariffs, on up to "a couple of billion dollars" on imports from the U.S. But he said the counter-tariffs would not be there to "have an impact on the economy. They're there to change the views, and really change the hearts and minds of specific policy decision makers within the U.S."
Trudeau said this week he supports the principle of matching Trump's threat of "across the board" tariffs on all Canadian products with reciprocal "dollar-for-dollar" counter-tariffs. Ottawa estimates that would entail levying $150 billion worth of penalties, to match 25 per cent of the $600 billion in imported goods that Trump says he will target.
So far, however, Trudeau's government says it will roll out a series of graduated, escalating tariffs - depending on what Trump ultimately imposes - and has made clear Canada's strategy could include export tariffs on or curtailment of energy exports to the U.S.
"There are other things that we can do, and those would include broader tariffs, but they would also include things like imposing some kind of duty on products like energy, and that's not just oil and gas. That's hydroelectricity ... and on critical minerals, which includes uranium and potash and a range of other critical minerals that the United States needs from us in significant quantities. So those are all on the table," Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson told the Star in an interview Wednesday.
Liberal leadership contenders Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland have both publicly declared support for robust "dollar-for-dollar" countermeasures.
Moe told reporters that talk of broad-based counter-tariffs is "escalating the entire conversation" with Americans and would be "hurtful to Canadians" and to efforts to achieve North American energy, food and manufacturing security.
Other federal officials have told the Star that Canada could use other measures, such as redirecting some of its federal defence procurement contracts away from U.S. suppliers to other countries, like South Korea and Japan.
Ontario and B.C. have mused about stopping orders for U.S. liquor by their provincial alcohol sales outlets.
Premiers, including Ontario's Doug Ford and Quebec's François Legault, called Wednesday for a wider range of responses to Trump's trade threat, urging concerted "Buy Canadian," "Buy Local" or "Buy Quebec" efforts, but all stopped short of calling for a boycott of U.S. goods.
"If you're walking through a grocery store today, pick up the package and look at where it's made, and if it's made in the U.S., maybe you take a pass and look for something that's made in Canada," Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said on a radio program.
Premiers reiterated long-standing calls to drop interprovincial trade barriers, saying Canada should diversify its own markets and reduce dependence on American consumers.
Ford called Wednesday's virtual get-together a "really productive meeting" and, pointing to Trump's threat to slap tariffs on Canada and Mexico as soon as Feb. 1, expressed full-throated support for a robust retaliatory strategy.
"We know these tariffs are coming on Feb. 1. We need to match those tariffs, dollar for dollar, tariff for tariff, and make sure that it hurts the Americans as much as it hurts Canadians, because it's going to hurt Canadians right across the board," Ford said.
Asked if the premiers and prime minister were able to bring Alberta's premier onside, Ford conceded, "not right at this point, but I think we're moving her along.
"She understands we need to be united, and I understand that she wants to protect her main commodity, but country comes first over anything," he said.
"You can't throw a tool away from the tool box. As I said to the premiers, you can't bring a knife to a gunfight. Usually, the guy with a knife loses in a gunfight."
The reality is nobody knows exactly when Trump may order tariffs be applied, at what level, and on what products.
Despite his musings, Trump's tariffs may not come until April 1, the deadline he set for a report on how the U.S. can eliminate trade deficits, illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling at the northern and southern borders, and how to close gaps in the recently renegotiated North American free trade pact, which is scheduled to be reviewed in 2026.
So the question of how far Canadian counter-tariffs should go and whether Ottawa should impose export tariffs remains a critical one.
Legault said he doesn't like the phrase "dollar-for-dollar" countermeasures because it sounds too much like an "eye for an eye, tooth for a tooth."
But while he agreed that nothing should be taken off the table, Legault stressed that does not mean Quebec has given its "consent" to blocking or taxing its energy exports.
In a statement, Smith called the virtual first ministers' meeting "more positive this week, as there appears to be a growing consensus (though not entirely unanimous)" on several points. She said premiers agreed on the need to demonstrate Canada's commitment to "tighter border security and to accelerating" its commitment to meet the NATO target of spending two per cent of GDP on defence.
With files from Alex Ballingall
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Alex Ballingall

Subject: International trade; Potash; Critical minerals; Prime ministers; Energy exports & imports; Tariffs
