Document 22 of 500

Goodbye Trudeau, Hello Trump 2.0. We're about to enter a new era in world politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/goodbye-trudeau-hello-trump-2-0-were-about-enter/docview/3158398008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Goodbye+Trudeau%2C+Hello+Trump+2.0.+We%27re+about+to+enter+a+new+era+in+world+politics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By mid-March, Justin Trudeau will be out as Liberal leader and prime minister of Canada. By May, the country could quite likely have a Conservative government, bringing an end to a decade of progressive centrist rule.
The right, in Canada and around much of the Western world, is ascendant - at least for now. Trudeau's departure is significant for Canada. But occurring so close to this week's inauguration of U.S. President Donald Trump, it also feels like the end of a certain period in world history. It was of an in-between era in which centrist governments, led by the likes of Trudeau, Barack Obama, and Emmanuel Macron, formed an uneasy centrist bulwark against a growing, emboldened, and authoritarian right.
Now, suddenly, the world looks quite different. Today, in France, Germany, and the United States the right - particularly the radical right - has or is set to replace incumbent centre or centre-left governments or legislatures. Even in the United Kingdom, where Labour, now less a left party and more a centrist outfit, won a summer election replacing the Conservatives, the right is still popular, including the radical Reform UK party, which is polling at 24 percent.
Not all right wingers are the same. Parties range from traditional, market worshipping, tax-cutting, program-gutting conservatives to an extremist authoritarian variety that is typically anti-immigrant, anti-gay, virulently nationalist, and belligerent. In Canada, the Conservatives are much the former sort, like their Tory counterparts in the U.K. (though they're not above flirting with the far right). In Russia, India, and Hungary, the right has proven to be more the latter, but there are plenty of their sympathizers or clones in the West. The German AfD is one example, and it has even caught the attention, and approval, of X owner and Donald Trump backer Elon Musk.
National interests also tend to trump ideology so that even parties that share cross-border sympathies may become rivals. For instance, Pierre Poilievre has no choice but to oppose Donald Trump's talk of annexing Canada with "economic force."
But at the same time, the right often shares networks, grievances, strategies, policies, and ways of speaking. And they're now in or set to be in power around the world.
We have been here before, and there is both legitimate reason for worry, and for hope, too.
The 1980s saw a wave of conservatism dominate the West, from Ronald Reagan in the US to Margaret Thatcher in the UK, and Brian Mulroney in Canada. They ushered in an era of tax cuts, deregulation, and deindustrialization. Scholars and writers including David Harvey and Alex Himelfarb argue that era set up the problems we face today, including shabby state programs, wealth and income inequality, and the concern that globalization was a bad deal for day-to-day workers - but not so much for the wealthy.
Like the right's failures in the 1980s, the current iteration of conservative wave stands to generate problems for years, or even decades to come. Anti-immigrant policies in the UK won't save that country from a collapsing healthcare system and lacklustre economy any more than cutting the capital gains tax and ending carbon pricing will make life more affordable for Canadians. Trump's tariffs will make life miserable for consumers in the US, and this administration is unlikely to revive a manufacturing economy that's been dismantled by presidents and congresses, Republican and Democrat, for the better part of five decades.
Those missteps, however, also provide an opportunity, and are also a reason for at least some hope when few seem to exist. When these "solutions" fail to solve anyone's problems, the risk of another backlash will be high. In that time, the left ought to be ready to offer a grassroots movement that speaks to the material needs of working class people. And the best time to start selling that program is now as the progressive liberal era wanes and the conservative counter-era rises. For all our sakes, let us hope it may not last long.
CAPTION: Elon Musk with President Donald Trump as he arrives to watch SpaceX's mega rocket Starship lift off for a test flight from Starbase in Boca Chica, Texas, Tuesday, Nov. 19, 2024.
CREDIT: David Moscrop Contributor

Subject: Conservatism; Authoritarianism; Grass roots movement; Decades

Location: United States--US; Canada; United Kingdom--UK

People: Trump, Donald J; Musk, Elon

Title: Goodbye Trudeau, Hello Trump 2.0. We're about to enter a new era in world politics

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 23, 2025

Section: opinion/contributors

Document 23 of 500

Trudeau has one last chance to shine

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-has-one-last-chance-shine/docview/3158397999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+has+one+last+chance+to+shine&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is facing down one of the most powerful threats to Canada at a moment when his own power is waning.
It may turn out to be one of the defining chapters for Trudeau himself and Canada as a whole. Critics have been saying since he resigned a few weeks ago: Is this any time for a lame-duck prime minister?
Clearly the big-picture answer to that is No. In an ideal world, Trudeau would be in the same position he was when Donald Trump first became president in 2017 - leader of a majority government, with premiers and political parties united behind Team Canada.
But that was never going to be the case. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the man who wants Trudeau's job and sees it within his grasp, was never going to give the prime minister the endorsement of solidarity. Premiers, though mostly united now (Alberta's Danielle Smith excepted) had already been talking of going their own way with Trump and not waiting for Trudeau to act.
In short, Trudeau and Trump find themselves as usual in opposite spots. Trump has never been more powerful; Trudeau, arguably, is at his weakest.
But a case can be made that maybe this is the time when Canada needs a prime minister with just one job - staring down the threat that this even more erratic, unpredictable president presents to this country.
Had Trudeau not resigned and prorogued Parliament, he would have been juggling an imminent confidence vote and the potential collapse of his government, plunging Liberals into an election that would have been more than a distraction.
Had he still prorogued Parliament to buy himself a bit of time, Trudeau still would have been facing all the demands of election preparation whenever he could take a break from dealing with Trump. He and his Prime Minister's Office would also have to come up with a throne speech that could double as an election platform.
That task falls now to the person who replaces Trudeau. How much help he or she would want from the outgoing PMO is an open question - probably not much.
It's no doubt a disappointment for Trudeau that he won't be acting as host when Canada holds the G7 summit in June at Kananaskis, Alta. But here again is a burden lifted. Trump made the last G7 meeting in Canada a little too interesting in 2018, departing in a flood of angry tweets at the prime minister over Trudeau's audacity in condemning tariffs.
Trudeau is now free to trash talk those tariffs to his heart's content, and he can be reasonably confident Canadians are united behind him.
It's been interesting to watch Canada's premiers - again, Smith excepted - since Trudeau resigned. The usual shots at Ottawa are conspicuously absent, even from premiers such as Saskatchewan's Scott Moe, who can usually be relied upon to use his podium to say why Trudeau has to go.
Trudeau is going. Point made, taken. As heard among the flood of commentary over the past week, it's a bit like watching what happens when the dog catches the car it's been chasing: now what?
Of course, Trudeau has prime ministerial duties to manage beyond Trump in the weeks leading up to when Liberals choose his replacement on March 9.
There are the usual affairs of state, as well as whatever is within his limited power to do to seal his legacy - how much he can Poilievre-proof some signature achievements, whether that's on child care or the yet-unfinished business of pharmacare and dental care.
A wave of Liberal ads, prepared when Trudeau was still planning on fighting the next election, are very much organized around this theme - all that's at risk if Poilievre becomes the next prime minister.
Liberals will still hammer away at this idea, no matter who is the next leader, but with Trudeau leaving, this looks less like securing a personal legacy than a progressive one in the face of a conservative revival.
Trump, with his shock-and-awe assault on progressive policies just this week, may turn out to be the Liberals' best example of all that's imperiled by a re-energized right. Conservatives may applaud some of what Trump is doing to beat back the "woke" politics of progressivism, but they're going to be a little more careful as the full extent of Trump's rights rollbacks become evident.
What must be a relief for Trudeau is that he really doesn't need to give Poilievre a second thought right now. He is free to do what he has always seemed to do with the Conservative leader: ignore him. Poilievre didn't defeat Trudeau; that loss was suffered because of disunity in Liberal ranks.
Maybe that's why Trudeau has seemed to look somewhat liberated in his recent public appearances. He has one main job now - handling Trump, and how well he pulls it off may well define his career, and his decision to step down.
CAPTION: How he deals with the threats from U.S. President Donald Trump could be one of the defining chapters of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's career, Susan Delacourt writes.Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press

Document 24 of 500

Wave of conservatism an opportunity for progressives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wave-conservatism-opportunity-progressives/docview/3158397174/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wave+of+conservatism+an+opportunity+for+progressives&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By mid-March, Justin Trudeau will be out as Liberal leader and prime minister of Canada. By May, the country could quite likely have a Conservative government, bringing an end to a decade of progressive centrist rule.
The right, in Canada and around much of the western world, is ascendant - at least for now. Trudeau's departure is significant for Canada. But occurring so close to this week's inauguration of U.S. President Donald Trump, it also feels like the end of a certain period in world history. It was of an in-between era in which centrist governments, led by the likes of Trudeau, Barack Obama and Emmanuel Macron, formed an uneasy centrist bulwark against a growing, emboldened and authoritarian right.
Now, suddenly, the world looks quite different. Today, in France, Germany and the United States, the right - particularly the radical right - has or is set to replace incumbent centre or centre-left governments or legislatures. Even in the United Kingdom, where Labour, now less a left party and more a centrist outfit, won a summer election replacing the Conservatives, the right is still popular, including the radical Reform UK party, which is polling at 24 per cent.
Not all right-wingers are the same. Parties range from traditional, market worshipping, tax-cutting, program-gutting conservatives to an extremist authoritarian variety that is typically anti-immigrant, anti-gay, virulently nationalist and belligerent.
In Canada, the Conservatives are much the former sort, like their Tory counterparts in the U.K. (though they're not above flirting with the far right). In Russia, India and Hungary, the right has proven to be more the latter, but there are plenty of their sympathizers or clones in the West. Germany's AfD is one example and it has even caught the attention, and approval, of X owner and Donald Trump-backer Elon Musk.
National interests also tend to trump ideology, so that even parties that share cross-border sympathies may become rivals. For instance, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has no choice but to oppose Trump's talk of annexing Canada with "economic force."
But at the same time, the right often shares networks, grievances, strategies, policies and ways of speaking. And they're now in or set to be in power around the world.
We have been here before, and there is legitimate reason for both worry and hope.
The 1980s saw a wave of conservatism dominate the West, from Ronald Reagan in the U.S. to Margaret Thatcher in the U.K. and Brian Mulroney in Canada. They ushered in an era of tax cuts, deregulation and deindustrialization.
Scholars and writers including David Harvey and Alex Himelfarb argue that era set up the problems we face today, including shabby state programs, wealth and income inequality, and the concern that globalization was a bad deal for day-to-day workers - but not so much for the wealthy.
Like the right's failures in the 1980s, the current conservative wave stands to generate problems for years, or even decades, to come.
Anti-immigrant policies in the U.K. won't save that country from a collapsing health care system and lacklustre economy any more than cutting the capital gains tax and ending carbon pricing will make life more affordable for Canadians.
Trump's tariffs will make life miserable for consumers in the U.S., and this administration is unlikely to revive a manufacturing economy that's been dismantled by presidents and congresses, Republican and Democrat, for the better part of five decades.
Those missteps, however, also provide an opportunity and a reason for at least some hope. When these "solutions" fail to solve anyone's problems, the risk of another backlash will be high.
In that time, the left ought to be ready to offer a grassroots movement that speaks to the material needs of working-class people. And the best time to start selling that program is now, as the progressive liberal era wanes and the conservative counter-era rises. For all our sakes, let us hope it may not last long.
David Moscrop is an Ottawa-based politics writer and
author of "Too Dumb For Democracy? Why We Make Bad
Political Decisions and How We Can Make Better Ones."
CAPTION: When the "solutions" promoted by Elon Musk and U.S. President Donald Trump fail to solve anyone's problems, the left must be ready to offer a grassroots movement that speaks to the needs of working-class people, David Moscrop writes.Brandon Bell The Associated Press
CREDIT: David Moscrop Contributor David Moscrop is an Ottawa-based politics writer and author of "Too Dumb For Democracy? Why We Make Bad Political Decisions and How We Can Make Better Ones.";

Document 25 of 500

No mandate to call an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-mandate-call-early-election/docview/3158397173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+mandate+to+call+an+early+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the looming threat of extreme tariffs levied against our country, some have stepped up to promote a strong, unified Team Canada approach to the new Trump administration. First among them is Premier Doug Ford, who has cloaked himself in the mantle of Captain Canada and done an admirable job in the sabre-rattling phase of this trade war.
Even Ford's toughest critics are impressed by his clarity and conviction as chair of the Council of the Federation, putting country over politics and trying to save Canada from a hostile U.S. government.
But while the country looks to him for more tough talk against U.S. President Donald Trump, Ford is trying to parlay this profile boost into a justification for calling an early election.
Ford is making his argument for a snap election on two fronts: that Ontario needs a loud voice and that his government needs a mandate to spend tens of billions of dollars to protect us from the impact of tariffs, which could claim half a million jobs. In 2018, when other tariffs were threatened, Ford did not seek a new mandate. Now, he says the aggressive tone of Trump 2.0 is the reason for the urgency.
While it is true that the new Trump regime's posture and "Golden Age of America" rhetoric echo the 19th-century doctrine of Manifest Destiny as they eye our land mass, call our prime minister a "governor" and talk of erasing our border, Ford's argument for the need to throw Ontario into an expensive election doesn't make sense.
It is, in fact, at this very moment when our federal government is prorogued and the country hangs in suspended animation, that we need Ford to focus on his role as a leader among the premiers. Like Ford or not, the country needs a steady hand on the wheel while Trudeau finishes his self-imposed lame-duck phase as prime minister and the Liberal party selects their new leader.
A campaigning Ford under daily assault for his policy record and a litany of scandals is not as loud a voice as a premier with almost 500 days to go in his second majority. His rush to call an election is more likely to leverage this moment and gain cover from policy failures and scandals.
With the tariff war's Sword of Damocles hanging over our province, the people of Ontario may not reflect on Ford's record of overpaying for private nurses, failing to meet his own housing targets, underfunding ODSP adding to 81,000 people suffering from homelessness and his government's inability to complete infrastructure projects on time, such as the Eglington Crosstown LRT. The spectre of the RCMP report on the Greenbelt scandal may also be overlooked in a snap election called under the auspices of saving our economy.
The second part of Ford's argument is about needing a new mandate to spend billions of tax dollars.
Why does that suddenly matter to a premier who made the unpopular decision to retrofit Ontario Place, which could now cost us as much as $2.2 billion?
Why does our permission to spend money matter to a premier who spent almost a quarter of a billion to prematurely back out of a deal with the Beer Store to rush alcohol into corner stores?
Why does the careful allocation of tax dollars matter now when recently he mused about an unnecessary tunnel under Highway 401 that would cost between $50 billion and $100 billion?
For a premier who spent $103 million in the past year on advertisements telling us how he's "getting it done for the people" and who has all but dismissed scathing reports from the auditor general about his overspending, the argument that he suddenly needs our permission to spend our money is pretty rich.
No other premiers have seized on this trade war crisis to make their own political opportunities. Ford risks losing his Captain Canada power if he puts his ambition over the country.
Laura Babcock is president of POWERGROUP Communications and host of "The OShow"
podcast.
CREDIT: Laura Babcock Contributor Laura Babcock is president of POWERGROUP Communications and host of "The OShow" podcast.;

Document 26 of 500

Province replaces CEO of Health atHome: Patients, doctors have faced shortages of medical supplies for several months

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-replaces-ceo-health-athome/docview/3158397143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+replaces+CEO+of+Health+atHome%3A+Patients%2C+doctors+have+faced+shortages+of+medical+supplies+for+several+months&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The CEO of Ontario Health atHome, the beleaguered provincial agency responsible for the provision of home-care medical supplies, has been fired.
A source confirmed to the Star that Cynthia Martineau was let go Tuesday from her role as CEO of the agency, which has been under fire following reports last fall that patients who receive medical care at home, and the doctors and nurses who treat them, could not access basic medical supplies, such as syringes, pain pumps and catheters.
"Cynthia Martineau is no longer with Ontario Health atHome," the agency said in a statement to the Star. "We understand how vitally important home and community care is to families across the province. That is why, together with our partners, we will continue to take action to improve the delivery of high-quality, accessible and connected care."
Martineau could not be reached for comment.
In a memo to employees announcing the leadership change Tuesday, Carol Annett, chair of Ontario Health atHome's board of directors, said Martineau "has led our organization with compassion, dedication and an unwavering commitment to serving patients and their families."
"She has been instrumental in advancing our organizational goals and we are grateful for her many contributions," reads the memo, obtained by the Star.
The memo says that Anna Greenberg, executive vice-president and COO of Ontario Health, will take on the role of interim CEO of Ontario Health atHome.
Queen's Park online news site The Trillium and CBC News initially reported that Martineau was no longer with the agency.
Home-care patients and their caregivers began to notice in October 2024 that shipments of medical supplies were arriving late, incomplete or in some cases, not at all. Doctors reported that the shortages also affected pain-relief drugs and some medical-assistance-in-dying (MAID) kits, which were showing up incomplete.
The problems coincided with changes to provincial contracts with private companies that procure medical supplies and fulfil deliveries across Ontario.
In late September 2024, the province launched a new system in which two companies, Cardinal Health and Ontario Medical Supply (OMS), became the master service providers responsible for procurement of medical supplies, while four companies, including OMS, Bayshore Specialty Rx, Shoppers Drug Mart and Robinsons, became responsible for distribution to patients' homes.
This was a departure from previous years in which many local suppliers, such as pharmacies, were responsible for managing the procurement and delivery of medical supplies in regions that roughly coincided with the geographic areas covered by the province's 14 former Local Health Integration Networks (LHINs).
Patients and their caregivers the Star spoke to say supply shortages were never an issue under the old system.
Sources from the medical supply industry told the Star in November that the shortages were the result of several problems with the way the new system was rolled out.
First, the list of supplies and medications needed for home care, known as the "provincial formulary," wasn't ready until just before the new system was launched, the sources said.
Second, the quantities forecast by the province were too low to meet demand.
And third, a reduction in the number of suppliers responsible for larger geographic areas resulted in logistical and delivery issues.
"In the future, any centralized procurement body needs to consult with the people who do the work with patients on the front lines," said one industry source involved with procurement. "That was the key failure here. There was no consultation."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said Premier Doug Ford's government should have taken responsibility for a "bungled" launch of the agency.
"The minister of health dropped the ball," Stiles told reporters Wednesday.
"This is a government that cannot get medicine and medical supplies to sick people. This is the most basic responsibility ... and they are failing. So I think the real answer here is we need to get rid of this government."
Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, said the government expects "accountability and timely delivery of critical medical items that patients in home care need."
"While the NDP have voted against additional support to grow and modernize the home and community care sector, our government invested an additional $2 billion last year. This is in addition to the $1 billion our government invested through budget 2022, doubling our investment in the delivery of home and community care," she said.
None of these explanations are of any comfort to Penny Moore, a home-care patient in London, Ont. She says deliveries of catheters and drainage bags that she relies on have been disrupted since the province launched the new system.
In the past two weeks alone, Moore says catheters she expected to receive did not arrive, and when they did, they were made of latex, which she cannot use due to an allergy.
When the right kind of catheters and drainage bags did show up, the quality was "garbage" compared to what she received previously, she says.
"If I went out in this weather right now with those bags, because they're so thin, they will split, and they don't drain," Moore says.
Last fall, the catheter system Moore was using delivered by Bayshore Specialty Rx, one of the new provincial distributors, stopped draining properly and backed up into her kidneys, requiring her to be hospitalized.
Bayshore did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
"Home care is supposed to keep us out of hospital, not put us in the hospital," she says. "It's supposed to keep us out of the emerg and not in emerg waiting 15 hours to do what should be done at home."
"But if we're not getting supplies, what are we supposed to do?"
Brett Geneau, a representative of CUPE, which has about 1,800 members at the provincial agency, said the home-care medical supply problems are a symptom of a massive restructuring of Ontario's 14 former LHINs into Ontario Health atHome that began in 2023 to oversee all in-home and community-based care across the province.
Geneau, who works as a team assistant for Ontario Health atHome, says the agency has been in "chaos" since the first week the supply system was changed.
He says employees dealing with patients calling to report problems with supply deliveries were given scripts of what to say "so that we wouldn't say something that we shouldn't."
"These products were not available or not showing up and it was weeks and months on end," Geneau says. "There was no answer on when these products were coming in."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 27 of 500

Ford seeks 'very strong mandate': Premier did not mince words about snap election he is expected to trigger as early as next week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-seeks-very-strong-mandate/docview/3158396433/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+seeks+%27very+strong+mandate%27%3A+Premier+did+not+mince+words+about+snap+election+he+is+expected+to+trigger+as+early+as+next+week&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has told Progressive Conservative supporters he needs a "very strong mandate" in his upcoming snap election in order to send President Donald Trump a "message" that Ontarians won't tolerate U.S. tariffs.
Speaking to about 400 people at a Markham fundraiser that was closed to the media, Ford did not mince words about a vote he is expected to trigger as early as next week, which will cost taxpayers between $150 million and $175 million.
"We really need a very strong mandate, probably the strongest we've ever had, we've ever asked for, and it sends a clear message," the two-term premier said at the Hilton Toronto/Markham Suites Conference Centre on Tuesday night.
A recording of the speech, which road-tested some of the "protect Ontario" themes he plans to use in an election provisionally set for Feb. 27, was obtained by the Star.
"In politics, folks, when you go into an election, and if people feel you're a little weak, they try to take advantage of you," the Tory leader said.
"And I'm not saying anyone is, but I can tell you one thing: a strong mandate from the Ontario people sends a message down to the U.S."
"It sends a message to whoever is in (power) in Ottawa and it sends a message we're working all well together with my great colleagues, all the premiers, across the (country)."
Asked by reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday when the election would come, the premier said, "Stay tuned."
Although Ford already has a stable majority government - and an Ontario vote is not scheduled until June 2026 - he wants to move quickly because of the Trump 25 per cent tariff threat. His government also faces the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
The premier also hopes to exploit the fact that Liberal activists might be too busy in the coming weeks with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Bonnie Crombie's provincial campaign.
Finally, he wants to face voters before a federal election expected this spring that polls suggest Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will win.
That's because Ontario voters traditionally opt for different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, meaning a Poilievre victory would be bad for Ford.
The major parties have been building war chests in anticipation of an early election. The governing Conservatives raised more than $10.6 million last year, while Marit Stiles's New Democrats brought in $5.34 million and Bonnie Crombie's Liberals collected $5.4 million.
Although Stiles, Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have said there's no need for an imminent election because they will support the tens of billions in stimulus funding that a tariff-battered Ontario economy might need, Ford told supporters, "Trust me, in politics, you have to have the mandate from the people.
"Politicians don't give other politicians mandates, OK? Who gives people mandates, a politician mandate? Other people."
Stiles said Wednesday the election will be about more than tariffs and the 500,000 jobs at risk, because the NDP will be hammering Ford on other issues, such as affordability challenges and problems Ontarians experience daily in an overburdened health-care system hobbled by a growing doctor shortage that has left more than 2.5 million residents without a physician.
"They don't trust Mr. Ford to protect their jobs because they can't trust Mr. Ford to deliver them a family doctor ... people have seen housing costs go through the roof," the NDP leader told reporters at a "safe schools summit" organized by the Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation.
"On election day, people are going to be asking themselves, 'Is my life any better today than it was seven years ago?' "
For her part, Crombie has maintained "the answer to chaos is not more chaos," and implored the premier to fulfil his four-year term instead of triggering a vote.
"Calling an early election is unnecessary, expensive and creates instability and uncertainty when we need a calm and firm resolve," she said Tuesday at the Rural Ontario Municipalities Association conference in Toronto.
"It's a ridiculous argument that the premier needs special permission and a new mandate to do his job - the one he was elected to do."
In terms of campaign readiness, the Tories have 88 candidates nominated in Ontario's 124 ridings, while the New Democrats have 34, the Liberals have 46 and the Greens have 58.
The election timing coincides with voter-friendly measures like $200 "rebate" cheques that the government is mailing out to 15 million Ontario adults and children in the coming days.
That $3-billion payout - despite a $6.6-billion provincial deficit - is designed to offset Ontarians' affordability concerns at a time of both rising prices and unemployment, but opposition leaders have accused Ford of trying to buy votes.
Currently in the legislature, there are 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at Canada's Premier's Conference in Toronto in December. Although Ford has a stable majority government, he says he wants a snap election because of the threat of tariffs from the U.S.GEOFF ROBINSAFP via GETTY IMAGES file photo

Document 28 of 500

Justin Trudeau looked destined to fade. Now he's getting a boost from an unlikely source

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-looked-destined-fade-now-hes/docview/3158921683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+looked+destined+to+fade.+Now+he%27s+getting+a+boost+from+an+unlikely+source&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is facing down one of the most powerful threats to Canada at a moment when his own power is waning.
It may turn out to be one of the defining chapters for Trudeau himself and Canada as a whole. Critics have been saying since he resigned a few weeks ago: is this any time for a lame-duck prime minister?
Clearly the big-picture answer to that is No. In an ideal world, Trudeau would be in the same position he was when Donald Trump first became president in 2017 - leader of a majority government, with premiers and political parties united behind Team Canada.
But that was never going to be the case. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the man who wants Trudeau's job and sees it within his grasp, was never going to give the prime minister the endorsement of solidarity. Premiers, though mostly united now (Alberta's Danielle Smith excepted) had already been talking of going their own way with Trump and not waiting for Trudeau to act.
In short, Trudeau and Trump find themselves as usual in opposite spots. Trump has never been more powerful; Trudeau, arguably, is at his weakest.
But a case can be made that maybe this is the time when Canada needs a prime minister with just one job - staring down the threat that this even more erratic, unpredictable president presents to this country.
Had Trudeau not resigned and prorogued Parliament, he would have been juggling an imminent confidence vote and the potential collapse of his government, plunging Liberals into an election that would have been more than a distraction.
Had he still prorogued Parliament to buy himself a bit of time, Trudeau still would have been facing all the demands of election preparation whenever he could take a break from dealing with Trump. He and his Prime Minister's Office would also have to come up with a throne speech that could double as an election platform.
That task falls now to the person who replaces Trudeau. How much help he or she would want from the outgoing PMO is an open question - probably not much.
It's no doubt a disappointment for Trudeau that he won't be acting as host when Canada holds the G7 summit in June this year at Kananaskis, AB. But here again is a burden lifted. Trump made the last G7 meeting in Canada a little too interesting in 2018, departing in a flood of angry tweets at the prime minister over Trudeau's audacity in condemning tariffs.
Trudeau is now free to trash talk those tariffs to his heart's content, and he can be reasonably confident Canadians are united behind him.
It's been interesting to watch Canada's premiers - again, Smith excepted - since Trudeau resigned. The usual shots at Ottawa are conspicuously absent, even from premiers such as Saskatchewan's Scott Moe, who can usually be relied upon to use his podium to say why Trudeau has to go.
Trudeau is going. Point made, taken. As heard among the flood of commentary over the past last week, it's a bit like watching what happens when the dog catches the car it's been chasing: now what?
Of course, Trudeau has prime ministerial duties to manage beyond Trump in the weeks leading up to when Liberals choose his replacement on March 9.
There are the usual affairs of state, as well as whatever is within his limited power to do to seal his legacy - how much he can Poilievre-proof some signature achievements, whether that's on child care or the yet-unfinished business of pharmacare and dental care.
A wave of Liberal ads, prepared when Trudeau was still planning on fighting the next election, are very much organized around this theme - all that's at risk if Poilievre becomes the next prime minister.
Liberals will still hammer away at this idea, no matter who is the next leader, but with Trudeau leaving, this looks less like securing a personal legacy than a progressive one in the face of a conservative revival.
Trump, with his shock-and-awe assault on progressive policies just this week, may turn out to be the Liberals' best example of all that's imperiled by a re-energized right. Conservatives may applaud some of what Trump is doing to beat back the "woke" politics of progressivism, but they're going to be a little more careful as the full extent of Trump's rights rollbacks become evident.
What must be a relief for Trudeau is that he really doesn't need to give Poilievre a second thought right now. He is free to do what he has always seemed to do with the Conservative leader: ignore him. Poilievre didn't defeat Trudeau; that loss was suffered because of disunity in Liberal ranks.
Maybe that's why Trudeau has seemed to look somewhat liberated in his recent public appearances. He has one main job now - handling Trump, and how well he pulls it off may well define his career, and his decision to step down.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau leaves following a cabinet retreat at Chateau Montebello in Montebello, Que., on Jan. 21, 2025.

Document 29 of 500

Doug Ford asks for a 'very strong mandate' in snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-asks-very-strong-mandate-snap-election/docview/3158921405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+asks+for+a+%27very+strong+mandate%27+in+snap+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has told Progressive Conservative supporters he needs a "very strong mandate" in his upcoming snap election in order to send President Donald Trump a "message" that Ontarians won't tolerate U.S. tariffs.
Speaking to about 400 people at a Markham fundraiser that was closed to the media, Ford did not mince words about a vote he is expected to trigger as early as next week, which will cost taxpayers between $150 million and $175 million.
"We really need a very strong mandate, probably the strongest we've ever had, we've ever asked for, and it sends a clear message," the two-term premier said at the Hilton Toronto/Markham Suites Conference Centre on Tuesday night.
A recording of the speech, which road-tested some of the "protect Ontario" themes he plans to use in an election provisionally set for Feb. 27, was obtained by the Star.
"In politics, folks, when you go into an election, and if people feel you're a little weak, they try to take advantage of you," the Tory leader said.
"And I'm not saying anyone is, but I can tell you one thing: a strong mandate from the Ontario people sends a message down to the U.S.," said Ford.
"It sends a message to whoever is in (power) in Ottawa and it sends a message we're working all well together with my great colleagues, all the premiers, across the (country)," he said.
Asked by reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday when the election would come, the premier said: "Stay tuned."
While Ford already has a stable majority government - and an Ontario vote is not scheduled until June 2026 - he wants to move quickly because of the Trump 25 per cent tariff threat as well as the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
The premier also hopes to exploit the fact that Liberal activists might be too busy in the coming weeks with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Bonnie Crombie's provincial campaign.
Finally, he wants to face voters before a federal election expected this spring that polls suggest Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will win.
That's because Ontario voters traditionally opt for different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, meaning a Poilievre victory would be bad for Ford.
The major parties have been building war chests in anticipation of an early election. The governing Conservatives raised more than $10.6 million last year while Marit Stiles' New Democrats brought in $10.4 million and Bonnie Crombie's Liberals collected $5.4 million.
While Stiles, Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have said there's no need for an imminent election because they will support the tens of billions in stimulus funds that a tariff-battered Ontario economy might need, Ford told supporters: "trust me, in politics, you have to have the mandate from the people.
"Politicians don't give other politicians mandates, OK? Who gives people mandates, a politician mandate? Other people."
Stiles said Wednesday the election will be about more than tariffs and the 500,000 jobs at risk because the NDP will be hammering Ford on other issues, such as problems Ontarians experience daily in an overburdened health-care system hobbled by a growing doctor shortage that has left more than 2.5 million residents without a physician, and affordability challenges.
"They don't trust Mr. Ford to protect their jobs because they can't trust Mr. Ford to deliver them a family doctor ... people have seen housing costs go through the roof," the NDP leader told reporters at a "safe schools summit" organized by the Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation.
"On Election Day, people are going to be asking themselves: 'Is my life any better today than it was seven years ago?'"
For her part, Crombie has maintained "the answer to chaos is not more chaos," and implored the premier to Ford to fulfil his four-year term instead of triggering a vote.
"Calling an early election is unnecessary, expensive and creates instability and uncertainty when we need a calm and firm resolve," she said Tuesday at the Rural Ontario Municipalities Association conference in Toronto.
"It's a ridiculous argument that the premier needs special permission and a new mandate to do his job - the one he was elected to do."
In terms of campaign readiness, the Tories have 88 candidates nominated in Ontario's 124 ridings while the New Democrats have 34, the Liberals have 46 and the Greens have 58.
The election timing coincides with voter-friendly measures like $200 "rebate" cheques that the government is mailing out to 15 million Ontario adults and children in coming days.
That $3-billion payout - despite a $6.6 billion provincial deficit - is designed to offset Ontarians' affordability concerns at a time of both rising prices and unemployment, but opposition leaders have accused Ford of trying to buy votes.
Currently in the legislature, there are 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford reacts to a comment made by federal Finance and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc, not shown, as they speak to reporters following their meeting at the Ontario Legislature on Jan. 14, 2025.

Ontario Heath atHome CEO fired following home-care medical supply shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-heath-athome-ceo-fired-following-home/docview/3158070383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Heath+atHome+CEO+fired+following+home-care+medical+supply+shortage&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The CEO of Ontario Health atHome, the provincial agency responsible for the provision of home-care medical supplies, has been fired.
A source confirmed to the Star that Cynthia Martineau was let go yesterday from her role as CEO of the beleaguered provincial agency, which has been at the under fire following reports in September that patients who receive medical care at home, and the doctors and nurses who treat them, could not access basic medical supplies, such as syringes, pain pumps and catheters. The Trillium and CBC News initially reported that Martineau was no longer with the agency.
Neither Ontario Health atHome nor the Ontario Ministry of Health immediately responded to the Star's request for comment.
Home-care patients and their caregivers began to notice in October 2024 that shipments of medical supplies were delayed, arriving incomplete or not showing up at all. The problems coincided with changes to provincial contracts with companies that procure supplies and fulfil deliveries in late September.
More to come

Canada does right in honouring Terry Fox

Full text: Honouring Canadian hero Terry Fox is long overdueTerry Fox will be the next Canadian featured on the $5 bill, Dec. 16
It is long overdue Terry Fox will be featured on the Canadian $5 bill, where we will now see the face of this young man, our inspirational national hero.
I have just read the book 'Forever Terry' published in 2020 by his brother Darrell, essentially a collection of inspirational letters voluntarily submitted by 50 well-known Canadians to create a unique inspirational memoire. To name a few contributors Bobby Orr, Jann Arden, Darryl Sittler, Rick Hanse
Terry Fox's legacy must live on; he was a person whose achievements in the face of tremendous odds stand as an example for everyone. The next generation of Canadians need to have a ready reply when those pessimists among us state "one person can't really make a difference."
The response can be found in two simple words: Terry Fox.
This is a 'must read' for every Canadian.
Bruce Walker, Oakville
Rebate cheques a joke as health care system crumbles'Sad state of affairs': Family doctor's arrival in Walkerton leads hundreds to line up in snow to sign up, Jan. 15
The Star's articles on several hundred people lining up in the dark and cold in Walkerton to have the chance to register for a family doctor reflect an important issue.
This, just when the provincial government, which is running a deficit of approximately $6.6 billion, is about to send every Ontario taxpayer a cheque for $200 at a cost of $3 billion.
Of course, some Ontarians need the $200 but for others it will make very little difference. If Premier Doug Ford actually cared and was not trying to bribe us with our own money, more funds could be invested in our crumbling health care system.
I am trying to decide if my $200 is going to be donated to a food bank or to an opposition party.
David Crawford, Toronto

Chrystia Freeland's name says it all. Canada is a 'free land' and we Canadians will not be bullied by President Donald Trump, or anyone else.
For her name alone she deserves to be the next Liberal leader and prime minister.
Hazel Regan, Toronto
What's happened to the Science Centre is a disgraceWorkers say goodbye to an almost-empty Ontario Science Centre as repairs get underway, Dec. 10
I am so disheartened at what has become of our beloved Science Centre.
It was a place I visited many, many times and loved it. Last month, I took my grandchildren to Sherway Gardens to see some exhibits put on by the Science Centre. There were only two, a Lego collection and a circus-themed event. In the Lego collection, there were lovely Lego structures to look at and pieces of Lego the children could build from. This took about five minutes.
The circus theme had some costumes and a couple of other displays, and the only thing of interest there was a tight-rope walk. Two of my four grandchildren participated. It was a good thing the display wasn't busy, otherwise the entire event would have been useless.
I am so very angry with Doug Ford for decimating what was a world-class venture and adventure. For what? And for what cost?
It will never equal what was there at Don Mills and Eglinton.
Marilyn Herbert, Toronto
Tranjan's column nails it when it comes to our premierDoug Ford always had a mandate to invest in Ontario, he just didn't do his job, Jan. 19
Ricardo Tranjan's column on Doug Ford's follies past, and current musings about a possible early election, is right on the money.
I'm sick and tired of hearing advertisements telling me how much Ford is doing for Ontarians which are "paid for by the government."
No, he forgets or hopes we will forget that government money is our money being used on these advertisements, just as the gimmicky $200 rebate cheques being sent out soon also come from our money. As was the totally unnecessary $225 million paid to Brewers Retail to get beer into corner stores a year earlier than the contract allowed.
Just think how much good could have been done with those tax dollars.
And we do not need an early election costing millions of Ontarians money to give him a mandate to act " for the people." He already has a mandate for that in the form of a majority government.
Ford's modus operandi is to try to distract attention from things like the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal .
These cynical manipulations are an insult to the intelligence of the people.
Being sent $200 of my money won't bribe me to vote for him in any election, whether it be sooner or later.
Caroline Harris, Stouffville, Ont.
Bumbling Blue Jays a team going nowhere fastWhy the staggering Roki Sasaki screw-up by the Blue Jays front office is a fireable offence, Jan. 18
Great Mike Wilner column regarding the Sasaki fiasco, although it could have pointed out this is yet another Jays/Guardians deal.
Do Shapiro/Atkins know how to deal with any club other than their former employer? As a former business leader myself and a devout Blue Jays fan, I'm completely mystified as to how these two keep their jobs.
I guess it's mostly about putting bums in seats, and that's all that Rogers cares about. So until Jays' fans vote with their rear ends, we're stuck with this bumbling duo and a team going nowhere.
And to think, we had Alex Anthopoulos right there and ready to spend the rest of his professional life with his beloved Blue Jays.
Sigh.
David Stones, Toronto

Document 32 of 500

Mayor Olivia Chow is spending big to make sure you notice Toronto's parks are improving. But there's one thing that won't change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mayor-olivia-chow-is-spending-big-make-sure-you/docview/3157828255/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+Olivia+Chow+is+spending+big+to+make+sure+you+notice+Toronto%27s+parks+are+improving.+But+there%27s+one+thing+that+won%27t+change&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's easy to overlook, but there's a slogan slapped on most of the standard city of Toronto park signs: "a city within a park."
It's meant to invoke the idea of a city with so many parks, ravines and public spaces that they're the dominant feature of our cityscape. While the slogan has a nice ring to it, it's never felt very accurate. Sure, Toronto has some very nice parks, but proper maintenance has been a perpetual struggle. And the amount of green space has not kept up with our growing population, especially downtown.
But let's not write off the signage sloganeering just yet. Toronto city hall might just finally have a plan to try to live up to those lofty city-within-a-park ideals. The city's budget for 2025, unveiled in draft form last week and set to be finalized by Mayor Olivia Chow next month, contains the most ambitious spending plan for parks and public spaces in memory.
At a proposed $599 million, the total parks and recreation operating budget will go up a huge 13 per cent this year, as the city hires 478 more workers to look after new and existing city facilities. On the capital side - money dedicated to building and repairing parks and recreation facilities - the 10-year spending plan is an even bigger $4.3 billion, up significantly from the $3.2-billion plan left by former mayor John Tory in his last budget in 2023.
In fact, the increased spending on parks and recreation is one of the starkest differences between Tory and Chow's budget strategies. As a point of comparison, between 2015 and 2023 - his entire eight-and-a-bit-year mayoral tenure - Tory saw the parks operating budget grow by a total of about 12 per cent - about half the rate of inflation - from $438 million in 2015 to $490 million in 2023. Chow will exceed that increase in a single year.
For the mayor, the political calculation is straightforward. With a record-high residential 9.5 per cent property tax hike last year and a significant 6.9 per cent hike proposed for this year, Chow is facing pressure to demonstrate that homeowners are getting something for their money. Next to TTC improvements, better-maintained parks and better recreation programming are probably the most visible changes city hall can make that signal to Toronto residents, "see, we're trying to get better."
To that end, this year's parks budget contains a lot of promising promises. Starting this summer, all 59 outdoor pools in the city will open for two extra hours each day in July and August. And the city will finally accept the reality that summer weather doesn't start on Canada Day and end on Labour Day - some pools will be open on weeknights and weekends for the last two weeks of June and the first three weeks of September. Beach season will also be extended, with lifeguards on duty at four locations for the first week of September. And the city is now waiving its fee for booking park space for community activities.
But it's the basic upkeep stuff in the budget that I'd bet will pay off most. The plan proposes $4 million for better cleaning at rec centres, another $1 million for general parks beautification, and $450,000 to improve the dreadful experience of waiting for a ferry at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal.
On the capital side - the side that builds new parks and repairs old ones - a long list of projects have now been funded thanks in part to $420 million in money that would have been spent on fixing the money pit we call the Gardiner Expressway before Premier Doug Ford agreed to take it off city hall's hands. (Seriously, Doug, no takebacks.)
When Tory left office, the parks department was projecting its backlog of projects in need of repair would grow every year over the next decade as conditions deteriorated. The new plan shows the backlog slowly but surely getting smaller over the same period.
Achieving all of this, of course, is easier said than done. Making a bunch of budget promises is easy. Shovelling money out the door is simple too. Ensuring that money is invested to the point where people can visit their local park or community centre and note an improvement is the challenge.
In fact, the biggest barrier might be another city hall department. The 2025 budget for the division that manages Toronto's shelters makes no bones about the fact that the city will be leaving a lot of people on the street this year. The department is projecting 250 people seeking shelter each day will be "unmatched" to a shelter bed.
The parks department is hoping a plan to spend another $1.3 million to expand the "encampment office" and send more support workers to parks will help prevent the spread of more tents, but until the underlying issues of a lack of shelter space and a lack of housing are addressed, it's hard to feel optimistic.
Still, it's also hard to see how constraining spending on our parks to less than the rate of inflation, as Toronto did under Tory, would ever achieve a better outcome. So far, Chow's budgets at least include some ambition for something better than the status quo. They've also given voters one clear metric for measuring the success of her tax-and-invest strategy. How do you know if the city is getting better? It's like the signs say: look within your park.
CAPTION: The city's parks department hopes spending another $1.3 million to expand the "encampment office" send more support workers to parks will help prevent the spread of more tents.

Subject: Public spaces; Mayors; Parks & recreation areas; Budgets

Full text: Honouring Fox long overdue
Terry Fox will be the next Canadian featured on the $5 bill, Dec. 16
It is long overdue Terry Fox will be featured on the Canadian $5 bill, where we will now see the face of this young man, our inspirational national hero.
I have just read the book "Forever Terry," published in 2020 by his brother Darrell. It is essentially a collection of inspirational letters voluntarily submitted by 50 well-known Canadians to create a unique inspirational memoire. To name a few contributors: Bobby Orr, Jann Arden, Darryl Sittler, Rick Hanse.
Terry Fox's legacy must live on; he was a person whose achievements in the face of tremendous odds stand as an example for everyone. The next generation of Canadians need to have a ready reply when those pessimists among us state "one person can't really make a difference."
The response can be found in two simple words: Terry Fox.
This is a "must read" for every Canadian.
Bruce Walker, Oakville
Building code should include accessibility
It is completely baffling that the province of Ontario refuses to require housing to be accessible, when it would save money. The savings would come from fewer ambulance calls and hospitalizations, and reduced costs for long-term care, among other things.
And such housing does not cost significantly more to build than conventional housing. As the population ages, it's long past time for the province to change the building code to require accessibility.
Carol Damioli, Toronto
Rebates a joke
as health care system crumbles
'Sad state of affairs': Family doctor's arrival in Walkerton leads hundreds to line up in snow to sign up, Jan. 15
The Star's articles on several hundred people lining up in the dark and cold in Walkerton to have the chance to register for a family doctor reflect an important issue.
This, just when the provincial government, which is running a deficit of approximately $6.6 billion, is about to send every Ontario taxpayer a cheque for $200 at a cost of $3 billion.
Of course, some Ontarians need the $200, but for others it will make very little difference. If Premier Doug Ford actually cared and was not trying to bribe us with our own money, more funds could be invested in our crumbling health care system.
I am trying to decide if my $200 is going to be donated to a food bank or to an opposition party.
David Crawford, Toronto
What's
in a name? Everything
Chrystia Freeland officially launches Liberal leadership bid to replace Trudeau as protesters disrupt event, Jan. 19
Chrystia Freeland's name says it all. Canada is a "free land" and we Canadians will not be bullied by U.S. President Donald Trump, or anyone else.
For her name alone she deserves to be the next Liberal leader and prime minister.
Hazel Regan, Toronto
The plight of our Science Centre is a disgrace
Workers say goodbye to an almost-empty Ontario Science Centre as repairs get underway, Dec. 10
I am so disheartened at what has become of our beloved Science Centre.
It was a place I visited many, many times and loved it. Last month, I took my grandchildren to Sherway Gardens to see some exhibits put on by the Science Centre. There were only two, a Lego collection and a circus-themed event. In the Lego collection, there were lovely Lego structures to look at and pieces of Lego the children could build from. This took about five minutes.
The circus theme had some costumes and a couple of other displays, and the only thing of interest there was a tightrope walk. Two of my four grandchildren participated. It was a good thing the display wasn't busy, otherwise the entire event would have been useless.
I am so very angry with Premier Doug Ford for decimating what was a world-class venture and adventure. For what? And for what cost?
It will never equal what was there at Don Mills and Eglinton.
Marilyn Herbert, Toronto
Ford's tactics insult our intelligence
Doug Ford always had a mandate to invest in Ontario, he just didn't do his job, Jan. 19
Ricardo Tranjan's column on Doug Ford's follies past, and current musings about a possible early election, is right on the money.
I'm sick and tired of hearing advertisements that tell me how much Ford is doing for Ontarians and are "paid for by the government."
No, he forgets, or hopes we will forget, that government money being used on these advertisements is our money, just as the gimmicky $200 rebate cheques being sent out soon also come from our money. As was the totally unnecessary $225 million paid to the Beer Store to get beer into corner stores a year earlier than the contract allowed.
Just think how much good could have been done with those tax dollars.
Ford's modus operandi is to try to distract attention from things like the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
These cynical manipulations are an insult to the intelligence of the people.
Being sent $200 of my own money won't bribe me to vote for him in any election, whether it be sooner or later.
Caroline Harris, Stouffville
Jays going nowhere fast
Why the staggering Roki Sasaki screw-up by the Blue Jays front office is a fireable offence, Jan. 18
Great Mike Wilner column regarding the Sasaki fiasco, although it could have pointed out this is yet another Jays/Guardians deal.
Do Shapiro/Atkins know how to deal with any club other than their former employer? As a former business leader myself and a devout Blue Jays fan, I'm completely mystified as to how these two keep their jobs.
I guess it's mostly about putting bums in seats, and that's all that Rogers cares about. So until Jays' fans vote with their rear ends, we're stuck with this bumbling duo and a team going nowhere.
And to think, we had Alex Anthopoulos right there and ready to spend the rest of his professional life with his beloved Blue Jays.
Sigh.
David Stones, Toronto

Document 34 of 500

Ford eyes election call as soon as next week: Tories say Trump's tariff threat calls for fresh mandate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-eyes-election-call-as-soon-next-week/docview/3157827841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+eyes+election+call+as+soon+as+next+week%3A+Tories+say+Trump%27s+tariff+threat+calls+for+fresh+mandate&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are eyeing a snap provincial election call as early as next Wednesday, sources told the Star.
The premier, who signalled he needs a "clear mandate" to spend the tens of billions of dollars in stimulus funds to keep the province's economy afloat after U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs hit next month, is looking at sending voters to the polls on Feb. 27 or March 6.
That would entail him visiting Lt.-Gov. Edith Dumont on either Jan. 29 or Feb. 5 to dissolve the legislature and issue the writs for an election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million to run.
Even though an Ontario vote is not scheduled until June 2026, and Ford already has a stable majority government, the premier huddled with his top aides Tuesday to determine next moves.
There's added urgency because the window for a provincial election is narrow - and not just because Trump's tariffs could cripple Ontario's economy by shuttering factories that depend on a seamless supply chain with the U.S., throwing people out of work.
The Tories hope Liberal activists will be too busy in the coming weeks helping with the March 9 federal leadership race to volunteer on the provincial campaign.
They would also prefer to face voters before a national election expected this spring that polls suggest federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is poised to win.
That's because Ford's PCs are aware that Ontario voters have historically opted for different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, which means a Poilievre victory could be bad for them.
But the Tories' election trump card is the mercurial American president.
Minutes after Trump said Monday night he would likely slap tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods within days, the premier's chief of staff, Patrick Sackville, emailed Tory MPPs and ministerial chiefs of staff and told them to be prepared.
"Moments ago, on live television, the president openly commented about his plan to bring 25 per cent tariffs against Canada as of Feb. 1," wrote Sackville in separate internal missives obtained by the Star.
"Make no mistake: this is only the beginning of what will be a long, hard fight. Ontario's workers and businesses will look to us to step up with the financial aid and leadership they need to support them in outlasting and overcoming these existential threats," he wrote.
"As we enter a period of unparalleled economic risk and critical negotiations, our government will need a strong mandate from the people to stand up for Ontario with President Trump, Canada's federal government, and other provinces to ensure we can effectively respond to, outlast and, ultimately, win this fight," continued Sackville.
"The stronger the mandate the better."
That echoed what Ford had told reporters hours earlier - before Trump dropped the Feb. 1 bombshell - that he needs the backing of voters before launching a stimulus program to offset the potential loss of 500,000 jobs.
The premier's main rivals warned an early provincial election would play right into Trump's hands, given the political uncertainty in Ottawa in the wake of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's pending resignation.
"A bully like Donald Trump, they sense weakness, and creating this insecurity in this moment is going to be a sign of weakness, not of strength," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"This is about (the premier) protecting his own job. That's why he sees this as an opportunity and not ... the threat that it really is," said Stiles, predicting "this won't just be an election about tariffs; this will be an election on Doug Ford's government and his record."
That's a reference to the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, criticism that 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor, and lengthy emergency room wait times.
Ford also faces challenges over rising unemployment, a shortage of affordable housing and increasing homelessness, with tent encampments springing up in parks and vacant lots across Ontario.
In a speech Tuesday to the Rural Ontario Municipal Association, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "the answer to chaos is not more chaos," and urged Ford to serve his full four-year term instead of launching an election.
"Calling an early election is unnecessary, expensive and creates instability and uncertainty when we need a calm and firm resolve," said Crombie.
"It's a ridiculous argument that the premier needs special permission and a new mandate to do his job - the one he was elected to do."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Leadership; Presidents; Voters; Tariffs

Document 35 of 500

The real reason Ford wants an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/real-reason-ford-wants-election/docview/3157827709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+real+reason+Ford+wants+an+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In Doug Ford's Ontario, our democratic timetable is being dictated by Donald Trump.
Canada is not yet the 51st state, and Ontarians never voted for the U.S. president. Why should Trump's musings now determine when we next vote in a provincial election?
Our premier well understands the power of Ontario's antipathy to America's bullying over tariffs. Ford wants to harness the visceral anger over Trump's provocations, the better to harvest the angry votes here at home.
Ever since Trump won the last U.S. election, the premier has been promising to save the day. Now it seems his plan all along was to save the date for the next Ontario election, that he might win the day for himself.
The premier's rhetorical juggling act is pulling him in two directions.
On the one hand, he bangs the war drums for the battle ahead. In the next breath, he decries the vacuum of power in Ottawa.
Then, with a straight face, the premier proposes to dissolve the legislature in Ontario, triggering more uncertainty at Queen's Park. How that strengthens Canada's hand, in the moment, he has yet to explain.
The premier governs with a massive majority mandate that runs until June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election date law. Yet he contrives to seek a newer, bigger mandate 16 months ahead of schedule, without a credible pitch.
Oh, Ford keeps repeating his "mandate" mantra, as if he needs a vote of confidence from the people. But for what?
To send a signal to Trump that Ontario stands united? While our rival politicians spend the next month on the campaign trail taking partisan potshots at one another?
To win approval for billions of dollars in bailouts in the face of massive job losses? When he did precisely that during the pandemic without worrying about a new mandate?
The problem with Ford's premise is that the opposition is onside. The NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie have publicly pledged their co-operation in standing united against tariffs and any economic fallout.
Ford boasts about rising above party lines when he's conferring with his first ministers about coping with the American threat. Why seek contrived political combat with his provincial opposition rivals on the campaign trail at the very same time?
Not to mention that setting tariffs is hardly a provincial prerogative. Or that fending off American attacks is properly in federal jurisdiction. And that no election result over here will be heard over there.
Justin Trudeau is now a lame duck prime minister, which has created more space for Ford to step in as a persuasive advocate on American TV making the case for Canada. Why convert Queen's Park into a caretaker government at this very moment, requiring him as premier to step back from strategic decision-making while senior civil servants hold sway during the campaign period?
Ford keeps stressing his travel plans for February, flying to Washington with his fellow premiers on two trips in hopes of getting Trump's attention. I can think of no better way to be dismissed as a poseur by the Americans than by showing up as an inauthentic and peripatetic politician on the campaign trail, each photo-op crafted as performance art for the voters back home.
The premier has won plaudits across the spectrum for rising above the partisan divide, calling for national unity and hard-line strategy to combat the threat of 25 per cent tariffs. He has articulated Canada's case and Ontario's position with passion and persuasion, at home and abroad.
Why let it unravel by being so suddenly unserious?
A truly temperate leader would resist the temptation to put his personal and political fortunes ahead of the province's fate, merely because he's riding high in public opinion polls at our lowest point. If we are really facing an unprecedented economic crisis that requires unity in our polity, an unnecessary election is the height of folly and hypocrisy.
We still don't know if the tariff talk is deadly serious or empty rhetoric. On inauguration day Monday, Trump seemed to set it aside in his prepared speech only to raise the spectre of tariffs in passing remarks later in the day.
If Trump clarifies that Canada won't be targeted, the premier will have talked himself out of an election. And if the president continues to troll us on tariffs with studied ambiguity, how will Ford keep whipping up anti-Trump fever for the duration of a sustained 29-day campaign?
Either way, voters will quickly notice there's no discernible discord among rival leaders about how to respond, turning the tariff question into a point of agreement in any debate. The campaign will inevitably shift to local issues that people refuse to forget about - from a housing emergency to hallway health care in emergency rooms, and from allegations of impropriety on the Greenbelt to frustrations over unfinished work on transit.
We need the Ontario government on the job, fighting to protect the 500,000 jobs Ford says are imperilled. What we don't need is a premier campaigning for his own job.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's calls for a "mandate" to take action against U.S. trade threats overlooks the fact that both opposition parties have pledged their co-operation to stand united against tariffs, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Justin Tang The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION
