Document 36 of 500

Disputed tax proposals falter ahead of debate: Staff reports recommend against plans for parking lot levy, stormwater charge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/disputed-tax-proposals-falter-ahead-debate/docview/3157827316/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Disputed+tax+proposals+falter+ahead+of+debate%3A+Staff+reports+recommend+against+plans+for+parking+lot+levy%2C+stormwater+charge&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two controversial city tax proposals appear to have hit the skids.
A pair of reports headed to Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee next Tuesday would pump the brakes on plans to impose a levy on commercial parking spaces and to introduce a stormwater charge - the latter of which has been lampooned by critics as a "rain tax."
The measures were put forward to advance city climate goals, and raise badly needed revenue for city services and infrastructure. But they faced headwinds from critics who said they would unfairly burden local businesses.
At an unrelated press conference Tuesday, Chow said she believed the parking tax was "an idea that is really worthy of exploration," but had "hit a roadblock" with the Municipal Property Assessment Corporation (MPAC) and provincial government.
"So it would not make sense for us to proceed unless we get some assurance that we're not just wasting time and energy," she said.
The mayor - who is in the midst of trying to shepherd a 6.9 per cent property tax increase through the 2025 city budget process - didn't express support for implementing a stormwater charge and instead emphasized other flood mitigation measures going to her committee next week.
A levy on commercial parking spaces has been contemplated at city hall for years, with proponents arguing it would discourage driving, reduce traffic and raise revenue for vital services like public transit.
In a report last February, staff estimated a commercial parking levy could generate up to $150 million a year. The report released Tuesday provides a lower estimate of between $100 million and $108 million.
Last year, staff asked council to endorse the tax in principle with the view of implementing it in 2025, but instead Chow's executive committee voted to send it back to staff for further study.
The new report said staff have concluded Toronto would require "the unique service offerings" of MPAC - the not-for-profit corporation in charge of property assessments in Ontario - to implement the tax.
In turn, MPAC, which counts provincial representatives as members of its board, has indicated its participation "may require engagement with the appropriate provincial officials."
Staff recommended deferring any consideration of the parking levy while the city continues discussions with MPAC.
In a statement, the assessment corporation said it "is pleased to explore this and other opportunities to support municipal objectives."
But the Ontario PC government signalled it won't back a parking tax.
"Our government does not support raising taxes or fees on the people of Ontario," said Colin Blachar, spokesperson for Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy. He added that the province signed the "historic" $9-billion "new deal" with Toronto to "help ensure their financial stability without the need to raise taxes."
In a separate report released Tuesday, city staff recommended "indefinitely suspend(ing)" the idea of a stormwater charge.
The levy would tax properties with large paved surfaces that generate stormwater runoff and risk overwhelming sewer systems during heavy rain. The idea is to incentivize owners to make their properties more permeable and raise funds for infrastructure improvements that would mitigate the flooding that has hit Toronto hard in recent years.
Last April, Chow paused consultations on the proposal after public opposition, including from the likes of Donald Trump Jr., who inaccurately described it as a tax on rain.
The mayor said at the time she agreed with critics that the charge shouldn't apply to residential property owners, in part because the city had no way of inspecting every homeowner's property for permeability, and planned to charge them all the same rate.
After a major storm deluged Toronto in July, council voted to explore flood mitigation strategies that could have included a potential stormwater charge on non-residential properties.
But, according to Tuesday's staff report, given that the city is now looking at a green infrastructure incentive program that would help property owners reduce impermeable surfaces and install green infrastructure so it can absorb excess rain water, a stormwater charge should be off the table.
Staff also cited the fact commercial stakeholders "raised significant concerns" about the proposed stormwater charge during consultations that also asked about the parking levy. Commercial property owners worried the stormwater measure would unfairly apply to the same paved surfaces that would be subject to the parking tax.
Sarah Buchanan, campaigns director for the Toronto Environmental Alliance, said sidelining both proposals was "a real double whammy of bad news for us and good news for corporate parking lot owners."
She described the conclusions of the two reports as contradictory. "The recommendation to shelve a stormwater charge seems to hinge on a false premise that the commercial parking levy is merrily moving ahead when that is not the case," she said.
Coun. Dianne Saxe (Ward 11, University-Rosedale), said council had pursued the commercial parking levy because it's one of the few revenue tools Toronto can implement without a provincial legislative change, unlike a city income or sales tax.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Subject: Councils; Floods; Green infrastructure; Stormwater; Proposals; Tax increases

Document 37 of 500

Will the real Mark Carney please stand up?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-real-mark-carney-please-stand-up/docview/3157827196/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+the+real+Mark+Carney+please+stand+up%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I'm the last person to criticize Mark Carney for throwing his goalie mask into the Liberal leadership ring. Canada probably benefits when broadly experienced 59-year-old Bay Streeters named Mark who once played goal volunteer for meaty public service roles.
The former investment banker was quick to ditch traditional Bay Street decorum at his campaign launch last Thursday. Speaking to a crowd in Edmonton, he took a shot at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre: "Populists don't understand how the economy in our society actually works."
Has Carney forgotten his own populist instincts? As governor of the Bank of Canada, he told the country's banks that if they didn't want to increase their capital levels, they could always cut bank employee compensation by 10 per cent instead. What's more populist than beating up on bankers' salaries?
Carney's populist credentials were solidified when he apparently rebuffed both a knighthood and a peerage from the late Queen Elizabeth II, honours that have been held by every other U.K. bank governor since the First World War. I imagine he anticipated that if he were ever to run for office, being introduced to Rotarians as "Sir Mark" might be a turnoff.
I first noticed Carney almost 20 years ago, and I took an early shine to his efforts within then-prime minister Stephen Harper's Ottawa.
Long before Carney tried to jawbone our energy sector into oblivion, I even recommended that Harper appoint our then-governor to his cabinet as minister of economic transformation. Finance minister Jim Flaherty had his hands full, and I thought Carney would make a good junior partner as Canada's economy slowly recovered from the global financial crisis.
Every great team can benefit from additional talent, and my premise was that you don't make your way from Fort Smith, N.W.T., to Goldman Sachs - the pinnacle of Wall Street - unless you're special. But Carney will face a host of hurdles in this next challenge, some of his own making.
No fans of white-shoe banks, NDP swing voters may despise that phase of Carney's career. This, after all, is a bank that Rolling Stone once called a "great vampire squid wrapped around the face of humanity, relentlessly jamming its blood funnel into anything that smells like money." It remains unclear whether Carney will drive Ottawa's blood funnel deeper into taxpayers' pocketbooks should he become prime minister.
Although he's never met a payroll himself, Carney is trying to distance himself from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau by showcasing his business credentials. A Cato Institute review of his 2021 book, "Value(s): Building a Better World for All," implies they're two peas in a pod: "when [Carney] suggests that we place too much faith in the wisdom of markets ... what he is really saying is that individuals have too much decision-making power and that greater power should be transferred to worthies to decide what is best for us."
I tried to warn Carney in a column last summer that this instinct for central planning was problematic.
In 2015, as chairman of the Global Financial Stability Board, Carney advised financial institutions to plan for "the likely future cost of doing business" with "companies that produce and use fossil fuels." Canada's Superintendent of Financial Institutions evidently got the message, citing climate change risk as an area of "increased focus" in his 2019 annual report.
It's hard not to link the regulatory push for "sound climate risk management" to the fact that our Big Six banks shed $23 billion (48 per cent) of their drawn oil and gas loan books between 2020 and 2023.
Carney might have chosen energy-rich Alberta as the venue for his first campaign speech, but given that he predicted a "tragedy on the horizon" as a regulator, voters will want to understand whether his climate change mantra undermined a sector that employs hundreds of thousands of Canadians.
By promising a welcome change from "politics as usual," Carney is channelling former prime minister Kim Campbell's 1993 leadership campaign. The Progressive Conservatives were polling dismally, just as Carney's Liberals are today.
And Campbell was no more Brian Mulroney than Carney is Trudeau.
In her election-night concession speech, as she lost her limousine, Campbell had one piece of advice for people in Carney's unenviable position: "I'm glad I didn't sell my car!"
Mark McQueen is a Toronto-based entrepreneur who has worked on Bay Street for more than 30 years. He is the former board chair of two federal agencies and was an adviser in the Brian Mulroney PMO. He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.

Document 38 of 500

Doug Ford is trying to protect himself, not Ontario, with an early election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-trying-protect-himself-not-ontario/docview/3158202115/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+trying+to+protect+himself%2C+not+Ontario%2C+with+an+early+election+call&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In Doug Ford's Ontario, our democratic timetable is being dictated by Donald Trump.
Canada is not yet the 51st state, and Ontarians never voted for the U.S. president. Why should Trump's musings now determine when we next vote in a provincial election?
Our premier well understands the power of Ontario's antipathy to America's bullying over tariffs. Ford wants to harness the visceral anger over Trump's provocations, the better to harvest the angry votes here at home.
Ever since Trump won the last U.S. election, the premier has been promising to save the day. Now it seems his plan all along was to save the date for the next Ontario election, that he might win the day for himself.
The premier's rhetorical juggling act is pulling him in two directions.
On the one hand, he bangs the war drums for the battle ahead. In the next breath, he decries the vacuum of power in Ottawa.
Then, with a straight face, the premier proposes to dissolve the legislature in Ontario, triggering more uncertainty at Queen's Park. How that strengthens Canada's hand, in the moment, he has yet to explain.
The premier governs with a massive majority mandate that runs until June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election date law. Yet he contrives to seek a newer, bigger mandate 16 months ahead of schedule, without a credible pitch.
Oh, Ford keeps repeating his "mandate" mantra, as if he needs a vote of confidence from the people. But for what?
To send a signal to Trump that Ontario stands united? While our rival politicians spend the next month on the campaign trail taking partisan potshots at one another?
To win approval for billions of dollars in bailouts in the face of massive job losses? When he did precisely that during the pandemic without worrying about a new mandate?
The problem with Ford's premise is that the opposition is onside. The NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie have publicly pledged their co-operation in standing united against tariffs and any economic fallout.
Ford boasts about rising above party lines when he's conferring with his first ministers about coping with the American threat. Why seek contrived political combat with his provincial opposition rivals on the campaign trail at the very same time?
Not to mention that setting tariffs is hardly a provincial prerogative. Or that fending off American attacks is properly in federal jurisdiction. And that no election result over here will be heard over there.
Justin Trudeau is now a lame duck prime minister, which has created more space for Ford to step in as a persuasive advocate on American TV making the case for Canada. Why convert Queen's Park into a caretaker government at this very moment, requiring him as premier to step back from strategic decision-making while senior civil servants hold sway during the campaign period?
Ford keeps stressing his travel plans for February, flying to Washington with his fellow premiers on two trips in hopes of getting Trump's attention. I can think of no better way to be dismissed as a poseur by the Americans than by showing up as an inauthentic and peripatetic politician on the campaign trail, each photo-op crafted as performance art for the voters back home.
The premier has won plaudits across the spectrum for rising above the partisan divide, calling for national unity and hardline strategy to combat the threat of 25 per cent tariffs. He has articulated Canada's case and Ontario's position with passion and persuasion, at home and abroad.
Why let it unravel by being so suddenly unserious?
A truly temperate leader would resist the temptation to put his personal and political fortunes ahead of the province's fate, merely because he's riding high in public opinion polls at our lowest point. If we are really facing an unprecedented economic crisis that requires unity in our polity, an unnecessary election is the height of folly and hypocrisy.
We still don't know if the tariff talk is deadly serious or empty rhetoric. On inauguration day Monday, Trump seemed to set it aside in his prepared speech only to raise the spectre of tariffs in passing remarks later in the day.
If Trump clarifies that Canada won't be targeted, the premier will have talked himself out of an election. And if the president continues to troll us on tariffs with studied ambiguity, how will Ford keep whipping up anti-Trump fever for the duration of a sustained 29-day campaign?
Either way, voters will quickly notice there's no discernible discord among rival leaders about how to respond, turning the tariff question into a point of agreement in any debate. The campaign will inevitably shift to local issues that people refuse to forget about - from a housing emergency to hallway health care in emergency rooms, and from allegations of impropriety on the Greenbelt to frustrations over unfinished work on transit.
We need the Ontario government on the job, fighting to protect the 500,000 jobs Ford says are imperilled. What we don't need is a premier campaigning for his own job.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, wearing a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Provincial elections; Political campaigns; Voters; Tariffs

Title: Doug Ford is trying to protect himself, not Ontario, with an early election call

Document 39 of 500

Doug Ford may call Ontario election as early as next week: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-may-call-ontario-election-as-early-next/docview/3158201969/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+may+call+Ontario+election+as+early+as+next+week%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are eyeing a snap provincial election call as early as next Wednesday, sources told the Star.
The premier, who signalled he needs a "clear mandate" to spend the tens of billions of dollars in stimulus funds to keep the province's economy afloat after President Donald Trump's tariffs hit next month, is looking at sending voters to the polls on Feb. 27 or March 6.
That would entail him visiting Lieut. Gov. Edith Dumont on either Jan. 29 or Feb. 5 to dissolve the legislature and issue the writs for an election that will cost between $150 million and $175 million to run.
Even though an Ontario vote is not scheduled until June 2026, and Ford already has a stable majority government, the premier huddled with his top aides Tuesday to determine next moves.
Minutes after Trump said Monday night he would likely slap tariffs on Canadian and Mexican goods within days, the premier's chief of staff, Patrick Sackville, emailed Tory MPPs and ministerial chiefs of staff and told them to be prepared.
"Moments ago, on live television, the president openly commented about his plan to bring 25 per cent tariffs against Canada as of Feb. 1," wrote Sackville in separate internal missives obtained by the Star.
"Make no mistake: this is only the beginning of what will be a long, hard fight. Ontario's workers and businesses will look to us to step up with the financial aid and leadership they need to support them in outlasting and overcoming these existential threats," he wrote.
"As we enter a period of unparalleled economic risk and critical negotiations, our government will need a strong mandate from the people to stand up for Ontario with President Trump, Canada's federal government, and other provinces to ensure we can effectively respond to, outlast and, ultimately, win this fight," continued Sackville.
"The stronger the mandate the better."
That echoed what Ford had told reporters hours earlier - before Trump dropped the Feb. 1 bombshell - that he needs the backing of voters before launching a stimulus program to offset the potential loss of 500,000 jobs.
The premier's main rivals warned an early provincial election would play right into Trump's hands, given the political uncertainty in Ottawa in the wake of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's pending resignation.
"A bully like Donald Trump, they sense weakness, and creating this insecurity in this moment is going to be a sign of weakness, not of strength," NDP Leader Marit Stiles told reporters Tuesday at Queen's Park.
"This is about (the premier) protecting his own job. That's why he sees this as an opportunity and not ... the threat that it really is," said Stiles, predicting "this won't just be an election about tariffs, this will be an election on Doug Ford's government and his record."
That's a reference to the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Tories' $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, criticism that 2.5 million Ontarians don't have a family doctor, and lengthy emergency room wait times.
Ford also faces challenges over rising unemployment, a shortage of affordable housing and increasing homelessness, with tent encampments springing up in parks and vacant lots across Ontario.
In a speech Tuesday to the Rural Ontario Municipal Association, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "the answer to chaos is not more chaos," and urged Ford to serve his full four-year term instead of launching an election.
"Calling an early election is unnecessary, expensive and creates instability and uncertainty when we need a calm and firm resolve," said Crombie.
"It's a ridiculous argument that the premier needs special permission and a new mandate to do his job - the one he was elected to do."
One reason Ford wants to face voters soon is his party believes Liberals are distracted by their federal leadership race, which will pick a successor for Trudeau on March 9.
Because many Liberal activists volunteer to help both the federal and provincial parties, the Tories feel the Grits might be too preoccupied by that contest to help Crombie provincially.
Finally, the provincial Tories would like to head to the polls before a national election expected this spring that federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre appears poised to win.
That's because Ford's PCs are aware that Ontario voters have historically opted for different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, which means a Poilievre victory could be bad for them.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford are shown at a new conference following a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Document 40 of 500

2025 inauguration: Donald Trump signs first round of executive orders, issues pardons for Jan. 6 rioters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/2025-inauguration-donald-trump-signs-first-round/docview/3157721854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=2025+inauguration%3A+Donald+Trump+signs+first+round+of+executive+orders%2C+issues+pardons+for+Jan.+6+rioters&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President-elect Donald Trump's inauguration for his second presidential term took place in Washington, D.C. on Monday. Trump has officially become the 47th president of the United States and Vice-President-elect JD Vance has also take the oath of office.
10:19 p.m.: Attorney for former Proud Boys chairman says his client's pardon is a 'turning point'
Nayib Hassan, an attorney for former Proud Boys national chairman Enrique Tarrio, said in a statement that his client's pardon "symbolizes a turning point for our nation."
"Throughout this journey, we have steadfastly maintained that the charges and the subsequent prosecution were politically motivated, and today, with President Trump's act of clemency, that long-fought battle concludes," Hassan said.
'Thanks America,' DC officer says
Metropolitan police officer Daniel Hodges, who was nearly crushed in a doorway while defending the Capitol during the Jan. 6, 2021, insurrection, posted a message on social media that he learned about Trump's mass pardons of rioters after working all day to ensure that Monday's inauguration was "secure and peaceful."
"Thanks America," he wrote.
Trump raises possibility of invoking wartime power act to deport gang members
Trump is raising the possibility of invoking a wartime power act for the first time since World War II to deport gang members who are deemed members of a foreign terrorist organization.
He is directing the Homeland Security and Justice departments to prepare for him to use the Alien Enemies Act of 1798, which allows the president to detain or deport any non-citizen from a country considered an enemy of the U.S.
The law has been invoked only three times: during the War of 1812, First World War and World War II. It was last was used to detain Japanese citizens following the bombing of Pearl Harbor, according to the Brennan Center for Justice.
Trump's executive order paves the way for criminal organizations such as Tren de Aragua or MS-13 to be named "foreign terrorist organizations."
"The Cartels functionally control, through a campaign of assassination, terror, rape, and brute force nearly all illegal traffic across the southern border of the United States," the order reads.
9:55 p.m.: Trump signs order halting offshore wind expansion
Trump signed an executive order halting offshore wind lease sales and pausing the issuance of approvals, permits and loans for onshore and offshore wind projects.
Trump's order says the interior secretary will review federal wind leasing and permitting practices. The assessment will consider the environmental impact of onshore and offshore wind projects, the economic costs associated with the intermittent generation of electricity and the effect of subsidies on the viability of the wind industry, the order states.
Trump wants to increase drilling for oil and gas and has been hostile to renewable energy, particularly offshore wind.
Trump directs attorney general to help states get lethal injection drugs
Trump has signed a sweeping execution order on the death penalty, directing the attorney general to "take all necessary and lawful action" to ensure that states have enough lethal injection drugs to carry out executions.
Trump wrote that "politicians and judges who oppose capital punishment have defied and subverted the laws of our country."
A moratorium on federal executions had been in place since 2021, and only three defendants remain on federal death row after Biden converted 37 of their sentences to life in prison.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Trump is not confident ceasefire in Gaza will hold
Trump said Hamas is weakened, but he's hardly certain that the temporary truce between Israel and Hamas will hold.
"I'm not confident," Trump told reporters. "That's not our war. It's their war."
He said his administration "might" help rebuild Gaza, which he compared to a "massive demolition site."
"Some beautiful things could be done with it," said Trump, the real estate developer turned commander in chief, noting the territory's coastline and "phenomenal" weather and location. "Some fantastic things could be done with Gaza. Some beautiful things could be done with Gaza."
Trump suspends U.S. foreign assistance for 90 days pending reviews
Trump has signed an executive order temporarily suspending all U.S. foreign assistance programs for 90 days pending reviews to determine whether they are aligned with his policy goals.
It was not immediately clear how much assistance would be initially affected by the order. Funding for many programs has already been appropriated by Congress and obligated to be spent, if not already spent.
Pelosi calls pardons for Jan. 6 defendants 'shameful'
House Speaker Emerita Nancy Pelosi said Trump's actions to pardon and commute sentences of those convicted in the Jan. 6, 2021, Capito; attack are "shameful."
"The President's actions are an outrageous insult to our justice system and the heroes who suffered physical scars and emotional trauma as they protected the Capitol, the Congress and the Constitution," Pelosi said in a statement. "It is shameful that the President has decided to make one of his top priorities the abandonment and betrayal of police officers who put their lives on the line to stop an attempt to subvert the peaceful transfer of power."
She said that despite Trump's decision, the country must remember the "extraordinary courage and valour of the law enforcement heroes who stood in the breach and ensured that democracy survived on that dark day."
Trump praises Bill Clinton's 'great political sense'
As he sat down to sign executive orders, Trump was asked which former president he would call for advice.
He responded that Bill Clinton was a "very interesting politician" and said he had a "great political sense."
Trump said Clinton was "disrespected" and "not used properly."
Trump was also asked about his friendly chat with former president Barack Obama during former president Jimmy Carter's state funeral.
"We were having some crazy conversations," he said.
8:55 p.m.: Trump moves to withdraw the U.S. from the World Health Organization - again
Trump has signed an executive order beginning the process of withdrawing the U.S. from the World Health Organization.
It was the second time in less than five years that he's ordered the country to withdraw from the organization, despite it being a move many scientists fear could roll back decades-long gains made in fighting infectious diseases like AIDS, malaria and tuberculosis.
Experts also warn it could weaken the world's defences against dangerous new outbreaks capable of triggering pandemics.
8:27 p.m.: Trump says the U.S. should own half of TikTok
Trump says the U.S. should own half of TikTok, which he estimated could be worth $1 trillion.
"I think the U.S. should be entitled to get half of TikTok and, congratulations, TikTok has a good partner and that would be worth, you know, could be $500 billion," Trump said.
He signed an order to keep the China-based social media platform open so that it can find a potential buyer and avoid being shut down on national security grounds.
8:24 p.m.: Trump says he favours legal immigration as he signs order declaring border emergency
Trump said he favoured legal immigration as he signed orders declaring a national emergency on the U.S.-Mexico border, suspending refugee resettlement and ending automatic citizenship for anyone born in the U.S.
Trump acknowledged an imminent legal challenge to overturning birthright citizenship, which has been enshrined in the U.S. Constitution since 1868. He said automatic citizenship was "just ridiculous" and that he believes he was on "good (legal) ground" to change it.
"That's a big one," he bantered with reporters while signing an order declaring the border emergency.
Trump said immigrant labour was needed for investment that he anticipates will accompany higher tariffs.
"I'm fine with legal immigration. I like it. We need people," he said.
8:13 p.m.: Trump says 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico could start Feb. 1
Trump says he could place 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico starting Feb. 1. He declined to give a date on China tariffs.
Trump has talked extensively about his tariff plans and his affection for the levies on imported goods during his multiple public remarks today.
8 p.m.: No West Wing office for Elon Musk
Trump says Elon Musk won't get a desk in the West Wing.
Trump made the comment while signing executive orders in the Oval Office.
Trump has named Musk, the Tesla and X CEO, as head of the Department of Government Efficiency.
7:56 p.m.: A new round of executive actions
Trump is using the first appearance of his second term in the Oval Office to sign another series of executive actions.
Here are some of the key things he's signed:
Pardons and commutations that Trump said would cover about 1,500 people criminally charged in the attack on the Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021Overhauling the refugee admission program to better align with American principles and interestsDeclaring a "national emergency" at the U.S.-Mexico borderDesignating drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations7:55 p.m.: Trump issues sweeping pardon for Jan. 6 rioters
Trump has issued pardons for people charged with participating in the Jan. 6 riot at the U.S. Capitol.
Trump said he was pardoning about 1,500 defendants and issuing six commutations.
The pardons fulfil Trump's promise to release supporters who tried to help him overturn his election defeat four years ago.
"These are the hostages," he said while signing the paperwork in the Oval Office
7:50 p.m.: Trump makes his Oval Office debut
After hours spent celebrating his new administration, Trump is making his first Oval Office appearance.
Trump has pledged to sign a series of executive actions from behind the oval-shaped room's famous Resolute Desk.
He signed an initial flurry at Capital One Arena in downtown Washington, where thousands of his supporters gathered to celebrate an inaugural parade that was moved indoors due to the cold.
7:40 p.m.: Trump rescinds 2021 Title IX order
Trump rescinded a 2021 order signalling the Education Department would use Title IX to protect against discrimination based on gender identity or sexual orientation.
The Biden administration later went further to cement that interpretation into federal regulation, but it was overturned after Republican-led states challenged the rule in federal court.
Rescinding the 2021 order won't have much effect on schools and colleges, but it clears the slate for other action by the Trump administration.
7:30 p.m.: A closer look at Trump's executive order on the federal hiring freeze
Trump has ordered a federal hiring freeze on his first day back in office, mirroring an action he took at the start of his first term to try to reduce the size of government.
The order suspends hiring for new positions and many open ones. It includes exceptions for posts related to national security and public safety, as well as the military.
During his campaign, Trump pledged to dismantle a federal bureaucracy that he derided as the "deep state."
7 p.m.: What Trump has signed orders on so far
Trump is showing off his signature Sharpie scribble as he signs an initial round of executive orders.
So far, Trump has signed orders on:
Halting 78 Biden-era executive actionsA regulatory freeze preventing bureaucrats from issuing regulations until the Trump administration has full control of the governmentA freeze on all federal hiring except for military and a few other essential areasA requirement that federal workers return to full-time in-person workA directive to every department and agency to address the cost of living crisisWithdrawal from the Paris climate treatyA government order restoring freedom of speech and preventing censorship of free speechEnding the "weaponization of government against the political adversaries of the previous administration"
6:40 p.m.: Trump is promising to pardon 'a lot of people'
Trump announced he will pardon people charged in connection with the Jan. 6 riot, calling them "hostages" while flanked by the families of people taken hostage by Hamas as militants attacked Israel on Oct. 7, 2023.
The jarring juxtaposition came moments after Steve Witkoff, Trump's Middle East envoy, introduced the relatives, some of whom are seeking the return of remains of their loves ones who were killed while held captive.
"Tonight, I'm going to be signing on the J6 hostages, pardons, to get them out," Trump said, using a shorthand for people charged with crimes for their alleged actions on Jan. 6, 2021. "And as soon as I leave I'm going to the Oval Office and we'll be signing pardons for a lot of people. A lot of people."
Trump went on to welcome home people who were released by Hamas as part of a ceasefire deal with Israel, which was finalized in the waning days of Biden's administration.
6:42 p.m.: Senate passes immigrant detention bill
The Senate on Monday passed a bill that would require federal authorities to detain migrants accused of theft and violent crimes, the first measure he likely will sign into law and giving more weight to his plans to deport millions of migrants.
Trump has made a broad crackdown on illegal immigration his top priority, and Congress, with Republicans in control and some Democrats willing to go along, is showing it is ready to follow suit.
Passage of the Laken Riley Act - named after a Georgia nursing student whose murder by a Venezuelan man last year became a rallying cry for Trump's White House campaign - was a sign of how Congress has shifted sharply right on border security and immigration.
Read the full story.
6:25 p.m. Families of Israeli hostages attend parade
Families of hostages from Israel attended the inauguration parade, including several with family members still in captivity and others whose loved ones have died.
The family members took the stage, lining up to shake hands with Trump.
Steve Witkoff, Trump's appointee for special envoy for peace in the Middle East, kicked off the speaking portion following the parade by celebrating the return of three Israeli hostages Sunday.
"For the former hostages here today, welcome home," Trump said after being introduced by Witkoff.
5:40 p.m. How Canada is reacting to the inauguration of U.S. President Donald Trump
Here's how notable Canadian politicians and groups are reacting to Donald Trump's inauguration as president of the United States Monday.
While Trump initially promised to impose damaging tariffs on Canada on his first day in office, his team is now indicating those levies won't come on Day 1.
Read the full comments from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and more.
5:36 p.m. Trump arrives at Capitol One Arena for inaugural parade
Trump has arrived at Capital One Arena for an indoor inaugural parade, taking a red, white and blue stage to raucous cheers from the crowd.
As the new president entered, he placed a binder on a podium where he would later address the crowd.
Trump then stood and smiled, pumping his fist as sustained cheers continued.
Inauguration Day traditionally features a large parade down Pennsylvania Avenue, but frigid temperatures prompted Trump to direct that the day's festivities be held indoors.
That meant moving the parade inside Capital One Arena, normally home to Washington's NBA and NHL teams.
The new venue would seem to stretch the definition of a parade and likely won't feature marching or floats.
As things were kicking off, even the DJ acknowledged the festivities weren't like a normal parade.
5:30 p.m. Protests denouncing Trump break out across the country
Thousands are protesting in Chicago as Trump takes office, chanting "The people united will never be defeated" and "Free Palestine Now" as they march downtown toward Trump Tower.
They're carrying signs declaring "Stop the Trump agenda," "Dismantle systems of oppression" and "Keep immigrant families together."
The city is bracing for Trump's promised mass deportations amid reports that his initial push would be in the Chicago area.
Similar protests have broken out across the country. Thousands marched along Sixth Avenue in Manhattan, demanding a free Palestine, calling for protections for immigrant families and honouring Martin Luther King Jr.
In Columbus, Ohio, more than 100 people gathered at a downtown church after a rally outside the statehouse moved inside due to the cold. And in Rhode Island, hundreds gathered in the capital for a rally meant to honour King and denounce Trump's policies.
5:25 p.m. Migrants have appointments to enter the U.S. cancelled immediately after Trump sworn in
A wide-ranging series of steps that President Donald Trump has promised to take to beef up security at the southern border began taking effect soon after he was inaugurated Monday, making good on his defining political promise to crack down on immigration.
Some of the moves revive policies from his first administration, including forcing asylum-seekers to wait in Mexico, cracking down on asylum access and finishing the border wall. But others will mark sweeping new strategies, like an effort to end automatic citizenship for anyone born in America, pulling the military into border security and ending use of a Biden-era app used by nearly a million migrants to enter America.
Actual execution of such a far-reaching immigration agenda, expected under several executive orders, is certain to face legal and logistical challenges. And few details have been released so far.
Read the story here.
5:15 p.m. Trump inauguration: 10 most viral moments, from Melania's kiss-proof hat to Elon Musk's controversial 'salute'
Donald Trump is the president of the United States again. It's a development that will have a significant political impact on the U.S., Canada and around the world.
But, if the inauguration events taking place in Washington, D.C. this week are any indication, Trump's presidency will also be filled with a chaotic stream of bizarre memes and viral moments.
Here are 10 moments that are lighting up the internet.
5:05 p.m. Elon Musk left to run DOGE by himself after Ramaswamy exits
The Department of Government Efficiency's first order of business was itself: It is now down to one member.
Vivek Ramaswamy will no longer serve in the non-governmental agency alongside Elon Musk, a spokesperson for the agency confirmed Monday. Ramaswamy has signalled plans to run for governor of Ohio.
"Vivek Ramaswamy played a critical role in helping us create DOGE," spokesperson Anna Kelly said in a statement. "He intends to run for elected office soon, which requires him to remain outside of DOGE, based on the structure that we announced today. We thank him immensely for his contributions over the last 2 months and expect him to play a vital role in making America great again."
Read the full story here.
4:52 p.m. Inauguration fashion choices suggest new attitude toward Republican power players
First daughter Ivanka Trump chose head-to-toe Dior: a dark green skirt suit and fascinator that was reminiscent of the French house's 1948 postwar New Look collection. (Some noted that the colour and silhouette looked remarkably similar to The Handmaid's Tale costumes.) It was a politic choice: Dior's CEO Delphine Arnault attended the inauguration, alongside her father and LVMH CEO Bernard Arnault, his wife Helene and son Alexandre.
Usha Vance, wife of J.D. Vance and the new Second Lady, wore a sculptural pink dress with built-in scarf by Oscar de la Renta, with Manolo Blahnik boots. The night before, Vance wore a black velvet Oscar de la Renta gown; the brand's co-creative director Laura Kim posted the image to her Instagram Stories with heart-eyed emojis. Over the weekend, Ivanka Trump also wore a beaded gown and an embellished camel coat from the house. The American brand, which was founded by the late Dominican designer of the same name in 1965, has dressed many first and second ladies, but the past few days signal a new embrace of the current administration's key figures.
Meanwhile, both President Biden and Dr. Jill Biden wore Ralph Lauren. Jill wore a purple coat, which struck the right note of "optimistic, looking forward, colourful and positive," said image consultant Tracy Richardson, who declared her "by far the best dressed person in the room." The outgoing president, known for understated tailoring and grooming, wore a blue tie that complemented his wife's coat. "Elegance, grace, appropriateness. It is all in the details," said Richardson. "If young people are watching, I'm hoping they see how important a dress code is." - Leanne Delap/Special to the Star
4:40 p.m. Environment minister says Donald Trump pulling out of Paris pact is "deplorable"
Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault says it's "deplorable" that President Donald Trump is again pulling the U.S. out of the 2015 Paris Accord.
He says it's "quite ironic" that President Trump is abandoning the global environmental pact while California is experiencing one of its worst forest fire seasons ever.
Guilbeault also points out it's not the first time Trump has taken the U.S. out of the accord - he did the same thing during his first term in the Oval Office.
The environment minister says the nearly 200 nations still in the pact will forge ahead in the fight against climate change without the U.S. on board.
Read the full story.
3:28 p.m. Elon Musk draws fire for salute
Elon Musk delivered a "odd-looking salute" on live television during his speech at Trump's inaugural parade, pounding his chest and shooting out his right arm in a familiar manner. He then turned around and repeated the gesture: "My heart goes out to you," he said.
"You saw him come out with that odd-looking salute," CNN anchor Erin Burnett noted during the broadcast.
The ensuing fervour on social media was more blunt: "Yes, that is very clearly a Nazi salute," one X user wrote.
But not everyone saw it that way. Frequent Musk critic Batya Ungar-Sargon said his motion looked more like "a man with Aspergers exuberantly throwing his heart to the crowd. We don't need to invent outrage."
Read the full story here
3:05 p.m. Trump may pardon Jan. 6 convicts: reports
Trump is reportedly preparing to issue sweeping pardons for the defendants charged in the Jan. 6 attack on the U.S. Capitol, according to ABC News.
This would include commuting the sentences of hundreds of his followers convicted of assaulting law enforcement on that day, and full pardons to those not charged with engaging with violence, ABC reports, citing unnamed sources.
It's possible the plans could change or be scaled back before they're formally signed.
2:51 p.m. DOGE hit by lawsuits
The nascent Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), to be headed by Trump ally Elon Musk, has reportedly been hit by a flurry of lawsuits mere minutes after Trump's inauguration.
Several of the suits accused the Trump administration of flouting transparency rules that required DOGE to disclose its hiring practices and interactions, and seek to bar the government from taking on any of the advisory group's recommendations until it complies with the law.
The suits come from public interest law firm National Security Counselors; Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics, representing a coalition of teachers' unions and veterans' organizations; and a trio of organizations including non-profit Public Citizen, the American Federation of Government Employees and the State Democracy Defenders Fund.
2:43 p.m. Wayne Gretzky, Janet Gretzky among celebrities at Trump inauguration
Wayne and Janet Gretzky were among the many celebrities and high profile public figures who attended Trump's inauguration on Monday Morning.
"We are so very proud," Janet wrote under a photo alongside Wayne shared on Instagram. "We love America, We love Canada. God Bless everyone!"
The Great One's friendly relationship with Trump has been under scrutiny in recent weeks. In December, the president-elect told his followers on Truth Social that he had encouraged Gretzky to run for office: "I said, 'Wayne, why don't you run for prime minister of Canada, soon to be known as the governor of Canada - you would win easily, you wouldn't even have to campaign.' He had no interest," Trump wrote.
2:38 p.m. Danielle Smith urges Canadian officials to allay Trump concerns
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith issued a statement saying she was "pleased" but that "despite the promising news today, the threat of U.S. tariffs is still very real."
In a post on X, Smith urged Canadian federal and provincial leaders to stop talking about "retaliatory measures including export tariffs or cutting off energy to the U.S." and instead allay Trump's concerns through tighter border security, more defence spending, tougher immigration policies, and the repeal of "anti-energy" policies.
2:29 p.m. A wave of Trump-demanded departures hits senior leadership at the State Department
A large number of senior career diplomats who served in politically appointed top leadership positions at the State Department have left their posts at the demand of the incoming Trump administration, which plans to install its own people in those positions, according to current and outgoing U.S. officials.
Personnel changes in the senior ranks of the department, like those at all federal agencies, are not uncommon after a presidential election, and career officials serving in those roles are required, just as noncareer political appointees, to submit letters of resignation before an incoming administration takes office.
Read the full story.
2:22 p.m. Biden reacts to Trump's speech
Biden reacted to Trump's inaugural address in his first speech as a former president at Joint Base Andrews just before leaving for California.
"We heard the inaugural address today - you know, we got a lot more to do," he said, before crossing himself to laughter from the crowd and his former staffers.
"No president gets to choose the moment they enter history. But they get to choose the team they enter history with. And we chose the best damn team in the world," he said. "We're leaving office. But we're not leaving the fight."
Biden exited to chants of "thank you, Joe."
2:06 p.m. "Nelk Boys" co-founder attends with Conor McGregor and Jake and Logan Paul
Internet personality Kyle Forgeard - the Mississauga-born co-founder of the absurdly popular YouTube channel "Nelk Boys" - shared a video on Instagram Monday en route to Trump's inauguration alongside influencers Jake and Logan Paul and Irish MMA fighter Conor McGregor.
The Nelk Boys started out as a prank show, but has in recent years become a platform known for promoting right-wing political causes. In 2022, Forbes named Forgeard as one of the top social media influencers under 30.
McGregor was recently sued for alleged sexual battery over an incident that took place at the 2023 NBA Finals. In November of last year, a civil court jury in Ireland awarded 250,000 euros to a woman who said she was "brutally raped and battered" by the MMA fighter.
2 p.m. (Updated) Trump rebuffs Biden's pardons
Trump rebuffed Biden's pardons of his family members and others including Anthony Fauci, Liz Cheney and the committee investigating the Jan. 6 insurrection in a rambling speech at Emancipation Hall, claiming that some of the people were "very, very guilty of very bad crimes."
These included the Jan. 6 Unselect Committee, who he claimed "deleted all the information on Nancy Pelosi having turned down the offer of 10,000 soldiers" at the event. It's a repeated claim of Trump's that the committee found no evidence of ever happening.
Trump also claimed Biden pardoned "33 murderers" and denounced Cheney as a "crying lunatic."
Biden commuted the sentences of 37 people facing the death sentence. They must still serve life in prison without parole - they were not "pardoned" as Trump implied.
Earlier Monday, Biden also commuted the life sentence of Native American activist Leonard Peltier, who was convicted of murdering two FBI agents. Peltier will serve out the rest of his sentence in home confinement.
Read the full transcript of Trump's speech.
1:54 p.m. Sen. John Fetterman keeps it casual
Trump's official swearing-in ceremony was moved inside the Capitol Rotunda due to forecasts of "intense cold weather," with temperatures dipping below zero around noon. But the chilly air did not seem to bother Democratic Sen. John Fetterman, who arrived at the Capitol wearing a baggy pair of shorts and a black Carhartt hoodie. Though Fetterman is known for his casual attire, he was spotted wearing a suit for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's address to Congress last summer.
1:45 p.m. Donald Trump becomes U.S. president for a second time
Donald Trump, the populist disrupter who has vowed political revenge, the mass deportation of "animals" from other countries, and the use of tariffs to win influence and prosperity from abroad - including, perhaps, from Canada - is now the 47th president of the United States.
At a ceremony inside the rotunda of the domed U.S. Capitol Building, where his supporters rioted over the lost presidential election four years ago, Trump officially returned to power at noon on Monday. The moment capped a political restoration that saw Trump survive a criminal conviction, get past investigations into his alleged role in seeking to overturn the 2020 presidential election, and clinch expanded support from American voters in winning November's presidential vote.
"The golden age of America begins right now," said Trump, 78, in his second inaugural address.
Read the full story from the Star's Alex Ballingall
1:31 p.m. Trump is giving a speech to an inauguration crowd after being introduced by JD Vance.
"You're a younger, far more beautiful audience than the one I just spoke to," he joked. "I want to keep it off the record."
Trump also addressed Biden's pre-emptive pardons of his family members and others hours before the inauguration, noting they were "very, very guilty of very bad crimes."
1:30 p.m. A group of men clad in the garb of the Proud Boys marched through the streets of Washington shortly after Trump was sworn in. A handful of counter protesters yelled through megaphones that they were "losers."
1:25 p.m. Trump administration shuts down CBP One
The new Trump administration has already shut down CBP One, a government mobile app that previously helped migrants submit information and schedule appointments at ports of entry.
"Effective January 20, 2025, the functionalities of CBP One that previously allowed undocumented aliens to submit advance information and schedule appointments at eight southwest border ports of entry is no longer available, and existing appointments have been cancelled," an alert on the app's website reads.
Roughly 270,000 Mexican migrants were waiting to get an appointment through the app before it shuttered, CBS reports. As of Jan. 16, nearly 919,000 migrants were allowed into the States through the app.
1:16 p.m. Trudeau congratulates Trump
In a social media post, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau congratulated Trump on his inauguration. "We have the chance to work together again - to create more jobs and prosperity for both our nations," Trudeau said.
In his speech, Trump did not mention Canada but reiterated his threat of tariffs on foreign nations. Trump had previously threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
FBI acting chief Paul Abbate says he's retiring
Paul Abbate, who became the FBI's acting director following the departure of Christopher Wray, is retiring from the bureau, according to an email he sent to senior executives that was obtained by The Associated Press on Monday.
Abbate had been expected to run the FBI while Senate confirmation proceedings get underway for Kash Patel, who is President Donald Trump's pick for director. But with Abbate's retirement effective Monday, when Trump took office, it was not immediately clear who would fill that role.
1:02 p.m. Government site gets makeover
The whitehouse.gov website got a makeover, welcoming visitors with a video glorifying Trump to soaring orchestral music before giving way to a landing page boasting that "America is back" in large lettering and an image of Trump.
"Every single day I will be fighting for you with every breath in my body," an accompanying caption reads. "I will not rest until we have delivered the strong, safe and prosperous America that our children deserve and that you deserve. This will truly be the golden age of America."
12:43 p.m. Carrie Underwood's performance beset by technical difficulties
Carrie Underwood's highly-anticipated performance at Trump's inauguration did not exactly go as planned.
After being introduced, the Grammy-winning country singer stood in awkward silence caused by technical difficulties as she waited for the musical track to begin. After about a minute, Underwood could be seen mouthing the words "I can just sing it," before requesting that the audience "help me out here." She then proceeded to perform the song a cappella without further issue.
An eight-time Grammy winner, Underwood, 41, is the highest profile artist slated to perform at Trump's inauguration. Her decision to perform at the inauguration was met with significant negative reactions from her fans, particularly her LGBTQ+ fans. Underwood has not publicly responded to those criticisms.
12:54 p.m. What did Melania Trump wear?
Incoming First Lady Melania Trump, who was mostly shunned by America's fashion establishment during her husband's last term in office, arrived at the church service ahead of her husband's second inauguration in a slim-fit navy silk-and-wool coat and skirt set by American designer Adam Lippes.
Notably, she accessorized it with a flat-brimmed, low-slung hat with white trim by Eric Javits, an American milliner since the 1980s with celebrity clients including Dua Lipa and Lady Gaga. Sitting low on her face Spy vs. Spy-style, the hat is destined to become an instant meme, while preventing the cameras from catching any accidental facial expressions - Melania has been captured seemingly giving side-eye at events in the past.
The choice of Adam Lippes for her outfit seems calculated to show support for a smaller, independent fashion designer. Lippes, who worked for Oscar de la Renta before starting his own label a decade ago, specializes in American sportswear; his dresses run in the $3,000 to $4,000 range. For Trump's first inauguration, Melanie wore a light blue dress by heritage brand Ralph Lauren (also a favourite designer of the Biden family) that drew comparisons to Jackie Kennedy's style.
"Melania's look is restrained, classic, uniform like. It looks like a dramatic governess outfit," said Tracy Richardson, a Canadian image consultant who has dressed clients for the White House, Buckingham Palace, the Oscars, TIFF and the Junos. "The hat is dramatic. But she looks neat. We aren't distracted by what she is wearing." Leanne Delap/Special to the Star
12:53 p.m. Trump stops short of immediately imposing Canadian tariffs
U.S. President Donald Trump has stopped short of immediately imposing his threatened 25-percent tariff on Canadian and Mexican goods.
But in his inaugural address minutes after taking office, Trump left no doubt he has foreign manufacturers and America's allies and competitors in his crosshairs.
He vowed he would set up a new agency to collect tariff revenues, and that he will fill American coffers with tariff and tax revenue from foreign countries, saying he was "saved by God to make America great again."
Trump declared a series of national emergencies to deploy soldiers to the Mexican border, ramp up oil drilling, end electric vehicle mandates, and began what he called "an overhaul" of U.S. trade relations.
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles

"Nothing will stand in our way because we are Americans, the future is ours, and our golden age has just begun," Trump finished to raucous cheers and a standing ovation.
12:42 p.m. "We stand on the verge of the four greatest years in American history," Trump says. "I am with you, I will fight for you, and I will win for you. We're going to win like never before."
12:40 p.m. Many people thought it was impossible for me to stage such a historic political comeback. But as you see today, here I am. The American people have spoken," Trump said to a standing ovation.
12:38 p.m. (updated) Trump is now running through a litany of promises for his administration, including taking back the Panama Canal, renaming the Gulf of Mexico, imposing tariffs on foreign countries and ending government DEI policies. He also lashed out to efforts to limit climate change, vowing to "drill, baby, drill."
With Elon Musk, CEO of SpaceX, sitting only feet away, Trump promises that America will "pursue our manifest destiny into the stars" and send American astronauts to Mars.
"Many people thought it was impossible for me to stage such a historic political comeback. But as you see today, here I am. The American people have spoken," Trump said to a standing ovation.
12:27 p.m. (updated) Trump says he is overhauling the trade system to protect Americans. "We will tariff and tax foreign countries to enrich out citizens," he says, vowing to establish an "external revenue service" to collect this money. Trump has previously threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
Trump promised to immediately sign an executive order to "immediately stop all government censorship and bring back free speech to America."
Trump says it is the official policy of the U.S. government that there are only two genders.
Trump promises to reinstate any military members ejected for not being vaccinated, with full backpay.
He also reiterated his promise to end government DEI policies.
"A short time from now, we are going to be changing the name of the Gulf of Mexico to the Gulf of America," Trunp said.
12:26 p.m. (updated) Trump promises 'golden age of America'
In his inaugural address, Trump promised the "golden age of America begins right now," reiterating his vow to "put America first."
"From this day forward, our country will flourish and be respected again all over the world," he said. "We will be the envy of every nation, and we will not allow ourselves to be taken advantage of any longer."
"I was saved by God to make America great again," Trump says.
Trump says today is "liberation day" for Americans, and hopes his election will be viewed as among the most influential in American history.
Trump says he will declare a national emergency at the Mexican-U. S. border, a line that draws a standing ovation from the rotunda - with Biden, Harris, the Clintons and the Bushs notably sitting.
Trump vowed to end the Green New Deal and revoke the "electric vehicle mandate."
"In other words, you'll be able to buy the car of your choice. We will build automobiles in America again at a rate that nobody could have dreamt possible just a few years ago."
How much is Trump's crypto coin worth?
On Monday, Donald Trump's newly created cryptocurrency, a "meme coin" known as $TRUMP, soared to more than $8 billion in market value, as of writing. Launched on Sunday, the collectible token, which has no inherent value, currently ranks third among other "meme coins" behind digital assets Dogecoin and Shiba Inu.
First Lady Melania Trump also launched a crypto coin, called $MELANIA, on Sunday, which is currently trading just under $1 billion, behind other popular coins like "Pepe" and "Fartcoin."
Some crypto enthusiasts hailed the Trump meme coin's release, saying it's symbolic of the incoming president's support for an industry that felt unfairly targeted by the Biden administration. Trump has promised to usher in crypto-friendly regulations and picked crypto cheerleaders for key government positions.
Critics said the Trump meme coin could be a dangerous way for special interests and foreign governments to try and buy influence with the president.
How tall are Barron Trump and Donald Trump?
Since Trump was first elected, much ink has been spilled about the president's "large adult sons," Donald Jr. and Eric. But Trump's youngest child, Barron has largely evaded the spotlight.
That may be about to change, given that Barron is now 18 and looks like he could an up-and-coming shooting guard for Washington Wizards. Indeed, standing behind the president's podium at Monday's inauguration, the baby-faced Trump towered over the other attendees.
But exactly how tall is Barron? Google has him listed at 2.06 metres, which is almost 6 foot 8. However, he was listed at 6 foot 7 during his graduation from Oxbridge Academy in West Palm Beach last May. That's at least four inches taller than his father, who is listed at 6 foot 3, or 1.9 metres.
12:08 p.m. Both Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre have been quiet so far on social media regarding Trump's inauguration.
Mark Carney and Christia Freeland, front-runners in the Liberal Party's leadership race, have also not posted.
12:03 p.m. Trump and JD Vance sworn in
Trump was sworn in right after as his family watched, finishing to rapturous applause.
"I, Donald John Trump, do solemnly swear that I will faithfully execute the Office of President of the United States, and will to the best of my ability, preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the United States. So help me God," Trump said in his oath as he was sworn in by Chief Justice John Roberts.

JD Vance raised his right hand and recited his vow at 12:01 p.m. as he was sworn in by Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh as his wife Usha and three children watched on.
