11:50 a.m. Trump enters to cheers
Trump entered the rotunda to rousing cheers, shaking hands with Joe Biden and Apple CEO Tim Cook. He also gave an air kiss to wife Melania. Chants of "USA" broke out before Trump sat next to vice president-elect JD Vance.
11:47 a.m. Biden pardon family members
In the final minutes of his presidency, Joe Biden has pardoned the Biden family, his brothers James and Francis, sister-in-law Sara Jones, sister Valerie and brother-in-law John.
"My family has been subjected to unrelenting attacks and threats, motivated solely by a desire to hurt me - the worst kind of partisan politics," Biden said in a statement. "Unfortunately, I have no reason to believe these attacks will end."
Biden also commuted the life sentence of Leonard Peltier. The 80-year-old was convicted for the 1975 killings of two FBI agents during a standoff on a reservation in South Dakota.
"This commutation will enable Mr. Peltier to spend his remaining days in home confinement but will not pardon him for his underlying crimes," a White House statement said.
11:40 a.m. The crowd erupts in cheers for JD Vance.
11:35 a.m. (updated) Guests arriving for the inauguration
Guests have arrived at the U.S. Capitol for the inauguration, which has been moved indoors due freezing temperatures brought by the Polar Vortex.
Former president Barack Obama has arrived without former first lady Michelle Obama, who announced last week she would forego Monday's event.
Met with significant boos by the crowd, former Vice President Mike Pence, Former President Bill Clinton, and former first lady Hillary Clinton are also amongst the guests in attendance.
Tech titan and major Trump donor Elon Musk, who will be heading the new Department of Government Efficiency, has also arrived, alongside Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Apple CEO Tim Cook. Podcast host Joe Rogan has also been seen in attendance.
Argentine President Javier Milei and Italian Premier Giorgia Meloni have also been seen in the Rotunda, the first foreign leaders to attend the inauguration since 1874.
Trump's cabinet picks, including future secretary of state Marco Rubio and Pete Hegseth, the controversial pick for defence secretary, have also been seen at the capitol.
11:32 a.m. Trump will be the oldest president ever inaugurated
At 78 years and 220 days old, Trump is five months older than Joe Biden when he was inaugurated in 2021.
Meanwhile, at 40 years old, JD Vance will be the third-youngest vice president in U.S. history.
11:30 a.m. Canada's Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne was inside the Capitol building, attending the inauguration, watching the swearing-in with a number of Republican governors from Emancipation Hall, an overflow room, where the ceremony was shown on a big screen.
How did Trump spend his morning?
Trump kicked off his morning Monday with a pre-inauguration visit to St. John's Church. Among those in attendance were several billionaires and tech tycoons, including Amazon founder Jeff Bezos (net worth $239.4 billion (U.S.), Meta founder Mark Zuckerberg (net worth $211.8 billion (U.S.), Apple CEO Tim Cook (net worth $2.2 billion (U.S.) and Google CEO Sundar Pichai (net worth $2.1 billion (U.S.).
Among the other notable figures spotted at the church were media tycoon Rupert Murdoch (net worth $22.2 billion (U.S.), plus FIFA president Gianni Infantino. Former U.K. Conservative Prime Minister Boris Johnson and Javier Milei, the ultraconservative president of Argentina were also in attendance.
11:20 a.m. Who will swear in Trump?
Chief Justice John Roberts will swear in Trump as president. Brett Kavanaugh will swear in JD Vance as vice president.
11:18 a.m. George Bush has entered the rotunda, as has Barack Obama.
11:12 a.m. Trump does the 'Trump dance' during Village People performance
The Village People, a campy disco group, known for their goofy lyrics and problematic costumes, performed their hit song "Y.M.C.A." on stage with Trump on Sunday - assless chaps, Native American headdresses and all. The song has become a staple at Trump rallies - you've likely seen the President-elect's awkward, jerky dance moves, which first went viral during his 2020 election campaign. Since then, the "Trump" dance has been embraced by Trump supporters, frat boys and several pro athletes, including San Francisco 49ers star Nick Bosa and U.S. men's soccer team captain Christian Pulisic.
Over the decades, the 1978 hit has been embraced by the gay community. However, according to Victor Willis, the band's litigious lead singer (and only remaining original member) has insisted the song is not a "gay anthem." In fact, he recently stated that he will take legal action against anyone who suggests it is.
11:10 a.m. Trump's children have entered the Rotunda.
11:08 a.m. Putin congratulates Trump
Russian President Vladimir Putin congratulated Trump on his win earlier Monday morning during a televised meeting of Russia's Security Council.
"We see the statements by the newly elected president of the United States and members of his team about the desire to restore direct contacts with Russia," Putin said. "We also hear his statement about the need to do everything possible to prevent World War Three.
"We of course welcome this attitude and congratulate the elected president of the United States of America on taking office."
Canada bracing for Donald Trump's decision amid reports 25 per cent tariffs not immediately on the table
The Canadian government has not received any official confirmation that incoming U.S. President Donald Trump will not impose tariffs on Canadian products, as threatened.
Two senior Canadian officials said Monday the government was still waiting for official word that would confirm reports by the Wall Street Journal and New York Times that Trump is instead going to order a trade deficit review which would include a study of the North American free-trade pact.
One official said it is encouraging if true that Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian goods is not immediately on the table.
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles
10:55 a.m. Guests arriving at the U.S. Capitol for the inauguration
Guests have arrived at the U.S. Capitol for the inauguration, which has been moved indoors due freezing temperatures brought by the polar vortex.
Former president Bill Clinton and former first lady Hillary Clinton and Mike Pence have been seen among the guests. Tech titan and major Trump donor Elon Musk, who will be heading the new Department of Government Efficiency, has also arrived, alongside Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Apple CEO Tim Cook. Podcast host Joe Rogan has also been seen in attendance.
Trump's cabinet picks, including future secretary of state Marco Rubio and Pete Hegseth, the controversial pick for defence secretary, have also been seen at the capitol.
10:45 a.m. Who is performing at the inauguration? Carrie Underwood, Nelly, Jason Aldean and the Village People
Trump has managed to secure a hodgepodge of significantly more popular - if not exactly zeitgeist-defining - artists, who will perform at the inauguration today and the three balls (the Liberty Ball, the Starlight Ball and the Commander-in-Chief Ball), taking place on Monday.
Read the full story from the Star's Richie Assaly
10:40 a.m. Biden and Trump just entered the same limo in the presidential motorcade, upholding a long-running tradition for the outgoing and incoming U.S. presidents to ride together to the swearing-in ceremony.
The two did not ride together to Biden's swearing-in ceremony four years prior, when Trump opted to skip Biden's inauguration.
The incoming and outgoing vice presidents and first ladies will ride separately to the ceremony.
10:40 a.m. Harris and Biden make final posts on X
"It has been the honour of our lifetimes to serve you, the American people," the vice president and president wrote in identical posts Monday morning on the social platform X.
The posts both featured a portrait of Biden, first lady Jill Biden, Harris and second gentleman Doug Emhoff.
10:33 a.m. Mike Pence attending inauguration
Former Vice President Mike Pence announced on X that he will attend Trump's inauguration on Monday, four years after speaking out against a government insurrection that rendered him a persona non grata in the MAGA movement.
"This is a day when every American does well to celebrate our democracy and the peaceful transfer of power under the Constitution of the United States," he wrote. "We encourage all our fellow Americans to join us praying for President Trump and Vice President Vance as they assume the awesome responsibility of leading this great Nation."
Doug Ford warns Trump tariffs would be an 'absolute disaster'
Premier Doug Ford is warning Ontarians to brace for the impact of Donald Trump's tariffs - and signalling Canada will hit back hard, including by removing American booze from LCBO stores.
In a campaign-style speech Monday to delegates at the Rural Ontario Municipal Association conference, Ford emphasized that tumultuous times are looming.
"We can't ignore the uncertain times we are facing right now," the premier said at the Sheraton Centre conference.
Read the full story from the Star's Robert Benzie
10:07 a.m. Melania Trump cryptocurrency ahead of inauguration
Incoming first lady Melania Trump revealed her own official meme coin cryptocurrency the night before her husband's inauguration.
She announced her token Sunday evening in a post to X: "The official Melania Meme is live!", she wrote, adding that "You can buy $Melania now."
Her coin is currently trading at $9.08 as of time of writing, up over 24 per cent in the last day, according to CoinMarketCap. It has a market cap of $1.76 billion.
A disclaimer on the cryptocurrency's website cautions the tokens are "not intended to be, or to be the subject of, an investment opportunity, investment contract, or security of any type."
Instead, they're being marketed as "digital collectibles intended to function as an expression of support for and engagement with the values embodied by the symbol MELANIA. and the associated artwork."
Melania's announcement came days after the president-elect unveiled his own meme coin $Trump on Friday; as of time of writing, his coin is down more than 13 per cent at $53.91, according to CoinMarketCap.
"Official Trump" is currently the most-traded meme coin on the Solana platform, according to CoinGecko. "Melania Meme" is in sixth place, as of the time of writing.
Who is J.D. Vance? How the former anti-Donald Trump author became the right hand of the incoming president
From a chaotic upbringing in poverty to bestselling author, U.S. senator and now vice president-elect of the United States, J.D. Vance has walked a winding, often contradictory road to his seat at the right hand of Donald Trump.
His latest role is a major turn to say the least for a man who once publicly called Trump "loathsome" and an "idiot," and privately referred to him as "America's Hitler."
Today, Vance has fallen in line with MAGA rhetoric, espousing narratives he once denounced. In so doing, he will become one of the most powerful men in the country come Monday.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang
Monday 7:30 a.m. Biden pardons Fauci and Milley in an effort to guard against potential 'revenge' by Trump
President Joe Biden has pardoned Dr. Anthony Fauci, retired Gen. Mark Milley and members of the House committee that investigated the Jan. 6 attack on the Capitol, using the extraordinary powers of his office in his final hours to guard against potential "revenge" by the incoming Trump administration.
The decision by Biden comes after Donald Trump warned of an enemies list filled with those who have crossed him politically or sought to hold him accountable for his attempt to overturn his 2020 election loss and his role in the storming of the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 6, 2021. Trump has selected Cabinet nominees who backed his election lies and who have pledged to punish those involved in efforts to investigate him.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Small businesses in Canada losing jobs as Trump's tariff threat looms
A new report has found that Canadian small business job growth has been "lagging" since last summer, particularly within manufacturing and professional services, signalling possible trouble ahead as president-elect Donald Trump's threat of U.S. tariffs looms.
The report by Intuit QuickBooks found that Canadian small businesses have lost more than 84,000 jobs since July, despite adding a cumulative 126,600 jobs between October 2023 and October 2024.
The year-over-year increase in small business jobs is in stark contrast to the second half losses in 2024 and reflects a tale of two trends, said Simon Worsfold, head of data communications at Intuit QuickBooks.
Read the full story from the Star's Hayden Godfrey
Sunday 7 p.m. A raucous victory rally on Sunday highlighted Trump 's first full day back in Washington since the election
Republicans are celebrating his return to the White House and projecting defiant optimism despite deep national political divisions on the eve of his inauguration.
Prominent Trump supporters including musicians Lee Greenwood and Kid Rock graced the stage at Capital One Arena along with tech-billionaire Elon Musk and actor Jon Voight.
Read the latest from the Associated Press
At Donald Trump's inauguration rally, here's what his supporters think about annexing Canada
A new sphere of American hegemony, with Canada as the 51st state. That sounds fantastic to the man in the purple-hemmed toga and the gold laurel wreath that's perched around his "Make America Great Again" ballcap.
He's going for irony, donning the garb of a Caesar for laughs when so many have warned that Donald Trump could imperil democracy in the American republic. Like a centurion, he holds a long staff with a banner that portrays a silhouette of Trump after a would-be assassin's bullet grazed his ear last summer. A flag with the stars and stripes streams down Trump's face, instead of blood.
"I think it would be fantastic ... for global, political and economic power," says the man, 27, whose name is Logan Howard.
Read the full story from the Star's Alex Ballingall
The second Donald Trump era is set to begin. Here's why the stakes for Canada are enormous
You can feel it in the city of presidents, this metropolis of neo-Roman edifices that once claimed to lead to the "free world," and is now soon to experience its "Make America Great Again" restoration. The anticipation, the trepidation, the excitement, the dread. A weekend of galas and rallies; celebrants in their signature red hats meandering past the fences and porta potties on the National Mall; fireworks scheduled for Saturday night at Donald Trump's golf course outside of town. And all of it hurtling towards the single, tremulous question.
What is about to happen?
On Monday at noon, the prelude to Trump's second presidency will end at the U.S. Capitol Building his supporters attacked four years ago. He will assume power again at age 78, a convicted criminal whose brash and unpredictable populism is already reviving - and, in some cases, expanding - the anxieties of his first term in office.
Read the full story from the Star's Alex Ballingall
Donald Trump still weighs heavily on Liberal leadership race
He is not a Canadian and he is certainly not a Liberal, but U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has had a huge influence on the race to replace Justin Trudeau.
Before the end of the weekend, the Liberal leadership field should be set. Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney announced Thursday that he would seek the job of party leader and prime minister. Government House Leader Karina Gould is expected to announce her bid this weekend, and former finance minster Chrystia Freeland announced her candidacy on social media Friday morning, saying she was "running to fight for Canada."
Freeland, who will formally launch her campaign in Toronto on Sunday, presented herself in a column published in the Star on Friday as the best candidate to fight Trump's threatened trade war with Canada.
Read the full story from the Star's Ryan Tumilty
Elon Musk has Donald Trump's ear and a new-found interest in Canada. How much should we worry?
Donald Trump's return to the White House on Monday has already proven to be a thorn in the side of Canadian officials, but his second presidency brings additional baggage for Canada: Elon Musk.
The close adviser to Trump, owner of the social media platform X and richest man on Earth has a new-found interest in Canada, a ratio of five X followers for every Canadian and the stamina to post dozens of times a day. And he's not afraid to dish out his political opinions online.
Musk weighed in recently when outgoing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau pushed back against Trump's public musings about annexing Canada. Trudeau wrote on X that there "isn't a snowball's chance in hell that Canada would become part of the United States."
Read the full story from the Star's Mark Ramzy and Alex Boyd
Elon Musk, The Village People, Joe Rogan: The celebrities behind Trump as his inauguration approaches
U.S. president-elect Donald Trump is to be sworn into office at the White House once again. Here's a look at some of the most notable names to come out in support of him ahead of the historic day.
Read the full story from the Star's Serena Austin
Trump swearing-in will move inside Capitol Rotunda because of intense cold
President-elect Donald Trump may take the oath of office from inside the Capitol Rotunda on Monday due to forecasts of intense cold weather.
"The weather forecast for Washington, D.C., with the wind chill factor, could take temperatures into severe record lows," Trump posted on his Truth Social platform. "There is an Arctic blast sweeping the Country. I don't want to see people hurt, or injured, in any way."
The Rotunda is prepared as the inclement weather alternative for each inauguration in the event of inclement weather. The swearing-in was last moved indoors in 1985, when President Ronald Reagan began his second term. Monday's forecast calls for the lowest inauguration day temperatures since that day.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Canada ready to respond should tariffs be imposed as soon as Monday
Canada should brace for U.S. tariffs to be imposed as soon as Monday, and if so, Ottawa will be ready to respond immediately, says Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly who held out hope on Friday there's still time to avoid a trade war.
Joly said the federal government estimates the impact, if U.S. President Donald Trump follows through on his threatened 25-percent tariff on all Canadian goods, would be massive. It would increase the cost of living and mean job losses across Canada and in key U.S. states but she refused to provide federal estimates of what the national job losses or the hit to economic activity could be.
"Definitely we have numbers that are highlighting how much this could be devastating," Joly told reporters in a virtual news conference. "The Americans would be starting a trade war against us. And this would be the biggest trade war between Canada and U.S. in decades."
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles
Canada is gearing up to counterattack Trump's tariff threat, Justin Trudeau says
Canada is getting ready to strike back against Donald Trump's tariffs, even though there is dissension in the nation's ranks.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau emerged from a meeting with all 13 premiers Wednesday armed with a proposal to slap counter-tariffs on up to $150 billion worth of American imports, depending on how far the incoming U.S. president goes.
But the first ministers' attempt to display unity in a potential trade war with the U.S. was soured after Alberta Premier Danielle Smith declared she won't stand for how the federal government and other leaders have refused to rule out slapping tariffs on Canadian fossil fuel exports.
Read the full story from Tonda MacCharles Alex Ballingall and Robert Benzie
Canadian business leaders prepare for Trump tariff announcement as early as Monday - but there will be exceptions, they predict
With Donald Trump set to take the oath of office Monday, the North American economy stands on the verge of a trade war.
But will the U.S. president-elect actually follow through on repeated threats of across-the-board, 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian imports on Day One?
The answer, say business groups, trade experts and economists, is probably not.
Read the full story from the Star's Josh Rubin
Everything you need to know about Trump's inauguration
At 12 p.m. Trump and J.D. Vance will be sworn in as the 47th president and 50th vice-president outside of the U.S. Capitol as part of an inauguration ceremony that happens every four years for every incoming president. (This year's inauguration will be the 60th in the country's history.)
While many are familiar with the photos of presidents and vice-presidents bundled up in winter coats and scarves with their right hands raised to take their oaths of office, the swearing-in ceremony isn't the only thing that happens on Inauguration Day - and there are often events planned leading up to the day as well.
Here's everything you need to know about Monday's ceremony and festivities
Who is attending the inauguration?
With incoming U.S. president Donald Trump's inauguration days away, much may be gleaned about the upcoming administration by who's on the guest list, who isn't - and who declined to take part.
From what we know so far, the ceremony on Monday will be attended by former presidents, government officials, tech billionaires and a smattering of far-right leaders including Argentinian President Javier Milei and U.K. politician Nigel Farage.
Notably, former first lady Michelle Obama will be skipping the ceremony, according to her office. Her husband, former president Barack Obama, has confirmed his attendance.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang
Is Donald Trump's inauguration on Martin Luther King Jr. Day?
Yes, this year, Inauguration Day falls on the same day as Martin Luther King Jr. Day.
What time is the inauguration?
Donald Trump's presidential inauguration is scheduled to begin at noon (EST).
Trump to rally in D.C. ahead of inauguration
Donald Trump is expected to hold a big Washington rally the day before he's sworn in as the nation's 47th president.
The victory rally, as it's being billed, will be held at the Capitol One Arena in the District of Columbia on Jan. 19. Trump has always preferred rally-style events where he can freewheel and joke with his supporters. He'll be inaugurated in a formal ceremony at the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 20th.
"This is President Trump's VICTORY RALLY the DAY before being sworn in as the 47th President - the crowd needs to be HUGE!" read an email to supporters advertising the event.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
U.S. Capitol flags raised to full height for Trump's inauguration
House Speaker Mike Johnson on Tuesday ordered that flags at the U.S. Capitol be raised to their full height on Inauguration Day, pausing a 30-day flag-lowering order following the death of former president Jimmy Carter.
The Republican leader's decision means that President-elect Donald Trump will not take the oath of office for his second term under a half-staff flag, a prospect that he had previously complained about.
It mirrors actions taken in recent days by some Republican governors who have announced that flags in their states would be raised on Inauguration Day to mark Trump's second term.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Will Michelle Obama attend the inauguration?
Former first lady Michelle Obama will skip the inauguration of President-elect Donald Trump, the second time in two weeks that she is not attending a gathering of former U.S. leaders and their spouses, but former Presidents Barack Obama, George W. Bush and Bill Clinton will be there. Laura Bush and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will join their husbands for the Jan. 20 swearing-in ceremony at the Capitol, representatives said.
Read the full story from the Associated Press

Ford says 'clear mandate' key to deal with Trump: In push for election, premier says U.S. president more 'aggressive' toward Canada than last term

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-says-clear-mandate-key-deal-with-trump/docview/3157390985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+says+%27clear+mandate%27+key+to+deal+with+Trump%3A+In+push+for+election%2C+premier+says+U.S.+president+more+%27aggressive%27+toward+Canada+than+last+term&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A snap Ontario election appears imminent with U.S. President Donald Trump threatening 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods as of Feb. 1.
Premier Doug Ford, who has been setting the stage for an early vote by warning Trump levies would require tens of billions of dollars in stimulus spending to keep the province's economy afloat, said there is a rationale for an election.
"This is going to be for four years with Donald Trump. He wants to undermine us any which way he can. He wants to take jobs away from Ontarians. He wants to attract businesses from Ontario to come down to the United States," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park on Monday.
"We need a clear mandate, not for tomorrow, not for the next day, for four years of dealing with our American friends."
The next scheduled Ontario election is not until June 2026. A provincewide vote, which could be held as early as Feb. 20, will cost about $175 million.
Ford's Progressive Conservatives have also been considering Feb. 27 and March 6.
"It's a lot worse today than it was yesterday. I need a clear mandate from the people of Ontario to fight against these tariffs, to make sure that we have a loud voice," said Ford, who last week predicted tariffs could cost 500,000 jobs in the province.
"He's creating the department to go after countries on tariffs. This gives more uncertainty," he said, referring to Trump's "External Revenue Service," which appeared to duplicate work already done by the U.S. Commerce Department and Customs and Border Protection.
Ford said he was alarmed by the new president's speech.
"No mistake about it, he's coming for us," he said, adding it's "night and day" between Trump's first term that ended four years ago and now because he is much more "aggressive" toward Canada.
"Donald Trump 1.0 is a lot different than Donald Trump with a massive mandate, with (the) popular vote, controlling the Senate, controlling Congress and the White House. There's a big, huge difference."
While NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have said a provincial election is not necessary because their parties support Ford's plans for any stimulus spending, the Tory premier brushed that aside.
"My opposition, I'm glad they have confidence in our government. But when you go out there and you're going to spend billions of dollars, it's the people, it's not about the politicians," said Ford.
"Politicians don't give other politicians a mandate, the people who give a mandate are the people of Ontario."
Compounding the urgency for Ford's Tories is getting ahead of a federal vote expected this spring.
With polls suggesting federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre would win that election, the provincial PCs are mindful Ontario voters traditionally prefer different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time.
Crombie, for her part, said Ford "has spent the 76 days since Trump was re-elected preparing for an unnecessary early election rather than coming up with a plan to help you and your family."
"Doug Ford is selfishly creating chaos to justify going to the polls a year-and-a-half early for one reason: to outrun an RCMP investigation into his Greenbelt scandal," said the Liberal leader.
Stiles said "the premier needs to focus on the 500,000 jobs at risk, not his own."
Earlier Monday, Ford told delegates at the Rural Ontario Municipal Association conference he was prepared to remove American booze from LCBO stores if Trump slaps levies on Canadian products.
The premier said the U.S. "will feel the pain" because he would instruct the Crown liquor monopoly, one of the world's largest purchasers of American wines and spirits, to "clear off every bit of U.S. alcohol off the shelves."
Ford said he spoke recently with Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear, whose state produces bourbon exported around the world, and told him: " 'you've got to talk to your president because the first thing we're doing is we're getting the bourbon.' "
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

City's budget plan for parks a good sign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/citys-budget-plan-parks-good-sign/docview/3157389765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City%27s+budget+plan+for+parks+a+good+sign&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's easy to overlook, but there's a slogan slapped on most of the standard city of Toronto park signs: "a city within a park."
It's meant to invoke the idea of a city with so many parks, ravines and public spaces that they're the dominant feature of our cityscape. While the slogan has a nice ring to it, it's never felt very accurate. Sure, Toronto has some very nice parks, but proper maintenance has been a perpetual struggle. And the amount of green space has not kept up with our growing population, especially downtown.
But let's not write off the signage sloganeering just yet. Toronto city hall might just finally have a plan to try to live up to those lofty city-within-a-park ideals. The city's budget for 2025, unveiled in draft form last week and set to be finalized by Mayor Olivia Chow next month, contains the most ambitious spending plan for parks and public spaces in memory.
At a proposed $599 million, the total parks and recreation operating budget will go up a huge 13 per cent this year, as the city hires 478 more workers to look after new and existing city facilities. On the capital side - money dedicated to building and repairing parks and recreation facilities - the 10-year spending plan is an even bigger $4.3 billion, up significantly from the $3.2-billion plan left by former mayor John Tory in his last budget in 2023.
In fact, the increased spending on parks and recreation is one of the starkest differences between Tory and Chow's budget strategies. As a point of comparison, between 2015 and 2023 - his entire eight-and-a-bit-year mayoral tenure - Tory saw the parks operating budget grow by a total of about 12 per cent - about half the rate of inflation - from $438 million in 2015 to $490 million in 2023. Chow will exceed that increase in a single year.
For the mayor, the political calculation is straightforward. With a record-high residential 9.5 per cent property tax hike last year and a significant 6.9 per cent hike proposed for this year, Chow is facing pressure to demonstrate that homeowners are getting something for their money. Next to TTC improvements, better-maintained parks and better recreation programming are probably the most visible changes city hall can make that signal to Toronto residents, "see, we're trying to get better."
To that end, this year's parks budget contains a lot of promising promises. Starting this summer, all 59 outdoor pools in the city will open for two extra hours each day in July and August. And the city will finally accept the reality that summer weather doesn't start on Canada Day and end on Labour Day - some pools will be open on weeknights and weekends for the last two weeks of June and the first three weeks of September. Beach season will also be extended, with lifeguards on duty at four locations for the first week of September. And the city is now waiving its fee for booking park space for community activities.
But it's the basic upkeep stuff in the budget that I'd bet will pay off most. The plan proposes $4 million for better cleaning at rec centres, another $1 million for general parks beautification, and $450,000 to improve the dreadful experience of waiting for a ferry at the Jack Layton Ferry Terminal.
On the capital side - the side that builds new parks and repairs old ones - a long list of projects have now been funded thanks in part to $420 million in money that would have been spent on fixing the money pit we call the Gardiner Expressway before Premier Doug Ford agreed to take it off city hall's hands. (Seriously, Doug, no takebacks.)
When Tory left office, the parks department was projecting its backlog of projects in need of repair would grow every year over the next decade as conditions deteriorated. The new plan shows the backlog slowly but surely getting smaller over the same period.
Achieving all of this, of course, is easier said than done. Making a bunch of budget promises is easy. Shovelling money out the door is simple too. Ensuring that money is invested to the point where people can visit their local park or community centre and note an improvement is the challenge.
In fact, the biggest barrier might be another city hall department. The 2025 budget for the division that manages Toronto's shelters makes no bones about the fact that the city will be leaving a lot of people on the street this year. The department is projecting 250 people seeking shelter each day will be "unmatched" to a shelter bed.
The parks department is hoping a plan to spend another $1.3 million to expand the "encampment office" and send more support workers to parks will help prevent the spread of more tents, but until the underlying issues of a lack of shelter space and a lack of housing are addressed, it's hard to feel optimistic.
Still, it's also hard to see how constraining spending on our parks to less than the rate of inflation, as Toronto did under Tory, would ever achieve a better outcome. So far, Chow's budgets at least include some ambition for something better than the status quo. They've also given voters one clear metric for measuring the success of her tax-and-invest strategy.
How do you know if the city is getting better?
It's like the signs say: look within your park.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

The U.S. should brace for the biggest trade blow it has ever endured, Jan. 17 Mayor Olivia Chow launches 2025 Toronto budget with 6.9 per cent property tax hike, Jan. 13

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3157389737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+signals+he+may+need+a+new+%27mandate%27+from+voters+as+Donald+Trump+tariffs+loom%2C+Jan.+16+Holy+heck%2C+that+Mark+Carney+Liberal+leadership+launch+was+a+letdown%2C+Jan.+16+Mark+Carney%2C+Chrystia+Freeland+would+scrap+consumer+carbon+tax+if+elected+Liberal+leader%2C+sources+say%2C+Jan.+16+Chrystia+Freeland%3A+We+won%27t+back+down+to+Donald+Trump.+The+U.S.+should+brace+for+the+biggest+trade+blow+it+has+ever+endured%2C+Jan.+17+Mayor+Olivia+Chow+launches+2025+Toronto+budget+with+6.9+per+cent+property+tax+hike%2C+Jan.+13&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford should zero in on real issues
Doug Ford signals he may need a new 'mandate' from voters as Donald Trump tariffs loom, Jan. 16
Premier Doug Ford should be ashamed for even thinking of calling an election now, well before his term is over.
Most Canadian leaders, including Ford, are advising a "Team Canada" response to Donald Trump's threats of tariffs. Ford's musings about needing a new mandate to "put tens of billions into the economy" seem self-serving and distracting. Ford has seen fit to pump billions into costly (and unnecessary) nuclear developments, for example. Why would he think he needs a new mandate to provide economic protections to Ontarians, something squarely within his current mandate?
Clearly, Ford wants an election before the RCMP reports on Ford's Greenbelt scandal. And he wants to be able to point to the "carbon tax" as the source of Ontario's troubles but this, like Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, will soon disappear from his political calculations.
Ford should focus on issues that build our strength and unity. Topics such as health care, affordable housing, education and protection from climate change.
Moya Beall, Scarborough
Carney is what Canada needs
Holy heck, that Mark Carney Liberal leadership launch was a letdown, Jan. 16
I was OK with Mark Carney's responses to questions fielded by reporters, despite Star columnist Andrew Phillips' disappointment.
Carney has the goods and I should know, because I was in the financial institutions industry while he was governor of both the Bank of Canada and the Bank of England. He is competent with an outstanding educational background in economics (Harvard and Oxford), just what Canada needs these uncertain days. And he's an outsider who cannot be blamed for the mess the Liberals are in. In fact, I just joined the Liberal Party of Canada to make sure he becomes the party's next leader.
As to what riding Carney should run in for member of Parliament, I would be very surprised if he ran in the city where he grew up. His father ran for MP in Edmonton in 1980 and came in second place. Rather, I strongly suggest he run in my riding of Scarborough-Guildwood. It is currently held by Liberal member John McKay, who plans to retire from politics.
Carney would be most welcome here as our next MP instead of someone from Pierre Poilievre's Conservative party.
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
Scrapping carbon tax wrong move
Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland would scrap consumer carbon tax if elected Liberal leader, sources say, Jan. 16
With 2024 marking the hottest year on record and income inequality at its highest level when records began in 1999, the potential decision by Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland to scrap the Canadian Carbon Rebate program is deeply concerning.
This policy is responsible for eight per cent to 14 per cent of Canada's emissions reductions in 2030, and it also serves as an affordability measure for Canadians because, despite all the disinformation, most of us come out ahead.
I understand their rationale, given the well-funded disinformation campaign against pollution pricing. The lies and emotional manipulation on social media platforms have been shameful.
I urge Carney and Freeland to explore alternatives that maintain progress in reducing fossil fuel pollution and addressing income inequality. Citizens' Assemblies or Pollution Pricing Town Halls after the election could foster informed public dialogue, sustaining support for effective climate solutions.
Cathy Orlando, Sudbury, Ont.
Referendum on carbon pricing?
Both Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland are backing off from carbon pricing, likely because of verbal attacks from Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Freeland says she'll make sure big polluters pay for their outsized emissions. But wouldn't these nameless polluters be the ones who build and create the products we consumers use? Therefore, wouldn't the big polluters simply add a surcharge to consumers, thereby making them hidden costs?
The carbon levy, carbon price or carbon tax, whatever you wish to call it, is and should be a necessary responsibility of the consumer. Unfortunately, no matter how much the carbon pricing theory is drilled into our brains, many of us can't quite seem grasp the concept of how it works. Each and every carbon-pricing penny we pay to purchase pollution-generating products is pooled and returned to Canadians quarterly.
What the new Liberal leader should do immediately after assuming the role of prime minister is have a referendum on carbon pricing. In other words, allow Canadian voters to be fully responsible for their own global warming decisions.
Jack Drury, Toronto
Freeland stands up for Canadians
Chrystia Freeland: We won't back down to Donald Trump. The U.S. should brace for the biggest trade blow it has ever endured, Jan. 17
Chrystia Freeland has hit the nail right on the head.
Canadians are a country of people with a history of fighting for its rights, and will continue to do so.
Donald Trump will be faced with immediate retaliation with the 25 per cent tax he keeps saying he'll impose once he's in, and it won't stop there, because we are prepared. Our U.S. neighbours will quickly recognize their mistake of voting him back in but I think they would not have expected him threatening Canada, their best neighbour, including helping with their recent massive fires.
Good luck in the Liberal leadership race, Chrystia. We're behind you.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
City must better manage spending
Mayor Olivia Chow launches 2025 Toronto budget with 6.9 per cent property tax hike, Jan. 13
I agree with the proposed tax increase to maintain/improve service levels. However, with a city budget deficit of about $1.5 billion, every time the city hires a new employee, with its generous indexed pension and benefits, it is committing the city to millions of dollars of new expense over the lifetime of that employee. Few corporations have indexed pensions and reports of layoffs/downsizing are in the news almost daily when businesses endure challenging financial times. With savings through improvements in technology, automation, processes, etc., I'd like to see a story on how the city has managed their most expensive line item over the past five years.
Peter Young, Thornhill

Ford's hopes for an early election are simply a diversionary tactic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-hopes-early-election-are-simply/docview/3157389566/se-2?accountid=15090

Peter Young, Thornhill, Ont.
It's time for Ontario to include accessibility in the building code
It is completely baffling that the province of Ontario refuses to require housing to be accessible, when it would save money. The savings would come from fewer ambulance calls and hospitalizations, and reduced costs for long-term care, among other things.
And such housing does not cost significantly more to build than conventional housing. As the population ages, it's long past time for the province to change the building code to require accessibility.
Carol Damioli, Toronto
CAPTION: A letter writer states Doug Ford's hopes for an early election are simply a diversionary tactic to take the spotlight off the real issues at hand.

Title: Ford's hopes for an early election are simply a diversionary tactic

Document 45 of 500

The Trump tariff threat: A wake-up call for Canada - and the case for optimism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trump-tariff-threat-wake-up-call-canada-case/docview/3157389559/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Trump+tariff+threat%3A+A+wake-up+call+for+Canada+-+and+the+case+for+optimism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump is the U.S. president once more, but Canada remains strong and free.
While the tariff threat still looms, it feels like Canada is slightly stronger than we were a few months ago.
In a bizarre way, we have Trump to thank for that.
The reality-TV star turned politician sees the world like a conflict drama: Allies are adversaries, and negotiation is a game of winners and losers.
In Canada, he saw weakness: a sluggish economy. Falling productivity. The rapid-fire resignations of both the finance minister and the prime minister. As an incredulous CNN anchor said to Ontario Premier Doug Ford, "the entire government has gone into chaos."
In November, Trump put the loaded gun of tariffs on the table, rooted in the falsehood that the U.S. trade deficit with Canada - driven by American consumption of Canadian oil - was a "subsidy." In January, he pressed his advantage, threatening to use "economic force" to make Canada the 51st state.
Was he serious? Probably not. More likely, he said it to keep Canada off balance. In doing so, he united Canada in a way Justin Trudeau hasn't in many years.
It was Ford who captured the national mood - without saying a word - when he emulated Trump's tactic of putting a message on a baseball cap: "CANADA IS NOT FOR SALE." Within a day, the Ottawa-based small business that made the cap sold 50,000.
The Ontario premier understands the best strategy to make peace with an aggressive opponent: Show strength - and a willingness to use it. Reduce your vulnerabilities. And offer a better alternative.
Show strength
Yes, a trade war would hurt Canada badly. But the best path to averting or at least mitigating it is to show that it would cause economic devastation on both sides of the border. A trade war has no winners.
Canada's strength comes from its many assets - the things American businesses and consumers need. We represent 56 per cent of its imported oil, almost half of its nickel and potash, and more than a quarter of their uranium and germanium. Ontario alone provides electricity to almost 1.3 million homes in New York, Michigan and Minnesota.
Reduce your vulnerabilities
If it's going to get rough, Canada has to get buff.
In the last decade, Canada has done little to arrest its declining productivity. Faced with an existential threat, we must replace complacency with competitiveness.
For example, the Ontario Chamber of Commerce has called on provincial and federal leaders to dismantle interprovincial trade barriers (which alone could neutralize the economic impact of any Trump tariffs); lower the cost of doing business; accelerate the development of strategic industries such as mining; invest in infrastructure, post-secondary education and community health; and help businesses diversify their trade relationships.
Just as governments acted decisively to protect businesses, workers and communities during the COVID-19 pandemic, the same level of focus is required today - this time less through broad-based spending, and more through targeted investment, policy and regulatory reform.
Finally, our political leaders - federal and provincial - must stand together. Those who seek to use the Trump threat to benefit their party, or to prioritize local interests over the national interest, must be made to pay a heavy price.
Offer a better alternative
Ironically, Trump himself pointed the way to a better way. "Canada and the United States: that would really be something," he said. This is what we already have: an economic partnership that delivers massive - and equal - benefit to both countries.
While the president's comments about annexing Canada were disrespectful and disappointing, they were also distracting to both Americans and Canadians to a much greater risk and opportunity.
In the next four years, America's single greatest geopolitical imperative is to strengthen its hand relative to China, and Canada is an essential partner in achieving that goal. Doing business with us is a far smarter alternative.
The case for optimism
The silver lining in all this comes from both Canada's new-found unity and Trump's new-found reality.
Now that Trump has been sworn in, his words no longer come unburdened with consequences.
He now has many interests to satisfy, and many partners essential to anything he wants to do: cabinet members, congressional leaders, governors, senior staff and business leaders. Most of them acutely understand what's at stake, and see little benefit to a trade war with Canada.
Canada is stronger than we were in November because we're more united. But unity alone isn't enough. It's time to show strength; to reduce our vulnerabilities; and to offer our American partners a better alternative. This may involve short-term sacrifice, but the long-term returns can be handsome, with 2025 being The Year of Living Courageously.
Canada has assets the world needs, and the talent to turn these assets into an enduring competitive advantage.
Today's currents of political and economic change are a wake-up call to government - and a call to action for business.
Together, we must answer it.
CAPTION: U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats and 51st state bluster, writes Daniel Tisch, united Canada in a way Prime Minister Justin Trudeau never could.
CREDIT: Daniel Tisch Contributor

Subject: Trade disputes; Competitive advantage; Prime ministers; Regulatory reform; Tariffs

Donald Trump supporters react to his Canadian annexation threats + Toronto wants to spend billions on infrastructure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/donald-trump-supporters-react-his-canadian/docview/3157221640/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Donald+Trump+supporters+react+to+his+Canadian+annexation+threats+%2B+Toronto+wants+to+spend+billions+on+infrastructure&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 47 of 500

Doug Ford says a 'clear mandate' from Ontario voters is the best way to deal with Donald Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-clear-mandate-ontario-voters-is/docview/3157221471/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+a+%27clear+mandate%27+from+Ontario+voters+is+the+best+way+to+deal+with+Donald+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A snap Ontario election appears imminent even though U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threat is on the back burner for now.
Premier Doug Ford, who has been setting the stage for an early vote by warning Trump levies would require tens of billions of dollars in stimulus spending to keep the province's economy afloat, said there is a rationale for an election.
"This is going to be for four years with Donald Trump. He wants to undermine us any which way he can. He wants to take jobs away from Ontarians. He wants to attract businesses from Ontario to come down to the United States," Ford told reporters at Queen's Park on Monday.
"We need a clear mandate, not for tomorrow, not for the next day, for four years of dealing with our American friends," he said.
The next scheduled Ontario election is not until June 2026. A provincewide vote, which could be held as early as Feb. 20, will cost about $175 million. Ford's Progressive Conservatives have also been considering Feb. 27 and March 6.
Even though Trump did not follow through with his threatened 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods, the premier noted the new president highlighted the levies in his inaugural address.
"It's a lot worse today than it was yesterday. I need a clear mandate from the people of Ontario to fight against these tariffs, to make sure that we have a loud voice," said Ford, who last week predicted tariffs could cost 500,000 jobs in the province.
"He's creating the department to go after countries on tariffs. This gives more uncertainty," he said, referring to Trump's "External Revenue Service," which appeared to duplicate work already done by the U.S. Commerce Department and Customs and Border Protection.
Ford said he was alarmed by the new president's speech.
"No mistake about it, he's coming for us," he said, adding it's "night and day" between Trump's first term that ended four years ago and now because he is much more "aggressive" toward Canada.
"Donald Trump 1.0 is a lot different than Donald Trump with a massive mandate, with (the) popular vote, controlling the Senate, controlling Congress and the White House. There's a big, huge difference."
While NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have said a provincial election is not necessary because their parties support Ford's plans for any stimulus spending, the Tory premier brushed that aside.
"My opposition, I'm glad they have confidence in our government. But when you go out there and you're going to spend billions of dollars, it's the people, it's not about the politicians," said Ford.
"Politicians don't give other politicians a mandate, the people who give a mandate are the people of Ontario."
Compounding the urgency for Ford's Tories is getting ahead of a federal vote expected this spring.
With polls suggesting federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre would win that election, the provincial PCs are mindful Ontario voters traditionally prefer different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time.
That means a Poilievre victory could be politically disadvantageous to Ford.
Crombie, for her part, said Ford "has spent the 76 days since Trump was re-elected preparing for an unnecessary early election rather than coming up with a plan to help you and your family."
"Doug Ford is selfishly creating chaos to justify going to the polls a year-and-a-half early for one reason: to outrun an RCMP investigation into his Greenbelt scandal," said the Liberal leader.
"Right now in Ontario, unemployment is already at a 10-year high. Taxes are far too high. There are 2.5 million people waiting for a family doctor. Evictions and defaults are rampant because homes are unaffordable. A million people are using food banks and a record number of people in Ontario are homeless," she said.
Stiles said "the premier needs to focus on the 500,000 jobs at risk, not his own."
"The moment we're in calls for unity of purpose. We need to stay focused on what we can do here in Ontario to support every sector at risk and defend every single job in the face of this tariff threat," said the New Democratic leader.
Earlier Monday, Ford told delegates at the Rural Ontario Municipal Association conference he was prepared to remove American booze from LCBO stores if Trump slaps levies on Canadian products.
The premier said the U.S. "will feel the pain" because he would instruct the Crown liquor monopoly, one of the world's largest purchasers of American wines and spirits, to "clear off every bit of U.S. alcohol off the shelves."
Ford said he spoke recently with Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear, whose state produces bourbon exported around the world, and told him: "'you've got to talk to your president because the first thing we're doing is we're getting the bourbon.'"
It is unclear if Beshear, a Democrat who campaigned against Trump in last November's U.S. election, will be able to persuade the new president to hold off on tariffs.
In sharp contrast to Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who has sought a "carve out" from tariffs for her province's oil and gas exports and opposes retaliatory tariffs, Ford said Canada must fight back against the new U.S. administration.
"I promise you as premier of Ontario that if President Trump's tariffs make retaliation necessary, I will stand shoulder to shoulder with the rest of Team Canada to fight back using every tool in our tool kit."
That's a message shared by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and all the other premiers except Smith.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, wearing a "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Wednesday.

Document 48 of 500

How Canada can stick it to Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-canada-can-stick-trump/docview/3157114223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Canada+can+stick+it+to+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Who among our best and brightest will step up in Canada's hour of greatest need? Jan. 12
Jamie Watt argues that our leaders need to step up and protect Canada's identity. He suggests that all of us have a part to play in this, though he doesn't provide specifics. Here are some things regular Canadians can do to send the right message to President-elect Donald Trump and his followers.
If Americans wants to impose dramatic tariffs on Canadian products, then Canadians should stop buying American ones. We should cancel all vacations to the United States and visit other destinations instead, such as the Caribbean, Bermuda, or better still, somewhere in Canada. We should walk right past American retailers like Walmart and Costco when other options are available and look for "Made in Canada" tags, leaving those "Made in USA" products on store shelves. Finally, we should select the foods that our grandparents relied upon: apple juice rather than orange juice, blueberries and pears from Canada rather than from California. Garden centre greenhouses need to move quickly to grow fresh vegetables such as lettuce and peas.
These are just few ideas. I'm sure my fellow-Canadians can develop other ways to bypass American goods in favour of supporting Canadian producers so that we can build a stronger, more resilient economy - even as Trump tries to destroy it.
Brian Moore, Brantford
Canada is passing through difficult times because of a lack of leadership at the federal level and an absence of unity at the provincial level. The outcome of the first ministers meeting in Ottawa last Tuesday was disappointing and discouraging. There's disagreement in the nation's ranks, and everyone is playing their own flute.
Ontario premier Doug Ford took a stand and emphasized the need for unity. Alberta premier Danielle Smith took a different kind of stand: she appeared to care only for her province, not for Canada as a whole. A nation is more than just a geographical region: it is a complex union of cultures, histories and values. In a situation such as this, we should be focusing on the importance of unity, the role of the provinces in our federation and the factors that contribute to a nation's strength. Yet our first minsters failed to come together to form a common front against Donald Trump's tariff threats.
Still, we have time. Canadian leaders need to take unified stand to face the challenges ahead. It needs courage, and courage is nothing more than an affirmative answer to the shocks of existence.
Mehdi Rizvi, Pickering
Let's remember that Justin Trudeau is still the prime minister. International relations is his bailiwick. He should be facing off with Donald Trump, flanked by the leaders of the opposition parties and the premiers of the provinces and territories. It must be made clear that regardless of the outcome of the federal election in Canada, Trump will face a united front. Instead, Doug Ford appears to be angling for the job of prime minister - a job that is not his to apply for - when he ought to be rallying the Ontario electorate. He ought to be reminding them that we've faced bullies in the past and won. Adolf Hitler was defeated by the Allied powers. That's how you deal with bullies, by forming defensive alliances.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
I have never voted for Doug Ford and probably never will, but I am 100 per cent behind him in his approach to Donald Trump. If you don't stand up to bullies, they will always pick on you. If you do - and if you can land a few punches of your own - they will think twice before messing with you again.
Elliot Keller, Toronto
Alberta's refusal to stand with the rest of Canada against Donald Trumps tariff threats is unacceptable but not surprising. Alberta's political leaders have been all about themselves for decades. Their only concern has appeared to be selling oil to American interests at Canadians' expense. They need to stop playing into Trump's hands for their own selfish purposes and start supporting the best country in the world. That country is Canada, and Alberta needs to realize as much.
Rick Lockman, Orillia
Whooo will protect Toronto's owls?
'We have to act now': Deaths of two snowy owls at Tommy Thompson Park re-ignite calls for rodenticide ban, Jan. 5
I was disappointed that the Star went ahead with publishing the story "'We have to act now': Deaths of two snowy owls at Tommy Thompson Park re-ignite calls for rodenticide ban" before having determined whether rodenticide was, in fact, the cause of death for these birds. I can't stress enough how essential it is to wait for a pathologist to confirm the presence of rodenticide in an animal's tissues before jumping to conclusions. If you come across a sick or dead wild animal in Ontario, your first call should be to the Canadian Wildlife Health Cooperative. Submitting samples to them allows us to monitor rodenticides' impact on wildlife.
Carly Davenport, Toronto
TDSB paid-leave stats shocking
The TDSB has spent tens of millions placing school staff on paid leave. It's working to change its practices, Jan. 16
The Toronto District School Board paid $35 million in salary to 621 teachers, principals and staff over a three-year period to stay home while under investigation for misconduct. The board calls it "home assignment," but this a misnomer, as the word "assignment" suggests that the employee in question is working from home. It should be termed "suspension with pay" to reflect the reality that the employee is not allowed in the school while being investigated.
I was shocked that the average length of a "home assignment" for the TDSB principals and vice-principals was eight months, while the provincial average was under four. Even more striking was the fact that some investigations take years to complete. I look forward to the upcoming report, which is expected to identify the specific types of misconduct that have allegedly been occurring within the TDSB.
Mike Faye, Toronto
Why Edmonton leads Toronto on housing
Edmonton is crushing Toronto when it comes to building new homes. An astounding Canadian first is a big reason why, Jan. 16
David Olive correctly points out that Edmonton is leading Toronto, a city four times its size, in building new homes and rental units. Both municipalities face obstacles to housing construction - including high interest rates, an economic slump and costly materials - although Edmonton has benefitted from less onerous zoning bylaws and a strong focus on affordable housing. Yet Olive omits one of the greatest differences: unlike Ontario, Alberta has no rent control. Since Ontario implemented rent control in 1975, the number of designated rental units built has plummeted, resulting in a dearth of vacant units. This has created artificial pressure to increase rents when apartments do become available. It's another example of good government intentions gone wrong.
Andy Suboch, Toronto
Carney no fiscal conservativeMark Carney and Pierre Poilievre say a lot about themselves with their choices of interviewers, Jan. 16
Bruce Arthur was wrong to state that Mark Carney is "likely a financially conservative" politician. I am currently reading Carney's 2021 book, "Value(s): Building a Better World for All." His economic analysis offers a progressive perspective on how we can use the values of "sustainability, solidarity and responsibility" to deal with existential problems such as climate change, world hunger, and the obscene and growing gap between rich and poor. He lays out a blueprint for building a just future for our children, our grandchildren and generations to come.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump speaks to members of the media during a news conference at the Mar-a-Lago Club on Jan. 7.
