Document 49 of 500

Live 2025 inauguration: Trump rebuffs Biden's pardons in speech; Biden says his team 'is not leaving the fight'; Trudeau congratulates Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/live-2025-inauguration-trump-rebuffs-bidens/docview/3157113827/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Live+2025+inauguration%3A+Trump+rebuffs+Biden%27s+pardons+in+speech%3B+Biden+says+his+team+%27is+not+leaving+the+fight%27%3B+Trudeau+congratulates+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: President-elect Donald Trump's inauguration for his second presidential term took place in Washington, D.C. on Monday. Trump has officially become the 47th president of the United States and Vice-President-elect JD Vance has also take the oath of office. Follow the Star's live updates all day.
2:22 p.m. Biden reacts to Trump's speech
Biden reacted to Trump's inaugural address in his first speech as a former president at Joint Base Andrews just before leaving for California.
"We heard the inaugural address today - you know, we got a lot more to do," he said, before crossing himself to laughter from the crowd and his former staffers.
"No president gets to choose the moment they enter history. But they get to choose the team they enter history with. And we chose the best damn team in the world," he said. "We're leaving office. But we're not leaving the fight."
Biden exited to chants of "thank you, Joe."
2:06 p.m. "Nelk Boys" co-founder attends with Conor McGregor and Jake and Logan Paul
Internet personality Kyle Forgeard - the Mississauga-born co-founder of the absurdly popular YouTube channel "Nelk Boys" - shared a video on Instagram Monday en route to Trump's inauguration alongside influencers Jake and Logan Paul and Irish MMA fighter Conor McGregor.
The Nelk Boys started out as a prank show, but has in recent years become a platform known for promoting right-wing political causes. In 2022, Forbes named Forgeard as one of the top social media influencers under 30.
McGregor was recently sued for alleged sexual battery over an incident that took place at the 2023 NBA finals. In November of last year, a civil court jury in Ireland awarded 250,000 euros to a woman who said she was "brutally raped and battered" by the MMA fighter.
2 p.m. (Updated) Trump rebuffs Biden's pardons
Trump rebuffed Biden's pardons of his family members and others including Anthony Fauci, Liz Cheney and the committee investigating the January 6 insurrection in a rambling speech at Emancipation Hall, claiming that some of the people were "very, very guilty of very bad crimes."
These included the January 6th Unselect Committee, who he claimed "deleted all the information on Nancy Pelosi having turned down the offer of 10,000 soldiers" at the event. It's a repeated claim of Trump's that the committee found no evidence of ever happening.
Trump also claimed Biden pardoned "33 murderers" and denounced Cheney as a "crying lunatic."
Biden commuted the sentences of 37 people facing the death sentence. They must still serve life in prison without parole - they were not "pardoned" as Trump implied.
Earlier Monday, Biden also commuted the life sentence of Native American activist Leonard Peltier, who was convicted of murdering two FBI agents. Peltier will serve out the rest of his sentence in home confinement.
Read the full transcript of Trump's speech.
1:54 p.m. Senator John Fetterman keeps it casual
Trump's official swearing-in ceremony was moved inside the Capitol Rotunda due to forecasts of "intense cold weather," with temperatures dipping below zero around noon. But the chilly air did not seem to bother Democratic Senator John Fetterman, who arrived at the Capitol wearing a baggy pair of shorts and a black Carhartt hoodie. Though Fetterman is known for his casual attire, he was spotted wearing a suit for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's address to Congress last summer.
1:45 p.m. Donald Trump becomes U.S. president for a second time
Donald Trump, the populist disrupter who has vowed political revenge, the mass deportation of "animals" from other countries, and the use of tariffs to win influence and prosperity from abroad - including, perhaps, from Canada - is now the 47th president of the United States.
At a ceremony inside the rotunda of the domed U.S. Capitol Building, where his supporters rioted over the lost presidential election four years ago, Trump officially returned to power at noon on Monday. The moment capped a political restoration that saw Trump survive a criminal conviction, get past investigations into his alleged role in seeking to overturn the 2020 presidential election, and clinch expanded support from American voters in winning November's presidential vote.
"The golden age of America begins right now," said Trump, 78, in his second inaugural address.
Read the full story from the Star's Alex Ballingall
1:31 p.m. Trump is giving a speech to an inauguration crowd after being introduced by JD Vance.
"You're a younger, far more beautiful audience than the one I just spoke to," he joked. "I want to keep it off the record."
Trump also addressed Biden's pre-emptive pardons of his family members and others hours before the inauguration, noting they were "very, very guilty of very bad crimes."
1:30 p.m. A group of men clad in the garb of the Proud Boys marched through the streets of Washington shortly after Trump was sworn in. A handful of counter protesters yelled through megaphones that they were "losers."
1:25 p.m. Trump administration shuts down CBP One
The new Trump administration has already shut down CBP One, a government mobile app that previously helped migrants submit information and schedule appointments at ports of entry.
"Effective January 20, 2025, the functionalities of CBP One that previously allowed undocumented aliens to submit advance information and schedule appointments at eight southwest border ports of entry is no longer available, and existing appointments have been cancelled," an alert on the app's website reads.
Roughly 270,000 Mexican migrants were waiting to get an appointment through the app before it shuttered, CBS reports. As of Jan. 16, nearly 919,000 migrants were allowed into the States through the app.
1:16 p.m. Trudeau congratulates Trump
In a social media post, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau congratulated Trump on his inauguration. "We have the chance to work together again -- to create more jobs and prosperity for both our nations," Trudeau said.
In his speech, Trump did not mention Canada but reiterated his threat of tariffs on foreign nations. Trump had previously threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
FBI acting chief Paul Abbate says he's retiring
Paul Abbate, who became the FBI's acting director following the departure of Christopher Wray, is retiring from the bureau, according to an email he sent to senior executives that was obtained by The Associated Press on Monday.
Abbate had been expected to run the FBI while Senate confirmation proceedings get underway for Kash Patel, who is President Donald Trump's pick for director. But with Abbate's retirement effective Monday, when Trump took office, it was not immediately clear who would fill that role.
1:02 p.m. Government site gets makeover
The whitehouse.gov website got a makeover, welcoming visitors with a video glorifying Trump to soaring orchestral music before giving way to a landing page boasting that "America is back" in large lettering and an image of Trump.
"Every single day I will be fighting for you with every breath in my body," an accompanying caption reads. "I will not rest until we have delivered the strong, safe and prosperous America that our children deserve and that you deserve. This will truly be the golden age of America."
12:43 p.m. Carrie Underwood's performance beset by technical difficulties
Carrie Underwood's highly-anticipated performance at Trump's inauguration did not exactly go as planned.
After being introduced, the Grammy-winning country singer stood in awkward silence caused by technical difficulties as she waited for the musical track to begin. After about a minute, Underwood could be seen mouthing the words "just sing?" before requesting that the audience "help me out here." She then proceeded to perform the song a cappella without further issue.
An eight-time Grammy winner, Underwood, 41, is the highest profile artist slated to perform at Trump's inauguration. Her decision to perform at the inauguration was met with significant negative reactions from her fans, particularly her LGBTQ+ fans. Underwood has not publicly responded to those criticisms.
12:54 p.m. What did Melania Trump wear?
Incoming First Lady Melania Trump, who was mostly shunned by America's fashion establishment during her husband's last term in office, arrived at the church service ahead of her husband's second inauguration in a slim-fit navy silk-and-wool coat and skirt set by American designer Adam Lippes.
Notably, she accessorized it with a flat-brimmed, low-slung hat with white trim by Eric Javits, an American milliner since the 1980s with celebrity clients including Dua Lipa and Lady Gaga. Sitting low on her face Spy vs. Spy-style, the hat is destined to become an instant meme, while preventing the cameras from catching any accidental facial expressions-Melania has been captured seemingly giving side-eye at events in the past.
The choice of Adam Lippes for her outfit seems calculated to show support for a smaller, independent fashion designer. Lippes, who worked for Oscar de la Renta before starting his own label a decade ago, specializes in American sportswear; his dresses run in the $3,000 to $4,000 range. For Trump's first inauguration, Melanie wore a light blue dress by heritage brand Ralph Lauren (also a favourite designer of the Biden family) that drew comparisons to Jackie Kennedy's style.
"Melania's look is restrained, classic, uniform like. It looks like a dramatic governess outfit," said Tracy Richardson, a Canadian image consultant who has dressed clients for the White House, Buckingham Palace, the Oscars, TIFF and the Junos. "The hat is dramatic. But she looks neat. We aren't distracted by what she is wearing." Leanne Delap/Special to the Star
12:53 p.m. Trump stops short of immediately imposing Canadian tariffs
U.S. President Donald Trump has stopped short of immediately imposing his threatened 25-percent tariff on Canadian and Mexican goods.
But in his inaugural address minutes after taking office, Trump left no doubt he has foreign manufacturers and America's allies and competitors in his crosshairs.
He vowed he would set up a new agency to collect tariff revenues, and that he will fill American coffers with tariff and tax revenue from foreign countries, saying he was "saved by God to make America great again."
Trump declared a series of national emergencies to deploy soldiers to the Mexican border, ramp up oil drilling, end electric vehicle mandates, and began what he called "an overhaul" of U.S. trade relations.
Read the full story from the Star's Tonda MacCharles
12:44 p.m. Trump finished his speech by reiterating his hopes to be remembered as a peacemaker, assuring that a new American golden age had begun.
"Nothing will stand in our way because we are Americans, the future is ours, and our golden age has just begun," Trump finished to raucous cheers and a standing ovation.
12:42 p.m. "We stand on the verge of the four greatest years in American history," Trump says. "I am with you, I will fight for you, and I will win for you. We're going to win like never before."
12:40 p.m. Many people thought it was impossible for me to stage such a historic political comeback. But as you see today, here I am. The American people have spoken," Trump said to a standing ovation.
12:38 p.m. (updated) Trump is now running through a litany of promises for his administration, including taking back the Panama Canal, renaming the Gulf of Mexico, imposing tariffs on foreign countries and ending government DEI policies. He also lashed out to efforts to limit climate change, vowing to "drill, baby, drill."
With Elon Musk, CEO of SpaceX, sitting only feet away, Trump promises that America will "pursue our manifest destiny into the stars" and send American astronauts to Mars.
"Many people thought it was impossible for me to stage such a historic political comeback. But as you see today, here I am. The American people have spoken," Trump said to a standing ovation.
12:27 p.m. (updated) Trump says he is overhauling the trade system to protect Americans. "We will tariff and tax foreign countries to enrich out citizens," he says, vowing to establish an "external revenue service" to collect this money. Trump has previously threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
Trump promised to immediately sign an executive order to "immediately stop all government censorship and bring back free speech to America."
Trump says it is the official policy of the U.S. government that there are only two genders.
Trump promises to reinstate any military members ejected for not being vaccinated, with full backpay.
He also reiterated his promise to end government DEI policies.
"A short time from now, we are going to be changing the name of the Gulf of Mexico to the Gulf of America," Trunp said.
12:26 p.m. (updated) Trump promises 'golden age of America'
In his inaugural address, Trump promised the "golden age of America begins right now," reiterating his vow to "put America first."
"From this day forward, our country will flourish and be respected again all over the world," he said. "We will be the envy of every nation, and we will not allow ourselves to be taken advantage of any longer."
"I was saved by God to make America great again," Trump says.
Trump says today is "liberation day" for Americans, and hopes his election will be viewed as among the most influential in American history.
Trump says he will declare a national emergency at the Mexican-U.S. border, a line that draws a standing ovation from the rotunda -- with Biden, Harris, the Clintons and the Bushs notably sitting.
Trump vowed to end the Green New Deal and revoke the "electric vehicle mandate."
"In other words, you'll be able to buy the car of your choice. We will build automobiles in America again at a rate that nobody could have dreamt possible just a few years ago."
How much is Trump's crypto coin worth?
On Monday, Donald Trump's newly created cryptocurrency, a "meme coin" known as $TRUMP, soared to more than $8 billion dollars in market value, as of writing. Launched on Sunday, the collectible token, which has no inherent value, currently ranks third among other "meme coins" behind digital assets Dogecoin and Shiba Inu.
First Lady Melania Trump also launched a crypto coin, called $MELANIA,, on Sunday, which is currently trading just under $1 billion, behind other popular coins like "Pepe" and "Fartcoin."
Some crypto enthusiasts hailed the Trump meme coin's release, saying it's symbolic of the incoming president's support for an industry that felt unfairly targeted by the Biden administration. Trump has promised to usher in crypto-friendly regulations and picked crypto cheerleaders for key government positions.
Critics said the Trump meme coin could be a dangerous way for special interests and foreign governments to try and buy influence with the president.
How tall are Barron Trump and Donald Trump?
Since Trump was first elected, much ink has been spilled about the president's "large adult sons," Donald Jr. and Eric. But Trump's youngest child, Barron has largely evaded the spotlight.
That may be about to change, given that Barron is now 18 and looks looks like he could an up-and-coming shooting guard for Washington Wizards. Indeed, standing behind the president's podium at Monday's inauguration, the baby-faced Trump towered over the other attendees.
But exactly how tall is Barron? Google has him listed at 2.06 metres, which is almost 6 foot 8. However, he was listed at 6 foot 7 during his graduation from Oxbridge Academy in West Palm Beach last May. That's at least four inches taller than his father, who is listed at 6 foot 3, or 1.9 metres.

11:47 a.m. Biden pardon family members
In the final minutes of his presidency, Joe Biden has pardoned the Biden family, his brothers James and Francis, sister-in-law Sara Jones, sister Valerie and brother-in-law John.
"My family has been subjected to unrelenting attacks and threats, motivated solely by a desire to hurt me-the worst kind of partisan politics," Biden said in a statement. "Unfortunately, I have no reason to believe these attacks will end."
Biden also commuted the life sentence of Leonard Peltier. The 80-year-old was convicted for the 1975 killings of two FBI agents during a standoff on a reservation in South Dakota.
"This commutation will enable Mr. Peltier to spend his remaining days in home confinement but will not pardon him for his underlying crimes," a White House statement said.

11:35 a.m. (updated) Guests arriving for the inauguration
Guests have arrived at the U.S. Capitol for the inauguration, which has been moved indoors due freezing temperatures brought by the Polar Vortex.
Former president Barack Obama has arrived without former first lady Michelle Obama, who announced last week she would forego Monday's event.
Met with significant boos by the crowd, former Vice President Mike Pence, Former President Bill Clinton, and former first lady Hillary Clinton are also amongst the guests in attendance.
Tech titan and major Trump donor Elon Musk, who will be heading the new Department of Government Efficiency, has also arrived, alongside Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Apple CEO Tim Cook. Podcast host Joe Rogan has also been seen in attendance.
Argentine President Javier Milei and Italian Premier Giorgia Meloni have also been seen in the Rotunda, the first foreign leaders to attend the inauguration since 1874.
Trump's cabinet picks, including future secretary of state Marco Rubio and Pete Hegseth, the controversial pick for defence secretary, have also been seen at the capitol.
11:32 a.m. Trump will be the oldest president ever inaugurated
At 78 years and 220 days old, Trump is five months older than Joe Biden when he was inaugurated in 2021.
Meanwhile, at 40 years old, JD Vance will be the third-youngest vice president in U.S. history.

Trump kicked off his morning Monday with a pre-inauguration visit to St. John's Church. Among those in attendance were several billionaires and tech tycoons, including Amazon founder Jeff Bezos (net worth $239.4 billion (U.S.), Meta founder Mark Zuckerberg (net worth $211.8 billion (U.S.), Apple CEO Tim Cook (net worth $2.2 billion (U.S.) and Google CEO Sundar Pichai (net worth $2.1 billion (U.S.).
Among the other notable figures spotted at the church were media tycoon Rupert Murdoch (net worth $22.2 billion (U.S.), plus FIFA president Gianni Infantino. Former UK Conservative Prime Minister Boris Johnson and Javier Milei, the ultraconservative president of Argentina were also in attendance.

11:12 a.m. Trump does the 'Trump dance' during Village People performance
The Village People, a campy disco group, known for their goofy lyrics and problematic costumes, performed their hit song "Y.M.C.A." on stage with Trump on Sunday - assless chaps, Native American headdresses and all. The song has become a staple at Trump rallies - you've likely seen the President-elect's awkward, jerky dance moves, which first went viral during his 2020 election campaign. Since then, the "Trump" dance has been embraced by Trump supporters, frat boys and several pro athletes, including San Francisco 49ers star Nick Bosa and U.S. men's soccer team captain Christian Pulisic.
Over the decades, the 1978 hit has been embraced by the gay community. However, according to Victor Willis, the band's litigious lead singer (and only remaining original member) has insisted the song is not a "gay anthem." In fact, he recently stated that he will take legal action against anyone who suggests it is.

"We of course welcome this attitude and congratulate the elected president of the United States of America on taking office."
Canada bracing for Donald Trump's decision amid reports 25 per cent tariffs not immediately on the table
The Canadian government has not received any official confirmation that incoming U.S. President Donald Trump will not impose tariffs on Canadian products, as threatened.
Two senior Canadian officials said Monday the government was still waiting for official word that would confirm reports by the Wall Street Journal and New York Times that Trump is instead going to order a trade deficit review which would include a study of the North American free-trade pact.
One official said it is encouraging if true that Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian goods is not immediately on the table.

10:07 a.m. Melania Trump cryptocurrency ahead of inauguration
Incoming first lady Melania Trump revealed her own official meme coin cryptocurrency the night before her husband's inauguration.
She announced her token Sunday evening in a post to X: "The official Melania Meme is live!", she wrote, adding that "You can buy $Melania now."
Her coin is currently trading at $9.08 as of time of writing, up over 24 per cent in the last day, according to CoinMarketCap. It has a market cap of $1.76 billion.
A disclaimer on the cryptocurrency's website cautions the tokens are "not intended to be, or to be the subject of, an investment opportunity, investment contract, or security of any type."
Instead, they're being marketed as "digital collectibles intended to function as an expression of support for and engagement with the values embodied by the symbol MELANIA. and the associated artwork."
Melania's announcement came days after the president-elect unveiled his own meme coin $Trump on Friday; as of time of writing, his coin is down more than 13 per cent at $53.91, according to CoinMarketCap.
"Official Trump" is currently the most-traded meme coin on the Solana platform, according to CoinGecko. "Melania Meme" is in sixth place, as of the time of writing.
Who is J.D. Vance? How the former anti-Donald Trump author became the right hand of the incoming president

Small businesses in Canada losing jobs as Trump's tariff threat looms
A new report has found that Canadian small business job growth has been "lagging" since last summer, particularly within manufacturing and professional services, signalling possible trouble ahead as president-elect Donald Trump's threat of U.S. tariffs looms.
The report by Intuit QuickBooks found that Canadian small businesses have lost more than 84,000 jobs since July, despite adding a cumulative 126,600 jobs between October 2023 and October 2024.
The year-over-year increase in small business jobs is in stark contrast to the second half losses in 2024 and reflects a tale of two trends, said Simon Worsfold, head of data communications at Intuit QuickBooks.

Trump swearing-in will move inside Capitol Rotunda because of intense cold
President-elect Donald Trump may take the oath of office from inside the Capitol Rotunda on Monday due to forecasts of intense cold weather.
"The weather forecast for Washington, D.C., with the windchill factor, could take temperatures into severe record lows," Trump posted on his Truth Social platform. "There is an Arctic blast sweeping the Country. I don't want to see people hurt, or injured, in any way."
The Rotunda is prepared as the inclement weather alternative for each inauguration in the event of inclement weather. The swearing-in was last moved indoors in 1985, when President Ronald Reagan began his second term. Monday's forecast calls for the lowest inauguration day temperatures since that day.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Canada ready to respond should tariffs be imposed as soon as Monday
Canada should brace for U.S. tariffs to be imposed as soon as Monday, and if so, Ottawa will be ready to respond immediately, says Foreign Affairs Minister Melanie Joly who held out hope on Friday there's still time to avoid a trade war.
Joly said the federal government estimates the impact, if U.S. President Donald Trump follows through on his threatened 25-percent tariff on all Canadian goods, would be massive. It would increase the cost of living and mean job losses across Canada and in key U.S. states but she refused to provide federal estimates of what the national job losses or the hit to economic activity could be.
"Definitely we have numbers that are highlighting how much this could be devastating," Joly told reporters in a virtual news conference. "The Americans would be starting a trade war against us. And this would be the biggest trade war between Canada and U.S. in decades."

Everything you need to know about Trump's inauguration
At 12 p.m. Trump and J.D. Vance will be sworn in as the 47th president and 50th vice-president outside of the U.S. Capitol as part of an inauguration ceremony that happens every four years for every incoming president. (This year's inauguration will be the 60th in the country's history.)
While many are familiar with the photos of presidents and vice-presidents bundled up in winter coats and scarves with their right hands raised to take their oaths of office, the swearing-in ceremony isn't the only thing that happens on Inauguration Day - and there are often events planned leading up to the day as well.
Here's everything you need to know about Monday's ceremony and festivities
Who is attending the inauguration?
With incoming U.S. president Donald Trump's inauguration days away, much may be gleaned about the upcoming administration by who's on the guest list, who isn't - and who declined to take part.
From what we know so far, the ceremony on Monday will be attended by former presidents, government officials, tech billionaires and a smattering of far-right leaders including Argentinian President Javier Milei and U.K. politician Nigel Farage.
Notably, former first lady Michelle Obama will be skipping the ceremony, according to her office. Her husband, former president Barack Obama, has confirmed his attendance.
Read the full story from the Star's Kevin Jiang
Is Donald Trump's inauguration on Martin Luther King Jr. Day?
Yes, this year, Inauguration Day falls on the same day as Martin Luther King Jr. Day.
What time is the inauguration?
Donald Trump's presidential inauguration is scheduled to begin at noon (EST).
Trump to rally in D.C. ahead of inauguration
Donald Trump is expected to hold a big Washington rally the day before he's sworn in as the nation's 47th president.
The victory rally, as it's being billed, will be held at the Capitol One Arena in the District of Columbia on Jan. 19. Trump has always preferred rally-style events where he can freewheel and joke with his supporters. He'll be inaugurated in a formal ceremony at the U.S. Capitol on Jan. 20th.
"This is President Trump's VICTORY RALLY the DAY before being sworn in as the 47th President - the crowd needs to be HUGE!" read an email to supporters advertising the event.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
U.S. Capitol flags raised to full height for Trump's inauguration
House Speaker Mike Johnson on Tuesday ordered that flags at the U.S. Capitol be raised to their full height on Inauguration Day, pausing a 30-day flag-lowering order following the death of former President Jimmy Carter.
The Republican leader's decision means that President-elect Donald Trump will not take the oath of office for his second term under a half-staff flag, a prospect that he had previously complained about.
It mirrors actions taken in recent days by some Republican governors who have announced that flags in their states would be raised on Inauguration Day to mark Trump's second term.
Read the full story from the Associated Press
Will Michelle Obama attend the inauguration?
Former first lady Michelle Obama will skip the inauguration of President-elect Donald Trump, the second time in two weeks that she is not attending a gathering of former U.S. leaders and their spouses, but former Presidents Barack Obama, George W. Bush and Bill Clinton will be there. Laura Bush and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will join their husbands for the Jan. 20 swearing-in ceremony at the Capitol, representatives said.

'I want to know the whole story': Just hours after he was put on suicide watch, Line Bissonnette's son was found dead in his cell

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-want-know-whole-story/docview/3157113812/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+want+to+know+the+whole+story%27%3A+Just+hours+after+he+was+put+on+suicide+watch%2C+Line+Bissonnette%27s+son+was+found+dead+in+his+cell&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For more than a year, Line Bissonnette was unable to read the details of how her son died.
It wasn't that she didn't want to know how Stéphane Bissonnette died while in federal custody, but rather, that she had been advised to wait to read the report until she could obtain professional support - something she doesn't have the means to secure for herself.
It was only last month that her lawyers organized a counsellor to be present while she reviewed the redacted document. In doing so, many of the circumstances surrounding Stéphane's death were illuminated for his mother. She now knows he died just hours after being placed on a suicide watch by prison staff, who did not discover his body till hours later.
It did not, however, offer a full explanation. The report lacks a cause of death, categorizing it as "undetermined."
Stéphane died on the floor of his cell sometime in the early morning on Dec. 17, 2021, hours after being placed on a modified suicide watch.
Despite multiple security patrols and wellness checks conducted that night, staff at Millhaven Institution, located just outside Kingston, failed to notice him lying motionless on his cell floor for at least six hours, according to a report prepared and released last fall by the Office of the Correctional Investigator.
"It's awful," Line told the Star from her home in the Montreal borough of Lachine. "It's not right that they still don't know why he died. I want to know the whole story of my son, how he died, why it happened."
In the corner of the small apartment is a makeshift memorial to Stéphane, 39, containing family photos, art and his boom box, which Line has left on since his death.
"The music is on 24/7," she said. "I listen to the music, I cry, and I think of my son."
Last fall, his family filed a wrongful-death lawsuitagainst the Attorney General of Canada, claiming staff inside the prison's Regional Treatment Centre failed to monitor him as required or provide adequate health care, describing the treatment he received while serving his sentence as "degrading, dehumanizing and intrinsically incompatible with human dignity."
The case follows a familiar pattern of mismanagement of inmates with serious mental health issues inside Canadian correctional institutions, the Office of the Correctional Investigator said.
When reached for comment by the Star, the Attorney General's office directed questions to Correctional Service Canada (CSC), which said it takes inmate deaths "very seriously," but declined further comment while the matter remained before the courts.
A statement of defence had not been filed as of mid-January.
'I don't know if I'll be here in 2022'
According to the Correctional Investigator's report, Bissonnette dealt with serious mental health issues over nearly a decade in different maximum-security facilities - including significant time in segregation.
In 2012, Bissonnette pleaded guilty to a number of charges, including robbery with a firearm, impersonation and several counts of break and enter, and was initially sentenced to 12 years in federal custody.
Over the course of his federal incarceration, he spent a cumulative 461 days in solitary confinement before it was banned in 2019. Between then and 2021, he spent another 158 days in "structured intervention units," which have replaced solitary confinement for inmates in Canada. Prisoners transferred to these units are meant to get a total of four hours each day outside of their cell, according to CSC, with two of those hours engaged in "meaningful human contact."
In the same time period, he spent an estimated nine days in the Pinel Restraint System, a system to be used as "only a last resort" for self-injurious behaviour, and 67 days on high or modified suicide watch, the report states.
In 2021, he was transferred to Millhaven's Regional Treatment Centre, one of five facilities across Canada offering clinical, in-patient treatment to inmates with severe mental health issues.
Just weeks before his death, he penned a letter to his brother, also a federal inmate sentenced on charges of robbery and possession.
"I would like to be with you for Christmas, it's been a long time," he wrote in the letter. "I don't know if I'll be here in 2022. A guard tells me if I die, I'll be number seven to die in this cell."
"I knew he wasn't good in the head," his brother, Olivier Laplante, told the Star in an interview inside Archambault Penitentiary in Sainte-Anne-des-Plaines, Que. "He was losing his memory and becoming more aggressive."
The day before he died, Bissonnette was recorded repeatedly hitting his head against the cell door window, striking a mirror with his fist, andmiming slitting his throat with his finger to the camera in his cell. A few hours later, he informed staff he was suicidal and that he had hurt himself using a broken compact disc. His arm, CSC noted, was bloodied and wrapped in a pillow case.
He was placed on a modified suicide watch, requiring staff to observe him - at least via surveillance cameras - at all times.
According to the lawsuit, however, the fact that Bissonnette had been placed on the watch was not communicated to overnight staff, who failed to adequately monitor him. The staff did not read documents sent to them by his clinical case worker, outlining his condition, before starting their shifts, it alleges.
The claim states that during 11 security patrols conducted between 10 a.m. on Dec. 16 and 5 a.m. the next morning, staff either failed to check Bissonnette's cell or glanced in for "one second or less." Meanwhile, the lawsuit alleges, the employees responsible for monitoring the CCTV footage of the cell were "watching television during their shifts."
The next morning, he was found dead.
At 5 a.m. on Dec. 17, 2021, the officer responsible for monitoring the CCTV asked another officer to check on Bissonnette because "he was not exhibiting signs of life."
That staff member, the claim states, did not do so immediately, but rather, continued to conduct head counts on two more areas before attending the cell about five minutes later. When they found him on the floor, motionless, the claim alleges they did not notify nursing staff right away, but instead finished the count first.
Next, when nurses conducting an unrelated health check happened to see Stéphane in his cell, "rather than immediately asking for his door to be opened, they inexplicably waited for the officer to return from counting other ranges," the lawsuit reads.
All told, it took more than 10 minutes from the time staff first noticed Bissonnette lying motionless for them to open the cell door. After attempting to jolt him awake and remarking he was "cold and likely dead," nursing staff used a defibrillator on him.
By that time, however, his body had already entered rigor mortis, according to the lawsuit.
'Why was no one watching?'
Laplante was informed of his brother's death shortly after, by guards at Archambault Institution.
"I started freaking out, crying," he said. "I could not understand, my brother could not be dead. Why was no one watching? Why did no one check on him that night? How did nobody know he was dead for hours, if he was supposed to be on suicide watch?"
The lawsuit alleges an absence of quality control standards and adequate training concerning patrols, rounds and counts at the facility, something the claim says the Office of the Correctional Investigator has consistently identified as "the top contributing factor to deaths in custody since 2007." At the time of his death, the filings allege that CSC had no written procedures on how nursing staff should conduct wellness checks on inmates.
In total, six staff members were disciplined in connection with the incident, the most serious penalty being the firing of one of the registered nurses involved. Four correctional officers were handed suspensions without pay, ranging from two to 30 days.
'Persistent, known, and recurring issues'
Both CSC and the Correctional Investigator launched investigations into Bissonnette's death.
The CSC investigation, a standard process when an inmate dies of non-natural cases, began in June 2022, six months after his death, and was completed more than a year later, in August 2023. While a summary of CSC's investigation and the resulting recommendations to the prison have been released online, the agency has not yet released its full findings to the Bissonnette family.
Instead, his mother had to contact CSC in 2023 to request the results. Over a year later, in June 2024, they released a redacted version to the family. According to the lawsuit, however, "CSC specifically discouraged Line from reading the redacted version without professional support," which she couldn't afford for many months. As a result, she read the document for the first time in December.
In October 2023, the Office of the Correctional Investigator released the findings of its investigation as a case study in its annual report.
In the report, investigator Ivan Zinger wrote that CSC failed to advise their office of his death. Instead, it learned of the incident from a media release and subsequent inquiry from his family before initiating its own investigation.
The case study identified a number of "systemic factors, gaps and compliance issues" at the prison, including inadequate security patrols, a blurred line between health and operation teams at the penitentiary, and a "security-driven" response to behaviours associated with mental illness, among other issues.
It found his death could not be blamed on any one staff member but rather, "must be understood in the context of significant, persistent, known, and recurring issues that continue to lead to tragic and preventable deaths in (federal) custody."
In total, Zinger made six recommendations to CSC, including participating in a full compliance review of patient safety, conducted by an independent, external mental health expert.
Many of the recommendations made in the report reflect those issued as part of the provincial inquest into the homicide of Soleiman Faqiri, who was detained at the Central East Correctional Centre in Lindsay, Ont. Faqiri, from Ajax, died in a segregation cell on Dec. 15, 2016 while experiencing what medical experts described as a psychiatric emergency.
"The top line recommendation of that inquest was for the province to recognize that corrections is not an adequate place for someone living with mental illness," said NDP MPP Kristyn Wong-Tam, critic to Ontario's Attorney General.
Isolation or restraints should be a last resort of enforcement for inmates with mental health issues, Wong-Tam said.
"Why would you leave someone who's so unwell, who's got a long history documented of mental illness in what is essentially solitary confinement?"
Jeffrey Hartman, counsel for Line and Laplante, echoed concerns over a lack of sufficient mental health care in carceral facilities.
"I want Canadians to ask questions about what we're doing when we put people who have clear mental health needs in a place that is notionally a psychiatric hospital for prisoners, but is run the same as a maximum-security prison, where a guy can be dead for six hours and no one notices," said Hartman.
The failures of the CSC in this case are "inexplicable," he said.
Hartman, alongside co-counsel Jeffery Couse, have requested the case be heard by trial in Kingston.
Meanwhile, back in Montreal, Line still hasn't laid her son's ashes to rest.
Due to financial constraints, Line still hasn't laid her son's ashes to rest. A spot in the cemetery is too costly for his family at the moment, Laplante explained.
Sometime in the not-so-distant future, however, he says he's expecting a share of the settlement the class action lawsuit awarded against Canada for mistreatment of inmates in solitary confinement.
When the money comes in, Laplante said he will give it to his mother "so she can buy Stéphane a spot in the cemetery."
CREDIT: AbO'Brien and Marisa Coulton Toronto Star

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Who+among+our+best+and+brightest+will+step+up+in+Canada%27s+hour+of+greatest+need%3F+Jan.+12+%27We+have+to+act+now%27%3A+Deaths+of+two+snowy+owls+at+Tommy+Thompson+Park+reignite+calls+for+rodenticide+ban%2C+Jan.+5+The+TDSB+has+spent+tens+of+millions+placing+school+staff+on+paid+leave.+It%27s+working+to+change+its+practices%2C+Jan.+16+Mark+Carney+and+Pierre+Poilievre+say+a+lot+about+themselves+with+their+choices+of+interviewers%2C+Arthur%2C+Jan.+16&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How Canada can stick it to Trump
Who among our best and brightest will step up in Canada's hour of greatest need? Jan. 12
Jamie Watt argues that our leaders need to step up and protect Canada's identity. He suggests that all of us have a part to play in this, though he doesn't provide specifics. Here are some things regular Canadians can do to send the right message to U.S. president-elect Donald Trump and his followers.
If Americans wants to impose dramatic tariffs on Canadian products, then Canadians should stop buying American ones. We should cancel all vacations to the United States and visit other destinations instead, such as the Caribbean, Bermuda, or better still, somewhere in Canada. We should walk right past American retailers like Walmart and Costco when other options are available and look for "Made in Canada" tags, leaving those "Made in USA" products on store shelves. Finally, we should select the foods that our grandparents relied upon: apple juice rather than orange juice, blueberries and pears from Canada rather than from California. Garden-centre greenhouses need to move quickly to grow fresh vegetables such as lettuce and peas. These are just few ideas. I'm sure my fellow Canadians can develop other ways to bypass American goods in favour of supporting Canadian producers so that we can build a stronger, more resilient economy - even as Trump tries to destroy it.
Brian Moore, Brantford, Ont.
Canada is passing through difficult times because of a lack of leadership at the federal level and an absence of unity at the provincial level. The outcome of the first ministers meeting in Ottawa last Tuesday was disappointing and discouraging. There's disagreement in the nation's ranks and everyone is playing their own flute.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford took a stand and emphasized the need for unity. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith took a different kind of stand: she appeared to care only for her province, not for Canada as a whole. A nation is more than just a geographical region: it is a complex union of cultures, histories and values. In a situation such as this, we should be focusing on the importance of unity, the role of the provinces in our federation and the factors that contribute to a nation's strength. Yet our first minsters failed to come together to form a common front against Donald Trump's tariff threats.
Still, we have time. Canadian leaders need to take unified stand to face the challenges ahead. It needs courage, and courage is nothing more than an affirmative answer to the shocks of existence.
Mehdi Rizvi, Pickering
Let's remember that Justin Trudeau is still the prime minister. International relations is his bailiwick. He should be facing off with Donald Trump, flanked by the leaders of the opposition parties and the premiers of the provinces and territories. It must be made clear that regardless of the outcome of the federal election in Canada, Trump will face a united front. Instead, Doug Ford appears to be angling for the job of prime minister - a job that is not his to apply for - when he ought to be rallying the Ontario electorate. He ought to be reminding them that we've faced bullies in the past and won. Adolf Hitler was defeated by the Allied powers. That's how you deal with bullies, by forming defensive alliances.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
I have never voted for Doug Ford and probably never will, but I am 100 per cent behind him in his approach to Donald Trump. If you don't stand up to bullies, they will always pick on you. If you do - and if you can land a few punches of your own - they will think twice before messing with you again.
Elliot Keller, Toronto
Alberta's refusal to stand with the rest of Canada against Donald Trumps tariff threats is unacceptable but not surprising. Alberta's political leaders have been all about themselves for decades. Their only concern has appeared to be selling oil to American interests at Canadians' expense. They need to stop playing into Trump's hands for their own selfish purposes and start supporting the best country in the world. That country is Canada, and Alberta needs to realize as much.
Rick Lockman, Orillia, Ont.
Who will protect Toronto's owls?
'We have to act now': Deaths of two snowy owls at Tommy Thompson Park reignite calls for rodenticide ban, Jan. 5
I was disappointed that the Star went ahead with publishing this story before having determined whether rodenticide was, in fact, the cause of death for these birds. I can't stress enough how essential it is to wait for a pathologist to confirm the presence of rodenticide in an animal's tissues before jumping to conclusions. If you come across a sick or dead wild animal in Ontario, your first call should be to the Canadian Wildlife Health Cooperative. Submitting samples to them lets us monitor rodenticides' effect on wildlife.
Carly Davenport, Toronto
TDSB paid-leave stats shocking
The TDSB has spent tens of millions placing school staff on paid leave. It's working to change its practices, Jan. 16
The Toronto District School Board paid $35 million in salary to 621 teachers, principals and staff over a three-year period to stay home while under investigation for misconduct. The board calls it "home assignment," but this a misnomer, as the word "assignment" suggests that the employee in question is working from home. It should be termed "suspension with pay" to reflect the reality that the employee is not allowed in the school while being investigated. I was shocked that the average length of a "home assignment" for the TDSB principals and vice-principals was eight months, while the provincial average was under four. Even more striking was the fact that some investigations take years to complete. I look forward to the upcoming report, which is expected to identify the specific types of misconduct that have allegedly been occurring within the TDSB.
Mike Faye, Toronto
Carney no fiscal conservative
Mark Carney and Pierre
Poilievre say a lot about
themselves with their
choices of interviewers,
Arthur, Jan. 16
Bruce Arthur was wrong to state that Mark Carney is "likely a financially conservative" politician. I am currently reading Carney's 2021 book, "Value(s): Building a Better World for All." His economic analysis offers a progressive perspective on how we can use the values of "sustainability, solidarity and responsibility" to deal with existential problems such as climate change, world hunger and the obscene and growing gap between rich and poor. He lays out a blueprint for building a just future for our children, our grandchildren and generations to come.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto

Document 52 of 500

Mystery brain disease patients welcome new probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mystery-brain-disease-patients-welcome-new-probe/docview/3157113589/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mystery+brain+disease+patients+welcome+new+probe&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Melissa Hawkes began to feel unwell during a visit to a friend's house in March 2021. All that she remembers before blacking out is that she went to the bathroom. When she opened her eyes, she found herself lying on the floor, her friends and fiancée looking on worriedly.
"They're saying, 'Oh no. You had a seizure,' " said Hawkes, 27, who lived in Moncton, N.B., when the symptoms began. "I'd never been to this person's house before. I was just meeting them with my fiancée. I was so embarrassed."
What started off with "mild" problems, she said, such as intense exhaustion and nausea, has turned into a serious illness: she had a second seizure in 2023, and has developed nerve damage in her wrist and necrotizing gingivitis.
She is one of nearly 400 New Brunswick residents who suffer from what the province calls a "neurological syndrome of unknown cause" - a mystery brain disease that seems to mostly affect people living in the Acadian Peninsula and Moncton areas. Her fiancé, Sarah Nesbitt, is also afflicted.
In February 2022, the Health Department under the Progressive Conservatives said a team of six neurologists and other health experts found no evidence a cluster of cases existed.
But patients, who reported symptoms such as memory problems, balance issues, behavioural changes, muscle spasms and bursts of intense pain, said the province should never have closed the probe. In response, the Liberals campaigned on a promise to reopen the investigation, and since winning the October election, New Brunswick's new government is back on the case.
A lot has changed since 2022, Health Minister John Dornan said in a recent interview, noting that there were fewer than 50 patients who had presented with symptoms at the time. Now, he says, there are more than 400.
The Health Department's report in 2022 said that 46 of the 48 cases were referred to the government by a single neurologist - Dr. Alier Marrero - and for 41 patients, experts found possible alternative diagnoses: Alzheimer's disease, various forms of dementia, post-concussion syndrome and cancer. Ten of the 48 patients died and six had autopsies completed. "These diagnoses included such conditions as Alzheimer's disease, Lewy body disease, or cancer," the report said of the autopsy results.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Subject: Alzheimer's disease; Brain diseases

Title: Mystery brain disease patients welcome new probe

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication year: 2025

Document 53 of 500

City's top doctor slams closure plan: Province's move to reduce services will be deadly and costly, says acting medical officer of health

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/citys-top-doctor-slams-closure-plan/docview/3157113585/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City%27s+top+doctor+slams+closure+plan%3A+Province%27s+move+to+reduce+services+will+be+deadly+and+costly%2C+says+acting+medical+officer+of+health&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's acting medical officer of health said the province's plan to close half the city's supervised consumption sites for drugs will be deadly for the city - and costly for the health care system.
"These facilities save lives, connect people to social services and are pathways to treatment," Dr. Na-Koshie Lamptey wrote in a new report to the city's board of health.
The report comes a month after Premier Doug Ford's government passed the Community Care and Recovery Act that bans operation of the supervised sites within 200 metres of schools or daycares. The change would force five of 10 sites in Toronto alone to close by April, including Toronto Public Health's own The Works on Victoria Street downtown.
"The new legislation will reduce access to an evidence-based clinical health care service leading to an anticipated increase in preventable fatal and non-fatal overdoses," Lamptey wrote.
Supervised consumption sites give people a place to use drugs that they already have in a safe environment - under the watch of trained staff and nurses who monitor for signs of overdose and infection. Sites also participate in checking drug samples for toxic substances and providing sterile equipment and safe disposal for needles. Starting in 2020, the federal government also began funding some sites to provide pharmaceutical opioids to approved clients in hopes of reducing their reliance on an increasingly toxic drug supply.
Ford's new rules propose sites slated for closure be converted to new "Homelessness and Addictions Recovery Treatment (HART) sites, but those sites cannot provide consumption services, safe supply of drugs or needle exchange programs.
Since the first supervised consumption site opened in Toronto in 2017, the services have proven popular and life-saving.
Between March 2020 and May 2024, the report documents, the 10 sites collectively had 390,986 visits - more than 250 daily - and saw 10,959 non-fatal overdoses. None of the overdoses resulted in death.
At The Works, staff have responded to 4,060 overdoses since 2017, more than half of which required staff to use naloxone - a drug that can reverse the effects of an overdose.
Lamptey's report also highlights the impact that closing half the city's sites would have on emergency services and hospitals.
The closure is expected to have a particular impact on Toronto Paramedic Services, Lamptey wrote, with paramedics already seeing a 54 per cent increase in calls for drug toxicity since 2019.
With a decrease in supervised consumption services, Lamptey wrote, people who take drugs may be more likely to use alone, increasing the "frequency and severity of overdoses."
"Toronto Paramedic Services expects an increase in overdose-related calls, greater demand for naloxone administration, and more frequent hospital transports," the report says.
It may also fall to paramedics to be responsible for public education, distributing naloxone kits and other services with the closure of five consumption sites, Lamptey wrote.
The city's board of health is scheduled to discuss the report at a meeting Monday.
Lamptey recommended the board urge Ford's government to increase access to consumption services, permit needle exchanges within the new model and provide details of how the province will help address the burden on the health care system.
In an emailed statement, Hannah Jensen, spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, said communities have "made it clear that the presence of drug injection sites near schools and daycares is leading to serious safety concerns."
She said the new HART model will provide "more comprehensive care" than the existing consumption sites, but did not immediately respond to a follow-up email asking for a specific response to the medical officer of health's concerns these sites will no longer offer supervised consumption or needle exchange, which have been proven to prevent overdose deaths and disease.
CAPTION: Dr. Na-Koshie Lamptey, Toronto's acting medical officer of health, said in a report that decreased supervised consumption services means drug users will be more likely to do it alone, increasing the "frequency and severity of overdoses."Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro staff Reporter

Subject: Drug overdose; Paramedics; Toxicity; Pharmacists; Real estate closings

Doug Ford's plan to slash drug consumption sites in half is deadly, costly says Toronto's top doctor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-plan-slash-drug-consumption-sites-half/docview/3157242478/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+slash+drug+consumption+sites+in+half+is+deadly%2C+costly+says+Toronto%27s+top+doctor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 55 of 500

Vast majority of Canadians fear impact of Donald Trump's tariffs, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vast-majority-canadians-fear-impact-donald-trumps/docview/3156967461/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Vast+majority+of+Canadians+fear+impact+of+Donald+Trump%27s+tariffs%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than four in five Canadians believe Donald Trump's 25-per-cent tariffs will hurt the economy, with two-thirds fearing his levies could impact them personally, a new poll suggests.
A staggering 84 per cent of those surveyed have a "negative" view of what the incoming U.S. president's measures could do to Canada with only three per cent being "positive" about them and eight per cent unsure.
At the same time, Pollara Strategic Insights found 65 per cent believe a trade war could affect their day-to-day lives in a negative way while five per cent held a positive view and 12 per cent didn't know.
Dan Arnold, chief strategy officer at Pollara, said that's significant.
"Generally, when we ask questions about a policy - either good or bad - people don't usually think it's going to impact them at the end of the day," said Arnold.
"They say, 'Well, this will hurt some people, but maybe it won't hurt me,' but you've got two-thirds of Canadians who actually feel like this could have a negative impact on their lives day-to-day," he said.
"They have a sense that it's going to be bad - whether or not that impacts their job or whether or not it means things just get more expensive at the grocery store because we're into a trade war."
Pollara surveyed 1,506 people across Canada from Dec. 4 until Dec. 13 - after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visited Trump at the president-elect's Mara-a-Lago resort in Florida.
While online panel samples cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
With Trump threatening tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods immediately after his inauguration Monday, political leaders here have been readying a counterattack.
Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, have been warning that all options are on the table if Canada is forced to retaliate.
"If the president-elect does choose to move forward on tariffs, Canada will, of course, respond," the prime minister said Friday in Toronto.
Ford, who has warned the U.S. tariffs could cost 500,000 job in Ontario, stressed "the retaliatory tariffs need to be hard."
"You can't let someone hit you over the head with a sledgehammer without hitting him back twice as hard," the premier said at the first minister's meeting Wednesday in Ottawa with Trudeau seated beside him nodding in agreement.
But Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, concerned about her province's flow of oil and natural gas to the U.S., strongly disagreed with the other first ministers.
"Federal government officials continue to publicly and privately float the idea of cutting off energy supply to the U.S. and imposing export tariffs on Alberta energy and other products to the United States," Smith, who attended Wednesday's meeting virtually as she was on vacation, said on social media.
"Until these threats cease, Alberta will not be able to fully support the federal government's plan in dealing with the threatened tariffs."
But Pollara found Albertans are just as worried about the effect of Trump's levies as other Canadians.
"It is pretty consistent across the board. In Ontario, 67 per cent think it's going to have an impact on their day-to-day life; it's 61 per cent in Alberta," said Arnold.
"So even though premiers are reacting differently to this issue, I think Canadians themselves everywhere are worried about tariffs and the impact this is going to have on them," he said.
Pollara also found that Canadians are souring on the U.S. in the wake of Trump's return to the White House.
Last month, 59 per cent of those surveyed had a negative view of Canada's closest neighbour and largest trading partner while only 33 per cent had positive feelings and eight per cent didn't know.
That compared to findings last June, before President Joe Biden announced he wouldn't seek re-election, when 40 per cent had a negative view of the U.S. and 53 per cent had positive feelings and seven per cent were unsure.
"That's because (Trump) is the United States in the eyes of most Canadians right now," said Arnold.
When respondents were asked to describe Trump in different ways, 70 per cent said he "wants to help the rich," 69 per cent said he's "corrupt," 54 per cent believe he's a "fascist" and 50 per cent felt he's "senile."
Only 36 per cent said his actions are because he "wants to help working-class Americans get ahead" while 31 per cent said he was "doing things I wish more Canadian politicians would do."
About one-quarter - 27 per cent - described Trump as "competent" with 22 per cent declaring he's "stable" and 21 per cent "rational."
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump speaks during a meeting with Republican governors at Mar-a-Lago, in Palm Beach, Fla., on Jan. 9, 2025.

Subject: Trade disputes; Polls & surveys; Presidents; Canadians; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Title: Vast majority of Canadians fear impact of Donald Trump's tariffs, survey shows

Document 56 of 500

An inmate on suicide-watch was dead in his cell for six hours before he was found. His family still doesn't know how he died

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/inmate-on-suicide-watch-was-dead-his-cell-six/docview/3156967437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+inmate+on+suicide-watch+was+dead+in+his+cell+for+six+hours+before+he+was+found.+His+family+still+doesn%27t+know+how+he+died&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For more than a year, Line Bissonnette was unable to read the details of how her son died.
It wasn't that she didn't want to know how Stéphane Bissonnette died while in federal custody, but rather, that she had been advised to wait to read the report until she could obtain professional support - something she doesn't have the means to secure for herself.
It was only last month that her lawyers organized a counsellor to be present while she reviewed the redacted document. In doing so, many of the circumstances surrounding Stéphane's death were illuminated for his mother. She now knows he died just hours after being placed on a suicide watch by prison staff, who did not discover his body till hours later.
It did not, however, offer a full explanation. The report lacks a cause of death, categorizing it as "undetermined."
Stéphane died on the floor of his cell sometime in the early morning on Dec. 17, 2021, hours after being placed on a modified suicide watch.
Despite multiple security patrols and wellness checks conducted that night, staff at Millhaven Institution, located just outside Kingston, failed to notice Bissonnette lying motionless on his cell floor for at least six hours, according to a report prepared and released last fall by the Office of the Correctional Investigator.
"It's awful," Line told the Star from her home in Lachine, Montreal. "It's not right that they still don't know why he died. I want to know the whole story of my son, how he died, why it happened."
In the corner of the small apartment is a makeshift memorial to Stéphane, containing family photos, art and his boom box, which Line has left on since his death.
"The music is on 24/7," she said. "I listen to the music, I cry, and I think of my son."
Last fall, Stéphane's family filed a wrongful-death lawsuit against the Attorney General of Canada, claiming staff inside the prison's Regional Treatment Centre failed to monitor him as required or provide adequate health care, describing the treatment he received while serving his sentence as "degrading, dehumanizing, and intrinsically incompatible with human dignity."
The case follows a familiar pattern of mismanagement of inmates with serious mental health issues inside Canadian correctional institutions, the Office of the Correctional Investigator said.
When reached for comment by the Star, the Attorney General's office directed questions to CSC, which said it takes inmate deaths "very seriously," but declined further comment while the matter remained before the courts.
A statement of defence has not been filed as of mid-January.
'I don't know if I'll be here in 2022'
According to the Correctional Investigator's report, Stéphane dealt with serious mental health issues over nearly a decade in different maximum-security facilities - including significant time in segregation.
In 2012, Stéphane pleaded guilty to a number of charges, including robbery with a firearm, impersonation and several counts of break and enter, and was initially sentenced to 12 years in federal custody.
Over the course of his federal incarceration, he spent a cumulative 461 days in solitary confinement before it was banned in 2019. Between then and 2021, he spent another 158 days in "structured intervention units," which have replaced solitary confinement for inmates in Canada. Prisoners transferred to these units are meant to get a total of four hours each day outside of their cell, according to CSC, with two of those hours engaged in "meaningful human contact."
In the same time period, Stéphane spent an estimated nine days in the Pinel Restraint System, a system to be used "only a last resort" for self-injurious behaviour, and 67 days on high or modified suicide watch, the report states.
In 2021, Stéphane was transferred to Millhaven's Regional Treatment Centre, one of five facilities across Canada offering clinical, in-patient treatment to inmates with severe mental health issues.
Just weeks before his death, he penned a letter to his brother, also a federal inmate sentenced on charges of robbery and possession.
"I would like to be with you for Christmas, it's been a long time," he wrote in the letter. "I don't know if I'll be here in 2022. A guard tells me if I die, I'll be number seven to die in this cell."
"I knew he wasn't good in the head," Stéphane's brother, Olivier Laplante, told the Star in an interview inside Archambault Penitentiary in Sainte-Anne-des-Plaines, Que. "He was losing his memory and becoming more aggressive."
The day before he died, Stéphane was recorded repeatedly hitting his head against the cell door window, striking a mirror with his fist, and miming slitting his throat with his finger to the camera in his cell. A few hours later, he informed staff he was suicidal and that he had hurt himself using a broken compact disc. His arm, CSC noted, was bloodied and wrapped in a pillow case.
He was placed on a modified suicide watch, requiring staff to observe him - at least via surveillance cameras - at all times.
According to the lawsuit, however, the fact that Stéphane had been placed on the watch was not communicated to overnight staff, who failed to adequately monitor him. The staff did not read documents sent to them by Stéphane's clinical case worker, outlining his condition, before starting their shifts, it alleges.
The claim states that during 11 security patrols conducted between 10 a.m. on Dec. 16 and 5 a.m. the next morning, staff either failed to check Bissonnette's cell or glanced in for "one second or less." Meanwhile, the lawsuit alleges, the employees responsible for monitoring the CCTV footage of the cell were "watching television during their shifts."
The next morning, Stéphane was found dead.
At 5 a.m. on Dec. 17, 2021 the officer responsible for monitoring the CCTV asked another officer to check on Stéphane because "he was not exhibiting signs of life."
That staff member, the claim states, did not do so immediately, but rather, continued to conduct head counts on two more areas before attending the cell about five minutes later. When they found Stéphane on the floor, motionless, the claim alleges they did not notify nursing staff right away, but instead finished the count first.
Next, when nurses conducting an unrelated health check happened to see Stéphane in his cell, "rather than immediately asking for his door to be opened, they inexplicably waited for the officer to return from counting other ranges," the lawsuit reads.
All told, it took more than 10 minutes from the time staff first noticed Stéphane lying motionless for them to open the cell door. After attempting to jolt him awake and remarking he was "cold and likely dead," nursing staff used a defibrillator on him.
By that time, however, his body had already entered rigor mortis, according to the lawsuit.
'Why was no one watching?'
Laplante was informed of his brother's death shortly after, by guards at Archambault Institution.
"I started freaking out, crying," he said. "I could not understand, my brother could not be dead. Why was no one watching? Why did no one check on him that night? How did nobody know he was dead for hours, if he was supposed to be on suicide watch?"
The lawsuit alleges an absence of quality control standards and adequate training concerning patrols, rounds and counts at the facility, something the claim says the Office of the Correctional Investigator has consistently identified as "the top contributing factor to deaths in custody since 2007." At the time of Stéphane's death, the filings allege that CSC had no written procedures on how nursing staff should conduct wellness checks on inmates.
In total, six staff members were disciplined in connection with the incident, the most serious penalty being the firing of one of the registered nurses involved. Four correctional officers were handed suspensions without pay, ranging from two to 30 days.
'Persistent, known, and recurring issues'
Both CSC and the Correctional Investigator launched investigations into Stéphane's death.
The CSC investigation, a standard process when an inmate dies of non-natural cases, began in June 2022, six months after Stéphane's death, and was completed more than a year later, in August 2023. While a summary of CSC's investigation and the resulting recommendations to the prison have been released online, the agency has not yet released its full findings to the Bissonnette family.
Instead, his mother had to contact CSC in 2023 to request the results. Over a year later, in June 2024, they released a redacted version to the family. According to the lawsuit, however, "CSC specifically discouraged Line from reading the redacted version without professional support," which she couldn't afford for many months. As a result, she read the document for the first time in December.
In October 2023, the Office of the Correctional Investigator released the findings of its investigation as a case study in its annual report.
In the report, investigator Ivan Zinger wrote that CSC failed to advise their office of Stéphane's death. Instead, it learned of the incident from a media release and subsequent inquiry from his family before initiating its own investigation.
The case study identified a number of "systemic factors, gaps and compliance issues" at the prison, including inadequate security patrols, a blurred line between health and operation teams at the penitentiary, and a "security-driven" response to behaviours associated with mental illness, among other issues.
It found that Stéphane's death could not be blamed on any one staff member but rather, "must be understood in the context of significant, persistent, known, and recurring issues that continue to lead to tragic and preventable deaths in (federal) custody."
In total, Zinger made six recommendations to CSC, including participating in a full compliance review of patient safety, conducted by an independent, external mental health expert.
Many of the recommendations made in the report reflect those issued as part of the provincial inquest into the homicide of Soleiman Faqiri, who was detained at the Central East Correctional Centre in Lindsay, Ont. Faqiri, from Ajax, died in a segregation cell on Dec. 15, 2016 while experiencing what medical experts described as a psychiatric emergency.
"The top line recommendation of that inquest was for the province to recognize that corrections is not an adequate place for someone living with mental illness," said NDP MPP Kristyn Wong-Tam, critic to Ontario's Attorney General.
Isolation or restraints should be a last resort of enforcement for inmates with mental health issues, Wong-Tam said.
"Why would you leave someone who's so unwell, who's got a long history documented of mental illness in what is essentially solitary confinement?"
Jeffrey Hartman, counsel for Line and Laplante, echoed concerns over a lack of sufficient mental health care in carceral facilities.
"I want Canadians to ask questions about what we're doing when we put people who have clear mental health needs in a place that is notionally a psychiatric hospital for prisoners, but is run the same as a maximum-security prison, where a guy can be dead for six hours and no one notices," said Hartman.
The failures of the Correctional Service of Canada (CSC) in this case are "inexplicable," he said.
Hartman, alongside co-counsel Jeffery Couse, have requested the case be heard by trial in Toronto.
Meanwhile, back in Montreal, Line still hasn't laid her son's ashes to rest.
Due to financial constraints, Line still hasn't laid her son's ashes to rest. A spot in the cemetery is too costly for his family at the moment, Laplante explained.
Sometime in the not-so-distant future, however, he says he's expecting a share of the settlement the class action lawsuit awarded against Canada for mistreatment of inmates in solitary confinement.
When the money comes in, Laplante said he will give it to his mother "so she can buy Stéphane a spot in the cemetery."
