Document 57 of 500

A legal bid by developers to strike down Toronto's green building rules would 'gut' the city's housing standards, say critics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/legal-bid-developers-strike-down-torontos-green/docview/3156967434/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+legal+bid+by+developers+to+strike+down+Toronto%27s+green+building+rules+would+%27gut%27+the+city%27s+housing+standards%2C+say+critics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A group of developers is asking a court to strike down the city of Toronto's sustainable building standards - a legal challenge that the builders say would make homes cheaper but that critics say will make them substandard and "gut" the city's already flagging climate change strategy.
The association, the Residential Construction Council of Ontario (RESCON), represents developers of residential buildings. In a legal application filed in mid-November, RESCON asked an Ontario court to prohibit the city from imposing its Toronto Green Standard, and to rule that the city doesn't have jurisdiction to set its own requirements for new construction above and beyond what is already in the provincial building code.
RESCON president Richard Lyall said the association hopes winning the legal challenge will set a precedent that applies to all Ontario municipalities, who are increasingly adopting their own green building standards. Many are modelled on Toronto's, which sets mandatory performance requirements for energy efficiency, electric vehicle charging stations, green space and more, as well as higher, voluntary tiers. It applies those standards to new low- and high-rise residential construction.
"We're not anti-green," said Lyall. The goal of the lawsuit, he said, is to clear away requirements that are duplicative or inconsistent, and that he said add extra costs that are born by consumers.
"If you want to introduce new measures and whatever, let's let's do it through the building code process where all parties are there," said Lyall. "Let's do it through a disciplined process ... it's just about bringing clarity."
Others in the industry say the consequences of invalidating the Toronto Green Standard would be severe, and would last for generations.
"Given the phenomenal amount of growth we're going to witness in the Toronto region over the next 20 to 30 years, we're going to be building so many buildings to accommodate the population growth," said Ted Kesik, a professor of building science at the University of Toronto. Kesik was retained by the city as a third-party researcher to lead a cost-benefit analysis of the standards in 2008.
"And what are we going to then do, hand over a bunch of these substandard buildings to the next generation and say, 'You fix them up, because we were too cheap to do it?'"
The city solicitor's office served a notice to the court in December saying it intends to respond to the lawsuit; a spokesperson said the department "will respond to the application in due course." No hearing date has been set.
The first version of the Toronto Green Standard was rolled out almost 20 years ago in an effort to curtail Toronto's contributions to the climate crisis and tackle a list of other environmental woes. Buildings, and the fossil fuels used to heat them, are Toronto's top source of greenhouse gas emissions. Impermeable surfaces such as concrete also contribute to stormwater runoff that exacerbates flooding and pollutes Lake Ontario, while losing green space ratchets up the urban heat island effect and worsens air quality.
The city also intended the standards to make homes more resilient. In a power outage, energy-efficient designs hold indoor temperatures for longer, lowering the risks from ice storms, summer floods and other severe weather events that are becoming more likely because of climate change. The city has strengthened the measures over time.
While the court challenge hinges on a narrow legal question of jurisdiction, the battle for public approval revolves around whether these standards make life better for residents of this city or worse.
"We've got the single biggest housing crisis ever, and affordability for young people - I mean, the numbers are just brutal," said Lyall, adding families are being "crushed."
"You can have the most energy-efficient, greenest buildings in the world, which is great. But if you can't build them because nobody can afford them, where does that get you?"
Lyall suggested that money and effort would be better spent on retrofitting old buildings, which are the least energy efficient but hardest to fix. "Politicians are good at creating new things, not fixing existing problems," he said, adding that he would be fine with the Toronto's requirements if they were adopted by the province and applied consistently across municipalities.
Proponents of the Toronto Green Standard say that abandoning these measures now would foist expensive repairs and retrofits onto homeowners later, and leave residents in homes ill-equipped for the threats and transformations brought on by climate change.
"It's much more cost effective to build it from the start rather than try to change it later," said Bryan Purcell, the Toronto Atmospheric Fund's vice-president of policy and programs.
As an example, Purcell pointed to electric vehicle charging stations. Premier Doug Ford's government stripped requirements for EV charging stations from the building code in 2019 after developers complained they added $500 to the cost of a new home; it chose not to reverse that decision last year. The Toronto Green Standard makes it a requirement to build in a power source that can be used for charging EVs if needed.
Electric vehicles made up just 3.1 per cent of the market in 2019 when the provincial code was changed, but hit a record high of 16.5 in the third quarter of last year; gas-powered car sales, meanwhile, have dropped significantly in recent years. Federal rules require all new cars to be zero-emission by 2035.
Purcell noted that the cost to homeowners of adding an EV plug after a home is built is up to five times higher than roughing it in from the get-go, and said that the standards create "just generally better-quality buildings that will preserve the health and well-being of the residents."
"We're building a city here, not just buildings."
A 2017 cost-benefit analysis done for the city found that the current Toronto Green Standards would add 2.1 to 3.5 per cent to the costs of building low- and highrise multi-unit residential buildings compared to the minimum requirements in the Ontario building code. They would also result in life cycle cost savings of $1.15 to almost $2 per square foot from lower energy bills and other cost reductions over 25 years.
Hanging over this local problem is a global one: climate change. The city has set an ambitious goal of reaching net-zero emissions by 2040, and the Toronto Green Standard has been "the most successful part of their plan, and would really gut their climate action strategy" if lost, said Purcell.
Even with the green standards, however, the city recently acknowledged it was unlikely to meet its 2025 targets. In a budget document released this week the environment and climate division also said the pace of emissions reductions made it unlikely it would meet its 2030 targets, too.
"Losing Toronto's Green Standard would be catastrophic for the city's climate progress," said Sarah Buchanan, campaigns director for the Toronto Environmental Alliance. " We have a province that has largely abdicated from climate action. If cities lose the ability to take action on their own, what do we have left?"
It's unclear to what extent opposition to the Toronto Green Standard is shared among developers. RESCON said it could not share its membership list for privacy reasons.
EllisDon, a major developer involved in residential projects in Toronto, submitted a letter in 2017 in support of a previous version of the Toronto Green Standard, the Toronto Atmospheric Fund noted in a fact-check on its website. It reads, in part: "We believe that the focused priorities and metrics are necessary if we want to transform into a low carbon economy and meet our climate change commitments. EllisDon is prepared to support this standard and framework in its implementation, promotion and awareness."
The company did not respond to emails asking if it supported RESCON's legal challenge.
Tridel, another major developer that frequently touts its green credentials and in a recent press release said that "innovation and sustainability are core to Tridel's mission," sits on the steering committee of the city's Net Zero Transition Study, which will help inform the next generation of the Toronto Green Standard, due to be considered at city hall later this year.
Tridel's executive vice-president of project management innovation and sustainability also sits on the board of RESCON and was previously its chair.
A spokesperson for the company said it will "continue to collaborate with Toronto Green Standards staff and other municipalities, serving as a resource for their policy development. While we support efforts to reduce emissions and enhance energy efficiency, it's essential to balance these goals with policies that maintain consistency across the industry."
With files from Marco Chown Oved
CAPTION: Toronto's Green Standards sets mandatory performance requirements for new buildings for energy efficiency, electric vehicle charging stations, green space and more, as well as higher, voluntary tiers.

Document 58 of 500

Ford has always had mandate to invest in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-has-always-had-mandate-invest-ontario/docview/3156952041/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+has+always+had+mandate+to+invest+in+Ontario&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In response to Donald Trump's threat to impose high tariffs on Canadian products, Premier Doug Ford said he might have to call an early election to secure a mandate to protect Ontario's economy.
That's a bad excuse to call an early election. It's like firefighters saying they must ring the doorbell and ask permission to put out a fire and save the people trapped inside the house.
The current government already has a mandate to invest in Ontario's economy and people. Given the province's stable finances, it also had the means to do so. It simply chooses to stash money away and spend it on vain populist measures.
Take the first years of the COVID-19 pandemic, for example.
Ottawa picked up the tab for 92 per cent of emergency measures, giving provinces the fiscal room to invest in public services people needed more than ever. Instead, at the start of 2021, amid high death counts and economic turmoil, Ontario was sitting on $6.4 billion of unspent COVID emergency funds. The job was obvious. The money was there. But Ford chose not to spend it on Ontarians.
Fast forward to today, and we will see the same pattern.
The provincial government's revenue is anticipated to rise from $205.7 billion in 2024 to $217.4 billion in 2025 - a solid 5.7 per cent increase.
Spending is set to rise by 2.6 per cent, or less than half the revenue increase.
Does the current government have the mandate to expand child-care provision, tackle the colossal school repair backlog, reduce emergency room waiting times and assist the more than 100,000 Torontonians relying on food banks and 80,000 Ontarians experiencing homelessness?
It does.
Yet, that's not the focus. Year in and year out, the Ontario government's attention and dollars have been poured into populist and nonsense measures nobody asked for.
It's a long list. Let's look only at the three most recent ones.
Last May, the Ontario government announced it would spend $225 million to breach a contract with the Beer Store and speed up the expansion of alcohol sales in the province. The measure is expected to result in higher alcohol consumption and lower sales tax revenues.
Last September, Ford vowed to build a traffic tunnel under Highway 401, even though Highway 407 is highly underutilized. The project is already consuming tonnes of planning hours and political attention that could have been invested in things Ontarians actually need.
Last October, the Ford government announced it would send a $200 cheque to every Ontarian, rich and poor, for a total cost of $3.2 billion. Targeting the cash transfers to low-income families and investing the rest in universal public services would have been easy, but less popular.
If the house is on fire, people expect firefighters and majority governments to go in and deal with it. They don't need to ask for a mandate to do what they are supposed to do.
Ricardo Tranjan is a senior researcher on housing and social policy with the
Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives' Ontario office, @ricardotranjan.bsky.social
CREDIT: Ricardo Tranjan Contributor Ricardo Tranjan is a senior researcher on housing and social policy with the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives' Ontario office, @ricardotranjan.bsky.social;

Document 59 of 500

The stakes for Canada are enormous: Economy is vulnerable to a president who is convinced the U.S. is getting a raw deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stakes-canada-are-enormous/docview/3156952020/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+stakes+for+Canada+are+enormous%3A+Economy+is+vulnerable+to+a+president+who+is+convinced+the+U.S.+is+getting+a+raw+deal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: And so we arrive at the cusp.
You can feel it in the city of presidents, this metropolis of neo-Roman edifices that once claimed to lead to the "free world," and is now soon to experience its "Make America Great Again" restoration. The anticipation, the trepidation, the excitement, the dread. A weekend of galas and rallies; celebrants in their signature red hats meandering past the fences and porta potties on the National Mall; fireworks scheduled for Saturday night at Donald Trump's golf course outside of town. And all of it hurtling towards the single, tremulous question.
What is about to happen?
On Monday at noon, the prelude to Trump's second presidency will end at the U.S. Capitol Building his supporters attacked four years ago. He will assume power again at age 78, a convicted criminal whose brash and unpredictable populism is already reviving - and, in some cases, expanding - the anxieties of his first term in office.
Mercantilist rumblings emanating from Mar-a-Lago, the gaudy Florida resort where Trump has held court since winning the November election, have rattled business leaders and politicians around the world. With eyes on Greenland, the Panama Canal and a threat to annex Canada through "economic force," Trump has drawn the contours of a new American imperialism that might be real, but could be bluster.
Hopes of American leadership in the climate crisis are evaporating, as Trump promises to "drill, baby, drill." He has even raised fears for the democracy of the U.S., having sworn vengeance against his political enemies and declared that he will be a dictator, if only for one day.
His re-election has also rewired Canadian politics. In Ottawa and provincial capitals, Trump's threat of punishing 25 per cent tariffs on everything this country sells to the U.S. has spawned a whole genre of public service devoted to figuring out how to deal with him.
When Chrystia Freeland resigned as federal finance minister in a shock-departure that helped trigger Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's own resignation this month, she pointed to Trump's "aggressive economic nationalism" and suggested the government needs to save funds for the trade war that could be coming.
Now the candidates vying to replace Trudeau are positioning themselves as defenders of the nation who can best stand up to Trump, with Liberal leadership contenders reaching for hockey metaphors and name-checking past glories like the victory at Vimy Ridge.
For Jeremy Broadhurst, a veteran Liberal strategist who was a top adviser to Freeland during Trump's first term, the national response to U.S. trade disruptions appears less unified than it was from 2017 to 2021, when almost anyone Trudeau called was eager to help his government. That was a significant help in building a vast network of influence with Americans, which was key in figuring out how to satisfy the Trump administration after it triggered the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), Broadhurst said.
But this time, Alberta has refused to stand with Ottawa and the other provinces and territories, as the Trudeau government refuses to rule out using Canada's lucrative fossil fuel exports to the U.S. as a cudgel to beat back any Trump tariff onslaught.
"There's still time to change," Broadhurst said, describing how he hopes whatever happens rallies political leaders to stand together to confront the tariff threat.
"The stakes for us are obviously huge," he said.
If Trump follows through with 25 per cent tariffs, the Canadian Chamber of Commerce estimates national gross domestic product (GDP) would contract by 2.6 per cent. That's an almost $80-billion chunk out of the national economy, based on the GDP level in 2024 that was reported in December's federal fiscal update. Job losses could be massive.
Last week, the British Columbia government warned that province alone could lose 124,000 jobs due to Trump's tariffs by 2028. According to Statistics Canada, 8.8 per cent of the Canadian workforce - 1.8 million people - is in industries where more than a third of the jobs exist because of exports to the U.S.
It's tangible evidence of Canada's geographic destiny as the northern neighbour to the world's biggest economy, as well as its failure to branch out with deeper trade ties to other regions. Seventy-seven per cent of Canada's exports went to the U.S. in 2023, according to StatCan. Such reliance leaves the country vulnerable to an "America First" president with an attraction to tariffs and a conviction that the U.S. is getting a raw deal out of its relationships with even some of its closest and friendliest partners.
"In a worst-case scenario" the tariffs will be "economically devastating" and could trigger an immediate recession, said Meredith Lilly, a professor at Carleton University who was a foreign affairs and trade adviser to former prime minister Stephen Harper. Canada's economy is already on shaky ground, Lilly said, pointing to lagging productivity, cost-of-living struggles and a recent climb in the unemployment rate.
"We're close to the knife's edge," she said.
David Paterson, Ontario's envoy to Washington who works out of the Canadian Embassy, said he believes the federal government was slow to see the new character of the tariff threat from a restored Trump administration, which appears to be partly motivated by the incoming president's desire to raise funds to pay for the huge tax cuts he has promised.
"I think that there was perhaps a bit of 'past is prologue,' " Paterson said, referring to how the Liberals in Ottawa dealt with Trump on trade negotiations and tariffs during his first term. "Our feeling was that it was something different, and something more," he said.
But the vulnerability isn't just economic. Canada has leaned on U.S. military security for a long time, which has exposed the country to Trump's ire as Canada lags behind most other members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in failing to spend at least two per cent of its GDP on defence, said Kerry Buck, a former diplomat who was Canada's NATO ambassador during Trump's first term.
Buck recalled a meeting in which Trump, who has since suggested the U.S. won't help protect allies that don't spend their share on defence, walked into a closed diplomatic session and said NATO members should spend at least four per cent of GDP on defence. Canada, as of last year, was at 1.37 per cent, according to NATO figures. The government has also not spelled out how to get to two per cent defence spending, though Trudeau has insisted Canada will hit that threshold in 2032.
That's "much too late," Buck said - especially in the age of Trump.
"We have relied overly on the U.S. for decades and decades, assuming they will come to our rescue, and we've underinvested," Buck said. "That has to change."
By this point, however, it's a truism to say that Trump is hard to predict, even when his statements of intent are clear.
On the one hand, Canadians can have the "calmness" that comes with having dealt with Trump before, said Broadhurst, the Liberal strategist. On the other hand, Trump and those around him are better prepared for government this time, and likely to be ready with a team of confirmed cabinet members much earlier than in his first term, Broadhurst added.
Paterson, Ontario's envoy to Washington, agreed it's not clear exactly when or how the incoming administration will impose the tariffs Trump has threatened. But that lack of certainty is part of the Trump playbook - and dealing with it will be a big part of Canadian political life, for as long as he's in power.
"Disruption is the modus operandi of the new president," Paterson said. "We understand that."
And now we wait to see what happens.
CAPTION: President-elect Donald Trump, with wife Melania and son Barron, boards an Air Force airplane in Florida, en route to Washington on Saturday. With eyes on Greenland, the Panama Canal and a threat to annex Canada through "economic force," Trump has drawn the contours of a new American imperialism that might be real, but could be bluster,Alex Ballingall writes.Lynne Sladky The Associated Press
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star

Title: The stakes for Canada are enormous:   Economy is vulnerable to a president who is convinced the U.S. is getting a raw deal

Document 60 of 500

With Trump's return, and our sovereignty to defend, boomers are ready to return to their activist pasts. Age-related impairments be damned!

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-trumps-return-our-sovereignty-defend-boomers/docview/3156951993/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+Trump%27s+return%2C+and+our+sovereignty+to+defend%2C+boomers+are+ready+to+return+to+their+activist+pasts.+Age-related+impairments+be+damned%21&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At a dinner party of three-quarter-lifers last weekend, Donald Trump's economic and possibly tactical invasion of Canada dominated the conversation. After hearing our resistance plans, I'll say this to Trump: you have no idea of the freedom-fighter you've unleashed in Canada's over 60s.
The zesty host, to my right, plotted a subversive cell of himself and four friends, all lifelong backwoods skiers. "We'll dress in white, and with our white hair against the white snow, they'll never see us coming," he said, eyes ablaze. "We'll call ourselves the 69ers."
"Hasn't C put his back out, though?" said the guest to my left. A quick tally of the 69ers revealed one was deaf, one partially-blind, the third had a torn meniscus, and the fourth, trouble standing after a recent fall - while cross-country skiing. "Minor impairments!" said the host. "So long as everybody's in bed with a heating pad by 8 p.m."
One woman said she'd pass rebel messages in her homemade focaccia. My own plan was to talk Canadian. "I was just out and about," I'll say under the glare of the interrogation lamp. "Oot! A booot!" the American's will collapse to the ground screaming." Brown toast! Homo milk! Saskatoon, Saskatchewan! I won't let up, and there's plenty more where those came from on Useful Vocabulary from Canada, eh!
We goofed around at dinner, until we didn't. The leader of the most powerful country in the world, with whom we share the longest international border in the world, says he's coming for us. Our high spirits waned at the dessert course (a delicious pistachio bundt cake), when a deep foreboding took hold of our party.
"Everyone is so sure that Trump is grandstanding, but what if he's not?" said S, forlorn at the far end of the table. "Who could have imagined what they've done to women's rights? It was inconceivable that they could engineer such a backslide." The idea that the world would come to our rescue was also quickly put to rest. People will wear "I 'heart' Canada" T-shirts for a while, then move on to other things. We have vast stores of what everyone wants: water, minerals and energy. And now not one but two superpowers, Russia to the north and the U.S to the south, with a yen for expansion.
"Today's threats to our climate, economy and security are unprecedented," said Emily Hunter, a 40-year-old climate activist in Toronto. I'd called her after the dinner party, and she did not mock me and my companions. Instead, she doubled down on our own superpower.
"What we're seeing right now is a regalvanizing of the boomer activist base from the '60s and '70s." Hunter is intimately acquainted with some of the players from those days: her father is the late Robert Hunter, first president of Greenpeace. "Boomers are concerned about their grandchildren and their children, but also they're part of the counterculture movement that fought for civil rights." Which doesn't mean Hunter's generation, coming of age in a long period of social and political stability, can't teach boomers how best to connect with a culture that's reshaping itself, "from identity politics to the feminist backlash of trad wives," she said.
Hunter's most urgent message, however, crosses generational divides: "Get off of your phones and computers - they're being used against us - and get back to the tried-and-true community tactics. We're stronger together and in person."
Harvard professor Erica Chenoweth, a civil resistance expert, told NPR there was originally "a lot of optimism" about the internet creating virtual civic space under threat by autocratic regimes. But in Myanmar, to use one example, it was used to surveil and repress high-profile activists: they'd shut down the internet to prevent people using it to organize, she said, then turn it back on only long enough for people to see fearful acts of violence against those well-known activists.
My own resistance cell, where we'll go without the internet (how will I check my daily step count or The New York Times top recipes? Sacrifices must be made), includes four Canadian politicians: Doug "come-for-us, we'll-come-for-you" Ford; the super chill former Prime Minister Jean "U.S. will 'suffer much more" Chrétien. "Trump says he doesn't need our electricity," the 91-year-old told CTV with a grin. "But he'll be walking up the Trump Tower by candlelight"; Stephen Harper, who talked tough on American conservative podcast Standpoint. "Our desire as Canadians, to be strong and beside the United States, is hinged on us believing that the U.S. doesn't want to conquer or annex Canada." But if the U.S. continues to threaten our sovereignty, "we will be forced to take a very different approach." And last, not least, Chrystia Freeland, who spat in the eye of Russian President Vladimir Putin in 2022 and did again with Trump as she announced her leadership this week. "If you hit us, we will hit back," she wrote for this paper on Friday. "We will not escalate, but we will never back down." Mark Carney's not invited to join my resistance cell, after he chose to slyly tease his campaign for liberal leadership on Jon Stewart - precisely when we are under threat of annexation from the south. It felt like a smack in the face to Canadians, and, to me, his off-the-cuff humour sounded scripted, especially after his rambling responses to the media questions at his official leadership launch in Edmonton on Thursday.
I was born in St. Catharines, Ont., very near the American border. Our regular Sunday picnic was to Queenston Heights, where we climbed the statue of General Brock. His outstretched arm, successfully fending off American invaders in the war of 1812, was often lopped off after being struck by lightning, but me and my siblings absorbed the message young: Canadians will defend Canada. And we'll win again, too.
I called my daughter, Mary, 31, to see how she was coping. "I will fight for my country, obviously, if it comes to that." (She's her mother's daughter.) "But a leader is only one person." Mary doesn't believe many Americans would follow Trump into a battle with Canada. "Look how Canadians sent our firefighters to L.A. That's what good neighbours do. I have faith that most people want to do the right thing."
Mary could not have known that's also the message of "The Russians Are Coming, The Russians Are Coming," Norman Jewison's slyly subversive 1966 film that came out in the heart of the Cold War. I watched it again, and it's still funny 60 years later, with Alan Arkin, Carl Reiner and Jonathan Winters (all dead now) hamming it up. A Russian submarine has run aground off the coast of a small New England island. Tensions build until the Americans and the Russians are about to open fire on each other, when (spoiler!) a little boy falls from a steep church roof and dangles for life from a high hook. Both sides throw down their weapons and form a human ladder to rescue the boy, Russians and Americans standing on each other's shoulders.
I wept watching. And I hoped Mary was right.
CAPTION: InNorman Jewison's subversive 1966 Cold Ward film'The Russians Are Coming, The Russians Are Coming,' set off the coast of a small New England island, tensions between the Americans and the Russians are defused when a little boy falls from a church roof.
CREDIT: Cathrin Bradbury, Contributor

Title: With Trump's return, and our sovereignty to defend, boomers are ready to return to their activist pasts. Age-related impairments be damned!

Document 61 of 500

Doug Ford always had a mandate to invest in Ontario, he just didn't do his job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-always-had-mandate-invest-ontario-he/docview/3156951987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+always+had+a+mandate+to+invest+in+Ontario%2C+he+just+didn%27t+do+his+job&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In response to Donald Trump's threat to impose high tariffs on Canadian products, Premier Doug Ford said he might have to call an early election to secure a mandate to protect Ontario's economy.
That's a bad excuse to call an early election. It's like firefighters saying they must ring the doorbell and ask permission to put out a fire and save the people trapped inside the house.
The current government already has a mandate to invest in Ontario's economy and people. Given the province's stable finances, it also had the means to do so. It simply chooses to stash money away and spend it on vain populist measures.
Take the first years of the COVID-19 pandemic, for example.
Ottawa picked up the tab for 92 per cent of emergency measures, giving provinces the fiscal room to invest in public services people needed more than ever. Instead, at the start of 2021, amid high death counts and economic turmoil, Ontario was sitting on $6.4 billion of unspent COVID emergency funds. The job was obvious. The money was there. But Ford chose not to spend it on Ontarians.
Fast forward to today, and we will see the same pattern.
The provincial government's revenue is anticipated to rise from $205.7 billion in 2024 to $217.4 billion in 2025 - a solid 5.7 per cent increase. Spending is set to rise by 2.6 per cent, or less than half the revenue increase.
Does the current government have the mandate to expand child care provision, tackle the colossal school repair backlog, reduce emergency room waiting times and assist the more than 100,000 Torontonians relying on food banks and 80,000 Ontarians experiencing homelessness? It does.
Yet, that's not the focus. Year in and year out, the Ontario government's attention and dollars have been poured into populist and nonsense measures nobody asked for.
It's a long list. Let's look only at the three most recent ones.
Last May, the Ontario government announced it would spend $225 million to breach a contract with The Beer Store and speed up the expansion of alcohol sales in the province. The measure is expected to result in higher alcohol consumption and lower sales tax revenues.
Last September, Premier Ford vowed to build a traffic tunnel under Highway 401, even though Highway 407 is highly underutilized. The project is already consuming tonnes of planning hours and political attention that could have been invested in things Ontarians actually need.
Last October, the Ford government announced it would send a $200 cheque to every Ontarian, rich and poor, for a total cost of $3.2 billion. Targeting the cash transfers to low-income families and investing the rest in universal public services would have been easy, but less popular.
If the house is on fire, people expect firefighters and majority governments to go in and deal with it. They don't need to ask for a mandate to do what they are supposed to do.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford at Heddle Shipyards in Hamilton.
CREDIT: Ricardo Tranjan, Contributor

Subject: Emergency medical care; Public services; Populism

Aging boomers are ready to return to their activist pasts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/aging-boomers-are-ready-return-their-activist/docview/3156951968/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Aging+boomers+are+ready+to+return+to+their+activist+pasts&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At a dinner party of three-quarter-lifers last weekend, Donald Trump's economic and possibly tactical invasion of Canada dominated the conversation. After hearing our resistance plans, I'll say this to Trump: you have no idea of the freedom-fighter you've unleashed in Canada's over 60s.
The zesty host, to my right, plotted a subversive cell of himself and four friends, all lifelong backwoods skiers.
"We'll dress in white, and with our white hair against the white snow, they'll never see us coming," he said, eyes ablaze. "We'll call ourselves the 69ers."
"Hasn't C put his back out, though?" said the guest to my left. A quick tally of the 69ers revealed one was deaf, one partially-blind, the third had a torn meniscus, and the fourth, trouble standing after a recent fall - while cross-country skiing.
"Minor impairments!" said the host. "So long as everybody's in bed with a heating pad by 8 p.m."
One woman said she'd pass rebel messages in her homemade focaccia. My own plan was to talk Canadian. "I was just out and about," I'll say under the glare of the interrogation lamp. "Oot! A booot!" the American's will collapse to the ground screaming. Brown toast! Homo milk! Saskatoon, Saskatchewan! I won't let up, and there's plenty more where those came from on Useful Vocabulary from Canada, eh!
We goofed around at dinner, until we didn't. The leader of the most powerful country in the world, with whom we share the longest international border in the world, says he's coming for us. Our high spirits waned at the dessert course (a delicious pistachio bundt cake), when a deep foreboding took hold of our party.
"Everyone is so sure that Trump is grandstanding, but what if he's not?" said S, forlorn at the far end of the table. "Who could have imagined what they've done to women's rights? It was inconceivable that they could engineer such a backslide."
The idea that the world would come to our rescue was also quickly put to rest. People will wear "I 'heart' Canada" T-shirts for a while, then move on to other things. We have vast stores of what everyone wants: water, minerals and energy. And now not one but two superpowers, Russia to the north and the U.S to the south, with a yen for expansion.
"Today's threats to our climate, economy and security are unprecedented," said Emily Hunter, a 40-year-old climate activist in Toronto. I'd called her after the dinner party, and she did not mock me and my companions. Instead, she doubled down on our own superpower.
"What we're seeing right now is a regalvanizing of the boomer activist base from the '60s and '70s." Hunter is intimately acquainted with some of the players from those days: her father is the late Robert Hunter, first president of Greenpeace. "Boomers are concerned about their grandchildren and their children, but also they're part of the counterculture movement that fought for civil rights."
Which doesn't mean Hunter's generation, coming of age in a long period of social and political stability, can't teach boomers how best to connect with a culture that's reshaping itself, "from identity politics to the feminist backlash of trad wives," she said.
Hunter's most urgent message, however, crosses generational divides: "Get off of your phones and computers - they're being used against us - and get back to the tried-and-true community tactics. We're stronger together and in person."
Harvard professor Erica Chenoweth, a civil resistance expert, agrees. There was originally "a lot of optimism" about the internet creating virtual civic space under threat by autocratic regimes, she told NPR. But in Myanmar, to use one example, it was used to surveil and repress high-profile activists: they'd shut down the internet to prevent people using it to organize, she said, then turn it back on only long enough for people to see fearful acts of violence against those well-known activists.
My own resistance cell, where we'll go without the internet (how will I check my daily step count or the New York Times top recipes? Sacrifices must be made), includes four Canadian politicians: Doug "come-for-us, we'll-come-for-you" Ford; the super chill former Prime Minister Jean "U.S. will 'suffer much more" Chrétien. "Trump says he doesn't need our electricity," the 91-year-old told CTV with a grin. "But he'll be walking up the Trump Tower by candlelight"; Stephen Harper, who talked tough on American conservative podcast Standpoint. "Our desire as Canadians, to be strong and beside the United States, is hinged on us believing that the U.S. doesn't want to conquer or annex Canada." But if the U.S. continues to threaten our sovereignty, "we will be forced to take a very different approach." And last, not least, Chrystia Freeland, who spat in the eye of Russian President Vladimir Putin in 2022 and did again with Trump as she announced her leadership this week. "If you hit us, we will hit back," she wrote for this paper on Friday. "We will not escalate, but we will never back down." Mark Carney's not invited to join my resistance cell, after he chose to slyly tease his campaign for Liberal leadership on Jon Stewart - precisely when we are under threat of annexation from the south.
It felt like a smack in the face to Canadians, and, to me, his off-the-cuff humour sounded scripted, especially after his rambling responses to the media questions at his official leadership launch in Edmonton on Thursday.
I was born in St. Catharines, very near the American border. Our regular Sunday picnic was to Queenston Heights, where we climbed the statue of General Brock. His outstretched arm, successfully fending off American invaders in the War of 1812, was often lopped off after being struck by lightning, but me and my siblings absorbed the message young: Canadians will defend Canada. And we'll win again, too.
I called my daughter, Mary, 31, to see how she was coping. "I will fight for my country, obviously, if it comes to that." (She's her mother's daughter.) "But a leader is only one person." Mary doesn't believe many Americans would follow Trump into a battle with Canada. "Look how Canadians sent our firefighters to L.A. That's what good neighbours do. I have faith that most people want to do the right thing."
Mary could not have known that's also the message of "The Russians Are Coming, The Russians Are Coming," Norman Jewison's slyly subversive 1966 film that came out in the heart of the Cold War. I watched it again, and it's still funny 60 years later, with Alan Arkin, Carl Reiner and Jonathan Winters (all dead now) hamming it up. A Russian submarine has run aground off the coast of a small New England island. Tensions build until the Americans and the Russians are about to open fire on each other, when (spoiler!) a little boy falls from a steep church roof and dangles for life from a high hook. Both sides throw down their weapons and form a human ladder to rescue the boy, Russians and Americans standing on each other's shoulders.
I wept watching. And I hoped Mary was right.
Cathrin Bradbury is a
Toronto-based journalist and freelance contributor for the Star. Her forthcoming book "This Way Up" will be published by Penguin Random House Canada this Spring. Reach her via email:
mcbradbury@gmail.com.

Document 63 of 500

le-letters-feature-jan18

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/le-letters-feature-jan18/docview/3157027499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=le-letters-feature-jan18&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TK
It's been distressing to watch as Canadian politics has become increasingly uncivil. Respect and empathy once characterized the political scene in this country. Not so today: for years now, Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre has made a habit of criticizing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau not only for his policies, but also for his character. Is this the new way of doing politics in Canada?
How can we trust Poilievre to lead the country when it seems as if his sole aim has been to demean a sitting prime minister and all his accomplishments? He forgets that Trudeau stood with Canadians during the COVID-19 pandemic while other world leaders dithered. He forgets that Trudeau sent planes to pick up Canadians stranded abroad, giving them hope in their darkest hour.
No prime minister is perfect, but Trudeau tried his best in difficult circumstances. If the Conservatives had truly wanted to help Canadians, they could have worked with the prime minister to find better solutions to the issues, economic and otherwise, facing this country. Instead, they are pushing us toward an early election while offering few ideas to address the rising cost of living, the housing crisis, and other challenges.
Rahma Ahmad, Toronto
TK
In "Justin Trudeau's own walk in the snow launched a historic week in federal politics" the Star provides, among other facts, a list of Trudeau's achievements It is all too clear, however, that said list is painfully short after a decade of government. From basic decency (his COVID-19 response) to too little, too late (his carbon pricing implementation), Trudeau's legacy can be summed up more easily by all that wasn't delivered. Much of the boastful talk heard in 2015 just smacks of cynicism in 2025.
But where do we go from here? We already know we're in for a rough ride, with the resurgence of Donald Trump south of the border and our ecosystem rapidly going up in flames (quite literally).
Conservative Pierre Polievre, the frontrunner, has gained much exposure by unleashing very printable zingers and squarely blaming Trudeau for all the world's evils. His successes on the campaign trail betray, however, the current disengagement from the truth routinely practiced by right-wing parties and his pedigree as a minister in Stephen Harper's own disastrous decade.
That leaves Jagmeet Singh's NDP. Unlike Polievre, Singh has not managed to capitalize on the Liberals' failings, and he has shown a penchant for political opportunism in his hesitancy dropping confidence in Trudeau. He can, however, take credit for achievements the Liberals boasted as their own, like child and dental care, and has shown greater coherence between his claimed goals and his actions. But can he truly reach towards the working class until May?
Ultimately, the choice will be made by Canadian voters. But are we truly paying attention? Can we aim for a future that works for all of us?
Chris Godinho, Mississauga
TK
We once again in this critical time in our history need great leadership now. We have a serious external threat that we can deal with however not if we are a country divided and have leaders that speak so negatively about this great country of ours. This country is not broken. The political rhetoric for election purposes is not welcomed now. Yes we can continue to improve but we are an incredible country. Look at the facts.
To all the parties, Premiers , party leaders and in particular the Federal Opposition Leader, please stop. The stakes are too high. Put Canada first party and personal aspirations second. Look south and see where Party first gets you.
Bob Shelton, Newmarket
TK
I remember a time when politicians were treated with respect in The House Of Commons by the leader of the opposition. Poilievre has no class and is not worthy to lead our great country. I wonder now who Delacourt and Moudakis will debase all the time.
Robert Desjardins, Toronto
TK
I love Alberta. I've lived here over a decade after moving here from the Hammer. I've watched Justin Trudeau attack Alberta mercilessly with devastating consequences. Imagine a Canadian Prime Minister doing everything in his power to destroy the Ontario Auto Industry, wiping it off the map including the thousands jobs that people depend on to feed their families.... Imagine the devastation to the Ontario economy, if there were no more Auto worker's salaries to support the communities like Windsor, Woodstock, Oakville, Brampton, Alliston and Oshawa. Unthinkable? Well Alberta has been living with unthinkable under Justin Trudeau. So I want to know what is "every" Liberal Leadership Candidate's plan to "welcome" Alberta back into Canada? We were never welcome in Trudeau's Canada, so I want to know what will change under your Leadership as a prospective Liberal Leader, if anything, when it comes to Alberta? Thank goodness, we now have the Iron Lady Premier Danielle Smith protecting Alberta. I'm sure Premier Smith wants to know your answers as a Liberal Leadership Candidate, because today in Alberta, we have options.... Alberta, will no longer be a doormat for Liberals to wipe their feet on!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain (AB)

Subject: Political leadership; Prime ministers; COVID-19

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: le-letters-feature-jan18

Document 64 of 500

The second Donald Trump era is set to begin. Here's why the stakes for Canada are enormous

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/second-donald-trump-era-is-set-begin-heres-why/docview/3157027498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+second+Donald+Trump+era+is+set+to+begin.+Here%27s+why+the+stakes+for+Canada+are+enormous&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WASHINGTON, D.C. - And so we arrive at the cusp.
You can feel it in the city of presidents, this metropolis of neo-Roman edifices that once claimed to lead to the "free world," and is now soon to experience its "Make America Great Again" restoration. The anticipation, the trepidation, the excitement, the dread. A weekend of galas and rallies; celebrants in their signature red hats meandering past the fences and porta potties on the National Mall; fireworks scheduled for Saturday night at Donald Trump's golf course outside of town. And all of it hurtling towards the single, tremulous question.
What is about to happen?
On Monday at noon, the prelude to Trump's second presidency will end at the U.S. Capitol Building his supporters attacked four years ago. He will assume power again at age 78, a convicted criminal whose brash and unpredictable populism is already reviving - and, in some cases, expanding - the anxieties of his first term in office.
Mercantilist rumblings emanating from Mar-a-Lago, the gaudy Florida resort where Trump has held court since winning the November election, have rattled business leaders and politicians around the world. With eyes on Greenland, the Panama Canal and a threat to annex Canada through "economic force," Trump has drawn the contours of a new American imperialism that might be real, but could be bluster.
Hopes of American leadership in the climate crisis are evaporating, as Trump promises to "drill, baby, drill." He has even raised fears for the democracy of the U.S., having sworn vengeance against his political enemies and declared that he will be a dictator, if only for one day.
His re-election has also rewired Canadian politics. In Ottawa and provincial capitals, Trump's threat of punishing 25-per-cent tariffs on everything this country sells to the U.S. has spawned a whole genre of public service devoted to figuring out how to deal with him. When Chrystia Freeland resigned as federal finance minister in a shock-departure that helped trigger Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's own resignation earlier this month, she pointed to Trump's "aggressive economic nationalism" and suggested the government needs to save funds for the trade war that could be coming.
Now the candidates vying to replace Trudeau are positioning themselves as defenders of the nation who can best stand up to Trump, with Liberal leadership contenders reaching for hockey metaphors and name-checking past glories like the victory at Vimy Ridge.
For Jeremy Broadhurst, a veteran Liberal strategist who was a top adviser to Freeland during Trump's first term, the national response to U.S. trade disruptions appears less unified than it was from 2017 to 2021, when almost anyone Trudeau called was eager to help his government. That was a significant help in building a vast network of influence with Americans, which was key in figuring out how to satisfy the Trump administration after it triggered the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), Broadhurst said.
But this time, Alberta has refused to stand with Ottawa and the other provinces and territories, as the Trudeau government refuses to rule out using Canada's lucrative fossil fuel exports to the U.S. as a cudgel to beat back any Trump tariff onslaught. The prime minister said Friday that Ottawa is ready to retaliate - with sources describing counter-tariffs that could spread to $150 billion worth of American imports - if Trump acts as promised on Monday after he is sworn in.
"There's still time to change," Broadhurst said, describing how he hopes whatever happens rallies political leaders to stand together to confront the tariff threat.
"The stakes for us are obviously huge," he said.
If Trump follows through with 25-per-cent tariffs, the Canadian Chamber of Commerce estimates national gross domestic product (GDP) would contract by 2.6 per cent. That's an almost $80-billion chunk out of the national economy, based on the GDP level in 2024 that was reported in December's federal fiscal update. Job losses could be massive.
Earlier this week, the British Columbia government warned that province alone could lose 124,000 jobs due to Trump's tariffs by 2028. According to Statistics Canada, 8.8 per cent of the Canadian workforce - 1.8 million people - is in industries where more than a third of the jobs exist because of exports to the U.S.
It's tangible evidence of Canada's geographic destiny as the northern neighbour to the world's biggest economy, as well as its failure to branch out with deeper trade ties to other regions. Seventy-seven per cent of Canada's exports went to the U.S. in 2023, according to StatCan. Such reliance leaves the country vulnerable to an "America First" president with an attraction to tariffs and a conviction that the U.S. is getting a raw deal out of its relationships with even some of its closest and friendliest partners.
"In a worst-case scenario" the tariffs will be "economically devastating" and could trigger an immediate recession, said Meredith Lilly, a professor at Carleton University who was a foreign affairs and trade adviser to former prime minister Stephen Harper. Canada's economy is already on shaky ground, Lilly said, pointing to lagging productivity, cost-of-living struggles and a recent climb in the unemployment rate.
"We're close to the knife's edge," she said.
David Paterson, Ontario's envoy to Washington who works out of the Canadian embassy, said he believes the federal government was slow to see the new character of the tariff threat from a restored Trump administration, which appears to be partly motivated by the incoming president's desire to raise funds to pay for the huge tax cuts he has promised.
"I think that there was perhaps a bit of 'past is prologue'," Paterson said, referring to how the Liberals in Ottawa dealt with Trump on trade negotiations and tariffs during his first term. "Our feeling was that it was something different, and something more," he said.
But the vulnerability isn't just economic. Canada has leaned on U.S. military security for a long time, which has exposed the country to Trump's ire as Canada lags behind most other members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in failing to spend at least two per cent of its GDP on defence, said Kerry Buck, a former diplomat who was Canada's NATO ambassador during Trump's first term.
Buck recalled a meeting in which Trump, who has since suggested the U.S. won't help protect allies that don't spend their share on defence, walked into a closed diplomatic session and said NATO members should spend at least four per cent of GDP on defence. Canada, as of last year, was at 1.37 per cent, according to NATO figures. The government has also not spelled out how to get to two per cent defence spending, though Trudeau has insisted Canada will hit that threshold in 2032.
That's "much too late," Buck said - especially in the age of Trump.
"We have relied overly on the U.S. for decades and decades, assuming they will come to our rescue, and we've underinvested," Buck said. "That has to change."
The questions Trump has raised about American commitment to NATO could also be concerning for Canada's troops stationed in Latvia as part of the alliance's efforts to deter Russian aggression in eastern Europe, said Roy Norton, a former Canadian diplomat who spent years in the U.S. and is now a professor at the University of Waterloo.
If Russian autocrat Vladimir Putin concludes he can invade the Baltics without fear of a conflict with America, that could put Canadian forces on the front lines, Norton said.
Trump's pledge to stage mass deportations of illegal immigrants could also "potentially increase massively" the already unprecedented backlog of refugee claims that Canada is facing, Norton said. On Friday, the New York Times reported that immigration raids could start in the coming week.
Another concern for some is how Trump is expected to weaken U.S. climate action. When last in power, Trump - who has described climate change as a hoax - pulled out of the Paris Agreement, the international accord that is designed to limit the worst extremes of global warming.
This time, Trump is promising to use his authority to increase American fossil fuel production, something that would inevitably lead to increased emissions that cause climate change, said Kathryn Harrison, a political scientist who specializes in climate policy at the University of British Columbia.
"It's discouraging," she said, noting that the U.S. is the second-largest emitter in the world.
By this point, however, it's a truism to say that Trump is hard to predict, even when his statements of intent are clear.
On the one hand, Canadians can have the "calmness" that comes with having dealt with Trump before, said Broadhurst, the Liberal strategist. On the other hand, Trump and those around him are better prepared for government this time, and likely to be ready with a team of confirmed cabinet members much earlier than in his first term, Broadhurst added.
"You're facing a more formidable political opponent at this point," he said.
Paterson, Ontario's envoy to Washington, agreed it's not clear exactly when or how the incoming administration will impose the tariffs Trump has threatened. But that lack of certainty is part of the Trump playbook - and dealing with it will be a big part of Canadian political life, for as long as he's in power.
"Disruption is the modus operandi of the new president," Paterson said. "We understand that."
And now we wait to see what happens.
CAPTION: Organizers on Saturday work to move the inauguration day swearing-in ceremony into the Capitol Rotunda due to frigid weather expected for Monday's ceremony.President-elect Donald Trump, with wife Melania and son Barron, waves on Saturday as they board an Air Force Special Mission airplane in West Palm Beach, Fla., en route to Washington.

Subject: North American Free Trade Agreement; Exports; Fossil fuels; Political leadership; Diplomatic & consular services; Tariffs; International trade; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Economic growth; Prime ministers; Climate change

Ontario's nurse practitioners urge province for clarity on publicly funded care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-nurse-practitioners-urge-province/docview/3156767573/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+nurse+practitioners+urge+province+for+clarity+on+publicly+funded+care&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With about a quarter of Ontarians expected to be without a family doctor by 2026, nurse practitioners are urging the provincial government to create a public funding model for their profession "as soon as possible."The call comes after federal Health Minister Mark Holland told provinces and territories in early January that medically necessary care, whether provided by a doctor or a "physician equivalent," which would include nurse practitioners (NPs), should be covered by public health insurance. NPs are nurses that have completed additional education and can offer many of the same services as family doctors, including treating patients, referring them to specialists, ordering diagnostic tests and writing drug prescriptions. Currently, most NPs in Ontario are salaried. There are more than two dozen NP-led clinics which the province already funds, while many NPs are employed at hospitals, long-term-care homes and community health centres. Some run their own private clinics and charge patients for care, which Holland says the public health system should now pay for if they are providing the same medically necessary care as doctors."Premier Doug Ford and Minister of Health Sylvia Jones must focus on creating a funding model to support nurse practitioners in providing comprehensive care, especially in primary care, because the primary-care crisis is urgent and real," said Lhamo Dolkar, an Oshawa-based nurse practitioner and president of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO). "The time to act is now."Under the Canada Health Act, only medically necessary care provided by physicians and hospitals is considered eligible for coverage by Canada Health Transfer payments to the provinces and territories. Enacted in 1984, the Act does not address care provided by other health-care providers, such as NPs, or contemplate the provision of virtual care. In his long-awaited interpretation of the Act sent to provinces on Jan. 9, Holland said there has been an expansion in the scopes of practice for many health professionals, such as NPs, pharmacists and midwives, who deliver some of the same services as doctors. As such, patients should not be charged out-of-pocket for medically necessary services when those services would otherwise be covered if provided by a doctor, the minister says.Holland is giving the provinces and territories until April 1, 2026, to incorporate the changes into their public health-care plans.Ema Popovic, a spokesperson for Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones, said the health ministry is reviewing Holland's new interpretation "to understand how it will impact the delivery of high-quality, publicly funded care in Ontario and across the country." Last year, Jones warned Holland that the Canada Health Act's silence on non-physician health-care professionals providing medically necessary care created a loophole that some were taking advantage of "knowing there is no legal consequence or risk of getting shut down."In March 2024, the Ontario government announced $110 million in funding to launch an expansion of interprofessional primary-care teams across the province, such as nurse practitioner-led clinics and family health teams."Continuing to allow non-physician providers to operate in fee-charging primary-care clinics will undermine our public investment in these new teams and our expansion of publicly funded primary care across the province," Jones wrote to Holland last April.NP Michelle Acorn, CEO of the Nurse Practitioners' Association of Ontario, said whatever funding model for her profession the province decides on, flexibility is key. This could mean integrating nurse practitioners into OHIP and having a schedule of benefits for insured services provided by the profession; allowing a fee-for-service payment model in which nurse practitioners are paid for each patient visit, much like doctors are paid; or an expansion of NP-led clinics or community health centres in which NP salaries are paid by the province, she says.Acorn also said NPs who provide care virtually should be covered by provincial funding.In recent years, Canada has witnessed the arrival of companies offering for-profit medical care, including care delivered online, in which patients are charged a fee to access primary care from both doctors and NPs. Holland's office said the minister's new interpretation of the Canada Health Act leaves it up to the provinces and territories to decide if virtual health services are incorporated into their public health plans. "If people have an opportunity to connect with their primary-care provider, whether that's an NP, physician or NPs that do specialization, it shouldn't matter how," Acorn said. "People have a choice of what provider they want to see. They should also have a choice of how they wish to be seen."
CAPTION: Lhamo Dolkar, an Oshawa-basednurse practitionerand president of theRegistered Nurses' Association of Ontario, says "the primary-care crisis is urgent and real," and the "time to act is now."

Everyone benefits from Toronto bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/everyone-benefits-toronto-bike-lanes/docview/3156767467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Everyone+benefits+from+Toronto+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Who actually uses Toronto's bike lanes - and who really benefits from them? Jan. 14
In his column about winter cycling in Toronto, Martin Regg Cohn appears to make several points - but what those points might be remains a mystery, even after a careful reading.
Cohn assures us that he will support his article with "facts and stats." Then he uses a single figure for citywide bicycle commuting that neither segregates out winter cycling nor distinguishes between roads with and without bike lanes. How many people risk cycling on arterials such as Bathurst Street, Lawrence Avenue or Islington Avenue in the absence of bike lanes?
He also tells us - without data - that it's mostly food couriers using bike lanes in the winter, as if this were a compelling argument against them. If bike lanes didn't exist, we'd still have bike couriers, but they would be deprived of their safety. As to why many couriers are on heavy e-bikes, ask Premier Doug Ford: he's the one who's dropped the ball on proper regulation. And if Cohn is truly concerned about the exploitation of food couriers, he should push the Ford government to deem these vulnerable workers employees instead of independent contractors without rights.
Cycling is less popular in Toronto during the winter than in other seasons, though there's still substantial participation where good infrastructure exists. As I write this letter, the temperature outside is -7 C, yet the Bloor Street bike lane is well-used by commuters.
Perhaps if Cohn is concerned about funding for city programs, he might ask how many millions of dollars are spent and thousands of tonnes of toxic salt spread on city roads each year to clear the way for motorists in single-occupant cars. This would be more productive than challenging small investments in bike lanes that allow thousands of Torontonians to get around our city in an affordable, safe and healthy way - and many others to remain in the warmth of their homes waiting for a courier to deliver hot food.
Albert Koehl, Toronto
Advice for when you're on the ice
'No ice is safe ice': Police, safety advocates offer advice after a fatal Toronto Islands skating accident
I was profoundly saddened to read about the tragic drowning of the 4man who fell through the ice while skating on Lake Ontario near the Toronto Islands.
Every year, when the weather turns cold enough, many residents of this city seem to develop wild-ice fever. Authorities have made efforts to stop Torontonians from skating on patches of ice in High Park, in the Rouge River Estuary, and near the Islands, but perhaps it's in our DNA to seek out the joyful and liberating experience of gliding along the city's frozen waterways. It's a part of our culture, and it's delightful.
The article does well to say that "no ice is safe ice" while still providing safety tips to those readers who nevertheless can't get enough of it. Yet one critical piece of safety gear was not mentioned: ice picks worn around the shoulders, which make it much easier to slide back onto an ice shelf if one has fallen through. I don't venture out onto the ice without them, regardless of how cold it has been and for how long.
Mark Schemeit, Toronto
Would Singh invest in Canada's military?
Jagmeet Singh calls for aggressive response to Donald Trump's proposed tariffs, Jan. 13
I'm so pleased to hear that federal NDP leader Jagmeet Singh is calling for an "aggressive response" to U.S. President-elect Donald Trump's tariff threats. Should I therefore take it that he supports meeting Canada's NATO commitments by investing in our military?
Bob Ciupa, Niagara Falls
The view from south of the border
As an American, I am disturbed and embarrassed to see our president-elect make insulting comments about Canada, our much-loved neighbour. I do hope that Canadians won't come to think he represents the sentiment of all or even most Americans. We love Canada and Canadians, and no one I know subscribes to this ridiculous idea. It is beyond belief to think that Canadians would want their sovereign nation to become a U.S. state. Canada is its own beautiful and wonderful country, and it has no reason to want anything other than a continuation of its storied history. This is just one of many of Donald Trump's unhinged schemes to grab attention: that's all he seems to care about. Please accept my apologies for this nonsense. Sadly, there will be more of it over the next four years.
Ralph Cooke, Elkin, North Carolina
Canada-as-state a threat to Republican Senate
Wyoming is the least populous of the United States, with 584,057 residents as of 2023. That makes it smaller than eight Canadian provinces. If we were to add the populations of the two remaining provinces, Newfoundland and Labrador and Prince Edward Island, they would exceed that of Wyoming. If we were forced to join the United States one day, that would easily add nine states on top of the 50 that exist now. As a consequence, we would presumably send 18 senators to the Senate. I am not sure that President-elect Donald Trump would appreciate this "socialist" contingent from the north, given the Republicans are currently struggling to maintain their Senate majority.
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
Where is Charles when you need him?
Where is our King? Where is Charles III? With U.S. President-elect Donald Trump threatening to take over Canada and turn it into the 51st state, our own head of state is nowhere to be seen. He has not had a single word to say in Canada's defence. I am not suggesting that Charles put forward some sort of policy statement, but he must publicly defend the sovereignty of this country. If he cannot stand up for us, then why do we need him at all?
Michael Liotta, Toronto

Where does Canada go from here?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-does-canada-go-here/docview/3156767318/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+does+Canada+go+from+here%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's own walk in the snow launched a historic week in federal politics, Jan. 12
In "Justin Trudeau's own walk in the snow launched a historic week in federal politics," the Star provides a list of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's achievements - but after a decade in government, that list is painfully short. Whether it's a lack of basic decency (his lacklustre response to COVID-19, for example) to too-little-too-late policymaking (his implementation of carbon pricing), Trudeau's legacy can be more easily summed up by all that his government failed to deliver. Much of the boastful talk Canadians heard from him in 2015 is remembered with cynicism in 2025.
But where do we go from here? We know we're in for a rough ride over the next few years, given the resurgence of President-elect Donald Trump south of the border and the fact that our ecosystem is quite literally going up in flames.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre, the frontrunner in Canada's coming federal election, has gained much exposure by unleashing printable zingers and blaming Trudeau for all the world's evils. Yet his successes on the campaign trail betray the disengagement from truth typical of many of today's right-wing parties.
Then there's Jagmeet Singh and the NDP. Unlike Poilievre, Singh has not managed to capitalize on the Liberals' failings. He also showed an unfortunate penchant for political opportunism in his hesitancy to drop confidence in Trudeau. Singh can take credit for some achievements the Liberals touted as their own, such as child care and dental care - and his actions more closely align with his stated goals than do Poilievre's. But can he reach the working class and keep their attention until May?
Ultimately, Canadian voters will make their choice. That said, are we truly paying attention? Can we find a path forward that works for all of us?
Chris Godinho, Mississauga
Politicians should put country over party
Once again, at a critical time in our history, Canadians need great leadership. In U.S. President-elect Donald Trump, we face a serious external threat. It's a threat that we can deal with, but not if we are divided and beset with leaders who speak so negatively about this great country of ours. Canada is not broken, and political rhetoric to that effect, leveraged for election purposes, is not welcome. Yes, we can improve, but we are an incredible country. Look at the facts.
To all the parties, premiers, and party leaders - particularly federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre - please stop. The stakes are too high. Put Canada first, party and personal aspirations second. You only have to look south to see where a party-first mentality gets you.
Bob Shelton, Newmarket
Alberta is no Liberal doormat
I love Alberta. I moved here from Hamilton more than a decade ago. Since then, I've watched Prime Minister Justin Trudeau attack Alberta mercilessly and with devastating consequences. Imagine a Canadian prime minister doing everything in his power to destroy Ontario's auto industry, to wipe it off the map and eliminate thousands of jobs that people depend on to feed their families. Imagine the devastation to Ontario's economy if there were no more autoworker salaries to support communities such as Windsor, Woodstock, Oakville, Brampton, Alliston, and Oshawa. Unthinkable? Well, Alberta has been living with the unthinkable under Trudeau. That's why I want to know what every Liberal leadership candidate's plan is to welcome Alberta back into the country. Many of us never felt as if we belonged in Trudeau's Canada, so I want to know how that will change under new leadership. Thank goodness we have Premier Danielle Smith protecting Alberta. This province will no longer be a doormat for the Liberals to wipe their feet on.
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain (AB)
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement of his pending resignation outside Rideau Cottage in Ottawa on Jan.6, 2025.

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin
