Chow's tax hike is on the money

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chows-tax-hike-is-on-money/docview/3156387455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow%27s+tax+hike+is+on+the+money&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As my city chores increase, I tire. Bring on the property tax increase, Jan. 15
Good for Heather Mallick for explaining to those who don't get it that paying $500 to repair a leaky roof today is better than paying $25,000 to replace it five years from now. We're lucky to have these smart women in our midst. They're gutsy and know the value of a dollar.
Gail Rutherford, Toronto
I am happy to pay a few dollars more per week for a city that is healthier and works better for all. Maintaining a smooth-running transit system is expensive, as is providing housing for those who need it. I am ashamed that so many Torontonians are compelled to live in tents in Canada's wealthiest city. Responsibility for transit and housing has been downloaded to the city by successive provincial and federal governments looking to cut their deficits. We need a share of the federal income tax to be directed to the city to help us fund these urgent needs. In the meantime, I think we should all pay a bit more. I can cover the increase with the carbon rebate we'll be receiving this week. So let's pay the additional 6.9 per cent and build a city we can be proud of.
Lyn Adamson, Toronto
I, for one, am not as excited about higher property taxes as Heather Mallick is. So I have a proposal for her: I visit her next December, rake in hand, and clean the final blast of fallen leaves from the street in front of her home - and she pays the increase to my 2025 tax bill. Deal?
Richard Kadziewicz, Toronto
Homelessness a growing crisis
Last week, I visited the Royal Ontario Museum and was surprised to see two tents near Museum Station. To me, it was a reminder that all levels of governments have failed to provide housing for some of our neighbours - and we can surely agree that a tent in a park is not an appropriate place to live.
On January 9, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario reported that a survey of its members documented a minimum of 80,000 people in Ontario who are "known to be homeless." There is no question that homelessness is a growing humanitarian crisis in this province.
We know that the solution to homelessness is to provide homes to people who need them. We cannot solve this crisis through individual action, although we can care for individuals on a short-term basis. We can end homelessness only through collective action - that is, through our governments. The City of Toronto, the Province of Ontario and the Government of Canada need to reinstate programs that once built and maintained housing, in the days before homeless encampments popped up in municipalities across Ontario and Canada.
"Overcoming poverty is not a gesture of charity," Nelson Mandela once said. "It is an act of justice. It is the protection of a fundamental human right, the right to dignity and a decent life." His words are as true today as they were when he first spoke them 20 years ago.
There may be economic advantages to this collective solution, too. Finland, for example, has nearly eliminated homelessness - and officials there estimate that the Finnish people have saved almost 32 million euros annually, mainly through reduced health-care costs. Just imagine what that could mean here in Canada.
Our budgets for housing programs must increase across all three levels of government. Together, we can make life better for everyone.
Allan Baker, Toronto
Canada must look beyond U.S.
Premier Doug Ford says that Ontario could lose 500,000 jobs if President-elect Donald Trump were to impose his threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods entering the United States. Add to this the 334,000 manufacturing jobs lost between 1988 and 1994 due to the Canada-U.S. Free Trade Agreement (according to the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives), and it turns out that trade with the U.S. is not necessarily the basket we should be placing all our eggs in. A better policy would be to direct our goods and services to domestic markets, as well as non-U.S. markets in Africa, Europe, Asia, and the Pacific Rim, including China. Canadians cannot afford to be so closely tied to a completely unreliable neighbour.
Danny Heap, Toronto
Trump's tariffs are not primarily a negotiating tactic. He has broadcast his true rationale many times, boasting that tariffs will create a massive influx of revenue to the U.S. Treasury from foreign countries - and after 10 years of indoctrination, his MAGA faithful appear believe this transparent lie.
In fact, every economist (and most thinking people) know that the tariffs will effectively serve as a national sales tax on all Americans, one that will pay for Trump's planned tax cuts.
Trump is sly and amoral, but he's not stupid. He understands that trade deficits are not about subsidies, and he knows that our border isn't a major issue. He greets supplicating Canadian politicians, pretending to listen to their anti-tariff arguments purely for show - all for the benefit of himself and his billionaire friends. Grifters grift.
Buckle up, Canada. Rough ride ahead.
Jim Lang, Toronto
A very personal rejection
Michelle Obama will skip Trump inauguration, but ex-Presidents Obama, Clinton and Bush will be there, Jan. 14
Michelle Obama has my complete respect. If she sticks to her decision not to attend President-elect Donald Trump's inauguration, she will be acting in support of all that's decent in this world - in support of the rights of women, minority groups and immigrants, in support of simple respect for others. By refusing to celebrate the election of a convicted felon to the presidency, she will rise above those who choose not to stand with her. The Bidens, the Bushes and the Clintons, to name a few, should stay home. Obama is merely giving Donald Trump what he deserves: a very personal rejection.
Rochelle Carrière Hatton, Sudbury
Gaza ceasefire affects us all
'Tears of joy' at Gaza ceasefire, but protesting groups in Canada say they won't stop, Jan. 15
I heard the wonderful news about the ceasefire in Gaza. It has been 15 months since we learned that peace had left that corner of the world. Islam teaches us that humanity is like a single body: if one part feels pain, then the entire body experience distress.
As an Ahmadi Muslim, it has been deeply painful for me and my community to watch as this war has marred our beautiful world. The impact of war in any country is felt in every country. It harms the global economy and creates emotional distress.
Both Palestine and Israel are wise to have brought an end to this war. I hope the ceasefire lasts forever - and that no shadow of sorrow ever falls upon Gaza again.
Khalida Iffat, Hamilton

Document 79 of 500

PM blasts Poilievre and Smith: Trudeau and all but one premier agree to present a united front against Trump's tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-blasts-poilievre-smith/docview/3156385532/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+blasts+Poilievre+and+Smith%3A+Trudeau+and+all+but+one+premier+agree+to+present+a+united+front+against+Trump%27s+tariff+threat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau slammed Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith for not joining a common Canadian opposition to incoming U.S. president Donald Trump's threatened tariffs, just days before a potential trade war erupts.
Trudeau and 12 of 13 premiers agreed on Wednesday to form a united front and get behind a pledge that "everything" is on the table in Ottawa's effort to fight a potential tariff war, including restrictions on, or higher costs for, Canadian oil and gas shipped to the U.S.
But Poilievre, who polls suggest could become prime minister in the next federal election, repeatedly refused Thursday to say whether Canada's energy exports should be part of a Canadian retaliatory strategy.
Poilievre's dodge came after Alberta's leader broke ranks with her provincial counterparts and Trudeau and refused to support any plan that did not have a carve-out for oil and gas.
Trudeau, speaking in Windsor on Thursday, said he doesn't "blame" Smith for advocating for her province's interests, saying it's "important" that all premiers do. "But," he said, "every single premier other than Danielle Smith then chose to put Canada first and say ... we're all part of Team Canada."
"All Canadians" stood up for Alberta when Canadian taxpayers funded the purchase of Trans Mountain pipeline expansion to "get Albertan oil to new markets," Trudeau said. "So, yes, premiers should be advocating for their own industries ... their own communities, but they should also put their country first," Trudeau said.
The Liberal leader said he has clashed with conservative premiers like Doug Ford, Saskatchewan's Scott Moe, and Nova Scotia's Tim Houston, or with New Democrats David Eby and Wab Kinew and others.
"But when push comes to shove, every single one of them are Canadians first," said Trudeau.
He then pivoted to say it is up to Poilievre "to say whether he is part of Team Canada, along with all premiers and the federal government, or whether he will stand with Danielle Smith, Kevin O'Leary and, of course, Donald Trump."
Trudeau touted an outside advisory council on Canada-U.S. relations that he named on Thursday and the efforts of other business and labour leaders who are making Canada's case.
Trump's proposed 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian products represent a "declaration of economic war" on Canada, said B.C. Premier David Eby, as provinces warned of massive impacts on jobs and sectors important to the economies of their regions.
But in B.C., Poilievre repeatedly refused to answer direct questions on his stance about energy exports, and his office did not reply to an emailed request from the Star.
At a news conference in Delta, Poilievre said the first thing Trump should do "is to send a big bouquet of flowers to the Liberal government in Ottawa."
"By blocking pipelines and LNG plants in Canada, the Liberals have forced Canadians to sell almost all of our energy to the United States, giving President Trump massive leverage in making these tariffs threats."
Poilievre blasted the outgoing Trudeau - and presumptive Liberal leadership front-runners Chrystia Freeland and Mark Carney - saying they "weakened and divided our country and have given (Trump) the upper hand."
"I will quickly approve energy projects to send our energy to the rest of the world without relying on Americans, and we will make our country more self-reliant and independent," Poilievre said.
He repeated that answer when asked what he made of Smith's decision to bail out of the hoped-for display of unity among the country's political leaders.
"We have, effectively, 10 different foreign affairs ministers, one from each province, trying to fight separately," Poilievre said.
At one point, Poilievre challenged a question on energy tariffs, saying he didn't understand it because "we don't import almost any oil and gas from the Americans, at least raw oil and gas. We mostly export it."
Canada's energy regulator says the U.S. remains the top destination for Canadian crude oil, but also says the U.S. is the largest source of Canada's imported crude oil, with 72.4 per cent of Canada's oil imports originating from the U.S. that same year.
In Mississauga, Ford said he is taking "a different approach" than Smith.
"I respect Minister (sic) Smith, but I just believe Canada comes first," Ford said Thursday, noting he isn't putting Ontario's auto sector or agri-food businesses ahead of the national interest and neither are most other premiers.
"So everyone has something that they're saying, 'we may have to sacrifice a little bit,' " he said.
"I'll just leave it at ... country comes first."
Smith pushed back at her critics on X, writing: "How about this for building a 'Team Canada' approach?"
"Stop threatening the livelihoods of tens of thousands of Albertans & Canadians via an energy export tax or ban."
She listed four other demands: construction of pipelines to diversify exports to Europe, Asia and domestically; more aggressive marketing of Canadian oil and gas; repeal of federal laws that Smith has long argued kill jobs and resource projects; and respect for Alberta taxpayers "that have been transferring hundreds of billions to Quebec, the Maritimes and even Ontario, for decades."
Former central banker Carney, who declared Thursday he is running to replace Trudeau as Liberal leader and prime minister, agreed in part with Poilievre when he was asked about Smith's rejection of Trudeau's strategy. Carney said it's important to build "the strongest economy right here in Canada" to counter Trump.
But he also stressed that, "All of us need to stand up for Canada. We need to be united. I've done a lot of negotiation over the years. I've dealt with President Trump in a different setting, when he was previously president. He's a negotiator. He's transactional."
Carney said he, too, would take nothing off the table: "In a negotiation you don't show your cards," he said. "You don't take cards off the table. You stay united."
With a file from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and 12 of 13 premiers agreed on Wednesday that "everything" is on the table in Ottawa's effort to fight a potential trade war with the U.S., including oil and gas exports.Nathan Denette THE CANADIAN PRES
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star With a file from Robert Benzie

Watchdog won't probe complaint on Therme: NDP still questions 'shady deal' for spa at Ontario Place

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/watchdog-wont-probe-complaint-on-therme/docview/3156385506/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Watchdog+won%27t+probe+complaint+on+Therme%3A+NDP+still+questions+%27shady+deal%27+for+spa+at+Ontario+Place&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's integrity commissioner says he will not investigate an NDP complaint that the planned Therme spa at Ontario Place got "preferential treatment" from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Citing "insufficient grounds" for a probe into the actions of Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, commissioner J. David Wake released a 15-page report Thursday following the request from New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles last October.
Stiles alleged that Surma breached the Members' Integrity Act, which is ethics legislation governing MPPs, in regard to "events connected to the Ontario Place revitalization project, including negotiation and approval of a long-term agreement with Therme Group Canada."
The integrity commissioner noted Surma, the MPP for Etobicoke Centre, was not the minister when the deal was struck, with negotiations taking place from July 2020 to April 2022, when the government approved final terms of a lease for the spa site on the west island.
"Ministers are not responsible for the actions of their predecessors," Wake wrote. "Minister Surma ... did not have responsibility for Ontario Place until Aug. 29, 2022."
He added "Ms. Stiles has made no specific allegation as to how Minister Surma, after she became the minister responsible for Ontario Place, improperly furthered Therme's interest."
In Timmins on Thursday, Stiles said her party will continue digging into the government's dealings with Austria-based Therme.
"He's not going to dig any further at this moment," she said of Wake. "I just want to say that doesn't change the reality of this shady deal. Ontarians know there's something not right about this.
"Many questions still remain."
Surma thanked the integrity commissioner for his review, and said "we are proud of the work we are doing for the taxpayers of this province and will continue to build Ontario."
The government signed a 95-year lease for Therme to locate at the Ontario Place site, which will also see a water park and new Ontario Science Centre built.
Ontario Place was shuttered more than a decade ago after its attractions declined in popularity. Several groups have opposed the new development plans, saying it is public space that should be preserved for public use and not hived off to a private company.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 81 of 500

Can Toronto get a new housing deal before Canada gets a new government?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/can-toronto-get-new-housing-deal-before-canada/docview/3156385256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Toronto+get+a+new+housing+deal+before+Canada+gets+a+new+government%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, member of Parliament for Beaches-East York, was sworn in as Minister of Housing, Infrastructure, and Communities last December, it was surely a mixed blessing. The rising-star MP was finally getting a cabinet portfolio. But he was being tasked with managing one of the toughest problems in Canada on a timeline that, depending on the next election, could be incredibly short.
But Erskine-Smith aims to make the most of things. In an interview that has been edited for clarity and length, he spoke about the Trudeau government's successes and failures on the housing front, and why he's looking forward to the months ahead, despite the challenges his party - and country - now face.
The federal government announced its national housing strategy in 2017, with the intention of reducing the number of people in housing need. Since then, that number has increased by 660,000. What went wrong?
With the national housing strategy there was, for the first time in decades, a reassertion of the federal role in housing. Was it sufficient to address the scale of the problem? No. We've got to double down. But the federal government can't act alone. We need provincial leadership. We're seeing success in some provinces, but Ontario is dragging the country down when it comes to housing starts.
Is it your opinion that the national housing strategy was doing the right things but at a scale that was incommensurate with the problem?
I would add that the 2017 version of the strategy was missing components. The original plan was focused on providing low-interest loans [to repair or build affordable homes]. You've got to marry that loans program, though, with significant grants, specifically for non-profit housing. Recently, we've expanded the loans programs. We've also added the Housing Accelerator Fund to address red tape.
The Housing Accelerator Fund is money given to cities if they meet certain benchmarks in terms of red-tape reduction, yes?
Exactly. This was not part of the 2017 federal agenda but is now.
Recently, there's also been a push to better use federally owned land - land that has, maybe, a two-storey post office or a parking lot on it.
We want to unlock those lands for housing.
Unlocking, I presume, means making the land available, perhaps at low cost or no cost, so that developers will build affordable or community housing?
Yes. This is where I see a contrast between our approach and what you hear from the federal Conservatives. They want to sell federal land off to the highest bidder. We want to partner with actors who are committed to affordability.
When it comes to housing, it seems, you want government to be both more and less involved. Fair?
Pierre Poilievre thinks the government should get out of the way entirely. I would say, no, the government should get out of the way specifically when there are barriers to the market building for the market. There are pain points - exclusionary zoning, excessive development charges - that must be removed. But the market is not going to deliver affordable housing, social housing, or community housing. So the government needs to reassert itself in those areas.
Your predecessor, Sean Fraser, made a comment in a Bloomberg interview, which seemed to imply that we can, somehow, make housing more affordable without undermining the value of people's existing homes. I felt like he was evading the tension between the interests of people seeking to get into the market (who benefit when prices come down) versus people already in the market (who benefit when prices stay high).
It's not the government's job to say that a home should be a certain price, but we should be concerned when home prices run away from incomes. There is a generational unfairness to the housing market.
But the unfairness benefits certain people. Those people are voters. Many are Liberal voters. One question on my mind is: Why have governments been slow to address the housing crisis? One possible answer is that there are political incentives to move slowly. A lot of voters are, frankly, happy with the way things are.
I'm not going to disagree that there are competing interests in the housing market. But I think the question of "what price should a home be?" is a distraction. It's not the government's job to protect a certain amount of equity that has built up in a person's home.
One criticism of programs that make it easier to buy a home is that they're inflationary. If you're not paying the land-transfer tax because you're a first-time home buyer - or if you only need a 5 percent down payment instead of, say, 20 percent - then you have more financial leverage. You can bid higher, thereby driving prices up. Do you agree that these demand-side policies are counter-productive?
These policies tend to be put in place because they're good politics. But if you're solutions-oriented, you should be driving supply. As housing minister, I'm not going to push demand-driven policies.
You may not be housing minister for long. What can you do with the time you have?
There are three big categories. One is the Affordable Housing Fund, [which offers low-interest and forgivable loans to partnered organizations] and the Apartment Construction Loan Program. I'm leaning into those programs to prioritize social and non-profit housing and to get as many conditional approvals in place. The second piece is the $6 billion Housing Infrastructure Fund. You can think of it as the Housing Accelerator Fund 2.0. It's focused on delivering for municipalities and provinces when they're reducing red tape and stabilizing development charges. Ontario's share is $2 billion. If we can't get a deal done with Ontario, we're going to do deals directly with municipalities. The third piece is around transit. I'm hoping to land a bigger deal with Toronto, and with other municipalities and provinces on transit, especially where there are ambitious proposals ready to go.
You've commented recently that the number one government priority is US-Canada relations. Housing is number two. But those issues are linked. A trade war could make housing construction more expensive at the exact moment we're trying to make it cheaper.
A trade war is linked with all sorts of negative outcomes for our economy. We have to squarely focus our efforts on responding to the tariff threat - we have to make sure there are retaliatory measures in place - because it's a threat across the board to productivity. But another threat to productivity is housing.
Can you explain why housing is a productivity issue?
If you can't attract talented people because housing is too expensive, that's a problem. Also, we need to be treating housing as a home first and investment second. I want to encourage investment dollars to come in to build housing. But it's an unproductive use of investment dollars if people are just competing in the residential resale space.
The first time Trump was elected and there were threats of a trade war, the Trudeau government had a coordinated response. Seemingly everybody was talking with US business leaders and Republican operatives and state governors to counter Trump's threat. Do you feel like the government is in a position to put up a united front again?
The prime minister has done this before, the government has done this before, and they're ready to respond as necessary. But the difference is the timing of our electoral cycles. We're facing repeated calls for an election from the Conservative opposition leader, who doesn't want to be seen working with Trudeau at all.
To achieve a unified front, should the Liberals have acted faster to install a new leader and call an election?
Imagine we were in an election now. Do you think that would add stability or instability? I think it would add instability. And having stability, especially heading into the inauguration, is important for the same reason that it was important that the prime minister didn't step down as prime minister. If he'd done that, we would have an interim leader who wouldn't be able to hit the ground running. We're going to have an election this year no matter what. Is it ideal in the context of the Trump tariffs? No. But that's the world we live in.
Given the Trump threat, do you think ministers should not be running for party leader?
If I were a minister who was running in the leadership race, I would resign my portfolio.
I guess you're not running then.
I am not.
Can the government respond to Trump without a parliament?
If you look at the first iteration of the response to Trump, it was a government response that was coordinated across party lines. It was not a parliamentary response. It will be important if the tariffs drag on into the spring- and if that response is not working - that there's an accountability function for parliament to play. In the meantime, the government continues to govern.
What's the mood like in the party now?
There's a sense of optimism that this moment can be a reset. The timing is going to be short between the leadership race and the general election. So anyone who's running for leadership has to think of this as one big race. They are introducing themselves to the Canadian public. There is an opportunity here to have a campaign of ideas, to establish renewal. There's more optimism in the party than there's been in a while. Whether it translates into better outcomes remains to be seen.
CAPTION: Nathaniel Erskine-Smith pictured in conversation with reporter Simon Lewsen at his constituency office on Danforth Ave.
CREDIT: Simon Lewson Contributor

Document 82 of 500

Ford hints he may need new 'mandate': Premier says province may have to spend billions to save economy if Trump's tariffs materialize

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-hints-he-may-need-new-mandate/docview/3156385226/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+hints+he+may+need+new+%27mandate%27%3A+Premier+says+province+may+have+to+spend+billions+to+save+economy+if+Trump%27s+tariffs+materialize&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wait and see.
That's the message from Premier Doug Ford on whether he'll take Ontario into a snap winter election.
It all depends on what U.S. president-elect Donald Trump does with his threat to put 25 per cent tariffs on goods imported from Canada, which Ford has warned could throw as many as 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
"We may have to put tens of billions of dollars into the economy to keep people moving, keep people afloat," Ford said after a speech Thursday to the Mississauga Board of Trade.
"And to be very frank, I do not have the mandate to spend tens of billions of dollars right now," he added in a question-and-answer session with board president Trevor McPherson.
"We are going to feel a few bumps on the road," the premier warned.
Trump begins his second term Monday and Canadian officials will be watching closely to see if tariffs are in an expected flurry of executive orders.
"We'll see what happens on Jan. 20 or 21. I think he's going to come out of the gate real quick and start hitting us with tariffs," said Ford, who has already promised to spend $3 billion by sending $200 "tax rebate" cheques to most Ontarians in coming days.
The premier's two-term Progressive Conservatives enjoy a strong lead over opposition parties in public opinion polls as Ford contemplates an early election that would cost $175 million.
Ontario's next scheduled election is not until June 2026 - almost 17 months away - prompting rivals to charge he risks plunging Ontario into uncertainty at a critical time and when the federal government is in flux following the resignation of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
He has been pondering an expedited provincial vote since last May, long before the prospect of any Trump levies.
Earlier Thursday in Port Credit, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie launched a fresh attack at Ford's election gambit in the face of the threatened Trump tariffs.
She cited long lineups of people to sign up for a new family doctor in Walkerton on Wednesday amid a worsening shortage that has left at least 2.5 million Ontarians without a physician.
"We saw the outrageous reality in Walkerton yesterday as hundreds of people lined up in the wee hours of the morning, in freezing cold weather and in snow, bundled up in their parkas," Crombie said.
"Bringing with them their walkers, some in wheelchairs, desperately trying to get a spot with the new doctor who was taking new patients in Ontario. This shouldn't be happening."
As the Star reported, people at the front of the line waited more than six hours before the doors were opened at the local branch of the Canadian Legion, desperate to be one of the first 500 who could join the medical practice at the South Bruce Grey Health Centre.
It's a sign of the malaise under Ford, said Crombie, who also cited queues at food banks, tent encampments in communities across the province, affordability problems and rising unemployment rates.
"People are feeling really anxious right now about the future of Ontario, the threat of tariffs coming in from Donald Trump is real and rightly has people on edge," the former mayor of Mississauga added, noting the potential job losses Ford himself has mentioned.
"This is Doug's opportunity to call an election to make sure that his job is safe while your job is on the line. Doug Ford is looking for any excuse to call an early election to get ahead of an RCMP investigation on the Greenbelt scandal."
That's a reference to the Mounties' ongoing criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles also raised the Walkerton lineups during a campaign-style stop in Timmins on Thursday.
"This is the 'Hunger Games' now everywhere across Ontario," she told supporters in the riding her party lost to Ford's Tories in the 2022 election. Timmins had been an NDP stronghold until then.
Both Stiles and Crombie say they support Ford's plan for a multibillion-dollar economic stimulus package to offset any impact from tariffs that could be imposed.
"We should all be sitting down together and looking at this," Stiles said in a signal to Ford.
"The NDP will be with you on that 100 per cent. So there is no excuse for this premier to go to the polls early. It's just about saving his job," she said. "What we don't need now is more instability and insecurity in this province."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has been pondering an expedited provincial vote since last May, long before the prospect of any Trump administration levies. Ontario's next election is scheduled for June 2026.Justin Tang The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 83 of 500

Doug Ford signals he may need a new 'mandate' from voters as Donald Trump tariffs loom

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-signals-he-may-need-new-mandate-voters/docview/3156624600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+signals+he+may+need+a+new+%27mandate%27+from+voters+as+Donald+Trump+tariffs+loom&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wait and see.
That's the message from Premier Doug Ford on whether he'll take Ontario into a snap winter election.
It all depends on what incoming U.S. president Donald Trump does with his threat to put 25-per-cent tariffs on goods imported from Canada, which Ford has warned could throw as many as 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
"We may have to put tens of billions of dollars into the economy to keep people moving, keep people afloat," Ford said after a speech Thursday to the Mississauga Board of Trade.
"And to be very frank, I do not have the mandate to spend tens of billions of dollars right now," he added in a question-and-answer session with board president Trevor McPherson.
"We are going to feel a few bumps on the road," the premier warned.
Trump begins his second term Monday and Canadian officials will be watching closely to see if tariffs are in an expected flurry of executive orders.
"We'll see what happens on Jan. 20 or 21. I think he's going to come out of the gate real quick and start hitting us with tariffs," said Ford, who has already promised to spend $3 billion by sending $200 "tax rebate" cheques to most Ontarians in coming days.
The premier's two-term Progressive Conservatives enjoy a strong lead over opposition parties in public opinion polls as Ford contemplates an early election that would cost $175 million.
Ontario's next scheduled election is not until June 2026 - almost 17 months away - prompting rivals to charge he risks plunging Ontario into uncertainty at a critical time and when the federal government is in flux following the resignation of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
He has been pondering an expedited provincial vote since last May, long before the prospect of any Trump levies.
Earlier Thursday in Port Credit, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie launched a fresh attack at Ford's election gambit in the face of the threatened Trump tariffs.
She cited long lineups of people to sign up for a new family doctor in Walkerton on Wednesday amid a worsening shortage that has left at least 2.5 million Ontarians without a physician.
"We saw the outrageous reality in Walkerton yesterday as hundreds of people lined up in the wee hours of the morning, in freezing cold weather and in snow, bundled up in their parkas," Crombie said.
"Bringing with them their walkers, some in wheelchairs, desperately trying to get a spot with the new doctor who was taking new patients in Ontario. This shouldn't be happening."
As the Star reported, people at the front of the line waited more than six hours before the doors were opened at the local branch of the Canadian Legion, desperate to be one of the first 500 who could join the medical practice at the South Bruce Grey Health Centre.
It's a sign of the malaise under Ford, said Crombie, who also cited queues at food banks, tent encampments in communities across the province, affordability problems and rising unemployment rates.
"People are feeling really anxious right now about the future of Ontario, the threat of tariffs coming in from Donald Trump is real and rightly has people on edge," the former mayor of Mississauga added, noting the potential job losses Ford himself has mentioned.
"This is Doug's opportunity to call an election to make sure that his job is safe while your job is on the line. Doug Ford is looking for any excuse to call an early election to get ahead of an RCMP investigation on the Greenbelt scandal."
That's a reference to the Mounties' ongoing criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles also raised the Walkerton lineups during a campaign-style stop in Timmins on Thursday.
"This is the 'Hunger Games' now everywhere across Ontario," she told supporters in the riding her party lost to Ford's Tories in the 2022 election. Timmins had been an NDP stronghold until then.
Both Stiles and Crombie say they support Ford's plan for a multibillion-dollar economic stimulus package to offset any impact from tariffs that could be imposed as soon as Monday, when Trump is sworn in.
"We should all be sitting down together and looking at this," Stiles said in a signal to Ford.
"The NDP will be with you on that 100 per cent. So there is no excuse for this premier to go to the polls early. It's just about saving his job," she said.
"What we don't need now is more instability and insecurity in this province."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, wearing a 'Canada Is Not For Sale' hat, speaks as he arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Document 84 of 500

Justin Trudeau slams Pierre Poilievre and Alberta's Danielle Smith for breaking ranks over Trump tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-slams-pierre-poilievre-albertas/docview/3156624548/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+slams+Pierre+Poilievre+and+Alberta%27s+Danielle+Smith+for+breaking+ranks+over+Trump+tariffs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau slammed Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith for not joining a common Canadian opposition to incoming U.S. president Donald Trump's threatened tariffs, just days before a potential trade war erupts.
Trudeau and 12 of 13 premiers agreed on Wednesday to form a united front and get behind a pledge that "everything" is on the table in Ottawa's effort to fight a potential tariff war, including restrictions on or higher costs for Canadian oil and gas shipped to the U.S.
But Poilievre, who polls suggest could become prime minister in the next federal election, repeatedly refused Thursday to say whether Canada's energy exports should be part of a Canadian retaliatory strategy.
Poilievre's dodge came after Alberta's leader broke ranks with her provincial counterparts and Trudeau and refused to support any plan that did not have a carve-out for oil and gas.
Trudeau, speaking in Windsor on Thursday, said he doesn't "blame" Smith for advocating for her province's interests, saying it's "important" that all premiers do. "But," he said, "every single premier other than Danielle Smith then chose to put Canada first and say ... we're all part of Team Canada."
"All Canadians" stood up for Alberta when Canadian taxpayers funded the purchase of Trans Mountain pipeline expansion to "get Albertan oil to new markets," Trudeau said. "So, yes, premiers should be advocating for their own industries ... their own communities, but they should also put their country first," Trudeau said.
The Liberal leader said he has clashed with conservative premiers like Doug Ford, Saskatchewan's Scott Moe, and Nova Scotia's Tim Houston, or with New Democrats David Eby and Wab Kinew and others. "But when push comes to shove, every single one of them are Canadians first," said Trudeau.
He then pivoted to say it is up to Poilievre "to say whether he is part of Team Canada, along with all premiers on the federal government, or whether he will stand with Danielle Smith, Kevin O'Leary, and, of course, Donald Trump."
Trudeau touted an outside advisory council on Canada-U.S. relations that he named on Thursday and the efforts of other business and labour leaders who are making Canada's case.
Trump's proposed 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian products represent a "declaration of economic war" on Canada, said B.C. Premier David Eby, as provinces warned of massive impacts on jobs and sectors important to the economies of their regions.
But in B.C., Poilievre repeatedly refused to answer direct questions on his stance about energy exports, and his office did not reply to an emailed request from the Star.
At a news conference in Delta, Poilievre said the first thing Trump should do "is to send a big bouquet of flowers to the Liberal government in Ottawa."
"By blocking pipelines and LNG plants in Canada, the Liberals have forced Canadians to sell almost all of our energy to the United States, giving President Trump massive leverage in making these tariffs threats."
Poilievre blasted the outgoing Trudeau - and presumptive Liberal leadership front-runners Chrystia Freeland and Mark Carney - saying they "weakened and divided our country and have given (Trump) the upper hand."
"I will quickly approve energy projects to send our energy to the rest of the world without relying on Americans, and we will make our country more self-reliant and independent," Poilievre said.
He repeated that answer when asked what he made of Smith's decision to bail out of the hoped-for display of unity among the country's political leaders.
"We have, effectively, 10 different foreign affairs ministers, one from each province, trying to fight separately," Poilievre said.
Poilievre at one point challenged a question on energy tariffs, saying he didn't understand it because "we don't import almost any oil and gas from the Americans, at least raw oil and gas. We mostly export it."
Canada's energy regulator says the U.S. remains the top destination for Canadian crude oil, but also says the U.S. is the largest source of Canada's imported crude oil, with 72.4 per cent of Canada's oil imports originating from the U.S. that same year.
In Mississauga, Ford said he is taking "a different approach" than Smith.
"I respect Minister (sic) Smith, but I just believe Canada comes first," Ford said Thursday, noting he isn't putting Ontario's auto sector or agri-food businesses ahead of the national interest and neither are most other premiers.
"So everyone has something that they're saying, 'we may have to sacrifice a little bit,'" he said.
"I'll just leave it at ... country comes first."
Smith pushed back at her critics on "X", writing: "How about this for building a 'Team Canada' approach?"
"Stop threatening the livelihoods of tens of thousands of Albertans & Canadians via an energy export tax or ban."
She listed four other demands: construction of pipelines to diversify exports to Europe, Asia and domestically; more aggressive marketing of Canadian oil and gas; repeal of federal laws that Smith has long argued kill jobs and resource projects; and respect for Alberta taxpayers "that have been transferring hundreds of billions to Quebec, the Maritimes and even Ontario, for decades."
Former central banker Carney, who declared Thursday he is running to replace Trudeau as Liberal leader and prime minister, agreed in part with Poilievre when he was asked about Smith's rejection of Trudeau's strategy. Carney said it's important to build "the strongest economy right here in Canada" to counter Trump.
But he also stressed that, "All of us need to stand up for Canada. We need to be united. I've done a lot of negotiation over the years. I've dealt with President Trump in a different setting, when he was previously president. He's a negotiator. He's transactional."
Carney said he, too, would take nothing off the table: "In a negotiation you don't show your cards," he said. "You don't take cards off the table. You stay united."
With a file from Robert Benzie

Document 85 of 500

As Trump plants landmines, now's the time for Canadians to pull together

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-trump-plants-landmines-nows-time-canadians/docview/3156624361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+Trump+plants+landmines%2C+now%27s+the+time+for+Canadians+to+pull+together&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Next Monday an international bully will be inaugurated as U.S. president and the next four years will be an economic and political minefield for Canada. Yes, the land mines will dot the planet generally, but I like my country and want to defend it in every way possible.
Now is the time for Canadians to pull together.
We have fallen into bad habits. Canada has allowed itself to become fragmented by regionalism, identity politics, religious quarrels, absurd Prairie battles over pronouns and a general air of dilapidation in the national courtesy infrastructure.
Shy columnists should be sticking their heads above the parapet right now to defend Canadian unity. Trumpies are throwing rocks. I find that Canadians are throwing rocks, not at Trump, but at each other.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith wouldn't sign a joint statement by the PM and all the premiers opposing Trump's devastating tariffs. Is this treachery or cowardice or both?
Smith grovelled to Trump personally in Mar-a-Lago. Doesn't she understand that premiers are supposed to grovel to Trump as a team, backing up the federal government, which handles retaliation? We can't whimper and collapse. It's going to be rough. Not quite plant-your-own-potatoes, but close.
And take Ontario Liberal MP Chandra Arya who plans to run for the Liberal leadership. Possible contenders are the experienced Chrystia Freeland, the unknown Mark Carney, and various others. They'll need a lot of money, high name recognition, the common touch, people skills, economic experience and decades of having worked to make Canada an even better place.
Arya, lacking a number of these attributes, brushes them away. The main problem is that he doesn't speak French, a requirement so basic that I didn't list it.
Arya says Quebeckers won't mind because, as he claimed in an interview with the CBC's un-rattleable David Cochrane, it's not English or French that matters, "it's what is delivered to them."
"But the language matters very much in Quebec," Cochrane said, rattled. "No," Arya said calmly.
Arya has a history of such clangers. At the height of the crisis after the Indian government assassinated an opponent on Canadian soil, Arya did an Alberta. He went to visit Indian Prime Minister Modi himself.
Then Arya got in an argument with another Liberal MP of another religion over a motion about 1984 anti-Sikh riots in India, tweeting that Hindu-Canadians should lobby their MPs. Unfortunately, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh then started arguing with Arya, who hit back.
What has this to do with Canada as a nation?
I don't write about religion. I had an irreligious Indian father who never bothered to return to India because he liked Canada just so much more. It was one of the most charming things about him. He never looked back. My Scottish mother would mutter about the Highland Clearances but I, a callow teen, cared not about the 18th century.
Arya misunderstands Quebec just as Alberta misunderstands its fellow provinces. Is Alberta now going to issue its own passport?
We are often told about "diaspora" quarrels. Diasporas exist, not to fight on new ground, but to bring established Canadians and new Canadians much joy. They give us better food, a new energy, a welcome influx of young people in our aging nation, new viewpoints, and a modernized landscape.
A united Canada puts Canada first. Old-country quarrels come second.
I'd like to go back to the old ways: rational discussion of inter-provincial relations; Indigenous fishing rights; the best trees to plant for climate change; and the right way to make historic Scottish porridge (don't make it at all, I say).
In the wider world, everyone hates everyone else. In Canada, we love all Canadians, all 41 million of them.
CAPTION: A large Canadian flag is passed through a crowd in this Friday, Oct. 27, 1995 file picture, as thousands streamed into Montreal from all over Canada to join Quebecers rallying for national unity.Canadian fans celebrating hockey.

Subject: Canadians

Location: Canada; India; Quebec Canada

Integrity commissioner won't take up Ontario Place complaint

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/integrity-commissioner-wont-take-up-ontario-place/docview/3156624221/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Integrity+commissioner+won%27t+take+up+Ontario+Place+complaint&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's integrity commissioner says he will not investigate an NDP complaint that the planned Therme spa at Ontario Place got "preferential treatment" from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Citing "insufficient grounds" for a probe into the actions of Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, commissioner J. David Wake released a 15-page report Thursday following the request from New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles last October.
Stiles alleged that Surma breached the Members' Integrity Act, which is ethics legislation governing MPPs, in regard to "events connected to the Ontario Place revitalization project, including negotiation and approval of a long-term agreement with Therme Group Canada."
The integrity commissioner noted Surma, the MPP for Etobicoke Centre, was not the minister when the deal was struck, with negotiations taking place from July 2020 to April 2022, when the government approved final terms of a lease for the spa site on the west island.
"Ministers are not responsible for the actions of their predecessors," Wake wrote. "Minister Surma ... did not have responsibility for Ontario Place until Aug. 29, 2022."
He added "Ms. Stiles has made no specific allegation as to how Minister Surma, after she became the minister responsible for Ontario Place, improperly furthered Therme's interest."
In Timmins on Thursday, Stiles said her party will continue digging into the government's dealings with Austria-based Therme.
"He's not going to dig any further at this moment," she said of Wake. "I just want to say that doesn't change the reality of this shady deal. Ontarians know there's something not right about this.
"Many questions still remain," Stiles added.
The government signed a 95-year lease for Therme to locate at the old Ontario Place site, which will also see a water park and new Ontario Science Centre built.
Ontario Place was shuttered more than a decade ago after its attractions declined in popularity. Several groups have opposed the new development plans, saying it is public space that should be preserved for public use and not hived off to a private company.
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles is shown in Toronto on Feb. 13, 2024.

Title: Integrity commissioner won't take up Ontario Place complaint

Document 87 of 500

Canada Carbon Rebate payments go out this week. Here's how much most Ontarians will get

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-carbon-rebate-payments-go-out-this-week/docview/3156097254/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Carbon+Rebate+payments+go+out+this+week.+Here%27s+how+much+most+Ontarians+will+get&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada Carbon Rebate payments will go out on Wednesday across Canada. But with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promising to "Axe the Tax" should he be elected, the future of the payments remains uncertain.
Canadians will receive anywhere between $95 to $225 per quarter from the carbon price rebate, with most Ontarians receiving at least $140 per quarter, up from $122 last year thanks to a boost to the rebate in April 2024.
The rebate is delivered every three months through deposits as a direct refund for the Liberal government's carbon levy - a program, often referred to by the opposition Conservatives as a "carbon tax," that has two components. The program includes a pricing system for carbon-emitting heavy industries and a fuel charge on gasoline and home heating.
Here's what you need to know about the program and whether you're eligible.
What is the carbon tax rebate?
The credit is meant to help individuals and families offset the extra costs from the federal government's pollution pricing, according to the Canada Revenue Agency. In a review of the program, the parliamentary budget officer found in July 2023 that most Canadians in 2021 got more from the Canada Carbon Rebate (CCR) than they paid through the carbon levy. But, the budget watchdog, in a more expansive analysis, found that Canadians would see a "net loss" in the coming years from the carbon pricing scheme and rebate.
Another more recent analysis by the parliamentary budget officer, from Oct. 10, found that the industrial carbon price was more effective in slashing greenhouse gas emission than the consumer price.
The Chiefs of Ontario and the Attawapiskat First Nation called for a judicial review of the carbon levy in November 2023 over their concerns about the disproportionate impact of the pricing scheme.
The government made some changes to the carbon pricing system in late October 2023, doubling the top-up to the rebate for rural Canadians and temporarily pausing the fuel charge for heating oil.
In Ontario, the carbon pricing program is administered at a federal level, with the federal government returning 90 per cent proceeds from those charges to eligible residents.
The CCR has gained attention not only for its rebrand - which the government implemented in an attempt to make its contentious climate policy more palatable - but also for its resistance from provincial leaders. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe, in February, said his province would not submit the money it owed for the carbon price on natural gas, leading to the federal government responding by threatening to deny payment of the rebate to Saskatchewan residents.
Several premiers across the country, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, called on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to pause the carbon price increase on April 1. Trudeau did not oblige, accusing conservative premiers of "(making) political hay" out of the carbon price and challenging them to develop an alternative policy to reduce emissions.
In April 2024, Jagmeet Singh backpedaled on his support for the consumer portion of the carbon levy, emphasizing the need for climate policies that target "big polluters."
Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative party, who have a definitive lead in the polls ahead of an expected spring election once the Liberal party elects a new leader, have long rallied against the carbon levy.
Who is eligible for the carbon tax rebate?
Residents of Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario were eligible when the program first rolled out and the program was expanded in July 2023 to include Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and New Brunswick.
To receive the benefit, you must be a resident of a CCR-eligible province on the first day of the payment month. You must also be at least 19-years old. If you are under that age, you must either have (or previously had) a spouse or common-law partner, or are (or previously were) a parent and live (or previously lived) with your child.
How much will I receive in carbon tax rebate credits?
Ontarians eligible for the rebate will receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19. A family of four in Ontario, according to the federal government, would receive $280.
The payments are universal and not dependent on income levels. Here's how it breaks down by province, for an individual, per quarter:
Alberta: $225Manitoba: $150New Brunswick: $95Newfoundland and Labrador: $149Nova Scotia: $103Ontario: $140Prince Edward Island: $110Saskatchewan: $188
The CCR also includes a 20 per cent supplemental bump for residents in small and rural communities, which includes all eligible P.E.I. residents, according to the government. However, some rural communities have spoken out about the rules demarcating rural towns, saying the government has unfairly looped them in with nearby cities.
Recipients can also expect quarterly payments in April, July and October this year.
How do I receive the carbon tax payment?
Eligible CCR recipients should see the credit in the form of a cheque or direct deposit automatically, without needing to apply. Canadians who file their taxes electronically before March 15 should receive their payment on the quarterly schedule, according to the government's website. Those filing later can expect their CCR payments six to eight weeks after their tax return is assessed.
If you have a spouse or common-law partner, the person whose tax return was assessed first will receive the total credit for the family. It doesn't matter which person receives the rebate as the total amount will be the same.
People with tax debt will see the credit automatically subtracted from the amount they owe.
The Canada Revenue Agency noted it may take up to 10 working days to receive a CCR payment.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre, speaking at a press conference on Parliament Hill in the summer, has promised to "axe the tax" and end the Liberal government's carbon pricing program if the Conservatives win a spring election.

Subject: Conservatism; Climate policy; Carbon; Judicial reviews; Electronic filing of tax returns; Political parties; Environmental tax; Federal government; Canadians; Rural areas

Document 88 of 500

City halts crackdown on illegal pot shops: 'We are just no longer effective,' bylaw director explains of costly, dangerous enforcement efforts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-halts-crackdown-on-illegal-pot-shops/docview/3155989388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+halts+crackdown+on+illegal+pot+shops%3A+%27We+are+just+no+longer+effective%2C%27+bylaw+director+explains+of+costly%2C+dangerous+enforcement+efforts&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The head of Toronto's licensing division says city bylaw officers will stop enforcing rules against illegal pot shops in 2025, due to a lack of provincial funding and the dangers of doing the job.
At a meeting of council's budget committee on Wednesday, Carleton Grant, executive director of municipal licensing and standards, said that since legalization in 2018, his division has shut down dozens of illegal cannabis stores and at one point there were only about 10 in operation.
But he said the potential profits are so lucrative that dozens of operators, some of them backed by organized crime, have simply paid fines and reopened. "We are just no longer effective," he said.
Pot shops - both legal and illegal - have proliferated across Toronto with the advent of legalization. Under the regime established by the province in 2018, Ontario is responsible for licensing retail cannabis stores, but the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario doesn't enforce the rules against storefronts selling pot without a licence.
That responsibility is left to the city, which is empowered to issue closure orders, conduct seizures, bar entry to storefronts and lay non-criminal charges under the Cannabis Control Act. Penalties for convictions under the act include fines of up to $250,000, and a maximum of two years in jail.
Between 2019 and 2021, Premier Doug Ford's government provided Toronto almost $9 million to help address the fallout from legalization, according to city documents. Grant said that money ran out last year and "we no longer have funding for cannabis enforcement."
He also suggested bylaw officers, who are accompanied by police when enforcing pot shops but are equipped with little more than flashlights and vests, shouldn't be subjected to the associated risks, and that stopping illegal drug sales was a criminal matter that was the responsibility of the Toronto Police Services or the Ontario Provincial Police.
"It is criminal, it is organized crime, and it is not appropriate for me to send my staff into these locations where there are weapons and there's criminal activity taking place," he said.
Grant bluntly told the committee that now that funding has run out, the proposed 2025 city budget, which Mayor Olivia Chow unveiled on Monday, "signals my exit from cannabis enforcement."
His comments alarmed some councillors, including Lily Cheng (Ward 18, Willowdale).
"I'm really concerned," she told the Star after the meeting. "Police are definitely not going to start enforcing licensing, so to me there's a huge gap, and how are we going to bridge that gap?"
Cheng said illegal weed stores pose a threat to community safety, and the city should have a backup plan before deciding to stop enforcement. She said the cash-strapped municipality can't afford to replace the lost provincial funding, and council should ask Queen's Park to restore it.
But the city can't just say "now we're not doing anything" and hope someone else steps up enforcement, Cheng said.
Spokespeople for Toronto police and the provincial government didn't immediately return requests for comment.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Document 89 of 500

Smith playing right into Trump's hands: Alberta premier appears to believe it's every province for itself in the face of devastating tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-playing-right-into-trumps-hands/docview/3155988872/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+playing+right+into+Trump%27s+hands%3A+Alberta+premier+appears+to+believe+it%27s+every+province+for+itself+in+the+face+of+devastating+tariffs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: United we stand. Undermined we don't stand a chance.
The prime minister and all the premiers in the room came together to forge a common front against Donald Trump's tariff threats Wednesday. But one premier got away from them, sounding a note of division and disunity from afar.
Alberta's Danielle Smith, tuning in virtually on vacation, was the odd Canadian out - out of the room, out of the country, opting out of their consensus. The premier who presides over Canada's biggest energy exports refused to sign on to their plan of action.
Ignoring pleas from Ontario's Doug Ford and her fellow premiers, Smith went her own way, though it's unclear where that will take her or where it leaves all of us. Facing the threat of devastating tariffs that might be imposed by the president-elect, Alberta's premier appears to believe it's every province for itself.
National unity is elusive in the best of times, but it should come easily in the worst of times. Facing our new-found frenemy next door, Canadian leaders need to come together to be ready for what's coming.
That's the case Ford is making as chair of the premiers' council, echoing the appeals from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. The fissures between Ford and Smith, two right-leaning populists, have been opening up for weeks, but Wednesday's meeting was the time for the premiers to come together for the greater good amid all the bad news.
Alberta aside, even the most unlikely suspects are rallying to the cause of Team Canada.
Scott Moe, the occasionally cantankerous and parochial premier of Saskatchewan - whose province is also heavily dependent on energy and resource exports - signed on to the pan-Canadian consensus. Quebec's François Legault, who typically indulges in jurisdictional squabbles, pointedly agreed on a common front of tough talk against Trump - even on energy.
Ahead of the president-elect taking power next week, this is the time for Canadians to keep Americans guessing. And worrying about overplaying their hand.
Hats off to Doug Ford for donning a baseball cap on his way into the meeting that proclaimed, "Canada is not for sale." Ford's photo-op hit the mark - message received.
But Trump's musings and trollings about turning Canada into the 51st state - with Quebec perhaps the 52nd - have always been a phoney war inside a sideshow. It is a tactic to distract us.
Rather than the rarefied rhetoric on annexation, beware the real threat of decimation from global 10 per cent tariffs for all countries across the board - boosted to 25 per cent across the border with Canada and Mexico. It amounts to a declaration of economic warfare, turning faithful allies into adversaries.
Ford knows the difference, because he understands the stakes for Ontario, from the auto industry to the mining sector. Setting aside Trump's mind games about erasing the border, Ford is deadly serious about the threat of tariffs that would wreak economic havoc and political panic - provincially, nationally, continentally.
We won't know until next week whether Trump is serious, or whether he's willing to claim victory and grant us an exemption after the first ministers endorsed an ambitious new border plan to address his demands. But we must prepare for a worst-case scenario.
To be sure, his complaints about our open border never made sense - the guns and fentanyl flow north, not south, and the people smuggling goes both ways.
But our billion-dollar border security response should count for something.
Under pressure, Canada will belatedly deploy a couple of the Black Hawk helicopters made famous by the U.S. Armed Forces. A formidable fighting machine overseas, the chopper is being repurposed into a showpiece closer to home.
Ostensibly the task is to survey our undefended border. The true mission is to satisfy our unrestrained neighbour as Trump takes power.
So be it. Will he buy it?
Everyone thinks they know how Trump thinks. And how best to handle his worst instincts.
Walk and talk softly, or carry a big stick? Retaliate with tariffs, or close the energy tap?
The only certainty is that if the tariffs roll out next week, Canada will have to respond forcefully, intelligently. For acquiescence is no alternative.
Pick your analogy - high-stakes poker, high-level chess, bargaining in the bazaar or outmanoeuvring a bully. This is not the time to be measured, it's the moment to send a message:
You can make us hurt, but we will both hurt together. Pay attention.
"You can't let someone hit you in the head with a sledgehammer without hitting them back twice as hard, in my opinion," Ford argued. "I'm gonna fight like you've never seen before."
As for Alberta going its own way, "that's her choice, but I have a little different theory ... our country comes first."
In any war game, there is a sweet spot between hollow bluffs and hopeless defeatism that keeps your opponent guessing, off balance, uncertain, hesitant. By trying desperately to take energy off the table, Smith is playing into Trump's hands - as Legault well understands.
"Nothing is off the table, including what we can do on hydro power," the Quebec premier stressed.
By running to Mar-a-Lago uninvited, in hot pursuit of a show-off photo-op with the president-elect, Alberta's premier only appeared desperate to appease. Now, from her vacation hideaway, she seems even more out of sync with her fellow premiers.
The politician who loses his or her nerve loses all. Canadians - whether in Alberta or Saskatchewan, Ontario or Quebec, can ill-afford to be fighting one another rather than facing off against Trump.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives for a first ministers meeting Wednesday in Ottawa wearing a "Canada is not for sale" hat. Ford is firing back at U.S. president-elect Donald Trump and his economic threats to turn Canada into the 51st state.Sean Kilpatrick THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Presidents; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 90 of 500

OSSTF slams Ford with ad campaign: Teachers' union says system is 'at a breaking point'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/osstf-slams-ford-with-ad-campaign/docview/3155986519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=OSSTF+slams+Ford+with+ad+campaign%3A+Teachers%27+union+says+system+is+%27at+a+breaking+point%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's public high school teachers' union has launched an ad campaign, saying the education system is "at a breaking point.
"Our schools are falling apart, our teachers and education workers are burning out, and students are being left behind," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.
The ads show dismal conditions at "Ford High," which is "set in a dystopian near-future where public education is crumbling due to defunding and neglect. Using dark humour and poignant storytelling, the campaign sheds light on the current trajectory of Ontario's education system under Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government," the union said.
The ads will be posted on social media and broadcast on Crave, CTV and CBC Gem, among others, and come as Ford continues to refuse to rule out calling an election.
In late 2023, the OSSTF, school boards and the province turned to binding arbitration to reach a tentative agreement.
School boards have said funding, while increasing by $745 million this school year, has not kept up with their actual costs. The Ontario Public School Boards' Association estimates that, when adjusted for inflation, the province is spending $776 less per student this year.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
