Document 91 of 500

Ottawa, provinces gear up for trade war with U.S.: Alberta premier slams threat to target energy exports

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-provinces-gear-up-trade-war-with-u-s/docview/3155986407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa%2C+provinces+gear+up+for+trade+war+with+U.S.%3A+Alberta+premier+slams+threat+to+target+energy+exports&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada is getting ready to strike back against Donald Trump's tariffs, even though there is dissension in the nation's ranks.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau emerged from a meeting with all 13 premiers Wednesday armed with a proposal to slap counter-tariffs on up to $150 billion worth of American imports, depending on how far the incoming U.S. president goes.
But the first ministers' attempt to display unity in a potential trade war with the U.S. was soured after Alberta Premier Danielle Smith declared she won't stand for how the federal government and other leaders have refused to rule out slapping tariffs on Canadian fossil fuel exports.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who showed up in a Trump-style ball cap that read, "Canada Is Not For Sale," repeatedly stressed the need for unity, and took a shot at Smith, saying it's "her choice" to break ranks. But for him and the others, now "country comes first."
Ford called for a strong federal response after Ontario's government was initially dissatisfied with an early proposed tariff list that fell short of a "strong, proportional" response, said provincial sources, speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations.
"The retaliatory tariffs need to be hard. We have to send a message," Ford said at a joint press conference with the other attending premiers and prime minister.
While Trudeau and others said it still remains to be seen exactly what - if anything - Trump will do in the coming days, the prime minister said it is "very" likely the incoming American administration will impose tariffs.
"Everything is on the table," the prime minister said.
Trudeau said he supports the "principle of a proportional dollar-for-dollar response," but the goal of Canada's response would be to convince the Trump administration to back off.
"If the new administration chooses to bring in tariffs, what is it that we can do that results in the lifting of those tariffs as quickly as possible? ... That is first and foremost," Trudeau said.
Canada's ambassador to the U.S., Kirsten Hillman, said in an interview she has talked to many close to Trump, and there are "a lot of different views as to what will happen. Some say absolutely he will do what he said he's going to do. Some say he will do it, but on fewer than 100 per cent of products. Some say, you know, maybe it will be a different formulation altogether. So nobody knows.
"And we know this from President Trump last time, he's the only one who knows what he's going to do, and he makes up his decision often quite late in the day," she said.
Trudeau conveyed to the premiers his government's reluctance to immediately levy dollar-for-dollar counter-tariffs to match Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat. Instead, premiers had been told of an initial list of Canadian counter-tariffs on 10 politically sensitive items like Florida orange juice that could roll out, and then of another list on $37.6 billion worth of American imports. The broader list of tariffs on $150 billion worth of imports would be an escalation as needed, depending on what Trump does, said a federal government official.
If the Americans go ahead with the 25 per cent "across-the-board" tariffs, it would represent an "absolutely massive" surcharge on Canadian products shipped stateside, with the official saying a Canadian dollar-for-dollar counterpunch could require Canadian tariffs on $600 billion worth of imports from the U.S.
It's a far cry from the 2018 Canada-U.S. tariff battle when Trump put surcharges of 10 per cent to 25 per cent on Canada's steel and aluminum, and Canada matched with tariffs totalling $16.6 billion on a range of American imports like Kentucky bourbon, playing cards, Harley-Davidson motorcycles and other goods.
But the proposal did not satisfy Alberta's premier, who blasted the possible federal response in a missive she posted on social media after the meeting.
"Federal government officials continue to publicly and privately float the idea of cutting off energy supply to the U.S. and imposing export tariffs on Alberta energy and other products to the United States," Smith wrote.
"Until these threats cease, Alberta will not be able to fully support the federal government's plan in dealing with the threatened tariffs."
Another federal official, speaking on a background-only basis to brief reporters, said if Trump issues tariffs but exempts Canadian oil and gas, for example, that would show where the Americans feel vulnerable. Canada is a big supplier of oil and gas to the U.S. and when those energy exports are taken out of the trade balance equation, Smith said, the U.S. has a trade surplus with Canada.
Trudeau has not ruled out any move to make Americans feel the pain via higher-priced Canadian oil exports - over the objection of Alberta. The aim is to to take actions that would force the U.S. to lift its tariffs, but not so much that it would hurt Canadians or lead to a decade-long tariff war.
A federal official said three premiers in the room urged colleagues not to voice complaints about Canada's strategy publicly, but to express dissent with each other in private - remarks that were construed as aimed at Smith.
Smith attended the meeting virtually, and a pool reporter said her screen was a tropical backdrop, prompting Quebec Premier François Legault to ask if she was still at Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort - which she visited last weekend.
Canada's counter-tariff list would not take immediate effect but would be subject to a period of public consultation.
Earlier, leaders of Ontario, Quebec and Newfoundland insisted energy, critical minerals and hydroelectricity should be in play to counter Trump's tariff threats.
Ford said the U.S. president-elect is going to "try to devastate our country," and provinces with different interests cannot get in the way of Canada's national response. Key commodity exports like energy, nickel, aluminum and potash are among Canada's "aces" in any trade war, he said.
"You're in a card game, but you don't show your opposition your cards. You have some aces to hold on to, those aces, but it's all part of negotiation tactics," he said.
"I understand different jurisdictions around the country have their concerns," said Ford. "But nothing is more important than standing united."
Legault echoed that sentiment in French, saying while his top priority is to prevent Trump from executing his threat of 25 per cent levies on all Canadian products, "we have to prepare a retaliation plan."
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe, who, like Smith, opposes any export tariffs on energy, did sign the joint statement at the end, but in a conciliatory statement agreed it is important for political leaders to work together in the face of Trump's tariff challenge.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles and Alex Ballingall Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Trade disputes; International trade; Federal government; Exports; Tariffs; Canada-US relations; Political leadership

Document 92 of 500

How Canada is playing into Trump's hands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-canada-is-playing-into-trumps-hands/docview/3155986236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Canada+is+playing+into+Trump%27s+hands&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada playing into Trump's hands
As Canadians, why are we engaging with President-elect Donald Trump? Let's ignore the bully. Every time we react to something he says about Canada, it's a win for Trump. Placing more security along our border is a silly idea: we don't have drugs or thousands of illegal immigrants pouring across the border into the United States. That's a notion Trump seems to have invented to create chaos. If he and the U.S. want to unnecessarily secure the border to solve a made-up problem, then let them incur the cost. We certainly don't need more border security on the Canadian side, but we are playing into Trump's hands anyway. If Trump wants to place tariffs on Canadian goods, I doubt any amount of lobbying or common-sense talk is going to change his mind. He'll do it if he decides it's in his best interest - regardless of the facts. If he needs facts, he'll simply make them up, repeating them over and over again until people start to believe him. What Canadians should do is quietly prepare for tariffs and quickly strengthen our military. Under Trump, it seems unlikely that we would receive military assistance from the U.S. were, for example, Russia or China to provoke conflict. If those countries perceive weakness in us or cracks in our relationship with the U.S., I fear they will take advantage.
David Bell, Toronto
Canadian politicians have gotten their shorts in a knot over Donald Trump, and that means the President-elect has won: he loves upsetting people who are foolish enough to fall into his trap. Our political leaders should have done what Canadians are best known for instead of overreacting and rushing to judgments - but it seems that the quality of our elected officials has declined in that regard.
Ken Demerling, Toronto
Three cheers for Michelle Obama
Michelle Obama will skip Trump inauguration, but ex-Presidents Obama, Clinton and Bush will be there, Jan. 14
Kudos to Michelle Obama. Non-MAGA people, especially those with big public profiles, should not attend functions at which President-elect Donald Trump is present. As Obama's example illustrates, their absence serves as an opportunity to highlight the fact that the office of the President will soon be the office of a dictatorial felon who has openly mused about jailing many of his opponents. Trump is surrounding himself with like-minded people who seem interested only in enriching themselves and their already-rich backers. Obama clearly knows the game, and she shows us the way. There is no government without the support of the governed.
Brian Clow, Pickering
Canadians need a pre-election epiphany
It is tiresome to read and listen to the constant attacks heaped on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his much-scorned Liberal Party. The relentless and often misleading venom directed at Trudeau would ruin the best of reputations. It seems clear to me that this orchestrated attack from the right is driven by forces similar to those that exist in the United States, where President-elect Donald Trump and his billionaire friends will soon rule in favour of the rich and their vested interests. It's unfortunate that Canadian voters will likely sleepwalk to the polls and elect a band of right-wingers intent on destroying the programs and institutions that make Canada a healthier, more inclusive nation. Let's hope voters have an epiphany prior to the election.
Desmond Pouyat, Toronto
Carney quick on his feet
Mark Carney reveals on Jon Stewart's The Daily Show a side of himself he hasn't shown Canadians, Jan. 14
When a former central banker walks onto The Daily Show and actually kills it, you'd think we'd celebrate. Instead, critics such as Tonda McCharles dismiss Mark Carney's viral moment as a low-risk no-show.
Let's be clear: comedy is a high-wire act, especially for someone eyeing the Prime Minister's Office. One awkward pause, one failed joke, and you're a cringeworthy meme, immortalized on the internet as a loser's punchline. Given that Carney is a banker fighting the stuffed-suit stereotype, his appearance with Jon Stewart was a revelation. An economist with a spreadsheet mindset stepped into the arena of comedy - and somehow walked away having won the audience's approval. That's a big deal.
While Trudeau prorogued and Poilievre complained, Carney showed up with actual substance wrapped in wit. He demonstrated a depth of economic knowledge that went beyond the tax-cut talking points conservatives recycle every election, but without putting everyone to sleep. He framed climate change as a solvable crisis without sounding like a doomsday prophet. And he managed to be progressive without merely parroting a script. That's leadership without the usual political theatre.
The cynics can sneer all they want. Carney has shown us that maybe, just maybe, the current Canadian political race has been altered. Perhaps Poilievre is no longer a sure thing: progressive Canadian voters now have a real chance to avoid political disaster.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Mark Carney and Jon Stewart appeared to have so much fun sparring about the Canada-U.S. relationship this week on The Daily Show. When asked what he thought about the idea of turning Canada into the 51st state, Carney told Stewart, "You're attractive, but we're not moving in with you. It's not you; it's us."
Carney was funny, humble, well-spoken and knowledgeable about Canadian affairs and politics. He displayed his skills as an economist and banker, and I believe he would be the perfect prime minister to deal with President-elect Donald Trump's massive tariffs. Carney was the governor of the Bank of Canada during the 2008 financial crisis and helped get the United Kingdom through Brexit as governor of the Bank of England from 2013 to 2020.
It was wonderful to see two men so quick on their feet!
Ellie McCaig, Kitchener
Bike lanes on blast
Who actually uses Toronto's bike lanes - and who really benefits from them? Jan. 14
Right on, Martin Regg Cohn. Your column on bike lanes is very accurate. I live on Woodbine Avenue, and the bike lane here is a joke. It would be funny if it were not so wasteful. Thanks for the article.
Melina Crane, Toronto
By his own admission, Cohn does not bike in the wintertime. As someone who biked before Toronto's cycling infrastructure was built and who continues to do so today, I have been impressed by the number of people who have joined me in commuting by bicycle. Of course traffic is a major problem in this city, but only with facts and data can we begin to address it.
Senwung Luk, Toronto

Document 93 of 500

Ottawa unveils border security plan: Blackhawk helicopters and surveillance drones part of $1.3B response to Trump's tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-unveils-border-security-plan/docview/3155986191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+unveils+border+security+plan%3A+Blackhawk+helicopters+and+surveillance+drones+part+of+%241.3B+response+to+Trump%27s+tariff+threat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government will soon deploy two leased Blackhawk helicopters as part of its $1.3-billion border security plan that was made in the shadow of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threats to trigger a trade war with Canada.
Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration Minister Marc Miller detailed part of that plan on Wednesday, ahead of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's gathering to discuss Trump's threatened tariffs with the premiers of the provinces and territories.
Alongside the two Blackhawks, which are being leased for $5.3 million, the government is also adding 60 new surveillance drones and is looking to acquire new "mobile surveillance towers" and other new technologies to beef up border security.
The plan was part of last fall's fiscal update, and came after Trump first threatened to impose punishing 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian imports into the United States. In laying out that threat, Trump cited the flow of illegal migrants and drugs like fentanyl from Canada and Mexico into the U.S.
On Wednesday, both cabinet ministers repeatedly made the case that apprehensions of asylum-seekers and fentanyl at the northern U.S. border is a fraction of the levels at its frontier with Mexico. They also argued that Canada has been nonetheless taking steps to improve security at the border, and shared statistics like an 89 per cent decrease in the number of apprehensions heading into the U.S. from June to December 2024.
After being briefed on the plan this week, Premier Doug Ford called it "phenomenal."
Asked why the border plan has not caused Trump to withdraw his threat of tariffs - which he said he would impose on Monday, after he is sworn-in for a second time as U.S. president - Miller said there is no "magic potion" to solve all issues with the incoming American administration.
McGuinty added that Canadian leaders need to work together and avoid "intemperate" pronouncements that shouldn't be taken seriously, and noted Trump's repeated taunts that Canada is a "51st state." Trump also said last week he would use "economic force" to annex Canada, and spoke of erasing the border.
"We're not interested in drama, we're interested in making progress," McGuinty said.
"We are hopeful, and remain hopeful, that the new incoming administration will understand just how incredibly serious we are."
Since Trump's tariff threat, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has claimed the government has lost control of the U.S.-Canada border, and has demanded an immediate election so that his party can better deal with Trump.
At the same time, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has described Trump as a bully and insisted the government must retaliate against any tariffs the Americans impose.
The Liberal government has been working on a potential response to the threatened tariffs, as the Star has reported. It has also not ruled out more extreme measures, such as slapping an export tax on Canadian fossil fuels that get sent to the U.S. - exports that Statistics Canada has said were worth almost $120 billion in 2023.
Premiers have put forward proposals as well. Ford has pitched co-operation with the U.S. on critical minerals and energy, after previously musing about cutting off electricity that is sent to the U.S. if Trump follows through with his tariffs.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has pushed back against proposals to restrict energy exports, which are a boon to her province's economy. In December, Smith's United Conservative government announced a $29-million plan to increase security at its border with the U.S. state of Montana.
Smith refused to sign on to a joint statement that Trudeau and the premiers released on Wednesday, over the refusal of other leaders to take export tariffs on fossil fuels off the table of any Canadian response to Trump's tariffs.
On Wednesday, the public safety and immigration ministers insisted Canada is also preparing in case Trump's campaign pledge to start mass deportations of illegal immigrants from the U.S. prompts a surge of asylum-seekers trying to claim refuge in Canada.
Noting no spike has happened yet, Miller said the U.S. needs to make sure no such surge happens, and said migrants who enter into Canada without stopping at a border crossing are "not welcome."
McGuinty added that he plans to invite Trump's incoming "border czar" Tom Homan to go fishing, and wants to discuss the promised mass deportations when they first meet.
CAPTION: Canada will lease two Blackhawk helicopters for $5.3 million, add 60 new drones and consider other technologies like "mobile surveillance towers" to beef up protection along the U.S. border.David ZalubowskiThe Associated Press file photo
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star

Cohn, Jan. 14

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3155986083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Michelle+Obama+will+skip+Trump+inauguration%2C+but+ex-presidents+Obama%2C+Clinton+and+Bush+will+be+there%2C+Jan.+14+Mark+Carney+reveals+on+Jon+Stewart%27s+The+Daily+Show+a+side+of+himself+he+hasn%27t+shown+Canadians%2C+Jan.+14+Who+actually+uses+Toronto%27s+bike+lanes+-+and+who+really+benefits+from+them%3F+Cohn%2C+Jan.+14&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why is Canada playing into Trump's hands?
As Canadians, why are we engaging with president-elect Donald Trump? Let's ignore the bully. Every time we react to something he says about Canada, it's a win for Trump.
Placing more security along our border is a silly idea: we don't have drugs or thousands of illegal immigrants pouring across the border into the United States. That's a notion Trump seems to have invented to create chaos. If he and the U.S. want to unnecessarily secure the border to solve a made-up problem, then let them incur the cost. We certainly don't need more border security on the Canadian side, but we are playing into Trump's hands anyway.
If Trump wants to place tariffs on Canadian goods, I doubt any amount of lobbying or common-sense talk is going to change his mind. He'll do it if he decides it's in his best interest - regardless of the facts. If he needs facts, he'll simply make them up, repeating them over and over again until people start to believe him.
What Canadians should do is quietly prepare for tariffs and quickly strengthen our military. Under Trump, it seems unlikely that we would receive military assistance from the U.S. were, for example, Russia or China to provoke conflict. If those countries perceive weakness in us or cracks in our relationship with the U.S., I fear they will take advantage.
David Bell, Toronto
Canadian politicians have gotten their shorts in a knot over Donald Trump, and that means the president-elect has won: he loves upsetting people who are foolish enough to fall into his trap.
Our political leaders should have done what Canadians are best known for instead of overreacting and rushing to judgments - but it seems that the quality of our elected officials has declined in that regard.
Ken Demerling, Toronto
Three cheers for Michelle Obama
Michelle Obama will skip Trump inauguration, but ex-presidents Obama, Clinton and Bush will be there, Jan. 14
Kudos to Michelle Obama. Non-MAGA people, especially those with big public profiles, should not attend functions at which president-elect Donald Trump is present. As Obama's example illustrates, their absence serves as an opportunity to highlight the fact that the office of the president will soon be the office of a dictatorial felon who has openly mused about jailing many of his opponents. Trump is surrounding himself with like-minded people who seem interested only in enriching themselves and their already-rich backers. Obama clearly knows the game, and she shows us the way.
Brian Clow, Pickering
Canadians need
an epiphany before election
It is tiresome to read and listen to the constant attacks heaped on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and his much-scorned Liberal party. The relentless and often misleading venom directed at Trudeau would ruin the best of reputations.
It seems clear to me that this orchestrated attack from the right is driven by forces similar to those in the United States, where president-elect Donald Trump and his billionaire friends will soon rule in favour of the rich and their vested interests.
It's unfortunate that Canadian voters will likely sleepwalk to the polls and elect a band of right-wingers intent on destroying the programs and institutions that make Canada a healthier, more inclusive nation. Let's hope voters have an epiphany prior to the election.
Desmond Pouyat, Toronto
Carney shows voters substance wrapped in wit
Mark Carney reveals on Jon Stewart's The Daily Show a side of himself he hasn't shown Canadians, Jan. 14
When a former central banker walks onto "The Daily Show" and actually kills it, you'd think we'd celebrate. Instead, critics such as Tonda MacCharles dismiss Mark Carney's viral moment as a low-risk no-show.
Let's be clear: comedy is a high-wire act, especially for someone eyeing the Prime Minister's Office. One awkward pause, one failed joke, and you're a cringeworthy meme, immortalized on the internet as a loser's punchline.
Given that Carney is a banker fighting the stuffed-suit stereotype, his appearance with Jon Stewart was a revelation. An economist with a spreadsheet mindset stepped into the arena of comedy - and somehow walked away having won the audience's approval. That's a big deal.
While Trudeau prorogued and Poilievre complained, Carney showed up with actual substance wrapped in wit. He demonstrated a depth of economic knowledge that went beyond the tax-cut talking points conservatives recycle every election, but without putting everyone to sleep. He framed climate change as a solvable crisis without sounding like a doomsday prophet. And he managed to be progressive without merely parroting a script. That's leadership without the usual political theatre.
The cynics can sneer all they want. Carney has shown us that maybe, just maybe, the current Canadian political race has been altered. Perhaps Poilievre is no longer a sure thing: progressive Canadian voters now have a real chance to avoid political disaster.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Mark Carney and Jon Stewart appeared to have so much fun sparring about the Canada-U.S. relationship. When asked what he thought about the idea of turning Canada into the 51st state, Carney told Stewart, "You're attractive, but we're not moving in with you. It's not you; it's us."
Carney was funny, humble, well-spoken and knowledgeable about Canadian affairs and politics. He displayed his skills as an economist and banker, and I believe he would be the perfect prime minister to deal with president-elect Donald Trump's massive tariffs.
Ellie McCaig, Kitchener, Ont.

to city bike lanes

Document 95 of 500

Port Hope may get nuclear plant: Ford government's proposed project could power 10 million homes, energy minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/port-hope-may-get-nuclear-plant/docview/3155986044/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Port+Hope+may+get+nuclear+plant%3A+Ford+government%27s+proposed+project+could+power+10+million+homes%2C+energy+minister+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is focusing on the Port Hope area - no stranger to the nuclear industry - as the best bet for a massive new nuclear power station to meet forecasts for soaring electricity needs in the next 25 years.
But there are many hurdles in terms of community consultation, studies and approvals that make it unlikely that a power plant producing up to 10,000 megawatts would be up and running until the 2040s.
"We have a lot of work ahead of us," Ontario Power Generation (OPG) president Nicole Butcher told a news conference Wednesday announcing the Lake Ontario town east of Oshawa has expressed interest in being the host.
The site in Wesleyville has long been owned by OPG and was destined to become an oil-fired power plant until a 1970s oil crisis sent fuel prices skyrocketing and scuttled that plan. It has been sitting idle but is zoned for a power plant and is close to transmission lines, as well as the existing Darlington nuclear plant.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce said the new plant, if it is built following extensive consultations with Port Hope and the Williams Treaties First Nations, would be equivalent to "two Darlingtons," making it one of the biggest nuclear generating stations in the world and capable of powering 10 million homes.
While Darlington and the older Pickering nuclear plant east of it use Canada-designed CANDU nuclear technology, no decisions have been made on the type of reactors that could be built on the 1,300-acre Wesleyville site.
Officials at a background briefing on the plan said if all goes well, the first reactor could be operating in the 2040s, although Lecce spoke of a "10-to-15 year time frame."
Port Hope was the only one of three municipalities that expressed interest in a power plant after the province in late November named sites it owns in Wesleyville, Nanticoke on the north shore of Lake Erie and along the St. Clair River south of Sarnia, as possible locations.
The Nanticoke and Sarnia-area sites were home to massive coal-fired generation stations shut down years ago in the fight against climate change.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner has called for public hearings on a new energy plan the Ford government is developing in the wake of forecasts that the need for electricity will jump 75 per cent by 2050.
He wants to see a "competitive procurement process for the lowest-cost, emission-free electricity" instead of automatically choosing nuclear power.
Lecce has said the government is considering "all types of new energy generation," but nuclear is needed as emission-free baseload power.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 96 of 500

ServiceOntario move to Staples will cost more than estimated: But watchdog says expanded hours will result in savings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/serviceontario-move-staples-will-cost-more-than/docview/3155985876/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=ServiceOntario+move+to+Staples+will+cost+more+than+estimated%3A+But+watchdog+says+expanded+hours+will+result+in+savings&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Moving nine ServiceOntario locations into existing Staples stores will cost the government more than it estimated, says a new report from Ontario's financial watchdog.
However, when factoring in the expanded hours offered by the retail store locations, the province will be saving more money than expected, says the report from the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario released Wednesday.
While the province estimated it would cost $10.2 million to "relocate and operate" the nine centres, the actual cost is $11.7 million by 2027 - with $1.7 million for start-up costs and then $10 million for labour and overhead, the report says.
However, that extra cost is due to the longer operating hours - which increased by 47.7 per cent and were a key point Premier Doug Ford touted when promoting the Staples deal - which the government didn't fully account for, said the FAO.
A year ago, the province unveiled its plan to move nine of the privately operated centres into existing Staples locations for a three-year pilot program running to 2027, with longer hours, access to free parking and some additional services as deals with the independent operators came to an end.
When accounting for the expanded hours the Staples locations are open, and comparing to a scenario where the old ServiceOntario locations would have matched those longer hours, the province saves $1.2 million, given the original locations would cost $12.9 million, compared to $11.7 million for Staples.
In a statement, a spokesperson for Minister of Public and Business Service Delivery Todd McCarthy said the FAO report "confirms that our partnership with Staples Canada offers better services for people at a better price for taxpayers."
With "more than $1.2 million in cost-savings and 50 per cent more hours of service, nearly one third of transactions are taking place during the extended hours. It is clear that this new approach to deliver more convenience and access is working," said Matthew D'Amico, McCarthy's press secretary and issues manager.
But the deal came under fire from critics for being sole-sourced instead of undergoing a competitive bidding process.
The FAO review was prompted by Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), but the government had welcomed the probe, saying Ontarians were gaining "more convenience, more access, longer hours, weeknights to eight or nine ... all day Saturdays."
Bowman said Wednesday that "the report found that the numbers the Ford government gave the public were wrong."
The Ford government, she added, "has no problem taking profits from Ontario's small businesses and giving them to American companies ... this deal has lacked transparency from the beginning."
McCarthy has previously said the Staples deal is not a new concept for the province because it has, for years, been using retailers - such as Canadian Tire - to house ServiceOntario locations, including when the previous Liberal government was in power.
But Ontario Greens' Leader Mike Schreiner, the MPP for Guelph, accused the government of furthering "their privatization agenda and put profits before the people of Ontario ... Everything this government does is designed to help well-connected corporate insiders and the richest turn a profit at the expense of everyday people in our communities that are struggling to get by."
CAPTION: The province estimated it would cost $10.2 million to move nine ServiceOntario centres into existing Staples locations, but the Financial Accountability Office has pegged the cost at $11.7 million by 2027, the end of the three-year pilot program.Rob BeintemaMetroland file photo
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 97 of 500

Doug Ford sports a 'CANADA IS NOT FOR SALE' hat amid Trump's 51st state taunts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-sports-canada-is-not-sale-hat-amid/docview/3156204287/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+sports+a+%27CANADA+IS+NOT+FOR+SALE%27+hat+amid+Trump%27s+51st+state+taunts&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is pulling an Uno-reverse card on Donald Trump, firing back on the U.S. president-elect's threats to turn Canada into the 51st state.
At a press conference Tuesday, Ford sported a reverse of Donald Trump's now-iconic, glaring red hat, usually embossed with Trump's infamous catch phrase, "MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN."
Ford's version? Tory blue, with a Canadian flag, that read "CANADA IS NOT FOR SALE."
It's the latest stunt in Ford's efforts to combat Trump's economic threats, as Canada's premiers meet in Ottawa to discuss the looming prospect of a trade war. The Ontario premier has repeatedly taken to U.S. news outlets, threatened to cut off Canadian energy exports and warned of a half-million job losses as he ramps up his rhetoric to take on Trump.
The U.S. president-to-be, who will take office Monday, has repeatedly trolled Canada and its leaders about becoming a state.
"Many people in Canada LOVE being the 51st State," Trump posted to Truth Social shortly after Justin Trudeau's resignation announcement. "Justin Trudeau knew this, and resigned."
"If Canada merged with the U.S., there would no Tariffs, taxes would go way down, and they would be TOTALLY SECURE from the threat of the Russian and Chinese Ships that are constantly surrounding them," he continued.
Trump doubled down on his comments in a news conference last week, repeating the false notion that the States are "subsidizing" Canada "to the tune of about $200 billion a year" and claiming he approached hockey legend Wayne Gretzky to run for prime minister.
It's a quip experts and senior Canadian government officials had roundly dismissed as a "joke" from the former president - but are now taking it more seriously. Trump has continued trolling Canada since he first broached the subject with Trudeau during his visit to Mar-a-Lago in early December, including writing in a Christmas social media post that Canadians would pay lower taxes and have better military protection under his command.
A new poll from Angus Reid released Tuesday showed that the vast majority of Canadians are opposed to Trump's proposal, though Americans are more open to a more unified North American continent.
The hats were sourced from Jackpine Dynamic Branding, a creative consulting firm led by Emma Cochrane and Liam Mooney. According to their storefront for a "Strong and Free Hat," the hats were a "small act of patriotism, born out of a moment that demanded a response."
The hats are sourced from Vietnam or Bangladesh, according to Strong and Free Hat website.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford arrives for a first ministers meeting in Ottawa on Wednesday.

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Ottawa reveals new details of border security plan ahead of Donald Trump's inauguration

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-reveals-new-details-border-security-plan/docview/3156204153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+reveals+new+details+of+border+security+plan+ahead+of+Donald+Trump%27s+inauguration&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government will soon deploy two leased Blackhawk helicopters as part of its $1.3-billion border security plan that was made in the shadow of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threats to trigger a trade war with Canada.
Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration Minister Marc Miller detailed part of that plan on Wednesday, ahead of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's gathering to discuss Trump's threatened tariffs with the premiers of the provinces and territories.
Alongside the two Blackhawks, which are being leased for $5.3 million, the government is also adding 60 new surveillance drones and is looking to acquire new "mobile surveillance towers" and other new technologies to beef up border security.
The plan was part of last fall's fiscal update, and came after Trump first threatened to impose punishing 25-per-cent tariffs on all Canadian imports into the United States. In laying out that threat, Trump cited the flow of illegal migrants and drugs like fentanyl from Canada and Mexico into the U.S.
On Wednesday, both cabinet ministers repeatedly made the case that apprehensions of asylum-seekers and fentanyl at the northern U.S. border is a fraction of the levels at its frontier with Mexico. They also argued that Canada has been nonetheless taking steps to improve security at the border, and shared statistics like an 89-per-cent decrease in the number of apprehensions heading into the U.S. from June to December 2024.
Earlier this week, after being briefed on the plan, Premier Doug Ford called it "phenomenal."
Asked about how the border plan has not caused Trump to withdraw his threat of tariffs - which he said he would impose on Monday, after he is sworn-in for a second time as U.S. president - Miller said there is no "magic potion" to solve all issues with the incoming American administration.
McGuinty added that Canadian leaders need to work together and avoid "intemperate" pronouncements that shouldn't be taken seriously, and noted Trump's repeated taunts that Canada is a "51st state." Trump also said last week he would use "economic force" to annex Canada, and spoke of erasing the border.
"We're not interested in drama, we're interested in making progress," McGuinty said.
"We are hopeful, and remain hopeful, that the new incoming administration will understand just how incredibly serious we are."
Since Trump's tariff threat, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has claimed the government has lost control of the U.S.-Canada border, and has demanded an immediate election so that his party can better deal with Trump.
At the same time, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has described Trump as a bully and insisted the government must retaliate against any tariffs the Americans impose.
The Liberal government has been working on a potential response to the threatened tariffs, as the Star has reported. It has also not ruled out more extreme measures, such as slapping an export tax on Canadian fossil fuels that get sent to the U.S. - exports that Statistics Canada has said were worth almost $120 billion in 2023.
Premiers, meanwhile, have put forward proposals as well. Ford has pitched co-operation with the U.S. on critical minerals and energy, after previously musing about cutting off electricity that is sent to the U.S. if Trump follows through with his tariffs.
Alberta premier Danielle Smith has pushed back against proposals to restrict energy exports, which are a boon to her province's economy. In December, Smith's United Conservative government announced a $29-million plan to increase security at its border with the U.S. state of Montana.
Smith refused to sign on to a joint statement that Trudeau and the premiers released on Wednesday, over the refusal of other leaders to take export tariffs on fossil fuels off the table of any Canadian response to Trump's tariffs.
On Wednesday, the public safety and immigration ministers insisted Canada is also preparing in case Trump's campaign pledge to start mass deportations of illegal immigrants from the U.S. prompts a surge of asylum-seekers trying to claim refuge in Canada.
Noting no spike has happened yet, Miller said the U.S. needs to make sure no such surge happens, and said migrants who enter into Canada without stopping at a border crossing are "not welcome."
McGuinty added that he plans to invite Trump's incoming "border czar" Tom Homan to go fishing, and wants to discuss the promised mass deportations when they first meet.
CAPTION: Public Safety Minister David McGuinty detailed the security plan on Wednesday.

Subject: Deportation; Exports; Public safety; Fossil fuels; Border security; Tariffs; Political campaigns; Surveillance; Political asylum; Fentanyl; Immigration

Document 99 of 500

Ford government's ServiceOntario move to Staples will cost more than estimated, financial watchdog says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-governments-serviceontario-move-staples-will/docview/3156204134/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government%27s+ServiceOntario+move+to+Staples+will+cost+more+than+estimated%2C+financial+watchdog+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Moving nine ServiceOntario locations into existing Staples stores will cost the government more than it estimated, says a new report from Ontario's financial watchdog.
However, when factoring in the expanded hours offered by the retail store locations, the province will be saving more money than expected, says the report from the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario released Wednesday morning.
While the province estimated it would cost $10.2 million to "relocate and operate" the nine centres, the actual cost is $11.7 million by 2027 - with $1.7 million for start-up costs and then $10 million for labour and overhead, the report says.
However, that extra cost is due to the longer operating hours - which increased by 47.7 per cent and were a key point Premier Doug Ford touted when promoting the Staples deal - which the government didn't fully account for, said the FAO.
A year ago, the province unveiled its plan to move nine of the privately operated centres into existing Staples locations for a three-year pilot program running to 2027, with longer hours, access to free parking and some additional services as deals with the independent operators came to an end.
When accounting for the expanded hours the Staples locations are open, and comparing to a scenario where the old ServiceOntario locations would have matched those longer hours, the province saves $1.2 million, given the original locations would cost $12.9 million, compared to $11.7 million for Staples.
In a statement, a spokesperson for Minister of Public and Business Service Delivery Todd McCarthy said the FAO report "confirms that our partnership with Staples Canada offers better services for people at a better price for taxpayers."
With "more than $1.2 million in cost-savings and 50 per cent more hours of service, nearly one third of transactions are taking place during the extended hours. It is clear that this new approach to deliver more convenience and access is working," said Matthew D'Amico, McCarthy's press secretary and issues manager.
But the deal came under fire from critics for being sole-sourced instead of undergoing a competitive bidding process.
The FAO review was prompted by Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), but the government had welcomed the probe, saying Ontarians were gaining "more convenience, more access, longer hours, weeknights to eight or nine ... all day Saturdays."
Bowman said Wednesday that "the report found that the numbers the Ford government gave the public were wrong."
The Ford government, she added, "has no problem taking profits from Ontario's small businesses and giving them to American companies ... this deal has lacked transparency from the beginning."
McCarthy has previously said the Staples deal is not a new concept for the province because it has, for years, been using retailers - such as Canadian Tire - to house ServiceOntario locations, including when the previous Liberal government was in power.
But Ontario Greens' Leader Mike Schreiner, the MPP for Guelph, accused the government of furthering "their privatization agenda and put profits before the people of Ontario ... Everything this government does is designed to help well-connected corporate insiders and the richest turn a profit at the expense of everyday people in our communities that are struggling to get by."
CAPTION: A year ago, the province unveiled its plan to move nine of the privately operated centres into existing Staples locations for a pilot program.

Subject: Pilot projects; Cost control

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost control

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Secondary teachers' union slams funding with 'Ford High' ad campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/secondary-teachers-union-slams-funding-with-ford/docview/3156204071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Secondary+teachers%27+union+slams+funding+with+%27Ford+High%27+ad+campaign&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's public high school teachers' union has launched a new ad campaign, saying the education system is "at a breaking point" and calling on the provincial government to provide more funding.
"Our schools are falling apart, our teachers and education workers are burning out, and students are being left behind," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.
The campaign, which features ads fashioned as a TV series trailer about the dismal conditions in "Ford High," is "set in a dystopian near-future where public education is crumbling due to defunding and neglect. Using dark humour and poignant storytelling, the campaign sheds light on the current trajectory of Ontario's education system under Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government," the union said in a news release.
The ads will be posted on social media and broadcast on Crave, CTV and CBC Gem, among others, and come as Ford continues to refuse to rule out calling a snap provincial election.
The union did not provide details of the cost of the ad campaign.
In late 2023, the high school teachers' union, school boards and the provincial government turned to binding arbitration to reach a tentative agreement and settle some issues, including salary increases. All other teacher unions followed suit, leading to labour peace in schools.
School boards have said provincial funding, while increasing by $745 million to $29 billion this school year, has not kept up with their actual costs. The Ontario Public School Boards' Association estimates that, when adjusted for inflation, the province is actually spending $776 less per student this year.
Ontario's Catholic teachers have launched their own online campaign at knowmore.ca, saying the government needs to address class sizes and staffing in schools.
CAPTION: On Jan. 15, 2025, the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation launched an ad campaign that says the province's education system is "at a breaking point" and calls on the Ford government to provide more funding.Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation president Karen Littlewood speaks to union members at their annual general meeting in Toronto on Aug. 18, 2022.

Document 101 of 500

Canada Carbon Rebate payments go out today. Here's how much most Ontarians will get

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-carbon-rebate-payments-go-out-today-heres/docview/3155707242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Carbon+Rebate+payments+go+out+today.+Here%27s+how+much+most+Ontarians+will+get&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada Carbon Rebate payments will go out on Wednesday across Canada. But with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre promising to "Axe the Tax" should he be elected, the future of the payments remains uncertain.
Canadians will receive anywhere between $95 to $225 per quarter from the carbon price rebate, with most Ontarians receiving at least $140 per quarter, up from $122 last year thanks to a boost to the rebate in April 2024.
The rebate is delivered every three months through deposits as a direct refund for the Liberal government's carbon levy - a program, often referred to by the opposition Conservatives as a "carbon tax," that has two components. The program includes a pricing system for carbon-emitting heavy industries and a fuel charge on gasoline and home heating.
Here's what you need to know about the program and whether you're eligible.
What is the carbon tax rebate?
The credit is meant to help individuals and families offset the extra costs from the federal government's pollution pricing, according to the Canada Revenue Agency. In a review of the program, the parliamentary budget officer found in July 2023 that most Canadians in 2021 got more from the Canada Carbon Rebate (CCR) than they paid through the carbon levy. But, the budget watchdog, in a more expansive analysis, found that Canadians would see a "net loss" in the coming years from the carbon pricing scheme and rebate.
Another more recent analysis by the parliamentary budget officer, from Oct. 10, found that the industrial carbon price was more effective in slashing greenhouse gas emission than the consumer price.
The Chiefs of Ontario and the Attawapiskat First Nation called for a judicial review of the carbon levy in November 2023 over their concerns about the disproportionate impact of the pricing scheme.
The government made some changes to the carbon pricing system in late October 2023, doubling the top-up to the rebate for rural Canadians and temporarily pausing the fuel charge for heating oil.
In Ontario, the carbon pricing program is administered at a federal level, with the federal government returning 90 per cent proceeds from those charges to eligible residents.
The CCR has gained attention not only for its rebrand - which the government implemented in an attempt to make its contentious climate policy more palatable - but also for its resistance from provincial leaders. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe, in February, said his province would not submit the money it owed for the carbon price on natural gas, leading to the federal government responding by threatening to deny payment of the rebate to Saskatchewan residents.
Several premiers across the country, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, called on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to pause the carbon price increase on April 1. Trudeau did not oblige, accusing conservative premiers of "(making) political hay" out of the carbon price and challenging them to develop an alternative policy to reduce emissions.
In April 2024, Jagmeet Singh backpedaled on his support for the consumer portion of the carbon levy, emphasizing the need for climate policies that target "big polluters."
Pierre Poilievre and his Conservative party, who have a definitive lead in the polls ahead of an expected spring election once the Liberal party elects a new leader, have long rallied against the carbon levy.
Who is eligible for the carbon tax rebate?
Residents of Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario were eligible when the program first rolled out and the program was expanded in July 2023 to include Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and New Brunswick.
To receive the benefit, you must be a resident of a CCR-eligible province on the first day of the payment month. You must also be at least 19-years old. If you are under that age, you must either have (or previously had) a spouse or common-law partner, or are (or previously were) a parent and live (or previously lived) with your child.
How much will I receive in carbon tax rebate credits?
Ontarians eligible for the rebate will receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19. A family of four in Ontario, according to the federal government, would receive $280.
The payments are universal and not dependent on income levels. Here's how it breaks down by province, for an individual, per quarter:
Alberta: $225Manitoba: $150New Brunswick: $95Newfoundland and Labrador: $149Nova Scotia: $103Ontario: $140Prince Edward Island: $110Saskatchewan: $188
The CCR also includes a 20 per cent supplemental bump for residents in small and rural communities, which includes all eligible P.E.I. residents, according to the government. However, some rural communities have spoken out about the rules demarcating rural towns, saying the government has unfairly looped them in with nearby cities.
Recipients can also expect quarterly payments in April, July and October this year.
How do I receive the carbon tax payment?
Eligible CCR recipients should see the credit in the form of a cheque or direct deposit automatically, without needing to apply. Canadians who file their taxes electronically before March 15 should receive their payment on the quarterly schedule, according to the government's website. Those filing later can expect their CCR payments six to eight weeks after their tax return is assessed.
If you have a spouse or common-law partner, the person whose tax return was assessed first will receive the total credit for the family. It doesn't matter which person receives the rebate as the total amount will be the same.
People with tax debt will see the credit automatically subtracted from the amount they owe.
The Canada Revenue Agency noted it may take up to 10 working days to receive a CCR payment.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre, speaking at a press conference on Parliament Hill in the summer, has promised to "axe the tax" and end the Liberal government's carbon pricing program if the Conservatives win a spring election.

Subject: Conservatism; Climate policy; Carbon; Judicial reviews; Electronic filing of tax returns; Political parties; Environmental tax; Federal government; Canadians; Rural areas

Business indexing term: Subject: Electronic filing of tax returns Environmental tax

Location: Canada; Saskatchewan Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Company / organization: Name: Canada Revenue Agency; NAICS: 921130

Title: Canada Carbon Rebate payments go out today. Here's how much most Ontarians will get

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Section: news/canada

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 102 of 500

Canada would not immediately match Donald Trump tariffs with 'dollar for dollar' penalties: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-would-not-immediately-match-donald-trump/docview/3155707067/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+would+not+immediately+match+Donald+Trump+tariffs+with+%27dollar+for+dollar%27+penalties%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Canada would not immediately match any U.S. tariffs with "dollar for dollar" penalties on American products coming into Canada - an approach that Ontario Premier Doug Ford views as not "proportional" or strong enough to counter Donald Trump's threats, the Star has learned.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told premiers "everything is on the table" as he met them behind closed doors Wednesday, sources said.
But Trudeau conveyed his government's reluctance to immediately levy dollar-for-dollar counter-tariffs because Ottawa does not yet know whether the incoming Trump administration would follow through on Trump's vow to slap a 25-per-cent surcharge on everything Canada and Mexico ship to the U.S.
If the Americans go ahead with that threat, it would represent an "absolutely massive" surcharge on Canadian exports to the U.S. with one government official saying a dollar-for-dollar counterpunch could cost $600 billion.
Instead the federal government discussed an initial list that amounts to more than $150 billion in counter-tariffs, said a senior federal official.
Trudeau told premiers it is not necessarily the end of what Canada would do, the official added.
Another senior federal official, speaking on a background-only basis to brief reporters, said that if Trump issues tariffs but exempts Canadian oil and gas, for example, that would show where the Americans feel they are vulnerable.
But the official said Trudeau has not ruled out any move to potentially make Americans feel the pain of higher priced Canadian oil exports - over the objection of Alberta. The second official said the point is to to take actions that would make the Americans feel pain and lift their tariffs, but not so much that it would hurt Canadians or lead to a decade-long tariff war. The official noted that America refines and sells fuel products back to Canada and the federal government is aware that export tariffs could also end up hurting Canadian households at the gas pump.
Trudeau also urged premiers that everyone has to be prepared for a period of pain.
The federal official said three premiers in the room also urged their colleagues not to voice their complaints about Canada's strategy publicly, but to express dissent with each other in private - remarks that were construed as aimed at Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
Smith attended the meeting virtually, and a pool reporter said her screen was a tropical backdrop, prompting Québec Premier François Legault to ask if she was still at Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort - which she visited last weekend.
Canada's counter-tariff list would not take immediate effect but would be subject to a period of public consultation.
At the end of their meeting, first ministers tried to paper over cracks in Team Canada.
Premiers reversed a decision to hold a separate news conference from the prime minister.
Earlier, differences were on full display.
Leaders of Ontario, Quebec and Newfoundland insisted energy, critical minerals and hydroelectricity should be in play to counter Trump's tariff threats -
Premier Doug Ford, sporting a ball cap with a "Canada Is Not For Sale" logo, said the U.S. president-elect is going to "try to devastate our country," and provinces with different interests cannot get in the way Canada's national response.
Ford said key commodity exports are among Canada's "aces" in any trade war.
"You're in a card game, but you don't show your opposition your cards. You have some aces to hold on to, those aces, but it's all part of negotiation tactics," he said.
"I understand different jurisdictions around the country have their concerns," said Ford. "But nothing is more important than standing united.
"Nothing is more important than keeping every tool in our tool box, no matter if it's energy or other commodities - high-grade nickel from Ontario, great aluminum from Quebec ... potash from Saskatchewan. These are the tools that we have. You don't negotiate through weakness, you negotiate through strength."
Legault echoed that sentiment in French, saying while his top priority is to prevent Trump from executing his threat of a 25-per-cent levies on all Canadian products, "we have to prepare a retaliation plan."
Legault said the premiers will discuss "many scenarios" behind closed doors Wednesday for how Canada should react, but "we are excluding nothing" in terms of what actions the federal government should take.
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey used a chess analogy to make clear that oil and hydroelectricity exports should be considered part of how Canada could apply pressure to the U.S.
"I see energy as Canada's queen in this game of chess. We don't need to expose our queen too early, and the opposition does need to know that the queen exist, but they don't need to know what we're going to do with the queen. So let's see what the tariffs actually are first, and have a proportional, but let me be very clear, a strong response to the United States," Furey said.
"But we need to in no way, shape or form, let the urgency of this economic and fiscal crisis jeopardize our national identity."
Smith is on record as opposing any federal move to weaponize oil and gas exports against the United States.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe also warned that Canada should not levy tariffs on its own products, or restrict energy or other vital exports to the U.S. in other ways. He said the leaders' proposals should not escalate "any of the divisions that we have in this nation or on this continent."
Instead, Moe wants talks to focus on how integrated the economies of Canada and the U.S. are, and to "morph the discussion" with Trump back to what the countries can do together to ensure energy, food and manufacturing "security."
"Taxing Canadian-produced goods in the form of export tariffs ... would be not only counterproductive to this entire conversation," he said, but would hurt Canada. "Canada does not do well in that environment. I would say North Americans will not do well."
The premiers met against a backdrop of increasing national anxiety as Trump's inauguration looms, along with his vow to impose tariffs on Canadian products on his first day in office. That all increases political pressure on Trudeau - who took no questions before the meeting - and the premiers.
In a speech in Washington on Wednesday, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson pitched the benefits of free trade, including a proposal for an "alliance" between Canada and the U.S. on energy and minerals, an idea that Ontario's premier has also raised.
Wilkinson said there are chances for both countries to put money toward projects to produce more minerals like germanium and gallium in a way that displaces supplies currently provided by China.
He also said the countries can collaborate to increase uranium supplies to wean themselves off purchases from Russia, and to "enhance the flow" of crude oil from Alberta into the U.S. to help Trump achieve his goal of "energy dominance."
Trump, in a departure from President Joe Biden's emphasis on climate action, has promised to "drill, baby drill" and increase American fossil-fuel production.
"While a single, talented player can make a difference, a well-coordinated team is unstoppable. Such is the energy and minerals relationship between Canada and the United States," Wilkinson said, according to a draft of his speech that was provided to the Star.
The threat of tariffs has spooked Canadian businesses, with industry associations warning about the damaging fallout of a trade war with the country's biggest commercial partner.
In a letter to Trudeau and opposition leaders, the head of the Canadian Manufacturers and Exporters urged the federal government to respond to any American tariffs in a careful and targeted manner "to minimize collateral damage."
The letter also called for government relief to cover tariff-related costs that exporters might incur for at least three months, along with other aid like deferred taxes to help weather a potential trade spat.
The association said MPs should be recalled if any legislation is necessary to confront the situation, after Trudeau prorogued Parliament when he announced his intention to resign after the Liberal leadership race to replace him.
CAPTION: Left to right, Ontario Premier Doug Ford, Quebec Premier Francois Legault and New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt speak before a first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on Jan. 15, 2025.

Ford government reveals proposed location for massive nuclear power plant

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-reveals-proposed-location-massive/docview/3155706864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+reveals+proposed+location+for+massive+nuclear+power+plant&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is focusing on the Port Hope area - no stranger to the nuclear industry - as the best bet for a massive new nuclear power station to meet forecasts for soaring electricity needs in the next 25 years.
But there are many hurdles in terms of community consultation, studies and approvals that make it unlikely that a power plant producing up to 10,000 megawatts would be up and running until the 2040s.
"We have a lot of work ahead of us," Ontario Power Generation (OPG) president Nicole Butcher told a news conference Wednesday announcing the Lake Ontario town east of Oshawa has expressed interest in being the host.
The site in Wesleyville has long been owned by OPG and was destined to become an oil-fired power plant until a 1970s oil crisis sent fuel prices skyrocketing and scuttled that plan. It has been sitting idle but is zoned for a power plant and is close to transmission lines, as well as the existing Darlington nuclear plant.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce said the new plant, if it is built following extensive consultations with Port Hope and the Williams Treaties First Nations, would be equivalent to "two Darlingtons," making it one of the biggest nuclear generating stations in the world and capable of powering 10 million homes.
While Darlington and the older Pickering nuclear plant east of it use Canada-designed CANDU nuclear technology, no decisions have been made on the type of reactors that could be built on the 1,300-acre Wesleyville site.
Officials at a background briefing on the plan said if all goes well, the first reactor could be operating in the 2040s, although Lecce spoke of a "10-to-15 year time frame."
Port Hope was the only one of three municipalities that expressed interest in a power plant after the province in late November named sites it owns in Wesleyville, Nanticoke on the north shore of Lake Erie and along the St. Clair River south of Sarnia, as possible locations.
The Nanticoke and Sarnia-area sites were home to massive coal-fired generation stations shut down years ago in the fight against climate change.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner has called for public hearings on a new energy plan the Ford government is developing in the wake of forecasts that the need for electricity will jump 75 per cent by 2050.
He wants to see a "competitive procurement process for the lowest-cost, emission-free electricity" instead of automatically choosing nuclear power.
Lecce has said the government is considering "all types of new energy generation" but nuclear is needed as emission-free baseload power.
CAPTION: The Darlington nuclear plant sits close to the site in Wesleyville where the Ontario government would like to build a massive power plant.

Document 104 of 500

U.S. tariffs could cost Ontario 500,000 jobs: Ford: Crisis may require billions in stimulus funds, premier says - and early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/u-s-tariffs-could-cost-ontario-500-000-jobs-ford/docview/3155545418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+tariffs+could+cost+Ontario+500%2C000+jobs%3A+Ford%3A+Crisis+may+require+billions+in+stimulus+funds%2C+premier+says+-+and+early+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Half a million Ontario workers could lose their jobs if American tariffs hit full-force on a wide range of industries once Donald Trump is back in the White House, Premier Doug Ford warns.
Ford painted a stark unemployment picture for the manufacturing engine of Canada as premiers head to Ottawa to meet with departing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Wednesday.
It will be the first in-person leaders' meeting in two years and comes against the backdrop of concerns that Canada's response to Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat has been hamstrung by federal Liberal leadership tumult.
"This is serious, it's unprecedented, and let's hope it doesn't cost 500,000 jobs," Ford said Tuesday, noting that number is based on estimates provided by provincial ministries this week with the fear that factories could be shuttered.
"It all depends on the amount of tariffs, what sectors they're affecting," said the premier, who backs Canadian retaliatory levies if Trump doesn't back down.
Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, Trudeau's point person on the U.S. trade war threat, said "we're not going to speculate" on how many people could be thrown out of work across Canada if the U.S. imposes levies on Canadian products shipped to America. Nor would he say whether Canada would respond by putting an export surcharge on oil and gas shipments to U.S. customers - a move Alberta and Saskatchewan adamantly oppose.
"We'll be ready, of course, to respond from a position of strength, and, we hope, as a unified country," LeBlanc said at Queen's Park with Ford at his side.
Trump begins his second term as president on Monday.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who visited the president-elect at his Mar-a-Lago resort over the weekend, has spoken out against retaliatory tariffs against the U.S. that would impact her province's lucrative oil export revenues.
But LeBlanc stressed "the only responsible thing for the national government is to acknowledge that all options are on the table."
"We need to be ready to deploy all of the measures necessary to defend the Canadian economy. But we're not going to publicly speculate what is or what isn't an option," he said as Ford nodded approvingly.
New Brunswick Premier Susan Holt said in an interview that despite political turmoil in Ottawa, the premiers have worked with the federal government on potential tariff lists, and have reached out with American counterparts to try and pre-empt any levies.
Holt wants to see how the federal government intends to deal with any influx of migrants to Canada should Trump also issue a mass deportation order.
"We'll need clear processes in terms of how we detain folks who have crossed over into Canada illegally and then where their final destination is, whether it's back to the U.S., where they're being deported from, or to a country of origin."
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said"a unified Team Canada approach" is essential after Wednesday's confab.
"I would like to see a cohesive strategy about a proportional response," said Furey, adding it could entail "fiscal tariffs" or more targeted "political" levies that would directly impact Trump and his sphere of influencers.
Ford praised LeBlanc for Ottawa's "solid" $1.3-billion border plan that includes long-range surveillance drones, new canine units, helicopters and more RCMP and Canada Border Services Agency patrols.
It is hoped that might appease Trump, who launched his tariff salvo against Canada and Mexico with a demand that America's neighbours tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Details of the border security plan will be made public Wednesday morning at an Ottawa news conference by Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Minister Marc Miller.
With Ontario doing $500 billion in two-way trade annually with the U.S., Ford has repeatedly said his province would be hurt more than any other.
LeBlanc expressed gratitude to the premier for being "an effective advocate for the interests of the Canadian economy."
Ford said Trump's plan for slapping 25 per cent tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico could necessitate "tens of billions" of dollars in government supports for Ontario workers and industries.
Ottawa would be there to help just as the two governments worked in unison during the COVID-19 pandemic, added LeBlanc.
"The government of Canada will, of course, be there to support industries and workers in collaboration with Premier Ford and his government and (Wednesday's) conversation allows all of us to discuss how we can be ready next week," the finance minister said.
With speculation rampant about a snap provincial election within weeks, Ford argued the level of spending is so significant it may require him to seek a new mandate.
But he would not detail how any such government expenditures could break down.
According to the provincial government, there were 8.1 million people working in Ontario last month - 82 per cent in full-time jobs and the unemployment rate is 7.5 per cent. So the loss of 450,000 to 500,000 jobs would be a serious blow.
Ford, who will be in Washington twice next month to lobby U.S. lawmakers against the measures, said "these tariffs will affect every single person, even if they don't think there's a tariff in their sector - it's going to indirectly affect them, it's not going to be good."
Ford dismissed concerns that plunging Ontario into an election, instead of waiting until the next scheduled provincewide vote in June 2026, would add to the "uncertainty" he has been complaining about in Ottawa.
"I'll still be premier if that happens. We haven't decided. I'll still be working, answering the calls, speaking to U.S. governors and congresspeople, speaking to other premiers ... you never stop working."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie have urged Ford not to call an early election and said they would work with his majority government on economic stimulus packages.
"You've got a mandate," Stiles told Ford on Monday. "You don't need to go to the polls."
Crombie said "Donald Trump and his tariffs should be treated as a threat, not as an excuse for an illegitimate early election call" that would cost taxpayers $175 million.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star
