Document 130 of 500

Chow under pressure as budget talks begin: Property tax increase expected to be less than last year's record hike but ?nding funds for better services could be a challenge for mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-under-pressure-as-budget-talks-begin/docview/3154671453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+under+pressure+as+budget+talks+begin%3A+Property+tax+increase+expected+to+be+less+than+last+year%27s+record+hike+but+%3Fnding+funds+for+better+services+could+be+a+challenge+for+mayor&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The property tax hike in this year's city budget will likely be less painful for Torontonians than the historic increase Mayor Olivia Chow imposed in 2024.
But while the spending plan set to launch this week is expected to include more money for transit, libraries, emergency services and housing, allies of the mayor warned there won't be across-the-board improvements to many services residents depend on as Toronto still faces serious financial challenges.
Crafting the annual spending plan will be a delicate balancing act for Chow, who was elected on a pledge to improve day-to-day services, but who is also facing pressure to control spending and ease residents' tax burden. She will have to find ways to pay for increasingly expensive essential services while making up an opening operating shortfall that has been estimated at more than $1 billion.
"It's going to be a tough year," said Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre), who sits on the budget committee. He warned the cost of recent labour settlements and uncertainty over a potential change in the federal government will make the process even harder.
The mayor and city staff will launch the budget Monday, kicking off weeks of consultations and committee meetings. Under the strong-mayor system imposed by the province, Chowis required to formally propose her version of the spending plan by Feb. 1. It will go to council on Feb. 11.
The 2024 budget - Chow's first after being elected - was funded through a 9.5 per cent property tax increase, the largest one-year hike in post-amalgamation Toronto. Moise predicted this year's property tax hikewon't be a repeat of that eye-watering raise this time.
"It won't be as much as last year," said Moise. "I think the city is doing the best it can, the mayor and council are doing the best they can, to keep taxes at a minimum this year."
That won't be easy.
Even before full details of the budget are public, it's clear city spending - and the taxes residents pay for them - is set to rise, with the operating budget expected to exceed $17 billion.
As a starting point, in 2023 council endorsed annual property tax increases of 1.5 per cent every year until 2035 for the "city building fund," which raises money for capital projects such as transit and housing.
Then there's the 2025 opening budget pressure - the inflation- and growth-related costs required just to maintain existing service levels - which last fall the city estimated at $1.2 billion.
By law, the city can't run a deficit. It also gets the smallest share of taxpayer dollars compared to higher levels of government - for every one dollar collected, 10 cents flow to local government - and each year the city scrambles to fill the opening gap through a mixture of tax increases, internal savings, assistance from other levels of government and other strategies.
In addition to bridging the shortfall, the mayor and council will have to account for new spending already built into the budget. That includes new contracts for city workers, such as the agreement reached with transit employees last June to avoid a TTC strike that will require about $55 million this year. Settlements for police, firefighters and the city workers' two main unions are either still being worked out or have yet to be announced, and are also expected to increase costs.
There's also new spending on city programs Chow has identified as priorities, including an additional $46.2 million for the Toronto Police Service to hire more officers, and $85 million for TTC operating costs that will go toward freezing fares and additional transit service.
Chow has also promised $2 million to expand library hours, $3.9 million to enrol more children in student nutrition programs and $3 million to deploy more traffic agents.
"Mayor Chow is clear, we want to invest in the services that the people of Toronto rely on," her spokesperson Bradley Hammond said in an email, adding that includes "looking for efficiencies and savings to return best value for the people of Toronto."
The mayor and council will have some breathing room thanks to the "new deal" Chow reached with Premier Doug Ford in 2023, which will provide hundreds of millions of dollars for shelter and transit operations, and help slice the TTC's estimated 10-year state-of-good-repair backlog by almost 50 per cent, to a projected $4.34 billion.
Still, with costs piling up, Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16, Don Valley East), predicted the property tax increase this year could be between six to eight per cent.
Although new spending can also be offset by savings found elsewhere in the budget and other measures, Burnside noted the increase to the TTC budget alone is equivalent to about a two-per-cent property tax hike - each one per cent hike is expected to generate more than $45 million in new revenue.
The mayor's budget chief, Coun. Shelley Carroll (Ward 17, Don Valley North), wouldn't confirm what the increase will be, saying only "stay tuned."
Whatever the final number, Burnside argued the mayor wouldn't need such a steep hike if she had done more to rein in spending. "I think we've seen really no effort at any sort of cost control," he said.
Burnside said some of the city's expensive housing initiatives should be covered by other levels of government, and added that under Chow Toronto is "wading into areas that aren't our responsibility."
Coun. Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park), budget vice-chair and Chow ally, blamed Toronto's tight financial situation on successive fiscally conservative mayors who ushered in "13 years of austerity" and left the city "in a terrible mess."
He said that while Chow has made commitments to boost funding for "fundamental services" to improve people's lives, there will likely only be enough money for "targeted improvements" this year. As a starting point many departments were asked to prepare for a zero per cent budget increase.
"We can't make improvements across the board," Perks said.
The fractious debates over police spending and federal refugee supports that dominated last year's budget process will likely be avoided in 2025. Chow agreed to boost spending for the force's $1.2-billion budget by 3.9 per cent this year after police presented a multi-year hiring plan.
While a change in federal government could come early this year, Carroll said there will be "no surprises" on refugee funding in Monday's budget after the public battle the city waged with Ottawa over the issue in 2024.
Last year, the only service on the chopping block was the city's $16-million windrow clearing program, which removes the pile of snow that blocks the end of driveways after a snow plow has cleared the street. It was eventually saved after outcry from suburban councillors.
Carroll said the public shouldn't expect similar cuts to be proposed in this year's spending plan. "We set guidelines to avoid that as much as possible," she said, adding that the direction to city departments was "what are more efficient ways of doing things, but still doing the things."
CREDIT: Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Subject: Municipal employees; Councils; Cost control; Property taxes; Tax increases; Federal budget; Public safety; Emergency services; Housing prices

Full text: Time for King to address Trump's play for Canada
Trudeau says Trump is trying to distract from cost of tariffs with 51st state remarks, Jan. 9
Isn't it about time for the King of England (and incidentally of Canada) to let the soon-to-be-crowned king of America know that the lands north of the 49th parallel are not for sale or for the taking?
A short statement in this regard would go a long way to strengthen his otherwise doubtful usefulness.
Vittorio Frigerio, Toronto
Tariffs are
no problem
Trump says the U.S. doesn't need anything Canada has. Experts say there are 594 billion reasons why he's wrong, Jan. 9
My response to Donald Trump's 25 per cent tariffs would be to eliminate all tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles. Who wouldn't want to be able to purchase a brand-new EV for $20,000?
John Selmys, Toronto
Will wildfires cause Trump
to rethink tariffs?
The level of destruction in the California wildfires is beyond comprehension. Will Donald Trump decide to not impose a 25 per cent tariff on the Canadian softwood that they need to rebuild?
Geoff Moore, Woodbridge
Poilievre mum on Trump diatribes on annexation
Threats, insults and misinformation by Donald Trump toward Canada on a daily basis. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly have put up a fight for Canada. Meanwhile, Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre, an insulter and bully to Canadians, curled up cowardly in a corner. Instead of making a meaningful comment, he is busy preparing to go to Trump's inauguration to suck up to Trump and Elon Musk.
I have no doubt whatsoever that Poilievre, Danielle Smith and Scott Moe will sell out Canada to Donald Trump. They have the same ideologies as the Republicans - they do not believe in climate change or vaccines; they support religious extremists and conspiracy theories and align themselves with racist groups and personalities like Tucker Carlson. Where are you Poilievre?
Taij Chand, Toronto
Difficult to watch long-time ally turn against us
It sickens me that our neighbour and friend, who we've fought beside in the Great War, the Second World War, Korea and Afghanistan, suddenly has turned against us by threatening to break our backs economically with a 25 per cent tariff while also being unsure whether they'd defend us if we were attacked by a hostile nation. Sadly, few Americans are pointing out Canada's help after 9/11 and the care and compassion they received from Newfoundlanders during the crisis.
Peter J Middlemore Sr.,
Windsor, Ont.
Canada must
be prepared to shut our doors
We need to let it be known that we will react immediately if the U.S. Head Fool implements his threats against Canada. The U.S. will be in a nasty position when he does his usual foolish stuff but we need to be ready to immediately shut our doors. This includes our exports of fuel, water, etc ... so that Americans realize they made a huge mistake bringing the old, crooked guy back. Stand up Canada, support your governments and make the U.S. understand their mistake.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
Finally, Trudeau is acting like a prime minister
Not a 'snowball's chance in hell' of Canada becoming 51st state: Trudeau, Jan. 8
"Not a snowball's chance in hell" are, for me, the first words in a long while where Justin Trudeau has sounded like the prime minister of Canada rather than Katie Telford's scripted automaton.
How ironic for him to start sounding like a PM only when he hardly is one anymore. The snowball phrase is just right, neither Trumpian vulgarity nor demeaning obsequiousness. It reaches the Jean Chrétien standard of plain speaking.
John Goyder, Oakville
Let's settle this with a hockey tournament
It's time to settle this 51st state business and there is only one way to do it: a four out of seven hockey tournament. Only players born in Canada or the U.S. can play on their country's team.
If the U.S. wins, they get Canada. If we win, we get all the states that touch Canada. Put up or shut up! Let's see what the Vegas odds are on this sporting event.
Roger Hilderley, Stratford, Ont.
Partisan ad indefensible
Thomas Caldwell ad, Jan. 7
I am not opposed to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau receiving legitimate criticism and I recognize that it is past time for him to step down as Liberal leader and prime minister. I also accept that I will sometimes disagree with Star columnists and contributors, but that is part of a balanced newspaper's appeal. This sort of partisan ad, however, is indefensible. Sadly, it reminded me of the puerile "F--- Trudeau" messages we've all seen the past few years. I don't know how Thomas Caldwell presumes he speaks for all "Canadians to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau" or why he feels comparing Trudeau to the feckless, Hitler-emboldening Neville Chamberlain is justified. It is ironic that the ad appeared only three days after we learned of the Star's worthy initiative, Toronto the Better, which spoke of the importance of positive influences on our happiness, such as having "a gracious mindset," something this ad's writer clearly doesn't possess.
Craig Leach, Scarborough
Ad makes me question chair
of company
It's rather bold of the chair of Caldwell Securities Ltd. and Urbana Corporation to use his privately held investment company funds to pay for a full-page advertisement. It is fine to have an opinion, but to use company funds or position to fund this makes me not want to use Caldwell Securities.
Anne Varey, Oakville

Document 132 of 500

Alberta premier takes her case to Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-premier-takes-her-case-trump/docview/3154671370/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+premier+takes+her+case+to+Trump&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said she had a "friendly and constructive conversation" with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump this weekend at Mar-a-Lago, Trump's resort in Palm Beach, Fla.
The United Conservative Party leader posted photos of her visit, including ones with Trump and Canadian businessman Kevin O'Leary, on X on Sunday afternoon.
"I emphasized the mutual importance of the U.S.-Canadian energy relationship, and specifically, how hundreds of thousands of American jobs are supported by energy exports from Alberta," Smith wrote on the social media platform.
Trump, who will be sworn into office on Jan. 20, has threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian products. He has also recently mused about using "economic force" to annex Canada and make it the 51st state.
Smith wrote that she also had discussions with other members of Trump's incoming administration and was "encouraged to hear their support for a strong energy and security relationship with Canada."
"On behalf of Albertans, I will continue to engage in constructive dialogue and diplomacy with the incoming administration and elected federal and state officials from both parties, and will do all I can to further Alberta's and Canada's interests," she wrote.
Smith has previously said that her energy-rich province is in the best position to make the case that Canada-U.S. trade benefits both countries.
"We can use oil and gas as a point of leverage to say, because of this strong relationship, all Canadian goods should be tariff-free," she told the CBC in a year-end interview.
Smith isn't the only provincial leader pitching the importance of Canada-U.S. trade relations stateside.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has been on an unprecedented U.S. media blitz, appearing on Fox News, CNN, NBC and other networks to speak directly to American audiences about the potential consequences of Trump's proposed tariffs and how Canada might retaliate.
O'Leary, the Canadian investor of "Shark Tank" and "Dragons' Den" fame, also posted a photo of Smith's visit with Trump. O'Leary has embraced Trump's talk of making Canada part of the U.S, saying it could be the start of an "economic union."
"Think about the power of combining the two economies, erasing the border between Canada and the United States and putting all that resource up to the northern borders where China and Russia are knocking on the door," he said in a Fox News interview on Dec. 26.
In the same interview, O'Leary, who once ran for the leadership of the federal Conservative party, said removing all trade barriers, using a "common currency" and creating an "almost EU-like passport" would be beneficial to both countries.
CREDIT: Brendan Kennedy Toronto Star

Document 133 of 500

After big tax hike, voters will be looking for value

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/after-big-tax-hike-voters-will-be-looking-value/docview/3154671233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+big+tax+hike%2C+voters+will+be+looking+for+value&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Budget season in Toronto is a cold, grey time - not only because it takes place during the short, dark days of January and February, and not only because of the combination of boredom and dread that many people feel when forced to confront a balance sheet.
It's also because it's the time of year when it's hard to avoid facing consequences. The bills are coming due, the roof we refused to fix has collapsed, it is time to stop putting off thinking about it and make decisions about what we're going to do.
Too often in the past, our city council has decided to take the least action possible that would allow them to postpone a reckoning for another year. City managers and other staff would put up graphics of cliffs and icebergs and employ other metaphors to portray the staggering dimensions of the infrastructure deficit that had accumulated; people would line up to make speeches about how essential fraying services were to their families and their communities; there'd be talk about facing up to our responsibilities and paying for the things we value.
And then, we would take the cheap way out and kick the can a little further down the road.
In 2024, then new Mayor Olivia Chow took a different approach and raised taxes more than they'd ever been raised. While it was the main subject of debate (and cost the mayor some popular support), when it went to city council it wound up seeming hardly controversial at all. We'd put off taking our medicine for too long; it was time to swallow hard and pay up.
This year, the size of the proposed property tax increase is still going to be a hot topic. But I think a different question is increasingly going to accompany the mayor through budget season. If the old question is, "What do we have to pay?" then the new one becomes, "What do we get for that money?"
Many Torontonians may be willing - not eager, but willing - to pay substantially higher property tax rates than they have in the past specifically because they are frustrated with the strangled quality of city services and infrastructure. The roads have become potholed, TTC service is slow and inconsistent, the parks are full of homeless encampments, there are long waiting lists for kids' swimming lessons - and so on and so forth on down the line. Many of us are willing to pay great city rates, because we don't want small-town cheap services.
But that may be where that long history I mentioned at the beginning of this column becomes an obstacle for Chow: so many dollars are needed just to catch things up to some bare minimum standard and feed decades-long backlogs in funding for decades-long maintenance projects. What can the mayor get the city to do that can make Torontonians feel - today, in ways they can see in their own lives - like things are getting better?
As Chow gets to a point where she's almost halfway through her shortened first term in office, that becomes the pressing question: She is getting us to pay more, do we feel like she's giving us a better city as a result? In some ways, this a harder challenge progressive politicians face than the one tax-cutting populists set for themselves.
Someone like Doug Ford, who argues that government is generally incompetent and that taxpayers should keep their money, can demonstrate success by his own standards simply by mailing out cheques. Someone like Chow, who argues that government is useful and valuable and worth paying more for, has to find ways - not just over the course of generations, but over the course of an election cycle - to demonstrate to people how her approach is working for them and making their lives and their city better.
Chow often mentions the work underway to expand library hours and school meal programs, and her initiatives that will shelter homeless people. I think that's because those things are important to her, but also because they are things many people will be able to notice in their own communities. Chow did two press conferences in the past week leading up to the launch of budget deliberations: one was about keeping TTC fares flat while expanding service, the other was about quadrupling the number of traffic wardens on the street trying to prevent gridlock at the choke points of the road network: two issues that are frustratingly eye level for a lot of people.
How many such initiatives add up to a conclusion of "worth it" in the minds of voters? That's a subjective bar for each of them to set. But it's one the mayor will need to try to clear.
Every budget has those two questions lurking behind all the talk of numbers: What does it cost, and what value does it provide? Last year, all the emphasis was on the first of those. Now, increasingly, many of us will also look all the harder at the second.

Subject: Tax rates; Voters; Homeless people; Property taxes; Tax increases

Document 134 of 500

Ford steps up to set Trump straight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-steps-up-set-trump-straight/docview/3154671155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+steps+up+to+set+Trump+straight&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's taken a mind-bendingly bizarre set of circumstances to turn Doug Ford into the beau ideal of Captain Canada. But such are the times we're in.
First, Donald Trump was elected to a second term - a second term! - as president of the United States, and is about to take office more experienced, more emboldened and less opposed than during his appalling first.
Second, Trump seems to have noticed Canada.
For generations, we turned cartwheels trying to get American leaders to pay attention to us. Now one does. And he wants to own us, just another real-estate acquisition, a fixer-upper with good bones but a lot of expensive frills and architectural details he'd promptly tear down or bulldoze.
Trump has mused - publicly, preposterously and insultingly - about Canada becoming a 51st state. Not very neighbourly of him. Not one little bit.
Say this for him, though: the master troll knows precisely where the blade goes. His instinct for another's weakness and how to make critics yelp is pretty nearly unparalleled.
Well, Trump seems to have got our attention. And the next four years promise to be more ludicrous than any in the history of our role as next-door neighbour to the United States of Egomania.
So who is best fitted to take him on?
It wouldn't be an intellectual type, nor a sober restrained voice of reason. It wouldn't be a master of data and statistical analysis, nor the builder of rational argument.
It turns out the best Trump retardant we have is a man once many regarded as similarly unfit and temperamentally ill-suited to high office.
That would be Douglas Robert Ford Jr., man of the hour.
Trump detests leaders like Justin Trudeau. He wouldn't much like Pierre Poilievre. He would not at all care for any female counterpart.
Trump favours men. Men's men. Plain-talkin', locker-room jokin', Rat Pack sort of 1950s' men. Men who cut deals. Men just like Ford.
Recent dispatches from the premier's office have shown Ford gets this.
They have been couched in the sort of militaristic language that is catnip for the again-to-be-commander-in-chief Trump.
In recently proposing to strengthen security along the Ontario-U.S. border - a long stretch of irritant that pains Trump more than a case of the shingles - Ford launched "Operation Deterrence."
He followed that coup by proposing to build "Fortress Am-Can to power economic prosperity in Canada and the United States."
Other than making the small error of failing to put the U.S. first, the premier sounded more American than the Americans.
He made the Pentagon PR staff who soundtracked the U.S. invasions of Iraq sound like a bunch of pantywaists.
The premier understands Trump's America. He knows it loves nothing so much as political discourse that sounds like a bunch of nine-year-old boys from the 1950s playing at being soldiers.
But that wasn't all. Far from it.
To cap his stellar week, Ford bravely survived a fender-bender on Wednesday, "sideswiped" on Highway 401 after leaving the Darlington Nuclear Generating Station where, as it happens, he was pitching a new energy partnership with the U.S.
A lesser premier would have delivered a bloodless commentary, downplaying the impact and the disaster narrowly dodged.
Not Ford. He told it the way a working stiff does across the lunch table at the plant.
"Oh yeah, we were leaving OPG, driving down the road on the highway and the next thing you know, out of nowhere, the car got side-swiped and that is all I can say," he said.
But, of course, it wasn't.
"We were driving straight and next thing we know we got hammered, just hammered. I'm OK. This head is like limestone. It is as hard as a rock."
The gentleman is a Trump clone. He all but took a bullet for democracy. And he's more fluent in the vernacular of Common Man than the incoming president himself.
Ford will shut down this 51st state nonsense faster than you can say socialized medicine and cannabis on every corner.
All he has to do is invite his soulmate north for a visit, tour him around some of the scruffier parts of the province, tell him Canada is just one big Detroit or Milwaukee, and Trump's goldfish attention span will be swimming laps toward a takeover of the Orkney Islands.
Jim Coyle is a former Toronto Star reporter and feature writer. In his 40-year career, he also wrote for The Canadian Press and the Ottawa Citizen.
CREDIT: Jim Coyle Contributor Jim Coyle is a former Toronto Star reporter and feature writer. In his 40-year career, he also wrote for The Canadian Press and the Ottawa Citizen.;

Document 135 of 500

Citizenship of 'lost Canadians' at risk with Parliament suspended: Families worried about fate of reforms for children born abroad

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/citizenship-lost-canadians-at-risk-with/docview/3154671154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Citizenship+of+%27lost+Canadians%27+at+risk+with+Parliament+suspended%3A+Families+worried+about+fate+of+reforms+for+children+born+abroad&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shortly after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau suspended Parliament last Monday, Carol Sutherland-Brown's phone started ringing.
The calls were coming from other people whose families have been caught up in the long-standing "lost Canadians" fiasco and are concerned about the fate of the highly anticipated citizenship reforms proposed in Bill C-71.
The Ottawa grandmother and others have been fighting to reclaim the citizenship rights taken away from their families under Canada's current second-generation cut-off rule, which denies automatic citizenship to children born abroad because their parents also happened to be born overseas.
More than a year ago, an Ontario court found the law unconstitutional and gave the federal government six months to change it to make it Charter-compliant. The Liberal government introduced Bill C-71 to fix the problem, but the deadline has already been extended three times, to March 19.
This legislation would automatically confer Canadian citizenship on people born abroad before the changes are enacted to a Canadian parent who was also born abroad. Anyone born outside the country subsequently would need to prove their foreign-born Canadian parent had a "substantial connection" with Canada by meeting a residency requirement.
With the prorogation of Parliament until March 24, the bill has now died on the order paper, and a new one would have to be tabled when the House returns. It would be subject to the legislative process from scratch again.
The lost Canadians' families fear that if a confidence vote follows, as expected, it will topple the Liberals and usher in a Conservative government. It was a Conservative government that brought in the second-generation citizenship cut-off in 2009 to begin with.
"This has been an ordeal for me and for the other families, an ordeal that doesn't end," said Sutherland-Brown. Her daughter was born in Saudi Arabia, and two grandchildren were born in the U.K. As a result, the grandchildren lost their Canadian citizenship rights by descent. "We don't know what a new government will do."
Lawyer Sujit Choudhry, who represented lost Canadians in the successful court challenge, said the government has two options: to go before the court for another extension or let the citizenship law be declared unconstitutional.
However, both are problematic.
While there have been similar precedents where the government was granted extensions to comply with court orders, he said the requests were made because Parliament was dissolved and an election was called, and not due to prorogation.
"The question in March will be whether this is the time for this to come to an end , or whether, given the unique political circumstances, some more time should be given," said Choudhry. "What will the government say in court? Well, they'd say a dissolution is imminent, but it hasn't happened yet."
If Ottawa lets the deadline lapse and the two-generation cut-off is thus voided, affected lost Canadians could just come reclaim their citizenship. If the court cuts the government some slack and grants another extension in light of the circumstances, the uncertainty will continue.
"During that extension period, we could very well be in an election, in which case, no bills could be passed," said MP Jenny Kwan, immigration critic of the opposition NDP, and a staunch supporter of the bill.
"After the election, whoever forms government would have to take further actions to be compliant with the court decision. We have to remember that it was the Conservatives who brought in this unconstitutional provision 15 years ago."
In 2009, the then-Conservative government changed the citizenship law and imposed the second-generation cut-off on Canadians born abroad, after Ottawa had faced a massive effort to evacuate 15,000 Lebanese Canadians stranded in Beirut during Israel's month-long war against Hezbollah in 2006.
The $85-million price tag of the evacuation effort sparked a debate over "Canadians of convenience." The government abolished the existing "substantial connection" regime and adopted a blanket rule that denies the first generation born abroad the right to pass on citizenship by descent outside Canada to the second generation born abroad.
Tunisian-born Majda Dabaghi, whose two children were born in France and hence can't be Canadian citizens by descent, is concerned about a Conservative return to power given the party's efforts to block C-71 and a similar bill previously. (The Conservative party didn't respond to a request for comment.)
The cut-off rule "was a racist response to the evacuation of dual nationals out of Lebanon," said Dabaghi, who has continued to vote in Canadian elections after she left Canada in 2007 for a job in international law in the U.K.
"They have done everything humanly possible to filibuster the passing of the legislation, both in the form of Bill C-71 and earlier in the form of Bill S-245. They have put their own politics and political gamesmanship above sound policy, people's lives and our Constitution."
Calling Bill C-71 "a crucial piece of legislation," Immigration Minister Marc Miller also blamed the Conservatives for stalling it. While his office would not say if the government will request that the court grant another extension, it said "Minister Miller is confident that a Liberal government would reintroduce this important bill to the House once resumed."
Although Christina Matula's two children - born in England and Hong Kong - are Canadian citizens, she said she's fighting for their rights to explore the world and work abroad without having to worry about where to start a family.
And she said both the Liberals and Conservatives are at fault because the former also failed to prioritize and expedite the introduction and passing of the bill despite the court order in December 2023.
Her children, now 17 and 14, have attended Canadian international schools, participated in Terry Fox runs and visited family in Canada every summer and Christmas, said Matula, whose family now lives in Finland.
"My children are Canadian by descent and have strong ties to Canada," she said "I want them to have clear and fair criteria to prove their connection to Canada, so they can have the same rights as Canadian-born and naturalized citizens."
CREDIT: Nicholas Keung Toronto Star

Document 136 of 500

Who will step up for this country?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-will-step-up-this-country/docview/3154670877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+will+step+up+for+this+country%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the aftermath of a major historical event, the causative chain that produced it is often shrouded in mystery. The real drivers are almost always impossible to discover in real time. Clarity only emerges decades later, when long-classified documents are finally released from the archives, illuminating what truly transpired.
But sometimes, history doesn't make us wait. This is undeniably true of the recent announcement by our 23rd Prime Minister to resign his office this past week.
No sane human being on planet Earth is asking: "Why?" or "How?" They're asking, "What the hell took him so long?" And maybe that's the question only the historians can solve. But, at the moment, it doesn't matter in the least.
What matters is that this country is staring down the barrel of existential threats. And to appreciate their magnitude, we only need to put two things side by side.
One: We are facing the greatest threat to our sovereignty and prosperity in generations.
Two: We are currently led by a lame-duck Prime Minister who the majority of Canadians have exactly zero confidence in to lead us - let alone guide us through the storm. Whose perhaps final decision of consequence - to prorogue Parliament - has plunged our nation into a state of paralysis.
Trudeau's resignation has left a vacuum for genuine, popularly supported leadership in this country.
So, what happens next? Who steps up in this moment of historic challenge?
It's a painfully familiar feeling: Just when you need something the very most, you find it's hiding from you. Right now, I wish I meant my car keys.
The simple fact is that in our hour of greatest need, we need our greatest people to lead us. And in the wake of the fallout to Justin Trudeau's resignation and Trump's very real threat of economic war, this is what concerns me: that many of our best and brightest will succumb to the temptation to bury their heads in the sand and pretend this is all a dream.
It's not. It's anything but a dream. And nothing is a foregone conclusion.
Leadership is not a title; it's behaviour. It is how you act. It is what you do. And it is what you are prepared to sacrifice for the good of your country.
Fortunately, across jurisdictional and partisan lines we've seen examples of just that. Premier Doug Ford is going into the snake pit on Fox News. Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc has decided it's better to focus his attention on the threat of U.S. tariffs than his personal ambitions to become Liberal leader. These are commendable actions, but we need far more - and not just from our politicians.
In a recent interview with Jordan Peterson, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre underlined that Canadians cannot assume, "by simply voting in an election that ... all the problems are going to reverse instantaneously."
That's neither cynicism nor mere expectation management. It's an acknowledgment of reality. National challenges aren't solved at the ballot box alone - they're solved by extraordinary people stepping up, not just in politics, but in every corner of public life.
Trump himself said he does not care who wins our next federal election. What he does care about is pretty clear: beating us.
That kind of pressure can drive a country into despair, numbing it with a fatalistic sense of inevitability. Or it can do the opposite. It can light a fire. It can inspire resilience, defiance, and the will to fight back.
The most critical question we ought to be asking in this historic moment is not whether Trudeau deserved the slings and arrows or how his legacy will take shape. That will be a rigidly partisan, tiresome tug of war.
The real question is where do we go from here and who rises to the occasion?
"History never looks like history when you are living through it," so the saying goes. Except when it does. This week, many Canadians woke up to the realization that they are living through a turning point. The Prime Minister's resignation isn't the end of the story. It's the prologue to one of the most challenging chapters in our history.
Now comes the hard part.
Jaime Watt is the executive chairman of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc decided it's better to focus his attention on U.S. tariffs than his personal ambitions to become Liberal leader. Commendable, but we need far more, writes Jaime Watt.Justin Tang The associated press file photo
CREDIT: JAIME WATT OPINION Jaime Watt is the executive chairman of Navigator Ltd. and a Conservative strategist. He is a freelance columnist for the Star.;

Document 137 of 500

Alberta Premier Danielle Smith meets with Donald Trump at Mar-a-Lago

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alberta-premier-danielle-smith-meets-with-donald/docview/3154935779/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+Premier+Danielle+Smith+meets+with+Donald+Trump+at+Mar-a-Lago&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said she had a "friendly and constructive conversation" with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump this weekend at Mar-a-Lago, Trump's resort in Palm Beach, Fla.
The United Conservative Party leader posted photos of her visit, including ones with Trump and Canadian businessman Kevin O'Leary, on X on Sunday afternoon.
"I emphasized the mutual importance of the U.S.-Canadian energy relationship, and specifically, how hundreds of thousands of American jobs are supported by energy exports from Alberta," Smith wrote on the social media platform.
Trump, who will be sworn into office on Jan. 20, has threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian products. He has also recently mused about using "economic force" to annex Canada and make it the 51st state.
Smith wrote that she also had discussions with other members of Trump's incoming administration and was "encouraged to hear their support for a strong energy and security relationship with Canada."
"On behalf of Albertans, I will continue to engage in constructive dialogue and diplomacy with the incoming administration and elected federal and state officials from both parties, and will do all I can to further Alberta's and Canada's interests," she wrote.
Smith has previously said that her energy-rich province is in the best position to make the case that Canada-U.S. trade benefits both countries.
"We can use oil and gas as a point of leverage to say, because of this strong relationship, all Canadian goods should be tariff-free," she told the CBC in a year-end interview.
Smith isn't the only provincial leader pitching the importance of Canada-U.S. trade relations stateside. Ontario Premier Doug Ford has been on an unprecedented U.S. media blitz, appearing on Fox News, CNN, NBC and other networks to speak directly to American audiences about the potential consequences of Trump's proposed tariffs and how Canada might retaliate.
O'Leary, the Canadian investor of "Shark Tank" and "Dragons' Den" fame, also posted a photo of Smith's visit with Trump. O'Leary has embraced Trump's talk of making Canada part of the U.S, saying it could be the start of an "economic union."
"Think about the power of combining the two economies, erasing the border between Canada and the United States and putting all that resource up to the northern borders where China and Russia are knocking on the door," he said in a Fox News interview on Dec. 26.
In the same interview, O'Leary, who once ran for the leadership of the federal Conservative party, said removing all trade barriers, using a "common currency" and creating an "almost EU-like passport" would be beneficial to both countries.
CAPTION: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith posted photos to X after meeting with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his Mar-a-Lago club in Florida. She was joined by Canadian businessman Kevin O'Leary, right.
CREDIT: Brendan Kennedy Staff Reporter

Document 138 of 500

What kind property tax hike will Toronto see? Here's what we know about the budget being launched Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-kind-property-tax-hike-will-toronto-see/docview/3154395364/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+kind+property+tax+hike+will+Toronto+see%3F+Here%27s+what+we+know+about+the+budget+being+launched+Monday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The property tax hike in this year's city budget will likely be less painful for Torontonians than the historic increase Mayor Olivia Chow imposed in 2024.
But while the spending plan set to launch next week is expected to include more money for transit, libraries, emergency services and housing, allies of the mayor warned there won't be across-the-board improvements to many services residents depend on as Toronto still faces serious financial challenges.
Crafting the annual spending plan will be a delicate balancing act for Chow, who was elected on a pledge to improve day-to-day services, but who is also facing pressure to control spending and ease residents' tax burden. She will have to find ways to pay for increasingly expensive essential services while making up an opening operating shortfall that has been estimated at more than a billion dollars.
"It's going to be a tough year," said Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre), who sits on the budget committee. He warned the cost of recent labour settlements and uncertainty over a potential change in the federal government will make the process even harder.
The mayor and city staff will launch the budget Monday, kicking off weeks of consultations and committee meetings. Under the strong-mayor system imposed by the province, Chow is required to formally propose her version of the spending plan by Feb. 1. It will go to council on Feb. 11.
Spending on the rise
The 2024 budget - Chow's first after being elected - was funded through a 9.5 per cent property tax increase, the largest one-year hike in post-amalgamation Toronto. Moise predicted this year's property tax hike won't be a repeat of that eye-watering raise this time.
"It won't be as much as last year," said Moise. "I think the city is doing the best it can, the mayor and council are doing the best they can, to keep taxes at a minimum this year."
That won't be easy. Even before full details of the budget are public, it's clear city spending - and the taxes residents pay for them - are set to rise, with the operating budget expected to exceed $17 billion.
As a starting point, in 2023 council endorsed annual property tax increases of 1.5 per cent every year until 2035 for the "city building fund," which raises money for capital projects such as transit and housing.
Then there's the 2025 opening budget pressure - the inflation- and growth-related costs required just to maintain existing service levels - which last fall the city estimated at $1.2 billion.
By law, the city can't run a deficit. It also gets the smallest share of taxpayer dollars compared to higher levels of government - for every one dollar collected, 10 cents flow to local government - and each year the city scrambles to fill the opening gap through a mixture of tax increases, internal savings, assistance from other levels of government and other strategies.
In addition to bridging the shortfall, the mayor and council will have to account for new spending already built into the budget. That includes new contracts for city workers, such as the agreement reached with transit employees last June to avoid a TTC strike that will require about $55 million this year. Settlements for police, firefighters and the city workers' two main unions are either still being worked out or have yet to be announced, and are also expected to increase costs.
New spending adds to the bill
There's also new spending on city programs Chow has identified as priorities, including an additional $46.2 million for the Toronto Police Service to hire more officers, and $85 million for TTC operating costs that will go toward freezing fares and additional transit service.
Chow has also promised $2 million to expand library hours, $3.9 million to enrol more children in student nutrition programs and $3 million to deploy more traffic agents.
"Mayor Chow is clear, we want to invest in the services that the people of Toronto rely on," her spokesperson Bradley Hammond said in an email, adding that includes "looking for efficiencies and savings to return best value for the people of Toronto."
The mayor and council will have some breathing room thanks to the "new deal" Chow reached with Premier Doug Ford in 2023, which will provide hundreds of millions of dollars for shelter and transit operations, and help slice the TTC's estimated 10-year state-of-good-repair backlog by almost 50 per cent, to a projected $4.34 billion.
Property tax hike predictions
Still, with costs piling up, Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16, Don Valley East), predicted the property tax increase this year could be between six to eight per cent.
Although new spending can also be offset by savings found elsewhere in the budget and other measures, Burnside noted the increase to the TTC budget alone is equivalent to about a two per cent property tax hike - each one per cent hike is expected to generate more than $45 million in new revenue.
The mayor's budget chief, Coun. Shelley Carroll (Ward 17, Don Valley North), wouldn't confirm what the increase will be, saying only "stay tuned."
Whatever the final number, Burnside argued the mayor wouldn't need such a steep hike if she had done more to rein in spending. "I think we've seen really no effort at any sort of cost control," he said.
Burnside said some of the city's expensive housing initiatives should be covered by other levels of government, and added that under Chow Toronto is "wading into areas that aren't our responsibility."
Coun. Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park), budget vice-chair and Chow ally, blamed Toronto's tight financial situation on successive fiscally conservative mayors who ushered in "13 years of austerity" and left the city "in a terrible mess."
He said that while Chow has made commitments to boost funding for "fundamental services" to improve people's lives, there will likely only be enough money for "targeted improvements" this year. As a starting point many departments were asked to prepare for a zero per cent budget increase.
"We can't make improvements across the board," Perks said.
Program cuts unlikely
The fractious debates over police spending and federal refugee supports that dominated last year's budget process will likely be avoided in 2025. Chow agreed to boost spending for the force's $1.2-billion budget by 3.9 per cent this year after police presented a multi-year hiring plan.
While a change in federal government could come early this year, Carroll said there will be "no surprises" on refugee funding in Monday's budget after the public battle the city waged with Ottawa over the issue in 2024.
Last year, the only service on the chopping block was the city's $16-million windrow clearing program, which removes the pile of snow that blocks the end of driveways after a snow plow has cleared the street. It was eventually saved after outcry from suburban councillors.
Carroll said the public shouldn't expect similar cuts to be proposed in this year's spending plan. "We set guidelines to avoid that as much as possible," she said, adding that the direction to city departments was "what are more efficient ways of doing things, but still doing the things."
CAPTION: City council will vote on the final version of the budget on Feb. 11.Crafting this year's budget will be a delicate balancing act for Mayor Olivia Chow, who was elected on a pledge to improve day-to-day services, but who is also facing pressure to control spending and ease residents' tax burden.

Document 139 of 500

Doug Ford has gone on an unprecedented American media blitz. Here's what is behind it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-gone-on-unprecedented-american/docview/3154395334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+gone+on+an+unprecedented+American+media+blitz.+Here%27s+what+is+behind+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jesse Watters, the bombastic Fox News host, was hectoring a prominent Canadian political leader the other day about president-elect Donald Trump's bizarre scheme to annex Canada.
"What's your problem with the United States absorbing Canada?" barked Watters as his guest waited patiently to respond.
The Canadian replied firmly but politely.
"I get it. President-elect Trump is a real-estate tycoon. He's made billions, but that property is not for sale, as simple as that," he said.
No, it wasn't departing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau or Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc or even newly minted Public Safety Minister David McGuinty defending the sanctity of Canada's border on "Jesse Watters Primetime," one of the many Fox shows the TV-obsessed Trump is known to monitor.
It was Premier Doug Ford, who has been on an unprecedented U.S. media blitz in the weeks since the incoming president vowed to slap 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian and Mexican products after his Jan. 20 inauguration.
The trade war threat has escalated with Trump's social-media trolling about making Canada "the 51st state" and deriding Trudeau as "governor of the great state of Canada."
That gambit is juicy red meat to Trumpian TV personalities like Watters.
"If I were a citizen of another country and I was a neighbour of the United States, I would consider it a privilege to be taken over by the United States of America," the right-wing cable news stalwart intoned to Ford last Tuesday.
"That's what everybody else in the world wants: American citizenship. For some reason that's repellent to you Canadians - and I find that personally offensive."
But Ford, whose communication skills improved dramatically during the COVID-19 pandemic when his daily health briefings were broadcast live across the province to Ontarians locked down at home, didn't take the bait.
Maintaining message discipline, he pivoted to his central theme: averting the tariffs he fears could devastate Ontario's economy.
"You know something, Jesse, we're proud Canadians - just like there's proud Americans - and if we join together and take on the world with a great trade deal between us, I think that would be fabulous," emphasized the premier.
Along with repeated appearances on Fox, Ford has done interviews on CNN, NBC and CNBC, among other U.S. networks, to argue against tariffs that would lead to higher prices for consumers at home and stateside.
He will be in Washington twice next month, leading a Council of the Federation delegation on Feb. 12, and attending the National Governors Association conference on Feb. 20.
His government is spending millions of dollars on a slick advertising campaign in the U.S. to remind Americans of how important trade with Ontario is to their economic well-being.
And he is on the phone daily with any governor, senator or representative who will take his call to plead Canada's case.
While the premier may indeed be basking in the media attention, his high profile is helped by the fact that politics, like nature, abhors a vacuum.
"There's a lack of leadership right now in Ottawa," Ford complained to reporters at Queen's Park after Trudeau announced his resignation Monday.
With the federal Liberals distracted by a nascent leadership contest that concludes March 9 and a national election looming as early as this spring, the premier has stepped into the void.
The federal chaos comes at an opportune time for a provincial leader who is polling so well against Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles that he is still considering an Ontario election this year, even though he doesn't have to face voters until June 2026.
Ford pointedly and repeatedly refused to rule out such a move last Monday when journalists asked him five times about the possibility of a snap provincial election.
"Right now what I'm focused on - right this second and moving forward - is making sure that we don't have tariffs. I can tell you, my eye hasn't been taken off the ball for a second here," he insisted.
But the prospect of a relatively easy re-election to a third term - with the rationale to voters that he needs a fresh mandate to spend the tens of billions of dollars that could be required to keep the Ontario economy afloat if Trump's levies come to fruition - might be hard for him to resist.
His political advisers believe his standing with the public is as high as it was at the peak of the pandemic, when he won praise for working closely with Trudeau and then intergovernmental affairs minister Chrystia Freeland, who remains a personal friend and is now a front-runner for the Liberal leadership.
The premier's campaign team, however, is mindful of the limitations of what he can actually do as the leader of a subnational jurisdiction when it comes to retaliating against Trump's tariffs - to say nothing of the potential backlash if he calls an Ontario election at an already uncertain time and is viewed as opportunistic.
That's one reason he has toned down his Dec. 11 threat to cut off the electricity Ontario exports to Michigan, New York and Wisconsin, which supplies power for 1.5 million Americans.
Instead, Ford is pitching a "Fortress Am-Can" energy alliance with the U.S. in order to ship more Ontario-generated power south of the border.
At the same time, he is signalling that American consumers would ultimately pay the price for any Trump tariffs.
"Someone in Texas buys a pickup truck that's produced here in Oshawa, not far away from here, they typically would pay $60,000," he said in Courtice, Ont. on Wednesday.
"Now, that same person in Texas is going to pay $75,000 if he puts tariffs on vehicles," said Ford.
"These tariffs would be devastating for both of our economies. They'd disrupt deeply integrated supply chains, create massive uncertainty for investors, raise costs for businesses and put countless jobs at risk on both sides of the border," he said.
"It doesn't have to be this way."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at Queen's Park in Toronto on Jan. 6, 2025.

Subject: Political campaigns; Provincial elections; Leadership; State elections; Governors; Voters; Pandemics; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Tariffs

Document 140 of 500

'An ordeal that doesn't end': Lost Canadians' citizenship at risk with Parliament suspended

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ordeal-that-doesnt-end-lost-canadians-citizenship/docview/3154395323/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27An+ordeal+that+doesn%27t+end%27%3A+Lost+Canadians%27+citizenship+at+risk+with+Parliament+suspended&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Shortly after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau suspended Parliament on Monday, Carol Sutherland-Brown's phone started ringing.
The calls were coming from other people whose families have been caught up in the longstanding "lost Canadians" fiasco and are concerned about the fate of the highly anticipated citizenship reforms proposed in Bill C-71.
The Ottawa grandmother and others have been fighting to reclaim the citizenship rights taken away from their families under Canada's current second-generation cut-off rule, which denies automatic citizenship to children born abroad because their parents also happened to be born overseas.
More than a year ago, an Ontario court found the law unconstitutional and gave the federal government six months to change it to make it Charter-compliant. The Liberal government introduced Bill C-71 to fix the problem, but the deadline has already been extended three times, to March 19.
This legislation would automatically confer Canadian citizenship on people born abroad before the changes are enacted to a Canadian parent who was also born abroad. Anyone born outside the country subsequently would need to prove their foreign-born Canadian parent had a "substantial connection" with Canada by meeting a residency requirement.
With the prorogation of Parliament until March 24, the bill has now died on the order paper, and a new one would have to be tabled when the House returns. It would be subject to the legislative process from scratch again.
The lost Canadians' families fear that if a confidence vote follows, as expected, it will topple the Liberals and usher in a Conservative government. It was a Conservative government that brought in the second-generation citizenship cut-off in 2009 to begin with.
"This has been an ordeal for me and for the other families, an ordeal that doesn't end," said Sutherland-Brown. Her daughter was born in Saudi Arabia, and two grandchildren were born in the U.K. As a result, the grandchildren lost their Canadian citizenship rights by descent. "We don't know what a new government will do."
Lawyer Sujit Choudhry, who represented lost Canadians in the successful court challenge, said the government has two options: to go before the court for another extension or let the citizenship law be declared unconstitutional.
However, both are problematic.
While there have been similar precedents where the government was granted extensions to comply with court orders, he said the requests were made because Parliament was dissolved and an election was called, and not due to prorogation.
"The question in March will be whether this is the time for this to come to an end , or whether, given the unique political circumstances, some more time should be given," said Choudhry. "What will the government say in court? Well, they'd say a dissolution is imminent, but it hasn't happened yet."
If Ottawa lets the deadline lapse and the two-generation cut-off is thus voided, affected lost Canadians could just come reclaim their citizenship. If the court cuts the government some slack and grants another extension in light of the circumstances, the uncertainty will continue.
"During that extension period, we could very well be in an election, in which case, no bills could be passed," said MP Jenny Kwan, immigration critic of the opposition NDP, and a staunch supporter of the bill.
"After the election, whoever forms government would have to take further actions to be compliant with the court decision. We have to remember that it was the Conservatives who brought in this unconstitutional provision 15 years ago."
In 2009, the then-Conservative government changed the citizenship law and imposed the second-generation cut-off on Canadians born abroad, after Ottawa had faced a massive effort to evacuate 15,000 Lebanese Canadians stranded in Beirut during Israel's month-long war against Hezbollah in 2006.
The $85-million price tag of the evacuation effort sparked a debate over "Canadians of convenience." The government abolished the existing "substantial connection" regime and adopted a blanket rule that denies the first generation born abroad the right to pass on citizenship by descent outside Canada to the second generation born abroad.
Tunisian-born Majda Dabaghi, whose two children were born in France and hence can't be Canadian citizens by descent, is concerned about a Conservative return to power given the party's efforts to block C-71 and a similar bill previously. (The Conservative party didn't respond to a request for comment.)
The cut-off rule "was a racist response to the evacuation of dual nationals out of Lebanon," said Dabaghi, who has continued to vote in Canadian elections after she left Canada in 2007 for a job in international law in the U.K.
"They have done everything humanly possible to filibuster the passing of the legislation, both in the form of Bill C-71 and earlier in the form of Bill S-245. They have put their own politics and political gamesmanship above sound policy, people's lives and our Constitution."
Calling Bill C-71 "a crucial piece of legislation," Immigration Minister Marc Miller also blamed the Conservatives for stalling it. While his office would not say if the government will request that the court grant another extension, it said "Minister Miller is confident that a Liberal government would reintroduce this important bill to the House once resumed."
Although Christina Matula's two children - born in England and Hong Kong - are Canadian citizens, she said she's fighting for their rights to explore the world and work abroad without having to worry about where to start a family.
And she said both the Liberals and Conservatives are at fault because the former also failed to prioritize and expedite the introduction and passing of the bill despite the court order in December 2023.
Her children, now 17 and 14, have attended Canadian international schools, participated in Terry Fox runs and visited family in Canada every summer and Christmas, said Matula, whose family now lives in Finland.
"My children are Canadian by descent and have strong ties to Canada," she said "I want them to have clear and fair criteria to prove their connection to Canada, so they can have the same rights as Canadian-born and naturalized citizens."
CAPTION: Although Christina Matula's two children born abroad are Canadian citizens, she's fighting for their rights to travel and work abroad without having to worry about where to start a family.Carol Sutherland-Brown (back middle), whose family has been Canadian for generations, is sad that her two grandchildren aren't eligible for citizenship by descent. She's pictured with partner Ted Willis, left, daughter Marisa Crimlis-Brown and son-in-law Sean, and granddaughter Sloane and grandson Findley.Lawyer Sujit Choudhry,

Anand won't seek top job as she plans to quit politics: Transport minister is returning to her academic career

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anand-wont-seek-top-job-as-she-plans-quit/docview/3154352163/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anand+won%27t+seek+top+job+as+she+plans+to+quit+politics%3A+Transport+minister+is+returning+to+her+academic+career&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It has been nearly a week since Justin Trudeau said he was stepping down as prime minister and Liberal leader, and more Liberals have declared they will not seek the party's leadership than have joined the race for the top job.
The latest to bow out of contention is Transport Minister Anita Anand, who released a statement on Saturday saying she was returning to academia and would not seek re-election.
Anand joins fellow cabinet ministers Dominic LeBlanc and Mélanie Joly, who last week said they would not be joining the race to be party leader.
While the early focus has been on who's out, the competition is expected to soon heat up, with former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney and former B.C. premier Christy Clark both potentially tossing their hats into the ring in a matter of days.
Other possible contenders - including former finance minister Chrystia Freeland, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne, Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson, Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon and Liberal House Leader Karina Gould - have yet to announce their intentions.
As it stands, just two candidates have confirmed they intend to run: backbench Liberal MP Chandra Arya, and former backbench MP Frank Baylis.
All candidates must declare their participation in the race by Jan. 23, the Liberal party announced on Thursday night, and the entrance fee for candidates is a record $350,000.
Only registered party members can vote in the leadership contest, which is scheduled to conclude on March 9, giving the new leader a little more than two weeks before Parliament is set to begin its next session. (New members must sign up by Jan. 27 to be eligible to vote.)
Even before she officially joins the race, Clark is already dealing with some controversy. CBC reported on Friday that she had become a member of the Conservative Party of Canada in order to support Jean Charest's leadership bid in 2022.
Clark initially denied the charge, but later posted to X: "Well, I misspoke. Sh*t happens. Lesson learned."
She added that she supported Charest to stop Pierre Poilievre.
"He's the most divisive politician we've seen in years and I felt it was my duty as a Cdn to stop him in his tracks," she wrote.
Anand, a lawyer and former law professor, was first elected to represent Oakville in 2019. She held several cabinet posts in Trudeau's government, including defence minister, Treasury Board president and minister of public services and procurement.
Anand served in the latter role during the first year and a half of the COVID-19 pandemic and was responsible for securing vaccines, personal protective equipment and rapid tests for the country. As defence minister she brought in reforms to address sexual misconduct in the military.
"While this chapter of my life has been challenging, it has been tremendously fulfilling to deliver results for Canadians by working collaboratively," she said in a statement posted to X, formerly Twitter.
Anand said that while she enjoyed working in government, she always intended to return to her academic career.
"Now that the Prime Minister has made his decision to move to his next chapter, I have determined the time is right for me to do the same, and to return to my prior professional life of teaching, research and public policy analyses."
In a December 2022 interview with the Star's editorial board, Anand admitted she missed "the solitude of academic life."
In her statement, Anand thanked Trudeau and her constituents in Oakville.
"During my first campaign, many people told me that a woman of Indian descent would not get elected in Oakville, Ontario," she said. "Yet Oakville rallied behind me not once but twice since 2019, an honour that I will hold in my heart forever."
CAPTION: Oakville MP Anita Anand, a lawyer and former law professor, held several cabinet posts in Justin Trudeau's government, including defence, Treasury Board and public services and procurement.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Brendan Kennedy Toronto Star

Document 142 of 500

Neighbourly response sends pointed message to provocative Trump: It's not the first time that Canadians have stepped up in America's dark hours

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/neighbourly-response-sends-pointed-message/docview/3154352097/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Neighbourly+response+sends+pointed+message+to+provocative+Trump%3A+It%27s+not+the+first+time+that+Canadians+have+stepped+up+in+America%27s+dark+hours&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The California wildfires raging and ripping through Los Angeles are catastrophic, even if the homes being destroyed belong to some of the world's wealthiest and most famous (and presumably well-insured).
But they're presenting Canada with a timely opportunity to do what Canada has always done when its southerly neighbour is in need - as well as a chance to send America a pointed political message.
At a time when U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has thrown the world into a tizzy with threats to invade Greenland and Panama, and turn Canada into the 51st state, government officials north of the border have responded with the video of a yellow Canadian water bomber - an iconic Canadair - buzzing through the smoke-darkened skies of L.A. and dropping its liquid load in defence of a home menaced by flames.
The video has been in heavy rotation on the social media account of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and shared by the Liberal caucus and cabinet. It's notable for its sly and simple three-word message: "Neighbours helping neighbors."
Keen readers will note Trudeau's inclusive spelling - a drop of the "u" that serves as the outgoing Liberal leader's wink to an American audience.
It's not the first time that Canadians have shown up in America's dark hours with a helping hand, a key piece of equipment, or neighbourly compassion and concern.
Think of the 9/11 terror attacks in New York City, when Canada took in diverted U.S.-bound airplanes and dispatched emergency workers to Ground Zero.
Think of the many hurricanes - from Katrina (2005) to Maria (2017) to Helene last fall, when Hydro One repair crews were sent with the crucial mission of empowering Canada's hard-hit southern neighbours.
The regular assistance comes out of a sense of duty, formalized in a range of mutual assistance treaties, including specific wildland firefighting agreements under which American crews have also come north.
But this time, fuelled by Trump's inflammatory comments suggesting Canada is an economic freeloader and a national security vulnerability, Canada is putting its all-of-country effort on full display.
Alberta is sending 40 front-line firefighters, known in the U.S. as "hotshots." British Columbia, which has a long-standing firefighting relationship with California, is dispatching a team of senior officials to help lead the efforts in Los Angeles.
And it's Quebec that is going video viral with its contribution of airplanes, known as Super Scoopers, which buzzed the beachfront while filling up on water, then soared to the skies on their mission to douse the flames, earning an official mention from the White House as well as another round of headlines when one was damaged in a mid-air collision with a drone.
Ontario has offered more water bombers and another 200 personnel. They're ready to answer if called upon, as are the Canadian Armed Forces, the Coast Guard and Natural Resources Canada.
Premier Doug Ford, who had been trying to tamp down Trump's aggression with threats of retaliation, said the province would "spare no expense to help our closest friends and allies."
That point was underscored by the federal government's unusual act of exhaustively listing the resources that have been sent and are standing by to help America in an emergency.
It should be seen as a response to Trump's dismissive comments about Canada at a Mar-a-Lago news conference on Wednesday: "We don't need anything they have."
Trump was referring to Canadian-made goods that the U.S. economy could do without. Automobiles that he would rather see built in Detroit. Lumber he would rather harvest from American forests. Dairy products he'd rather source from American cows.
It came on the same day that Trump was asked point-blank whether he would use American military force to take his other two apparent targets - Greenland and the Panama Canal - and refused to rule out such a drastic possibility.
But he forgot about one aspect of governing in a chaotic and unpredictable world that Canada has to offer in abundance - something that is more difficult to slap with a price tag and which America will always need, no matter how rich or powerful.
Trump forgot about friendship.
Canada and the U.S. have an alliance that is forged in the heat of wildfires and formed from the wreckage of hurricanes and tested in the chaos of war and bathed in the tears of terrorism.
Canada has many shortcomings. There are many credible sources of complaint.
We spend less on our military than our closest NATO allies. We commit to helping the world's poorest, but fall short on our promises. We preach about global warming while drilling for oil and gas.
But we as a country are generally pretty good about remembering that one good turn deserves another - particularly when it comes from a friend and neighbour.
And this is what Trudeau conjured up when recounting that he had spoken with California Gov. Gavin Newsom in recent days.
Both of the progressive liberal leaders know a thing or two about being the target of Trump's political intimidation tactics. But Trudeau chose to put the focus on their shared understanding of the Canada-U.S. bond, which he said is deeper than a matter of simple geography.
"We're friends - especially when times get tough," Trudeau wrote on social media. "California's always had our back when we battle wildfires up north. Now, Canada's got yours."
CAPTION: A resident sifts through his family's fire-ravaged property inthe Pacific Palisades neighbourhood on Saturday. Donald Trump forgot about one aspect of governing in a chaotic and unpredictable world that Canada has to offer in abundance - he forgot about friendship, writes Allan Woods.John LocherThe Associated Press
CREDIT: Allan Woods Toronto Star
