Document 153 of 500

A leader in love with the spotlight: I wrote the book on Justin Trudeau - and noticed a shortcoming. I didn't know it would be his Achilles heel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leader-love-with-spotlight/docview/3154084647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+leader+in+love+with+the+spotlight%3A+I+wrote+the+book+on+Justin+Trudeau+-+and+noticed+a+shortcoming.+I+didn%27t+know+it+would+be+his+Achilles+heel&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This time last year, when I was doing final edits on my book about Justin Trudeau, I asked my editors at Simon & Schuster if they thought that any passages in the book stuck out as unfair. After two years of researching and writing about Trudeau, I had started to find myself becoming irritated by him, and I was afraid that had crept into the text.
The wise and talented Rosemary Shipton, who has edited many books, replied by drawing my attention to one sentence "where you verge on being a touch unkind." I had written that perhaps Trudeau "sees politics largely as an opportunity for people to appreciate his princely magnificence."
In closely reviewing his nine years in office, and the rest of his life, I had started to think that he was often focused on the pleasure that others took in basking in his presence. I decided Shipton was right, though, that it was unkind, because there were other ways of looking at Trudeau, and we cut the line.
After all, he had revived a vision of progressive Canada when it looked as though Stephen Harper might have forged a permanent new conservative consensus. Once in office, he legalized marijuana, lifted tens of thousands of children out of poverty and made sustained efforts toward Indigenous reconciliation and the struggle to reduce emissions. He demonstrated a consistent commitment to his vision of the country. It seemed inaccurate to portray him as nothing but an egotistical nepo baby.
In recent months, though, as he clung to power after the point when it seemed clear he should let go, I became irritated again, and started to think that his love of the spotlight is his fatal flaw, part and parcel of the will to power that drove him to lead Canada.
That unusual compulsion seems to have been bred in the bone.
Trudeau had a strange and difficult childhood. He was born on Christmas Day, 1971, son of prime minister Pierre Trudeau and Margaret Sinclair, who was 29 years younger than her husband, and who was suffering from bipolar disorder, which was not diagnosed until many years later.
On March 3, 1977, on the night of her sixth wedding anniversary, when Justin was five, she caused a sensation when, instead of celebrating with her husband, she showed up at the El Mocambo in Toronto with Ron Wood and Mick Jagger, where the Rolling Stones were about to take the stage for two legendary nights. She followed them to New York, and then left her family for a jet set life, making headlines around the world with her romances - the most lurid news out of Canada until Rob Ford came along.
So Justin grew up with a largely absent mother, which traumatized him and left him with a burning desire for attention, as he described in his memoir. As Jonathan Kay, who ghost-wrote that memoir, "Common Ground," wrote later in the Walrus: "What good is the glitz of being a prime minister's son when you're living a childhood parched of mother's milk?"
As a child and young man, people were drawn to Trudeau. As the first born son of a famous leader, he had hereditary charisma, and he was energetic, tall and handsome, with leadership skills and good manners. He could effortlessly capture attention anywhere he went; he revelled in it, and consistently sought more. It was, for instance, why he wore blackface on so many occasions that he could later not recall them all. He liked to dress up, be the centre of attention.
But he didn't truly apply himself to anything worthy of his gifts until 2012, when he sought the Liberal leadership, a sure way of being at the centre of the action. From that point on, though, he did the work. To win his party's leadership, to go from third place to a majority government, besting Stephen Harper and Tom Mulcair, he had to do a lot of difficult things well, showing an admirable work ethic, policy skills and, crucially, an ability to connect with voters.
For his first year in office, he was a hot commodity, a new thing that people wanted to know about. He was thronged in Asia, appeared in Vogue and Rolling Stone magazines, was a global progressive icon, which was good for him, his party and, in a way, the country.
That period ended, suddenly and permanently, when he took a disastrous trip to India, which was set up like a royal tour, not a business trip, and he so overdid the costume changes that the Indians started laughing at him.
Comedian John Oliver said then that Trudeau had "singed his fingertips on people going, 'You're not quite that charismatic to pull off a trip like that.' "
Ever since, as a global media commodity, Trudeau has been damaged goods, but he has not stopped seeking the spotlight, tweeting at real celebrities - Trevor Noah and Taylor Swift - for example.
His media presence, which was once a source of pride even to Canadians who did not vote for him, became increasingly cringe, especially after the pandemic, the lockdowns, the convoy and a cost of living crisis. Inevitably, all of that strife made him a symbol of hard times and division for the growing number of Canadians.
There are things leaders can do when they become unpopular - pivots they can make - but Trudeau didn't have the cunning to change gears, to tell a new story. He just kept showing up in novelty socks, being a dashing progressive, hoping people would be pleased to see him - as almost everyone once had been. But the old magic has stopped working, and he looked like he was virtue signalling when he lectured Giorgia Meloni about gay rights. When he popped down to Mar-a-Lago, Donald Trump bullied him.
And at home, no matter what he did, the polls would not move, because voters had just had enough of him, and the Conservatives had selected an anti-Trudeau figure who succeeded in rallying the people who had come to see Trudeau as a pious scold, an anti-racist who had often worn blackface, a climate change warrior who was forever flying around in a private plane, a feminist who gaslit, demoted and exiled Jody Wilson-Raybould when she rightly objected to the PMO interfering in the administration of justice.
Trudeau and his people were trying to sideline the prosecution of corrupt engineering firm SNC-Lavalin, and then shuffled Wilson-Raybould when she refused to go along with the plan. At the climax of the SNC-Lavalin affair, Trudeau and Wilson-Raybould had a tense meeting at the Vancouver International Airport, after which he told reporters "In our system of government, her presence in cabinet should actually speak for itself."
Trudeau appeared to believe that his charm would keep her in line, a miscalculation that brought his government to the brink of collapse when she promptly resigned. It was much the same with Chrystia Freeland, whom he tried to demote when it ought to have been obvious that her continued presence at his right hand was vital to public confidence in his government.
In both cases, he seems to have thought that the force of his personality would carry the day. But it was in the best interests of both women to ditch him and when they did, Trudeau didn't have much to say in his own defence.
His government recovered from the SNC-Lavalin affair, but the second falling out was fatal. Trudeau, in the last days of his prime ministership, looks like a guy whose charm finally wore off.
It is too soon for the verdict of history - consider how Brian Mulroney was despised when he left office and how, after decades passed, people came to remember his many real accomplishments. Trudeau can look forward to that reassessment in the distant future, but at the moment, he looks like a guy who didn't know when to stop, which has put the country in peril.
The election of Trump, with his tariff threats and annexation jokes, requires a firm response. Trudeau has not been able to provide that, and he is so unpopular that most Canadians would not support him if he offered it.
At a time of national crisis, Team Canada is without an effective coach. That is Trudeau's doing. It has been clear in the polls, and on the ground, for a long time that Canadians wanted a change. It would have been better for the country, the party and Trudeau, personally, if he had recognized that sooner instead of clinging to the vain hope that Canadians would warm to him again. It looks like he could not bear giving up the spotlight.
Now he must serve for two more months as an unpopular lame-duck prime minister while his would-be replacements awkwardly try to distance themselves from his legacy, Pierre Poilievre howls for a "carbon tax election" and Trump and other right-wingers torment him.
Trudeau alone is responsible for creating this situation, and if it goes badly, that will be part of his legacy.
The best hope, for him, his party and the country, is that the race to replace him produces a competent leader, so that Poilievre will not win without being properly scrutinized and tested. But the hurried race to replace Trudeau, if it is not conducted carefully, could be a disaster, open to the foreign interference that he failed to counter effectively while prime minister.
Watching his obvious misery as he announced his resignation, it was possible to feel human sympathy for him, but his stubbornness in the face of the facts is what sticks out. We have a lot of other things to worry about because Trudeau stuck around after it was obvious that Canadians no longer wanted to follow him.
Stephen Maher is the author of "The Prince, The Turbulent Reign of Justin Trudeau."
CREDIT: Stephen Maher Special to the Star;

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Autobiographies; Engineering firms; Voters; Books; Prime ministers; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Engineering firms Tax elections

Location: Canada

People: Harper, Stephen

Company / organization: Name: AtkinsRealis; NAICS: 541330

Title: A leader in love with the spotlight:   I wrote the book on Justin Trudeau - and noticed a shortcoming. I didn't know it would be his Achilles heel

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 11, 2025

Document 154 of 500

Behind his big move: Sources say the catalyst for Trudeau's downfall was Freeland's explosive exit as finance minister and the PM's failed effort to replace her

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/behind-his-big-move/docview/3154084492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+his+big+move%3A+Sources+say+the+catalyst+for+Trudeau%27s+downfall+was+Freeland%27s+explosive+exit+as+finance+minister+and+the+PM%27s+failed+effort+to+replace+her&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau once considered quitting politics long before Monday.
On the day he announced his coming resignation, Trudeau wrote his own first draft of history. He told the country he is a fighter who never backs away from a fight - that he wouldn't be stepping down if it weren't for his internal caucus struggles that eclipsed his ability to stay on.
In fact, in early 2023, months before Trudeau publicly revealed his separation from his wife Sophie Grégoire Trudeau, the prime minister seriously weighed leaving office, the Star has learned.
Their 18-year marriage had been on the rocks for a while. His third-term Liberal government had emerged from the COVID-19 pandemic struggling to find its footing amid high interest rates and punishing inflation. An inquiry slammed a failure of federalism during his handling of the 2022 "Freedom Convoy" emergency. And national security leaks on foreign electoral interference pummelled the Trudeau government as incompetent and indifferent to a growing threat.
A visit to Canada by U.S. President Joe Biden in late March that year rallied Trudeau. By May's Liberal convention, he'd chosen to stay on - a fateful decision for him, his legacy, and his party's fortunes.
The prime minister believed then, and may still believe, that he alone can beat Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. His resolve only crumbled in the first days of this month.
The trigger for that decision is clear. The exact moment of it is not.
Multiple sources spoke to the Star for this story on condition their names would not be revealed.
One source said the prime minister came to his decision to leave over the holidays, that it evolved, and there was no one event or conversation that persuaded him, but "a combination of things," including calls for him to go from majorities of his Ontario, Atlantic and Quebec caucuses.
Trudeau talked about his future to very few trusted friends and advisers, including at least one outside politics, in the past several weeks.
Sources who spoke to the Star also agreed the catalyst for the prime minister's downfall was Chrystia Freeland's explosive exit as finance minister on Dec. 16, and Trudeau's failed effort to ensure Mark Carney, Canada's former central banker, would replace her.
Trudeau and his team didn't see it coming. They thought they had agreements that would see strong performers in two key roles - Carney as finance minister and Freeland on the critical Canada-U.S. challenge.
However, the seeds of the political disaster to come were sown long beforehand.
"This was the product of bruised egos over the course of six months," said one Liberal source who was close to the events as they unfolded.
Sources in both Trudeau's and Freeland's camps say Freeland was hurt by leaks in July suggesting Trudeau and the Prime Minister's Office viewed her as a poor communicator and had offered her job as finance minister to Carney.
Those leaks were wrong on a key point: Trudeau did not guarantee or even offer Carney the finance portfolio then or any time prior to December, sources say.
However, neither Trudeau nor his team - who viewed the leaks as "ridiculous" given how much Trudeau trusted Freeland - effectively dampened the speculation. In part, that was because some of them, and much of the Liberal caucus, did see Freeland as an ineffective communicator (for instance, when she urged Canadians who were worried about the cost of living to cancel their Disney Plus subscriptions) and wanted her shuffled, especially after the party's byelection loss in Toronto-St. Paul's rattled all of them.
But the leaked stories - which Freeland took to heart - were correct on one point: Trudeau and his advisers had been trying to persuade Carney, a godfather to Freeland's son, to join the team for a long time.
Carney always declined.
At least as far back as 2019, Trudeau's Liberals made failed efforts to recruit Carney to run in an Ottawa riding.
In September 2018, Carney had just agreed to extend his term as governor of the Bank of England to January 2020 to help navigate Brexit.
Although Carney chose not to run, he made clear to the Liberals that for family reasons, he would prefer an Ottawa riding if it ever came to that.
He did, however, deliver a keynote speech at the Liberals' virtual convention in 2021, held amid the pandemic, and promised to do "whatever I can to support the Liberal party."
Talks began anew last spring to lure Carney in and continued into the late summer, culminating with Carney agreeing in September to write an economic growth report for the Liberal Party of Canada that would be incorporated into a future platform.
"He's a serial fence-sitter," said one Liberal - a sentiment echoed by others.
In December, talks with Carney were again underway in earnest.
By that time, Freeland had had weeks of tense discussions with the prime minister over the fall economic statement. The government would need the support of New Democrats to pass any new measures, and they had publicly called for a GST cut as an affordability measure. The prime minister and his office hammered Freeland to deliver measures - like their proposed GST cut - that would fly with the New Democrats.
Two sources said she wanted to avoid ongoing structural commitments and that it was Freeland who proposed the time-limited, $1.6-billion GST holiday on certain goods. But Freeland was cool to the idea of sending Canadians $250 cheques that the PMO wanted.
Several sources said Freeland was determined that the fall update would hew to the fiscal "anchor" or goal of a declining debt-to-GDP ratio, even though she had to report a whopping $60-billion deficit for the year just ended.
On Dec. 2, Trudeau and Freeland met in her office. Sources agree Trudeau signalled then that he wanted her to stay on as his finance minister, although Freeland was having doubts that he actually did.
Freeland had found Trudeau's decision to exclude her from a Nov. 29 trip to Florida to meet with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump "perplexing" and "confusing," despite publicly suggesting she understood it as the "right choice," since borders were on the agenda and that was the purview of then-public safety minister Dominic LeBlanc.
The prime minister and Freeland met again in person on Dec. 8, at his residence in Rideau Cottage, and signed off on the fall economic update. While it would include the temporary GST break, Trudeau agreed not to book the $4.7 billion for the promised $250 cheques for Canadians who had earned income in 2023 - which his team saw as a multibillion-dollar win for Freeland.
Still to come that week was a decision on Freeland's proposal to book $12 billion in cuts and launch a review of program spending.
But on a Sunday night, six days before she quit, sources close to Trudeau said the pair discussed the prime minister's desire for Freeland to take on a new role on the Canada-U.S. file. Two sources said the prime minister was clear that Freeland would remain deputy prime minister, and that she said she looked forward to seeing how the proposed new role would be defined.
Another insider said that mischaracterizes the conversation. They say Freeland made clear she believed Trump's threat to impose tariffs on Canadian imports needed to be strategically addressed, that she knew Scott Bessent, Trump's pick for Treasury secretary who would be her counterpart, and she expected to lead the response from her position as finance minister.
Over the course of the week, there were other conversations between their offices, the sources said. The insiders disagree about whether Freeland was told she would additionally take on the title of intergovernmental affairs minister because of the need to co-ordinate with the premiers.
On the prime minister's side, he and his team viewed the new role not as a demotion - as Freeland's letter later portrayed it - but rather as a position Freeland was most suited for, having led Canada's response to the NAFTA renegotiations and steel and aluminum tariffs during Trump's first administration. The fact that Trump doesn't like her was not seen as a drawback, they said.
Yet sources close to Freeland say she was angered by the offer of an undefined role with no staff, separate department, or statutory authority to levy tariffs against the U.S., which reside in law with the finance minister.
"That's bulls--t," countered one of the sources familiar with their discussions. That source said Freeland would have directed Canada's response, including how to wield the authorities of the departments of Global Affairs and Finance, with all the resources of the Privy Council Office, a department and central agency that as deputy prime minister she would continue to have full access to.
On Wednesday, Dec. 11, the prime minister and his office put the kibosh on the $12 billion in cuts she proposed. A source close to Trudeau said she had pitched the package only about 10 days before, and it was not thoroughly thought through. A source close to Freeland said it had been "many weeks" in the offing, and she'd advocated it as part of Canada's readiness for the fallout of a future trade war.
By Friday, Dec. 13, it became clear during a video conference call - with Freeland in Toronto and Trudeau in Vancouver - that they were far apart on their understanding of what the other wanted.
According to Freeland's resignation letter, Trudeau told her then that Carney would become his finance minister after the economic statement she was to deliver the following Monday.
It was the first time, according to sources close to Freeland, that Trudeau explicitly told her she'd be out of that portfolio.
Freeland told Trudeau she did not want to go along with that, and the call ended badly, say both sides.
Freeland and Trudeau did not speak again. There were additional conversations between their senior staff members, but it was clear to some that Carney had over the weekend decided he, too, was out.
By Monday, Dec. 16, it was all over.
On the day she was to deliver the Liberal government's long-awaited fall fiscal update, Freeland phoned Trudeau just as he was arriving at Parliament Hill to lead a cabinet meeting.
Shortly after their call, Freeland posted her damning letter on X, accusing Trudeau of wanting to fire her because she had argued against blowing the budget on "costly political gimmicks."
She portrayed Trudeau as undermining Canada's fiscal capacity to respond to a looming tariff war with the Trump administration.
Several insiders say the prime minister was shocked and devastated. Trudeau immediately believed it was a blow that would make it impossible for him to stay on, said three sources.
One said the prime minister went into a dark place. Chaos reigned behind closed doors.
The media lock-up for what is essentially a mini-budget hadn't started, but the stock markets and the Canadian dollar dipped upon news of Freeland's resignation.
The PMO had already understood Carney was also no longer on board, and Trudeau named LeBlanc to replace Freeland. The prime minister was persuaded to take time to reflect, and to not make any sudden decisions.
The government determined the economic update - which revealed a $60-billion deficit - had to be released as scheduled. (Much of the eye-popping deficit was ascribed to liabilities for Indigenous legal cases, in part at Freeland's insistence.)
Carney's team says that in the 24 hours after Freeland's announcement, he made clear to the Trudeau government he was out.
A source close to Carney said he had originally agreed in December to be part of a bigger governing team that was going to help Canada navigate the economic peril posed by Trump's incoming administration. That source declined to say whether Carney had agreed specifically to take on the finance minister's job, which multiple sources confirmed Trudeau had asked him to fill.
In Carney's mind, Freeland had been an integral part of the team, and with that clearly no longer the case, he was not willing to sign on, the source said.
It was a colossal mess, one Trudeau could not recover from. He was now a fighter not just on the ropes, but down for the count.
CAPTION: Despite having thoughts of leaving office in early 2023, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau believed then, and may still believe, that he alone can beat Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. His resolve only crumbled in the first days of this month. The trigger for that decision is clear. The exact moment of it is not, Tonda MacCharles writes.Adrian WyldThe CanadianPress

Document 155 of 500

Looking ahead to life in Trump's 51st state

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/looking-ahead-life-trumps-51st-state/docview/3154084491/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Looking+ahead+to+life+in+Trump%27s+51st+state&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This imagined dispatch from
the future comes from the mind
of Star prognosticator Edward
Keenan
TORONTO, 2028 Well, I guess he wasn't trolling after all.
I mean, it's obvious now. Standing here on the steps of the University Avenue Statehouse and Casino in Toronto.
We're in what's now called the Great State of West Ohio (renamed from "Canada" on the same principle, President Donald Trump explained, that the state north of Virginia is called West Virginia). We're watching Gov. Wayne Gretzky being sworn in.
It makes sense, in a way. The biggest immediately noticeable benefit to Canadians - er, West Ohioans, sorry, still getting used to it - of our merger with the larger U.S.A. is that our new combined hockey team is an absolute world powerhouse. Connor McDavid, Auston Matthews, Cale Makar and Connor Hellebuyck all on the same team? Who's beating that? And women's hockey? There's hardly a competition left.
So sure, put the hockey guy in charge. Not that there was an election, what with the Great Greenland War underway, and the frequent "insurrectionist" attacks by Quebec separatists, and the deep state trials underway in Washington. Who knows when we Americans will see elections again?
People thought Trump was trolling when he said Canada should be the 51st state, and when he suggested Gretzky as governor. Like the American Republican establishment and the U.S. political mainstream before them, they first dismissed his talk as bluster, and then thought the best way to manage him was to feed his ego and try to give him what he wanted.
They kept thinking that under the 25 and then 50 per cent tariffs that led to the 13-cent dollar, and then right on through the great resource handover that was supposed to appease Trump once and for all. They kept thinking it right up until Operation "Grab Them By the Beaver" rolled into Ottawa.
So here we are. Gov. Gretzky and President Trump and Loo-tenant Gov. Jordan Peterson helicoptered down from the new "Summer White House" at Trump's Muskoka-Lago resort for the swearing in. It still feels weird that it's in Toronto, but Trump insisted this be the capital since it was a place he'd heard of.
"You miss 100 per cent of the shots you don't take," Gov. Gretzky is saying at his swearing in, repeating his single catchphrase. He's also accurately describing the defence Canada mounted when the Americans finally invaded. We missed 100 per cent of our shots, in as much as we didn't take any. The world's longest undefended border lived up to its name til the end.
Not that everyone was happy about it. Prime Minister Pierre Poilievre recorded a bunch of really long YouTube videos explaining why Trump shouldn't even want Canada because no one can afford a house here and woke mobs had infested the posts and beams of our farmhouse or something like that. But then Trump started calling him "Prime Minister Pee Pee" and then "Stinky Pee Pee," and the convoy crowd thought that was hilarious and that just about ended the last prime minister's reign.
Doug Ford and the other premiers talked really tough for a while, even as they were engineering the new "equal partners" trade agreement that allowed Americans unfettered access to all our mining, oil, maple syrup and fresh water rights in exchange for lowering tariffs to 15 per cent. Like I said, trying to be his best friend and keep him happy. Fat lot of good that did. Now those former premiers are all up in the Northwest Territories at "the best - everyone says it, the best in history, the best Gulag the world has ever seen" alongside the migrants awaiting deportation and the "woke enemies of free speech" awaiting re-education.
Many of us regular people tried to resist, of course. It's just we didn't anticipate that Trump would be just as immune to mass marches insisting Canadian sovereignty matters as he once had been to ones insisting that Black Lives Matter. The full-blast hectoring Marg Delahunty gave him on that one episode of "This Hour Has 22 Minutes" hardly fazed Trump at all, since he seemed to be under the impression she was one of his own lawyers. Drake somehow lost the exchange of diss tracks.
Meanwhile, the global community so many thought would defend us were pretty preoccupied with their own battles, once Trump signed the "Stay In Your Lane" treaty with Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping that disbanded NATO and recognized Russia's sphere of influence over Europe and China's over East Asia as long as they stayed out of Trump's way in the Americas. Looking at the situation in France and Taiwan, a lot of people here are just saying, "I guess it could be worse." We might put that on the licence plates.
There are a lot more shootings, granted, now that you can buy semi-automatic rifles at the corner store alongside beer and lottery tickets. And a lot of people miss having reproductive rights, and government health care. And the new "anti-woke free speech" laws requiring everyone to use the pronouns their drunkest uncle says they should appears to just be the beginning of a horrifically hard time for a population that was already pretty vulnerable.
On the other hand, Target came back, and the shelves are full this time. Just like the President promised, back when everyone said he was just trolling us.
CAPTION: Canadians thought U.S. president-elect Donald Trump was just trolling when he said Canada should be the 51st state, and suggested Wayne Gretzky as its governor, Edward Keenan writes. They kept thinking that right up until Operation "Grab Them By the Beaver" rolled into Ottawa.Jose Luis MaganaThe Associated Press / Ryan Remiorz THE CANADIAN PRESS

Document 156 of 500

'I've got two pairs of flip flops and some dirty sneakers': Canadians in L.A. rush to escape wildfires, and describe an apocalyptic scene

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ive-got-two-pairs-flip-flops-some-dirty-sneakers/docview/3153926509/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27ve+got+two+pairs+of+flip+flops+and+some+dirty+sneakers%27%3A+Canadians+in+L.A.+rush+to+escape+wildfires%2C+and+describe+an+apocalyptic+scene&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jens Lindemann grabbed eight trumpets, his Order of Canada, a box of cigars and a bottle of Dom Pérignon - and headed out into a scene that looked like a horror movie.
The 58-year-old Canadian and Grammy-nominated trumpeter said he'd thought his home, located on a mountain in the Pacific Palisades, would be safe. He was wrong and, on Thursday, it was destroyed in moments - one of the few that were hit by embers and burned down in his Los Angeles neighbourhood.
Even after he and his wife decided to evacuate, they found themselves trapped on the mountain for eight hours, he said, as so many cars had been abandoned on the roads he couldn't leave until the fire department literally bulldozed them out of the way.
"By the time we drove off the mountain, it was apocalyptic. There were cars all over the side of the drive. There was fires on both sides of the canyon. Buildings were completely burned out. It literally looked like a war zone," Lindemann told the Star.
"It's not uncommon in Southern California to have these little grass fires, but the way the winds were blowing, I've never felt it like that before."
Since Tuesday, fires have broken out in and around Los Angeles, killing at least five people and forcing tens of thousands of people to evacuate their homes as flames and heavy smoke engulfed neighbourhoods. Many blazes were fuelled by fierce winds gusting to more than 160 km/h.
"I took a picture of what just appeared to be a puff of smoke and then, five minutes later, you could see the hill had blown up with fire behind the house," Lindemann said. "It was at that point that I realized this was bigger than we or anybody had ever seen."
Beyond what he was able to grab as he left his home of 14 years, Lindemann said, "I've got two pairs of flip flops and some dirty sneakers."
Lindemann and his wife were on their way to Phoenix, Ariz., but he said they planned to stay the night at the home of a well-known Canadian friend.
"I'll be stopping in Palm Springs to see my friend Glen Sather, the hockey guru. He's a great friend from my Edmonton days. He heard about what happened, literally reached out 10 minutes ago and said, 'Anything we can do, we got an extra place.' And I said, 'OK, we'll stay with you tonight.'"
'A really quick mad dash'
Canadian filmmaker and producer Connor Malbeuf, meanwhile, said he considers himself among the fortunate.
On Wednesday evening, he was forced to flee his home in the Runyon Canyon area of the Hollywood Hills after a nearby fire erupted.
Dubbed the Sunset Fire, the blaze broke out in the Hollywood Hills - home to the iconic Hollywood sign - and eventually spread to 43 acres, which is roughly the size of 20 soccer fields.
"It was a really quick mad dash," Malbeuf said, describing how he frantically packed a bag of clothes, grabbed his passport and visa and drove off.
There was a flurry of misinformation online about what to do and where to go, and the roads were "absolute mayhem," said the 28-year-old of the motorists driving on the wrong side of the road and running red lights.
When Malbeuf returned home early Thursday morning, the air quality index (AQI) in areas near the fires in Los Angeles had surpassed 400, which is considered hazardous to public health. By comparison, Toronto had an AQI of 3, which indicates a low health risk.
Even outside the evacuation zones, air quality is poor, Malbeuf said.
Those with homes that have been destroyed are devastated. Kids whose schools have burned down are being sent to new classrooms miles away. Local businesses are shut down.
For those like Malbeuf, who have watched the terror unfold across the city, there is also an immense desire to help.
"It's this anxiety of, 'Well, I need to be safe, but I also am watching my community burn,'" he said.
At times, Malbeuf added, it is confusing, because so much information is circulating about the fires and it's hard to know what to believe or where to donate.
"We're just so inundated with information that there's not really a clear way of supporting and helping."
As of Thursday night, as the new Kenneth Fire started in the San Fernando Valley, more than 180,000 people were under evacuation orders. The fires have swallowed about 117 square kilometres - a little more than the size of the city of Vancouver.
"You're almost in a state of shock," said Kam Soltanzadeh, 26, from Toronto, who watched from his L.A. office on Tuesday as a large cloud of smoke rose from the Pacific Palisades.
"I've probably seen a scenario like this countless times watching any kind of apocalyptic movie," he said. "But when you're actually in it, it's totally different."
The scenes have elicited worldwide concern. The Canadian and Ontario governments have pledged to send supplies and support, as well as personnel, to help battle the fires.
On Thursday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau tweeted on X: "Canadian water bombers are already in action. 250 firefighters are ready to deploy. The @CanadianForces are standing by to move personnel and equipment. To our American neighbours: Canada's here to help."
Trudeau also posted a photo of a Canadian water bomber dropping water over the flames, with the line, "Neighbours helping neighbours."
"The scenes out of California are devastating," wrote Ontario Premier Doug Ford on X. "Ontario will always stand ready to serve. I've directed officials to send every available water bomber to fight these fires. We'll provide whatever personnel and equipment is needed and spare no expense to help our closest friends and allies."
According to the Ministry of Natural Resources, Ontario will be providing two water bombers, pilots and 165 urban firefighters with equipment such as hoses, pumps, axes and more. There is possibility to increase the numbers of firefighters, if required. There will also be an incident management team of as many as 20 additional staff with expertise in logistics, command and co-ordination, communications, response planning, safety and mapping.
"The request is for 2 CL-415 Skimmer Airtankers with flight crew to operate in Southern California," said Alexandria Jones, spokesperson for the Canadian Interagency Forest Fire Centre (CIFFC), in a statement. "There are no fire crew requests at this time. The request is being actioned but the delivery timeline is currently unavailable."
"We are also proactively working to identify potential resource availability, should more requests come in. We remain in close contact with our colleagues at the National Interagency Fire Center in Boise as the situation continues to evolve."
Harjit Sajjan, minister of emergency preparedness, also tweeted that he has been in contact with the head of the U.S. Federal Emergency Management Agency and Canadian Armed Forces were also preparing to deploy to offer assistance.
"Team Canada, with Ontario, Quebec, and Alberta, is ready to deploy 250 firefighters, aircraft equipment, and other resources as early as tonight. The Canadian Forces are standing by to move personnel and equipment. Canada stands ready to support our American neighbours during this challenging time," wrote Sajjan.
With files from The Associated Press

Document 157 of 500

Justin Trudeau says border issues were 'clear win' for Donald Trump - but also for Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-says-border-issues-were-clear-win/docview/3153926387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+says+border+issues+were+%27clear+win%27+for+Donald+Trump+-+but+also+for+Canada&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to American airwaves on Thursday to address U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's escalating threats against Canada - and admitted the incoming president's border concerns netted a win for both nations.
In an interview with CNN's Jake Tapper, the television anchor pointed to the $1.3 billion border security package the Trudeau government unveiled in December in response to Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat, which he said was necessary due to illegal immigration and drug smuggling across America's northern border.
The Canadian government's response to those threats were "a clear win" for Trump, Trudeau told Tapper, which "got us to do even more, to reduce even further, the minimal amounts coming in."
Trudeau was in Washington, D.C. to attend the funeral of former U.S. president Jimmy Carter, a visit that placed the outgoing prime minister and Liberal leader in close proximity to Trump, though Trudeau said the pair did not speak. He also taped an interview with American news channel MSNBC, which is expected to air Sunday.
The prime minister said Trump was a "very skilful negotiator" who was using talk of annexing Canada and making it America's 51st state to distract people from "the conversation around 25 per cent tariffs on oil and gas and electricity and steel and aluminum and lumber and concrete," and how everything Americans rely on from Canada "is suddenly going to get a lot more expensive."
"That's something that I think we need to be focusing on a little bit more," Trudeau said.
The prime minister's comments come as he is expected to meet with Canada's premiers in the capital next Wednesday to further craft Ottawa's response.
The Liberal government is working on more than one list of counter-tariffs, including politically sensitive products such as Kentucky bourbon or Florida orange juice, like it targeted in 2018 when Trump's first administration hit Canada with $16.6 billion in tariffs on steel and aluminum products. The move prompted Canada to respond with surcharges on select American goods that matched Trump's levies "dollar for dollar."
Also under consideration is a broader list to deal with Trump's threat of a 25 per cent "across the board" tariffs on all Canadian imports.
In dollar terms, Canada sells more to the U.S. - mainly because of energy exports - than America sells to Canada, resulting in a trade deficit, which is one of Trump's chief complaints. That means that if Canada were to match his move dollar-for-dollar this time, Ottawa would effectively be at tariffs above 25 per cent because this country has a smaller base of U.S. imports.
Although some have said the government is prepared to release details of a possible response, which the Star reported Wednesday, two senior officials insisted the Trudeau government has not yet decided exactly how or when it will respond, nor whether it will release its list of targets before Trump's inauguration or after Trump formally acts on his tariff threat against Canada and Mexico.
One official told the Star, on the condition of anonymity, that Canadian business groups have also fallen into two camps about how the government should respond. Some are urging swift aggressive action to show toughness to Trump, while others say Ottawa should not antagonize the incoming president and should only respond when he officially acts.
At Queen's Park on Thursday, Premier Doug Ford was asked about the possibility of Ottawa slapping tariffs on U.S. goods like orange juice, ceramics and some steel products.
"There's more to come, too," the Ontario premier warned.
"I agree with their list and other areas that I've seen, I agree with it," Ford said, adding that it's a "shame" that Canada is considering retaliatory action instead of working with its "closest ally."
"I've talked to endless, endless governors, endless congresspeople and senators - Republican, Democrat - I have yet to hear one of the elected officials say they agree with this approach," the premier said.
While Ford on Wednesday signalled his interest in forming an energy alliance with the U.S., he has also previously said he was prepared to cut off energy flowing across the border in response to Trump's threats.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, meanwhile, arguedagainst making "oil and gas and other energy" part of Canada's response during a news conference in Ottawa on Thursday.
He instead said it was the "last 60 days of Liberal chaos" and the Trudeau government's "weakness" that ultimately invited Trump to make the threats.
"We need to put Canada first by locking arms with American economic allies on the ground. That will put pressure on Washington to do the right thing," Poilievre told reporters.
"Look, I'm not prime minister. I respectfully ask the Canadian people to give me the chance to serve them in that role. But that's why we need an axe the tax election."
When asked whether the magnitude of Trump's threats against Canada had spurred Poilievre to shift his focus to issues beyond the federal carbon price, the Conservative leader called the measure a "devastating tariff" the Liberal government was levying against its own people.
He tied potential Trudeau successors, like former deputy prime minister and finance minister Chrystia Freeland and former B.C. premier Christy Clark, to the carbon levy, and said they all agree on "the same disastrous economic policies."
"Liberals are putting Liberals first. It's time to put Canada first," Poilievre said.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was in close proximity to U.S. president-elect Donald Trump on Thursday at the funeral of former president Jimmy Carter in Washington, D.C. Trudeau said he and Trump did not speak.

Document 158 of 500

The U.S. TikTok ban is going before the Supreme Court today. What does this mean for Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/u-s-tiktok-ban-is-going-before-supreme-court/docview/3153754058/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+U.S.+TikTok+ban+is+going+before+the+Supreme+Court+today.+What+does+this+mean+for+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TikTok's fate in the U.S. hangs in the balance as the Supreme Court weighs in on a proposed ban of the popular social media platform.
The TikTok ban made its way through the U.S. House and Senate early last year, tucked into an aid package for Ukraine, Israel and Taiwan, and was signed into law in April by President Joe Biden. Now the decision lays at the feet of the nine Supreme Court justices in the U.S., as they consider arguments on Friday. That renewed scrutiny on TikTok in Canada after the social media app was removed from government-issued devices nearly two years ago.
Despite those national security concerns, nearly 76 per cent of Canadians aged 18 to 24 were on TikTok, according to a 2022 survey from Toronto Metropolitan University's Social Media Lab. But another survey, from a Leger poll in March 2024, found that half of Canadians support the TikTok ban down south, with an increasing number of Canadians expressing concerns about their data security.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, speaking shortly after the TikTok ban became law, said he would not comment on the U.S. bill but added that "Canada will continue to look very closely at how we can make sure we're keeping Canadians safe, while making sure we're taking advantage of great technologies that spur innovation and opportunities for people right across the country."
"We know that the security, the privacy and the data protection of Canadians needs to be a first priority for us," Trudeau added.
The government later ordered TikTok's Canadian operations to be shut down, which the company is currently challenging in court. That order would not block Canadians' access to the TikTok app.
With widespread use among young Canadians, how would a ban in the U.S. affect users up north? And could Canada follow suit with its own ban?
Is the U.S. banning TikTok?
The timing of the TikTok ban has neatly coincided with president-elect Donald Trump's inauguration, leaving the ban up in the air.
The TikTok bill threatened a ban in an effort to force the sale of the social media platform from the company's China-based parent, ByteDance, setting a nine-month deadline for ByteDance to sell the company, with the possibility of a three-month extension if a sale is in progress.
TikTok's parent company, ByteDance, has said it won't sell the short-form video platform. But some investors have been eyeing it, including Trump's former treasury secretary Steven Mnuchin, billionaire businessman Frank McCourt and Canadian businessman Kevin O'Leary.
On Thursday, McCourt and O'Leary's Project Liberty initiative said it, along with its unnamed partners, presented a proposal to ByteDance to acquire TikTok's U.S. assets.
If TikTok isn't sold to an approved buyer, the federal law would prohibit app stores, such as those operated by Apple and Google, from offering the popular app. It would also bar internet hosting services from hosting TikTok.
TikTok, in April, said it would wage a legal challenge against what it called an "unconstitutional" effort by Congress.
"We believe the facts and the law are clearly on our side, and we will ultimately prevail," the company said in a statement to the Associated Press.
American lawmakers claim that TikTok's parent company, ByteDance, is beholden to the Chinese government and could be forced to provide user data to the government at any time in accordance with China's National Intelligence Law.
However, the U.S. government hasn't provided any evidence to back its claims, and TikTok has long denied that it has, or would ever, share user data with Chinese government.
TikTok's CEO was grilled at a U.S. congressional committee nearly a two years ago over national security concerns. Shou Zi Chew told the committee that TikTok's headquarters are in Los Angeles and in Singapore, with 7,000 employees in the U.S. and that TikTok is not available in China.
The ban's fate grew more complicated as Trump weighed in, voicing his opposition in a court filing, arguing that he could secure a "political resolution" after his inauguration on Jan. 20, the day after the ban is set to go into effect.
How would a U.S. TikTok ban work?
Divestiture, the forced sale of TikTok being proposed in the U.S., is usually a method of breaking up monopolies under competition and antitrust law, Vass Bednar, executive director of McMaster University's Master of Public Policy in a Digital Society, said, but that's not the case with TikTok.
Bednar explained that the fear of TikTok having to hand over its data is real, but that the conversation should instead be focused on cybersecurity and privacy policy.
The proposed ban sets a precedent for governments, Philip Mai - co-director of TMU's Social Media Lab - warned, setting up the ability for future presidents to ban or force the sale of social media platforms by citing national security concerns.
Even if a ban were to go through in the U.S., Mai said, it could be battled in court for years.
"This is probably going to be litigated for years to come," he added. "It's going to buy a lot of new mansions for a lot of lawyers in Washington, D.C. for the next few years."
Canada deletes TikTok from government-issued devices
The same national security concerns cited in the U.S. sparked a widespread ban of TikTok from federal and provincial government-issued phones, with Premier Doug Ford going even further, ordering all of his Ontario PC caucus members to remove the application from their personal mobile devices in March 2023.
The Canadian Press reported on March 14 that the federal government had ordered a national security review of the social media app, though Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne later said parents shouldn't be concerned about their children using TikTok.
But even the government hasn't cut its TikTok ties completely. Bednar pointed out that the government continued to spend over a million dollars on advertisements in 2022 to 2023 on a social media platform it had wiped from government devices.
Will Canada ban TikTok?
There's not much appetite in Canada for a widespread ban on TikTok, Mai said, especially as influencers in Canada rely on the popular social media platform for viewership.
If the U.S. were to pass its TikTok ban, Canada is likely to follow suit, Bednar added.
"We're definitely a follower country," she said, "And I think it would be difficult for us to rationalize why we're not following suit when our, when our American neighbour is taking a particular stance."
But even a Canadian TikTok ban wouldn't solve the issue of how companies handle, or mishandle, our data, Bednar said, and no major social media platform has ever been completely transparent about who has access to user data.
Even social media platforms that seem innocuous can have "secret vulnerabilities," she explained, giving the example of how Strava, a fitness app that helps runners track and share their routes, accidentally revealed the location of U.S. military bases in Syria.
The implication of a ban could be that TikTok users are somehow putting Canada's national security at risk, Bednar added, but data security is a bigger, and separate, issue.
"Really there's just this problem with privacy and information and who it's being shared with," she said.
"Other than the connection to the Chinese government, American social media apps behave in very similar ways when it comes to how greedy they are with data."
CAPTION: Sarah Baus of Charleston, S.C., holds a sign that reads "Keep TikTok" as she and other content creators Sallye Miley of Jackson, Miss., and Callie Goodwin of Columbia, S.C., stand outside the U.S. Supreme Court Building as the court hears oral arguments on whether to overturn or delay a law that could lead to a ban of TikTok in the U.S., on Friday in Washington, DC.

What might life be like in Donald Trump's 51st state? Here's what we can imagine from Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-might-life-be-like-donald-trumps-51st-state/docview/3153753648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+might+life+be+like+in+Donald+Trump%27s+51st+state%3F+Here%27s+what+we+can+imagine+from+Canada&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: (This imagined dispatch from the future comes from the mind of Star prognosticator Edward Keenan)
TORONTO, 2028-Well, I guess he wasn't trolling after all.
I mean, it's obvious now. Standing here, on the steps of the University Avenue Statehouse and Casino in Toronto.
We're in what's now called the Great State of West Ohio (renamed from "Canada" on the same principle, President Donald Trump explained, that the state north of Virginia is called West Virginia). We're watching Gov. Wayne Gretzky being sworn in.
It makes sense, in a way. The biggest immediately noticeable benefit to Canadians - er, West Ohioans, sorry, still getting used to it - of our merger with the larger U.S.A. is that our new combined hockey team is an absolute world powerhouse. Connor McDavid, Auston Matthews, Cale Makar and Connor Hellebuyck all on the same team? Who's beating that? And women's hockey? There's hardly a competition left.
So sure, put the hockey guy in charge. Not that there was an election, what with the Great Greenland War underway, and the frequent "insurrectionist" attacks by Quebec separatists, and the deep state trials underway in Washington. Who knows when we Americans will see elections again?
People thought Trump was trolling when he said Canada should be the 51st state, and when he suggested Gretzky as governor. Like the American Republican establishment and the U.S. political mainstream before them, they first dismissed his talk as bluster, and then thought the best way to manage him was to feed his ego and try to give him what he wanted.
They kept thinking that under the 25 and then 50 per cent tariffs that led to the 13-cent dollar, and then right on through the great resource handover that was supposed to appease Trump once and for all. They kept thinking it right up until Operation "Grab Them By the Beaver" rolled into Ottawa.
So here we are. Gov. Gretzky and President Trump and Loo-tenant Gov. Jordan Peterson helicoptered down from the new "Summer White House" at Trump's Muskoka-Lago resort for the swearing in. It still feels weird that it's in Toronto, but Trump insisted this be the capital since it was a place he'd heard of.
"You miss 100 per cent of the shots you don't take," Gov. Gretzky is saying at his swearing in, repeating his single catchphrase. He's also accurately describing the defence Canada mounted when the Americans finally invaded. We missed 100 per cent of our shots, in as much as we didn't take any. The world's longest undefended border lived up to its name til the end.
Not that everyone was happy about it. Prime Minister Pierre Poilievre recorded a bunch of really long YouTube videos explaining why Trump shouldn't even want Canada because no one can afford a house here and woke mobs had infested the posts and beams of our farmhouse or something like that. But then Trump started calling him "Prime Minister Pee Pee" and then "Stinky Pee Pee," and the convoy crowd thought that was hilarious and that just about ended the last prime minister's reign.
Doug Ford and the other premiers talked really tough for a while, even as they were engineering the new "equal partners" trade agreement that allowed Americans unfettered access to all our mining, oil, maple syrup and fresh water rights in exchange for lowering tariffs to 15 per cent. Like I said, trying to be his best friend and keep him happy. Fat lot of good that did. Now those former premiers are all up in the Northwest Territories at "the best - everyone says it, the best in history, the best Gulag the world has ever seen" alongside the migrants awaiting deportation and the "woke enemies of free speech" awaiting re-education.
Many of us regular people tried to resist, of course. It's just we didn't anticipate that Trump would be just as immune to mass marches insisting Canadian sovereignty matters as he once had been to ones insisting that Black Lives Matter. The full-blast hectoring Marg Delahunty gave him on that one episode of "This Hour Has 22 Minutes" hardly fazed Trump at all, since he seemed to be under the impression she was one of his own lawyers. Drake somehow lost the exchange of diss tracks.
Meanwhile, the global community so many thought would defend us were pretty preoccupied with their own battles, once Trump signed the "Stay In Your Lane" treaty with Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping that disbanded NATO and recognized Russia's sphere of influence over Europe and China's over East Asia as long as they stayed out of Trump's way in the Americas. Looking at the situation in France and Taiwan, a lot of people here are just saying "I guess it could be worse." We might put that on the licence plates.
There are a lot more shootings, granted, now that you can buy semi-automatic rifles at the corner store alongside beer and lottery tickets. And a lot of people miss having reproductive rights, and government health care. And the new "anti-woke free speech" laws requiring everyone to use the pronouns their drunkest uncle says they should appears to just be the beginning of a horrifically hard time for a population that was already pretty vulnerable.
On the other hand, Target came back, and the shelves are full this time. Just like the President promised, back when everyone said he was just trolling us.
CAPTION: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has said he told Wayne Gretzky that he should run for prime minister of Canada.U.S. president-elect Donald Trump is talking about annexation. Many say he's joking. What if we imagine, for a moment, that he's not? Edward Keenan imagines a Canada-under-Trump future.

Subject: Presidents; Prime ministers; Freedom of speech; Tariffs

Title: What might life be like in Donald Trump's 51st state? Here's what we can imagine from Canada

Document 160 of 500

In the face of Trump's threats, the man of the hour is... wait, Doug Ford?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/face-trumps-threats-man-hour-is-wait-doug-ford/docview/3153465147/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+the+face+of+Trump%27s+threats%2C+the+man+of+the+hour+is...+wait%2C+Doug+Ford%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's taken a mind-bendingly bizarre set of circumstances to turn Doug Ford into the beau ideal of Captain Canada. But such are the times we're in.
First, Donald Trump was elected to a second term - a second term! - as president of the United States, and is about to take office more experienced, more emboldened and less opposed than during his appalling first.
Second, Trump seems to have noticed Canada.
For generations, we turned cartwheels trying to get American leaders to pay attention to us. Now one does. And he wants to own us, just another real-estate acquisition, a fixer-upper with good bones but a lot of expensive frills and architectural details he'd promptly tear down or bulldoze.
Trump has mused - publicly, preposterously and insultingly - about Canada becoming a 51st state. Not very neighbourly of him. Not one little bit.
Say this for him, though: the master troll knows precisely where the blade goes. His instinct for another's weakness and how to make critics yelp is pretty nearly unparalleled.
Well, Trump seems to have got our attention. And the next four years promise to be more ludicrous than any in the history of our role as next-door neighbour to the United States of Egomania.
So who is best fitted to take him on?
It wouldn't be an intellectual type, nor a sober restrained voice of reason. It wouldn't be a master of data and statistical analysis, nor the builder of rational argument.
It turns out the best Trump retardant we have is a man once many regarded as similarly unfit and temperamentally ill-suited to high office.
That would be Douglas Robert Ford Jr., man of the hour.
Trump detests leaders like Justin Trudeau. He wouldn't much like Pierre Poilievre. He would not at all care for any female counterpart.
Trump favours men. Men's men. Plain-talkin', locker-room jokin', Rat Pack sort of 1950s' men. Men who cut deals. Men just like Ford.
Recent dispatches from the premier's office have shown Ford gets this.
They have been couched in the sort of militaristic language that is catnip for the again-to-be-commander-in-chief Trump.
In recently proposing to strengthen security along the Ontario-U.S. border - a long stretch of irritant that pains Trump more than a case of the shingles - Ford launched "Operation Deterrence."
He followed that coup by proposing to build "Fortress Am-Can to power economic prosperity in Canada and the United States."
Other than making the small error of failing to put the U.S. first, the premier sounded more American than the Americans.
He made the Pentagon PR staff who soundtracked the U.S. invasions of Iraq sound like a bunch of pantywaists.
The premier understands Trump's America. He knows it loves nothing so much as political discourse that sounds a bunch of nine-year-old boys from the 1950s playing at being soldiers.
But that wasn't all. Far from it.
To cap his stellar week, Ford bravely survived a fender-bender on Wednesday, "sideswiped" on Highway 401 after leaving the Darlington Nuclear Generating Station in Pickering where, as it happens, he was pitching a new energy partnership with the U.S..
A lesser premier would have delivered a bloodless commentary, downplaying the impact and the disaster narrowly dodged.
Not Ford. He told it the way a working stiff does across the lunch table at the plant.
"Oh yeah, we were leaving OPG, driving down the road on the highway and the next thing you know, out of nowhere, the car got sideswiped and that is all I can say," he said.
But, of course, it wasn't.
"We were driving straight and next thing we know we got hammered, just hammered. I'm okay. This head is like limestone. It is as hard as a rock."
The gentleman is a Trump clone. He all but took a bullet for democracy. And he's more fluent in the vernacular of Common Man than the incoming president himself.
Ford will shut down this 51st state nonsense faster than you can say socialized medicine and cannabis on every corner.
All he has to do is invite his soulmate north for a visit, tour him around some of the scruffier parts of the province, tell him Canada is just one big Detroit or Milwaukee, and Trump's goldfish attention span will be swimming laps toward a takeover of the Orkney Islands.
CAPTION: Doug Ford has emerged as an unlikely hero in responding to threats made by U.S. President-elect Donald Trump, writes Jim Coyle.

Document 161 of 500

'Two pairs of flip flops and some dirty sneakers'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-pairs-flip-flops-some-dirty-sneakers/docview/3153453901/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Two+pairs+of+flip+flops+and+some+dirty+sneakers%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jens Lindemann grabbed eight trumpets, his Order of Canada, a box of cigars and a bottle of Dom Pérignon - and headed out into a scene that looked like a horror movie.
The 58-year-old Canadian and Grammy-nominated trumpeter said he'd thought his home, located on a mountain in the Pacific Palisades, would be safe. He was wrong and, on Thursday, it was destroyed in moments - one of the few that were hit by embers and burned down in his Los Angeles neighbourhood.
Even after he and his wife decided to evacuate, they found themselves trapped on the mountain for eight hours, he said, as so many cars had been abandoned on the roads he couldn't leave until the fire department literally bulldozed them out of the way.
"By the time we drove off the mountain, it was apocalyptic. There were cars all over the side of the drive. There was fires on both sides of the canyon. Buildings were completely burned out. It literally looked like a war zone," Lindemann told the Star.
"I took a picture of what just appeared to be a puff of smoke and then, five minutes later, you could see the hill had blown up with fire behind the house," Lindemann said. "It was at that point that I realized this was bigger than we or anybody had ever seen."
Beyond what he was able to grab as he left his home of 14 years, Lindemann said, "I've got two pairs of flip flops and some dirty sneakers."
Lindemann and his wife were on their way to Phoenix, Ariz., but he said they planned to stay the night at the home of a well-known Canadian friend.
"I'll be stopping in Palm Springs to see my friend Glen Sather, the hockey guru. He's a great friend from my Edmonton days. He heard about what happened, literally reached out 10 minutes ago and said, 'Anything we can do, we got an extra place.' And I said, 'OK, we'll stay with you tonight.' "

Canadian filmmaker and producer Connor Malbeuf, meanwhile, said he considers himself among the fortunate.
On Wednesday evening, he was forced to flee his home in the Runyon Canyon area of the Hollywood Hills after a nearby fire erupted.
Dubbed the Sunset Fire, the blaze broke out in the Hollywood Hills - home to the iconic Hollywood sign - and eventually spread to 43 acres, which is roughly the size of 20 soccer fields.
"It was a really quick mad dash," Malbeuf said, describing how he frantically packed a bag of clothes, grabbed his passport and visa and drove off.
There was a flurry of misinformation online about what to do and where to go, and the roads were "absolute mayhem," said the 28-year-old of the motorists driving on the wrong side of the road and running red lights.
When Malbeuf returned home early Thursday morning, the air quality index (AQI) in areas near the fires in Los Angeles had surpassed 400, which is considered hazardous to public health. By comparison, Toronto had an AQI of 3, which indicates a low health risk.
For those like Malbeuf, who have watched the terror unfold across the city, there is also an immense desire to help.
"It's this anxiety of, 'Well, I need to be safe, but I also am watching my community burn,' " he said.
At times, Malbeuf added, it is confusing, because so much information is circulating about the fires and it's hard to know what to believe or where to donate.
"We're just so inundated with information that there's not really a clear way of supporting and helping."
"You're almost in a state of shock," said Kam Soltanzadeh, 26, from Toronto, who watched from his L.A. office on Tuesday as a large cloud of smoke rose from the Pacific Palisades.
"I've probably seen a scenario like this countless times watching any kind of apocalyptic movie," he said. "But when you're actually in it, it's totally different."
On Thursday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau tweeted on X: "Canadian water bombers are already in action. 250 firefighters are ready to deploy. The @CanadianForces are standing by to move personnel and equipment. To our American neighbours: Canada's here to help."
"The scenes out of California are devastating," wrote Ontario Premier Doug Ford on X. "Ontario will always stand ready to serve. I've directed officials to send every available water bomber to fight these fires. We'll provide whatever personnel and equipment is needed and spare no expense to help our closest friends and allies."
According to the Ministry of Natural Resources, Ontario will be providing two water bombers, pilots and 165 urban firefighters with equipment such as hoses, pumps, axes and more. There will also be an incident management team of as many as 20 staff with expertise in logistics, command and co-ordination, communications, response planning, safety and mapping.
With files from
The Associated Press
CAPTION: Canadian musician Jens Lindemann took photos as he and his wife left their Los Angeles home that burned Thursday in the Palisades Fire.Jens Lindemann

Document 162 of 500

More than 80,000 people were homeless in 2024: Report calls for co-operation between governments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-than-80-000-people-were-homeless-2024/docview/3153453794/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+than+80%2C000+people+were+homeless+in+2024%3A+Report+calls+for+co-operation+between+governments&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: About 81,500 people experienced homelessness in Ontario in 2024, a new report has found, a 25 per cent increase compared to 2022.
In the scenario of an economic downturn, the number of people experiencing homelessness could rise to more than 294,000 by 2035without significant government intervention, it added.
The first-of-its-kind analysis was released by the Association of Municipalities of Ontario in partnership with service agencies.
At a news conference on Thursday, AMO president and Westport Mayor Robin Jones said "municipalities are on the front lines of this crisis."
Jones noted the report finally puts a number on the extent of the homelessness crisis across Ontario- one that is about the size of the population of Peterborough.
"Let's understand that this is the floor, not the ceiling, of the issue," she said.
The report, which relied on data contributions from service managers, said municipalities have paid for 51.5 per cent of the $4.1 billion in total funding for housing and homelessness programs in Ontario in 2024 - almost doubling its funding contributions since 2020.
But it added that there needs to be targeted investment and co-ordination from all three levels of government for a path toward a future in which homelessness is "rare, brief and nonrecurring."
"Too many people are stuck in a cycle because our homeless response system is broken and poorly funded," Jones said at the conference, adding provincial and federal investments only contribute a fraction of what is necessary.
About 41,500 people - half of those who were homeless last year - experienced "chronic homelessness," the report said, meaning their experience was prolonged or repeated.
That number represents a "flashing red signal" of how strained the housing system is, and it reveals that "too many people are entering homelessness and too few people are exiting homelessness," the report said.
Additionally, there are roughly 1,400 homeless encampments across large and small communities in the province, said Karen Redman, chair of the Mayors and Regional Chairs of Ontario and chair of Waterloo Region, at the conference.
The report said that in order to end chronic homelessness in Ontario, there would need to be an additional $11 billion over 10 years to invest in affordable housing and prevention, plus services that support "outflow from homelessness."
A shorter-term scenario that focuses on housing and supporting people in encampments would require $2 billion, the report found.
The report doesn't make recommendations about how much funding each level of government should provide, but lays out different scenarios.
In a statement Thursday afternoon, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles called the report a "searing indictment" of inaction by the Doug Ford government.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner added that "for less than the amount of money (the Tories are) spending on wastefully building Highway 413 and tearing down Ontario Place, this government could end chronic homelessness - but they won't."
In an email to the Star, Housing Minister Paul Calandra's spokesperson said the government is investing, over the next three years, "a record $3 billion in affordable housing, anti-homelessness and emergency shelter supports."
Later at Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford said "we do have to continue investing" to ease homelessness, but he did not offer any new funding.
"It's concerning," he added. "I'd love to house every single person."
Ford called on the federal government to "step up" with more cash and expedited work permits for new arrivals to Canada.
Too few people are escaping homelessness because supportive housing in the province is in "critically short supply," the report said, and a longtime shortage of affordable housing forces people to depend on temporary arrangements like shelters for far longer than intended.
Across the province, the report found more than 268,000 households were on housing wait-lists with average wait time of more than five years. In some regions, wait times were more than 20 years.
It found only one non-health-operated supportive housing space was available for every 14 people known to the homelessness system.
"As a result, people become trapped in unsheltered homelessness or the emergency shelter system for extended periods, facing heightened risks of trauma, violence, worsening health outcomes and even loss of life."
The report also found some groups faced greater barriers and inequities, causing gaps in resources to have a disproportionate impact on them.
Indigenous Peoples accounted for nearly 45 per cent of those experiencing chronic homelessness in northern communities.
Barriers to culturally safe and Indigenous-led housing solutions include systemic racism, discrimination, intergenerational trauma and colonial policies, the report said. Insufficient funding has also caused a lack of adequate housing on reserve, increasing housing instability.
Children and youth (newborns to age 24) account for nearly 25 per cent of people experiencing chronic homelessness, the report said, highlighting the "growing vulnerability of young people and the systemic gaps in services to support them."
Additionally, refugees and asylum seekers have had a sixfold increase in chronic homelessness since 2020, "underscoring the challenges posed by immigration policies that fail to provide adequate support for housing and integration."
The inequities are systemic and shaped by "regional disparities" it added.
The report noted that known homelessness in northern Ontario has more than doubled since 2016, from 1,771 people to 5,377 people in 2024.
The report was published Thursday by The Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) in partnership with the Ontario Municipal Social Services Association, the Northern Ontario Service Deliverers Association and HelpSeeker Technologies.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Manuela Vega Housing Reporter With files from Rob Ferguson
