Document 163 of 500

Trudeau says border issue a win-win: PM praises Trump's negotiating tactics around tariff threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-says-border-issue-win/docview/3153453774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+says+border+issue+a+win-win%3A+PM+praises+Trump%27s+negotiating+tactics+around+tariff+threats&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to American airwaves on Thursday to address U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's escalating threats against Canada - and admitted the incoming president's border concerns netted a win for both nations.
In an interview with CNN's Jake Tapper, the television anchor pointed to the $1.3-billion border security package the Trudeau government unveiled in December in response to Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat, which he said was necessary due to illegal immigration and drug smuggling across America's northern border.
The Canadian government's response to those threats were "a clear win" for Trump, Trudeau told Tapper, which "got us to do even more, to reduce even further, the minimal amounts coming in."
Trudeau was in Washington to attend the funeral of former U.S. president Jimmy Carter, a visit that placed the outgoing prime minister and Liberal leader in proximity to Trump, though Trudeau said the pair did not speak. He also taped an interview with American news channel MSNBC, which is expected to air Sunday.
The prime minister said Trump was a "very skilful negotiator" who was using talk of annexing Canada and making it America's 51st state to distract people from "the conversation around 25 per cent tariffs on oil and gas and electricity and steel and aluminum and lumber and concrete," and how everything Americans rely on from Canada "is suddenly going to get a lot more expensive."
"That's something that I think we need to be focusing on a little bit more," Trudeau said.
The prime minister's comments come as he is expected to meet with Canada's premiers in the capital next Wednesday to further craft Ottawa's response.
The Liberal government is working on more than one list of counter-tariffs, including politically sensitive products such as Kentucky bourbon or Florida orange juice, like it targeted in 2018 when Trump's first administration hit Canada with $16.6 billion in tariffs on steel and aluminum products. The move prompted Canada to respond with surcharges on select American goods that matched Trump's levies "dollar for dollar."
Also under consideration is a broader list to deal with Trump's threat of a 25 per cent "across the board" tariffs on all Canadian imports.
In dollar terms, Canada sells more to the U.S. - mainly because of energy exports - than America sells to Canada, resulting in a trade deficit, which is one of Trump's chief complaints. That means that if Canada were to match his move dollar-for-dollar this time, Ottawa would effectively be at tariffs above 25 per cent because this country has a smaller base of U.S. imports.
As the Star reported Wednesday, the government is prepared to release details of a possible response soon. Two senior officials insisted the Trudeau government has not yet decided exactly how or when it will respond, nor whether it will release its list of targets before Trump's inauguration or after Trump formally acts on his tariff threat against Canada and Mexico.
One official told the Star, on the condition of anonymity, that Canadian business groups have also fallen into two camps about how the government should respond. Some are urging swift aggressive action to show toughness to Trump, while others say Ottawa should not antagonize the incoming president and should only respond when he officially acts.
At Queen's Park on Thursday, Premier Doug Ford was asked about the possibility of Ottawa slapping tariffs on U.S. goods like orange juice, ceramics and some steel products.
"There's more to come, too," the Ontario premier warned.
"I agree with their list and other areas that I've seen, I agree with it," Ford said, adding that it's a "shame" that Canada is considering retaliatory action instead of working with its "closest ally."
"I've talked to endless, endless governors, endless congresspeople and senators - Republican, Democrat - I have yet to hear one of the elected officials say they agree with this approach," the premier said.
While Ford on Wednesday signalled his interest in forming an energy alliance with the U.S., he has also previously said he was prepared to cut off energy flowing across the border in response to Trump's threats.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, meanwhile, argued against making "oil and gas and other energy" part of Canada's response during a news conference in Ottawa on Thursday.
He instead said it was the "last 60 days of Liberal chaos" and the Trudeau government's "weakness" that ultimately invited Trump to make the threats.
"We need to put Canada first by locking arms with American economic allies on the ground. That will put pressure on Washington to do the right thing," Poilievre told reporters.
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau told CNN Thursday that U.S. president- elect Donald Trump's annexation talk to distract people from how his tariff threat against Canada means things are "suddenly going to get a lot more expensive" for American consumers.Jose Luis Magana The Associated Press

Document 164 of 500

Canada doesn't just need a new government. It needs new political parties

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-doesnt-just-need-new-government-needs/docview/3153453726/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+doesn%27t+just+need+a+new+government.+It+needs+new+political+parties&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When members of the Reform Party met in Ottawa in 1999, they didn't agree on much. But when the party's 1,200 odd delegates were asked to vote on the party's priorities, only one idea proved very popular: Create a new political party.
Reform itself was barely a decade old. But by the next year's election, it would be reborn as the Canadian Alliance. Four years later, the party was once again remade into the Conservative Party we know today.
Far from being the exception, the machinations which gave us nearly a decade of Prime Minister Stephen Harper are actually the norm. Over Canada's 44 federal elections, roughly half have seen a new party elected to the House of Commons.
The past 15 years, however, have been bear times for political startups. That's odd in and of itself, but it's particularly strange when you consider how frustrated Canadians clearly are by the choices on offer.
Earlier this year, pollster Angus Reid released a depressing statistic. Going back to 1974, the pollster notes, "there has never been a time when leaders of all three parties simultaneously turn Canadians off to such an extent."
It suggests we're due for someone to come in and disrupt the staid and dysfunctional status quo. That we need some new political parties - and, perhaps, for some old ones to die.
For decades, the Liberals and NDP have tried to remain relevant with the same tactic: Swap one leader for another, then hope things improve. The Liberal Party will try this again in the coming months. But opting for a new cult of personality is no solution. Liberals, like partisans of other stripes, need to accept that their vehicle is rusted. The rest of the country needs to start looking for new models.
Political parties are democratic microcosms in their own right: Bound by their own internal constitutions, run by the will of their members, full of quirks and particularities. While they may have started as more elite clubs, by the late 20th century anyone could pay a small fee and access the room where government policy gets made.
But in recent decades, most of our political parties - the Liberals in particular - have shed those trappings of internal democracy to become cheering sections for their dear leaders. They have forsaken real-world organizing for digital engagement, and it has made them insular, disconnected, and delusional.
The fact that Trudeau was able to stay on for as long as he did, long after the thrill of Trudeaumania had gone, is testament to how little power his party now has. It required nearly every single MP to stand up and demand his ouster for him to finally leave: And, even then, all they could do was ask. Neither his caucus nor Liberal Party "supporters" - the nebulous category which replaced actual card-carrying members - had any power to actually remove him.
The other parties aren't much better. The NDP continues to drift aimlessly under its Tiktok-obsessed leader whilst the Green Party has found itself, once again, a one-woman show.
Rather than asking the parties to reinvent themselves, voters should be hoping for, at least, some new competition or, at best, the legacy players' collapse.
We are currently in the second-longest streak without a new party entering parliament in Canadian history. The longest stretch we've ever gone without a new party breaking through happened between 1968 and 1988. At the end of it, the Progressive Conservative Party collapsed and splintered, the Bloc Quebecois emerged, and the whole political order basically radically reinvented itself.
Dominic Cardy certainly believes we're due for another such realignment.
Leader of the Canadian Future Party, he believes Canadians are hungry for something other than the "technologically-driven dark age politics from the right" and the "the echo chamber" of upper-middle class bourgeois politics of the left. "We're now the resistance," Cardy told me last week.
Cardy has taken a winding path to leading the upstart party, from leading New Brunswick NDP to serving four years as a Progressive Conservative cabinet minister. He resigned abruptly in 2022, in protest of his government's rightward drift.
The movement began as the Center Ice Conservatives, born out of discomfort with Pierre Poilievre's reactionary populist approach, before rebranding to the Canadian Future Party last year. (Not to be confused with The Future Party, which relaunched as The Forward Party, which has no relation to the American Forward Party.) Even still, Cardy insists that his new group isn't "some milquetoast centrist party," but rather "a radical party."
Cardy says his party is growing carefully, building local infrastructure and engaging grassroots activists while avoiding the pitfalls of a "flash in the pan" project. "If you don't build that up at the local level, it's just me saying things," he says.
While they don't have a full policy book yet, Cardy rattles off a list of centre-right beliefs which defines his party: A belief in small government, a tough-on-crime approach, boosting defence spending, more competition in the healthcare sector, and a fervent defence of our institutions.
This should, in theory, work. Pollster Nanos found in 2022 that Canadians, overwhelmingly, prefer a party that puts practicality ahead of ideology and that about half of Canadians said they would be open to a "new political party with views that fall between the Liberals and the Conservative."
While the Party isn't yet registered with Elections Canada, their results thus far have been meagre. They don't register in the polls, and they failed to crack single-digits in two by-elections they contested last year. (Cardy points to one good data point: They raised $50,000 last month.)
Further right on the spectrum, Maxime Bernier's crackpot People's Party hasn't had much success, either - although it's certainly exerted some influence over Poilievre's Conservatives.
It's possible that Cardy and Bernier have just been doing things backwards. Rather than starting with ideology and working backwards, most new parties have succeeded by actually responding to why people are disgruntled and disaffected.
While it is largely forgotten these days, one of the most successful Canadian parties of the last century, the Social Credit Party, rose out of the economic and social anxieties of the Great Depression. While it was notionally right-wing, it mixed a novel approach to economic stimulus for working people and small businesses with staunch social conservatism. The party rose, radically reformed the Canadian political scene, went madly off in all directions, then disappeared.
The Reform Party and Bloc Quebecois were founded, primarily, as regionalist parties, not explicitly as left or right. The Co-operative Commonwealth Federation and Green Party both arrived on the scene to address workers' rights and environmental concerns being ignored by the mainstay parties. In their early days, all of these parties were pluralist, grassroots, and dynamic organizations. They were hodgepodges of countervailing social and economic policies and internal factions, full of internal debate and messy intra-party politics. It was only with time that their culture and beliefs solidified into more coherent and consistent - that consistency later became complacency.
Arguably the most functional organization in Parliament today is also the newest: The Conservative Party. It boasts, by far, the most members, a litany of internal factions and groups, and a variety of mechanisms designed to give power to both their card-carrying members and elected representatives.
If the Conservatives are heading towards a crushing majority, as polls suggest, it would be prime time for some other parties to die and allow for some rebirth.
That could take many forms. Given we are currently in a productivity crisis, we could make good on a longstanding joke to field an "economist party." Or perhaps we need another populist party to compete with the Conservatives, one more determined to rebuild institutions instead of just wrecking them.
New parties also come with interesting and exciting new ways of doing politics. In Quebec, the relatively-new Quebec Solidaire opts for co-spokespeople instead of a single leader. In Italy, the anti-establishment Five Star Movement burst on the scene by allowing their members a vote on virtually every decision they make.
Yet, despite a clear case for a political repolarization, we seem further away from a new party breaking through than ever. Why?
Cardy identifies a prime suspect: Social media.
Over the past two decades, political organizing has shifted aggressively from IRL politics and media relations towards slacktivism, e-petition signing, and furious tweeting. This shift has become so mundane that we've given up even complaining about it.
Social media is zapping our democratic mojo. It provides all the illusion of grassroots politics without actually achieving it. And yet there is no clear alternative for how we break out of this digital depressive spiral.
Cardy is optimistic about his party's chances. But at the end of our conversation, he offers a bit of caution. Our institutions and democracy, he says, are toast if we keep uploading our conversations to the fractious, manipulated, and unreal discourse online. People, Cardy says, need to "wake up and notice that people are losing our political space."
CAPTION: Dominic Cardy, left, pictured here in the New Brunswick Legislature in 2018 believes it's time for new federal political parties.

Subject: Conservatism; Canadians; Political activism; Democracy; Populism; Social networks; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Title: Canada doesn't just need a new government. It needs new political parties

Document 165 of 500

I'm excited about the year ahead for journalism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/im-excited-about-year-ahead-journalism/docview/3153453619/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I%27m+excited+about+the+year+ahead+for+journalism&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It has been an absolute feeding frenzy of late for journalists who cover the political scene here in Canada, with no sign things will quiet down any time soon.
Politics is bread and butter for the Toronto Star. Most days you'll see at least one story from one of our bureaus - Ottawa, Queen's Park or city hall - on the homepage of our website and A1 of the newspaper.
More so lately, with news out of our nation's capital and Ontario legislature and the U.S. keeping our readers' heads spinning, our journalists hopping and generating headlines around the world.
"Our bureaus have been going non-stop, pulling long days, working the phones constantly. It's been super interesting and fun to have a front-row seat to history right now. It's times like this that invigorate and energize us," Alison Mah, the Star's politics editor told me this week.
As public editor, I'm predicting this will be a great year for the Star and Canadian media in general, driven by major events here and abroad and by heightened engagement with the public.
As we know, the torrent of news began mid-December with Chrystia Freeland stepping down from cabinet in her role as finance minister and deputy prime minister - the most senior woman in the federal government - after her boss, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, decided to reassign her to another cabinet position.
Her move was a jaw dropper that stirred a resounding revolt in the Liberal caucus, an uprising that paved the way to Trudeau's decision this week to step down.
He prorogued Parliament until late March and in the meantime the Liberals will vote on a new leader who'll be road ready for a federal election in the spring.
But that's a long way away. Meanwhile, into the fray jumped U.S. president-elect Donald Trump with his talk about the benefits of Canada becoming America's 51st state and annexing Canada by "economic force."
Trump, the Smasher-in-Chief, delights in ruling by disruption - think of his former pledge to institute a Muslim ban, promises to build a wall in the U.S. to keep out Mexicans, his refusal to accept Joe Biden's election win, Trump's subsequent incitement of the Jan. 6 Capitol Hill riot and now his latest talk about annexing Greenland, the Panama Canal and Canada, and think of the anxiety and turmoil all of this has caused.
He has seized the world's attention yet again.
The war of words between Canada and the U.S. is a fast-moving story, complete with talk of 25 per cent trade tariffs from the U.S., retaliatory moves by Canada and Trump's complaints about how much the U.S. spends militarily to protect Canada.
Central to all of this is what it could all mean for the future of this country.
The ongoing Canada-U.S. tensions may very well lead us to some existential questions about what being Canadian means in terms of our collective identity, sovereignty, culture, history and economic independence.
The last time we had to think this deeply about what it means to be Canadian was during the Quebec Referendum in 1995. I was a journalist with the Star then and I remember well the tension in the newsroom, in the country, as the future of Canada hung in the balance.
A strong wave of support for Quebec sovereignty and independence coursed through that province at the time. Would Quebecers really break away and go off on their own?
For many Canadians the thought of this scenario was terrifying. The world watched and waited.
In the end, after a provincial referendum on Oct. 30, 1995, Quebecers said "no" to secession by a narrow margin of 50.58 per cent compared to 49.42 who voted yes to leaving.
Canadians breathed a collective sigh of relief and, in the aftermath, support for sovereignty in Quebec died down.
Still, as a country we continue to be riven by tensions with our Indigenous brothers and sisters, while Western alienation - the feeling from folks in provinces out west that their wants and needs are being ignored by the rest of the country, particularly Ontario and Quebec - remains.
In short, we are a country that has vulnerabilities. Will we hold things together amid this talk of annexation, Trump assuming office Jan. 20 and the upcoming federal election?
I remain very hopeful, but it's a blank canvas. No one knows for sure how events will unfold from here.
All of this will prove compelling and challenging in the coming months for Canadian media. And Star readers will benefit from our coverage.
"I expect the next few months will be a bit of a blur," says Mah, the politics editor. "There's a lot up in the air right now. How do the Liberals pull themselves together and can they field a competitive opponent to square off against Pierre Poilievre? Does Trump follow through with his tariff threat? Does Doug Ford call a provincial election on top of all of this?"
"We really are entering new terrain, and our big challenge will be to make sense of it all for readers," Mah adds.
Exciting times ahead.
Donovan Vincent is the Star's public editor and is based
in Toronto. Reach him by email at publiced@thestar.ca
or follow him on X: @donovanvincent
CREDIT: Donovan Vincent OPINION Donovan Vincent is the Star's public editor and is based in Toronto. Reach him email at publiced@thestar.ca or follow him on X: @donovanvincent;

Subject: Journalism; Provincial elections; Referendums; Prime ministers; Sovereignty

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Title: I'm excited about the year ahead for journalism

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.12

Publication year: 2025

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: Opinions, Commentary

ProQuest document ID: 3153453619

Document 166 of 500

Trump's threats of 'economic force' more than bluster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trumps-threats-economic-force-more-than-bluster/docview/3153453545/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+threats+of+%27economic+force%27+more+than+bluster&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's quite a week when the resignation of the prime minister isn't the most important thing in Canada's political life.
I say this because Justin Trudeau's impending departure and the machinations inside the Liberal party about who will replace him aren't as important as they might seem. And Donald Trump's jibes about Canada becoming the 51st state are more important than they appear to many people.
On the Liberals: Trudeau's announcement that he'll step down was completely predictable. Everyone knew he had to go - aside from him, it seems, until the last minute.
Now the Liberals will spend weeks finding someone to lead them, and I'll follow the twists and turns because that's what people like me do. But let's face it: it doesn't really matter.
Here's what's going to happen, almost certainly (the Guild of Columnists requires me to include at least that much of a caveat). The Liberals will pick a new leader by early March. The government will fall by the end of the month, early April tops. An election will follow, likely in May. The Liberals will be creamed. And the new leader will be discarded by the party well before voters sour on Prime Minister Pierre Poilievre toward the end of this decade.
So (almost certainly) the person the Liberals will crown around the first week of March will be the proverbial asterisk, the Kim Campbell or Stéphane Dion of our day. In other words, it hardly matters who wins that dubious honour. If a miracle occurs and the Liberals do find a saviour, I'll have egg on my face. But it sure doesn't look that way now.
What does matter this week? Trump's verbal assault on Canada, including his digs about making this country the 51st state.
There's a school of thought that says we shouldn't take any of it seriously, that Trump is just "trolling" us and "making liberals' heads explode." After all, we're told, he did rule out using military force against Canada; that's reassuring, right? I'm reading a lot along those lines in the National Post.
Of course, Canada isn't literally going to become the 51st state. Not even if we wanted to, which we clearly don't (a Leger poll last month found only 13 per cent of Canadians like the idea).
But if Trump doesn't change his tune in the next 10 days, it will be the policy of the president of the United States that Canada should be annexed - if only by "economic force." I don't believe that's been the case in more than two centuries, since the War of 1812-14.
In the short run, that will mean tariffs and other measures that will damage our economy. Trump was openly disdainful of this country and its importance to the U.S. ("we don't need their cars ... we don't need their lumber"). It's clear now that whatever we do on the border won't placate the incoming administration; we're going to be punished just for being who we are.
That's bad enough, but the longer-term implications are worse. Canada has spent decades trying to get closer to the U.S., to tie ourselves ever tighter into their economy precisely to avoid being left on the outside. It started long before the free trade agreements of the late '80s/early '90s. The Auto Pact of the 1960s secured Ontario's manufacturing base for generations to come.
We never stopped. I've been looking at photos of the Gordie Howe International Bridge linking Windsor and Detroit, which is set to open in September. It's a gorgeous structure and Canada paid the entire bill of $6.4 billion to construct a brand-new physical connection that will be (or was planned to be) the biggest portal for Canada-U.S. trade ever.
Trump will be gone in four years, but he will leave behind a huge question mark over the reliability of Canada's integration with the U.S. Investors will wonder whether locating in Canada really does guarantee access to the American market. Who knows how much that will cost us in the future? I fear it will be a lot.
We're only starting to take on board the implications of Trump's threats. When we look back on this week, they will stand out as the most consequential development.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca;

Document 167 of 500

Metrolinx to study rail bypass to expand GO service

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/metrolinx-study-rail-bypass-expand-go-service/docview/3153453528/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Metrolinx+to+study+rail+bypass+to+expand+GO+service&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has asked the provincial transit agency to study the possibility of building a bypass for freight trains between Brampton and Milton that would allow more frequent GO passenger service in the area.
The proposed bypass "has the potential to unlock new GO lines, stations and transit-oriented communities, supporting the province's plan to build more homes near transit," Ford wrote in a letter Thursday to the Metrolinx board.
The bypass, called the "Missing Link," would run between the Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CPKC) main line near Trafalgar Road in Milton to the Canadian National (CN) bypass line at Bramalea, adjacent to Highway 407. Currently, Bramalea and Milton's GO stations are only connected by bus, with trips ranging from an hour and 20 minutes to two hours.
Ford's letter, posted to X on Thursday, directs Metrolinx to explore the possibility of building the new freight rail bypass with the two railways and the federal government.
The province has also ordered a preliminary analysis of the benefits the proposed connections would bring, including possible increased service along the Kitchener and Milton corridors.
In 2015, the city of Mississauga looked into the Missing Link between Milton and Bramalea, as two-way, all-day GO service was made impossible due to freight rail traffic.
According to a feasibility study on the Missing Link plan created by Mississauga in 2015, the estimated cost of the project would be about $5.3 billion.
The proposed bypass is part of what the premier refers to as "GO 2.0," part of a plan to extend transit across the Golden Horseshoe.
"By leveraging existing freight rail infrastructure, GO 2.0 could help alleviate congestion and reduce travel times for passengers," according to the letter to Metrolinx.
The announcement is part of the Ford government's multibillion-dollar plan to expand public transit and highways over the next decade, including the promise of providing two-way, all-day GO service.
That plan also includes the Ontario Line, a Yonge North subway extension, a three-stop Scarborough subway extension and an underground extension of the Eglinton Crosstown to Pearson Airport.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Document 168 of 500

Ford emerges as Canada's top defence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-emerges-as-canadas-top-defence/docview/3153453414/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+emerges+as+Canada%27s+top+defence&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a single post on his X platform, Elon Musk made the power vacuum in Ottawa official around the world. And he didn't stand on ceremony: "Girl, you're not the governor of Canada anymore, so doesn't matter what you say."
He was responding to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's post asserting that "There isn't a snowball's chance in hell that Canada would become part of the United States."
Even before the resignation of our finance minister followed by our prime minister, Ottawa wasn't walking the talk on Team Canada. And meetings in Florida have so far yielded very few reassuring signs that Donald Trump's promised tariffs won't be as severe as feared.
In the absence of a strong response from Ottawa, and with the distraction of a leadership race to choose another temporary prime minister, it's Premier Doug Ford - and to a lesser degree Alberta Premier Danielle Smith - who have emerged as Canada's most effective advocates.
They show up where it matters most: on Fox News. It's where not only U.S. lawmakers and business leaders get what is offered as news, but also where Americans who are tickled by the idea of annexing Canada are told it's a real possibility.
Ford is visibly at ease on Fox News. He rolled with the punches this week when host Jesse Watters tried to get a rise out of him with the faux outrage typical of the network by framing Canadians' refusal to be annexed as offensive: "I would consider it a privilege to be taken over by the United States of America. That's what everybody else in the world wants," adding that "for some reason, that's repellent to you Canadians. And I find that personally offensive, Premier."
Ford kept his cool in the face of Watters' sensationalism, repeating his message to a broader audience. He did so as the premier of Canada's largest province, but also the way any Canadian entrepreneur trading with the U.S. would - in a constructive, businesslike manner.
Perhaps inspired by his slogan that Ontario is "Open for Business," he comes across as a straight-shooter who calls a spade a spade: "I get it. President-elect Trump is a real estate tycoon. He's made billions. But that property is not for sale. It's as simple as that."
Instead, Ford speaks of a win-win Am-Can partnership between two neighbours - a message that Canada should forcefully and factually communicate directly to all Americans.
The man who once sold sticky labels to Americans from his family's Chicago office is emerging as Canada's strongest advocate for integrated commerce that benefits both countries, without erasing the border. He should be readily accessible to every booker and segment producer in the U.S. media.
It's also time for Canada to use paid advertising to speak directly to those who influence state governors and legislators in Washington: American voters. They should know that when Trump claims the U.S. doesn't need anything from Canada, they actually get 60 per cent of their energy imports from us.
Ontario's multimillion-dollar U.S. advertising campaign against tariffs should be quickly revisited. While the intention is right, its message is wrong: "For generations, this ally to the north has been here - and for generations more we'll still be here, right by your side."
That soft approach was crafted before the tariff-related Mar-a-Lago dinner joke morphed into talks about annexation. One of the dinner guests who initially laughed it off, now Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, says the joke is over. Instead of this Hallmark card, the ads should feature Ford speaking directly to Americans.
As we learned from Jimmy Carter, Americans see good, decent guys as weak. They like no-nonsense men who are willing to fight back.
Ford should be open to it since he hasn't been shy about using government advertising to remind us of everything that's "Happening Here." Only this time, he could appear in ads running in other jurisdictions without contravening the rules against partisan government advertising.
Éric Blais is president of
Headspace Marketing Inc.
CREDIT: Éric Blais Contributor Éric Blais is president of Headspace Marketing Inc.;

Subject: Political campaigns; Advertising; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising Tariffs

Document 169 of 500

Contest to replace Trudeau looking like three-way race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/contest-replace-trudeau-looking-like-three-way/docview/3153452973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Contest+to+replace+Trudeau+looking+like+three-way+race&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal Liberal leadership race is gearing up to be a contest between formers: former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney, former finance minister Chrystia Freeland and former B.C. premier Christy Clark as the dark horse.
There are new names added to the list of potential Liberal leadership candidates each day, but these are the big three - those who have a real shot of not just winning the leadership but being elected prime minister, this time or next.
On Wednesday, after a lengthy caucus meeting, a picture emerged of a group of MPs, notably from Ontario, deeply saddened to learn that longtime New Brunswick MP and current Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc planned to bow out of the contest. On the sidelines of that meeting, MP after MP came by to urge LeBlanc to reconsider his decision to compete for the Liberal leadership. According to MPs who spoke with him, LeBlanc appeared touched and surprise by the show of affection, not realizing how deep support for his potential candidacy was outside of Atlantic Canada.
But with LeBlanc ruling himself out of the leadership race to focus on the greater threat - U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's tariff threat - MPs now must wrestle with the choices in front of them.
Not that caucus gets a say in who will be the next leader - that choice lies with the party membership. Right now, anyone 14 and over who lives in Canada, or any Canadians living abroad with an address in Canada, can sign up to be a member. (Though some in caucus have urged the party's national board to restrict the rule to permanent residents or citizens to address the foreign interference threat.)
Support from rank-and-file MPs might demonstrate a show of strength to the public for a certain candidate, an acknowledgment that that person has the confidence of their peers, but with an electorate thirsty for change, those endorsements might be less influential this time. Although MPs with their own networks, who are key organizers and fundraisers, who are willing to do the work, are very valuable to whomever can sign them up.
Freeland has been actively working the phones, meeting with MPs in small groups and trying to encourage her caucus mates to back her, hoping to outperform her challengers with endorsements when she announces her leadership.
Within caucus, however, there are still MPs deeply wounded by her betrayal of Trudeau - not her resignation from cabinet, but the death blow she delivered to the prime minister and the reputational harm she created by criticizing the very government she defended and spoke for during the past nine years.
Others, however, believe she will cut ties with unpopular policies in an effort to distinguish herself. They also point to her relations within caucus, her intellect, the fact she is incredibly hardworking and that she once took the fight to Trump during the NAFTA negotiations and can do it again. Trump's post on social media showing his dislike for Freeland - calling her behaviour "totally toxic" and posting on that "She will not be missed!!!" - is a feather in her cap. Compared to Carney, Freeland has experience in Parliament, in knocking on doors, in retail politics. But the big knock against her, even for those tempted to support her, is how can she be seen as "change"?
Carney is change, at least more so than Freeland, but he is untested. He's never run politically - and that's a big worry for many Liberal MPs. How will he fare against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's pugilistic tendencies? Will he flop in prime time? Is his French good enough to keep Quebec seats? What will his position on carbon pricing be? Will he be another Michael Ignatieff - an outside elite who can't connect to average Canadians?
Another concern for MPs is that Carney has not done much over the past few months to court their support or address their concerns. Many still haven't spoken to him - despite wanting to. Is he another Justin Trudeau, held up in his ivory tower?
Then there is Clark. While many Grit MPs have ruled her out - believing she is a Conservative in Liberal clothing, or that her French is inadequate - she has some backers in caucus. Clark's strategy, however, appears to be to sidestep caucus - and it may not be a terrible strategy. With national name recognition, Clark is one of the few outsiders who could mount a credible bid if the rules of the race give her the chance to sign up new members.
Those rules are what Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne cited this week when he was coy about whether he was in or out. How much will the entrance fee be? When will the membership cut-off be? How much time will the new leader have to write a throne speech and an election platform?
Those questions hide the key consideration keeping many potential candidates - Champagne, along with possibly Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, Transport Minister Anita Anand and a new generation of Liberal leaders within caucus - from jumping in. Will the next Liberal leader get one chance to become prime minister, or is this at least a two-election gamble? Is this next leader the one who will take the fall in the upcoming election, and then a new leader chosen to help the party do a proper rebuild in time for the 2028 election?
Many MPs believe the next leader gets one shot.
Ignatieff had one shot, but who could blame him from walking away after he lost his own seat in 2011 and brought the party to its worst showing ever. Stéphane Dion was pushed out in 2008 after a disastrous video address to the nation and a failed effort to form a coalition government against newly re-elected prime minister Stephen Harper. Paul Martin won in 2004 but walked away after losing the election in 2006. Not since John Turner has the leader lost a contest and competed again. He lost both the 1984 and 1988 elections.
But since no one expects the Liberals to win the next election, perhaps betting the next leader gets two tries seems wise.
CAPTION: The Liberal leadership race is so far led by former Bank of Canada chief Mark Carney, former finance minister Chrystia Freeland and former B.C. premier Christy Clark, Althia Raj writes.The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia Raj OPINION

Location: Canada

People: Carney, Mark; Clark, Christy; Freeland, Chrystia

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Canada; NAICS: 521110

Title: Contest to replace Trudeau looking like three-way race

White knight, dark horse or former loyalist? The fight to replace Justin Trudeau is shaping up to be a three-way race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/white-knight-dark-horse-former-loyalist-fight/docview/3153926371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=White+knight%2C+dark+horse+or+former+loyalist%3F+The+fight+to+replace+Justin+Trudeau+is+shaping+up+to+be+a+three-way+race&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Subject: Leadership; Endorsements; Prime ministers; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 171 of 500

How the Liberals' leadership race rules could favour some candidates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-liberals-leadership-race-rules-could-favour/docview/3153926297/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+the+Liberals%27+leadership+race+rules+could+favour+some+candidates&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - It's an arcane process involving people you've almost certainly never heard of. But what they decide in the coming hours and days could help determine who becomes Canada's next prime minister, and leads the embattled Liberal party into the next federal election.
On Thursday evening, top officials of the Liberal Party of Canada were set to hold a board meeting to hammer out the broad shape of the contest to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Since he announced his intention to resign, Trudeau's party has scrambled to figure out how to stage a solid competition in extraordinary circumstances. Opposition parties are clamouring to topple the Liberal minority government as soon as possible after Parliament returns March 24 from the forced shutdown Trudeau triggered when he decided to step down on Monday.
That means the Liberals are racing against the clock to find a new leader before an election, while governing a country that is weathering threats of economic damage from an incoming American president who keeps suggesting he wants to make Canada part of the United States.
How the party sets the rules for the leadership race will have a huge impact on how the contest plays out - and who might have an advantage in the sprint to the finish line, Liberal strategists say.
"The outcome of the game is determined by the rules," said Andrew Steele, a vice-president at the lobbying and consulting firm StrategyCorp who has held senior positions in campaign war rooms for the federal and Ontario Liberals.
Barriers to entry
One major consideration for the party is where to set the entry fee. When Trudeau was elected leader in 2013, candidates had to pay a total of $75,000 to enter the race. Some federal leadership races have set higher costs of entry; in the 2022 Conservative race that Pierre Poilievre won, it cost $300,000 to become a candidate.
Generally speaking, a higher fee weeds out candidates who aren't willing to risk incurring deep campaign debts, or who don't have the organizational heft to raise money in a constrained period of time, said Greg MacEachern, a Liberal strategist and founder of the consulting firm KAN Strategies.
"By setting a significant fee, one of the arguments for that would be ensuring people are actually very serious about seeking this," MacEachern said.
"If you are prepared to have that fee, it definitely does send a message about your ability to organize, and the interest in your campaign."
Ashley Csanady, a vice-president with the public affairs firm McMillan Vantage, said putting up a large deposit to enter the race is going to be much easier for some candidates than for others.
Csanady, who is a federal Liberal, said the question for the party is going to be whether it wants a broad, open race with many candidates, or a more narrow effort with just a few big names.
"A higher number will mean a smaller race, a lower number will mean a bigger race," she said. "And I guess the question is whether the party wants to weigh in and put a thumb on the scale either way."
Csanady said the Liberals also have to consider the impact a leadership race will have on the party's finances as it faces a near-certain spring election campaign immediately after. "It's not cheap to put on even a daylong leadership convention announcement. These things are expensive."
She added that there will also be plenty of administrative expenses for the party and "you need to more than recoup those right now, because we do need to have a war chest for whomever the winner is."
In 2022, when the federal Conservatives last held a leadership race, that party's financial statements listed $5.6 million in convention expenses, along with $5.4 million in revenues from the leadership contest.
Race for new members
In a shortened leadership contest, another key question is how long the party will allow candidates to sign up new Liberal "supporters" - the party did away with paid memberships after Trudeau became leader - who will be eligible to vote for Trudeau's replacement.
According to the party constitution, the cut-off to register new Liberals for a leadership contest is at least 41 days before the vote. If the party wants a new leader installed in time to prepare for the return of Parliament on March 24, that gives just weeks for candidates to sign up new supporters.
The compressed time frame could impact who has an advantage in the race, said Steele, arguing that the more the party consists of supporters who signed up under Trudeau's leadership, the more likely it is that they would vote for a "continuity candidate" with backers already inside the Liberal club.
And with a shorter window to bring in new supporters, any candidates that are "already organizing" could have a leg up, Steele said.
This week, former British Columbia premier Christy Clark held a meeting with more than 130 organizers, a source close to her told the Star. Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney and former finance minister Chrystia Freeland have also been calling around to caucus members, the Star has reported, as they consider whether to jump in the race.
For Csanady, "a shorter cut off favours Chrystia Freeland probably the most of everybody who is rumoured to be in the race."
Vandana Kattar, a Liberal strategist who worked in Trudeau's office for more than five years, said a truncated period to bring in new supporters will make the race largely a test of which candidates already have strong relationships. That includes with Liberal organizers and MPs with networks of political influence in different areas of the country.
"Anyone who wants this ... should have been building relationships across the country a while ago," Kattar said.
Interference concerns
Before Trudeau's selection as leader, the Liberals adopted new rules effectively opening up involvement in the party to anyone. The party charges no membership fee and anyone over the age of 14 can sign up, with no requirement the person be a Canadian citizen or even permanent resident.
Justice Marie-Josée Hogue, who is looking into foreign interference in Canadian politics, raised concerns about loose rules for Liberal party nominations in her preliminary report, with her final report expected this month.
"The eligibility criteria for voting in nomination contests do not seem very stringent, and the control measures in place do not seem very robust," she wrote.
For Steele, a higher entry fee and requirement for signatures would help limit the number of candidates and address concerns that the race could be impacted by outside actors or foreign interference.
"A signature and money gate helps to limit the field, and helps make that less likely," he said.
Csanady said opening up the membership in 2013 was done with the right intentions, but 2025 is not 2013 and the party has to be wary of threats from China, India, Russia and Iran among other countries that would seek to interfere.
"If we want Canadians to take us seriously as a party, we have to show that we take this foreign interference issue seriously."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks in Richmond Hill, Ont. on Jan. 8, 2025.

Misleading information, catchy slogans and a disregard for the truth

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/misleading-information-catchy-slogans-disregard/docview/3152880630/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Misleading+information%2C+catchy+slogans+and+a+disregard+for+the+truth&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The power to reduce global warming is on your dinner plate, Jan. 6
As I read Jessica Scott-Reid's account of the meat industry's attempt to rebrand itself as "regenerative agriculture," I was reminded of the similarities between today's factory farming and the cigarette industry's past - misleading information, catchy slogans, a disregard for the truth, and putting profits above people's health. Scott-Reid's discussion of the meat industry's manipulative marketing also reminds me of Donald Trump's successful use of the big lie (and the many, many little lies) and misinformation. Here are a few facts I consider when shopping for groceries: the average cow also produces as much carbon dioxide per day as a normal car releases for every 250 kilometres it is driven; consumption of red meat has been linked with increased incidences of heart disease, Type 2 diabetes, and deaths due to colorectal cancer; it takes more than 2,400 gallons of water to produce one pound of beef, a pound of tofu, by contrast, requires 244 gallons of water.
Douglas Baldwin, Toronto
Proof that supports my beliefsThe power to reduce global warming is on your dinner plate, Jan. 6
I have been trying to go vegan for several years and have now progressed to avoiding red meat and pork. Invariably, when I remind friends about this dietary choice when asked out for dinner, I mention something about reducing the risk of getting cancer, saving the forests and the oceans, and more importantly, the mistreatment of animals. Thank you, Jessica Scott-Reid, for providing me with a better foundation to support my beliefs.
Patricia Kessler, Toronto
Canadian patriotism
There was never any doubt that Prime Ministers Brian Mulroney, Jean Chrétien, Paul Martin, Steven Harper or Justin Trudeau were anything other than staunch Canadian patriots. Likewise, Andrew Scheer and Erin O'Toole, former leaders of the Conservative party and Jagmeet Singh of the NDP have made their affection for Canada very clear. On the other hand, when I hear Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre trash-talking Canada, repeatedly, I feel his primary loyalty is to the Conservative party - not our country. Poilievre will get no votes from my household.
Peter Bursztyn, Barrie
Stick to the facts when attacking Trudeau's recordSay what you want about Justin Trudeau - there's still no arguing Canadians became wealthier while he was in power, Jan. 6
I would urge David Olive to send his excellent, factual accounting of how Canadians fared financially under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership to Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre and NDP leader Jagmeet Singh so they can be reminded of the facts when they attack Trudeau's economic record.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
Ford's 'massive majority' another reason for electoral reformWhen Doug Ford looks at Donald Trump, he sees an opportunity, Jan. 4
Martin Regg Cohn is correct in stating that Ontario does not need a provincial election at this time of so much uncertainty. Premier Doug Ford's majority government can abide by the law and govern safely until June 2026. Changing the law for the party's own purposes would simply invite additional cynicism about the motives of the political class as a whole, something we certainly do not need. Cohn, by putting words into the mouth of Ford, makes theremark that Ford has, "a massive majority mandate." While this may well reflect the attitude of Ford, the fact is in the last election 60 per cent of voters in Ontario cast their ballots for other political parties. The Conservatives hold a majority of seats in the Legislature only because of a faulty electoral system. Our democratic system can, and must, be improved so that all votes have equal value.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did not engage in tiresome insults
Conservative, right-wing politicians - Donald Trump, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford - seem to thrive on insults, slogans and jabs at anyone who doesn't adopt their ideology. These insults are tiresome, unproductive and juvenile. Calling someone "elite," "weak," "expensive," or "wacko" is meaningless and does nothing to further government business or world diplomacy. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did not engage in this type of language and was respected on the world stage by many diverse heads of state and for his civility.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Thanks, but no thanks to joining America, President Trump!
First of all, a reminder to Canadians that the first Donald Trump presidency promised a wall between the U.S. and Mexico and that Mexico would pay for it. We heard that claim for months. Lots of wall was built, but not completed, and the U.S. taxpayer paid for it out of the defence budget. Trump also placed tariffs on imports from China. Once again the American taxpayer paid for it with massive government subsidy cheques to farmers after China stopped buying U.S. farm products. Bullies know how to deal with bullies, so we will be fine with the Pierre Poilievre/Doug Ford combination. An easy win would be to come out now and say we will hit our two per cent NATO responsibility by year end 2025 (and do it). This NATO commitment should have been dealt with years ago, both former Prime Minister Stephen Harper and Trudeau dropped the ball. Canadians for the most part have always liked our U.S. neighbours, but why would any Canadian want U.S. health care, education, racism and gun violence? With lower taxes you get what you pay for. No thanks to their Walmart society.
Mike Walker, Washago, ON
G7 may wish to reconsider Canada hosting summer summit
Canada is scheduled to host the G7 world leaders at a summit set for June 15-17 in Alberta. And while this country is supposed to take over the presidency of the group, Canada's laws pose a problem. President-elect Donald Trump is a convicted felon and Canada's law prohibit the entry of convicted felons into the country. Ways around this law include claiming criminal rehabilitation. However, it only applies to people who have completed all elements of their sentence over five years before. The incoming President doesn't qualify under this rule. Other convicted felons could also apply for a temporary resident permit. I don't think Trump would consider such a move nor would Canada consider such an application. Perhaps a "waiver" may be in order, but then he would have to show evidence of a good character. I think the best solution would be to allow some other country to host the event. I don't think the incoming President is assured of being allowed into the country.
Ken Moore, Stirling
A simple end of holiday season tradition
When we pack away the holiday decorations each year, we insert a current newspaper from the day we pack it away in the box with the tree. Packing away holiday decorations is also collecting memories of the past festive season and previous year and saving them in our hearts. Eleven months from now we will open the decoration box and reflect on where our lives have gone in the past year, what was happening then and what is happening now. Rediscovering the year-old newspaper in the box is a great way to begin the next holiday season with reflection on what has transpired during the year since. Try it this year, it's a simple yet meaningful experience!
Trucilla Macedo, Thornhill
CAPTION: "No matter how industry spins it, animal agriculture is still a leading cause of climate change, emitting 14-20 per cent of global greenhouse gasses, and contributing extensively to deforestation, ocean degradation, water pollution, and biodiversity loss," writes Jessica Scott-Reid.

Full text: Catchy slogans disregard the truth about meat
The power to reduce global warming is on your dinner plate, Opinion, Jan. 6
As I read Jessica Scott-Reid's account of the meat industry's attempt to rebrand itself as "regenerative agriculture," I was reminded of the similarities between today's factory farming and the cigarette industry's past - misleading information, catchy slogans, a disregard for the truth, and putting profits above people's health. Scott-Reid's discussion of the meat industry's manipulative marketing also reminds me of Donald Trump's successful use of the big lie (and the many, many little lies) and misinformation. Here are a few facts I consider when shopping for groceries: the average cow also produces as much carbon dioxide per day as a normal car releases every 250 kilometres it is driven; consumption of red meat has been linked with increased incidences of heart disease, Type 2 diabetes and deaths due to colorectal cancer; it takes more than 2,400 gallons of water to produce one pound of beef, a pound of tofu, by contrast, requires 244 gallons of water.
Douglas Baldwin, Toronto
I have been trying to go vegan for several years and have now progressed to avoiding red meat and pork. Invariably, when I remind friends about this dietary choice when asked out for dinner, I mention something about reducing the risk of getting cancer, saving the forests and the oceans and, more importantly, the mistreatment of animals. No deal without review, say N.L. Tories: Party leader unsure Hydro-Québec agreement comes at best price possible

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-deal-without-review-say-n-l-tories/docview/3152878812/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+deal+without+review%2C+say+N.L.+Tories%3A+Party+leader+unsure+Hydro-Qu%26eacute%3Bbec+agreement+comes+at+best+price+possible&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Newfoundland and Labrador's Opposition Progressive Conservatives vowed Wednesday that they will not vote on a draft energy deal with Quebec's hydro utility until it is scrutinized by independent experts.
Progressive Conservative Leader Tony Wakeham asked that a vote set for Thursday be suspended until there is an arm's-length review of the tentative contract. He made the request in the provincial legislature on the third day of debate on the memorandum of understanding signed last month between the two provinces.
"We won't vote on a resolution until this independent review takes place," Wakeham told reporters.
The tentative agreement would see Hydro-Québec pay far more than it currently does for power from the Churchill Falls hydroelectric plant and develop more hydroelectric projects with Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro. Wakeham said Wednesday that with the information released so far, it's impossible to know whether the contract is the best possible deal for the province.
Government officials have said independent firms were contracted to provide advice during negotiations. Representatives from two such companies - Power Advisory and JP Morgan - were called to appear before the legislature Wednesday.
"I think it is a very good deal," said Jason Chee-Aloy, managing director with Power Advisory. His Toronto-based firm was hired by Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro in 2023 to assess the market for Labrador energy.
He called the memorandum of understanding "a necessary step" before negotiation of a final agreement, expected in 2026. "So we'll know if it is absolutely the best deal - I'm sure it will be - with the (final) agreements," he said.
Premier Andrew Furey has promised to assemble an independent expert panel to provide advice as Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro negotiates the final details with Hydro-Québec. Chee-Aloy agreed about the need for independent oversight, and said he felt the panel was the right move.
The stakes for Newfoundland and Labrador are particularly high given its bruising history with hydroelectric projects.
Thanks to a contract signed in 1969, Hydro-Québec has been buying the lion's share of the power from the Churchill Falls plant at bargain-basement prices, and then selling it for a steep profit. The deal unveiled on Dec. 12 would end that arrangement about 16 years early and "right a historic wrong," according to Furey.
Under the new agreement, Hydro-Québec would pay about 30 times more for Churchill Falls power, netting the provincial treasury about $17 billion until 2041 - an average of about $1 billion a year. By 2056, the annual average revenue is forecast to hit $4 billion.
Under the current contract, the province receives about $20 million a year.
Unlike the 1969 deal, the new rates paid by Hydro-Québec would be tied to the energy market, meaning Quebec's payments could rise if energy prices go up. Chee-Aloy said he and his firm helped shape the framework to determine those prices.
Now is the right time for the provinces to strike a deal, he added, noting Quebec is hungry for energy and the Newfoundland and Labrador government has been pushing to end the lopsided 1969 contract for decades - even fighting it unsuccessfully in the Supreme Court of Canada.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Sarah Smellie The Canadian Press

Document 176 of 500

Singh lost progressive, working-class support

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-lost-progressive-working-class-support/docview/3152878735/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+lost+progressive%2C+working-class+support&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Immediately after Justin Trudeau announced he was resigning as prime minister, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh sent out a spicy statement detailing all the ways Liberals have failed Canadians.
"They let you down on health care. They let you down by allowing corporate greed to run wild. Even with the country facing another serious blow to Canadian jobs and our cost of living - this time from Donald Trump - they're still focused only on themselves and their political fate," Singh said.
Sure, Singh's tone was assertive. But he didn't make a case for the NDP's vision so much as he positioned it as the only option for progressives. Singh should have been ready for this moment. He's had years to rally people unhappy with housing, health care, and the high cost of living in many Canadian cities behind a cohesive, populist economic message. But despite some viral social media moments and policy wins, he has ultimately failed to mobilize a leftist movement in the country.
Singh and the NDP deserve credit for some things they advocated for with the supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals. They pushed to implement national dental care plan and pharmacare plans, and got 10 days of sick leave and anti-scab protections for federal workers. Yet, the Liberals have largely taken the credit for these wins. Meanwhile, the NDP is still trailing the Liberals in the polls and is firmly in third place. Because Singh has so far refused to vote non-confidence in the government, allowing Trudeau to resign, he will not be facing a deeply unpopular incumbent in the upcoming election, but a new Liberal leader, thereby reducing his chances of being able to form the official opposition.
Singh took over the party in 2017. A proud Sikh man who didn't shy away from talking about systemic racism but also had a knack for social media, Singh was refreshing and optimistic. When I interviewed him in October 2017, he joked that he was "younger and hipper" than Trudeau.
But quips and being followed by Rihanna on Instagram can only take you so far. Singh could have pushed a bolder agenda, calling for things like universal basic income and pressuring the government to build more affordable rental housing quickly, by either leveraging the supply and confidence agreement or ripping it up sooner. His plan, if elected, would be to build 500,000 affordable housing units, but he's also pushed for centrist positions such as subsidizing people's mortgages and lowering interest rates.
Filling the vacuum, the Conservatives have now positioned themselves as the voice for Canada's working class and are poised to form government in the next election.
"I think the moment really asked for a more populist opportunity, a more grassroots opportunity and a long-term project of changing minds," author and political commentator David Moscrop told me.
"A lot of people look at the party and ... they lump the Liberals and the NDP more or less together, and that gives you effectively no space in the spectrum."
Moscrop noted that Canada had a federal social housing program until the 1980s, but said Singh and the NDP avoided going "whole hog" on it "because they've run away from socialism, thinking it's a scary word."
Even in America, which worships at the altar of capitalism, Bernie Sanders found major traction by running on socialized health care and housing-for-all. But Singh has failed to galvanize or inspire leftist Canadians in the same way.
After U.S. Vice-President Kamala Harris decisively lost to Donald Trump, many Democrats said the party had gone too "woke" and it cost them the election.
Now that Trudeau is on his way out, Liberal insiders have been saying the same.
But the popularity of Sanders in the U.S., and even that of former Labour Party leader Jeremy Corbyn's in the United Kingdom, suggest that people are hungry for bold, progressive change.
Jagmeet Singh hasn't been able to meet the moment. The NDP needs a leader who can.
Manisha Krishnan is an Emmy award-winning journalist who covers drug policy, and a former Toronto Star reporter.
CREDIT: Manisha Krishnan Toronto Star Manisha Krishnan is an Emmy award-winning journalist who covers drug policy, and a former Toronto Star reporter.;

Document 177 of 500

Could a congestion charge work here?: Experts say toll system can reduce gridlock if city can offer viable transportation alternatives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/could-congestion-charge-work-here/docview/3152878720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Could+a+congestion+charge+work+here%3F%3A+Experts+say+toll+system+can+reduce+gridlock+if+city+can+offer+viable+transportation+alternatives&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A congestion charge, a long-debated idea in North America, has finally come into effect in New York City and is being praised by experts who say Toronto could benefit from a similar approach.
On Sunday, New York was the first U.S. city to adopt the system after years of studies and delays, joining the likes of London, Stockholm and Singapore which have seen lasting traffic reductions since implementing congestion charges.
The toll system, which is applied to the most congested parts of a city, is meant to reduce gridlock and pollution and raise revenue for public transit.
Toronto council once endorsed tolls on the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway in 2016 to help improve gridlock in a city that has some of the worst traffic in the world. But the plan was scuttled by the Liberal government at the time. In the past September, the city's transportation department once again said a congestion charge on motorists is not an option on the table.
Despite the Ontario government's efforts to double down on car mobility, Canadian transportation experts say a congestion charge in Toronto could be a more efficient way to alleviate traffic jams.
"I do think Toronto is at the point where the congestion is so bad. And, the drag on our economy and our quality of life is getting to the point where this does have to be on the table, and has to be studied very carefully," said Matti Siemiatycki, a geography and planning professor at the University of Toronto.
During peak traffic hours - between 5 a.m. and 9 p.m. on weekdays and on weekends between 9 a.m. and 9 p.m. - drivers of cars, SUVs, small vans and pickup trucks with an E-ZPass need to pay $9 (U.S.) once per day to enter Manhattan south of Central Park. During off hours, the toll is $2.25 for most vehicles.
Drivers without an E-ZPass will receive bills by mail and pay more: $13.50 for peak hours and $3.30 overnight.
When charging a price on the road, based on the laws of supply and demand, people will drive less and opt for alternative ways to travel to the city, Siemiatycki said.
Congestion has a price tag too, even though it is often not thought of in this way: people either pay in money, or in time and lost in economic productivity, he added. The latter was estimated at $11 billion annually in the GTA by a study in 2013.
Siemiatycki pointed to Highway 407 as an example of how road tolls work in Toronto and said no matter how much people dislike the cost, it ensures people get across the city in "a reliable fashion."
"This New York experiment, or an example, is so important because it has the potential to show what's possible across North America," he said.
While Jeff Casello applauded the congestion charge in New York as "a fantastic policy," the urban planning professor at the University of Waterloo said he is concerned about implementing a similar charge in Toronto when there "isn't any viable alternatives" for transportation.
"Imagine that there was a massive park and ride where you could park your car in a park-and-ride facility at Pearson Airport, for example, and then take the UP Express downtown. That's what we ought to be encouraging," he said.
Siemiatycki, who echoed the sentiment, said if the system is implemented, the city needs to have equity at its core and make sure the public transportation is in place.
"The other point that often doesn't get talked about is, at the moment, it's almost virtually impossible to run an effective public transit system on those highways because they're so congested," he said.
The key to the congestion pricing system in New York and London is their ability to collect the revenue to improve public transit, while the tolls of Highway 407 are not used for that benefit, said Bruce Hellinga, a transportation engineering professor at the University of Waterloo.
Toronto's city council has considered the road levy as a potential revenue tool in to relieve the city's financial pressures. However, the city determined that road pricing policy involves high costs as it will require the city to partner with a technology company to implement the licence plate capture system.
Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson from the Ministry of Transportation, said in a statement that they will remain focused on building the critical infrastructure including allocating nearly $100 billion toward new roads and transit.
"Unlike the Liberals, we will never add a tax or toll to any road in Ontario," he said.
Siemiatycki also acknowledged that road pricing is an unpopular idea in suburban areas while taking off road tolls, expanding highway networks and cutting bike lanes have "an effective political wedge."
"We do have to acknowledge that the approach that they're following is popular. The problem is it hasn't been effective. I mean congestion has gotten worse during their six years in office," Siemiatycki said.
"It might be good policy, but it's not good politics," he said.
CAPTION: Drivers cross the Brooklyn Bridge into Manhattan this week after New York became the first U.S. city to adopt a congestion pricing system.Michael M. Santiago Getty Images
CREDIT: Estella Ren Toronto Star
