Document 178 of 500

As Trudeau steps down, Danielle Smith is having a moment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-trudeau-steps-down-danielle-smith-is-having/docview/3152878175/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+Trudeau+steps+down%2C+Danielle+Smith+is+having+a+moment&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Look out Canada, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is on a roll.
Her political nemesis, Justin Trudeau, is stepping down.
Her political ally, "scrap-the-tax" Pierre Poilievre, seems destined to become prime minister.
And "drill-baby-drill" Donald Trump will move back into the White House on Jan. 20.
Oh, Trump might be threatening to impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian goods, including oil and gas, shipped to the U.S., but Smith is using that to her advantage, too.
While she talks about a "Team Canada" approach she is busy trying to make herself into something of a national leader when it comes to dealing with Trump.
Smith has already made several trips to the U.S. to tout Alberta's importance as a trusted energy supplier and thus should not face tariffs. And she plans to travel south for a few days every month this year to states that include Texas, Louisiana and Oklahoma. That's after she attends Trump's inaugural celebrations in Washington, D.C., where she will also, according to her office, meet with energy groups, congresspeople, and various officials.
Smith is opposed to Trump's tariffs but not to the man himself.
Last month, she praised Trump's political "style" as someone who makes "bold decisions" and suggested his tariff threat should be taken seriously - and was the first premier to declare plans to help boost border security.
But she also thought he was being "hilarious" for suggesting Canada become the 51st state - a "joke" that has morphed into Trump now suggesting the U.S. use "economic force" to coerce Canada into a union.
Part of Smith's strategy is not simply to suggest maintaining the status quo when it comes to pumping oil and gas to the U.S. but to significantly increase the pumping.
On Monday afternoon, just a few hours after Trudeau said he'd be stepping down, Smith announced an ambitious plan to double Alberta's oil production.
I'm sure the timing of Smith's announcement was coincidental but, then again, in politics there are no coincidences, just conspiracies and schemes and machinations.
At the very least, though, there was an important, if inadvertent, symbolism to the timing.
Goodbye to Trudeau's forceful environmentalism. Hello to Smith's aggressive fossil-fuelism (for lack of a better term).
While Trudeau had tried to walk a tightrope between fighting climate change (via the federal "carbon tax") and protecting Alberta's energy industry (spending $34 billion on the Trans Mountain pipeline expansion), Smith performs no such balancing act.
Hers is a bulldozed path forward clearing the way for more oil and gas production while introducing policies that could hobble the province's wind and solar industry.
It's a path she hopes will be made easier with a Poilievre government and, with some gentle arm twisting, a Trump administration.
Smith is reliably controversial but she retains a firm grip on power, having won 91.5 per cent support from United Conservative Party members at their annual general meeting in November.
She has pandered to her socially conservative base, relentlessly blamed the federal Liberals for most if not all of Alberta's problems, and flooded the zone with so many announcements, policies and distractions that the media, critics and the general public simply can't keep up.
She has vacuumed up power from Alberta's municipal governments while complaining about the federal government intruding on provincial jurisdiction.
She is articulate and resourceful and has built a personal power base thanks to luck, bravado and populism.
When Trudeau announced he would not step down until after Liberals choose a new leader, Smith kept up the attack, declaring that not calling an immediate federal election was "one of the most irresponsible and selfish acts of a government in Canadian history."
Smith is not wrong to say Trudeau's failed strategy to cling to power has left Canada in a precarious position with a power vacuum in Ottawa as Trump is about to pull the trigger on tariffs.
It's an argument she can use as yet another springboard in her quest to become not just a powerful player on the provincial scene but a Conservative leader on the national stage.
CAPTION: Alberta premier Danielle Smith, left, talks with colleagues during the fall meetings of Canada's premiers hosted by Ontario in Toronto on Dec. 16.

Subject: Energy industry; Politics; Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Title: As Trudeau steps down, Danielle Smith is having a moment

Document 179 of 500

Something irked me as I wrote Justin Trudeau's biography. I now know that thing was his fatal flaw

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/something-irked-me-as-i-wrote-justin-trudeaus/docview/3152878170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Something+irked+me+as+I+wrote+Justin+Trudeau%27s+biography.+I+now+know+that+thing+was+his+fatal+flaw&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This time last year, when I was doing final edits on my book about Justin Trudeau, I asked my editors at Simon & Schuster if they thought that any passages in the book stuck out as unfair. After two years of researching and writing about Trudeau, I had started to find myself becoming irritated by him, and I was afraid that had crept into the text.
The wise and talented Rosemary Shipton, who has edited many books, replied by drawing my attention to one sentence "where you verge on being a touch unkind." I had written that perhaps Trudeau "sees politics largely as an opportunity for people to appreciate his princely magnificence."
In closely reviewing his nine years in office, and the rest of his life, I had started to think that he was often focused on the pleasure that others took in basking in his presence. I decided Shipton was right, though, that it was unkind, because there were other ways of looking at Trudeau, and we cut the line.
After all, he had revived a vision of progressive Canada when it looked as though Stephen Harper might have forged a permanent new conservative consensus. Once in office, he legalized marijuana, lifted tens of thousands of children out of poverty and made sustained efforts toward Indigenous reconciliation and the struggle to reduce emissions. He demonstrated a consistent commitment to his vision of the country. It seemed inaccurate to portray him as nothing but an egotistical nepo baby.
In recent months, though, as he clung to power after the point when it seemed clear he should let go, I became irritated again, and started to think that his love of the spotlight is his fatal flaw, part and parcel of the will to power that drove him to lead Canada.
That unusual compulsion seems to have been bred in the bone.
Trudeau had a strange and difficult childhood. He was born on Christmas Day, 1971, son of Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau and Margaret Sinclair, who was 29 years younger than her husband, and who was suffering from bipolar disorder, which was not diagnosed until many years later.
On March 3, 1977, on the night of her sixth wedding anniversary, when Justin was five, she caused a sensation when, instead of celebrating with her husband, she showed up at the El Mocambo in Toronto with Ron Wood and Mick Jagger, where the Rolling Stones were about to take the stage for two legendary nights. She followed them to New York, and then left her family for a jet set life, making headlines around the world with her romances - the most lurid news out of Canada until Rob Ford came along.
So Justin grew up with a largely absent mother, which traumatized him, and left him with a burning desire for attention, as he described in his memoir. As Jonathan Kay, who ghost-wrote that memoir, "Common Ground," wrote later in the Walrus:"What good is the glitz of being a prime minister's son when you're living a childhood parched of mother's milk?"
As a child and young man, people were drawn to Trudeau. As the first born son of a famous leader, he had hereditary charisma, and he was energetic, tall and handsome, with leadership skills and good manners. He could effortlessly capture attention anywhere he went; he reveled in it, and consistently sought more. It was, for instance, why he wore blackface on so many occasions that he could later not recall them all. He liked to dress up, be the centre of attention.
But he didn't truly apply himself to anything worthy of his gifts until 2012, when he sought the Liberal leadership, a sure way of being at the centre of the action. From that point on, though, he did the work. To win his party's leadership, to go from third place to a majority government, besting Stephen Harper and Tom Mulcair, he had to do a lot of difficult things well, showing an admirable work ethic, policy skills and, crucially, an ability to connect with voters.
For his first year in office, he was a hot commodity, a new thing that people wanted to know about. He was thronged in Asia, appeared in Vogue and Rolling Stone magazines, was a global progressive icon, which was good for him, his party and, in a way, the country.
That period ended, suddenly and permanently, when he took a disastrous trip to India, which was set up like a royal tour, not a business trip, and he so overdid the costume changes that the Indians started laughing at him.
Comedian John Oliver said then that Trudeau had "singed his fingertips on people going, 'You're not quite that charismatic to pull off a trip like that'."
Ever since, as a global media commodity, Trudeau has been damaged goods, but he has not stopped seeking the spotlight, tweeting at real celebrities - Trevor Noah and Taylor Swift - for example.
His media presence, which was once a source of pride even to Canadians who did not vote for him, became increasingly cringe, especially after the pandemic, the lockdowns, the convoy and a cost of living crisis. Inevitably, all of that strife made him a symbol of hard times and division for the growing number of Canadians.
There are things leaders can do when they become unpopular - pivots they can make - but Trudeau didn't have the cunning to change gears, to tell a new story. He just kept showing up in novelty socks, being a dashing progressive, hoping people would be pleased to see him - as almost everyone once had been. But the old magic has stopped working, and he looked like he was virtue signalling when he lectured Giorgia Meloni about gay rights. When he popped down to Mar-a-Lago, Trump bullied him.
And at home, no matter what he did, the polls would not move, because voters had just had enough of him, and the Conservatives had selected an anti-Trudeau figure who succeeded in rallying the people who had come to see Trudeau as a pious scold, an anti-racist who had often worn blackface, a climate change warrior who was forever flying around in a private plane, a feminist who gaslit, demoted and exiled Jody Wilson-Raybould when she rightly objected to the PMO interfering in the administration of justice.
Trudeau and his people were trying to sideline the prosecution of corrupt engineering firm SNC-Lavalin, and then shuffled Wilson-Raybould when she refused to go along with the plan. At the climax of the SNC-Lavalin affair, Trudeau and Wilson-Raybould had a tense meeting at the Vancouver International Airport, after which he told reporters "In our system of government, her presence in cabinet should actually speak for itself."
Trudeau appeared to believe that his charm would keep her in line, a miscalculation that brought his government to the brink of collapse when she promptly resigned.
It was much the same with Chrystia Freeland, whom he tried to demote from Finance when it ought to have been obvious that her continued presence at his right hand was vital to public confidence in his government.
In both cases, he seems to have thought that the force of his personality would carry the day. But it was in the best interests of both women to ditch him and when they did, Trudeau didn't have much to say in his own defence.
His government recovered from the SNC-Lavalin affair, but the second falling out was fatal. Trudeau, in the last days of his prime ministership, looks like a guy whose charm finally wore off.
It is too soon for the verdict of history - consider how Brian Mulroney was despised when he left office and how, after decades passed, people came to remember his many real accomplishments. Trudeau can look forward to that reassessment in the distant future, but at the moment, he looks like a guy who didn't know when to stop, which has put the country in peril.
The election of Donald Trump, with his tariff threats and annexation jokes, requires a firm response. Trudeau has not been able to provide that, and he is so unpopular that most Canadians would not support him if he offered it.
At a time of national crisis, Team Canada is without an effective coach. That is Trudeau's doing. It has been clear in the polls, and on the ground, for a long time that Canadians wanted a change. It would have been better for the country, the party and Trudeau, personally, if he had recognized that sooner instead of clinging to the vain hope that Canadians would warm to him again. It looks like he could not bear giving up the spotlight.
Now he must serve for three more months as an unpopular lame-duck prime minister while his would-be replacements awkwardly try to distance themselves from his legacy, Pierre Poilievre howls for a "carbon tax election" and Trump and other right-wingers torment him.
Trudeau alone is responsible for creating this situation, and if it goes badly, that will be part of his legacy.
The best hope, for him, his party and the country, is that the race to replace him produces a competent leader, so that Pierre Poilievre will not win without being properly scrutinized and tested. But the hurried race to replace Trudeau, if it is not conducted carefully, could be a disaster, open to the foreign interference that Trudeau failed to counter effectively while prime minister.
Watching his obvious misery as he announced his resignation, it was possible to feel human sympathy for him, but his stubbornness in the face of the facts is what sticks out. We have a lot of other things to worry about because Justin Trudeau stuck around after it was obvious that Canadians no longer wanted to follow him.
CAPTION: Prime Minster Justin Trudeau fields a question at a town hall meeting at McMaster University in 2018.
CREDIT: Stephen Maher, Special to the Star

Document 180 of 500

Ford pitches energy alliance with U.S.: Premier hopes partnership will help avert trade war

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pitches-energy-alliance-with-u-s/docview/3152877970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pitches+energy+alliance+with+U.S.%3A+Premier+hopes+partnership+will+help+avert+trade+war&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After warning Ontario would cut off electricity exports stateside in retaliation for any Donald Trump tariffs, Premier Doug Ford wants to better integrate energy systems with the U.S. to avert a trade war.
With the president-elect threatening 25 per cent levies on Canadian and Mexican goods later this month unless America's neighbours tighten their borders, Ford on Wednesday pitched "Fortress Am-Can" fuelled by domestic energy production.
"These tariffs would be devastating for both of our economies. They'd disrupt deeply integrated supply chains, create massive uncertainty for investors, raise costs for businesses and put countless jobs at risk on both sides of the border. It doesn't have to be this way," the premier said.
"Fortress Am-Can is a vision that respects each country as proudly independent, a vision that celebrates what we can achieve together: a true partnership where Canada and the U.S. align on shared priorities, achieving free, fair and balanced trade, growing our economies, accelerating resource development and protecting our borders and our communities," he said.
Speaking at the Darlington energy complex in Courtice, east of Toronto, Ford touted Ontario's existing nuclear fleet and its planned small modular reactors, urging greater co-operation with American lawmakers and industry.
His comments come four weeks after he warned the province would not sit idly by if Trump makes good on his threat to slap 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian products after his Jan. 20 inauguration.
"We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy - going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin," Ford said on Dec. 18, noting Ontario supplies about 1.5 million Americans with electricity.
While that gambit got Trump's attention, the president-elect has ramped up his rhetoric, deriding America's largest trading partner as "the great state of Canada" and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as a state "governor."
At a rambling 70-minute news conference at his Florida resort on Tuesday, he said he would use "economic force" to make Canada a "51st state."
"Canada and the United States, that would really be something," said Trump.
"You get rid of that artificially drawn line, and you take a look at what that looks like ... it would also be much better for national security," said the president-elect, who also mused about annexing Greenland, which is part of Denmark, as well as the Panama Canal, and renaming the Gulf of Mexico the "Gulf of America."
In response, Trudeau, who announced his resignation as prime minister and Liberal leader Monday, said "there isn't a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would ever join the U.S.
"Workers and communities in both our countries benefit from being each other's biggest trading and security partner," he said.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who polls suggest would win a federal election expected this year, echoed Trudeau, saying "Canada will never be the 51st state."
On Wednesday in Courtice, Ford reiterated that Trump's ramblings are not constructive.
"Our country is not for sale. It will never be for sale. I'm always concerned when a president the most powerful country in the world says these statements, but that's not going to happen," the premier said, insisting he has no federal political aspirations.
"My main focus is to protect the people of Ontario and as chair of the (Council of the) Federation, I have to make sure I represent all the premiers across the country from coast to coast to coast - and that's my job."
Beyond further integration of the electricity grid, Ford wants Canadian and American lawmakers to forge a working group with energy and security experts "to ensure both countries adhere to best practices for power system security against foreign interference, cyber attacks, terrorism and extreme weather, among other threats."
As well, he would like to "align regulations and eliminate red tape that slows building cross-border energy infrastructure, including for transmission lines, interties and pipelines."
But the premier emphasized that does not mean Ontario couldn't counter any Trump moves by withholding energy exports to the U.S.
"You turn up the heat on them," he said, signalling American consumers would pay a price for the new president's tariffs.
"Someone in Texas buys a pickup truck that's produced here in Oshawa, not far away from here, they typically would pay $60,000. Now, that same person in Texas is going to pay $75,000 if he puts tariffs on vehicles."
Later Wednesday, Ford met virtually with the other provincial and territorial leaders to discuss the Trump threat and next moves after the prime minister's departure.
"There's a lack of leadership right now in Ottawa," he told reporters at Queen's Park.
On Jan. 15, Trudeau will convene the premiers for an in-person first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on the tariff crisis.
A month later, on Feb. 12, Ford is leading a COF delegation to Washington to plead Canada's case to members of the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 181 of 500

Ottawa girds for battles against Trump: Officials say they're preparing serious responses to threats of annexation, tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-girds-battles-against-trump/docview/3152877932/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+girds+for+battles+against+Trump%3A+Officials+say+they%27re+preparing+serious+responses+to+threats+of+annexation%2C+tariffs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Joke's over.
The federal government signalled Wednesday that it took U.S. president-elect Donald Trump seriously when he spoke this week of using "economic force" to annex Canada, after previously downplaying as a joke his repeated taunts about making this country the "51st state."
A senior government official, who spoke to the Star on condition of anonymity, said the latest and most extreme talk of erasing Canada's sovereignty has galvanized the government. Ottawa is now pushing to soon release details of a potential response to Trump's threat of a trade war, if he follows through on his plan to impose punishing 25 per cent levies on all Canadian goods entering the U.S., the official said.
Trump's comments came after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced he will step down, and suspended Parliament for almost three months while his Liberal party stages a leadership race to replace him.
On Wednesday, as Liberal MPs gathered on Parliament Hill to discuss that leadership contest, several cabinet ministers stressed the need to take Trump's belligerence seriously, and warned that the incoming American leader's threatened tariffs would jeopardize thousands of Canadian jobs.
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce released an analysis in November with University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe that predicted Trump's tariffs could take a $78-billion chunk out of the Canadian economy - the equivalent of 2.6 per cent of national gross domestic product (GDP).
International Trade Minister Mary Ng said Wednesday it's no longer a joke to hear Trump talk about making Canada part of the United States. "We are taking it seriously, and we will respond seriously as well," Ng said.
"We have to be concerned when tariffs and punishing tariffs are going to hurt the Canadian economy."
Dominic LeBlanc, who took over as finance minister after Chrystia Freeland resignation from cabinet in December, ruled out a run for Trudeau's job on Wednesday, in part to keep his focus on how to counter the danger of Trump's tariffs. LeBlanc, who earlier said he thought Trump's comment about Canada being the 51st state was a joke, told reporters that's no longer the case.
"I find he has far surpassed the idea of a joke," LeBlanc said in French.
"It is becoming, frankly, completely counterproductive," he added, suggesting it was a tactic to sow confusion.
LeBlanc said he was set to meet with top government officials Wednesday evening to discuss the tariff threats.
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford complained about the "lack of leadership right now in Ottawa," but had kinds words for LeBlanc.
"I have confidence as long as he's in his position, he's going to do a great job leading. All the premiers get along very well with Minister LeBlanc, and I'm glad he's at the table," said Ford, who implored newly minted Public Safety Minister David McGuinty to "be out there on a daily basis, telling the U.S. what we're doing to secure the border."
Ford - who has been appearing on American news channels to argue Trump's threatened tariffs are misguided - also proposed to better integrate energy systems in Canada and the U.S. to create a "Fortress Can-Am." Ford has also said Canada should ink a trade deal with the U.S. that excludes Mexico, a partner in North American trade deals since the 1990s.
The federal government committed $1.3 billion to beef up border security after Trump named illegal immigration and drug smuggling as justifications for the tariffs he threatened to impose.
After Trump made the comments about annexing Canada at a news conference at his Mar-a-Lago luxury club in Florida on Tuesday, Trudeau posted on social media there is not "a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would become part of the U.S.
Opposition parties also pushed back against Trump, with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stating online "we are a great and independent country," and promising to expand the military, improve border control and protect the Arctic from Russia and China if his party wins the next election. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called Trump a "bully" and said Canada should impose retaliatory tariffs on the U.S. if Trump follows through with his threats, which he said would harm the economies of both countries.
"You come for Canadians' jobs, Americans will pay a price," Singh wrote on social media.
During Trump's first term as president, from 2017 to 2021, he forced Canada and Mexico to renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement. In 2018, Trump slapped tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum, arguing the action was necessary for U.S. national security. At the time, Trudeau called the tariffs "an affront to the long-standing security partnership between Canada and the United States" and responded with duties on more than $16 billion worth of American goods.
This time, as Trump prepares to return to the White House and impose his new tariffs on Jan. 20, the federal government has not ruled out any response as it prepares a new list of American goods to target if the incoming president follows through on his threats.
"We have to be extremely ready because the threat of tariffs is real," said Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, who is contemplating a run for the Liberal party leadership.
"We need to take (Trump) very seriously. I never take his threats lightly," she added.
"At the same time, we can't take the bait, and we're extremely strong as a country," she said. "We will never back down."
For Roland Paris, a professor of international affairs at the University of Ottawaand a former foreign policy adviser to Trudeau, Trump's latest comments about using economic force to annex Canada smacks of American imperialism.
"Trump's language is really just an assertion of American dominance," Paris said, describing how it "upends" decades of Canadian foreign policy assumptions.
Aaron Ettinger, a professor who specializes in U.S. foreign policy at Carleton University, agreed Trump's posture echoes American "expansionism" in the 19th century.
But while Ettinger dismissed the idea that Trump could use economic tools to take over Canada, he said his statements mark a break with decades of co-operation.
"There's no escaping the economic pull of the United States," Ettinger said.
"(Trump) can do us harm."
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Document 182 of 500

Trudeau's resignation could have been a moment for Jagmeet Singh's NDP. It was instead a reminder of how he's failed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeaus-resignation-could-have-been-moment/docview/3152877792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+resignation+could+have+been+a+moment+for+Jagmeet+Singh%27s+NDP.+It+was+instead+a+reminder+of+how+he%27s+failed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Immediately after Justin Trudeau announced he was resigning as prime minister, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh sent out a spicy statement detailing all the ways Liberals have failed Canadians.
"They let you down on health care. They let you down by allowing corporate greed to run wild. Even with the country facing another serious blow to Canadian jobs and our cost of living - this time from Donald Trump - they're still focused only on themselves and their political fate," Singh said.
Sure, Singh's tone was assertive. But he didn't make a case for the NDP's vision so much as he positioned it as the only option for progressives. Singh should have been ready for this moment. He's had years to rally people unhappy with housing, healthcare, and the high cost of living in many Canadian cities behind a cohesive, populist economic message. But despite some viral social media moments and policy wins, he has ultimately failed to mobilize a leftist movement in the country.
Singh and the NDP deserve credit for some things they advocated for with the supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals. They pushed to implement national dental care plan and pharmacare plans, and got 10 days of sick leave and anti-scab protections for federal workers. Yet, the Liberals have largely taken the credit for these wins. Meanwhile, the NDP is still trailing the Liberals in the polls and is firmly in third place. Because Singh has so far refused to vote non-confidence in the government, allowing Trudeau to resign, he will not be facing a deeply unpopular incumbent in the upcoming election, but a new Liberal leader, thereby reducing his chances of being able to form the official opposition.
Singh took over the party in 2017. A proud Sikh man who didn't shy away from talking about systemic racism but also had a knack for social media, Singh was refreshing and optimistic. When I interviewed him in October 2017, he joked that he was "younger and hipper" than Trudeau.
But quips and being followed by Rihanna on Instagram can only take you so far. Singh could have pushed a bolder agenda, calling for things like universal basic income and pressuring the government to build more affordable rental housing quickly, by either leveraging the supply and confidence agreement or ripping it up sooner. His plan, if elected, would be to build 500,000 affordable housing units, but he's also pushed for centrist positions such as subsidizing people's mortgages and lowering interest rates.
Filling the vacuum, the Conservatives have now positioned themselves as the voice for Canada's working class and are poised to form government in the next election.
"I think the moment really asked for a more populist opportunity, a more grassroots opportunity, and a long term project of changing minds," author and political commentator David Moscrop told me.
"A lot of people look at the party and . . . they lump the Liberals and the NDP more or less together, and that gives you effectively no space in the spectrum."
Moscrop noted that Canada had a federal social housing program until the 1980s, but said Singh and the NDP avoided going "whole hog" on it "because they've run away from socialism, thinking it's a scary word."
Even in America, which worships at the altar of capitalism, Bernie Sanders found major traction by running on socialized health care and housing-for-all. But Singh has failed to galvanize or inspire leftist Canadians in the same way.
After US Vice President Kamala Harris decisively lost to Donald Trump, many Democrats said the party had gone too "woke" and it cost them the election. Now that Trudeau is on his way out, Liberal insiders have been saying the same.
But the popularity of Sanders in the U.S., and even that of former Labour Party leader Jeremy Corbyn's in the United Kingdom, suggest that people are hungry for bold, progressive change.
Jagmeet Singh hasn't been able to meet the moment. The NDP needs a leader who can.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Monday, Nov. 25, 2024.

Subject: Cost of living; Populism; Social networks

Doug Ford uninjured after his vehicle was involved in Hwy. 401 collision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-uninjured-after-his-vehicle-was/docview/3153254098/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+uninjured+after+his+vehicle+was+involved+in+Hwy.+401+collision&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford and members of his staff were uninjured after the vehicle they were travelling in was involved in an accident on Highway 401 as they returned to Queen's Park from an announcement at the Darlington nuclear plant.
"We were leaving OPG, driving down the road on the highway and next thing you know, out of nowhere, the car got side-swiped," Ford told reporters late Wednesday afternoon at the legislature.
"That's all I can say," the premier said, adding "we were just driving straight. Next thing you know, we got hammered, just hammered."
"I'm OK," the premier added. "This head's like limestone. It's hard as a rock."
Ford, staff members and OPP officers were in the SUV at the time and all are safe, said Grace Lee, director of media relations for the premier's office.
The driver of the other vehicle was injured and taken to hospital with non-life threatening injuries, she added.
"He was out of commission," Ford said. "He hit us and then ended up on the other side of the highway. It was bad, but I just pray he's OK."
More to come
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford was uninjured after his vehicle was hit by another car on Jan. 8, 2025.

Document 184 of 500

Ottawa preparing response to threatened U.S. tariffs as Donald Trump ramps up attacks on Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-preparing-response-threatened-u-s-tariffs/docview/3153254064/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+preparing+response+to+threatened+U.S.+tariffs+as+Donald+Trump+ramps+up+attacks+on+Canada&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Joke's over.
The federal government signalled Wednesday that it took U.S. president-elect Donald Trump seriously when he spoke this week of using "economic force" to annex Canada, after previously downplaying as a joke his repeated taunts about making this country the "51st state."
A senior government official, who spoke to the Star on condition of anonymity, said the latest and most extreme talk of erasing Canada's sovereignty has galvanized the government. Ottawa is now pushing to soon release details of a potential response to Trump's threat of a trade war, if he follows through on his plan to impose punishing 25-per-cent levies on all Canadian goods entering the United States, the official said.
Trump's comments came after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced he will step down, and suspended Parliament for almost three months while his Liberal party stages a leadership race to replace him.
On Wednesday, as Liberal MPs gathered on Parliament Hill to discuss that leadership contest, several cabinet ministers stressed the need to take Trump's belligerence seriously, and warned that the incoming American leader's threatened tariffs would jeopardize thousands of Canadian jobs.
The Canadian Chamber of Commerce released an analysis in November with University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe that predicted Trump's tariffs could take a $78-billion chunk out of the Canadian economy - the equivalent of 2.6 per cent of national gross domestic product (GDP).
International Trade Minister Mary Ng said Wednesday it's no longer a joke to hear Trump talk about making Canada part of the United States.
"We are taking it seriously, and we will respond seriously as well," Ng said.
"We have to be concerned when tariffs and punishing tariffs are going to hurt the Canadian economy."
Dominic LeBlanc, who took over as finance minister after Chrystia Freeland resignation from cabinet in December, ruled out a run for Trudeau's job on Wednesday, in part to keep his focus on how to counter the danger of Trump's tariffs. LeBlanc, who earlier said he thought Trump's comment about Canada being the 51st state was a joke, told reporters Wednesday that that's no longer the case.
"I find he has far surpassed the idea of a joke," LeBlanc said in French. "It is becoming, frankly, completely counterproductive," he added, suggesting it was a tactic to sow confusion.
LeBlanc said he was set to meet with top government officials Wednesday evening to discuss the tariff threats.
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford complained Wednesday about the "lack of leadership right now in Ottawa," but had kinds words for LeBlanc.
"I have confidence as long as he's in his position, he's going to do a great job leading. All the premiers get along very well with Minister LeBlanc, and I'm glad he's at the table," said Ford, who implored newly-minted Public Safety Minister David McGuinty to "be out there on a daily basis, telling the U.S. what we're doing to secure the border."
Doug Ford - who has been appearing on American news channels to argue Trump's threatened tariffs are misguided - also proposed to better integrate energy systems in Canada and the U.S. to create a "Fortress Can-Am." Ford has also said Canada should ink a trade deal with the U.S. that excludes Mexico, a partner in North American trade deals since the 1990s.
The federal government committed $1.3 billion to beef up border security after Trump named illegal immigration and drug smuggling as justifications for the tariffs he threatened to impose.
After Trump made the comments about annexing Canada at a news conference at his luxury club in Mar-a-Lago, Florida on Tuesday, Trudeau posted on social media that there is not "a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would become part of the U.S.
Opposition parties also pushed back against Trump, with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stating online that "we are a great and independent country," and promising to expand the military, improve border control and protect the Arctic from Russia and China if his party wins the next election. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called Trump a "bully" and said Canada should impose retaliatory tariffs on the U.S. if Trump follows through with his threats, which he said would harm the economies of both countries.
"You come for Canadians' jobs, Americans will pay a price," Singh wrote on social media.
During Trump's first term as president, from 2017 to 2021, he forced Canada and Mexico to renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement. In 2018, Trump slapped tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum, arguing the action was necessary for U.S. national security. At the time, Trudeau called the tariffs "an affront to the long-standing security partnership between Canada and the United States" and responded with duties on more than $16 billion worth of American goods.
This time, as Trump prepares to return to the White House and impose his new tariffs on Jan. 20, the federal government has not ruled out any response as it prepares a new list of American goods to target if the incoming president follows through on his threats.
"We have to be extremely ready because the threat of tariffs is real," said Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, who is contemplating a run for the Liberal party leadership.
"We need to take (Trump) very seriously. I never take his threats lightly," she added.
"At the same time, we can't take the bait, and we're extremely strong as a country," she said. "We will never back down."
For Roland Paris, a professor of international affairs at the University of Ottawa and a former foreign policy adviser to Trudeau, Trump's latest comments about using economic force to annex Canada smacks of American imperialism. The incoming U.S. president also refused this week to rule out using military force to take over the Panama Canal and Greenland, the latter a territory of Denmark, an American and Canadian ally in the North Atlantic Treaty Organization.
"Trump's language is really just an assertion of American dominance," Paris said, describing how it "upends" decades of Canadian foreign policy assumptions, as North America became more integrated and through economic partnerships like the 1960s Auto Pact, and military co-operation such that seen in the North American Aerospace Defence Command.
While noting that it's unclear whether Trump is sincere or merely blustering in an attempt to wrench trade concessions from Canada, Paris said the situation is nonetheless "unusual and disturbing."
Aaron Ettinger, a professor who specializes in U.S. foreign policy at Carleton University, agreed that Trump's posture echoes American "expansionism" in the 19th century. Some argued at the time that the U.S. was destined to control all of North America as part of a divinely inspired "manifest destiny."
But while Ettinger dismissed the idea that Trump could use economic tools to take over Canada, he said his statements mark a break with decades of co-operation that have defined the Canada-U.S. relationship.
They also highlight how the U.S. could seriously harm the Canadian economy, given that it is by far Canada's largest trading partner. According to Statistics Canada, 77 per cent of Canada's exports went to the U.S. and 50 per cent of its imports came from there in 2023.
"There's no escaping the economic pull of the United States," Ettinger said.
"(Trump) can do us harm."
With files from Robert Benzie

Document 186 of 500

Doug Ford pitches energy alliance with the U.S. to avert trade war with Donald Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pitches-energy-alliance-with-u-s-avert/docview/3152685287/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pitches+energy+alliance+with+the+U.S.+to+avert+trade+war+with+Donald+Trump&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After warning Ontario would cut off electricity exports stateside in retaliation for any Donald Trump tariffs, Premier Doug Ford wants to better integrate energy systems with the U.S. to avert a trade war.
With the president-elect threatening 25-per-cent levies on Canadian and Mexican goods later this month unless America's neighbours tighten their borders, Ford on Wednesday pitched "Fortress Am-Can" fuelled by domestic energy production.
"These tariffs would be devastating for both of our economies. They'd disrupt deeply integrated supply chains, create massive uncertainty for investors, raise costs for businesses and put countless jobs at risk on both sides of the border. It doesn't have to be this way," the premier said.
"Fortress Am-Can is a vision that respects each country as proudly independent, a vision that celebrates what we can achieve together: a true partnership where Canada and the U.S. align on shared priorities, achieving free, fair and balanced trade, growing our economies, accelerating resource development and protecting our borders and our communities," he said.
Speaking at the Darlington energy complex in Courtice, Ont., 70 kilometres east of Toronto, Ford touted Ontario's existing nuclear fleet and its planned small modular reactors, urging greater co-operation with American lawmakers and industry.
His comments come four weeks after he warned the province would not sit idly by if Trump makes good on his threat to slap 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian products after his Jan. 20 inauguration.
"We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy - going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin," Ford said on Dec. 18, noting Ontario supplies about 1.5 million Americans with electricity.
While that gambit got Trump's attention, the president-elect has ramped up his rhetoric, deriding America's largest trading partner as "the great state of Canada" and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as a state "governor."
At a rambling 70-minute news conference at his Florida resort on Tuesday, he said he would use "economic force" to make Canada a "51st state."
"Canada and the United States, that would really be something," said Trump.
"You get rid of that artificially drawn line, and you take a look at what that looks like ... it would also be much better for national security," said the president-elect, who also mused about annexing Greenland, which is part of Denmark, as well as the Panama Canal, and renaming the Gulf of Mexico the "Gulf of America."
In response, Trudeau, who announced his resignation as prime minister and Liberal leader Monday, said "there isn't a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada would ever join the U.S.
"Workers and communities in both our countries benefit from being each other's biggest trading and security partner," he said.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who polls suggest would win a federal election expected this year, echoed Trudeau, saying "Canada will never be the 51st state."
On Wednesday in Courtice, Ford reiterated that Trump's ramblings are not constructive.
"Our country is not for sale. It will never be for sale. I'm always concerned when a president the most powerful country in the world says these statements, but that's not going to happen," the premier said, insisting he has no federal political aspirations.
"My main focus is to protect the people of Ontario and as chair of the (Council of the) Federation, I have to make sure I represent all the premiers across the country from coast to coast to coast - and that's my job."
Beyond further integration of the electricity grid, Ford wants Canadian and American lawmakers to forge a working group with energy and security experts "to ensure both countries adhere to best practices for power system security against foreign interference, cyber attacks, terrorism and extreme weather, among other threats."
As well, he would like to "align regulations and eliminate red tape that slows building cross-border energy infrastructure, including for transmission lines, interties and pipelines."
But the premier emphasized that does not mean Ontario couldn't counter any Trump moves by withholding energy exports to the U.S.
"You turn up the heat on them," he said, signalling American consumers would pay a price for the new president's tariffs.
"Someone in Texas buys a pickup truck that's produced here in Oshawa, not far away from here, they typically would pay $60,000. Now, that same person in Texas is going to pay $75,000 if he puts tariffs on vehicles."
Later Wednesday, Ford met virtually with the other provincial and territorial leaders to discuss the Trump threat and next moves after the prime minister's departure.
"There's a lack of leadership right now in Ottawa," he told reporters at Queen's Park.
On Jan. 15, Trudeau will convene the premiers for an in-person first ministers' meeting in Ottawa on the tariff crisis.
A month later, on Feb. 12, Ford is leading a COF delegation to Washington to plead Canada's case to members of the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives.
CAPTION: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump (left) and Premier Doug Ford.

Full text: Poilievre showing his true colours
Justin Trudeau promises to resign as prime minister and Liberal leader after leadership race, Jan. 6
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's response to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's resignation announcement was a disgustingly classless act.
I am a lifelong conservative and not a huge fan of Trudeau, but am even less a fan of Poilievre. I recognize the Trudeau government did some good things for Canada and he deserves credit and our thanks for almost a decade of service to this country. It was not an easy decision for him to make and Poilievre should have had the decency to recognize that.
Instead, he pilloried Trudeau and attempted to blame him and all possible contenders for the Liberals' top job for all ills affecting the country. It was a crass performance and proves he is not suited to be our PM. I fervently hope Canadians pay close attention to what Liberal candidates have to say in the upcoming leadership race. Then compare that to Poilievre's meaningless slogans and lack of concrete plans.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
PM made this a drawn-out affair
The majority of Canadians are glad Justin Trudeau finally is going to quit, but he didn't quit Monday. He will quit only when the Liberals pick another leader, which means he's still the head of the government for a few more months.
He should've called an election and let the Canadian public decide the future of Canada. This means for the next few months the media will be giving non-stop coverage to the Liberal party at a time when Canada desperately needs leadership to deal with the things coming down the pipe.
Jim Church, Kelowna, B.C.
Media's lack of respect is galling
Although I am not a supporter of the current prime minister, he should be addressed as Prime Minister Trudeau rather than simply Justin.
Members of the media are frequent offenders in this regard. Failing to use the appropriate title demonstrates a lack of respect for the Office of the Prime Minister.
Peter Rozanec, Etobicoke
Canadians have wrongly accused PMs of arrogance
I had a blinding flash of reality Monday as I watched Justin Trudeau's resignation announcement.
It was a camera angle that captured him in front of the house he and his family chose to live in since he became PM, a humble house that could have been my grandparents' farm house a hundred years ago.
Over the years I have witnessed opposition parties, press and Canadians accusing the Trudeaus of being wealthy arrogant and entitled elitists who don't understand or care for the have-nots. In that moment, with our prime minister standing in the cold in front of a fairly modest house, I flashed to Pierre Poilievre, living in a mansion as official opposition, while never having held a real job, let alone displaying any signs of humility.
Wonder where will he choose to live if he's elected?
Who is the arrogant entitled one now?
Sharon Kirkham, Toronto
Resignation a sad day for Canada
I am saddened to see my prime minister leave like this.
Sure, he made mistakes like every person does, but he also did a lot of good. I know our life and our children's and friend's lives are better for his tenure as our leader.
He deserves a better end to his time as our PM.
Rest assured prime minister, you are still loved and respected by many Canadians. I wish you a happy future.
I fear where the Conservative leader would lead us.
Kathy Bennett, Orillia
It's time to give Trudeau his due
What transpired between Justin Trudeau and former finance minister Chrystia Freeland was their business; it was Freeland who resigned.
Not to defend Trudeau, but when Premier Doug Ford shuffled former education minister Stephen Lecce to another post, no one fussed about that move, nor was Ford asked to retire.
Now Trudeau shuffles Freeland's position around and he has stepped aside, with much criticism from the media.
Say what we will about Trudeau, but at least he admitted overestimating the number of immigrants he should have admitted to Canada and is trying to fix it, along with other things.
We should also credit Trudeau for acting swiftly on the tariff threat by meeting with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump.
I may not agree with some of Trudeau's ways, but, while he has held office, his diplomacy in dealing with other nations has maintained the peace.
Which is essential in these troubling times with nations rising against each other.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Rebate shameful vote grab by Ford
Doug Ford government says the $200 'rebate' cheques to Ontarians are coming soon. Here's when you can expect them, Jan. 3
These kinds of vote-seeking stunts are so "Mickey Mouse."
I'd be ashamed to be associated with any government that conducted such a sham, especially when implemented at the expense of out-of-control deficits.
Robert Woodcock, North York
Alcohol sales at ONroute stops?
You can buy beer, wine and cocktails at 4,000 convenience stores across Ontario. Here's what to expect, Sept. 5
On a recent trip to Ottawa and back I was astonished to see illuminated signs at the ONroute rest stops advertising beer sales.
I agree with selling beer and wine at corner stores, but selling and advertising it while driving along Highway 401 is too far for me. It's simply wrong.
This drive in winter can be a battle of whiteouts and slick roads at any given moment. Do we really need drivers having the opportunity to pick up a six-pack?
Sure, it's illegal to drink and drive but why promote the sale of beer and the chance of more drunk drivers travelling our busiest highway? It's an invitation to a fatality.
Premier Ford, there are a multitude of unsolvable problems, but this one can be solved very quickly. All you need to do is rescind the liquor sales at all ONroute rest stops.
It's the right thing to do and so very easy.
Is it worth the extra few dollars you will make on taxes? Is it worth the life of a family?
Please do the right thing and put an end to this.
Doug Axford, Toronto

Document 188 of 500

Video game captures the 'abject terror' of cycling in Toronto. Welcome to 'Loser Lane'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/video-game-captures-abject-terror-cycling-toronto/docview/3152478987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Video+game+captures+the+%27abject+terror%27+of+cycling+in+Toronto.+Welcome+to+%27Loser+Lane%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I die immediately. And then again seconds later.
Bobbing and weaving through traffic - an opening door on the right, cars within spitting distance on the left - the margin between life and death is a pixel large.
I make it to Gus Tacos before a car inches into the right lane, putting me on a collision course with an open car door. I collide, and then die again.
After death, a message in bright pink letters flashes before me: "I survived 18 seconds without a bike lane. Thanks Doug."
I have just lost "Loser Lane" - an impossible-to-beat video game designed by artist Marie LeBlanc Flanagan.
A former Toronto resident, now living in Montreal, Flanagan knows all too well the dangers of riding outside of a bike lane - their younger sister was in an accident that required years of rehab, they also lost a beloved uncle to a cycling accident.
When they heard about Doug Ford's new bike lane law, Flanagan felt an immediate "rush of frustration, anger and, I don't now what the word is for this feeling of being let down, like somebody else should have had our back."
The law, which passed in November, forces municipalities to get provincial approval for any new bike lanes that impact vehicular traffic and gives Queen's Park the power to remove existing lanes on University Avenue, Yonge Street and Bloor Street in Toronto. The legislation also indemnifies the province against any lawsuits from people or their families from suing if someone is killed or injured on a road where a bike lane was removed.
In response, Flanagan took their outrage and disappointment, and poured it into what they knew best: video games. It took them just 14 days to create the game.
In the game, Flanagan hoped to capture the "abject terror of trying to cycle" in Toronto, from "trying to cycle between the doors that might open, over the streetcar tracks ... and cars trying to park." And they warn you off the top, there's no beating the game, no cheat code or special technique - you always die.
Having cycled in cities across Canada and the world, from Vancouver to Tokyo, Flanagan has seen first-hand the coexistence of bike lanes and dense metropolitan areas. They've seen it here, too, as Toronto built up its own cycling infrastructure during their time living here - infrastructure that is now at risk of being torn out.
"There's no question that there are traffic problems," Flanagan said, "but I just don't feel like the solution is less bike lanes ... The solution is more public transit, free public transit, more bike lanes, more bikes or people."
The game is meant to illustrate the real human cost of removing bike lanes, Flanagan said, "People will die, and I want them to not be able to look away."
"This is really how it feels. And as an artist, I (like) helping people feel seen and helping people remember that they have a voice."
CAPTION: Bobbing and weaving through traffic - an opening door on the right, cars within spitting distance on the left - the margin between life and death is a pixel large. Marie LeBlanc Flanagan created "Loser Lane" after they heard about Doug Ford's bike lane legislation.Marie LeBlanc Flanagan created "Loser Lane" after they heard about Doug Ford's bike lane legislation.

Premier bolsters border security: Ford urges Ottawa to keep focus on U.S. tariff threats

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-bolsters-border-security/docview/3152478924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+bolsters+border+security%3A+Ford+urges+Ottawa+to+keep+focus+on+U.S.+tariff+threats&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Donald Trump threatens 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods unless the border with the U.S. is strengthened, Premier Doug Ford is enhancing security on the frontier.
"Ontario has been calling on the federal government to step up and address safety and security concerns at the border. We need to see words turned into visible action," Ford said Tuesday.
The premier's comments come as the U.S. president-elect is vowing to slap levies on Canadian and Mexican products after his Jan. 20 inauguration unless America's neighbours curb illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
"Ontario is stepping up with Operation Deterrence to crack down on illegal border crossings and illegal guns and drugs," said Ford, imploring Ottawa to also do more. "A more co-ordinated Team Canada approach that includes more boots on the ground is the only way to detect, deter and disrupt illegal activity and ensure the safety and security of Canadian and American communities."
Ford will meet virtually with the other premiers Wednesday to further discuss steps to mitigate Trump's gambit.
Operation Deterrence includes 200 Ontario Provincial Police officers, who are working in partnership with the RCMP.
In the past five weeks, the OPP has already done more than 6,000 hours of "focused patrols to deter, detect and disrupt illegal border activity using fixed-wing aircraft, helicopters, remotely piloted aircraft, boats, off-road vehicles, patrol vehicles and foot patrol," the province said.
As well, the government said "Operation Deterrence activity will be enhanced through the use of the emergency response team, front-line officer and additional specialty resources, including canine units, commercial motor vehicle inspections and criminal investigators."
The initiative is above and beyond what is already being done at Ontario's 14 official U.S. border crossings by the Canada Border Services Agency.
In the wake of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announcing his resignation Monday, Ford has been imploring Ottawa to remain focused on the Trump tariff threat.
Speaking to reporters after Trudeau told the nation he would prorogue Parliament and step down after nine years as prime minister, the premier said the federal uncertainty is troubling.
"We need all hands on deck. We need the prime minister to continue doing his job. Prorogation doesn't mean vacation," Ford said. "God forbid president-elect Trump gets in there and puts tariffs. We have to retaliate. We have to retaliate hard against our friends south of the border, which is unfortunate."
Since the incoming president's Nov. 25 tariff threat against Canada and Mexico, Ford, who is chair of the premiers' Council of the Federation, has been more aggressive than Trudeau or any federal leader.
With Trump repeatedly trolling Canadians on social media about annexing this country as a "51st state" and referring to the prime minister as the "governor of the great state of Canada," Ford stressed that joke isn't funny.
"To the president, I'll make him a counter-offer. How about if we buy Alaska and we'll throw in Minnesota or Minneapolis at the same time," the premier said Monday.
"It's not realistic. I know he likes to make these comments, he likes joking around, (but) I take that seriously," he said. "He may be joking, but under my watch, that will never, ever happen."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Aircraft; Presidents; Deterrence; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 190 of 500

This is no time for a provincial election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-is-no-time-provincial-election/docview/3152478770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+is+no+time+for+a+provincial+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't do it, Doug Ford.
Don't give us double electoral trouble.
As much as some Ontarians would be relieved to be rid of you as premier, this is no time to risk seeking re-election.
It's not that the province necessarily needs you to stick around. It's that you need not - dare not - stick it to Ontarians by saddling us with another political campaign at the provincial level, while federal leadership and election races are underway over the next few months.
At the very moment that our American neighbours are taking their best shots at us, in both tariffs and tirades, the federal government is now paralyzed by Justin Trudeau's resignation odyssey. An early election call in Ontario would compound the uncertainty amid the upheaval of a trade war.
A newly empowered U.S. President Donald Trump would be dealing not only with a disembowelled prime minister on Parliament Hill, but a disempowered government at Queen's Park. With America on the attack, we'd be defended by a lame duck prime minister in Ottawa and a caretaker premier in Ontario - leaving the country's biggest and second-biggest governments in limbo while Ford cavorts on the campaign trail.
Yet still, the premier refuses to rule out an early election.
Ford persists in ruminating about going to the polls prematurely so as to lock in his current lead in public opinion polls, lest he lose ground in any future economic downturn or face awkward questions from a police probe into his Greenbelt land swap fiasco. Since last spring, his Tories have obsessed over an early election footing that defies convention and, now, common sense.
Back then, when this early election notion first surfaced, a second Trump presidency seemed pure fantasy and the spectre of a tariff war seemed unfathomable. Today, more than a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote that is prescribed by Ontario's fixed election date law, Ford is still putting his personal ambition ahead of the tradition of four-year mandates.
Less than three years after winning the June 2022 election, the premier keeps conflating campaigning with governing.
On the one hand, he is making the most of Trudeau's troubles by insinuating himself into the Canada-U.S. mix, describing his fellow premiers as critical players who demand a seat at the bilateral table. Yet in the same breath, Ford can't bring himself to declare, clearly, that this is no time to put his personal and political self-interest over the provincial and national interest.
It's easy to see why Ford is so invested in an election. It's not just the risk of more Greenbelt bad news, or the weak state of the political opposition.
Ontarians are about to receive $200 cheques in the mail as part of the premier's thinly veiled pre-election vote-buying scheme, at a cost of more than $3 billion to the deficit-ridden, heavily indebted provincial treasury. The premier had planned to cash in on the political dividends from that voter giveaway.
And yet, a truly responsible and prudent premier would tell reporters that he has instructed his campaign team to stand down until further notice, something along these (press) lines: "I'm on the job, because I've got a job to do. I've got a massive majority mandate from voters that's valid through mid-2026, so the last thing I need to do is ask voters to repeat themselves. Therefore, we will not have a provincial vote until the lawful election date."
But Ford won't say what needs to be said. Given the chance by reporters, repeatedly, to clear the air this week after Trudeau's announcement, the premier left the door wide open to more electoral tumult when Canada least needs it.
Oddly, he publicly warned the PM not to get distracted by anything as grubby as partisan politics. Indeed, the premiers will keep up the pressure to ensure that "as long as he's prime minister, his main focus is not on the Liberal party, is not on anything else - it should be, the next 14 days, making sure that we avoid these tariffs," Ford intoned.
But would Ford make the same public pledge not to focus solely on the Progressive Conservative party he leads in Ontario, especially from the campaign trail? The premier couldn't, wouldn't do it, retreating instead to platitudes:
"What I can tell you today is I'm 100 per cent focused on the tariffs," he began. "I can tell you, my eye hasn't been taken off the ball for a second here. We've been leading the way - all the premiers."
Ford likes to remind people that he holds the rotating chair of the premiers' association, implying this makes him indispensable, not to mention electable. But why add campaigning to the responsibilities of governing when Ottawa is already sidelined?
"This is when all Canadians step up, this is when our provincial and territorial leaders step up."
Step up - not step aside and onto the campaign trail. We don't need two of our governments in limbo while the U.S. government lashes out.
Time to clear the air, premier. Time to declare that you'll stick to the fixed election law - with a fixed focus on our economic challenges, not your political ambitions.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has publicly warned Prime Minister Justin Trudeau not to get distracted by partisan politics, Martin Regg Cohn writes, advice the premier himself would do well to follow.Nathan DenetteTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Ford government offering rebates for home upgrades: Program to cover up to 30% of energy-saving renovations starting Jan. 28

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-offering-rebates-home-upgrades/docview/3152476020/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+offering+rebates+for+home+upgrades%3A+Program+to+cover+up+to+30%25+of+energy-saving+renovations+starting+Jan.+28&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province is promising dollars for doors, windows, smart thermostats and other energy-saving upgrades as part of a new home renovation rebate program.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce - who announced the initiative Tuesday alongside well-known contractor and TV host Mike Holmes at a Scarborough building supplies store - said the program will cover up to 30 per cent of costs for consumers starting Jan. 28.
Energy-efficient appliances such as refrigerators and freezers will be added to the program later this year, he added.
"What sets this program apart is its simplicity and its scope because, unlike programs of the past, this rebate will be available to all homeowners, regardless of how they heat their homes," Lecce said, meaning those using propane or oil will be included.
"And families will receive a service commitment that all rebates must be paid on time, within 60 days after a complete program application is approved - and the savings can be significant," he said.
The Ontario Home Renovation Savings Program could mean rebates of up to $8,900 for insulation, as much as $5,000 for rooftop solar panels, $600 for a home energy assessment and $100 for each new window or door.
"For years I've been telling everyone out there, you need to work from the outside in if you want to renovate your home, not the inside out," said Holmes, who is president and founder of Holmes Group.
"I think we're stepping in the right direction of helping homeowners do it right the first time."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said the Ford government cancelled energy efficiency programs after taking office and "it's unfortunate that people have had to wait six long years for this government to act, because home retrofits like heat pumps, solar panels or even just new windows and doors help Ontarians save money every single day."
Schreiner said the $7 billion the government spends annually on subsidizing Ontarians' electricity bills should instead be targeted to lower- and middle-income earners for home upgrades.
Lecce also announced an expansion of a program that will give small businesses additional funds for taking part in Peak Perks, a program that temporarily adjusts thermostats during high demand periods during hot summer days
Tuesday's announcement was part of a $10.9-billion spending commitment over the next 12 years to help with energy efficiency.
Lecce said government energy efficiency programs are expected to save the equivalent of taking three million homes off the grid over the next decade.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
