Full text: Trudeau resigns, and Poilievre shows his true coloursJustin Trudeau promises to resign as prime minister and Liberal leader after leadership race, Jan. 6
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's response to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's resignation announcement was a disgustingly classless act.
I am a lifelong conservative and not a huge fan of Trudeau, but am even less a fan of Poilievre. I recognize the Trudeau government did some good things for Canada and he deserves credit and our thanks for almost a decade of service to this country. It was not an easy decision for him to make and Poilievre should have had the decency to recognize that.
Instead, he pilloried Trudeau and attempted to blame him and all possible contenders for the Liberals' top job for all ills affecting the country. It was a crass performance and proves he is not suited to be our PM. I fervently hope Canadians pay close attention to what Liberal candidates have to say in the upcoming leadership race. Then compare that to Poilievre's meaningless slogans and lack of concrete plans .
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
PM's resignation a long, drawn out affair
The majority of Canadians are glad Trudeau finally is going to quit, but he didn't quit Monday. He will quit only when the Liberals pick another leader, which means he's still the head of the government for a few more months.
Big disappointment.
He should've called an election and let the Canadian public decide the future of Canada. This means for the next few months the media will be giving nonstop coverage to the Liberal Party at a time when Canada desperately needs leadership to deal with the things coming down the pipe.
Jim Church, Kelowna, B.C.
Media's lack of respect for PMO galling
Although I am not a supporter of the current prime minister, he should be addressed as Prime Minister Trudeau rather than simply Justin.
Members of the media are frequent offenders in this regard. Failing to use the appropriate title demonstrates a lack of respect for the Office of the Prime Minister.
Peter Rozanec, Etobicoke
Canadians have wrongly accused Trudeau of arrogance
I had a blinding flash of reality Monday as I watched Justin Trudeau's resignation announcement.
It was a camera angle that captured him in front of the house he and his family chose to live in since he became PM, a humble house that could have been my grandparents' farm house a hundred years ago.
Over the years I have witnessed opposition parties, press and Canadians accusing the Trudeaus of being wealthy arrogant and entitled elitists who don't understand or care for the have-nots. In that moment, with our prime Minister standing in the cold in front of a fairly modest house, I flashed to Pierre Poilievre, living in a mansion as official opposition, while never having held a real job let alone displaying any signs of humility.
Wonder where will he choose to live if he's elected?
Who is the arrogant entitled one now?
Sharon Kirkham, Toronto
PM's resignation a sad day for Canada
I am saddened to see my prime minister leave like this.
Sure, he made mistakes like every person does, but he also did a lot of good. I know our life and our children's and friend's lives are better for his tenure as our leader. He deserves a better end to his time as our PM.
Rest assured prime minister, you are still loved and respected by many Canadians. I wish you a happy future.
I fear where the Conservative leader would lead us.
Kathy Bennett, Orillia, Ont.
Give Trudeau his due. He has earned it
What transpired between Justin Trudeau and former finance minister Chrystia Freeland was their business; it was Freeland who resigned.
Not to defend Trudeau, but when Premier Doug Ford shuffled former Minister of Education Stephen Lecce to another post, no one fussed about that move, nor was Ford asked to retire. Now Trudeau shuffles Freeland's position around and he has stepped aside, with much criticism from the media.
Say what we will about Trudeau, but at least he admitted overestimating the number of immigrants he should have admitted to Canada and is trying to fix it, along with other things. We should also credit Trudeau for acting swiftly on the tariff threat by meeting with president elect Donald Trump.
I may not agree with some of Trudeau's ways but, while he has held office, his diplomacy in dealing with other nations has maintained the peace.
Which is essential in these troubling times with nations rising against each other.
Tina Maini, Hamilton
Ford's $200 rebate a shameful vote grabDoug Ford government says the $200 'rebate' cheques to Ontarians are coming soon. Here's when you can expect them, Jan. 3
These kinds of vote-seeking stunts are so 'Mickey Mouse.'
I'd be ashamed to be associated with any government that conducted such a sham, especially when implemented at the expense of out-of-control deficits.
Robert Woodcock, North York
Alcohol sales at OnRoute stops? LudicrousYou can buy beer, wine and cocktails at 4,000 convenience stores across Ontario. Here's what to expect, Sept. 5
On a recent trip to Ottawa and back I was astonished to see illuminated signs at the OnRoute rest stops advertising beer sales.
I agree with selling beer and wine at corner stores, but selling and advertising it while driving along the 401 is too far for me. It's simply wrong.
This drive in winter can be a battle of whiteouts and slick roads at any given moment. Do we really need drivers having the opportunity to pick up a 6-pack? Sure, it's illegal to drink and drive but why promote the sale of beer and the chance of more drunk drivers travelling our busiest highway? It's an invitation to a fatality.
Premier Ford, there are a multitude of unsolvable problems, but this one can be solved very quickly. All you need to do is rescind the liquor sales at all OnRoute rest stops. It's the right thing to do and so very easy. Is it worth the extra few dollars you will make on taxes? Is it worth the life of a family?
Please do the right thing and put an end to this.
Doug Axford, Toronto

Document 193 of 500

Past failures weighing on Quebec energy deal in N.L.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/past-failures-weighing-on-quebec-energy-deal-n-l/docview/3152475149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Past+failures+weighing+on+Quebec+energy+deal+in+N.L.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey promised Monday that an independent panel of experts will monitor negotiations ahead of a final energy agreement with Quebec's electric utility - but he wouldn't commit to a review of the deal as it stands now.
The Liberal government of Canada's easternmost province opened its legislature this week for four days of debate about a memorandum of understanding between Hydro-Québec and Newfoundland and Labrador promising thousands of jobs and billions of dollars in revenue. Opposition politicians spent much of the first day repeating their calls for an arm's-length review of the tentative agreement, pointing to the province's past megaproject failures.
"I just don't understand the reluctance to take this (contract), which has such a significant impact on the opportunities for the people in Newfoundland and Labrador, and allow it to be reviewed," said Tony Wakeham, leader of the Opposition Progressive Conservatives.
"Will you commit to an independent review of this (memorandum of understanding)?" he asked Furey.
In response, Furey said there have already been independent firms advising during negotiations. His government will provide "outside and independent advice to cabinet" at the final stages of the agreement.
The tentative deal would see Hydro-Québec pay significantly more than it currently does for electricity from the Churchill Falls hydroelectric plant in Labrador, and develop additional projects with Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro along the Churchill River.
The provinces have had tense relations since 1969, when a contract was signed allowing Hydro-Québec to buy most of the power from Churchill Falls at prices far below market value. Newfoundland and Labrador tried unsuccessfully to overturn the deal, in a fight that wound up in the Supreme Court of Canada in 2017. The arrangement was set to expire in 2041, but Hydro-Québec has agreed to end it now. Under the new agreement, the utility will pay about 30 times more for Churchill Falls power, resulting in an increase of about $17 billion to the Newfoundland and Labrador treasury between now and 2041.
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Document 194 of 500

PM's resignation is no domestic matter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pms-resignation-is-no-domestic-matter/docview/3152474760/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM%27s+resignation+is+no+domestic+matter&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Still looking for a new year's resolution? How about holotropic breathing?
This respiratory technique may prove valuable in 2025. The calendar only flipped a week ago and the news machine is panting. The big headline arrived at a press conference on Monday that was devoid of sunny ways. Justin Trudeau will resign and prorogue Parliament, a fancy way of saying "give democracy a time out."
Breathe. Hold it in. Exhale. Elevate your consciousness.
Knowing when to leave a party is a life hack - especially a political party.
Why did Trudeau wait this long to abdicate the throne when polls showed he'd now lose an election to Don Cherry's dog? Ego is a hell of a drug. So now his party needs to find a new leader before St. Patrick's Day. They might as well throw names in a hat and pray they pull out "Ryan Reynolds."
The Liberals are looking as doomed as the Branch Davidians.
In normal times, a PM resignation would be a domestic matter. But we aren't living in normal times when America's president-elect is making noises about "merging" with Canada. It's bananas. Donald Trump has a sudden craving for Canada like we are a hot fudge sundae at the drive-thru.
His gallant response to Trudeau's resignation included: "If Canada merged with the U.S., there would be no Tariffs, taxes would go way down, and they would be TOTALLY SECURE from the threat of the Russian and Chinese Ships that are constantly surrounding them. Together, what a great Nation it would be!!!"
He also declared, "Many people in Canada LOVE being the 51st State."
With the possible exception of Alberta, that's not true. But I did not have "imperial conquest" on my bingo card for worries about Trump 2.0. I can only assume he is not well-versed in the Louisiana Purchase or the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo.
It's not just Canada he's eyeing. Trump is also obsessed with buying Greenland like it was an undervalued skyscraper in Manhattan. Don Jr. is in Greenland as I write this. Local officials do not know the purpose of this visit, which suggests he's not there yet to haggle over Nuuk. But his trip comes after his father wrote: "Greenland is an incredible place, and the people will benefit tremendously if, and when, it becomes part of our Nation. We will protect it, and cherish it, from a very vicious outside World. MAKE GREENLAND GREAT AGAIN!"
MGGA? It's tempting to laugh off this lunacy. But this week, the King of Denmark, which owns Greenland, changed the coat of arms. It now more prominently features Greenland and the Faroe Islands, another Danish territory. This was seen as a passive-aggressive rebuke of Trump.
But trying to stop Trump is like trying to stop a runaway freight train with a feather duster. Ask Jack Smith.
Trudeau has resigned and Trump is already planning his first rally in Moose Jaw.
Did Santa give this guy a geography colouring book for Christmas? He's also salivating for the Panama Canal. Are there meetings in which lickspittles stare silently as the boss yammers on about turning Aruba into a golf course or leasing the Great Wall of China? Why does Niagara Falls have a Canadian side?
On Monday, Trudeau looked like he just took a sucker punch from Logan Paul. But as he exits stage right, battered and bruised, he should do right by this great nation that kept him in power for nearly a decade. He should be working behind closed doors with his political rivals to come up with a national strategy that fortifies our sovereignty and possible economic responses to Tariff Man. He should encourage every Canadian politician to take Trump's threats seriously.
Trump is a bully and he sees Canada as a weak nerd with lots of lunch money.
Quietly protecting our future could be Trudeau's legacy before he seeks new opportunities, possibly as a door-to-door socks salesman. All that woke nonsense got us nowhere - except further in debt and now in the crosshairs of a hostile takeover by a friendly neighbour.
Now is the time for precog pragmatism.
Liberals, Conservatives, NDP, Green, Bloc Québécois, Libertarians, Centrists, Futurists, Marxist-Leninists, Marijuana Enthusiasts, it's time to come together and figure out what may happen before it happens and we all need new passports. I don't want my daughters growing up in a heavily armed 51st state without health care.
You picked a fine time to leave us, Justin. Now you are a lame duck and Canada is a sitting duck for whatever dark impulses pop into Trump's noggin as billionaires stuff $100 bills into his thong while he strips down and gyrates interpretative dances at Mar-a-Lago. Is the big guy twerking? I think he wants us to bid on Turks & Caicos. Or maybe invade Ontario?
Justin Trudeau has resigned. He left the party too late.
And now Canada is left to blow in the winds of resignation.
CAPTION: In normal times, Justin Trudeau's resignation would only concern Canadians. But we aren't living in normal times when America's president-elect, Donald Trump, is making noises about "merging" with Canada, writes Vinay Menon.Carolyn Kaster The Associated Press file photo
CREDIT: Vinay Menon Toronto Star OPINION

Location: Canada; Greenland

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Ford government will offer rebates for energy efficient home renovations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-will-offer-rebates-energy/docview/3152758985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+will+offer+rebates+for+energy+efficient+home+renovations&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province is promising dollars for doors, windows, smart thermostats and other energy-saving upgrades as part of a new home renovation rebate program.
Energy Minister Stephen Lecce - who announced the initiative Tuesday alongside well-known contractor and TV host Mike Holmes at a Scarborough building supplies store - said the program will cover up to 30 per cent of costs for consumers starting Jan. 28.
Energy-efficient appliances such as refrigerators and freezers will be added to the program later this year, he added.
"What sets this program apart is its simplicity and its scope because, unlike programs of the past, this rebate will be available to all homeowners, regardless of how they heat their homes," Lecce said, meaning those using propane or oil will be included.
"And families will receive a service commitment that all rebates must be paid on time, within 60 days after a complete program application is approved - and the savings can be significant," he said.
The Ontario Home Renovation Savings Program could mean rebates of up to $8,900 for insulation, as much as $5,000 for rooftop solar panels, $600 for a home energy assessment and $100 for each new window or door.
"For years I've been telling everyone out there, you need to work from the outside in if you want to renovate your home, not the inside out," said Holmes, who is president and founder of Holmes Group.
"I think we're stepping in the right direction of helping homeowners do it right the first time."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said the Ford government cancelled energy efficiency programs after taking office and "it's unfortunate that people have had to wait six long years for this government to act, because home retrofits like heat pumps, solar panels or even just new windows and doors help Ontarians save money every single day."
Schreiner said the $7 billion the government spends annually on subsidizing Ontarians' electricity bills should instead be targeted to lower- and middle-income earners for home upgrades.
Lecce also announced an expansion of a program that will give small businesses additional funds for taking part in Peak Perks, a program that temporarily adjusts thermostats during high demand periods during hot summer days
Tuesday's announcement was part of a $10.9 billion spending commitment over the next 12 years to help with energy efficiency.
Lecce said government energy efficiency programs are expected to save the equivalent of taking three million homes off the grid over the next decade.
CAPTION: Mike Holmes, MPP Aris Babikian, Energy Minister Stephen Lecce and MPP Sam Oosterhoff announced a new program of rebates for energy efficient home renovations on Jan. 7, 2025.

Document 196 of 500

With Trump returning and Trudeau leaving, this is no time for Doug Ford to call an unnecessary election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-trump-returning-trudeau-leaving-this-is-no/docview/3152758763/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+Trump+returning+and+Trudeau+leaving%2C+this+is+no+time+for+Doug+Ford+to+call+an+unnecessary+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Don't do it, Doug Ford.
Don't give us double electoral trouble.
As much as some Ontarians would be relieved to be rid of you as premier, this is no time to risk seeking re-election.
It's not that the province necessarily needs you to stick around. It's that you need not - dare not - stick it to Ontarians by saddling us with another political campaign at the provincial level, while federal leadership and election races are underway over the next few months.
At the very moment that our American neighbours are taking their best shots at us, in both tariffs and tirades, the federal government is now paralyzed by Justin Trudeau's resignation odyssey. An early election call in Ontario would compound the uncertainty amid the upheaval of a trade war.
A newly empowered president Donald Trump would be dealing not only with a disembowelled prime minister on Parliament Hill, but a disempowered government at Queen's Park. With America on the attack, we'd be defended by a lame duck PM in Ottawa and a caretaker premier in Ontario - leaving the country's biggest and second-biggest governments in limbo while Ford cavorts on the campaign trail.
Yet still, the premier refuses to rule out an early election.
Ford persists in ruminating about going to the polls prematurely so as to lock in his current lead in public opinion polls, lest he lose ground in any future economic downturn or face awkward questions from a police probe into his Greenbelt land swap fiasco. Since last spring, his Tories have obsessed over an early election footing that defies convention and, now, common sense.
Back then, when this early election notion first surfaced, a second Trump presidency seemed pure fantasy and the spectre of a tariff war seemed unfathomable. Today, more than a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote that is prescribed by Ontario's fixed election date law, Ford is still putting his personal ambition ahead of the tradition of four-year mandates.
Less than three years after winning the June, 2022 election, the premier keeps conflating campaigning with governing.
On the one hand, he is making the most of Trudeau's troubles by insinuating himself into the Canada-U.S. mix, describing his fellow premiers as critical players who demand a seat at the bilateral table. Yet in the same breath, Ford can't bring himself to declare, clearly, that this is no time to put his personal and political self-interest over the provincial and national interest.
It's easy to see why Ford is so invested in an election. It's not just the risk of more Greenbelt bad news, or the weak state of the political opposition.
Ontarians are about to receive $200 cheques in the mail as part of the premier's thinly veiled pre-election vote-buying scheme, at a cost of more than $3 billion to the deficit-ridden, heavily indebted provincial treasury. The premier had planned to cash in on the political dividends from that voter giveaway.
And yet, a truly responsible and prudent premier would tell reporters that he has instructed his campaign team to stand down until further notice, something along these (press) lines: 'I'm on the job, because I've got a job to do. I've got a massive majority mandate from voters that's valid through mid-2026, so the last thing I need to do is ask voters to repeat themselves. Therefore, we will not have a provincial vote until the lawful election date.'
But Ford won't say what needs to be said. Given the chance by reporters, repeatedly, to clear the air this week after Trudeau's announcement, the premier left the door wide open to more electoral tumult when Canada least needs it.
Oddly, he publicly warned the PM not to get distracted by anything as grubby as partisan politics. Indeed, the premiers will keep up the pressure to ensure that "as long as he's prime minister, his main focus is not on the Liberal party, is not on anything else - it should be, the next 14 days, making sure that we avoid these tariffs," Ford intoned.
But would Ford make the same public pledge not to focus solely on the Progressive Conservative party he leads in Ontario, especially from the campaign trail? The premier couldn't, wouldn't do it, retreating instead to platitudes:
"What I can tell you today is I'm 100 per cent focused on the tariffs," he began. "I can tell you, my eye hasn't been taken off the ball for a second here. We've been leading the way - all the premiers."
Ford likes to remind people that he holds the rotating chair of the premiers' association, implying this makes him indispensable, not to mention electable. But why add campaigning to the responsibilities of governing when Ottawa is already sidelined?
"This is when all Canadians step up, this is when our provincial and territorial leaders step up."
Step up - not step aside and onto the campaign trail. We don't need two of our governments in limbo while the U.S. government lashes out.
Time to clear the air, premier. Time to declare that you'll stick to the fixed election law - with a fixed focus on our economic challenges, not your political ambitions.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media outside his office at Queen's Park in Toronto on Dec. 11, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; Election law; Voters; Politics; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 197 of 500

New York City has imposed a congestion charge on drivers entering Manhattan. Should Toronto follow suit?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-york-city-has-imposed-congestion-charge-on/docview/3152189697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+York+City+has+imposed+a+congestion+charge+on+drivers+entering+Manhattan.+Should+Toronto+follow+suit%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A congestion charge, a long-debated idea in North America, has finally come into effect in New York City and is being praised by experts who say Toronto could benefit from a similar approach .
On Sunday, New York was the first U.S. city to adopt the system after years of studies and delays, joining the likes of London, Stockholm and Singapore which have seen lasting traffic reductions since implementing congestion charges.
The toll system, which is applied to the most congested parts of a city, is mean to reduce gridlock and pollution and raise revenue for public transit.
Toronto council once endorsed tolls on the Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway in 2016 to help improve gridlock in a city that has some of the worst traffic in the world. But the plan was scuttled by the Liberal government at the time. In the past September, the city's transportation department once again said a congestion charge on motorists is not an option on the table.
Despite the Ontario government's efforts to double down on car mobility, Canadian transportation experts say a congestion charge in Toronto could be a more efficient way to alleviate traffic jams.
"I do think Toronto is at the point where the congestion is so bad. And, the drag on our economy and our quality of life is getting to the point where this does have to be on the table, and has to be studied very carefully," said Matti Siemiatycki, a geography and planning professor at the University of Toronto.
How does New York's congestion charge work?
During peak traffic hours - between 5 a.m. and 9 p.m. on weekdays and on weekends between 9 a.m. and 9 p.m. - drivers of cars, SUVs, small vans and pickup trucks with an E-ZPass need to pay $9 (US) once per day to enter Manhattan south of Central Park. During off hours, the toll is $2.25 (US) for most vehicles.
Drivers without an E-ZPass will receive bills by mail and pay more: $13.50 (US) for peak hours and $3.30 (US) overnight.
When charging a price on the road, based on the laws of supply and demand, people will drive less and opt for alternative ways to travel to the city, said Siemiatycki.
Congestion has a price tag too, even though it is often not thought of in this way, people either pay in money, or in time and lost in economic productivity, he added. The latter was estimated at $11 billion annually in the GTA by a study in 2013.
Siemiatycki pointed to Highway 407 as an example of how road tolls work in Toronto and said no matter how much people dislike the cost, it ensures people get across the city in "a reliable fashion."
"This New York experiment, or an example, is so important because it has the potential to show what's possible across North America," he said.
Should Toronto impose the congestion charge?
While Jeff Casello applauded the congestion charge in New York as "a fantastic policy," the urban planning professor at the University of Waterloo said he is concerned about implementing a similar charge in Toronto when there "isn't any viable alternatives" for transportation.
"Imagine that there was a massive park and ride where you could park your car in a park-and-ride facility at Pearson Airport, for example, and then take the UP Express downtown. That's what we ought to be encouraging," he said.
Siemiatycki who echoed the sentiment said if the system is implemented, the city needs to have equity at its core and make sure the public transportation is in place.
He's optimistic that Toronto will see more alternative transportation options with the opening of rail transit lines like the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, Finch West LRT and the Ontario Line.
"The other point that often doesn't get talked about is, at the moment, it's almost virtually impossible to run an effective public transit system on those highways because they're so congested," he said.
The key to the congestion pricing system in New York and London is their ability to collect the revenue to improve public transit, while the tolls of Highway 407 are not used for that benefit, said Bruce Hellinga, a transportation engineering professor at the University of Waterloo.
He added that the cost of deploying technology to operate the tolling system should also be taken into consideration.
"It can be a good idea if implemented well. And I guess we're going to see what happens with the New York implementation," Hellinga said.
City and Province show no interest in pushing for a charge
Toronto's city council has considered the road levy as a potential revenue tool in to relieve the city's financial pressures. However, the city determined that road pricing policy involves high costs as it will require the city to partner with a technology company to implement the license plate capture system.
Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson from the Ministry of Transportation said in a statement that they will remain focused on building the critical infrastructure including allocating nearly $100 billion toward new roads and transit.
"Unlike the Liberals, we will never add a tax or toll to any road in Ontario," he said.
Siemiatycki also acknowledged that road pricing is an unpopular idea in suburban areas while taking off road tolls, expanding highway networks and cutting bike lanes have "an effective political wedge."
"We do have to acknowledge that the approach that they're following is popular. The problem is it hasn't been effective. I mean congestion has gotten worse during their six years in office," Siemiatycki said.
"It might be good policy, but it's not good politics," he said.
CAPTION: Cars drivealong the Brooklyn Bridgepreparing to cross into Manhattan on Jan. 06 in New York City. Congestion pricing began on Sunday.

Subject: Transportation planning; Public transportation; Tolls; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Pricing policies

Business indexing term: Subject: Pricing policies

Location: New York City New York; New York; United States--US; North America

Company / organization: Name: University of Waterloo; NAICS: 611310

Title: New York City has imposed a congestion charge on drivers entering Manhattan. Should Toronto follow suit?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Section: news/gta

Document 198 of 500

Doug Ford boosts Ontario's border security amid Donald Trump tariff threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-boosts-ontarios-border-security-amid/docview/3152189507/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+boosts+Ontario%27s+border+security+amid+Donald+Trump+tariff+threat&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Donald Trump threatens 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods unless the border with the U.S. is strengthened, Premier Doug Ford is enhancing security on the frontier.
"Ontario has been calling on the federal government to step up and address safety and security concerns at the border. We need to see words turned into visible action," Ford said Tuesday.
The premier's comments come as the U.S. president-elect is vowing to slap levies on Canadian and Mexican products after his Jan. 20 inauguration unless America's neighbours curb illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
"Ontario is stepping up with Operation Deterrence to crack down on illegal border crossings and illegal guns and drugs," said Ford, imploring Ottawa to also do more.
"A more co-ordinated Team Canada approach that includes more boots on the ground is the only way to detect, deter and disrupt illegal activity and ensure the safety and security of Canadian and American communities."
Ford will meet virtually with the other premiers Wednesday afternoon to further discuss steps to mitigate Trump's gambit.
Operation Deterrence includes 200 Ontario Provincial Police officers, who are working in partnership with the RCMP.
In the past five weeks, the OPP has already done more than 6,000 hours of "focused patrols to deter, detect and disrupt illegal border activity using fixed-wing aircraft, helicopters, remotely piloted aircraft, boats, off-road vehicles, patrol vehicles and foot patrol," the province said.
As well, the government said "Operation Deterrence activity will be enhanced through the use of the emergency response team, front-line officer and additional specialty resources, including canine units, commercial motor vehicle inspections and criminal investigators."
The initiative is above and beyond what is already being done at Ontario's 14 official U.S. border crossings by the Canada Border Services Agency.
In the wake of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announcing his resignation Monday, Ford has been imploring Ottawa to remain focused on the Trump tariff threat.
Speaking to reporters after Trudeau told the nation he would prorogue Parliament and step down after nine years as prime minister, the premier said the federal uncertainty is troubling.
"We need all hands on deck. We need the prime minister to continue doing his job. Prorogation doesn't mean vacation," said Ford.
"God forbid president-elect Trump gets in there and puts tariffs. We have to retaliate. We have to retaliate hard against our friends south of the border, which is unfortunate."
Since the incoming president's Nov. 25 tariff threat against Canada and Mexico, Ford, who is chair of the premiers' Council of the Federation, has been more aggressive than Trudeau or any federal leader.
On Dec. 18, the premier warned Ontario could cut off electricity exports flowing stateside in retaliation for any tariffs on trade.
With Trump repeatedly trolling Canadians on social media about annexing this country as a "51st state" and referring to the prime minister as the "governor of the great state of Canada," Ford stressed that joke isn't funny.
"To the president, I'll make him a counter-offer. How about if we buy Alaska and we'll throw in Minnesota or Minneapolis at the same time," the premier said Monday.
"It's not realistic. I know he likes to make these comments, he likes joking around, (but) I take that seriously," he said.
"He may be joking, but under my watch, that will never, ever happen."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media outside of his office in the Queens Park Legislature, in Toronto on Dec. 11, 2024.

Emergency room issues are not the patients' responsibility

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/emergency-room-issues-are-not-patients/docview/3152189500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Emergency+room+issues+are+not+the+patients%27+responsibility&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Crowded ERs are not the patients' fault. Stop the blame gameQuebec emergency rooms reporting major overcrowding, some at more than 200% capacity, Jan. 3
It was disappointing to read the comments of a Montreal emergency physician who seemed to suggest emergency room crowding in Montreal was largely on the basis of a lack of informed choice by patients.
Regardless of the season, Quebec ERs are always dangerously overcrowded and concerningly dysfunctional, and patient choice has little to do with it.
Consecutive Quebec governments have consistently maintained crowding is caused by inappropriate overutilization by patients with nonurgent problems.
This is a myth.
The reality is that congested ERs are the result of an inability to transfer, in a timely manner, admitted patients to the wards. Thus, admitted patients languish on ER stretchers for hours or days, blocking access to those in the waiting room or in the back of am ambulance.
A crowded ER is a result of a crowded hospital.
This blaming and shaming of patients must end.
Alan Drummond, Perth, Ont.
Berardinetti's story a cautionary talePoliticians of all political stripes raise $25,000 to move former MPP Lorenzo Berardinetti out of homeless shelter, Jan. 3
The story of former MPP Lorenzo Berardinetti who became homeless shows why the Ontario government should reinstate a pension plan for MPPs.
Ontarians want the best people to go into politics and become the leaders of our province. Politics should not only be for wealthy people.
A person who becomes an MPP should know that when they leave politics they will have the certainty of a pension.
Otherwise, we may be discouraging good people from going into politics.
Elliott Katz, Toronto
Politicians smell blood, and it's patheticMark Carney calls dozens of Liberal MPs as he considers leadership bid, Jan. 3
As much as I dislike Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, I find this 'working the phones' by Chrystia Freeland and Mark Carney, among others, ratherdisgusting.
When the going was good, Freeland happily enabled Trudeau at every turn. However, when he turned on her, she wrote a resignation letter purporting that she alone cared about Canada and Canadians. Now, she's calling MPs, presumably to gauge and build support for a leadership run.
It's obvious she only cares about getting power.
Trudeau hasn't even left the building yet, but she and some others can't wait.
I almost feel sorry for Trudeau.
Igea Khan, Toronto
Mark Carney represents the kind of leader Canada no longer needs
Mark Carney's potential bid for the Liberal leadership demands scrutiny of his economic legacy.
While his crisis-era policies at the Bank of Canada maintained market stability in 2008, they accelerated wealth inequality by inflating asset prices that primarily benefited the already wealthy while eroding living standards for ordinary Canadians.
Canada's 2025 challenges (stagnant productivity, severe housing shortages, and declining real wages) require solutions fundamentally different from Carney's market-stability playbook. His policy instincts, shaped by managing financial crises, risk further entrenching the economic problems facing everyday Canadians.
We need leadership focused on boosting real productivity, dramatically increasing housing supply, and improving living standards - not someone whose toolkit was forged in crisis management. The Liberal Party should seek a leader who recognizes that stable markets mean little when Canadians can't afford homes, face declining purchasing power and struggle with stagnant wages.
Canadians deserve a real alternative to Pierre Poilievre's empty and pandering populist rhetoric, not a return to policies that have widened inequality. Across Western democracies, we've seen the same pattern: economic policies that pump up asset values for the wealthy while working families fall behind.
Canada needs a leader with fresh solutions who can break this cycle and put us on a path to genuine economic renewal.
D. Renton, Fergus, Ont.
Paying taxes to buy Canadian flag is ludicrous
I love seeing my Canadian flag flying proudly in the front and rear of my house. Unfortunately, due to their exorbitant cost, I fly them only during the warm summer months.
You may ask why, and I will tell you I have a longstanding hatred of the governments who force manufacturers to tax the flag.
I believe more Canadians would fly the Canadian flag if the taxes on them were permanently removed.
Mario C. Bonifacio, Wasaga Beach, Ont.
Trump epitomizes how society has long ago lost its wayDonald Trump has a new best friend; Justin Trudeau doesn't have any friends left, Jan. 3
Such a witty column, and so true.
Money should not be worshiped; it should not be the preeminent goal of individuals, and certainly not of society.
Trump, Trudeau, Musk...it is obvious these 'leaders' have a broken view of society. The main objective of power must not be more power. The main objective of money should not be more money, more conspicuous consumption.
Did we learn nothing from our religions; from thousands of years of community? Parables are simple to understand. These men, so successful in many ways, can only fake it when it comes to real charity, which is pure love.
Our societies' predominant destiny continues to be the accumulation of money, power and the destruction of charity. This is what these men embody.
Do we no longer care about charity?
Jay Butler, Etobicoke
Tax avoidance the real issue when it comes to exorbitant CEO compensationCanada's 100 highest-paid CEOs earned $13.2 million on average in 2023: report, Jan. 2
The average Canadian worker does not have access to the income tax avoidance strategies these CEO's are using to minimize their income tax. Their total compensation should be treated as employment income and taxed as such - like the earnings of the average worker.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
CAPTION: A letter writer states governments and hospitals need to stop blaming patients for the inefficiencies found in so many hospital emergency rooms.

Document 200 of 500

Stepping down was the only way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stepping-down-was-only-way/docview/3152067993/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stepping+down+was+the+only+way&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No one is surprised today that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has stepped down. Some are chagrined that it took so long. Some will be angry for the same reason. Some will gloat that their attacks have brought down the sitting prime minister. But no one should be surprised.
The path to this moment has been long and tortured. For a year there have been voices in the commentariat sounding the death knell of his term in office - calling on him to step aside. Pollsters have been breathless in their assessments of who could carry the Liberal Party of Canada to something less than the devastating defeat that would be a sure thing with Trudeau at the helm.
Among Liberals there has been hand-wringing and confusion. Those of us who believe in what Trudeau and his government have accomplished have wanted to continue to support his vision. With all his flaws, and as with most of us human creatures, there are flaws, Trudeau's view of the world, his optimism and his belief in the possibility of a country that is inclusive and caring stands in stark contrast to the mean, fear-mongering often dishonest, if not delusional rhetoric of Pierre Poilievre. But that support for Trudeau's leadership has been harder and harder to sustain in the face of such overwhelming public antipathy.
When Chrystia Freeland broke with the government, it was over for his leadership. The one thing this prime minister, or any leader, cannot survive is the loss of support of his team. He had lost the support of his most trusted colleagues.
I have really only known Trudeau personally since 2013 when we both became leaders of our parties. But I feel like I have known him much longer. I was 18 years old when Justin was born that Christmas Day in 1971. He belonged to the country as the newborn son of the prime minister and his birth was cause for happy celebration.
I believe that Trudeau lived his entire life with a sense of obligation to do what he could to make the country better than it was and to make the lives of Canadians healthier and happier. I believe he entered politics, not because he felt entitled to be prime minister, although he would have understood that job more completely than the rest of us, but because he really believed he could use his experience, his positive energy and yes, his name to do some good.
There is no shortage of cynicism about the man at this moment and for some time. But when I heard the clip of him saying to his colleagues and staff at their December holiday party that he loved them, I took it at face value. These are people he has worked closely with. They have sat with him in the House of Commons as he has been attacked and hammered by the Opposition every day for months. Every commentator across the country, including the Liberals in that category, has been relentlessly critical. These people have been his team and there is affection mingled with pain.
Once he lost their support, he had no choice. He had to step down.
Do I wish for Trudeau, the man, that he had left earlier before he had to face the public humiliation of losing his team? Sure, but he had to make this decision himself. He had the right to leave on his own terms and these are the terms he chose.
Trudeau changed forever the nature of the relationship between Indigenous and non-Indigenous people in Canada. He ended the debate about national child care. He moved boldly on climate action early in his term. He kept the vision of green investment real bringing recalcitrant provinces along. He lifted millions of children out of poverty. There is a long list of actions taken by Trudeau's government that have had positive, life-changing impacts on the lives of Canadians. None of them is perfect but they are life-affirming, optimistic endeavours.
His desire to stay in the saddle to fight the bleak vision of Canada offered by Poilievre was understandable. He knows better than anyone what is at risk any semblance of climate action, a child-care program that is in the midst of being implemented, a dental program. They are low-hanging fruit and are the things I worry about.
My circumstance in 2018 when we lost so badly in Ontario was different, of course. I was at the end of only my first term but our government had been in office for 15 years. When I stepped down from leadership of the party on the night of the election, there was no choice then, either.
None of that softened the feeling on my part of having let my team down. Nor did it take away my concern about what was in store for Ontario.
Today is a rough day for Trudeau, for his children and family and for his team. Those who have been cheering on his demise, will have a good day, no doubt, but in my experience, it is wise to be measured in both the jubilance in victory and the despair in loss. Neither lasts forever.
Kathleen Wynne was Ontario's first woman premier and leader of the province's Liberal party from 2013 to 2018.
CREDIT: Kathleen Wynne Special to the Star Kathleen Wynne was Ontario's first woman premier and leader of the province's Liberal party from 2013 to 2018.;

Document 201 of 500

What to expect from 2025's CES showcase: Just about every major exhibitor will be talking AI at annual Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-expect-2025s-ces-showcase/docview/3152067924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+to+expect+from+2025%27s+CES+showcase%3A+Just+about+every+major+exhibitor+will+be+talking+AI+at+annual+Consumer+Electronics+Show+in+Las+Vegas&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: CES, the annual trade show of all things tech, is upon us.
The multi-day event, organized by the Consumer Technology Association, kicks off this week in Las Vegas - where swaths of the latest gadgets and advances across industries like personal tech, transportation, health care, sustainability and more are set to be on display. And, like last year, artificial intelligence is everywhere you look.
CES 2024 saw more than 138,000 attendees, according to the CTA, and organizers expect to see at least that amount again for this year's show. More than 4,500 exhibitors, including 1,400 startups, are also anticipated across 2.5 million net square feet of floor space this week.
"These are the people getting together, focusing on solving some of the world's biggest problems - and magic occurs," Gary Shapiro, CEO and vice-chair of the CTA, told the Associated Press ahead of this week's show.
The AP spoke with Shapiro about what to expect for CES 2025:
CES 2025 is here. What are the main themes we can expect this week?
CES 2025 will be an exceptional leap forward in terms of innovation and technology, with a focus on a variety of themes important to the future of humanity. Over 4,000 companies will be here, on literally miles and miles of exhibit space. And they'll be showing solutions to fundamental human problems - like access to food, clean air, clean water and mobility, as well as health-care technology.
We also have newer categories. We're seeing more technology for the disability community, for example. Products that can serve people with disabilities had a large presence last year, and we expect that to be even bigger this year. Overall, the show is serving humanity and using technology and innovation to fill in gaps where we need a lot more work.
AI is everywhere again this year. Are there any specific trends we should keep an eye out for?
Generative AI is affecting virtually every area. Just about every major exhibitor will be talking about AI in one form or another at CES this week. For businesses, the focus is more on enterprise and productivity. But for individuals, it's about personalization.
We'll see exhibitors, such as PC companies, focusing on personalization in new products for the PC and laptop marketplace. And then there's AI being used in mobility - and not just in features for cars and self-driving. It's for all sorts of vehicles, including agricultural products from companies like Caterpillar and John Deere.
What kind of oversight is there for the gadgets and products we'll see at CES this week?
The industry representing the companies involved welcomes government regulation. The government's job is to say this is what is unsafe. And so the companies know what is legal and what is not. At the same time, a big challenge is if government stops innovation - so the question is for every country is finding that balance.
How could policies from the incoming Trump administration - like proposed tariffs on foreign goods - impact consumer products like those seen at CES?
We expect the Trump administration to take a much more pro-business approach that favours innovation, including a focus on all sorts of energy sources, which is very important. But tariffs are taxes. And we know that they are inflationary and paid for by consumers.
If we go forward with the proposals that the president-elect has indicated he may put in place, there would be severe impacts on the prosperity of the U.S. It depends on how exactly these tariffs are adopted but under some of the proposals, we estimate a range of a 30 to 50 per cent price increases for basic products like PCs, cellphones, laptops, tablets, smartwatches and all sorts of different products that consumers get a great value on today. That means that fewer people will be able to access these products. And, of course, other countries may then retaliate against U.S. exports. We don't want to go in that direction.
Are there any increased safety precautions CES is taking following last week's Cybertruck explosion?
CES has always focused on safety. There's a lot of things we can't talk about, but suffice it to say, we work with federal, state, local and facility officials for lots of planning. We've made some modest changes given the recent events, but we're always trying to do it better.
CES also arrives a month after the fatal shooting of UnitedHealthcare's CEO. Have companies made any additional security requests for their attending executives?
I think companies are excited to come here. And any requests we've gotten have been extremely isolated.
Having top executives here, especially after COVID, is very special. People are focused on coming to Las Vegas and having this opportunity to meet face-to-face and finding the serendipity and discovery that happens when you're making these business connections in person. That's why trade shows like CES and others are such important events for the world.
The Associated Press
CAPTION: Companies like John Deere and Caterpillar will be detailing new artificial intelligence features coming to their agricultural vehicles atthis year's Consumer Electronics Show.Abbie ParrThe Associated Press
CREDIT: Wyatte Grantham-philips The Associated Press

Document 202 of 500

Trudeau to resign: Will step down as PM after Liberals choose new leader 'I cannot be the best option' to ?ght next election, he says Parliament prorogued, resumes sitting March 24

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-resign/docview/3152067921/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+to+resign%3A+Will+step+down+as+PM+after+Liberals+choose+new+leader+%27I+cannot+be+the+best+option%27+to+%3Fght+next+election%2C+he+says+Parliament+prorogued%2C+resumes+sitting+March+24&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Embattled by internal party strife and under attack by political opponents, Justin Trudeau told Canadians Monday he will resign as prime minister and Liberal leader after the party chooses his successor sometime in the next few months.
Trudeau, in a nationally televised news conference, said that although his instinct was to stay on in the face of what he called a "critical moment" for the country, the internal battles in his own party that had flared into a crisis in the past three weeks made that impossible.
"My friends, as you all know, I am a fighter. Every bone in my body has always told me to fight because I care deeply about Canadians. I care deeply about this country," he said.
Yet he realized he could no longer continue, telling his family Sunday night he would step down, and announcing Monday in a sombre tone: "I intend to resign as party leader, as prime minister after the party selects its next leader through a robust nationwide competitive process."
That race unofficially kicked off with former MP Frank Bayliss confirming he intends to run and former central banker Mark Carney telling the Star he is "encouraged" by calls to jump in and is "considering this decision closely with my family over the coming few days."
For now, Trudeau will lead the government for another two-and-a-half months or more, as Donald Trump assumes the U.S. presidency with threats of crippling tariffs on all Canadian products.
Trudeau met with Gov. Gen. Mary Simon early Monday morning and received her approval to prorogue - or suspend - Parliament until March 24, citing the inability to conduct normal business because of Conservative filibustering tactics, he told reporters.
He rejected suggestions that it was anti-democratic or unfair not to call an immediate election, saying Parliament needs a "reset."
But he also acknowledged the obvious: so does the Liberal party.
Trudeau has faced a growing caucus mutiny since the Liberals lost key byelections in the past six months, and his ill-fated decision to fire Chrystia Freeland as finance minister in December triggered a flood of new calls by dozens of Liberal MPs for him to step down.
"This country deserves a real choice in the next election, and it has become clear to me that if I am having to fight internal battles, I cannot be the best option in that election," he said.
In response to Trudeau's announcement, Trump took to his social media platform Truth Social to taunt the prime minister and Canadians, saying his 25 per cent tariff threat and vow to ensure Canada paid a price to the U.S. drove Trudeau to act.
"Justin Trudeau knew this, and resigned," Trump wrote, repeating his claim that Canada would be better off as a 51st state. "If Canada merged with the U.S., there would be no Tariffs, taxes would go way down, and they would be TOTALLY SECURE from the threat of the Russian and Chinese Ships that are constantly surrounding them."
Trudeau declined to explain why he had wanted to shift Freeland out of finance last month, saying only that she rejected his appeal to remain as deputy prime minister and take on a key role in charge of the Canada-U.S. file, and wouldn't dish on their "private" conversations.
Her dramatic departure deeply shook Trudeau to the point where he had wanted to step down that week but, according to two sources with knowledge of the discussions speaking on condition of anonymity, was persuaded not to take any immediate decision about his future and take time to reflect over the holidays.
In the end, Trudeau came to the same conclusion: that his time was up.
He will not ask for an interim leader to be installed, saying prorogation allows "time for the temperature to come down, for people to have a fresh start in Parliament and to be able to navigate through these complex times both domestically and internationally."
It is not yet clear when a vote to replace him will take place, nor whether any of his current cabinet ministers on the front lines of the Canada-U.S. tensions will have to step aside if they want to vie for the leadership.
Liberal party sources say that a leadership election committee to be named this week by the party's board has up to 26 days to set rules for a race, including whether sitting cabinet ministers can compete. The prime minister also can have a say in that, but on Monday he deferred to the party.
The ultimate leadership selection date depends on several factors, including venue availability. The party has discretion to adapt other rules affecting timing, sources said.
Exiting more than 15 minutes late for his announcement in front of cameras - and several months too late in the eyes of his critics - Trudeau quipped that he'd "wing it" as his speech notes flew off a podium outside his home at Rideau Cottage. It was the same spot where he used to address the nation during the COVID-19 pandemic back when his approval ratings soared.
On this bitterly cold Ottawa day, a teleprompter rolled out his script for why he'd asked for the minority Parliament to be suspended until a new Liberal leader is picked.
The House of Commons has moved little legislation forward in months due to a privilege debate that ground almost all other business to a halt. Trudeau said it was time to break the logjam.
"Despite best efforts to work through it, Parliament has been paralyzed for months after what has been the longest session of a minority Parliament in Canadian history," he said.
Removing him from the equation should reduce the polarization in Canadian politics, Trudeau added. He insisted Canadians will see that the Conservative plan for Canada is a mistake.
"Pierre Poilievre's vision for this country is not the right one for Canadians," he said. "We need an ambitious, optimistic view of the future, and Pierre Poilievre is not offering that."
Would-be contenders for Trudeau's job including Freeland, Carney and former B.C. premier Christy Clark took to social media to issue perfunctory thanks to Trudeau for his "service" to the country and Canadians. "I wish him and his family the very best," Freeland posted on X.
Trudeau's other political rivals reacted quickly.
Poilievre said in a recorded video and statement that there should be an immediate election, charging that "Liberals want to protect their pensions and pay cheques by sweeping their hated leader under the rug months before an election to trick you, and then do it all over again."
Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet said while it is not a bad idea to suspend Parliament during a Liberal leadership race, the country needs an election as soon as possible. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said the Liberals do not deserve another chance at governing, no matter who their leader is.
"They should get fired," Singh said.
Only Green Party Leader Elizabeth May, who once sat with Trudeau on the opposition backbenches, spoke kindly and personally, saying Trudeau struggled with the decision to contest the party leadership in 2013 "weighing how it might impact his personal life. He was being recruited as someone who could rescue the Liberals" at a time when a majority win "was not a likelihood. If anyone knew what it would mean for his children if he were to become PM - as the child of a PM - Justin Trudeau knew."
Premier Doug Ford, responding to Trudeau's news, said in the next two weeks before Trump's tariffs turn into a reality, "the federal government needs to do everything humanly possible to avoid these tariffs, including by doing more to secure our border and offering a credible plan to invest more in Canada's military to meet and exceed our NATO spending commitments."
The Liberal government will continue to direct Canada's response to Trump.
For now, prorogation means Parliament will not return as scheduled at the end of January, and kills any legislation currently before the House of Commons. It will force the government to reopen March 24 with a throne speech when the members return, which will require a confidence vote in the days that follow in the Commons.
Assessing his own legacy, Trudeau said he regretted he had not brought in electoral reform, as promised in 2015. Yet he believes he leaves with a solid record of helping the middle class.
"We reduced their taxes. We increased the benefits to families. We made sure the economy was focused on working for everyone and not just a few," he said. "That has dropped poverty rates in Canada, that has brought more people into the workforce, that has moved us forward on reconciliation."
Politically, Trudeau is leaving his party with the Liberals more than 20 points behind the Conservatives in almost all opinion polls.
Pressed to say whether he thought another Liberal leader can succeed in defeating Poilievre in the next campaign, Trudeau said "absolutely," a new Liberal leader will have an opportunity to turn the party's fortunes around.
"A new prime minister and leader of the Liberal party will carry its values and ideals into that next election. I'm excited to see the process unfold in the months ahead."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles and Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star

Document 203 of 500

Singh, Poilievre poised for election: Party leaders say next Liberal leader will bear burden of Trudeau's legacy of bad public policy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-poilievre-poised-election/docview/3152067911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh%2C+Poilievre+poised+for+election%3A+Party+leaders+say+next+Liberal+leader+will+bear+burden+of+Trudeau%27s+legacy+of+bad+public+policy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is no new leader that could save the Liberal minority government from an election this spring, opposition parties said Monday, as each made the case that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's resignation does nothing to clear the stink of his unpopularity from those who will now battle to replace him.
Almost immediately after Trudeau announced his intention to step down as prime minister on Monday, opposition parties argued that any Liberal leader who takes his place will bear the burden of what they argue is a legacy of bad public policy.
Leaders of the federal Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and New Democratic Party made clear that they want an election as soon as possible. Since Gov. Gen. Mary Simon agreed to Trudeau's request Monday to suspend Parliament until March 24, the opposition parties will have their next chance to topple the Liberal government early this spring.
"What has really changed?" asked Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, speaking in a video that was posted online Monday morning.
"Every Liberal MP in power today, and every potential Liberal leadership contender fighting for the top job, helped Justin Trudeau break the country over the last nine years," he said.
"Their only objection is (Trudeau) is no longer popular enough to win an election and keep them in power," Poilievre continued. "They want to protect their pensions and paycheque by sweeping their hated leader under the rug months before an election to trick you and then do it all over again."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh similarly painted all Liberals with the Trudeau brush. At a virtual press conference, Singh repeatedly ruled out working with a new Liberal leader, as he did with Trudeau in a parliamentary alliance that made progress on left-wing priorities like public dental care and pharmacare coverage for diabetes medication and birth control.
Singh said his NDP will vote at the first chance to declare non-confidence in the Liberal government and try to trigger an election, which he framed as a contest between his New Democrats' "movement of working-class people" and a Conservative party keen to cut government programs.
"It does not matter who is chosen as the next Liberal leader. They have let you down. They do not deserve another chance," Singh said. "So as soon as there is a confidence vote, New Democrats will be voting against the government, and then we will have an election."
Yves-François Blanchet, the Bloc leader, also dismissed the idea that his separatist Québec party would work with Trudeau's successor. Calling the Liberal government's policies "catastrophic," Blanchet argued Trudeau's administration has centralized power in Ottawa, opposed Quebec state secularism, failed to adequately support the French language and irresponsibly favoured multiculturalism in a way that has allowed hate speech, among other things.
Blanchet said anyone in the Liberal party is linked to this political program, and that the government can't remake itself in a matter of weeks under a new leader ahead of an election he expects to start shortly after Parliament returns near the end of March.
"It's the same organization, the same values, the same ideology," Blanchet said of the Liberal party.
For more than a year, national opinion polls have shown Poilievre's Conservatives hold a commanding lead in voting intentions, with the Liberals sliding down into numbers that would cause them to lose a huge number of seats. But even before Trudeau announced his intention to resign on Monday, the Conservatives have tried to tie possible successors to Trudeau's policies. The Conservatives started calling Mark Carney - the former central banker in Canada and the United Kingdom whom a source told the Star is considering a leadership run - "Carbon Tax Carney."
That's a reference to the current Liberal government's carbon tax-and-rebate policy, which the Conservatives promise to scrap and attack as a wrong-headed climate policy that hikes costs without meaningfully cutting greenhouse gas emissions.
A recent study published by the Institute for Research on Public Policy found carbon pricing has had a "minimal" impact on inflation, while the Canadian Climate Institute has predicted consumer carbon pricing could account for between eight and 14 per cent of Canada's projected emissions reductions by 2030.
Jason Lietaer, the president of the strategic communications firm Enterprise Canada who worked for former prime minister Stephen Harper, said most Conservatives would rather face Trudeau in the next election. But he predicted Poilievre's party will be able to link any potential Liberal successor - including those who aren't serving in his government, like Carney and former British Columbia premier Christy Clark, who is also said to be mulling a run - to Trudeau's legacy in a way that benefits the Conservatives' chances in the next election.
"People aren't just tired of Trudeau. They are tired of the state of Canada" under his government, Lietaer said.
Meanwhile, the NDP sees an opportunity in Trudeau's departure - especially if he is replaced by a leader who tries to swing the Liberal party toward the right, said a senior New Democrat insider. Some Liberals have told the Star the party became damagingly associated with leftist politics under Trudeau. And the NDP believes leadership candidates might stress more fiscal conservatism, after Chrystia Freeland resigned as finance minister last month and suggested she was at odds with Trudeau over their government's spending levels, which blew past their own targets to restrain last year's budget deficit by more than $20 billion.
Freeland has also been calling MPs and is interested in a potential leadership run, sources told the Star last week.
Any rightward shift could open up progressive votes for the NDP, said the insider, who spoke on condition of anonymity.
Whatever the case, though, the source added that all parties are now acting like an election is in the offing, with a possible voting day in early or mid-May if the government falls when Parliament resumes.
"We're in a campaign now," they said.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and his Bloc Québécois and Conservative party counterparts made clear Monday that they want a federal election as soon as possible.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star
