Document 215 of 500

Kathleen Wynne: Justin Trudeau had no choice but to leave. I can understand the extraordinary weight of knowing that

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kathleen-wynne-justin-trudeau-had-no-choice-leave/docview/3151865623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kathleen+Wynne%3A+Justin+Trudeau+had+no+choice+but+to+leave.+I+can+understand+the+extraordinary+weight+of+knowing+that&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No one is surprised today that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has stepped down. Some are chagrined that it took so long. Some will be angry for the same reason. Some will gloat that their attacks have brought down the sitting Prime Minister. But no one should be surprised.
The path to this moment has been long and tortured. For a year there have been voices in the commentariat sounding the death knell of his term in office - calling on him to step aside. Pollsters have been breathless in their assessments of who could carry the Liberal Party of Canada to something less than the devastating defeat that would be a sure thing with Trudeau at the helm.
Among Liberals there has been handwringing and confusion. Those of us who believe in what Trudeau and his government have accomplished have wanted to continue to support his vision. With all his flaws, and as with most of us human creatures, there are flaws, Trudeau's view of the world, his optimism and his belief in the possibility of a country that is inclusive and caring stands in stark contrast to the mean, fear-mongering often dishonest, if not delusional rhetoric of Pierre Poilievre. But that support for Trudeau's leadership has been harder and harder to sustain in the face of such overwhelming public antipathy.
When Chrystia Freeland broke with the government, it was over for his leadership. The one thing this Prime Minister, or any leader, cannot survive is the loss of support of his team. He had lost the support of his most trusted colleagues.
I have really only known Trudeau personally since 2013 when we both became leaders of our parties. But I feel like I have known him much longer. I was 18 years old when Justin was born that Christmas Day in 1971. He belonged to the country as the newborn son of the Prime Minister and his birth was cause for happy celebration.
I believe that Trudeau lived his entire life with a sense of obligation to do what he could to make the country better than it was and to make the lives of Canadians healthier and happier. I believe he entered politics, not because he felt entitled to be Prime Minister, although he would have understood that job more completely than the rest of us, but because he really believed he could use his experience, his positive energy and yes, his name to do some good.
There is no shortage of cynicism about the man at this moment and for some time. But when I heard the clip of him saying to his colleagues and staff at their December holiday party that he loved them, I took it at face value. These are people he has worked closely with. They have sat with him in the House of Commons as he has been attacked and hammered by the Opposition every day for months. Every commentator across the country, including the Liberals in that category, has been relentlessly critical. These people have been his team and there is affection mingled with pain.
Once he lost their support, he had no choice. He had to step down.
Do I wish for Justin Trudeau, the man, that he had left earlier before he had to face the public humiliation of losing his team? Sure, but he had to make this decision himself. He had the right to leave on his own terms and these are the terms he chose.
Justin Trudeau changed forever the nature of the relationship between Indigenous and non-Indigenous people in Canada. He ended the debate about national child care. He moved boldly on climate action early in his term. He kept the vision of green investment real bringing recalcitrant provinces along. He lifted millions of children out of poverty. There is a long list of actions taken by Trudeau's government that have had positive, life-changing impacts on the lives of Canadians. None of them is perfect but they are life-affirming, optimistic endeavours.
His desire to stay in the saddle to fight the bleak vision of Canada offered by Pierre Poilievre was understandable. He knows better than anyone what is at risk any semblance of climate action, a child-care program that is in the midst of being implemented, a dental program. They are low-hanging fruit and are the things I worry about.
My circumstance in 2018 when we lost so badly in Ontario was different, of course. I was at the end of only my first term but our government had been in office for 15 years. When I stepped down from leadership of the Party on the night of the election, there was no choice then, either.
None of that softened the feeling on my part of having let my team down. Nor did it take away my concern about what was in store for Ontario.
Today is a rough day for Justin Trudeau, for his children and family and for his team. Those who have been cheering on his demise, will have a good day, no doubt, but in my experience, it is wise to be measured in both the jubilance in victory and the despair in loss. Neither lasts forever.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and then Ontario Liberal Premier Kathleen Wynne.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has announced he is resigning.
CREDIT: Kathleen Wynne, Special to the Star

Subject: Leadership; Climate action; Prime ministers

What will the world look like for babies born in 2025 and after, Jan. 1 Canada has win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump, Dec. 30

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3151832666/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Canada%27s+top+10+paid+CEOs+took+home+a+combined+%24314+million+in+2023.+Here%27s+a+list+of+who+got+what%2C+Jan.+2+Trudeau%27s+chosen+female+ministers+have+failed+him%2C+Jan.+3+Politicians+of+all+political+stripes+raise+%2425%2C000+to+move+former+MPP+Lorenzo+Berardinetti+out+of+homeless+shelter%2C+Jan.+2+Purpose+of+speed+cameras+is+to+save+lives%2C+Jan.+3+Generation+Beta+starts+today.+What+will+the+world+look+like+for+babies+born+in+2025+and+after%2C+Jan.+1+Canada+has+win-win+deals+it+can+make+to+satisfy+Donald+Trump%2C+Dec.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Still no women among 10 highest paid CEOs
Canada's top 10 paid CEOs took home a combined $314 million in 2023. Here's a list of who got what, Jan. 2
I read with interest your story about the top 10 highest-paid Canadian CEOs and what they are paid in relation to us Joes and Janes of the world. One quote that interested me was this one, "It's hard to conceive of income gaps that large," by economist David Macdonald. What bugs me more is the fact women aren't represented. Let's have that important conversation about pay inequality when it comes to men and women in those upper echelon positions of influence and power. Globally, women make up one tenth of global CEOs and there is a lack of leadership roles attained by women in the workforce and in politics as well. The classic phrase, "It's lonely at the top" could apply to women who have attained high level positions and yet, the pressures on them to perform are much greater than their male counterparts. Women know the old saying, "You have to be twice as good to be recognized as half as good," still applies. We have come a long way, but we continue to have a long way to go when not one of the top 10 CEOs in the country is a woman. I have no doubt there are hundreds of capable women who are qualified to hold those top-level positions.
Sue Nielsen, Cobalt, Ont.
Women need to stop being our own worst enemy
Trudeau's chosen female ministers have failed him, Jan. 3
I want to thank the letter writer for reminding all women that they should button their lips and take it because they are always to blame when political relationships go south. Of course, none of the male members with portfolios in the Liberal government exited their posts with anything but a stellar record of achievement and a thank you from the grand pooh-bah. It's about time that women call out misogynistic behaviours and beliefs from within our gender. We need to stop being our own worst enemies and take some responsibility for our struggles to break through the glass ceiling - unless we think our place still belongs in the home.
Julia Bowkun, Toronto
Fix the root cause
of Berardinetti's predicament
Politicians of all political stripes raise $25,000 to move former MPP Lorenzo Berardinetti out of homeless shelter, Jan. 2
I'm really happy that Lorenzo Berardinetti's former colleagues are helping him raise money to get him on his feet again. However, just mentioning how a safety net for MPPs is needed seems tone deaf in the current climate. Perhaps our politicians should wake up about the policies they make for everyone, not just their own members. We have no rent control policies that have any teeth nowadays and the landlord and tenant board is woefully understaffed as it takes years to get a dispute looked into. Meanwhile, Premier Doug Ford keeps going on about building more homes, but the majority of homes being built in our area are not affordable. This is also the same government that stopped the increases to the minimum wage when he first got into power. So, yes, it is lovely that they are helping out Berardinetti, well done. And he may be able to get to work as a lawyer. But now get behind fixing what caused him and others like him to get into this position in the first place.
Mary Sullivan, Lindsay, Ont.
Disdain for speeding a myth
Purpose of speed cameras is to save lives, Jan. 3
Let's be honest and admit the purpose of speed cameras is not to save lives. If society truly wanted to save lives, we would do speed enforcement with regular speeding tickets where you lose points and ultimately your driving privileges, not just your money. In fact, if society wants to stop speeding we could legislate putting speed governors - a device that controls a vehicle's speed to prevent it from exceeding a certain limit - in all new cars and make speeding impossible. How well do you think those cars would sell? In reality society's disdain for speeding is a myth. What we really want stopped is excessive speeding by others. One common cause of road rage is a car ahead obeying the speed limit in a non-passing environment. Contrary to this constant diatribe against speeding, stopping all speeding would upset most drivers, the police, carmakers and governments who receive revenue from speeding tickets. The Star should do a survey and determine how many drivers would support speed governors on all new cars. I bet the war against speeding will turn out to be a charade.
Steen I. Petersen, Nanaimo, B.C.
Canada can't be so casual about so-called joke
Generation Beta starts today. What will the world look like for babies born in 2025 and after, Jan. 1
It is shocking to see a Star reporter parroting the Donald Trump line by saying our gen Beta kids will potentially grow up in a world where Canada is the 51st state of America. What, we just hand over the country to the U.S., based on a late-night tweet? Canada cannot afford to be so casual about this so-called joke. Time for both our media industry and our government leaders to make clear our sovereignty is not up for debate.
E.A. Robson, Tillsonburg, Ont.
President-elect doesn't live in a win-win world
Canada has win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump, Dec. 30
Mark McQueen has his facts right, but he has misdiagnosed the man. Donald Trump does not want win-win deals. In Trump's world there are winners and losers. He will be a winner at any cost. Look at the trail of more than 3,000 lawsuits he pursued against people who entered into good-faith contracts. With enough money, he can subvert the legal system to force the "little guy" to give in to the rich. He did again by making ABC capitulate on a ridiculous charge against George Stephanopoulos. Trump has no moral compass. If he ever had one, it got broken off at the pivot decades ago. We aren't talking economics here, we're dealing with an individual who should never have been allowed to lie his way into public office, let alone the presidency of the United States.
Democracy is only successful if the voters vote and are equipped to make critical judgments in the face of lies by those who would subjugate them. History has shown that the rich and powerful will lead the unsuspecting into a dictatorship and from there to societal breakdown.
Wake up folks! We are on the slide.
Tom McElroy, Toronto
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Speed limits; Women; Politics; Homeless people

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Happiness is like the weather, ever changingWhy is happiness so elusive to us? Jan. 1
Happiness is elusive because it comes and goes. It is inconceivable that we should be in a perpetual state of happiness. We need to remember that happiness is like the weather, ever changing, cold, hot, windy, raining, sunny. If we can remember that, we will enjoy the elusive moments of true happiness.
Charles Campisi, Oakville

'Rebate' cheques coming by February

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rebate-cheques-coming-february/docview/3151810313/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Rebate%27+cheques+coming+by+February&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A little extra cash will pad the pockets of 15 million Ontarians by early February as the Doug Ford government prepares to send out its $200 "rebate" cheques.
The cash will take a $3-billion bite out of the provincial treasury, taking a chunk of the government's $6.9-billion tax windfall and leaving a deep-red $6.6-billion deficit.
"Our government is putting money back into taxpayers pockets with a $200 rebate cheque that is expected to land in mailboxes by late January or early February," said Colin Blachar, a spokesperson for Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy.
"This would include an additional $200 for each eligible child as we support families and keep costs down."
The tax-free cheques, first revealed by the Star on Oct. 16, will be sent to all taxpayers 18 and over, and families who qualify for the federal Canada Child Benefit (CCB) payment receive an additional $200 for every child under 18.
"For families with children who did not receive the CCB for 2024, the government would provide an opportunity for a taxpayer rebate payment of $200 per child through an alternative process," the Progressive Conservative government said in a statement from October.
That means 15 million payments will be sent out - 12.5 million for adults and 2.5 million for children. Only those Ontarians who were "bankrupt or incarcerated" this year will not receive a payment.
The federal government had announced a similar plan, promising $250 cheques to Canadians - though with the current chaos within the reigning Liberal party and opposition critics who want expanded eligibility for seniors, those plans have been stuck in limbo since December.
Ford has touted his "rebate" cheques as a way of "(putting) money into people's pockets."
"The difference between our party and the Liberals and NDP - they believe in one thing: tax the taxpayers to death; drive companies out of this province. We've taken a different approach," the premier said at a campaign-style announcement in October.
Opposition parties, on the other hand, have accused the government of using taxpayer money to buy their votes and criticized the payments for not being more targeted.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles called it a "cynical" ploy, while Green Leader Mike Schreiner said, "I don't understand why millionaires and billionaires like (Loblaw Chairman) Galen Weston should get a $200 cheque when there are so many Ontarians who can't afford a home, 2.5 million can't access a doctor."
With files from Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie and Kristin Rushowy

Document 219 of 500

Ontarians are hungry for an alternative to Doug Ford. Why isn't Bonnie Crombie providing?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-are-hungry-alternative-doug-ford-why/docview/3151581051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+are+hungry+for+an+alternative+to+Doug+Ford.+Why+isn%27t+Bonnie+Crombie+providing%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Give Bonnie Crombie points for honesty.
In an interview with the Star's Rob Ferguson to mark the one-year anniversary of her victory in the Ontario Liberal leadership race, the former Mississauga mayor said she thought the new job "would be easier."
"It's been tough," she said.
To an outsider, confusingly so. At a time when incumbent governments across the democratic world are being tossed out on their collective ears, Crombie's Liberals have been unable to make a dent in Ontario Premier Doug Ford's popular support. The latest provincial poll gave the Progressive Conservatives a 16-point lead over the Liberals. Last December, it was a 13-point lead. This despite the fact that Ford himself has only a 34 per cent approval rating, lowest of any Canadian premier.
The PCs have kept that healthy cushion even as there have been controversies big and small: A greenbelt land swap that Ford later reversed, a police investigation into the same issue, the sudden closure of the Ontario Science Centre, and an out-of-nowhere plan to tear up bike-lane infrastructure, among others.
And while Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal government has gone to great lengths in recent weeks to prolong its life in fear of an electoral drubbing, the Ontario Premier is by all accounts keen to hold an election this coming spring, ahead of the planned 2026 date, so confident is he in another win.
Crombie, at the helm of a party that ruled the province for 15 straight years not that long ago, doesn't seem to scare him in the slightest.
It is, as mentioned earlier, a bit confusing. How did the Ontario Liberals get so bad at this?
There are some practical reasons for their struggles. With only nine MPPs in the legislature, the Liberals don't have official party status that would grant them additional staff and resources. And Crombie herself doesn't have a seat at Queen's Park, though she might if she had decided to run in any of the three provincial by-elections that were held this year. Choosing to sit on the sidelines while Ford sets the agenda undoubtedly makes Crombie's job harder.
It's also true that there are some branding issues. The federal Liberals are deeply unpopular, and have been for many months. People are furious at Trudeau over inflation, housing costs and other affordability issues, and the provincial Liberals are likely taking shrapnel from the grenades lobbed at the prime minster simply because they are also the red party.
But, if a provincial election will soon be in the offing, it's fair to wonder about Crombie's plan for it. Her first big policy plank was a "middle class" income-tax cut that would be combined with lopping the provincial portion of the HST off residential heating and hydro bills. Those sound like the kinds of things a Conservative government might do, and in fact Crombie promises to bring in the tax cuts that Ford has "failed to deliver." So, you want to do a better job of being Doug Ford? (As if to prove the point, she's also said she disapproves of the federal carbon tax.)
Crombie has more recently pledged to bring housing costs down by eliminating the land-transfer tax and development charges on certain home purchases and vowed to spend billions on recruiting and training more doctors to make up a provincial shortfall. As laudable as those initiatives might be, they came and went without much fanfare, underscoring the problem that Crombie has had in getting anyone to pay attention to her. Many, probably most, Ontarians are likely unaware of the job she holds. That seems like a significant problem if an election is only a couple of months away.
With the holiday season over, will Crombie try to seize the public's attention with some kind of bold promise or sweeping plan? The longer she waits, the closer she will get to an election in which she's forced to try to introduce herself to voters on the fly.
Being the rookie leader of the third-place party was never going to be easy. Crombie is correct on that count. But it also shouldn't have been quite this hard. With 2025 just around the corner, it's hard not to think that, for the once-mighty Ontario Liberals, 2024 was a wasted year.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie is seen at Queen's Park in November 2024.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson

Subject: Provincial elections; Leadership; Criminal investigations

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Title: Ontarians are hungry for an alternative to Doug Ford. Why isn't Bonnie Crombie providing?

Document 220 of 500

City champion Chow is missing in action from political battles

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-champion-chow-is-missing-action-political/docview/3151553118/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+champion+Chow+is+missing+in+action+from+political+battles&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before city hall there was Shea's Hippodrome, a grand, rambling pile of a theatre on the west side of Bay Street, north of Queen Street, near where the Nathan Phillips Square skating rink is now.
Opened as a vaudeville house in 1914 and later showing films, it was razed in the mid-1950s for new city hall. The Wurlitzer organ inside eventually made its way to Casa Loma after a few years at Maple Leaf Gardens.
Still, the Hippodrome's spirit of performance lives on at city hall with the political theatre happening there. Of the three tiers of government, municipalities routinely offer the best politics for spectators because things happen out in the open more. Policy is shaped in public debate rather than in private caucus. Sometimes absurd, rhetorically pugilistic and messy, it's raw democracy at work.
The theatrical aspect of city hall shouldn't be thought of glibly: Skilled politicians are performers who can get their message out, rally people around issues, create excitement or channel outrage. Of late there's a growing sentiment there isn't enough fight at city hall, particularly in how the mayor, the star of our show, defends Toronto.
Chow's own political character is that of a "Happy Warrior." I'd argue she is the first mayor in years who really loves Toronto in a sincere, everyday kind of way, with intimate knowledge of the place after decades of public life here. That's happy and good, but Toronto feels quite besieged right now and needs the warrior part too.
Toronto mayors have often clashed with Ontario governments, a built-in dynamic due to the nature of the relationship where "cities are the children of the province." Former mayor John Tory famously said he was tired of being treated like "a little boy in short pants" when dealing with Queen's Park, and Mel Lastman was even more combative, once calling Premier Mike Harris a liar.
Chow has taken a more appeasing path, choosing not to confront or oppose all-powerful Premier Doug Ford so directly, despite some major incursions into areas conventionally Toronto's turf - while also destroying Ontario landmarks in the city for dubious reasons. Chow has also acquiesced to the police request for a budget increase after both the chief and police union fought viciously last year when politicians pushed back.
Many Torontonians, particularly those who voted for her, are asking, "Where's the fight?" Standing up publicly to bullying behaviour is important, and its absence creates a void in leadership.
There are parallels between the Toronto and Ontario relationship and that of Canada and the United States. As president-elect Donald Trump blusters on about Canada becoming the 51st state, it is startling to watch a fair chunk of the Canadian establishment - often conservative - bend their knee to his kind of bellicose and dangerous foreign policy.
Sometimes you have to say, "No, stop it," and establish a line, even if that line is later crossed or there's a power imbalance. It's performative, perhaps, but it galvanizes people and creates something to believe in and rally around.
It feels like nobody has drawn a line anywhere around the bundle of issues undermining Toronto right now. Disparate parts of civil society and the civil service push back in their own ways, but the star of the show is largely avoiding the main conflict publicly (or plot, to extend this metaphor).
With Ontario Place and Science Centre plans, opposition is being led by citizens because in 2023 Chow brokered a deal with the Ford government fixing the city's finances, which included uploading the Gardiner Expressway. The cost was not officially opposing Ontario Place redevelopment.
Citizens are doing yeoman's work, but the mayor could help give the opposition shape and heft, as well as help to focus the anger of thousands of people. Instead, she's essentially muzzled. The civil service is pushing back in solid but subtler ways, like last week when transportation staff issued a report indicating Ford's Ontario Place plan would increase congestion.
The bike lane issue is similar. Beyond a few perfunctory statements of support from the mayor, it's also citizen groups leading the protests, including a legal challenge. The mayor's near silence is strategic here while the city and province negotiate how it will actually unfold. In November, the city's transportation department stopped its usual work to calculate the cost of removing the lanes - a herculean effort. At $48 million, the staff report established an important benchmark.
In isolation, each of the decisions not to fight is understandable, even rational. But looking at the whole story, the main character of our political saga isn't talking about those efforts in a way that can connect them and inspire the hearts and minds of more Torontonians. It's risky because there's power in a performance that gets a whole city to fight back, too.
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow hoists the Grey Cup at the Argonauts' victory party in November. Chow's decision not to fight some key political issues is understandable, but leaves the city without someone to inspire it, Shawn Micallef writes.Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

How did the Ontario Liberal party get so bad at politics?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-did-ontario-liberal-party-get-so-bad-at/docview/3151553097/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+did+the+Ontario+Liberal+party+get+so+bad+at+politics%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.

Document 222 of 500

5 things we learned from Jordan Peterson's two-hour interview with Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-things-we-learned-jordan-petersons-two-hour/docview/3151458813/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+things+we+learned+from+Jordan+Peterson%27s+two-hour+interview+with+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Justin Trudeau dangling from the proverbial political cliff, his primary rival spent two hours in conversation with right-wing provocateur Jordan Peterson in a podcast released this week, in which Pierre Poilievre made his case for a Canada with more oil production, less "woke-ism" and a tighter relationship with Donald Trump's America.
"If you look at the history of President Trump, he negotiates very aggressively and he likes to win," Poilievre said. "But in the end, he doesn't appear to have a problem if his counterparty also wins," he added, arguing that he would show up to deliberations with strength. "I think that we can get a great deal that will make both countries safer, richer and stronger," he said. "That's the goal that I'll be coming into these negotiations with."
The marathon interview, conducted on Dec. 21, reunited two Canadian conservatives who have long admired each other. It was posted on Peterson's own podcast and The Daily Wire, a conservative American website (where viewers can pay to watch an extra half-hour of bonus content).
Peterson, the former University of Toronto professor who used a firestorm over transgender pronouns as fuel for a lucrative career in online commentary, is arguably one of Canada's biggest digital exports. Poilievre, meanwhile, is vying to become Canada's next prime minister.
As with any conversation about Canadian or progressive politics lately, Trudeau continued to loom large. "I don't think there was anyone in the West who was more of a poster boy for modern progressivism than Justin Trudeau," Peterson said. "I don't think any administration in the West has failed as cataclysmically as his administration."
A longtime Trudeau critic, Peterson clearly relished the idea of a Canadian government more aligned with the Trump administration, and the economic growth he argues that would spur. "It looks like Canada and the U.S. are going to be aligned sufficiently politically to make that a reality," he said. "And I would say, to some degree, that's probably really the first time that's been the case in living memory."
Poilievre's basic message remained the same: that by cutting regulation on things like oil production and housing development, and by clamping down on government spending, Canada can't help but unleash greater prosperity.
"We have an incredible country, and I think we have a very bright future," Poilievre said. "I can't wait to see what we do and the example we set for the world."
Here are five major takeaways from the interview.
Why young people are swinging conservative
A recent political trend in Canada and other Western democracies is a rightward shift among young voters. Bucking conventional wisdom about young people and progressive causes, an Abacus poll from last year found that just over a third of those surveyed between the ages of 18 and 29 would support the Conservatives, more than any other party.
Poilievre points to a return to basic values like family and home ownership, plus the simmering frustration with the cost of everything from fuel to housing to education. "When I meet young people today, they are exhausted. They have bags under their eyes, and all they do is work," he told Peterson. But, he says, basic ambitions like buying a house or having kids is much more expensive than it was for their parents.
"Young people say, 'Look, OK, I went through, I got an education, I work non-stop, and I have made the calculation that there is no mathematical path for me to own a house.' It's just not possible in Toronto here, for example."
On energy and the environment
The secret sauce to Canada's economy is our energy, Poilievre says. "Energy of all kinds, hydro, nuclear, natural gas, you name it," he said. "So let's unleash the production of these resources and bring all that money home you can."
If he becomes prime minister, he says he will do all he can to ease restrictions on projects, including refineries and liquified natural gas. As a counterpoint to "environmental loons who hate our energy sector," he points to advocates who say exporting natural gas would help wean counties like India off coal-fired power, thereby reducing global emissions.
He said he's already talked to people like Alberta Premier Danielle Smith to warn her to be ready to help reform approval laws to "get some resource projects going immediately."
The importance of Canadian identity and ditching the hyphens
In the wake of Liberal changes to how many people would be able to immigrate to Canada, Poilievre spoke in favour of continued reform to the immigration system, but also a need for newcomers to adapt to Canadian society.
That means "reminding people that they are when they get here, they are Canadian first," Poilievre said. "Leave the hyphens. We don't need to be hyphenated society."
He pointed to the example of Catholics and Protestants who were "ripping each other's eyeballs out in Europe for centuries" but then came to Canada and got along. "In the last nine years that's come apart. Foreign conflicts are now spilling onto our streets. I want to put an end to that," he said. "I want to say, 'Look, we're not interested in the world's ethnocultural conflicts.'"
Canadians should instead be celebrating our shared culture and history, he said. "In so doing, we can, by the way, put aside race, this obsession with race that woke-ism has reinserted."
Dealing with Trump
Poilievre said he would fund a more robust military and continental defence in order to show the Americans that Canada is doing its part.
While some of Trump's opening salvos - the jokes about Canada becoming the 51st state, the threat of tariffs on Canadian goods - had been "humiliating" to watch, Poilievre called Trump a "highly successful businessman" who can "spot weakness a mile away."
"What I would say to the Americans is, let's take out a map of our shared continent, and here's the 49th parallel. Where do you believe we need to do more to protect our shared security across that map?"
On personally earmarking cuts to spending
One of Poilievre's key points is the need to cut back on government spending as a means of curbing excess but also inflationary pressures. It's something he says will have to be done quickly, and that he's already spent time on.
"I'm going through these programs myself and looking at them one by one, because I want to go in with as much preparation as possible, so that when, God willing, I'm elected, the officials sit down and I can say, 'Well, I already know that program is a waste of money, and that one's a waste of money, and that one's a corrupt scandal,'" he said. "We're going to get rid of this, this and this."
At this point, Peterson says he has a DOGE dossier at hand, a reference to Trump's proposed Department of Government Efficiency - co-headed by tech billionaire Elon Musk - to cut spending in the United States.
"I wouldn't say it quite like that," Poilievre said, "but I have spent a lot of time looking at where I can find the savings."
CAPTION: Canada's Next Prime Minister | Pierre Poilievre | EP 511Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre holds a news conference in Ottawa on Dec. 20, 2024.

Document 223 of 500

Chrystia Freeland reaches out to Liberal MPs as speculation swirls about Justin Trudeau's future

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-reaches-out-liberal-mps-as/docview/3151269973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+reaches+out+to+Liberal+MPs+as+speculation+swirls+about+Justin+Trudeau%27s+future&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Former finance minister Chrystia Freeland has been calling Liberal MPs in the wake of her bombshell resignation that preceded the latest and biggest wave of pressure so far on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to step down.
Several MPs, speaking on condition of anonymity, told the Star that Freeland has reached out and spoken to them in recent days.
While the MPs said Freeland did not bring up the Liberal leadership, one of them felt the calls were connected to a potential leadership run, while another said they were "impressed" by the outreach.
Freeland's calls come as her fellow MPs, party insiders and officials grapple with how the Liberals might replace Trudeau as he ponders his political future. Among the considerations is how to ensure that Canada can adeptly address U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threatened 25-per-cent trade tariffs, while ensuring Liberal party supporters would have a chance to choose a new leader before opposition parties stage an election-triggering confidence vote in the House of Commons.
There is also speculation about who might run to replace Trudeau to lead the Liberals into the next federal election campaign against Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who hold a commanding lead in public opinion polls.
Two Liberal sources with knowledge of Freeland's thinking said this week that they believe Trudeau's long-time political partner is mulling a bid to replace him if he steps down in the coming days.
"I think she's actively considering," said one of the sources, who spoke on condition of anonymity.
"She would be a very capable contender."
The second source suggested Freeland - who served on Trudeau's front bench since 2015 and as his deputy prime minister since 2019 - might be better positioned for a leadership run since she resigned from cabinet last month.
In her resignation letter, which she posted online, the former finance minister said she had been "at odds" with Trudeau on the "best path forward for Canada," and suggested the federal government was spending too much money when fiscal reserves should be kept to deal with the threat of a "tariff war" by Trump.
Freeland resigned just hours before she was supposed to present a fiscal update that showed the government had blown past its target for the federal deficit by more than $20 billion.
The source said they believe Freeland is now making efforts to ensure the door is open for a potential leadership run if Trudeau resigns.
A spokesperson for Freeland did not say whether the former finance minister would seek the party leadership, but confirmed that she intends to run again in the upcoming federal election.
"Ms. Freeland looks forward to running in the next election as the Liberal candidate in her riding of University-Rosedale," Katherine Cuplinskas said.
A source close to Freeland suggested the chatter about a potential leadership bid was getting ahead of itself, arguing she resigned from cabinet because the prime minister had lost confidence in her.
Meanwhile, former British Columbia premier Christy Clark is preparing to run for leader if Trudeau steps down, said one Liberal insider with knowledge of the organizing that has gone on for Clark so far.
Clark called for Trudeau to resign last summer, and told the Star at the time that people are encouraging her to seek the Liberal leadership if it opened up.
"We know what a team would look like, and we have a theory for how to win," the insider said, describing Clark as "the only change candidate" in a potential leadership race.
The insider said a possible Clark campaign would aim to attract a broad range of voters and stress economic issues, affordability and fiscal responsibility.
Another potential candidate is Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly, according to a Liberal source with knowledge of her thinking. The source said Joly hasn't yet decided if she would run, as she focuses on working with the incoming Trump administration and its threat of trade tariffs. The source also said Joly is wary of vying for the leadership only to lose the next election to Poilievre's Conservatives.
Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney has also been the frequent subject of leadership speculation, but has never confirmed he intends to run.
Frank Baylis, a former Quebec Liberal MP and businessman, told the Star this week that "many people are encouraging" him to run for the leadership, and that he's considering it if Trudeau resigns.
"I will be happy to talk in detail about this, once the prime minister makes his decision about staying or leaving," Baylis said by email.
In recent days, signs have continued to emerge that Trudeau is losing the support of his own caucus. Liberal MPs from Ontario have said many of their colleagues agreed in a recent meeting that Trudeau should resign. The party's Atlantic MPs, in a letter from Nova Scotia's Kody Blois, have also called on him to leave and to hold a meeting to select an interim leader.
Two Quebec MPs told the Star there is a consensus in that province's Liberal caucus that Trudeau needs to resign.
The calls come after a group of 24 Liberal MPs pushed in October for Trudeau to step down. More have come forward publicly since Freeland's resignation, urging the prime minister to leave for the good of the party.
Marcus Powlowski, a Liberal MP from northern Ontario, said Thursday that he believes there are several "really good candidates" who could replace Trudeau and more effectively face off against the Conservatives.
He named Freeland, Joly, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne and Transport Minister Anita Anand as contenders he might favour.
"We would not do well in an election right now with the present leader," Powlowski said, referring to Trudeau.
"With a new leader, we would do better."
Some MPs who spoke to the Star this week said they would like to see the prime minister prorogue Parliament - temporarily shutting it down to reset the legislative agenda - to give the Liberals time to choose a new leader.
Under the Liberal party's constitution, the party's board of directors sets the date for a leadership vote.
The rules include timelines that leadership candidates must submit paperwork at least 90 days before the vote, but two Liberal MPs - who spoke Thursday on condition of anonymity - said they want a shorter race if Trudeau resigns, given the current circumstances.
"The sooner the better, because that gives us more time to hold an open, democratic leadership process," one MP said.
CAPTION: Chrystia Freeland, then Canada's finance minister and deputy prime minister, speaks with reporters in Ottawa on Dec. 10, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; State elections; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 225 of 500

Province approves treatment hubs: Nine banned safe-consumption sites will become 'homelessness and addiction recovery' centres

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-approves-treatment-hubs/docview/3151052633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+approves+treatment+hubs%3A+Nine+banned+safe-consumption+sites+will+become+%27homelessness+and+addiction+recovery%27+centres&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nine of the 10 Ontario supervised consumption sites forced to shut down through recent provincial legislation will be converted into treatment hubs over the next several months, the province said Thursday.
Each of the provincially funded sites was allowed to apply to turn into what it calls "homelessness and addiction recovery treatment hubs," the Ministry of Health said.
The nine sites in Toronto, Ottawa, Hamilton, Kitchener, Guelph and Thunder Bay were all approved and will make the transition by March 31, the date by which they were slated to close, the ministry said.
The three community health centres transitioning in Toronto are Regent Park, Parkdale Queen West and South Riverdale, according to a report provided by the Ministry.
"These hubs will be eligible, on average, to receive up to four times more funding to support treatment and recovery under the model than they receive from the province as a consumption site," the ministry said in a release, adding the transitioning sites will also get one-time funding to help cover the startup costs.
The Progressive Conservative government recently fast-tracked legislation banning supervised consumption sites from operating within 200 metres of a school or daycare.
The law, which passed last month, also requires municipalities to get the health minister's approval before seeking a federal exemption to launch new supervised consumption sites, something Health Minister Sylvia Jones has said she would not approve.
Advocates for supervised consumption sites have condemned the move to shutter them, saying the decision will put the lives of many substance users at risk.
But Premier Doug Ford has argued critics should be grateful his government is investing in treatment services while shifting away from the supervised consumption model.
The government's plan includes a total of 19 homelessness and addiction recovery treatment hubs, as well as 375 highly supportive housing units, at a cost of $378 million.
Applications for the remaining 10 hubs are under review and will be announced in the coming weeks, the province said, adding the goal is to have the facilities up and running by April 1.
Last month, the community organization that runs the 10th supervised consumption site set to close down - a privately funded operation in Toronto's Kensington Market neighbourhood - filed a legal challenge alleging the provincial legislation violates both the Charter of Rights and Freedoms and the Constitution.
With files from Star staff
CAPTION: The South Riverdale Community Health Centre is one of three Toronto safe-injection sites being converted to a treatment hub, along with six others across the province.Nick Lachance Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: The Canadian Press With files from Star staff

Document 228 of 500

Could François-Philippe Champagne succeed Trudeau as PM?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/could-françois-philippe-champagne-succeed-trudeau/docview/3151042336/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Could+Fran%26ccedil%3Bois-Philippe+Champagne+succeed+Trudeau+as+PM%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In November, the CEO of Sweden's LM Ericsson was in Ottawa to announce his firm's increased investment in R&D facilities in Ottawa and Montreal, now totalling $634 million.
At Börje Ekholm's side as he extolled an immigration-driven Canadian tech "ecosystem" ideal for developing artificial-intelligence communications gear was François-Philippe Champagne, the Canadian industry minister.
There have been many such red-letter days for Champagne since he took on the industry portfolio in 2021. Each is the culmination of Champagne's lengthy negotiations with offshore multinationals to build up Canada's manufacturing and industrial research prowess.
It was also probably among the last ribbon-cutting events for a minister with few historical rivals in his success in bolstering the country's industrial strength across a variety of sectors.
But Champagne's political fortunes are tied to a federal Liberal government in its twilight hours.
Champagne has not disguised his interest in someday becoming prime minister. It's a prospect that the veteran of private-sector business in Canada and Europe had mused about even before entering public life.
Champagne was first elected to Parliament in 2015 in a Quebec riding.
The best that Champagne, 54, can hope for now is to win the leadership of his party in a campaign to replace Justin Trudeau, whose resignation is expected within weeks.
But the euphoria around electric vehicles (EV) with which Champagne is most strongly associated has faded in the past year, and with it Champagne's leadership prospects.
Most of the big-ticket projects Champagne has co-authored are still underway, including three auto-related megaprojects in Ontario.
But a proposed Northvolt EV battery plant in Quebec is in limbo and a planned expansion of a Taiwanese EV materials project in Maple Ridge, B.C., has been cancelled, casualties of the declining growth rate in EV purchases.
And Ford Motor has postponed though not cancelled its planned assembly of EVs in Oakville.
Yet Champagne has been a highly effective minister.
Champagne has spearheaded more than $50 billion of private and public investment to build one of the world's most comprehensive EV supply chains.
Champagne convinced Volkswagen to build its first gigafactory outside of Europe in St. Thomas, Ont. The plant, which was expected to go to the U.S., carries a price tag of $7 billion.
That deal followed Champagne's success in landing a $1.5-billion EV battery materials plant by Belgium's Umicore SA in Kingston, Ont., and a similar-sized facility by German chemical giant BASF in Bécancour, Que.
Champagne also persuaded Moderna, based in Cambridge, Mass., and one of the leading makers of the COVID-19 vaccines used by North Americans in the pandemic, to build its first mRNA manufacturing plant outside the U.S. in Montreal.
And Ericsson, one of the few manufacturers of the most advanced telecommunications gear, along with China's Huawei and Finland's Nokia, chose to further entrench itself in Canada at Champagne's persistent urging.
Ericsson's goal, in its words, is to put Canada at the "forefront of global development in next-generation communications technologies."
Champagne is a gifted salesman, and his product is Canada.
In pitching some of the world's largest foreign enterprises to pour billions of dollars into Canada, Champagne argued that Canada is a safe place to invest in an increasingly risky geopolitical world.
"You go to Canada and it's very stable and predictable. It's very attractive," Champagne told Politico, the U.S. political journal, last year. Politico caught up with Champagne at the Paris Air Show, where he was negotiating new business for the Canadian aerospace sector.
With his exuberant optimism, Champagne has risked a reputation for naivete. But then he bags another improbable deal.
U.S. Sen. Chris Coons (D-Delaware) told the audience at last year's Eurasia Summit that Champagne, on whom Coons relies as a trusted liaison between Canada and the U.S., is the "minister of creativity, innovation and enthusiasm."
Conspicuously missing from Champagne's EV supply chain is robust production of Canadian critical minerals, a lure for many of the multinationals who've invested here and a link in the supply chain not yet in place.
That defies common sense, says law firm Fasken, which has a large natural resources practice.
Canada has "deeper mining financial, legal and technical expertise than any other jurisdiction globally," Fasken said in a recent mining report.
Yet it can take 12 to 15 years to get a new Canadian mine permitted.
Ottawa has committed to cutting the permitting process to two to five years.
But that still requires Champagne's playbook for coaxing offshore multinationals, this time BHP Group, Rio Tinto, Glencore and the other globally dominant mining enterprises, to invest heavily in production of Canadian copper, nickel, lithium, cobalt and other critical minerals.
Coming months will likely see Champagne relinquish his cabinet post.
But an incoming government will need to adopt Champagne's goals and sense of urgency if Canada is to become a clean-tech powerhouse with few rivals.
CAPTION: Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry Francois-Philippe Champagne has spearheaded more than $50 billion of private and public investment to build one of the world's most comprehensive EV supply chains, David Olive writes.

Subject: Supply chains; Mining; Manufacturing; Critical minerals; Electric vehicles

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Manufacturing

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada; Europe

Speed cameras aim to save lives not make money Strike ...[Derived Headline]: Strike Three, City's Highest-grossing speed enforcement camera vandalized for third time, but let's hit the brakes on calling the culprit a folk hero, Dec. 31 The Blue Jays are back to the bad old days of scaring off top free agents. https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/speed-cameras-aim-save-lives-not-make-money/docview/3151042315/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Speed+cameras+aim+to+save+lives+not+make+money+Strike+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Strike+Three%2C+City%27s+Highest-grossing+speed+enforcement+camera+vandalized+for+third+time%2C+but+let%27s+hit+the+brakes+on+calling+the+culprit+a+folk+hero%2C+Dec.+31+The+Blue+Jays+are+back+to+the+bad+old+days+of+scaring+off+top+free+agents.+But+for+a+different+reason%2C+Chisholm%2C+Dec.+28+Why+is+happiness+so+elusive+to+us%3F+Opinion%2C+Jan.+1&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Speed cameras aim to save lives, not make money
Strike Three, City's Highest-grossing speed enforcement camera vandalized for third time, but let's hit the brakes on calling the culprit a folk hero, Dec. 31
I'm very disappointed the Star continues to perpetuate the myth that speed cameras are about collecting money. In my opinion, the heading should read, "City's most notorious street that has a high rate of speeders."
Law enforcement officers, speed cameras, radars or any other type of device are meant to remind us that we are breaking the law and putting our own and others' lives at risk.
My daughter, who has lived in Australia the past 10 years and is visiting for Christmas, is quite perplexed by drivers who seem to care little about the rules of the road here in Toronto.
In Australia, if you go over the limit by 4 km/h and are caught you will be fined, and there is no side business by retired police or paralegals to get you off. You break the law, you pay.
The outcome of such an unthinkable attitude - that we should obey the law - is that people don't speed and countless lives have been saved.
The person or persons who are responsible for this are doing much more than vandalism, they are putting lives at risk, and they should feel the full force of the law when caught.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Loyal Jays fans deserve better
The Blue Jays are back to the bad old days of scaring off top free agents. But for a different reason, Chisholm, Dec. 28
I am dismayed at the dithering in Blue Jays head office. The incompetence of its senior management is unacceptable. The Jays had a dismal year last year and look to repeat that poor performance this year.
I do not understand why the Rogers organization tolerates this incompetence, as it will ultimately affect their bottom line. Spring training starts next month and there is no evident plan to improve the team.
The Jays are Canada's team, and its loyal fans deserve better. CEO Mark Shapiro and general manager Ross Atkins should be replaced immediately. They are clearly not up to the job.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
Ontario hospitals shouldn't need to beg for donations
On Dec. 28, there was full-page ad in the Star, placed by Lakeridge Health Foundation, soliciting donations towards the purchase of five life-saving devices: Automated External Defibrillators (AED).
I pay taxes. Those should be sufficient to equip a hospital in Ontario. That is the bargain between me and the government: tax me and take care of the health and education and general well-being of the citizens of Ontario. If the taxes don't cover the costs, raise the taxes.
Needed hospital equipment purchases should not require the hospital to put a tin cup on the sidewalk begging for donations.
Perhaps Premier Doug Ford could redirect the $200 vote-buying bribe cheques he has promised us and spend the $3 billion-plus on AEDs. They apparently cost $3,200 each. He could buy a million of them. They could be in every hospital ward, every bus, every subway car, every long-term-care facility, every beer- and wine- selling grocery store. Even at the Ontario Place spa, maybe.
Graeme Elliott, Toronto
Trudeau's chosen female ministers have failed him
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must be so disappointed. In 2015, he made international headlines by naming a cabinet that was 50 per cent women. Sadly, every single one turned out to be a dud.
After what I can only imagine were heartfelt efforts to position one of them as his successor, they all failed to demonstrate the necessary intelligence, character and gravitas for the job.
Jody Wilson-Raybould, a Crown prosecutor who held leadership positions in both Kwak'wala and Canadian governments. Not a team player.
Joyce Murray, who made a fortune planting trees. Can't think outside the box.
And Chrystia Freeland, who found common ground with Ontario Premier Doug Ford and defended Liberal trade policies from a Trump attack in 2017, couldn't see the upside of a few trifling Christmas gifts for all Canadians. The woman simply does not understand politics. Sad.
Facing a second Donald Trump presidency, Trudeau and his small circle of like-minded advisers stand alone, grimly shouldering the weight of Canada's future.
On behalf of all Canadian women, I want to apologize for leaving this lonely burden to them.
Catherine Murton Stoehr, North Bay, Ont.
O'Leary is out for his own interests, not Canada's
Kevin O'Leary posted on X that he is willing to go to Mar-a-Lago to negotiate on Canada's behalf to unify Canada and the U.S.
O'Leary's right to negotiate on our behalf exists only in his ego. He is no elected official. In fact, he recently became a citizen of the United Arab Emirates. Not only is there no income tax in the UAE, but there is also no need to file any report on your income.
Pack your ego away Mr. O'Leary. You don't represent anyone's interest but your own. Remember, we are the true north strong and free.
Bruce Kerr, Toronto
Positive friends create positive feelings
Why is happiness so elusive to us? Opinion, Jan. 1
People do not need to search deeply for happiness. Many of us should realize that happiness actually comes from other people.
How easy is it to walk into a room full of positive energy and feel complete joy and happiness? One could say that the emotion of happiness can be contagious.
How many of us felt depression or frustration during the COVID lockdown due to lack of or limited interaction? I truly find happiness through my family and friends, and I embrace the impact it has on my overall well-being.
Positive interactions give us positive feelings. I believe that many of us are very rich with relationships and when we are surrounded by supportive, kind, caring, inspirational, fun-loving people that make you feel important, loved and appreciated; you find happiness.
We are surrounded by happiness. We just need to recognize it.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby

Could Champagne be PM after Trudeau?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/could-champagne-be-pm-after-trudeau/docview/3151041729/se-2?accountid=15090

Editorial cartoon doesn't belong in the StarTheo Moudakis: Signs of the times, Jan. 1
My take on the bicycle issue is that the bike lanes, insufficiently used, are dangerous for pedestrians and vehicle drivers. Cyclists have generally abandoned common sense, their own safety and laws. The encampments that are used by otherwise homeless people are an indictment on our society. The answer to the illegal encampment is a bulldozer. The cartoon does not belong in the Star unless the editors are in favour of cyclists and protesters being exempt from the law.
Eric Bornstein, Toronto
Positive interactions give us positive feelings - that's happiness!Why is happiness so elusive to us? Jan. 1
People do not need to search deeply for happiness. Many of us should realize that happiness actually comes from other people. How easy is it to walk into a room full of positive energy and feel complete joy and happiness? One could say that the emotion of happiness can be contagious. Subject: Taxes; Vandalism; Speed limits; Cameras; Happiness; Automation; Editorial cartoons; Homeless people

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Automation

Location: Australia; Canada

Title: Purpose of speed cameras is to save lives

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2025

Publication date: Jan 3, 2025

Section: opinion/letters-to-the-editor

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Divi sion of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 232 of 500

Politicians of all stripes raise $25,000 to help homeless former MPP: Berardinetti fell ill, with no provincial pension to rely on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politicians-all-stripes-raise-25-000-help/docview/3151041045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians+of+all+stripes+raise+%2425%2C000+to+help+homeless+former+MPP%3A+Berardinetti+fell+ill%2C+with+no+provincial+pension+to+rely+on&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lorenzo Berardinetti, the former city councillor and Liberal MPP who became homeless last year after developing a seizure disorder in his 60s, is getting $25,000 to go toward renting and furnishing an apartment, thanks to donations from dozens of former colleagues.
"I'm very grateful for all who contributed," Berardinetti told the Star after the fundraiser hit its goal Thursday. "What impressed me the most was that donations came from members of all three parties."
That includes Dan Newman, the Progressive Conservative whom Berardinetti unseated in 2003, and Doly Begum, the NDP MPP who defeated Berardinetti in 2018.
Moments after fundraiser organizer Justin Van Dette read Berardinetti's account of how he became homeless after 30 years in politics, published in the Star last week, he started "working the phones."
Van Dette, senior director for government and community relations at Tridel, quickly assembled a coalition of politicians who met with Berardinetti over Zoom last week to offer their support.
"Whether you're on the right or the left, no matter what level of government, if you're involved in politics, we're all family," said Van Dette, who met Berardinetti while working at city hall for former deputy mayor Case Ootes about25 years ago. "There was a sense of urgency to help Lorenzo. The article concerned and bothered a lot of people."
On New Year's Eve, Van Dette and about two dozen of his political "family" members launched a GoFundMe for Berardinetti, which hit its target two days later after receiving 168 donations.
Berardinetti and his supporters have another call scheduled for Sunday. He said he doesn't want to touch the money himself, and hopes Van Dette and others can arrange to rent him an apartment in Ajax, where he has been living for the past couple of years. Van Dette said apartments are currently being scouted.
The goal has since been stretched to $35,000, with the aim of sending the $10,000 surplus to the Ontario Association of Former Parliamentarians "so that there is a fund in place in the event other former elected officials fall on hard times," Van Dette said.
The rest, he said, is going toward renting Berardinetti a home where he can "have some time to breathe, get back up on his feet, and open his law practice, which he is very keen to do."
Sponsors of the fundraiser included former mayor John Tory, ex-premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty, as well as Berardinetti's cousin, Toronto councillor Mike Colle.
For some politicians, Berardinetti's situation crystalized their need for better financial security. MPPs no longer have a pension plan, after former premier Mike Harris axed it in 1995. Base pay for an MPP has also been frozen at $116,000 since 2009.
"I think a more thoughtful approach, rather than a flippant and crassly political refusal of the reinstatement of a pension plan and transition benefits is necessary," said PC MPP and donor Lisa MacLeod.
With "no safety net" for MPPs, what happened to Berardinetti is "going to happen more and more often," she said. "It shouldn't be up to crowdfunding to support former MPPs, it's up to the legislature, which failed Lorenzo and several others who have not been public. This has been an utter embarrassment for the institution of Parliament."
CAPTION: Lorenzo Berardinetti, a former Toronto city councillor and MPP, has been living in a homeless shelter in Ajax for the past year.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

A mountain of potential workplace injury reports collecting dust + a New Year's massacre in New Orleans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mountain-potential-workplace-injury-reports/docview/3150832199/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+mountain+of+potential+workplace+injury+reports+collecting+dust+%2B+a+New+Year%27s+massacre+in+New+Orleans&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 234 of 500

Queen West used to be the heart of Toronto. How did it go so wrong?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/queen-west-used-be-heart-toronto-how-did-go-so/docview/3150519972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Queen+West+used+to+be+the+heart+of+Toronto.+How+did+it+go+so+wrong%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For decades, the rusting, weathered Live Eye car has hung over Queen Street West.
Its wheels have spun along with the wheels of time, watching over the endless evolution of one of Toronto's most iconic streets. Storefronts have changed. Watering holes have opened and closed. Condos have gone up, and up, and up.
The Live Eye car has remained.
And it will likely stay there moving forward, even after CP24 officially left the Bell Media offices at 299 Queen Street West at the end of November. Bell's emptying of that building - one that has stood at the corner of Queen and John streets since the 1910s - marks the latest transformation of a hallowed, if now hollowed, strip of Toronto culture and history.
Today's Queen West, the strip that runs roughly from University Avenue to Bathurst Street, is not the epicentre it once was. It is no longer the gritty, grassroots music and arts scene it was in the 1980s and '90s. It is no longer, for the most part, the place where young people go shopping at independent storefronts.
Instead, some complain, it has become an outdoor mall. Chipotle and Chick-fil-A and Subway are in. So too are Lululemon and Aritzia and Lush. There's banks - CIBC, TD, Scotiabank. There's even a Shoppers Drug Mart.
But once upon a time, with punk rockers and MuchMusic, Queen West was nothing short of magic.
"It was the greatest thing I had ever seen," said Adam Bunch, a Toronto historian who credits the strip for igniting his lifelong love affair with this city. "For a generation or two of Torontonians, it was sort of the centre of a lot of the city's cultural life."
It all began with punk.
1977. The grimy, crowded basement of a converted warehouse on Duncan Street, a few blocks south of Queen West. It was the home of the Diodes, a band formed by students at the Ontario College of Art up the street. They - along with the rest of Toronto's punk scene - had just been kicked out of another club, so they started their own.
They called it Crash 'n' Burn. Two decades later, the Star called it a "basement pit" and the "epicentre of much mayhem."
For one hot summer, it created chaos. It played host to Canadian punk upstarts - Teenage Head, the Viletones, the Dishes - and uncontrollable crowds. According to one account from a 20-year-old punk singer interviewed by the Star in 1977, the club was not violent. It just featured good fun: spitting, pouring beer on people, rolling on the floor, wrestling and doing the strangler, "a dance where the couple strangle each other's throats."
"Don't give the address," the singer pleaded, "because the straight kids will come and expect punks to fight."
After that summer, the club crashed and burned. It had the unfortunate fate of its upstairs neighbours being the federal Liberal party. In the words of a 1999 Star retrospective, the political class "finally grew weary of stepping past flesh-and-blood-spattered walls on their way to work."
But Crash 'n' Burn was at the forefront of an emerging Queen West neighbourhood. After all, it had been boring for more than a century.
The trail runs back to old Toronto, when Queen Street sat on the outskirts of this British outpost and a country estate, not a Bell Media building, occupied the corner of Queen and John. The estate, known as Beverly House, belonged to Sir John Beverley Robinson, a revered judge and politician who even entertained Charles Dickens on the property in 1842.
As the city expanded, Toronto swallowed Queen West. The Beverly House came down in 1913. In its place went a Methodist printing press which, with time, transformed into the national headquarters of the United Church of Canada.
And so it stayed for decades.
"Queen and John was very not important," said Morgan Cameron Ross, who runs the Old Toronto Series, "unless you're Methodist, and then it must have been wild."
For most of its life, Toronto had been stuffy, quiet and respectful, Bunch said. Queen West was an assembly of fabric stores, greasy spoon diners and abandoned buildings by the time the 1970s rolled around, Liz Worth wrote in the Star in 2006. It was a "frigid cultural landscape," she said, one that required "sidestepping the blood spilling onto the sidewalk from the chicken slaughterhouse."
The punk rockers helped change that. Cheap rent, Worth wrote, and a quick walk to school made Queen West as good a home as any for the students at the Ontario College of Art. In the late 1970s, it started to become a playground for experimental artists and unhinged partiers.
It was just what Toronto needed.
"Over the second half of the century, you had places like Yorkville and the Yonge Street strip and Queen West that helped turn (the city) into the Toronto we recognize today," Bunch said, "a place with vibrant art scenes and cultural scenes and music scenes."
Part of the music scene, joining institutions like the Horseshoe Tavern and BamBoo, arrived in March 1987. In desperate need of more space, Citytv moved its offices to the corner of Queen and John. The company spent $8 million renovating the old Methodist printing house, installing huge windows and turning the entire building into what amounted to a giant television studio.
Into the corner at Queen and John went MuchMusic. It was a sensation.
"The cultural draw of Citytv and MuchMusic was a force," said Ross, a musician who moved to Toronto in the summer of 2004 and made a point of visiting MuchMusic on his second day in the city. "Everyone knew of that building."
MuchMusic had no rules. It had fresh-faced VJs and a shoebox-sized studio with windows often open to the street. It was, in the words of Hamilton Spectator columnist Scott Radley, where pop culture lived.
The Backstreet Boys visited in January 1998. Fans waited 16 hours in freezing rain, closing Queen West for most of the day. Police wore earplugs. One teen was trampled and whisked away in an ambulance.
The Electric Circus show was a 90-minute, in-your-face dance party, one that broke ground for its acceptance of anyone and everyone. The annual Christmas tree toss created laughs out of a chore. Speaker's Corner gave every person a say.
Queen West began to change in the late 2000s. Depending on who you ask, that's when it went corporate. H&M opened in August 2007 and closed 16 years later. In 2010, a Star headline proclaimed, "Queen West gentrifies, again." By 2014, Vogue named West Queen West - not the University-to-Bathurst strip, but the Bathurst-to-Roncesvalles section - one of the coolest streets in the world. Queen West was absent.
Today, Queen West still has a live music scene. Institutions like the Horseshoe, the Cameron House, the Rivoli and the Rex are still playing away.
But for those who loved it, Queen West has lost its way.
"It's now been kind of just gobbled up by the corporate clothing stores and, it's just, there's nothing artistic about that area," Ross said. "There's no cultural reason to be there anymore."
In the '80s and '90s, there were more than a dozen street vendors; today, only one remains. The Black Bull had been in operation since 1833 and was one of Toronto's oldest bars; it closed in April, since reopening with new owners and a new name. Two Ontario Line stations - one at Queen and Spadina, another at Queen and University - promise to change Queen West even more.
And as of November, CP24 is gone. The rest of Bell Media is leaving.
And the wheels on the Live Eye car keep spinning.
CAPTION: An archival photo of the building at 280 Queen St. W., taken between 1982 and 1992.Revellers dance at Electric Circus, the electrifying TV dance party that aired live from the corner of Queen and John Streets in the '90s.For more than 30 years, Sheryl Genser, right, has been a jewelry vendor on Queen Street West. Pictured with her employee and longtime friend, Thea, left, the pair has worked together for 11 years. In 2003, there were five vendors. Over the years, that number dwindled to three, then two. Since 2017, Genser has been the only one left, earning the title of "the last vendor on Queen Street."The CP24 LiveEye car remains at the Bell Media building at Queen and John streets, even after the company officially vacated the space in November, marking another chapter in the ongoing transformation of Queen Street West.MuchMusic made Queen and John a celebrity hotspot, like in this photo of Justin Bieber from 2009.ARRIVED VIA EMAIL BY EDT PHOTO BBS ---------- From: Fraser, Garnet Sent: Wednesday, June 13, 2007 3:40 PM To: Photodesk - Toronto Star Subject: FW: CHUMTV Building Photo <> Please Cascade pic of MuchMusic HQ.
