Letters: If Toronto fails to deliver this one piece of infrastructure, it will reveal an ugly truth, Dec. 28 Doug Ford pushing to meet with Donald Trump to talk 'businessman to businessman,' Dec. 20 Trump takes another shot on Truth Social at Canada becoming 51st state, Dec. 18 Canada has win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump, Dec. 30

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3150480415/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+If+Toronto+fails+to+deliver+this+one+piece+of+infrastructure%2C+it+will+reveal+an+ugly+truth%2C+Dec.+28+Doug+Ford+pushing+to+meet+with+Donald+Trump+to+talk+%27businessman+to+businessman%2C%27+Dec.+20+Trump+takes+another+shot+on+Truth+Social+at+Canada+becoming+51st+state%2C+Dec.+18+Canada+has+win-win+deals+it+can+make+to+satisfy+Donald+Trump%2C+Dec.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A lack of vision is holding our city back
If Toronto fails to deliver this one piece of infrastructure, it will reveal an ugly truth, Dec. 28
Where is the vision?
Contributing columnist Shawn Micallef is correct in saying the City of Toronto is lacking a guiding master plan. Where is our vision for the long-term future?
I feel badly for the residents of Liberty Village. The word "village" could have been the clue. I was happy to have experienced that "village" atmosphere. In the 1990s, when I worked in film and inhabited the beautiful red brick warehouses, it really felt like a village. But Liberty Village has became a monolith of unattractive highrises and box stores without any thought of parkland, pedestrians and inhabitants because it lacks a long-term plan.
The reason we lack infrastructure is because our city is planned on political terms. For more than 30 years I have been asking, "Where is the master plan? Where is the vision?" The only answer I get from our official plan is the word "intent." What does intent even mean?
We can solve our infrastructure issues, and all our other issues, if only we had a master plan.
The city can solve its lack of vision by becoming urgent and bold.
Astra Burka, Toronto
We could learn from actual world-class cities
I grew up in Toronto. After marriage, we moved out to the GTA, still often heading downtown for work-related events and entertainment until traffic snarls and social problems became obstacles that led to our staying away almost permanently.
We moved out of the GTA and away from the sprawl.
We love to travel and often visit cities in other countries that have somehow got their act together. Efficient transportation, easy walking avenues, cafés, interesting shopping areas, expansive lovely parks and themed markets. All of which make tourists like us flock to world-class cities across oceans.
These cities have higher density, vibrant arts and culture and, although not without social issues, most have somehow worked their problems out and are able to provide housing for its residents.
Micallef was correct in his observation there is no central plan for Toronto.
A patchwork of projects that never seem to be completed, and an interfering Queen's Park that doesn't have the expertise to understand that one must actually hire professional planners and engineers to make strategic decisions, have all contributed to the chaotic mess we see with Toronto traffic and homelessness.
Until Toronto gets its act together we will constantly find ourselves in the quagmire of indecision.
Marion Bartlett,
Singhampton, Ont.
Toronto losing its lustre on world stage
Buffalo was once one of the great cities of America. When the Erie Canal lost its importance, Buffalo did also.
Toronto was once a great city, the envy of the world, a city that worked. However, thanks to civic and provincial politicians subservient to giant corporations, Toronto is losing its importance on the world stage as well.
It seems the only talent needed in Toronto now is how to afford to live in it.
Glenn Taylor, Innisfil, Ont.
Premiers are picking up Trudeau's slack
Doug Ford pushing to meet with Donald Trump to talk 'businessman to businessman,' Dec. 20
Thank you to Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and Ontario counterpart Doug Ford for their leadership in actively lobbying in the United States on American television networks to protect Canada from tariffs.
Even Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai is capitalizing on Donald Trump Jr.'s love of hunting to help with the lobbying effort.
Unfortunately, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is still in hiding and not doing his job. Why are we even paying him a salary to be prime minister if he won't carry out his responsibilities to lead Canada on the world stage?
Our sad excuse for a prime minister won't make the case to stop tariffs against Canada in the American media. He seems to not even be speaking to Canadian media.
Perhaps if we had a more effective prime minister the premiers wouldn't have to do his job for him.
Chris Robertson,
Stony Plain, Alta.
Trump's vision of Canada a sad state of affairs
Trump takes another shot on Truth Social at Canada becoming 51st state, Dec. 18
U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has to make a choice about Canada. If he wants a tighter border, then he can't make Canada the 51st state.
If he wants Canada to be the 51st state, then he doesn't need any border controls.
In the end, that just means we have nothing to worry about. Because by the time Trump manages to figure out what that means he'll be out of office.
Michael Liotta, Toronto
Dear Canada, we Americans appreciate you!
I'm not a politician; I am what could be considered an average American. Like many of my fellow Americans, I am genuinely baffled as to why our president-elect would choose to talk smack to our friends.
I feel like a parent apologizing for a child's bad manners in wanting to reach out on a personal level to all Canadians to say you know us better than that. With few exceptions, every American appreciates that we have in Canada one of the finest neighbours in the world.
Paul Bick, Palm Coast, Fla.
Right formula to lead Canada into the future
Canada has win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump, Dec. 30
This excellent opinion piece by Mark McQueen should be a must-read for both Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre and all of those who aspire to be Canada's next prime minister.
In fact, it is so far-sighted it need not even be headlined, "Win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump."
The article simply outlines the path Canada should be taking in 2025, with or without Trump.
Kevin Barry, Oakville

Document 236 of 500

Rose float celebrates IT program for vets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rose-float-celebrates-program-vets/docview/3150480294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rose+float+celebrates+IT+program+for+vets&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When veteran Ron Davidson waves to the crowd atop Canada's lone entry in the New Year's Day Rose Parade in sunny Pasadena, Calif., he'll be doing so on behalf of the country he served as a younger man - and a program that helps veterans transition to civilian life.
It's the second year entering a float in the Rose Parade for Coding for Veterans, an organization that helps Canadian veterans transition into careers in cybersecurity and software development through online courses in partnership with the University of Ottawa.
Davidson, 61, a retired teacher and former bombardier with the 5th Field Battery of the Royal Regiment of Canadian Artillery who now calls California home, said there wasn't such specialized support when he left the service.
"A lot of them, when they leave, they don't know what to do," Davidson said of some of the men and women with whom he served. "They had training, but they didn't know how to use it. They didn't know how to connect it to the civilian world. It's a lot better now."
Coding for Veterans' work is important to helping veterans transition to civilian life, Davidson says, especially in this high-tech era.
Coding for Veterans' executive director Jeff Musson says the program launched five years ago with just five students. There are now more than 800 veterans enrolled.
"You end up going from serving on the battlefield to now serving in cyberspace," Musson said, adding that the Rose Parade is "a great opportunity in which to tell our story."
Musson says veterans make ideal IT candidates bringing with them attention to detail, leadership and a strong sense of teamwork.
The programs take an average of 12 months to complete and have landed veterans jobs with IBM, Amazon and a host of banks, defence contractors and multinational corporations. They expanded the programs last year to U.S. veterans working in partnership with the University of Southern California's Marshall School of Business.
Daniel Hruka, a former infantry soldier from Richmond, B.C., is currently in training.
Hruka was always interested in computers and had his own PC from a young age. He said that the skills used in the military - situational awareness and critical thinking among them - makes for an easy transition to cybersecurity.
"Cybersecurity is a really good fit for veterans," Hruka said. "I'm hoping that with all the certifications I've gotten through the Coding for Veterans program, I'll be able to land an entry-level role in security."
At the cavernous, Amazon-sized warehouse in Pasadena, hundreds of volunteers hurried about after the arrival of the thousands of roses that need to be attached to the float.
The Coding for Veterans entry, started in June, includes replicas of a F-35 fighter jet made out of seaweed, a Black Hawk helicopter made out of parsley, a giant computer screen and of course the Canadian flag prominent on its side.
"It really symbolizes a military veteran transitioning from deployment to employment," Musson said.
Atop the float itself, Davidson will be there, proudly donning his Canadian Legion outfit.
"There's two types of people in the world," Davidson said matter-of-factly, "There's Canadians and those that want to be Canadians."
CREDIT: Hayden Godfrey Toronto Star

Subject: Cybersecurity

Title: Rose float celebrates IT program for vets

Document 237 of 500

Tougher impaired driving penalties among the changes facing Ontarians in the New Year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tougher-impaired-driving-penalties-among-changes/docview/3150480289/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tougher+impaired+driving+penalties+among+the+changes+facing+Ontarians+in+the+New+Year&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Drinking and driving could land you in bigger trouble in 2025.
Among changes in effect across Ontario for the New Year, some penalties for impaired driving are tougher, the threshold for reporting property damage to police in a traffic accident increases, it will be easier for authorized cannabis retailers to sell their stores as consolidation in the industry continues, and there is more support for women working in construction.
Premier Doug Ford, who is mulling an early provincial election instead of waiting until the next scheduled vote in June 2026, has also promised to send out $200 "rebate" cheques to about 15 million residents of the province in the coming weeks.
Impaired driving penalties have increased in a move aimed at scaring motorists straight and taking them off the roads longer or for good under the Safer Roads and Communities Act, which takes aim at increasingly brazen auto thieves as well.
"There is never an excuse for getting behind the wheel impaired," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said of the legislation introduced in May and passed into law Nov. 19.
Key elements of the bill impose longer roadside licence suspensions for driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs and provide for lifetime driving bans on anyone convicted of impaired driving causing death, although there is a path to early reinstatement in some cases.
Suspensions increase to seven days for a first offence, up from three, and to 14 days from seven for a second offence.
Defending the changes against concerns they won't have the preventative impact the government hopes because logic doesn't work on people under the influence, Sarkaria signalled that society is losing patience at crashes, injuries and deaths.
"There's always a choice ... plan ahead, call on a friend, call a cab or take public transit," he said, noting the changes were supported by Mothers Against Drunk Driving. "If you think there are no consequences for drinking and driving, my advice would be to start planning for life without your car."
Ontario Provincial Police laid more than 10,000 impaired driving charges in 2023, the last year for which full statistics were available. There were about 50 deaths.
The changes in penalties follow public outrage about cases like convicted drunk driver Marco Muzzo, the scion of a wealthy family, sentenced to 10 years in prison in 2016 after he drove through a stop sign on the way home from the airport after a boozy bachelor party in Miami.
Muzzo killed three children from the Neville-Lake family and their grandfather. His licence was suspended for 12 years. He was released on parole after five years in prison and will be eligible to drive again in three years. The new legislation is not retroactive and will not apply to him.
During debate on the bill in the legislature, New Democrat MPP Jennifer French (Oshawa) said she makes "no argument" against stiffer penalties for impaired driving but contrasted them with Ford's move to put beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails into convenience stores last fall, a year earlier than planned.
"People aren't going to find it any easier to stay away from alcohol when it's...everywhere. These convenience store booze nooks are going to be in front of drivers all the time," she warned.
"Much of this bill deals with penalty and punishment after a crime, which is very on-brand for the Conservatives ... but nowhere in this bill is there anything preventative to keep road users and drivers safe," French added.
On auto thefts, the legislation includes 10-year driver's licence suspensions for first convictions, 15 years on second convictions and indefinite suspensions for third and subsequent convictions.
For drivers involved in accidents of any kind, the Highway Traffic Act threshold for reporting property damage to police rises to $5,000 from $2,000.
To help families cope with the loss of a child, eligibility for the Ontario Child Benefit is extended six months to match federal changes in regard to the Canada Child Benefit.
Child care fees are being capped at $22 daily for Ontario families taking advantage of the Canada-wide Early Learning and Child Care system.
Under changes to the Occupational Health and Safety Act to support women in the skilled trades, construction companies are required to provide menstrual products for onsite crews of 20 or more and on projects expected to last at least three months.
Although long-term care homes were required to have sprinkler systems installed by Jan. 1 to better protect vulnerable residents and staff from fires, the compliance deadline has been extended 18 months to July 1, 2026 "to account for additional infrastructure work," the government said in a statement Tuesday.
As the storefront cannabis industry continues to evolve, regulations under the Cannabis Licence Act have been amended to "streamline" the process by which retailers authorized by the province can sell their businesses to other authorized retailers.
Ford's $200 "rebate" cheques to defray the increased cost of living following a bout of high inflation will cost the provincial treasury - which remains in a deficit - $3 billion and go totaxpayers 18 and over, providing they are not "bankrupt or incarcerated," the government said. Families who qualify for the Canada Child Benefit will get an additional $200 for every child under 18.
For example, that means two parents with three children would get $1,000.
Opposition parties have dubbed the cheques a cynical ploy.
"A lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
CAPTION: Key elements of a bill comes into effect in 2025 impose longer roadside licence suspensions for driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs and provide for lifetime driving bans on anyone convicted of impaired driving causing death, although there is a path to early reinstatement in some cases.

Subject: Property damage; Child care; Drunk driving; Fines & penalties; Families & family life; Alcohol; Legislation; Convictions; Convenience stores; Automobile drivers; Sprinkler systems; Marijuana; Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

Document 238 of 500

Tougher impaired penalties in place: Drunk drivers will be taken off the road for longer periods under provincial act

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tougher-impaired-penalties-place/docview/3150480195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tougher+impaired+penalties+in+place%3A+Drunk+drivers+will+be+taken+off+the+road+for+longer+periods+under+provincial+act&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 239 of 500

An inquest into my brother's death made 57 recommendations. Doug Ford still has not addressed them a year later

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/inquest-into-my-brothers-death-made-57/docview/3150480155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+inquest+into+my+brother%27s+death+made+57+recommendations.+Doug+Ford+still+has+not+addressed+them+a+year+later&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My younger brother Soleiman Faqiri was killed by correctional officers in the Central East Correctional Centre in Lindsay, Ont., on Dec. 15, 2016.
I fought long and hard for an inquest to reveal the truth about my beloved brother's death. Last year that inquest determined it was a homicide and made 57 recommendations to improve the correctional system and prevent a tragedy like ours from occurring again.
Now, one year later, nothing has been done to address this injustice. None of the correctional officers involved in Soleiman's beating death have been held criminally accountable. None of the jury's recommendations have been implemented. Disturbingly, during this time, at least two other individuals incarcerated with mental health issues have been killed in Ontario prisons.
Premier Doug Ford and his government have deliberately ignored the inquest's 57 recommendations and continue to do so. They have refused to apologize to our family in Parliament for his death. The system that killed my brother continues unchanged. This inaction is putting the lives of others incarcerated with mental health issues at risk.
Soleiman was a straight-A student and captain of his high school football team. He entered the University of Waterloo as an environmental engineering student with a bright future. He was especially close to our mother, whom he taught to read. But after a traumatic car accident when he was 19, he was diagnosed with schizophrenia.
Soleiman Faqiri was 30 years old and experiencing a mental health episode when he was arrested for assault and uttering threats on Dec. 4, 2016. During his 11 days in custody, my family was not allowed to visit or to see him.
The day Soleiman died, he was pepper-sprayed twice, shackled face down in a spit hood while, and according to an eyewitness, a guard held his knee on Soleiman's neck. These were the final moments of my late brother's life - in mental distress, cut off from family, surrounded like prey and attacked by people in positions on authority. Fifty blunt impact trauma bruises were later counted on his body by the coroner.
Since Soleiman's inquest, there have been other tragic deaths in Ontario correction facilities.
Ibrahim Ali, who had schizophrenia, was arrested for allegedly stealing a pack of cigarettes. In prison, he was "stripped naked and brutally attacked by his cellmate," while his family says he was denied adequate mental health care.
Euplio Cusano, suffering from epilepsy and a brain injury, was taken to a provincial jail after an altercation in his long-term-care home. He spent seven months awaiting a bail hearing, often "triple bunked" in a cell, and was beaten to death by another inmate.
Like Soleiman's, their deaths were preventable. Their lives mattered. They, and so many others, are the human faces of government inaction in our corrections system. It is painfully clear that Ontario Corrections cannot achieve meaningful systemic change on its own. The most critical recommendation from Soleiman's inquest is to establish an Independent Provincial Correctional Inspectorate to ensure accountability within the system.
However, Premier Ford has not implemented even one of the inquest's recommendations including the first one which is a simple commitment to the process: "Develop and issue a public position statement within 60 days of this verdict recognizing that correctional facilities are not an appropriate environment for persons in custody experiencing significant mental health issues."
If Ford implemented another crucial recommendation: establishing a provincial agency within the Ministry of Health to liaise with the Ministry of the Solicitor General to directly deliver health-care services in correctional facilities, he could save lives. My brother never saw a psychiatrist in the 11 days he was jailed. He suffered without his proper medications or any form of medical support. Soleiman Faqiri, Ibrahim Ali, Euplio Cusano and so many others should be here with us today.
On Dec. 15, 2024, we held a vigil to commemorate Soleiman and to shine a light on other families whose loved ones in our sorely inadequate prison system are also suffering from mental health conditions. Several hundred Canadians tuned in from the across the province and country to watch our national vigil. We also had several families and well-known leaders including Sen. Kim Pate speak at our vigil.
Ontario prisons are in crisis. According to government data, 81 per cent of people incarcerated in provincial jails are awaiting trial and presumed innocent. The costs are staggering: a 2012 report warned that rising remand populations were unsustainable. By 2020-2021, the cost to detain one person had more than doubled, from $183 to $409 per day. Incarcerating those who are mentally ill is not helping the situation.
Soleiman's harrowing last 11 days should be a wake-up call for all who care about our most vulnerable citizens. It is a call for meaningful, lasting change on behalf of those suffering from mental health conditions in our prisons.
Implementing the inquest's recommendations is not just about preventing future deaths - it's about sparing other families the anguish mine has endured.
We will not stop advocating until change happens. We are not going away, Premier Ford. I promise.
Soleiman was ill. He was not a criminal, and he should never have been in prison.
My younger brother deserved better. Canadians deserve better.
CAPTION: Yusef Faqiri holds a photo of his brother, Soleiman Faqiri, at a protest rally in front of the Ministry of the Solicitor General on Grosvenor Street in Toronto in August2020.
CREDIT: Yusuf Faqiri Contributor

Subject: Schizophrenia; Correctional personnel; Mental health; Prisons; Murders & murder attempts; Imprisonment; Correctional institutions; Mental disorders

Document 240 of 500

Toronto's lack of long-term vision holding it back

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-lack-long-term-vision-holding-back/docview/3150480122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+lack+of+long-term+vision+holding+it+back&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's lack of vision holding it backIf Toronto fails to deliver this one piece of infrastructure, it will reveal an ugly truth, Dec. 28
Where is the vision?
Contributing columnist Shawn Micallef is correct in saying the city of Toronto is lacking a guiding master plan. Where is our vision for the long-term future?
I feel badly for the residents of Liberty Village. The word 'village' could have been the clue. I was happy to have experienced that 'village' atmosphere. In the 1990s, when I worked in film and inhabited the beautiful red brick warehouses, it really felt like a village.
But Liberty Village has became a monolith of unattractive highrises and box stores without any thought of parkland, pedestrians and inhabitants because it lacks a long-term plan.
The reason we lack infrastructure is because our city is planned on political terms . For more than 30 years I have been asking, "Where is the master plan? Where is the vision?" The only answer I get from our official plan is the word 'intent.'
What does intent even mean?
We can solve our infrastructure issues, and all our other issues, if only we had a master plan.
The city can solve its lack of vision by becoming urgent and bold.
Astra Burka, Toronto
Toronto could learn from actual world-class cities
If Toronto fails to deliver this one piece of infrastructure, it will reveal an ugly truth, Dec. 28
I grew up in Toronto. After marriage, we moved out to the GTA, still often heading downtown for work-related events and entertainment until traffic snarls and social problems became obstacles that led to our staying away almost permanently.
We moved out of the GTA and away from the sprawl.
We love to travel and often visit cities in other countries that have somehow got their act together. Efficient transportation, easy walking avenues, cafes, interesting shopping areas, expansive lovely parks and themed markets. All of which make tourists like us flock to world-class cities across oceans.
These cities have higher density, vibrant arts and culture and, although not without social issues, most have somehow worked their problems out and are able to provide housing for its residents.
Shawn Micallef was correct in his observation there is no central plan for Toronto.
A patchwork of projects that never seem to be completed, and an interfering Queen's Park that doesn't have the expertise to understand that one must actually hire professional planners and engineers to make strategic decisions, have all contributed to the chaotic mess we see with Toronto traffic, and homelessness.
Until Toronto gets its act together we will constantly find ourselves in the quagmire of indecision.
Marion Bartlett, Singhampton, Ont.
Toronto losing its lustre on world stage
Buffalo was once one of the great cities of America. When the Erie Canal lost its importance, Buffalo did also.
Toronto was once a great city, the envy of the world, a city that worked. However, thanks to civic and provincial politicians subservient to giant corporations, Toronto is losing its importance on the world stage as well.
It seems the only talent needed in Toronto now is how to afford to live in it.
Glenn Taylor, Innisfil, Ont.
Premiers are picking up Trudeau's slack on tariff frontDoug Ford pushing to meet with Donald Trump to talk 'businessman to businessman', Dec. 20
Thank you to Alberta Premier Danielle Smith and Ontario counterpart Doug Ford for their leadership in actively lobbying in the United States on American television networks to protect Canada from tariffs.
Even Yukon Premier Ranj Pillai is capitalizing on Donald Trump Jr.'s love of hunting to help with the lobbying effort.
Unfortunately, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is still in hiding and not doing his job. Why are we even paying him a salary to be prime minister if he won't carry out his responsibilities to lead Canada on the world stage?
Our sad excuse for a prime minister won't make the case to stop tariffs against Canada in the American media. He seems to not even be speaking to Canadian media.
Perhaps if we had a more effective prime minister the premiers wouldn't have to do his job for him.
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
Trump's vision of Canada a real sad state of affairsTrump takes another shot on Truth Social at Canada becoming 51st state, Dec. 18
U.S. president-elect Donald Trump has to make a choice about Canada.
If he wants a tighter border, then he can't make Canada the 51st state.
If he wants Canada to be the 51st state, then he doesn't need any border controls.
In the end, that just means we have nothing to worry about. Because by the time Trump manages to figure out what that means he'll be out of office.
Michael Liotta, Toronto
American here: Canada, we appreciate you!
I'm not a politician; I am what could be considered an average American. Like many of my fellow Americans, I am genuinely baffled as to why our president-elect would choose to smack talk our friends.
I feel like a parent apologizing for a child's bad manners in wanting to reach out on a personal level to all Canadians to say you know us better than that. With few exceptions, every American appreciates that we have in Canada one of the finest neighbours in the world.
Paul Bick, Palm Coast, Fla.
Opinion piece outlines path Canada needs to take with TrumpCanada has win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump, Dec. 30
This excellent opinion piece by Mark McQueen should be a must-read for both Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre and all of those who aspire to be Canada's next prime minister.
In fact, it is so farsighted it need not even be headlined, 'Win-win deals it can make to satisfy Donald Trump.'
The article simply outlines the path Canada should be taking in 2025, with or without Trump.
Kevin Barry, Oakville
CAPTION: A letter writer opines that Liberty Village has became a monolithic culture of unattractive high rises and box stores without any thought for its inhabitants because it has no long-term plan.

Document 241 of 500

Coming up Roses: New Year's Day parade float celebrates a high-tech route some soldiers use to march into civilian life

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/coming-up-roses-new-years-day-parade-float/docview/3150480080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Coming+up+Roses%3A+New+Year%27s+Day+parade+float+celebrates+a+high-tech+route+some+soldiers+use+to+march+into+civilian+life&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When veteran Ron Davidson waves to the crowd atop Canada's lone entry in the New Year's Day Rose Parade in sunny Pasadena, Calif., he'll be doing so on behalf of the country he served as a younger man - and a program that helps veterans transition to civilian life.
It's the second year entering a float in the Rose Parade for Coding for Veterans, an organization that helps Canadian veterans transition into careers in cybersecurity and software development through online courses in partnership with the University of Ottawa.
Davidson, 61, a retired teacher and former bombardier with the 5th Field Battery of the Royal Regiment of Canadian Artillery who now calls California home, said there wasn't such specialized support when he left the service.
"A lot of them, when they leave, they don't know what to do," Davidson said of some of the men and women with whom he served. "They had training, but they didn't know how to use it. They didn't know how to connect it to the civilian world. It's a lot better now."
Coding for Veterans' work is important to helping veterans transition to civilian life, Davidson says, especially in this high-tech era.
Coding for Veterans' executive director Jeff Musson says the program launched five years ago with just five students. There are now more than 800 veterans enrolled.
"You end up going from serving on the battlefield to now serving in cyberspace," Musson said, adding that the Rose Parade is "a great opportunity in which to tell our story."
Musson says veterans make ideal IT candidates bringing with them attention to detail, leadership and a strong sense of teamwork.
The programs take an average of 12 months to complete and have landed veterans jobs with IBM, Amazon and a host of banks, defence contractors and multinational corporations.
They expanded the programs last year to U.S. veterans working in partnership with the University of Southern California's Marshall School of Business.
Daniel Hruka, a former infantry soldier from Richmond, B.C., is currently in training.
Hruka was always interested in computers and had his own PC from a young age. He said that the skills used in the military - situational awareness and critical thinking among them - makes for an easy transition to cybersecurity.
"Cybersecurity is a really good fit for veterans," Hruka said. "I'm hoping that with all the certifications I've gotten through the Coding for Veterans program, I'll be able to land an entry-level role in security."
At the cavernous, Amazon-sized warehouse in Pasadena, hundreds of volunteers hurried about after the arrival of the thousands of roses that need to be attached to the float.
The Coding for Veterans entry, started in June, includes replicas of a F-35 fighter jet made out of seaweed, a Black Hawk helicopter made out of parsley, a giant computer screen and of course the Canadian flag prominent on its side.
"It really symbolizes a military veteran transitioning from deployment to employment," Musson said.
Atop the float itself, Davidson will be there, proudly donning his Canadian Legion outfit.
"There's two types of people in the world," Davidson said matter-of-factly, "There's Canadians and those that want to be Canadians."
CAPTION: Coding for Veterans, Canada's lone entry into the annual New Year's Day Tournament of Roses parade in Pasadena, Calif., pulls out of the warehouse for judging.Coding for Veterans' executive director Jeff Musson, alongside the company's float, says soldiers make ideal IT candidates due to theirattention to detail, leadership and teamwork.

Document 242 of 500

Toll of government inaction in prisons

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/toll-government-inaction-prisons/docview/3150480076/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toll+of+government+inaction+in+prisons&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My younger brother Soleiman Faqiri was killed by correctional officers in the Central East Correctional Centre in Lindsay, Ont., on Dec. 15, 2016.
I fought long and hard for an inquest to reveal the truth about my beloved brother's death. Last year that inquest determined it was a homicide and made 57 recommendations to improve the correctional system and prevent a tragedy like ours from occurring again.
Now, one year later, nothing has been done to address this injustice. None of the correctional officers involved in Soleiman's beating death have been held criminally accountable. None of the jury's recommendations have been implemented. Disturbingly, during this time, at least two other individuals incarcerated with mental health issues have been killed in Ontario prisons.
Premier Doug Ford and his government have deliberately ignored the inquest's 57 recommendations and continue to do so. They have refused to apologize to our family in Parliament for his death. The system that killed my brother continues unchanged. This inaction is putting the lives of others incarcerated with mental health issues at risk.
Soleiman was a straight-A student and captain of his high school football team. He entered the University of Waterloo as an environmental engineering student with a bright future. He was especially close to our mother, whom he taught to read. But after a traumatic car accident when he was 19, he was diagnosed with schizophrenia.
Soleiman Faqiri was 30 years old and experiencing a mental health episode when he was arrested for assault and uttering threats on Dec. 4, 2016. During his 11 days in custody, my family was not allowed to visit or to see him.
The day Soleiman died, he was pepper-sprayed twice, shackled face down in a spit hood while, according to an eyewitness, a guard held his knee on Soleiman's neck. These were the final moments of my late brother's life - in mental distress, cut off from family, surrounded like prey and attacked by people in positions on authority. Fifty blunt impact traumabruises were later counted on his body by the coroner.
Since Soleiman's inquest, there have been other tragic deaths in Ontario correction facilities.
Ibrahim Ali, who had schizophrenia, was arrested for allegedly stealing a pack of cigarettes. In prison, he was "stripped naked and brutally attacked by his cellmate," while his family says he was denied adequate mental health care.
Euplio Cusano, suffering from epilepsy and a brain injury, was taken to a provincial jail after an altercation in his long-term-care home. He spent seven months awaiting a bail hearing, often "triple bunked" in a cell, and was beaten to death by another inmate.
Like Soleiman's, their deaths were preventable. Their lives mattered. They, and so many others, are the human faces of government inaction in our corrections system. It is painfully clear that Ontario Corrections cannot achieve meaningful systemic change on its own. The most critical recommendation from Soleiman's inquest is to establish an Independent Provincial Correctional Inspectorate to ensure accountability within the system.
However, Premier Ford has not implemented even one of the inquest's recommendations including the first one which is a simple commitment to the process: "Develop and issue a public position statement within 60 days of this verdict recognizing that correctional facilities are not an appropriate environment for persons in custody experiencing significant mental health issues."
If Ford implemented another crucial recommendation: establishing a provincial agency within the Ministry of Health to liaise with the Ministry of the Solicitor General to directly deliver health-care services in correctional facilities, he could save lives.
My brother never saw a psychiatrist in the 11 days he was jailed. He suffered without his proper medications or any form of medical support. Soleiman Faqiri, Ibrahim Ali, Euplio Cusano and so many others should be here with us today.
We held a vigil to commemorate Soleiman and to shine a light on other families whose loved ones in our sorely inadequate prison system are also suffering from mental health conditions. Several hundred Canadians tuned in from the across the province and country to watch our national vigil. We also had several families and well-known leaders, including Sen. Kim Pate, speak at our vigil.
Ontario prisons are in crisis. According to government data, 81 per cent of people incarcerated in provincial jails are awaiting trial and presumed innocent. The costs are staggering: a 2012 report warned that rising remand populations were unsustainable. By 2020-2021, the cost to detain one person had more than doubled, from $183 to $409 per day.
Incarcerating those who are mentally ill is not helping the situation.
Soleiman's harrowing last 11 days should be a wake-up call for all who care about our most vulnerable citizens. It is a call for meaningful, lasting change on behalf of those suffering from mental health conditions in our prisons.
Implementing the inquest's recommendations is not just about preventing future deaths - it's about sparing other families the anguish mine has endured.
We will not stop advocating until change happens. We are not going away, Premier Ford. I promise.
Soleiman was ill. He was not a criminal, and he should never have been in prison.
My younger brother deserved better. Canadians deserve better.
Yusuf Faqiri is Soleiman Faqiri's older brother and the founder of the Justice for Soli Movement.
CREDIT: Yusuf Faqiri Contributor Yusuf Faqiri is Soleiman Faqiri's older brother and the founder of the Justice for Soli Movement.;

Document 243 of 500

Hollowed history: For those who loved its music and arts scene, Queen West has lost its way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hollowed-history/docview/3150480075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hollowed+history%3A+For+those+who+loved+its+music+and+arts+scene%2C+Queen+West+has+lost+its+way&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2025-01-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For decades, the rusting, weathered Live Eye car has hung over Queen Street West.
Its wheels have spun along with the wheels of time, watching over the endless evolution of one of Toronto's most iconic streets. Storefronts have changed. Watering holes have opened and closed. Condos have gone up, and up, and up.
The Live Eye car has remained.
And it will likely stay there, even after CP24 officially left the Bell Media offices at 299 Queen St. W. at the end of November. Bell's emptying of that building - one that has stood at the corner of Queen and John streets since the 1910s - marks the latest transformation of a hallowed, if now hollowed, strip of Toronto culture and history.
Today's Queen West, the strip that runs roughly from University Avenue to Bathurst Street, is not the epicentre it once was. It is no longer the gritty, grassroots music and arts scene it was in the 1980s and '90s. It is no longer, for the most part, the place where young people go shopping at independent storefronts.
Instead, some complain, it has become an outdoor mall. Chipotle and Chick-fil-A and Subway are in. So too are Lululemon and Aritzia and Lush.
There are banks - CIBC, TD, Scotiabank. There's even a Shoppers Drug Mart.
But once upon a time, with punk rockers and MuchMusic, Queen West was nothing short of magic.
"It was the greatest thing I had ever seen," said Adam Bunch, a Toronto historian who credits the strip for igniting his lifelong love affair with this city. "For a generation or two of Torontonians, it was sort of the centre of a lot of the city's cultural life."
It all began with punk.
1977. The grimy, crowded basement of a converted warehouse on Duncan Street, a few blocks south of Queen West. It was the home of the Diodes, a band formed by students at the Ontario College of Art up the street. They - along with the rest of Toronto's punk scene - had just been kicked out of another club, so they started their own.
They called it Crash 'n' Burn. Two decades later, the Star called it a "basement pit" and the "epicentre of much mayhem."
For one hot summer, it created chaos. It played host to Canadian punk upstarts - Teenage Head, the Viletones, the Dishes - and uncontrollable crowds. According to one account from a 20-year-old punk singer interviewed by the Star in 1977, the club was not violent. It just featured good fun: spitting, pouring beer on people, rolling on the floor, wrestling and doing the strangler, "a dance where the couple strangle each other's throats."
"Don't give the address," the singer pleaded, "because the straight kids will come and expect punks to fight."
After that summer, the club crashed and burned. It had the unfortunate fate of its upstairs neighbours being the federal Liberal party. In the words of a 1999 Star retrospective, the political class "finally grew weary of stepping past flesh-and-blood-spattered walls on their way to work."
But Crash 'n' Burn was at the forefront of an emerging Queen West neighbourhood. After all, it had been boring for more than a century.
The trail runs back to old Toronto, when Queen Street sat on the outskirts of this British outpost and a country estate, not a Bell Media building, occupied the corner of Queen and John. The estate, known as Beverly House, belonged to Sir John Beverley Robinson, a revered judge and politician who even entertained Charles Dickens on the property in 1842.
As the city expanded, Toronto swallowed Queen West. The Beverly House came down in 1913. In its place went a Methodist printing press that, with time, transformed into the national headquarters of the United Church of Canada.
And so it stayed for decades.
"Queen and John was very not important," said Morgan Cameron Ross, who runs the Old Toronto Series, "unless you're Methodist, and then it must have been wild."
For most of its life, Toronto had been stuffy, quiet and respectful, Bunch said. Queen West was an assembly of fabric stores, greasy spoon diners and abandoned buildings by the time the 1970s rolled around, Liz Worth wrote in the Star in 2006.
It was a "frigid cultural landscape," she said, one that required "sidestepping the blood spilling onto the sidewalk from the chicken slaughterhouse."
The punk rockers helped change that. Cheap rent, Worth wrote, and a quick walk to school made Queen West as good a home as any for the students at the Ontario College of Art (now known as OCAD). In the late 1970s, it started to become a playground for experimental artists and unhinged partiers.
It was just what Toronto needed.
"Over the second half of the century, you had places like Yorkville and the Yonge Street strip and Queen West that helped turn (the city) into the Toronto we recognize today," Bunch said, "a place with vibrant art scenes and cultural scenes and music scenes."
Part of the music scene, joining institutions like the Horseshoe Tavern and BamBoo, arrived in March 1987. In desperate need of more space, Citytv moved its offices to the corner of Queen and John. The company spent $8 million renovating the old Methodist printing house, installing huge windows and turning the entire building into what amounted to a giant television studio.
Into the corner at Queen and John went MuchMusic. It was a sensation.
"The cultural draw of Citytv and MuchMusic was a force," said Ross, a musician who moved to Toronto in the summer of 2004 and made a point of visiting MuchMusic on his second day in the city. "Everyone knew of that building."
MuchMusic had no rules. It had fresh-faced VJs and a shoebox-sized studio with windows often open to the street. It was, in the words of Hamilton Spectator columnist Scott Radley, where pop culture lived.
The Backstreet Boys visited in January 1998. Fans waited 16 hours in freezing rain, closing Queen West for most of the day. Police wore earplugs. One teen was trampled and whisked away in an ambulance.
The "Electric Circus" show was a 90-minute, in-your-face dance party, one that broke ground for its acceptance of anyone and everyone. The annual Christmas tree toss created laughs out of a chore. Speaker's Corner gave every person a say.
Queen West began to change in the late 2000s. Depending on who you ask, that's when it went corporate. H&M opened in August 2007 and closed 16 years later. In 2010, a Star headline proclaimed, "Queen West gentrifies, again." By 2014, Vogue named West Queen West - not the University-to-Bathurst strip, but the Bathurst-to-Roncesvalles section - one of the coolest streets in the world. Queen West was absent.
Today, Queen West still has a live music scene. Institutions like the Horseshoe, the Cameron House, the Rivoli and the Rex are still playing away.
But for those who loved it, Queen West has lost its way.
"It's now been kind of just gobbled up by the corporate clothing stores and, it's just, there's nothing artistic about that area," Ross said. "There's no cultural reason to be there anymore."
In the '80s and '90s, there were more than a dozen street vendors; today, only one remains. The Black Bull had been in operation since 1833 and was one of Toronto's oldest bars; it closed in April, since reopening with new owners and a new name. Two Ontario Line stations - one at Queen and Spadina, another at Queen and University - promise to change Queen West even more.
And as of November, CP24 is gone. The rest of Bell Media is leaving.
And the wheels on the Live Eye car keep spinning.
CAPTION: Some complain Queen West has become an outdoor mall, far removed from the days of punk rockers and MuchMusic events. NICK LACHANCE TORONTO STARThe Live Eye car has become a Queen West landmark, one that will likely remain even after Bell Media departs. Revellers dance at Electric Circus, the electrifying TV dance party that aired live from the corner of Queen and John streets in the 1990s.MuchMusic made the corner of Queen and John a celebrity hotspot, as seen in this 2009 photo of Justin Bieber.Nick Lachance Toronto StarTony Bock toronto Star file photoIan WillmsToronto Star file photo

CREDIT: Mark Colley Toronto Star

Title: Hollowed history:   For those who loved its music and arts scene, Queen West has lost its way

Document 244 of 500

New Ontario Place: Traffic issues cannot be overlooked

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-ontario-place-traffic-issues-cannot-be/docview/3150342610/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Ontario+Place%3A+Traffic+issues+cannot+be+overlooked&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Traffic woes a real concern for new Ontario Place planDoug Ford's Ontario Place plan will make traffic congestion worse: report, Dec. 24
The authors of this report surely took into consideration the school buses taking kids to the Science Centre. Just think of them trundling down the DVP in the morning rush hour and back again for the late afternoon rush.
Oh but that will never happen. The schools are too smart. They will simply stop sending kids to discover the fun and excitement of science.
Mr. Ford, did you think of that?
Bruce McCallum, Toronto
Increased traffic simply reflects increased popularity
A report states that Ontario Place's rebirth will increase traffic on Lake Shore Boulevard by about 25 per cent.
Malarkey!
The rebirth of Ontario Place, with expanded free public access to shops and restaurants, a concert venue, a new and expanded Science Centre and a spa, even with excellent public transit connections on the new Ontario Line and GO Transit, may impact traffic far more than that.
In light of this traffic study we must trust the science and acknowledge that the reborn Ontario Place will be an unqualified and popular success for all Ontarians to enjoy.
Roman Botiuk, Etobicoke
'Race to the bottom' policy comes at high costOntario lacks a health-care plan for alcohol harms as sales expand: health coalition, Oct. 31
The cost of alcohol to society is immense. While sales generate revenue, the associated expenses - poor health outcomes, criminal justice costs and productivity losses - far outweigh the benefits. Alcohol and drug dependency also wreak havoc on users' families and countless others around them.
On the frontlines of homelessness, where I work as a neuropsychologist, the devastating impact of alcohol and drugs on the human brain and mind is undeniable. These substances offer a temporary, affordable escape from life's challenges but inflict long-term damage, often irreparable, on the brain.
So where do we draw the line?
For many Torontonians, the sight of a 24-pack of Coors Light priced at $27.08 (no tax) might seem like an early Christmas gift from Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford. It's a discount many hosting holiday gatherings may not even notice.
I've never seen prices this low in Ontario-perhaps not even in Quebec. But behind this political win lies a heavy toll.
The risks of this "race to the bottom" policy are real and profound. This isn't just about enjoying a cheap beer or two; it's about the lives of those who battle alcohol and drug dependency every day. Over the years, I've witnessed many patients lose everything, including their lives.
If I had one wish this holiday season, it would be for intelligent policy making to address some of society's most urgent challenges: affordable housing, mental health and addiction crises, and overall affordability.
Too many Canadian families are being crushed under the weight of skyrocketing grocery bills, soaring housing prices and intolerably long waits for mental health and addiction services.
We deserve better than superficial policies that distract us from the deeper, systemic failures at the provincial and federal levels. Record-high homelessness and mounting social inequities demand thoughtful, courageous solutions - not gimmicks.
Dr. Sylvain Roy, Toronto
Premier Ford, let's go for a bike rideDoug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Nov. 25
There is an old saying, "You can't understand another person's experience until you've walked a mile in their shoes."
Well, this time you'll be riding. With that in mind, I cordially invite Premier Ford to take a bike ride with me. This sir, is not a pleasure ride on a residential street with the sun shining and birds singing. I invite you first to ride with me as I commute to my place of work in the throng of morning rush hour, on a busy downtown Toronto street that has no bike lane.
For the second half of our ride we will cycle on a downtown street that currently has a bike lane. As an active cyclist, I have navigated these downtown streets for over 40 years, and for the same length of time I have been a licensed automobile driver. For six months of the year I choose to cycle to and from work, five days a week totalling 60 kms.
I do this to contribute my small part to our ailing planet and to stay physically fit. I would continue year-round however, with earlier darkening skies and potentially icy roads, safety comes first and I choose an alternate method of transportation instead.
After "riding in my shoes" and making the effort to better comprehend how cyclists of all ages feel, we may have a conversation about what best suits the people who move around Toronto everyday. And should Premier Ford require a bicycle, I am sure I can find one to loan him for the day.
Susie Whaley, Toronto
Canada's fresh water is at riskWhen Trump comes calling for our water, Canada must be ready, Nov. 16
I always thought with faint irony that Canada was a mere holding company for water and minerals for U.S. consumption when the time comes.
Either we sell it to them at reduced cost or they will simply take it. It doesn't have to be under Trump, just normalized by him for a future tyrant with bad intentions and political will.
Steven Fistell, Toronto
Thank you for shining light on housing issues for senior womenHer pension wasn't enough to cover Toronto rent. Then she found a lifeline in a different kind of housing, Dec. 24
I would like to congratulate Moira Welsh for a wonderful and well-researched article on housing options for senior women.
Those of us in the baby boomer generation do not intend to be put away in a long-term care institutions and forgotten about. We have active and fulfilling lives. We give back to family and our communities; occasionally we need a little extra help to maintain our independence.
We need more articles like Moira's to wake up the Ontario government. We do not want our tax dollars given to institutions. We have paid our taxes for many, many years and now want to see them directed to home care, NORC's and PACE programs to allow seniors to live their final years in dignity. Not locked up in an institution.
It has been proven over and over that health care dollars can be saved through fewer emergency room visits and shorter hospital stays when we implement these type of programs for the elderly.
If Premier Doug Ford is actually serious about redesigning our health care system to make it more effective, this is the perfect place to start. The U.S. and Denmark are just a few locations that can supply the data regarding what is actually required to save health care dollars while at the same time treating the elderly with respect.
Ceciley White, Ajax
CAPTION: By 2032, the Ontario Place redevelopment is projected to increase traffic volumes on Lake Shore Boulevard West by up to 27 per cent in the morning rush, 23 per cent in the evening rush and as much as 40 per cent during major events.

Subject: Alcohol; Mental health; Older people

Full text: Science Centre plan lacks logic
Doug Ford's Ontario Place plan will make traffic congestion worse: report, Dec. 24
The authors of this report surely took into consideration the school buses taking kids to the Science Centre. Just think of them trundling down the DVP in the morning rush hour and back again for the late afternoon rush.
Oh but that will never happen. The schools are too smart. They will simply stop sending kids to discover the fun and excitement of science. Mr. Ford, did you think of that?
Bruce McCallum, Toronto
Increased traffic reflects popularity
A report states that Ontario Place's rebirth will increase traffic on Lake Shore Boulevard by about 25 per cent. Malarkey!
The rebirth of Ontario Place, with expanded free public access to shops and restaurants, a concert venue, a new and expanded Science Centre and a spa, even with excellent public transit connections on the new Ontario Line and GO Transit, may impact traffic far more than that.
In light of this traffic study we must trust the science and acknowledge that the reborn Ontario Place will be an unqualified and popular success for all Ontarians to enjoy.
Roman Botiuk, Etobicoke
In need of smart policy making
Ontario lacks a health-care plan for alcohol harms as sales expand: health coalition, Oct. 31
The cost of alcohol to society is immense. While sales generate revenue, the associated expenses - poor health outcomes, criminal justice costs and productivity losses - far outweigh the benefits. Alcohol and drug dependency also wreak havoc on users' families and countless others around them.
On the front lines of homelessness, where I work as a neuropsychologist, the devastating impact of alcohol and drugs on the human brain and mind is undeniable. These substances offer a temporary, affordable escape from life's challenges but inflict long-term damage, often irreparable, on the brain.
So where do we draw the line?
For many Torontonians, the sight of a 24-pack of Coors Light priced at $27.08 (no tax) might seem like an early Christmas gift from Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford. It's a discount many hosting holiday gatherings may not even notice.
I've never seen prices this low in Ontario - perhaps not even in Quebec. But behind this political win lies a heavy toll.
The risks of this "race to the bottom" policy are real and profound. This isn't just about enjoying a cheap beer or two; it's about the lives of those who battle alcohol and drug dependency every day. Over the years, I've witnessed many patients lose everything, including their lives.
If I had one wish this holiday season, it would be for intelligent policy making to address some of society's most urgent challenges: affordable housing, mental health and addiction crises, and overall affordability.
Too many Canadian families are being crushed under the weight of skyrocketing grocery bills, soaring housing prices and intolerably long waits for mental health and addiction services.
We deserve better than superficial policies that distract us from the deeper, systemic failures at the provincial and federal levels.
Dr. Sylvain Roy, Toronto
Premier, let's go for a bike ride
Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Nov. 25
There is an old saying, "You can't understand another person's experience until you've walked a mile in their shoes."
Well, this time you'll be riding. With that in mind, I cordially invite Premier Doug Ford to take a bike ride with me. This, sir, is not a pleasure ride on a residential street with the sun shining and birds singing. I invite you first to ride with me as I commute to my place of work in the throng of morning rush hour, on a busy downtown Toronto street that has no bike lane.
For the second half of our ride we will cycle on a downtown street that currently has a bike lane. As an active cyclist, I have navigated these downtown streets for more than 40 years, and for the same length of time I have been a licensed automobile driver. For six months of the year I choose to cycle to and from work, five days a week totalling 60 kilometres.
After "riding in my shoes" and making the effort to better comprehend how cyclists of all ages feel, we may have a conversation about what best suits the people who move around Toronto everyday. And should Premier Ford require a bicycle, I am sure I can find one to loan him for the day.
Susie Whaley, Toronto
Canada's fresh water is at risk
When Trump comes calling for our water, Canada must be ready, Nov. 16
I always thought with faint irony that Canada was a mere holding company for water and minerals for U.S. consumption when the time comes.
Either we sell it to them at reduced cost or they will simply take it. It doesn't have to be under Donald Trump, just normalized by him for a future tyrant with bad intentions and political will.
Steven Fistell, Toronto
Shining light on housing issues
for senior women
Her pension wasn't enough to cover Toronto rent. Then she found a lifeline in a different kind of housing, Dec. 24
I would like to congratulate Moira Welsh for a wonderful and well-researched article on housing options for senior women.
Those of us in the baby boomer generation do not intend to be put away in a long-term-care institutions and forgotten about. We have active and fulfilling lives. We give back to family and our communities; occasionally we need a little extra help to maintain our independence.
We need more articles like Moira's to wake up the Ontario government. We do not want our tax dollars given to institutions. We have paid our taxes for many, many years and now want to see them directed to home care, NORC's and PACE programs to allow seniors to live their final years in dignity. Not locked up in an institution.
It has been proven over and over that health care dollars can be saved through fewer emergency room visits and shorter hospital stays when we implement these type of programs for the elderly.
If Premier Doug Ford is actually serious about redesigning our health care system to make it more effective, this is the perfect place to start. The U.S. and Denmark are just a few locations that can supply the data regarding what is actually required to save health care dollars while at the same time treating the elderly with respect.
Ceciley White, Ajax

Document 246 of 500

Canadians must stand up to Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-must-stand-up-trump/docview/3150264701/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+must+stand+up+to+Trump&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He isn't even president yet but Donald Trump has been mocking Canadians with ridiculous taunts; the demented ramblings of an old man.
I don't know about you but I would prefer if Canadians weren't so polite about all the insults coming our way from south of the border. How about we hurl a few insults back at Trump? Like: Most of us would have voted for Kamala Harris, not you. Or: Why don't you get a real socialized health care system instead of the pathetic one you have. Or: What's the matter? America running out of water?
First it was the joke about Canada becoming the 51st state and referring to Justin Trudeau as "governor." He even posted an image of himself on his Truth Social platform beside the Canadian flag staring triumphantly at the supposed Rocky Mountains; except it was a Swiss mountain.
His son, Eric, then posted on X a doctored image of Trump smugly looking at maps of Canada, Greenland, and Panama (he also wants them as part of his empire) on the Amazon website. "We are so back" the junior Trump wrote above it. As if annexing Canada would be that simple.
Of course this taunting is all about the 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods that Trump has threatened to impose the moment he takes office on Jan. 20 if Canada doesn't do more to stop the minuscule amounts of illegal immigrants and fentanyl from the crossing the U.S border. Canada quickly came up with plans to spend $1 billion to beef up our side.
Will it be enough? Who knows? Trump refers to the fact that Canada sells more to the U.S. than we buy from them as a "subsidy." Does he not understand how international trade works? It appears not.
This all comes at a particularly bad time for Trudeau, who is hanging on to power by a frayed thread. He not only has the majority of Canadians, his caucus and the opposition hounding him to leave office, he has Trump on the sidelines gleefully launching sour lemons.
When Trudeau's most faithful minister Chrystia Freeland resigned from cabinet a couple of weeks ago Trump jumped right in with a blistering post on Truth Social. "The Great State of Canada is stunned as the Finance Minister resigns, or was fired, from her position by Gov. Justin Trudeau. Her behaviour was totally toxic, and not at all conducive to making deals which are good for the very unhappy citizens of Canada. She will not be missed!!!"
He was referring to the hard-nosed response Freeland gave his negotiators during the wrangling over the new NAFTA.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has threatened to cut off the electricity the province sends to the U.S. if Trump goes ahead with the tariffs. But another premier, Alberta's Danielle Smith, is only too pleased to subjugate herself to Trump's demands.
She has already set up an Alberta border patrol and is planning on attending Trump's inauguration so she can talk to all his cronies and maybe even the man himself about exempting from the proposed tariff all the Alberta oil that is shipped to the U.S.
No Liberal - Trudeau, a member of his shrinking cabinet, or any Liberal MP - has taken a shot back. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Canada would never become the 51st state and has mumbled a few words about getting along with our neighbour. On the other hand, NDP MP Charlie Angus told Trump to "piss off" after he heard about the insults that he levelled at us on Christmas Day.
That's more like it. If you don't stand up to a bully he will keep on taunting until you are nothing more than a shrivelled piece of lettuce. And if it's like this now, it's only going to get worse after he assumes the presidency. Trump isn't backing down from his tariff threat, so it's time to start standing up for ourselves.
Gillian Steward is a Calgary
writer and journalist, and former
managing editor of the Calgary Herald. She is a freelance columnist for The Star.
CREDIT: Gillian Steward OPINION Gillian Steward is a Calgary writer and journalist, and former managing editor of the Calgary Herald. She is a freelance columnist for The Star.;

Subject: Canadians; Border patrol; Tariffs; Canada-US relations

Document 247 of 500

No more being polite. Canadians must stand up to Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-more-being-polite-canadians-must-stand-up/docview/3150264484/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

CAPTION: Former Foreign Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland was insulted on social media by president-elect Donald Trump this month after she announced her resignation from cabinet.

Document 248 of 500

Trudeau pushed to make decision on future: Business leaders raise concerns over 'chaos' as Trump's tariff threat looms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-pushed-make-decision-on-future/docview/3150264344/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+pushed+to+make+decision+on+future%3A+Business+leaders+raise+concerns+over+%27chaos%27+as+Trump%27s+tariff+threat+looms&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pressure is mounting on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to finally decide whether to resign or cling on, with some Liberal MPs urging him to quickly make up his mind, and one major business group raising concerns about "chaos" at the centre of Canadian political power.
The uncertainty over who will lead the country into the new year comes barely three weeks before Donald Trump is set to return to the White House, and on the heels of Chrystia Freeland's shock resignation as Trudeau's finance minister and deputy prime minister. Trump has promised to impose a punishing 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian goods on Jan. 20, the day he is sworn in as president of the United States, a threat that has rattled business and labour organizations across the country.
Mark Gerretsen, the Liberal MP for Kingston and the Islands, said on Monday that - as reported in the Star - the party's 75 caucus members from Ontario "overwhelmingly" agreed in a recent meeting that Trudeau must resign as prime minister. The Liberal party's Atlantic MPs have made a similar demand for the prime minister to step down and appoint an interim leader.
"It's really difficult for him to come to any other conclusion at this point," Gerretsen said of Trudeau's potential resignation, adding that it is hard to remain in leadership "if your team is losing faith in you."
Gerretsen added that most of his constituents want Trudeau to go, and that the prime minister should make his decision "right away."
To Matthew Holmes, chief of public policy at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, the federal government appears caught up in its own political drama.
He said business groups are worried that time spent on Liberal leadership intrigue would be better spent countering Trump's tariff threat.
"There's just a vacuum right now and there's chaos at the centre," Holmes said. "We need clear action from the government, and the business community needs certainty."
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, said it is not time to "panic" about the economic damage of Trump's promised actions, even though it is unclear who is the "point person" in Ottawa dealing with the machinations of the U.S.-Canada relationship.
That's especially the case after Freeland, whom Volpe called the most competent person in government on the issue, crashed out as Trudeau's second-in-command and finance minister. In her resignation letter, made public just hours before she was set to table an economic update in Parliament, Freeland said the government must take Trump's tariff threats "extremely seriously" and that she had been at odds with Trudeau about "the best path forward for Canada."
"Who's leading the narrative politically?" Volpe said. "That's not a rhetorical question. The answer is nobody."
Since Freeland's resignation, Trudeau has been silent about his leadership, even as pressure continues to grow. Dozens of Liberal MPs have now come forward to call for him to resign. Trudeau spoke briefly to reporters about the Canada-U.S. relationship after new cabinet ministers were sworn in more than a week ago, then headed to British Columbia for a ski vacation.
Meanwhile, opposition parties are positioning for an election, with the Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and most recently the New Democratic Party - who supported the minority Liberals for more than two years - signalling they are ready to declare no confidence in the Liberal government.
Such a move, which could happen in the coming weeks, would trigger a national election.
That timeline is only adding to the urgency felt by Liberals who believe the party - which has trailed badly in the polls for more than a year - is better off without Trudeau.
"Until he says he's stepping down, he's staying and digging in. And him staying is untenable for our party and the country," said New Brunswick MP Wayne Long, who, earlier this year, was the first elected Liberal to call publicly for Trudeau to resign.
"We will continue to speak and put as much pressure as we can on our colleagues, and especially cabinet, to speak up and do what's right. We all know it. We won't back off."
On Friday, Calgary MP George Chahal released an open letter calling for Trudeau to step down. He said there is no path forward for the prime minister now and that he needs to leave.
"Any rational individual in a position of leadership would resign. Any group of individuals providing advice on data and logic would reach the same conclusion," he said.
Chahal also said the prime minister is getting bad advice from people who aren't thinking about the long term.
"Unfortunately, a small cabal have decided to pursue a reckless strategy of mutual assured political destruction. It is clear the Liberal Party of Canada is not their priority," Chahal's letter said.
Kody Blois, the Nova Scotia MP who chairs the party's Atlantic caucus, also penned a letter on behalf of the region's MPs, writing the government cannot delay any further in the face of Trump's threatened tariffs and the possibility of looming confidence votes.
Blois's letter called for a national caucus meeting - something that many MPs outside of the Atlantic also want, said one Liberal MP, who spoke on condition of anonymity Monday to discuss internal party matters.
The MP - who personally wants the prime minister to resign - said such a meeting would give Trudeau the chance to provide an answer after months of pressure over his leadership.
"The prime minister needs to decide what he's doing," the MP said.
If Trudeau decides to resign, one key question will be whether he stays on until a new leader is chosen or whether he is immediately replaced by an interim prime minister.
For Gerretsen, the Liberal MP from Kingston, there is still time to hold a contest that would let party members choose a new leader before the next election.
He said the question of whether an interim leader would replace Trudeau should be up to the prime minister and Liberal party brass - if it comes down to that.
"It depends on where he sees himself in this moving forward," he said.
A spokesperson from Trudeau's office declined to comment Monday.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall and Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star
