Document 249 of 500

'There's chaos at the centre': Business leaders, MPs push Trudeau to decide on his future

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-chaos-at-centre-business-leaders-mps-push/docview/3150429699/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There%27s+chaos+at+the+centre%27%3A+Business+leaders%2C+MPs+push+Trudeau+to+decide+on+his+future&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Pressure is mounting on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to finally decide whether to resign or cling on, with some Liberal MPs urging him to quickly make up his mind, and one major business group raising concerns about "chaos" at the centre of Canadian political power.
The uncertainty over who will lead the country into the new year comes barely three weeks before Donald Trump is set to return to the White House, and on the heels of Chrystia Freeland's shock resignation as Trudeau's finance minister and deputy prime minister. Trump has promised to impose a punishing 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian goods on Jan. 20, the day he is sworn in as president of the United States, a threat that has rattled business and labour organizations across the country.
Mark Gerretsen, the Liberal MP for Kingston and the Islands, said on Monday that - as reported in the Star - the party's 75 caucus members from Ontario "overwhelmingly" agreed in a recent meeting that Trudeau must resign as prime minister. The Liberal party's Atlantic MPs have made a similar demand for the prime minister to step down and appoint an interim leader.
"It's really difficult for him to come to any other conclusion at this point," said Gerretsen of Trudeau's potential resignation, adding that it is hard to remain in leadership "if your team is losing faith in you."
Gerretsen added that most of his constituents want Trudeau to go, and that the prime minister should make his decision "right away."
To Matthew Holmes, chief of public policy at the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, the federal government appears caught up in its own political drama. He said business groups are worried that time spent on Liberal leadership intrigue would be better spent countering Trump's tariff threat.
"There's just a vacuum right now and there's chaos at the centre," Holmes said.
"We need clear action from the government, and the business community needs certainty."
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, said it is not time to "panic" about the economic damage of Trump's promised actions, even though it is unclear who is the "point person" in Ottawa dealing with the machinations of the U.S.-Canada relationship.
That's especially the case after Freeland, whom Volpe called the most competent person in government on the issue, crashed out as Trudeau's second-in-command and finance minister. In her resignation letter, made public just hours before she was set to table an economic update in Parliament, Freeland said the government must take Trump's tariff threats "extremely seriously" and that she had been at odds with Trudeau about "the best path forward for Canada."
"Who's leading the narrative politically?" Volpe said. "That's not a rhetorical question. The answer is nobody."
Since Freeland's resignation, Trudeau has been silent about his leadership, even as pressure continues to grow. Dozens of Liberal MPs have now come forward to call for him to resign. Trudeau spoke briefly to reporters about the Canada-U.S. relationship after new cabinet ministers were sworn in more than a week ago, then headed to British Columbia for a ski vacation.
Meanwhile, opposition parties are positioning for an election, with the Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and most recently the New Democratic Party - who supported the minority Liberals for more than two years - signalling they are ready to declare no confidence in the Liberal government.
Such a move, which could happen in the coming weeks, would trigger a national election.
That timeline is only adding to the urgency felt by Liberals who believe the party - which has trailed badly in the polls for more than a year - is better off without Trudeau.
"Until he says he's stepping down, he's staying and digging in. And him staying is untenable for our party and the country," said New Brunswick MP Wayne Long, who, earlier this year, was the first elected Liberal to call publicly for Trudeau to resign.
"We will continue to speak and put as much pressure as we can on our colleagues, and especially cabinet, to speak up and do what's right. We all know it. We won't back off."
On Friday, Calgary MP George Chahal released an open letter calling for Trudeau to step down. He said there is no path forward for the prime minister now and that he needs to leave.
"Any rational individual in a position of leadership would resign. Any group of individuals providing advice on data and logic would reach the same conclusion," he said.
Chahal also said the prime minister is getting bad advice from people who aren't thinking about the long term.
"Unfortunately, a small cabal have decided to pursue a reckless strategy of mutual assured political destruction. It is clear the Liberal Party of Canada is not their priority," Chahal's letter said.
Kody Blois, the Nova Scotia MP who chairs the party's Atlantic caucus, also penned a letter on behalf of the region's MPs, writing that the government cannot delay any further in the face of Trump's threatened tariffs and the possibility of looming confidence votes.
Blois's letter called for a national caucus meeting - something that many MPs outside the Atlantic also want, said one Liberal MP, who spoke on condition of anonymity Monday to discuss internal party matters.
The MP - who personally wants the prime minister to resign - said such a meeting would give Trudeau the chance to provide an answer after months of pressure over his leadership.
"The prime minister needs to decide what he's doing," the MP said.
If Trudeau decides to resign, one key question will be whether he stays on until a new leader is chosen, or whether he is immediately replaced by an interim prime minister.
For Gerretsen, the Liberal MP from Kingston, there is still time to hold a contest that would let party members choose a new leader before the next election. He said the question of whether an interim leader would replace Trudeau should be up to the prime minister and Liberal party brass - if it comes to that.
"It depends on where he sees himself in this moving forward," he said.
A spokesperson from Trudeau's office declined to comment on Monday.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau attends a swearing-in ceremony for cabinet members at Rideau Hall on Dec. 20. Trudeau has been silent about whether he intends to remain as leader.

Subject: Political power; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 250 of 500

Infrastructure the city must deliver on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/infrastructure-city-must-deliver-on/docview/3150093744/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Infrastructure+the+city+must+deliver+on&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto is accused of being an unplanned city. Chaotic, with metastasizing development, bad transit and roads that don't connect.
It's also accused of being planned too much, with suffocating, punitive and exclusionary rules. Snuffing out creativity and freedom, making the city boring. Like last week, when the possibility of small businesses in neighbourhoods was denied and sent for more consultation by Toronto city council. Endless consultation.
Both things are true in Toronto. Beyond a few small plans, there was never a guiding master plan for Toronto from the beginning. Toronto grew steadily but haphazardly as large estates were subdivided into neighbourhoods and smaller villages were gobbled up as the city expanded. It's why some streets, such as Dupont Street at Ossington Avenue, have odd kinks in them as different parts were knitted together in an imperfect quilt.
This is true for many big cities. Toronto isn't unique in making improvements while people are still living and working in it. It's a tricky thing, and that's where good planning comes in. It's why cities deal with constant construction: They are eternal renovation jobs. It's also why they're exciting, surprising and often frustrating.
Toronto is forever trying to catch up to the city it has become, scrambling to create new infrastructure. Not a new phenomenon, either. Before the Yonge Street subway was opened in 1954, photos from the preceding decade show a street completely clogged with traffic, both automobiles and streetcar after streetcar stuck in it. Twas ever thus, and surely that scene elicited the same kind of sentiments that Toronto traffic and big city life do today.
Frustration is something known very well to residents of Liberty Village, a neighbourhood many Torontonians love to hate. Nearly entirely made up of residential buildings, the neighbourhood goads on Toronto's age-old antipathy for apartment dwellers, a city where political power brokers, politicians and the cultural elite have largely been house owners.
It's also very dense, triggering Torontonian fear of actually being a big city. In other parts of town, when a neighbourhood is upset about a new development, I've seen "not another Liberty Village" messages.
I've written that Liberty Village represents a hero neighbourhood because its residents take on growth other neighbourhoods refuse. That's deliberate planning, too: Toronto forces new housing into relatively few places, slivers of land, really, where it has little choice but to go up high to accommodate all the demand, while barely allowing any growth or density in vast swaths of the city.
It's a planning regime supported and upheld by resident groups across the city, but also by politicians on the left and right alike, a neat political dovetail.
Even Premier Doug Ford, bullish on homebuilding in rhetoric, has proven to be deeply NIMBY in action by refusing to push through reforms that might actually allow more kinds of homes built in more places.
Consequently Toronto has heavy-lifting neighbourhoods like Liberty Village with big city density, which is completely fine, but lacking amenities, a quality public realm and transportation options.
There should be a grand bargain: If our leaders are going to stuff so many people into the same places, they ought to be provided with fantastic infrastructure. Theoretically, anyway.
There are efforts - more planning - to add some of this in. A big new park at 34 Hanna Ave. is currently in the early design phase and could be a great city square kind of place. It's a block north of the Exhibition GO station currently being upgraded for better train service, also anticipating the Ontario Line subway eventually terminating there. (It's a Metrolinx project, so predict when at your own risk.)
Critically, but unfortunately, there's Smart Track, former mayor John Tory's nearly imaginary 2014 campaign promise best known for its incredible and consistent shrinkage, from 22 stations to just five. It was to create King-Liberty station and give area residents another proper way to get in and out of their neighbourhood.
With ever-tighter budgets, city council voted to prioritize just three of the remaining SmartTrack stations during their December council meeting: St. Clair-Old Weston, Bloor-Lansdowne and East Harbour, leaving King-Liberty and Finch-Kennedy in limbo as good projects but waiting for somebody, anybody, to fund them.
With all due respect to the folks around any of those stations, none are located by the kind of heaving density that exists in Liberty Village. Work was underway, too, as properties on the north side of the proposed King-Liberty station were demolished to create space for it, including the loss of legendary music and club venue, 99 Sudbury.
Toronto's exclusionary planning regime is already troublesome. If both Toronto and the province of Ontario can't find funding for King-Liberty station quickly, it puts the lie to how Toronto has planned itself and is an insult to every hero resident of Liberty Village who might have thought there was a grand bargain at work. King-Liberty must be built.
CAPTION: A decade ago, residents of Liberty Village, with its heaving density and lack of transportation options, were promised King-Liberty station to give them another proper way to get in and out of their neighbourhood, writes Shawn Micallef.Nick LachanceToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Infrastructure; Political power; Neighborhoods; Planning; Quality of life; Urban planning; Criticism

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: Infrastructure the city must deliver on

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 30, 2024

Document 251 of 500

Let's close the book on yet another year of living stupidly

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lets-close-book-on-yet-another-year-living/docview/3150093537/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Let%27s+close+the+book+on+yet+another+year+of+living+stupidly&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was another year of living stupidly.
In Toronto and hereabouts.
A city council that went far left-away and a Queen's Park dictatorship that willy-nilly took away traditional municipal powers. Local pols chafed; the premier paid no mind.
Carjackings became the crime of massive choice, often with juvie-proof teens at the wheel. Catch me if you can nyah-nyah-nyah. But offenders were still mostly dumb as a sack of hammers. Cops too, on occasion.
We went gaga for a certain pop princess who graced us with her concert presence for six nights at the Rogers Centre, which was a hell of a lot more entertaining than the disastrous Blue Jays. Though it's impossible to un-see Prime Minister Justin Trudeau dad-dancing.
We shook ourselves woke and banished common sense. Raged in chronic traffic snarls and pedalled (or e-biked) in the entitled lanes. Picked our way around endless protests and demonstrations. Saw our parks turned into grotty encampments. But everybody has to be somewhere.
It was a very bad horrible no good year.
Good riddance, 2024.
Forthwith, one last cringing look back at the year that was:
Hup-hup-hike: Team Olivia Chow brings in a record 9.5 per cent property tax hike after proposed 16.5 per cent hike was shot down.
Cha-chinging in the rain: City bureaucrats consider bringing in a "rain tax."
Coffee, tea or flee: Growing number of crew members with Pakistan International Airlines vanish in Toronto during layovers to seek asylum.
Village idiots: Black Creek Pioneer Village drops "Pioneer" from its name in genuflecting to inclusivity.
Axe-holes: Under cover of darkness, province begins cutting down 865 trees at Ontario Place.
Sip of the tongue: Man going 50 kilometres per hour above the speed limit charged after cracking open a can of beer to celebrate his new driver's licence.
And you're mad that they lost your luggage: An Air Canada passenger opens a cabin door and falls out of the plane at Toronto's Pearson airport.
Nuts and bolt: Thieves steal $70,000 worth of pistachios from a parked transport truck.
M'academia nuts: U of T quietly grants amnesty to pro-Palestinian demonstrators, in exchange for ending their encampment occupation.
Heisted by their own petard: Bandits blow up an ATM bank drive-thru with explosives, come away with nothing but singed hair.
Bawdy and soul: Toronto is ranked seventh among cities for sex toy aficionados - handcuffs, vibrators and the like - according to Canada's largest online store for adult novelty products.
Kitschy coo: Former city councillor George Mammoliti advertises his Rob Ford bobblehead doll for sale at the reduced price of $1,800.
Déjà DUI: A man is charged with impaired driving after he drove to the police station to complain about an earlier arrest for public intoxication.
Spell chekcd: City apologizes for misspellings - "Lake Shore Trial'' and "Downton'' - on new bicycle path signs.
Jabber-wokey: Teachers with the Toronto District School Board are issued a document that describes education as a "colonial structure'' designed for "the benefit of the dominant culture (i.e. white, middle-upper class, male, Christian, cisgender, heterosexual, able-bodied, neurotypical)" and that "must be actively decolonized."
The missing linx: Metrolinx admits (again) that the Eglinton Crosstown is nowhere near ready to open. Construction began in 2011.
We're No. 1!Finally. At something. Dutch mapping firm labels Toronto the worst city in North America for traffic.
Or No. 2: Wrong again social media has a conniption over false claims that immigrants are defecating in the sand along Wasaga Beach.
Who's that girl anyway? During the first of two sold-out shows at Scotiabank Arena, Madonna greets the crowd with: "Hello Boston!"
Brinksmanship: Brink's and Air Canada sue each other over a $22-million gold heist of a cargo container at Pearson airport.
Undercarriage trade: Teen carjacker runs over owner with his own stolen Porsche, leaving him with minor injuries.
Taking the credits: Grade 12 students at a York Catholic school are given 100 per cent midterm marks because teachers couldn't be found to fill staff absences.
Crowd sourcing: Landlord offers $720-a-month shared rental space - three pup tents in one room.
Ban-ditto: A man already subject to five lifetime driving bans is charged again for getting behind the wheel of a stolen car.
Should have gone with the "Nigerian Prince" instead: Twenty-five-year-old man is accused of being behind a Ponzi scheme that scammed $40 million out of investors.
Flock off: Mating red-winged blackbirds attack humans, Hitchcockian style.
Tale from the crypts: Aggressive squirrels attack humans at Mount Pleasant Cemetery.
La dolce vita: Scarborough Coun. Nick Mantas's nine-day trip to Italy costs taxpayers $16,600 - $7,800 over his initial estimate.
Possessed by spirits: Man faces more than 280 charges for stealing booze valued at $88,000 from 45 LCBO locations.
Fut-the-bol: Toronto's tab for staging World Cup 2026 games: $380 million, $80 million over initial estimate.
Disintegrating centre of the universe: City executive report says Toronto needs $26 billion over the next 10 years to prevent infrastructure decay.
Shana-canned: After another playoff choke, Maple Leafs sack Sheldon Keefe.
Vroom with a view: First Canadian Place applies to convert vacant office space into giant go-kart track.
Rips The 6ix: Reflecting on his 2013 trade to the Raptors, Rudy Gay snipes: "You think I wanted to go to Toronto? If I wanted to get traded why the f--- would I go to Toronto?''
Swing and another miss: Stud free agent Juan Soto picks the Mets over the Blue Jays.
Cop & robber: Toronto drug squad detective, stopped after driving erratically, is charged with possession of cocaine and meth for the purpose of trafficking, allegedly seized after executing a search warrant earlier in the day.
Square au contraire: New cost of rebranding Yonge-Dundas Square as Sankofa Square: $860,000, or $525,000 more than what the city originally estimated.
God's Plan for people who make too much money recording crap rap: Drake loses $1 million betting on Edmonton Oilers to win the Stanley Cup and Dallas Mavericks to win the NBA Finals.
DIY arrest: Coun. (and ex-cop) Jon Burnside wrestles masked man to the ground who he suspected of stealing goods from Home Depot.
Tested negative for brains: Adamson Barbecue owner Adam Skelly, who famously staged anti-mask protests and ignored lockdowns at his establishment, is convicted on 17 municipal charges.
About face: Brampton Civic Hospital apologizes for idiotically shaving the beard off an elderly Sikh patient without his consent.
Conveyer belted: Consumer satisfaction survey declares Pearson the second-worst major airport in North America.
Monopoly money: Rogers pays $4.7 billion to acquire Bell's 37.5 per cent stake in Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment.
Crosshairs hairy: Police car and its two cop occupants are caught in the middle of a shootout on Queen Street West where some 100 shots were fired.
Watch this space: City unveils 22 street signs welcoming Taylor Swift to town.
Beep-beep whoop-whoop: Taylor Swift gets a motorcade and police escort to Rogers Centre.
Taylor $wift: Estimate of economic impact on Toronto from her six shows: $282 million.
Also armed with a bubble-blower: Fifty-eight-year-old Ontario woman is charged for allegedly squirting her neighbour with a child's water gun.
Tossing my cookies: Oreo is named new helmet sponsor for the Leafs.
Slide rulers: Toronto bans tobogganing at 45 hills across the city.
NOOOO Money: Bad Boy goes officially bankrupt.
Web of malice: Debilitating ransomware attack knocks out Toronto Public Library online service for four months.
Crispy critter: A raccoon knocks out power to a wide swath of downtown Toronto, leaving 7,000 customers in the dark.
Curtain call: A man is sentenced to 28 months in prison after tricking two other men into believing that they were talking to a woman online and inviting them to his home to have anonymous sex through a hole in a sheet hanging in a doorway.
Whisked AGO-go: Reception for G7 leaders at the Art Gallery of Ontario hosted by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is cancelled when slogan-shouting pro-Palestinian demonstrators block entrances.
No good deeds: City hall receives more than 55,000 complaints over botched rollout of its vacant home tax assessments.
Flushed Raptor: Jontay Porter is banned from the NBA for disclosing confidential information to sports betters and wagering on games, even betting on his own team to lose.
Bunco squad: Highest-ranking Black female Toronto cop pleads guilty to misconduct for providing confidential information to six Black constables she was mentoring ahead of promotional interviews.
Smack-ed down: Federal government rejects Toronto Board of Health request to decriminalize hard drugs, including heroin and meth, for personal use.
A place to stand, a place to grow, Ontari-ari-no-no-go: Ontario Science Centre abruptly closes its doors for good, ordered by Premier Doug Ford's government.
Gross anatomy: Matumaini, a two-year-old giraffe, dies while under anesthesia for castration at Toronto Zoo.
Drive, he said: Premier Ford vows to remove bike lanes on sections of Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue.
Rocks and roll: Jewelry thefts spike by more than 80 per cent across GTA.
Taking the piss: Senior Toronto detective accused of sexual harassment against four male constables for, among other allegations, "jokingly" giving a cop a cookie rubbed against his genitals and snapping photo of a cop on the toilet.
Bow-lagged: Famous British cellist has to cancel his concert in Toronto because Air Canada denied him a seat for his instrument.
MAGA carta: Controversial psychologist and former U of T professor Jordan Peterson announces he's moving to the U.S.
Sic 'em, Roxy: Carjacker makes off with Jeep Grand Cherokee and four-year-old chocolate Labrador in the back seat.
Laughing all the way: Porch bandits steal Christmas trees and decorations.
And hey, Happy New Year.
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Document 252 of 500

Toronto and a stupid 2024: From politics to crime to Taylor Swift, a retrospective on the cringe during a no good year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-stupid-2024-politics-crime-taylor-swift/docview/3150093325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+and+a+stupid+2024%3A+From+politics+to+crime+to+Taylor+Swift%2C+a+retrospective+on+the+cringe+during+a+no+good+year&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was another year of living stupidly.
In Toronto and hereabouts.
A city council that went far left-away and a Queen's Park dictatorship that willy-nilly took away traditional municipal powers. Local pols chafed; the premier paid no mind.
Carjackings became the crime of massive choice, often with juvie-proof teens at the wheel. Catch me if you can nyah-nyah-nyah. But offenders were still mostly dumb as a sack of hammers. Cops too, on occasion.
We went gaga for a certain pop princess who graced us with her concert presence for six nights at the Rogers Centre, which was a hell of a lot more entertaining than the disastrous Blue Jays. Though it's impossible to un-see Prime Minister Justin Trudeau dad-dancing.
We shook ourselves woke and banished common sense. Raged in chronic traffic snarls and pedalled (or e-biked) in the entitled lanes. Picked our way around endless protests and demonstrations. Saw our parks turned into grotty encampments. But everybody has to be somewhere.
It was a very bad horrible no good year.
Good riddance, 2024.
Forthwith, one last cringing look back at the year that was:
Hup-hup-hike: Team Olivia Chow brings in a record 9.5 per cent property tax hike after proposed 16.5 per cent hike was shot down.
Cha-chinging in the rain: City bureaucrats consider bringing in a "rain tax."
Coffee, tea or flee: Growing number of crew members with Pakistan International Airlines vanish in Toronto during layovers to seek asylum.
Village idiots: Black Creek Pioneer Village drops "Pioneer" from its name in genuflecting to inclusivity.
Axe-holes: Under cover of darkness, province begins cutting down 865 trees at Ontario Place.
Sip of the tongue: Man going 50 kilometres per hour above the speed limit charged after cracking open a can of beer to celebrate his new driver's licence.
And you're mad that they lost your luggage: An Air Canada passenger opens a cabin door and falls out of the plane at Toronto's Pearson airport.
Nuts and bolt: Thieves steal $70,000 worth of pistachios from a parked transport truck.
M'academia nuts: U of T quietly grants amnesty to pro-Palestinian demonstrators, in exchange for ending their encampment occupation.
Heisted by their own petard: Bandits blow up an ATM bank drive-thru with explosives, come away with nothing but singed hair.
Bawdy and soul: Toronto is ranked seventh among cities for sex toy aficionados - handcuffs, vibrators and the like - according to Canada's largest online store for adult novelty products.
Kitschy coo: Former city councillor George Mammoliti advertises his Rob Ford bobblehead doll for sale at the reduced price of $1,800.
Deja DUI: A man is charged with impaired driving after he drove to the police station to complain about an earlier arrest for public intoxication.
Spell chekcd: City apologizes for misspellings - "Lake Shore Trial'' and "Downton'' - on new bicycle path signs.
Jabber-wokey: Teachers with the Toronto District School Board are issued a document that describes education as a "colonial structure'' designed for "the benefit of the dominant culture (i.e. white, middle-upper class, male, Christian, cisgender, heterosexual, able-bodied, neurotypical'' and "must be actively decolonized."
The missing linx: Metrolinx admits (again) that the Eglinton Crosstown is nowhere near ready to open. Construction began in 2011.
We're Number One! Finally. At something. Dutch mapping firm labels Toronto the worst city in North America for traffic.
Or number two: Wrong again social media has a conniption over false claims that immigrants are defecating in the sand along Wasaga Beach.
Who's that girl anyway? During the first of two sold-out shows at Scotiabank Arena, Madonna greets the crowd with: "Hello Boston!"
Brinksmanship: Brink's and Air Canada sue each other over a $22-million gold heist of cargo container at Pearson Airport.
Undercarriage trade: Teen carjacker runs over owner with his own stolen Porsche, leaving him with minor injuries.
Taking the credits: Grade 12 students at a York Catholic school are given 100 per cent midterm marks because teachers couldn't be found to fill staff absences.
Crowd sourcing: Landlord offers $720-a-month shared rental space - three pup tents in one room.
Ban-ditto: A man already subject to five lifetime driving bans is charged again for getting behind the wheel of a stolen car.
Should have gone with the "Nigerian Prince" instead: Twenty-five-year-old man is accused of being behind a Ponzi scheme that scammed $40 million out of investors.
Flock off: Mating red-winged blackbirds attack humans, Hitchcockian style.
Tale from the crypts: Aggressive squirrels attack humans at Mount Pleasant Cemetery.
La dolce vita: Scarborough councillor Nick Mantas' nine-day trip to Italy costs taxpayers $16,600 - $7,800 over his initial estimate.
Possessed by spirits: Man faces more than 280 charges for stealing booze valued at $88,000 from 45 LCBO locations.
Fut-the-bol: Toronto's tab for staging World Cup 2026 games: $380 million, $80 million over initial estimate.
Disintegrating centre of the universe: City executive report says Toronto needs $26,000,000,000 over the next 10 years to prevent infrastructure decay.
Shana-canned: After another playoff choke, Maple Leafs sack Sheldon Keefe.
Vroom with a view: First Canadian Place applies to convert vacant office space into a massive go-kart track.
Rips The 6ix: Reflecting on his 2013 trade to the Raptors, Rudy Gay snipes: "You think I wanted to go to Toronto? If I wanted to get traded why the f--k would I go to Toronto?''
Swing and another miss: Stud free agent Juan Soto picks the Mets over the Blue Jays.
Cop & robber: Toronto drug squad detective, stopped after driving erratically, is charged with possession of cocaine and meth for the purpose of trafficking, allegedly seized after executing a search warrant earlier in the day.
Square au contraire: New cost of rebranding Yonge-Dundas Square as Sankofa Square: $860,000, or $525,000 more than what the city originally estimated.
God's Plan for people who make too much money recording crap rap: Drake loses $1 million betting on Edmonton Oilers to win the Stanley Cup and Dallas Mavericks to win the NBA finals.
DIY arrest: Councillor (and ex-cop) Jon Burnside wrestles masked man to the ground who he suspected of stealing goods from Home Depot.
Tested negative for brains: Adamson Barbecue owner Adam Skelly, who famously staged anti-mask protests and ignored lockdowns at his establishment, is convicted on 17 municipal charges.
About face: Brampton Civic Hospital apologizes for idiotically shaving the beard off an elderly Sikh patient without his consent.
Conveyer belted: Consumer satisfaction survey declares Pearson the second-worst major airport in North America.
Monopoly money: Rogers pays $4,700,000,000 to acquire Bell's 37.5 per cent stake in Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment.
Crosshairs hairy: Police car and its two cop occupants are caught in the middle of a shootout on Queen Street West where some 100 shots were fired.
Watch this space: City unveils 22 street signs welcoming Taylor Swift to town.
Beep-beep whoop-whoop: Taylor Swift gets a motorcade and police escort to Rogers Centre.
Taylor $wift: Estimate of economic impact on Toronto from her six shows: $282 million.
Also armed with a bubble-blower: Fifty-eight-year-old Ontario woman is charged for allegedly squirting her neighbour with a child's water gun.
Tossing my cookies: Oreo is named new helmet sponsor for the Leafs.
Slide rulers: Toronto bans tobogganing at 45 hills across the city.
NOOOO Money: Bad Boy goes officially bankrupt.
Web of malice: Debilitating ransomware attack knocks out Toronto Public Library online service for four months.
Crispy critter: A raccoon knocks out power to a wide swath of downtown Toronto, leaving 7,000 customers in the dark.
Curtain call: Man sentenced to 28 months in prison after tricking two men into believing they were talking to a woman online and inviting them to his home to have anonymous sex through a hole in a sheet hanging in a doorway.
Whisked AGO-go: Reception for G7 leaders at the Art Gallery of Ontario hosted by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is cancelled when slogan-shouting pro-Palestinian demonstrators block entrances.
No good deeds: City hall receives more than 55,000 complaints over botched rollout of its vacant home tax assessments.
Flushed Raptor: Jontay Porter is banned from the NBA for disclosing confidential information to sports betters and wagering on games, even betting on his own team to lose.
Bunco squad: Highest ranking Black female Toronto cop pleads guilty to misconduct for providing confidential information to six Black constables she was mentoring ahead of promotional interviews.
Smack-ed down: Federal government rejects Toronto Board of Health request to decriminalize hard drugs, including heroin and meth, for personal use.
A place to stand, a place to grow, Ontari-ari-no-no-go: Ontario Science Centre abruptly closes its doors for good, ordered by Premier Doug Ford's government.
Gross anatomy: Matumaini, a two-year-old giraffe, dies while under anesthesia for castration at Toronto Zoo.
Drive, he said: Premier Ford vows to remove bike lanes on sections of Bloor Street, Yonge Street and University Avenue.
Rocks and roll: Jewelry thefts spike by more than 80 per cent across GTA.
Taking the piss: Senior Toronto detective accused of sexual harassment against four male constables for, among other allegations, "jokingly'' giving a cop a cookie rubbed against his genitals and snapping photo of a cop on the toilet.
Bow-lagged: Famous British cellist has to cancel his concert in Toronto because Air Canada denied him a seat for his instrument.
MAGA carta: Controversial psychologist and former U of T professor Jordan Peterson announces he's moving to the U.S.
Sic 'em, Roxy: Carjacker makes off with Jeep Grand Cherokee and four-year-old chocolate Labrador in the back seat.
Laughing all the way: Porch bandits steal Christmas trees and decorations.
And hey, happy new year.
CAPTION: Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, left, with councillor Shelley Carroll during budget day at Toronto City Hall on Feb. 14.Tree removal at Ontario Place's West Island on Oct. 4.Pro-Palestine protesters dismantle the encampment at the U of T Kings College Circle on July 3.Metrolinx gives a technical update on the Eglinton Crosstown LRT on Dec. 8.Taylor Swift on the first night of her Eras tour concerts in Toronto.Bike lanes in Toronto became a focal point of the Doug Ford government.From crime to politics to the city's never-ending infrastructure issues, here's everything that went wrong in 2024, according to Star columnist Rosie DiManno.
