Document 274 of 500

Guys just want to have puns: In 'Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl,' Tom Cruise and 007 aren't safe from the spoofing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/guys-just-want-have-puns/docview/3148768498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guys+just+want+to+have+puns%3A+In+%27Wallace+%26amp%3B+Gromit%3A+Vengeance+Most+Fowl%2C%27+Tom+Cruise+and+007+aren%27t+safe+from+the+spoofing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Butter me crumpets! Getting a new "Wallace & Gromit" movie from Aardman Animations at Christmas is like receiving a tin of home-baked goodies from a beloved relative across the pond.
"Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl" continues the handmade quality and stop-motion whimsy of cheese-gobbling inventor Wallace (voiced by Ben Whitehead, replacing the late Peter Sallis) and his silently faithful canine pal, Gromit, in their quintessentially British exploits.
A bonus this time is the return of adorably evil nemesis Feathers McGraw, a mute and menacing penguin who hasn't had a major role in an Aardman movie for three decades, although he's made cameo appearances.
It's been 31 years since "The Wrong Trousers," Nick Park's stop-motion short that introduced us to the beady-eyed McGraw, a resourceful but delusional felon whose red rubber-glove headpiece fools only the foolish into thinking he's a chicken. McGraw is now also a jailbird, ignominiously incarcerated in a zoo after being thwarted by Wallace and his pooch for attempting to steal the much-prized Blue Diamond, a rare item of serious value in the blue-collar world of Park's creation.
"Vengeance Most Fowl," the second "Wallace & Gromit" feature (it follows 2005's Oscar-winning "The Curse of the Were-Rabbit"), picks up after the events from McGraw's earlier heist "many years ago," as an opening text note accurately states.
Ever the tinkerer, Wallace has moved on from inventing fancy pants that can scale walls and pivoted into "smart gnome" technology, much to the chagrin of Gromit, who feels his pal has gone overboard in replacing humans with machines.
Gnome rhymes with "home" and "roam," which leads to more of the outrageous puns that Aardman favours. Canine and cheese riffs abound, as do visual and sonic hat tips to classic movies - James Bond and Tom Cruise flicks among them. Feathers has an evil lair like 007 villain Ernst Stavro Blofeld's, but he strokes a pet baby seal instead of a Persian cat.
The bookish Gromit goes to bed reading such English Lit faves as "A Room of One's Own" by Virginia "Woof" and "Paradise Lost" by John "Stilton," sight gags that might baffle kids but should keep their parents amused.
Wallace's faux-friendly robot gnome, Norbot (voiced by Reece Shearsmith), is a relentlessly cheerful and helpful pal that just might be the solution to the pair's growing pile of past-due bills - although anti-tech Gromit's understandable instinct is to want to switch it off.
The voice-activated assistant is remarkably capable - it can trim a hedge in seconds flat - but has made Gromit feel as if he's become man's second-best friend.
Even with a factory setting of "good," Norbot's wide-eyed cheer is, admittedly, a little unsettling. It makes me think of the malevolent ventriloquist's dummy Fats in "Magic," the 1978 psychological horror film starring Anthony Hopkins.
But when Norbot's personality is hacked and dialed to "evil" by Feathers, the machine's eyes go dark and soulless, a sight some younger viewers might find scarier than intended.
Like a proud papa, Wallace fails to see Norbot's flaws, but at least some characters - Gromit, for one - trust their instincts. As the gnome invasion rises faster than a New Jersey mystery drone, and with the Blue Diamond once again threatened, the cops are forced to step in.
Bumbling Chief Inspector Albert Mackintosh (Peter Kay) and his competent new sidekick PC Mukherjee (Lauren Patel) are on the case. The question of automation (and automatons) in today's society is relevant, and "Vengeance Most Fowl" has mischievous fun with the premise, even if this inventive tale - with a running time of 79 minutes and a script trimmed to Norbot-approved neatness - isn't reinventing the wheel of cheese (ouch!).
Still, the gentle touch of the stop-motion animation feels distinctly human. That's welcome in these hard digital times since, as Wallace learns, some things, like patting his old pal Gromit, are simply better when done by hand.
Naive Wallace learns the hard way that machines don't want to be our friends, they want to replace us.
"Vengeance Most Foul" is a cracking good yarn, but Park and his team missed at least one opportunity for groan-worthy punnery. Could they not have worked in a reference to "Diamonds Are Furr-ever" in their Bond riffs?
Wallace
& Gromit:
Vengeance Most Fowl
(out of 4)
Starring the voices of Ben Whitehead, Reece Shearsmith, Peter Kay and Lauren Patel. Written by Mark Burton and Nick Park. Directed by Nick Park and Merlin Crossingham. Screening Dec. 18 to Jan. 2 at TIFF Lightbox. 79 minutes. STC
CREDIT: Peter Howell Special to the Star

Document 275 of 500

Province looks to top court on climate challenge: Group argues lower emissions target violates Charter

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-looks-top-court-on-climate-challenge/docview/3148768463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+looks+to+top+court+on+climate+challenge%3A+Group+argues+lower+emissions+target+violates+Charter&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has asked the Supreme Court to weigh in on a historic youth-led challenge of the province's climate plan, moving the case a step closer to a possible hearing before Canada's top court.
While the court only hears a fraction of the cases it's asked to review, lawyers for Ontario say this case strikes at an unresolved issue of national concern.
"This proposed appeal would ask this Court to determine, for the first time, whether and to what extent the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms imposes obligations on Canadian governments to combat climate change," read Ontario's application for leave to appeal.
The case was brought by seven young people who argue Ontario's weakened emissions target violated the Charter. They allege the target violated their right to life in part by committing Ontario to dangerously high levels of planet-warming emissions and discriminated against them as youth who will bear the brunt of the effects.
While their case was initially dismissed at trial, the young people secured a victory on appeal in October when Ontario's highest court sent the case back to a lower court for a new hearing and left open the possibility the constitutional challenge may prevail.
Fraser Thomson, a lawyer representing the young people, says Ontario's application "opens the door to a generation-defining hearing before Canada's highest court."
"The climate crisis isn't going away, and neither are we," Thomson, environmental law charity Ecojustice's climate director, said in a written statement.
The case dates back to when Premier Doug Ford's then-newly elected Progressive Conservative government repealed the law underpinning Ontario's cap-and-trade system for lowering emissions.
The government scrapped the system in 2018 and replaced the emissions target in that law - 37 per cent below 1990 levels by 2030 - with a new target of 30 per cent below 2005 levels.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Jordan Omstead The Canadian Press

Document 276 of 500

Building up a community: Without affordable rents and supports to help bridge the gender pension gap, some women will live in financial stress that can lead to loneliness, isolation and an increased risk of dementia

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/building-up-community/docview/3148768450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Building+up+a+community%3A+Without+affordable+rents+and+supports+to+help+bridge+the+gender+pension+gap%2C+some+women+will+live+in+financial+stress+that+can+lead+to+loneliness%2C+isolation+and+an+increased+risk+of+dementia&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Beth Guthrie had grown comfortable in her "sort of crappy" but respectable one-bedroom apartment with a balcony overlooking the forests of Toronto's High Park.
Like many older women, she spent her prime working years at home, raising children and running the household. Divorced at 40, Guthrie began working as a librarian, but the late start meant payments from her workplace and public pensions would never be enough.
Living alone, with rent rising, Guthrie's retirement increasingly focused on money instead of socializing and books. She realized that the cost of rent would soon overtake her pensions.
Something had to change.
For many of today's older women without wealth, the high cost of housing drains limited retirement income leaving them with little left for food or everyday expenses, let alone dinner with friends.
This precarity is in no small part due to the gender pension gap, the difference between men's and women's retirement income that is now starting to get attention from government researchers and advocates for women.
A lifeline on St. Clair Ave. W.
Those most affected are often single, divorced or widowed. They have no one with whom to share the costs of living. It can especially impact older women, who, like Guthrie, stayed home to raise children and later earned less than men, leading to a lower retirement income.
This wage gap is slowly closing, but Canada's gender pension gap hasn't improved since 1976 when it was first comprehensively measured, said a recent report by the Pay Equity Office of Ontario.
Retirement earnings include private workplace pensions and savings, along with the public Canada Pension Plan, Old Age Security, and for those on a very low income, the Guaranteed Income Supplement. These improvements are recognized internationally for their success, the PEO report said, but "not all Canadians benefit equally from this system."
The gender pension gap leaves Canadian women with 17 per cent less earnings than men, according to the PEO's calculation, although other measurements say the gap is wider.
In 2018, Guthrie, then 74, put her name on a wait-list for affordable housing in a St. Clair Avenue West building for older adults.
With a mix of market and subsidized rent, St. Matthew's Bracondale House offers social programs and provincially funded home care for those needing help with laundry, dressing, bathing or medication.
Without affordable rent mixed with health and social supports, many women will live in financial stress, a diminished existence that can lead to extreme loneliness and isolation, both of which heighten the risk of depression or dementia.
"That's why it's important to think about proactive solutions that can allow people to thrive in their communities, in less costly ways to all of us," said Dr. Samir Sinha, a leading Canadian geriatrician and director of health policy research at the National Institute on Ageing at Toronto Metropolitan University.
Cheaper rent and
her savings protected
Guthrie waited and worried as she dipped into her shrinking savings. In 2023, she got the call. A one-bedroom suite was available.
"I was starting to think my life was going to be completely unaffordable, and now," she said, pausing, "I don't have to worry about money."
At her new home at Bracondale House, Guthrie saves $600 a month on rent, doesn't pay extra for utilities and no longer has to withdraw money from her savings account.
Bracondale was built in the 1980s when federal and provincial governments routinely subsidized the construction of affordable housing for older adults and people of all ages.
Bracondale now offers home care services to older men and women, with funding from the Ontario Ministry of Health. Its low-cost social programs, such as chair yoga, painting or "singing for joy," are funded by the Ministry of Seniors and Accessibility and the City of Toronto. It also gets grant money from family foundations, such as the Norman and Margaret Jewison Foundation.
While Bracondale and its in-house services helped ease Guthrie's financial pressures, she knows it's a benefit that still eludes many.
Older women are being priced out of the rental market, and without housing and support services, many will end up in institutions, Sinha said.
"Ultimately, their choice becomes the shelter, it becomes the nursing home, or it becomes living in a hospital," he added.
Are nursing homes the answer?
As Ontario braces for a massive aging demographic, it has touted its plan to spend $6.4 billion on the construction of new nursing homes with 31,000 beds and the renovation of existing homes with 28,000 beds.
But in America, governments are taking the opposite approach - and the budget decisions made by officials there increasingly provide low-income earners with home care such as housekeeping, grocery shopping, meal preparation or transportation. These are the types of services that, in Ontario, could help women like Guthrie who don't need a nursing home.
In the U.S., where the gender pension gap is estimated at more than 30 per cent, many financially struggling women could not otherwise afford such home care supports.
National spending on home care and community services by Medicaid - health insurance for those on a low income - has jumped dramatically over 20 years, replacing funds once dedicated to nursing homes, said Robert Applebaum, Senior Research Scholar, Scripps Gerontology Center, Miami University in Ohio.
In 2001, Medicaid spent 71 per cent of its long-term care funds on nursing homes and two decades later, in 2021, that number had declined to 37 per cent, Applebaum said.
In the same period, Medicaid funding to home and community services jumped from 29 per cent to 63 per cent, he said.
In some states, nursing homes have empty beds as older Americans get the support they need - and repeatedly say they want - in the community, he said. Nursing homes increasingly care for residents who have no family caregivers and need 24-hour-a-day support or have significant dementia, Applebaum said.
In Canada, more government-funded home care could help older people stay healthier in the community, Sinha said.
A challenge to premiers
Sinha said he told last summer's Halifax gathering of Canadian premiers - including Ontario Premier Doug Ford - that they are more interested in nursing home photo opportunities than solving the health-care challenges of a fast-aging demographic.
"I said, 'I think you guys are just addicted to cutting ribbons in front of big buildings. It makes you feel good, because you feel like you're doing something.'
" 'But I have a solution for you: double your home-care budgets. Then you'll have thousands and thousands of individual homes where you can literally take a ribbon, put it across the doorway of Mrs. Jones or Mr. Smith and stand with them because they'll be really happy that you're helping them stay in their own home.' "
In 2024-25, Ontario will spend $9.2 billion on nursing homes - more than double the $4.3 billion it gives to home care for all ages, although province's budget last spring promised another $2 billion over the next three years. It's a commitment that Ford's office underscored when asked to comment on this article.
The Ministry of Health said the province has added nearly 25,000 front-line personal support care workers. Critics, however, say the PSWs, who are mostly female and racialized, still don't get paid enough to ensure they will stay in those jobs.
The ministry said the government is "taking action to provide more people with the right care, in the right place, at every stage of life."
For a group of fortunate older adults in a Halton Region pilot project called the Community Wellness Hub at 410 John St. in Burlington, the right care in the right place has been an affordable housing unit with communal programs focused on health and social connections. The results caught the attention of researchers with residents experiencing 14 per cent fewer non-urgent emergency department visits along with fewer and shorter hospitalizations for chronic conditions such as diabetes or heart failure. While the pilot project is spreading in Halton Region, for now only a lucky few live in such homes.
More social connections
In Toronto, Guthrie now has a built-in social life and the comfort of knowing that if she ever needs extra help with bathing, medication or navigating Ontario's complex health-care system, it is all offered on-site.
"It's just such a relief," she said, "because I know people who had so much trouble figuring out how to get that help."
Now 80, Guthrie just started getting a twice-a-month visit from a worker who launders her bedding and fits the bottom sheet on her mattress. She attends fitness classes for bone health and plays bridge with a fellow resident who, Guthrie says, is a certified bridge teacher.
When she walks to the local coffee shop, Guthrie often stops for a chat with people she recognizes from her fitness class.
"I go out on the street and I always meet somebody I know," she said.
Social connections make a difference in well-being, particularly for older women who often live alone, said Dr. Paula Rochon, a geriatrician and founder of Women's Age Lab at Women's College Hospital in Toronto.
On average, Canadian women outlive men by four years, according to Statistics Canada.
In an October article published in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society, Rochon and her team called for a re-evaluation of traditional housing models to address the poverty and loneliness of older adults, particularly women, citing the benefits of subsidized housing, shared housing or Naturally Occurring Retirement Communities (NORCs), all with the built-in supports.
Calls to fix the pension gap
As calls for more housing options grow, there are new demands for changes to the Canada Pension Plan. (CPP)
Workplace and public pensions are based on salary contributions made over decades. It requires roughly 40 years at an average salary to get a full CPP payment.
Canada's public and workplace pension models were designed decades ago to reflect "male pattern employment," which effectively punishes women who shoulder the care of families, said the Ontario Pay Equity Office report, called Understanding the Gender Pension Gap in Canada.
The PEO report cited ways to lessen the pension gap. While closing the wage gap would help, it still wouldn't compensate for the time away from work due to family responsibilities, said the report, written by Elizabeth Shilton, a legal scholar who practiced labour, employment and pension law for 25 years.
The CPP could reduce the number of years an individual must work (roughly 40) in order to qualify for full pension benefits, Shilton's report said.
It also said the CPP could fast-track its plan for a slight increase in pension payments. Without change to the timeline, that increase will not be fully realized until 2059 when the 40-year career of a young worker today is complete.
It has been a long time since Guthrie earned a wage while working on the library reference desk, a job that gave her a second start in life, after her marriage ended.
Now, Bracondale House, with its book clubs and bridge games, is giving Guthrie another shot at life anew, this time without the stress of money.
"It's a relief," she said. "It's such a relief."
This article was written with the support of a journalism fellowship from The Gerontological Society of America, The Journalists Network on Generations and The Silver Century Foundation.

Doug Ford's Ontario Place plan will make traffic congestion worse: report

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-ontario-place-plan-will-make-traffic/docview/3148884614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Ontario+Place+plan+will+make+traffic+congestion+worse%3A+report&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford vows to alleviate Toronto's infamous traffic congestion, his government's designs for Ontario Place would add to the problem.
That's the conclusion of a new report from the city of Toronto that examined the projected traffic impacts of the Ontario PC plan to redevelop the lakeside waterpark.
The report from city transportation staff noted that the $2.2-billion revamp - consisting of the Therme spa, expanded Live Nation concert venue, new public spaces and a mainland pavilion for Ontario Science Centre programming - will convert the Ontario Place site into a year-round destination.
By 2032, the redevelopment is projected to increase traffic volumes on Lake Shore Boulevard West by up to 27 per cent in the morning rush, 23 per cent in the evening rush and as much as 40 per cent during major events like Toronto FC games and the Canadian National Exhibition, according to the report.
That would add about 1 to 2 minutes to vehicle travel times on Lake Shore between British Columbia Road and Strachan Avenue during typical weekday or weekend peak periods, the city estimated. During major events, trips would take about 5 to 6.5 minutes longer.
The projections, which were based on a previous analysis performed for Infrastructure Ontario by LEA Consulting Ltd., take into account other factors that could affect traffic, such as development applications, corridor growth, new pedestrian promenades, and planned transit improvements in the Ontario Line, Lake Shore West GO upgrades and SmartTrack stations.
Ford's government has signalled that reducing gridlock in Ontario's biggest city is among its top priorities. In July it announced $73 million to speed up lane-reducing construction on the Gardiner Expressway one year early, and last month it took the extraordinary step of passing legislation that will allow it to remove bike lanes it claims are causing congestion on major Toronto thoroughfares.
Asked whether the province was concerned the Ontario Place redevelopment could undermine those traffic-fighting efforts, Ash Milton, press secretary for Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, said in an email that the government "is ensuring that Ontario Place and the surrounding area will be served by numerous transport options, including the Ontario Line, which is part of the largest subway expansion in Canadian history."
"We will keep ensuring that residents and businesses in Toronto have the transport options they need as the population continues to grow," Milton said.
But Norm Di Pasquale, co-chair of Ontario Place for All, an advocacy group that opposes the province's plan, said the report shows the Ontario Place plan would cause "an intense amount of traffic in downtown."
The premier "is manifesting the exact thing that he is saying he is trying to avoid," argued Di Pasquale.
He said that the Ford government's intention to build 2,500 parking spaces near the Therme spa and relocate the Ontario Science Centre from Flemingdon Park would be major drivers of the worsening congestion.
Di Pasquale, whose group has previously criticized the redevelopment over its cost to taxpayers and impact on public space, said the traffic data is further evidence that "this is ultimately the wrong site for both the mega spa and the Ontario Science Centre."
City staff produced the traffic analysis in response to a motion from Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik (Ward 10, Spadina-Fort York) at June's council meeting.
It's clear the transportation impacts of the province's proposal "had not been properly considered," Malik said in a statement Monday, and the new report shows "it will have significant impacts on an already busy corridor," as well as on congested areas like Liberty Village.
She said the Therme development does "not belong on our waterfront."
The report will be debated at Toronto and East York Community Council on Jan. 14, and is scheduled to go to city council the following month.
CAPTION: By 2032, the Ontario Place redevelopment is projected to increase traffic volumes on Lake Shore Boulevard West by up to 27 per cent in the morning rush, 23 per cent in the evening rush and as much as 40 per cent during major events.

From beloved pets to political differences, MPPs' holiday cards are a peek at family life

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/beloved-pets-political-differences-mpps-holiday/docview/3148492077/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=From+beloved+pets+to+political+differences%2C+MPPs%27+holiday+cards+are+a+peek+at+family+life&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Neither snow nor rain nor even a month-long Canada Post strike could stop politicians' Christmas cards from getting through.
While the postal work stoppage did dramatically reduce the number of cards received at the Star's Queen's Park bureau this holiday season, there were enough to liven up our office on the third floor of the Ontario legislature.
Premier Doug Ford's card, which is normally mailed out to 20,000 friends and supporters, wishes a "Merry Christmas & Happy Holidays" from "the Ford Family."
The front of the card features the premier and wife Karla with three of their four adult daughters, Kayla, Kyla and Kara. The family's dog, a mixed breed named Kobe, is shown inside the card, sporting a dashing beret.
As the Star first reported in 2022, the Fords' other daughter, Krista, has declined to appear in family portraits for public consumption due to political differences with her father over his support for vaccines and lockdowns during the COVID-19 pandemic.
While the family remains close away from the cameras, the premier has acknowledged in the past how the health emergency "polarized us in a way that we could have never imagined."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles's card boasts a picture of her and spouse, Jordan Berger, and their daughters, Lila and Mahala, along with their dogs, Stevie Licks and Hobbes, in front of a fireplace in their Toronto home.
Stiles is sending out 2,981 cards this year.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's card is her on a lakeside dock with her adult children and their partners.
"Wishing You and Your Family a Happy Holiday, Merry Christmas and Happy New Year! Bonnie, Alex & Katherine, Jonathan, Natasha & Brian," reads the inscription.
Crombie will be mailing out several hundred cards and emailing thousands more to Liberals across the province.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner's card is him and wife, Sandy Welsh, and their daughters, Isabelle and Beata, against an appropriately bucolic backdrop above the words: "Happy Holidays, Joyeuses fêtes."
Inside, also in both official languages, the card says: "Wishing you health, peace, prosperity and great happiness this holiday season and always."
Schreiner is mailing out 3,000 cards this holiday now that the postal strike is over.
The first seasonal greeting delivered to the Star bureau this yuletide was from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, whose card showcases her Siberian husky, Ayana.
Beloved pets also figured prominently on Progressive Conservative MPP Laurie Scott's offering.
Scott (Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock), who always has fun with her Christmas card, is shown seated in a harness racing sulky being pulled by her horse Mini Prince Hardy while her terrier Phineas jealously says in a cartoon thought bubble: "I've been replaced again...! What if I painted my nose red, do you think I could lead the sleigh?"
NDP MPP Wayne Gates (Niagara Falls), who also puts a lot of effort into his cards, had one drawn by his 18-year-old grandson Tanner Bidal.
It depicts Gates, with his trademark bushy moustache, sporting a Spider-Man Christmas sweater and a flowing scarf, throwing snowballs at a Grinch-like monster.
Another piece of original artwork was on Liberal Mary-Margaret McMahon's card.
The Beaches-East York MPP's holiday greeting is a wintery city scene with snowmen, sledders, tobogganers, skaters and Christmas trees that was drawn by Crescent Town Elementary School students with special thanks to their teachers.
She delivered it by hand to the Star and it was one of our favourites this year.

Innovative entrepreneur should be rewarded, instead she is being punished

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/innovative-entrepreneur-should-be-rewarded/docview/3148342411/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: She wants to sell coffee from her curbside trailer. Instead she's been ground down by the city's baffling bylaws, Dec. 18
Toronto is operating with yesterday's bylaws in tomorrow's world. The City of Toronto bylaws should reflect how we should move forward for the next 30 years and not how we have operated in the past. I looked up how many bylaws the city has. The city and its former municipalities have passed more than 198,000 bylaws since 1834. Most of these bylaws are still in effect, including the Official Plan and Zoning Amendments. The city is using out-of-date bylaws to punish someone who is trying to make change with a motorless vehicle that reduces our carbon footprint. Anastasiia Alieksieichuk is being fined for being innovative and creative in her approach to making a cosy atmosphere for serving coffee and baked goods. She should be rewarded, instead she is being punished. As City Councillor Dianne Saxe pointed out we are in a climate crisis. Food trucks should not be given a permit unless they are providing green solutions for generating the energy of their trucks. Doing a pilot study is a start .
Astra Burka, Toronto
Ford's fear of cyclists can be cured
Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Dec. 19
Our hard-working Premier Doug Ford appears to have developed cyclophobia. He has fought a relentless war on the bicycle. "Four wheels good, two wheels bad" seems to be his motto. There are more than 630 ghost bikes in Toronto - white bikes marking the spots where cyclists lost their lives to cars. Facts have no influence over a phobia, which is why Ford is impervious to all the statistics that contradict his assertion that bicycles are a major cause of traffic congestion. Like some reincarnation of Dr. Strangelove, Ford insists that bicycles are "clogging the arteries of traffic." Therefore, we must deny new bike lanes and pull out the old ones on major roadways. He insists that side roads are a better location for bike lanes. As a year-round cyclist, I avoid side roads. The sight lines are mostly blocked, people cross willy nilly, pets and children ramble about, etc. Now imagine a bike lane compounding all that confusion. As a cyclist I am protected by vulnerable textiles and skin; cars have sturdy shells . Yet Ford wants to protect drivers from cyclists. The good news is that phobias are curable. Cognitive behavioural therapy, exposure therapy, relaxation and breathing exercises, VR technology, medications, etc. have all been effective in the treatment of phobias.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
Burying of city shelter report shamefulThere were many ways council could have dealt with the refugee shelter report. It chose one of the worst, Dec. 20
I read with great interest Edward Keenan column regarding the decision of City Council to not debate the report but receive it for information only "to be buried is some faraway heap of forgotten trash." Keenan sums up his article with the words "which is the kind of behaviour you might engage in when you have something to be ashamed of," It's inconceivable that the city manager rejected the report outright without any discussion and begs the question, What is there to hide? I ask city council the same question.
Bill Melvin, Toronto
$40 million would buy a lot of treesCanadian carbon removal company scores US$40M grant from fund backed by Bill Gates, Dec. 18
Wouldn't it be easier to plant more trees? $40 million would buy a lot of trees. It seems to me that setting up a carbon removal company is another way to avoid the issue. Kicking the can down the road.
Carol Rawson, Toronto
Waiting for CarneyInside Chrystia Freeland's surprising resignation - and the fallout that has Justin Trudeau fighting for his political life, Dec. 17
In Samuel Beckett's absurdist play "Waiting for Godot," two homeless men spend their time in idle banter while waiting for a Mr. Godot to come rescue them from their predicament. The play ends without the hoped-for saviour ever appearing. I was reminded of it while watching the drama unfold around the Chrystia Freeland resignation. While only his name figured in all the fireworks former Bank of Canada head Mark Carney seemed cast in the role of Justin Trudeau's Godot. He never actually showed up to save the day, though apparently he was expected.
Orest Slepokura, Calgary, AB
Will Trump match Trudeau's actions?Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government announced the spending of $1.3 billion to reduce the export of illegal fentanyl and aliens to the USA in answer to President-elect Donald Trump's demands. I fully understand and agree with these measures. Now it is Trump's turn to reciprocate by eliminating the export of illegal firearms to Canada. Fair is fair.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
Expanding Canada would make better sense for these U.S. statesTrump takes another shot on Truth Social at Canada becoming 51st state, Dec. 18
There are six states that border Canada and that rejected the MAGA madness: Washington, Minnesota, New York, Vermont New Hampshire and Maine. There are another 10 states that are contiguous with those border states: Massachusetts, Rhode Island, Connecticut, New Jersey, Delaware, Maryland and Virginia in the east and Oregon, California and Hawaii in the west. There are also DC and Puerto Rico, both have been asking for recognition as states. That adds up to 18 non-MAGA entities that are contiguous with our borders, i.e. 18 potential new provinces. What can we offer them? Health care that will not bankrupt them; provincial politics that do not criminalize being different or having an abortion; a social security system that will not be bankrupt by 2034; a slightly smaller deficit that is mostly caused by social spending rather than by military spending; and judges, including those on the supreme court, who are guided by the law rather than by political ideology. We should invite them to join Canada.
Jacques Leduc, Toronto
Trump would rather deal with an inexperienced PM
Donald Trump publicly taunts Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and calls Chrystia Freeland toxic because he would rather deal with an immature, internationally inexperienced politician like Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre and like-minded conservative premiers that continue to put self-interest ahead of country.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga

Document 280 of 500

Supervised injection sites are a failed experiment in this city

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/supervised-injection-sites-are-failed-experiment/docview/3148337483/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supervised+injection+sites+are+a+failed+experiment+in+this+city&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "... just know I'm here for you babes."
In her professional capacity, Khalila Mohammed was a community harm-reduction health worker. But in her personal life, she was an agent of profound harm and an accessory to manslaughter.
One foolish decision on top of another, one not-so-impetuous act of aiding and abetting on top of another. One lie on top of another, spinning a web of deceit.
By doing so, she put the lie to the theory, advocated by activists and professed health experts, that unrestricted safe-injection sites are sanctuaries that mitigate the harm for vulnerable drug addicts, a benign service staffed by trustworthy individuals, posing no threat to the surrounding neighbourhood. She also put the final nail in the coffin - despite lawsuits and staged die-ins - for a purported health-driven practice that has taken neighbourhoods hostage.
So much so that even the great many who are fundamentally opposed to Premier Doug Ford's policies - I count myself among them - have applauded the Conservative government's Community Care and Recovery Act, which limits where injection sites can be located - specifically at least 200 metres from schools and daycare centres. Good lord, what were they doing in the vicinity in the first place?
"Like I told you benji you're special and I don't say that typa sh--- to just anyone. I wanna be there for you and by your side."
On July 7, 2023, while 44-year-old mother of two Karolina Huebner-Makurat lay bleeding out on the pavement near the South Riverdale Community Health Centre in Leslieville, hit by a stray bullet in an exchange of gunfire by alleged drug dealers, Mohammed was primarily occupied with collaborating in the hiding and the escaping of one of the suspects.
She allegedly brought the young man, who would shortly thereafter become her lover, into the facility, treated his wounds from the gun battle, concealed his bloodied clothes, provided him a clean hoodie, ushered him out of the building, put him into an Uber that she ordered on her account, urged him to lie low and provided him with Percocet pills.
Video evidence shows Mohammed exiting the side door of the clinic before the shooting starts. She ran toward the three men involved in an altercation. At the sound of the shots, Mohammed turns around and runs back inside. Ahmed Ibrahim flees the scene but returns, a grey sweater covering his head. Mohammed opens the side door and ushers him in. With a nurse, she provides medical assistance to Ibrahim. He's next seen on video being escorted out of the building by Mohammed and they walk through the neighbourhood together.
Ibrahim was not a stranger to Mohammed. He and two other men, all drug dealers according to a synopsis of events submitted in court, regularly frequented the parkette next to the health centre, openly selling their goods and talking about guns in conversations overheard by staff. One of them, Damian Hudson, had allegedly threatened to kill Mohammed in a text sent three days before the shooting, accusing her of working with his "opps" - believed to be a reference to Ibrahim and a third man, Ahmed Ali - following the purported robbery of Hudson.
Staff turned a blind eye and a deaf ear to all of it, as alleged by a former employee in a subsequent story in the National Post: staff drinking on the job, in her presence, the unwillingness to keep drug dealers away from the supervised injection site; indeed, as claimed, dealers occupying injection booths all day long, from which they conducted their business.
But Mohammed told Ibrahim - whom she calls Ben or Benji - about the death threat.
Their relationship "blossomed," as police put it, into a romance, in the days that followed the killing of Huebner-Makurat. Within mere hours of the shooting, Mohammad was texting Ibrahim, assuring him that his face was not visible on video, promising she wouldn't reveal his involvement to investigators.
"I'm not saying anything. They don't know about you and won't. My word."
"Big breath babe. I can't see s--- from that video."
"No face, no case."
What she told investigators when interviewed was that she'd treated a random client who'd been wounded, had no knowledge of this "Ben" person, no contact with the individual after the event and had no idea how he might be reached. But she was updating Ibrahim continually on what she knew about the state of the investigation, drawing ever closer in their folie à deux until finally spending the night together at an Airbnb she rented in Pickering.
But the jig was about to be up. When Mohammed was arrested on Aug. 14, 2023, she continued to deny any knowledge of Ibrahim.
Was South Riverdale Health Centre - long the source of community complaints and warnings from residents about open drug use and escalating crime - an aberration? I live not 50 metres from a supervised injection site. What happens inside is of no concern to me. I don't object out of hand to such facilities. But what happens outside is a menace and that does matter to everybody in the neighbourhood. It's unsafe and it's alarming. Unlike South Riverdale, it is not among the five Toronto consumption sites ordered shut by the province by March 31.
As haphazardly run, many of these sites amount to what Ford correctly described as a failed experiment, a view now somewhat shared by his ideological counterpart, British Columbia NDP Premier David Eby, who earlier this year significantly reversed a cutting-edge health care approach that decriminalized possession and use of small quantities of drugs including heroin, crack and meth. The public backlash from afflicted communities had been overwhelming. But supervised injection sites are still plentiful in the province - Vancouver alone has 50 of them.
I don't want people to die if their misery can be eased. But I no longer trust the very essence of these sites.
On Thursday, Mohammed, 25, stood up in a downtown Toronto courthouse, alongside her illustrious lawyer, and pleaded guilty to being an accessory to the shooting of Huebner-Makurat and obstruction of justice. Her sentencing hearing is scheduled for March.
"I f---ing hate cops so much."
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Shootings; Obstruction of justice; Investigations; Drug trafficking; Robbery; Neighborhoods

Who is best to take on Trump?: The Star asked four political experts to rank Liberal contenders for the prime minister's job

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-is-best-take-on-trump/docview/3148337459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+is+best+to+take+on+Trump%3F%3A+The+Star+asked+four+political+experts+to+rank+Liberal+contenders+for+the+prime+minister%27s+job&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau, abandoned by allies, besieged on all sides, is still, as of this writing, both the leader of the Liberal Party and the prime minister of Canada.
But when pundits and political insiders gather in the smoky backrooms (ed note: do vapes emit smoke?) of Ottawa and Toronto this Christmas season they will all be asking each other one question: Who is going to take his job?
In the long term, the answer is still likely Pierre Poilievre, the Conservative leader who holds a commanding lead in national polls. But if Trudeau does step down, a Liberal will have to take his place, at least temporarily.
To get ahead of the speculation, the Star asked some of Canada's keenest political minds to rank the contenders on one criterion: Who is best equipped to deal with Donald Trump? There was a clear winner among the living. But it may say something about Liberal prospects that two of the four pundits put a man who has been dead for 212 years at the top of their lists.
Each contender was allotted points based on the experts voting: nine points for a first-place vote, eight for second, etc. Our expert panel was made up of Justin Ling, freelance journalist and Star contributing columnist, Ashley Csanady, vice-president, McMillan Vantage, politics writer Matt Gurney and Amanda Galbraith, co-founder and partner at the Oyster Group.
Dominic LeBlanc
Finance minister, Trudeau confidant, man often thrown at things the boss would rather not deal with himself.
34 points
Ashley Csanady: 1
LeBlanc sits atop for two reasons: he is the only one on the list to have eaten recently at Mar-a-Lago and is the only one who has the personality to jell with a president who eschews elites, their vernacular and global norms. Word is Trump and LeBlanc developed a bit of a vibe already during the Florida hop, and with Trump, that's half the battle.
Justin Ling: 1
He's already the one dealing with Trump. I'm told he fared very well over dinner at Mar-a-Lago.
Amanda Galbraith: 2
A skilled political operator who has the respect of the premiers and opposition parties and could get them to fall in line with a Team Canada approach. One of the few liberals who could speak Trump, and I mean that as a compliment.
Matt Gurney: 2
The PM brought him to Mar-a-Lago for a reason. He's good at the schmooze, by every account. He's also a man, which I hate to admit probably carries more weight than it should with Trump. He would probably do no harm and might be able to charm a few concessions out of the Americans.
The ghost of Sir Isaac Brock
Native of Guernsey, captured Detroit in battle, Britain's military leader during the future Canada's 1812 war with the United States.
26 Points
Gurney: 1
I think my editor included this as a joke, but it's a brilliant idea. At a time when Canada's military spending has become a sore spot with allies, who better than a general with a proven combat record to lead the Liberals? Also, Brock is clearly willing to stand up to the Americans - he literally died in battle against them. Further, given the state of the party today, there's a natural synergy: the Liberals need someone with a proven ability to come back from the dead. Maj.-Gen. Brock all the way.
Galbraith: 1
Experienced at thwarting American ambitions since 1802 and would bring the BDE (can I say that in the Toronto Star?) we need to these negotiations. Was not so secretly contemptuous of Canada so he and Trump could bond over that.
Csanady: 4
The odds are about even that Canada will need Maj-Gen. Brock's very specific set of skills (defending the Niagara, saving Windsor, etc.) soon. That's why I'm putting him smack in the middle.
Ling: 8
Canada's archaic Challenger jets aren't retrofitted to carry ghosts. Brock would have to walk back-and-forth to Mar-a-Lago, wasting precious time.
Anita Anand
Minister of Transport and Internal Trade, ex-procurement, defence and Treasury Board, famous brooker of no nonsense
23 Points
Gurney: 3
Anand is smart and has a reputation for not tolerating BS. That's going to be an advantage. She has experience at defence and with critical financial files. She'd be understood to be serious and wouldn't be pushed around. That ain't nothing, and "ain't nothing" counts for more than it used to.
Ling: 3
Just as Joe Biden hates malarkey, Anita Anand hates nonsense. Given how much nonsense is coming from the White House, her work would be cut out for her.
Csanady: 5
Has the CV of someone who should be able to negotiate a trade deal with the U.S. She's also a Fulbright-award-winning lawyer and professor who guest lectured at Yale. But her academic pedigree and tone is exactly what would make it a challenge for her to build the personal rapport that Trump values.
Galbraith: 6
Smart and hard working but has never stood out as someone with the communication chops to go toe-to-toe with Trump.
François-Philippe Champagne
Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry, ex-international businessman, Shawinigan's second most famous political son
22 Points
Gurney: 4
Honestly, my list gets a bit grim starting here. I don't think Champagne would be an aggressively bad idea to lead Canada in dealing with Trump. That's enough to secure him the fourth spot.
Galbraith: 4
Brings energy and ambition Trump might like, but is too closely tied to Trudeau.
Csanady: 5
Could use his deep network to pull together top CEOs to drive a true Team Canada push from Bay Street. He's indefatigable. But I don't see Trump kicking back over a burger with him the same way he would with LeBlanc or Clark.
Ling: 5
Charming and business-minded, but Trump's people will have no idea what he's saying.
Mark Carney
Ex-governor, banks of Canada and England. The political equivalent of the guy who always says he's going to come out next time but never, ever comes out.
21 points
Ling: 2
The former England (and Canadian) central banker has played big-boy geopolitics for ages. He's got the chops.
Galbraith: 3
Has star power Trump might like. Savvy diplomat who stickhandled Brexit and dealt with Boris Johnson while at the Bank of England. Experienced in chaos, but his support for the carbon tax will hurt him.
Csanady: 6
I just can't see him jibing with Trump. He's a central banker twice over and Trump hates the global financial order. The one argument to put Carney higher on this list is his global network might allow him to tap into others who could bend Trump's ear. Maybe Boris Johnson could broker trade talks?
Gurney: 8
I can't really imagine any possible qualification for Carney here. I know he's a qualified guy in general. But he's ideologically wrong for Trump and seems to have alienated the only political party in Canada that has shown any interest in him. He's never been a worse choice for the job. And even he seems to get that.
Christy Clark
Former premier of British Columbia. Once tried to run for mayor of Vancouver while not living in Vancouver.
19 points
Csanady: 3
Has personal charm and Western connections, especially to natural resources, that would likely appeal to Trump. Plus, she knows how to play the media stunt game required to take on a game show host turned president.
Galbraith: 3
Gets both business and politics. Charismatic and has the unique ability to appeal to conservatives and liberals. Only person on this list who has openly criticized the PM - which would play in her favour with Trump.
Is, however, a woman, which is a disadvantage when dealing with Trump, unless you are Angela Merkel or a contestant for Miss Universe. (This also applies to Freeland, Anand and Joly.)
Gurney: 6
This would be such a baffling choice - most Canadians don't even know who Clark is - that maybe it would throw the Americans off? Worth a shot?
Ling: 9
Who cares? She'll never be leader.
Chrystia Freeland
Negotiated Canada's last trade deal with the United States. Last seen chucking her boss over the side of her boat.
16 points
Csanady: 2
Freeland already lives rent-free in Trump's head. So, for better or worse, he knows who she is and with Trump, that's a start. The gumption Freeland showed in her letter of resignation is the same force of will that brought back the Canada-EU Free Trade deal from the brink and saved free trade with the U.S. the first time around.
Ling: 6
She is, if nothing else, very good at pissing off Donald Trump.
Galbraith: 8
I mean, do I even have to explain it anymore?
Gurney: 9
If we are deliberately looking to insult Trump, Freeland is our best chance. He clearly can't stand her. We're not trying to insult him, are we? Are we?
Nathaniel Erskine-Smith
Newly crowned housing minister. Famous maverick. Known to sometimes change his mind about quitting.
14 points
Ling: 4
He's young, wonky, ambitious, optimistic, charismatic and critical of all the ways in which Trudeau has floundered. He's like an alternate-reality JD Vance.
Gurney: 5
Too young, too inexperienced, no particular heft or skill set that the Trump team would find valuable, unless they're really into podcasting and granular urban policy plans.
Galbraith: 9
A nice person who lacks the real-world experience to manage a crisis of this scale. But Godspeed if by some miracle he's the last man standing and has to try.
Csanady: 9
Really?
Mélanie Joly
Foreign Affairs Minister. Possibly more popular in New York than Ottawa.
11 points
Gurney: 7
Basically the ideological opposite of what the U.S. is going to want from us. They're demanding we up our military, she's extolling the virtue of the UN. Pass.
Galbraith: 7
Way too progressive and far too closely tied to the PM to make it work. Her past lectures on progressive values will land like a lead balloon.
Ling: 7
Foreign affairs are not Joly's strong suit.
Csanady: 8
Has a lot of strengths as a leadership contender: charisma, Quebec connections and a breadth of cabinet experience. But dealing with Trump means negotiating in public, via the media, with clean, crisp messaging, something Joly has struggled with on the Middle East file.
CAPTION: New Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc sits atop the list for two reasons: he is the only one to have eaten recently at Mar-a-Lago and is the only one who has the personality to jell with a president who eschews elites, their vernacular and global norms, writes Ashley Csanady, vice-president of McMillan Vantage.Justin Tang The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Justin Ling, Matt Gurney, Amanda Galbraith and Ashley Csanady Contributors;

Subject: Free trade; Politics; Political leadership; Politicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade

Location: England; United States--US; Canada; United Kingdom--UK

People: Trump, Donald J; Anand, Anita; Gurney, Matt; Johnson, Boris

Company / organization: Name: Mar-a-Lago Club; NAICS: 713910

Instead she's been ground down by the city's baffling bylaws, Dec. 18 Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Dec. 19 There were many ways council could have dealt with the refugee shelter report. https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3148337381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+She+wants+to+sell+coffee+from+her+curbside+trailer.+Instead+she%27s+been+ground+down+by+the+city%27s+baffling+bylaws%2C+Dec.+18+Doug+Ford%27s+government+pushes+through+law+restricting+bike+lanes%2C+Dec.+19+There+were+many+ways+council+could+have+dealt+with+the+refugee+shelter+report.+It+chose+one+of+the+worst%2C+Dec.+20+Canadian+carbon+removal+company+scores+%2440M+grant+from+fund+backed+by+Bill+Gates%2C+Dec.+18+Inside+Chrystia+Freeland%27s+surprising+resignation+-+and+the+fallout+that+has+Justin+Trudeau+fighting+for+his+political+life%2C+Dec.+17+Insider+says+Justin+Trudeau+has+a+%241B+border+plan+designed+to+counter+Trump%27s+tariff+threat%2C+Dec.+11+Trump+takes+another+shot+on+Truth+Social+at+Canada+becoming+51st+state%2C+Dec.+18&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Entrepreneur should be rewarded
She wants to sell coffee from her curbside trailer. Instead she's been ground down by the city's baffling bylaws, Dec. 18
Toronto is operating with yesterday's bylaws in tomorrow's world. The City of Toronto bylaws should reflect how we should move forward for the next 30 years and not how we have operated in the past. I looked up how many bylaws the city has. The city and its former municipalities have passed more than 198,000 bylaws since 1834. Most of these bylaws are still in effect, including the Official Plan and zoning amendments. The city is using out-of-date bylaws to punish someone who is trying to make change with a motorless vehicle that reduces our carbon footprint. Anastasiia Alieksieichuk is being fined for being innovative and creative in her approach to making a cosy atmosphere for serving coffee and baked goods. She should be rewarded, instead she is being punished. As Coun. Dianne Saxe pointed out, we are in a climate crisis. Food trucks should not be given a permit unless they are providing green solutions for generating the energy of their trucks. Doing a pilot study is a start.
Astra Burka, Toronto
Ford's fear
of cyclists
can be cured
Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Dec. 19
Our hard-working Premier Doug Ford appears to have developed cyclophobia. He has fought a relentless war on the bicycle. "Four wheels good, two wheels bad" seems to be his motto. There are more than 630 ghost bikes in Toronto - white bikes marking the spots where cyclists lost their lives to cars. Facts have no influence over a phobia, which is why Ford is impervious to all the statistics that contradict his assertion that bicycles are a major cause of traffic congestion. Like some reincarnation of Dr. Strangelove, Ford insists that bicycles are "clogging the arteries of traffic." Therefore, we must deny new bike lanes and pull out the old ones on major roadways. He insists that side roads are a better location for bike lanes. As a year-round cyclist, I avoid side roads. The sight lines are mostly blocked, people cross willy nilly, pets and children ramble about, etc. Now imagine a bike lane compounding all that confusion. As a cyclist I am protected by vulnerable textiles and skin; cars have sturdy shells. Yet Ford wants to protect drivers from cyclists. The good news is that phobias are curable. Cognitive behavioural therapy, exposure therapy, relaxation and breathing exercises, VR technology, medications, etc., have all been effective in the treatment of phobias.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
Council's burying of shelter report is shameful
There were many ways council could have dealt with the refugee shelter report. It chose one of the worst, Dec. 20
I read with great interest Edward Keenan's column regarding the decision of city council to not debate the report but receive it for information only "to be buried in some faraway heap of forgotten trash." Keenan sums up his article with the words "which is the kind of behaviour you might engage in when you have something to be ashamed of." It's inconceivable that the city manager rejected the report outright without any discussion and begs the question, what is there to hide? I ask city council the same question.
Bill Melvin, Toronto
$40M would
buy a lot of trees
Canadian carbon removal company scores $40M grant from fund backed by Bill Gates, Dec. 18
Wouldn't it be easier to plant more trees? $40 million would buy a lot of trees. It seems to me that setting up a carbon removal company is another way to avoid the issue. Kicking the can down the road.
Carol Rawson, Toronto
Waiting for Carney
Inside Chrystia Freeland's surprising resignation - and the fallout that has Justin Trudeau fighting for his political life, Dec. 17
In Samuel Beckett's absurdist play "Waiting for Godot," two homeless men spend their time in idle banter while waiting for a Mr. Godot to come rescue them from their predicament. The play ends without the hoped-for saviour ever appearing. I was reminded of it while watching the drama unfold around Chrystia Freeland's resignation. While only his name figured in all the fireworks former Bank of Canada head Mark Carney seemed cast in the role of Justin Trudeau's Godot. He never actually showed up to save the day, though apparently he was expected.
Orest Slepokura, Calgary
Will Trump match Trudeau's actions?
Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government announced the spending of $1.3 billion to reduce the export of illegal fentanyl and aliens to the U.S. in answer to president-elect Donald Trump's demands. I fully understand and agree with these measures. Now it is Trump's turn to reciprocate by eliminating the export of illegal firearms to Canada. Fair is fair.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
Joining Canada would be smart for some states
Trump takes another shot on Truth Social at Canada becoming 51st state, Dec. 18
There are six states that border Canada and that rejected the MAGA madness: Washington, Minnesota, New York, Vermont, New Hampshire and Maine. There are another 10 states that are contiguous with those border states: Massachusetts, Rhode Island, Connecticut, New Jersey, Delaware, Maryland and Virginia in the East and Oregon, California and Hawaii in the West. There are also D.C. and Puerto Rico, both of which have sought recognition as states. That adds up to 18 non-MAGA entities that are contiguous with our borders, i.e. 18 potential new provinces.
What can we offer them? Health care that will not bankrupt them; provincial politics that do not criminalize being different or having an abortion; a social security system that will not be bankrupt by 2034; a slightly smaller deficit that is mostly caused by social spending rather than by military spending; and judges, including those on the Supreme Court, who are guided by the law rather than by political ideology. We should invite them to join Canada.
Jacques Leduc, Toronto
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Fear & phobias; Bicycles; Councils; Carbon; Homeless people; Trucks; Bicycling

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin; Freeland, Chrystia

Title: Letters:   She wants to sell coffee from her curbside trailer. Instead she's been ground down by the city's baffling bylaws, Dec. 18 Doug Ford's government pushes through law restricting bike lanes, Dec. 19 There were many ways council could have dealt with the refugee shelter report. It chose one of the worst, Dec. 20 Canadian carbon removal company scores $40M grant from fund backed by Bill Gates, Dec. 18 Inside Chrystia Freeland's surprising resignation - and the fallout that has Justin Trudeau fighting for his political life, Dec. 17 Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11 Trump takes another shot on Truth Social at Canada becoming 51st state, Dec. 18

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.10

Document 283 of 500

MPPs' holiday cards a peek at family life

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpps-holiday-cards-peek-at-family-life/docview/3148337103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPPs%27+holiday+cards+a+peek+at+family+life&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Neither snow nor rain nor even a month-long Canada Post strike could stop politicians' Christmas cards from getting through.
While the postal work stoppage did dramatically reduce the number of cards received at the Star's Queen's Park bureau this holiday season, there were enough to liven up our office on the third floor of the Ontario legislature.
Premier Doug Ford's card, which is normally mailed out to 20,000 friends and supporters, wishes a "Merry Christmas & Happy Holidays" from "the Ford Family."
The front of the card features the premier and wife Karla with three of their four adult daughters, Kayla, Kyla and Kara. The family's dog, a mixed breed named Kobe, is shown inside the card
As the Star first reported in 2022, the Fords' other daughter, Krista, has declined to appear in family portraits for public consumption due to political differences with her father over his support for vaccines and lockdowns during the COVID-19 pandemic.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles's card boasts a picture of her and spouse, Jordan Berger, and their daughters, Lila and Mahala, along with their dogs, Stevie Licks and Hobbes, in front of a fireplace in their Toronto home. Stiles is sending out 2,981 cards this year.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's card is her on a lakeside dock with her adult children and their partners.
"Wishing You and Your Family a Happy Holiday, Merry Christmas and Happy New Year! Bonnie, Alex & Katherine, Jonathan, Natasha & Brian," reads the inscription. Crombie will be mailing out several hundred cards and emailing thousands more to Liberals across the province.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner's card is him and wife, Sandy Welsh, and their daughters, Isabelle and Beata, against an appropriately bucolic backdrop above the words: "Happy Holidays, Joyeuses fêtes." Schreiner is mailing out 3,000 cards this holiday now that the postal strike is over.
The first seasonal greeting delivered to the Star bureau this yuletide was from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, whose card showcases her Siberian husky, Ayana.
Beloved pets also figured prominently on Progressive Conservative MPP Laurie Scott's offering. Scott (Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock), who always has fun with her Christmas card, is shown seated in a harness racing sulky being pulled by her horse Mini Prince Hardy while her terrier Phineas jealously says in a cartoon thought bubble: "I've been replaced again ...! What if I painted my nose red, do you think I could lead the sleigh?"
NDP MPP Wayne Gates (Niagara Falls), who also puts a lot of effort into his cards, had one drawn by his 18-year-old grandson Tanner Bidal. It depicts Gates, with his trademark bushy moustache, sporting a Spider-Man Christmas sweater and a flowing scarf, throwing snowballs at a Grinch-like monster.
Another piece of original artwork was on Liberal Mary-Margaret McMahon's card. The Beaches-East York MPP's holiday greeting is a wintery city scene with snowmen, sledders, tobogganers, skaters and Christmas trees that was drawn by Crescent Town Elementary School students with special thanks to their teachers.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 284 of 500

Toronto's supervised injection sites are a failed experiment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-supervised-injection-sites-are-failed/docview/3148582587/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+supervised+injection+sites+are+a+failed+experiment&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: " ... just know I'm here for you babes."
In her professional capacity, Khalila Mohammed was a community harm-reduction health worker. But in her personal life, she was an agent of profound harm and an accessory to manslaughter.
One foolish decision on top of another, one not-so-impetuous act of aiding and abetting on top of another. One lie on top of another, spinning a web of deceit.
By doing so, she put the lie to the theory, advocated by activists and professed health experts, that unrestricted safe-injection sites are sanctuaries that mitigate the harm for vulnerable drug addicts, a benign service staffed by trustworthy individuals, posing no threat to the surrounding neighbourhood. She also put the final nail in the coffin - despite lawsuits and staged die-ins - for a purported health-driven practice that has taken neighbourhoods hostage.
So much so that even the great many who are fundamentally opposed to Premier Doug Ford's policies - I count myself among them - have applauded the Conservative government's Community Care and Recovery Act, which limits where injection sites can be located - specifically at least 200 metres from schools and daycare centres. Good lord, what were they doing in the vicinity in the first place?
"Like I told you benji you're special and I don't say that typa sh--- to just anyone. I wanna be there for you and by your side."
On July 7, 2023, while 44-year-old mother of two Karolina Huebner-Makurat lay bleeding out on the pavement near the South Riverdale Community Health Centre in Leslieville, hit by a stray bullet in an exchange of gunfire by alleged drug dealers, Mohammed was primarily occupied with collaborating in the hiding and the escaping, of one of the suspects. She allegedly brought the young man, who would shortly thereafter become her lover, into the facility, treated his wounds from the gun battle, concealed his bloodied clothes, provided him a clean hoodie, ushered him out of the building, put him into an Uber that she ordered on her account, urged him to lie low and provided him with Percocet pills.
Video evidence shows Mohammed exiting the side door of the clinic before the shooting starts. She ran toward the three men involved in an altercation. At the sound of the shots, Mohammed turns around and runs back inside. Ibrahim flees the scene but returns, a grey sweater covering his head. Mohammed opens the side door and ushers him in. With a nurse, she provides medical assistance to Ibrahim. He's next seen on video being escorted out of the building by Mohammed and they walk through the neighbourhood together.
Ahmed Ibrahim was not a stranger to Mohammed. He and two other men, all drug dealers according to a synopsis of events submitted in court, regularly frequented the parkette next to the health centre, openly selling their goods and talking about guns in conversations overheard by staff. One of them, Damian Hudson, had allegedly threatened to kill Mohammed in a text sent three days before the shooting, accusing her of working with his "opps" - believed to be a reference to Ibrahim and a third man, Ahmed Ali - following the purported robbery of Hudson.
Staff turned a blind eye and a deaf ear to all of it, as alleged by a former employee in a subsequent story in the National Post: staff drinking on the job, in her presence, the unwillingness to keep drug dealers away from the supervised injection site; indeed, as claimed, dealers occupying injection booths all day long, from which they conducted their business.
But Mohammed told Ibrahim - whom she calls Ben or Benji - about the death threat.
Their relationship "blossomed," as police put it, into a romance, in the days that followed the killing of Huebner-Makurat. Within mere hours of the shooting, Mohammad was texting Ibrahim, assuring him that his face was not visible on video, promising she wouldn't reveal his involvement to investigators.
"I'm not saying anything. They don't know about you and won't. My word."
"Big breath babe. I can't see s--- from that video."
"No face, no case."
What she told investigators when interviewed was that she'd treated a random client who'd been wounded, had no knowledge of this "Ben" person, no contact with the individual after the event and had no idea how he might be reached. But she was updating Ibrahim continually on what she knew about the state of the investigation, drawing ever closer in their folie à deux until finally spending the night together at an Airbnb she rented in Pickering.
But the jig was about to be up. When Mohammed was arrested on Aug. 14, 2023, she continued to deny any knowledge of Ibrahim.
Was South Riverdale Health Centre - long the source of community complaints and warnings from residents about open drug use and escalating crime - an aberration? I live not 50 metres from a supervised injection site. What happens inside is of no concern to me. I don't object out of hand to such facilities. But what happens outside is a menace and that does matter to everybody in the neighbourhood. It's unsafe and it's alarming. Unlike South Riverdale, it is not among the five Toronto consumption sites ordered shut by the province by March 31.
As haphazardly run, many of these sites amount to what Ford correctly described as a failed experiment, a view now somewhat shared by his ideological counterpart, British Columbia NDP Premier David Eby, who earlier this year significantly reversed a cutting-edge health care approach that decriminalized possession and use of small quantities of drugs including heroin, crack and meth. The public backlash from afflicted communities had been overwhelming. But supervised injection sites are still plentiful in the province - Vancouver alone has 50 of them.
I don't want people to die if their misery can be eased. But I no longer trust the very essence of these sites.
On Thursday, Mohammed, 25, stood up in a downtown Toronto courthouse, alongside her illustrious lawyer, and pleaded guilty to being an accessory to the shooting of Huebner-Makurat and obstruction of justice. Her sentencing hearing is scheduled for March.
"I f---ing hate cops so much."
CAPTION: A still from surveillance footage at the South Riverdale Community Health Centre shows Ahmed Ibrahim, in red, being escorted out of the front of the building by Khalila Mohammed, shortly after the gunfight that killed Karolina Huebner-Makurat.A sharps box outside of South Riverdale Community Health Centre, one of the safe injection sites the Ford government closed due to its proximity to schools.A sharps box outside of South Riverdale Community Health Centre, one of the safe injection sites the Ford government closed due to its proximity to schools.

Subject: Shootings; Obstruction of justice; Investigations; Drug trafficking; Robbery; Neighborhoods
