Document 300 of 500

Why is it so difficult to quit?: It seems everyone but the prime minister knows it's time for him to go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-is-so-difficult-quit/docview/3147640690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+it+so+difficult+to+quit%3F%3A+It+seems+everyone+but+the+prime+minister+knows+it%27s+time+for+him+to+go&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appears to be teetering on the brink.
He's dealt with scandal before. A family trip to a private Bahamas island yielded a damning 2017 ethics report. The SNC-Lavalin affair threatened his government. Photos of Trudeau wearing brownface and a turban rocked his 2019 election campaign.
But right now, things are grim. The latest polls indicate Trudeau's Liberal party is trailing the Conservatives by 20 percentage points. The number of Liberal MPs calling for Trudeau's resignation climbed to 20 this week, with as many as 45 signing a letter behind closed doors calling for him to step down, sources have told the Star. And, also this week, the biggest blow of all: deputy prime minister and minister of finance Chrystia Freeland resigned from Trudeau's cabinet in a stunning public rebuke.
Sources not authorized to speak to the media said Trudeau is reflecting on his political future. And yet, publicly, he has not committed to leaving. Perhaps it is because, according to experts in politics, history and psychology, quitting is simply hard. It is a tortuous internal process playing out in an extraordinarily public way. The weighing of identity against failure, humiliation against graceful defeat, public discontent against legacy. All the while, the sharks circle.
"It was very hard," said Kathleen Wynne of her experience in the 2018 Ontario election, when as premier she suffered a stunning loss that reduced her Liberal party to seven seats before she resigned as party leader. "The whole thing was brutal because it did feel like, as the leader, I was letting people down and that's a horrible feeling."
Knowing when to quit is part of the dilemma.
There are signs. According to Raymond Blake, a professor at the University of Regina who specializes in 20th century Canadian politics, two of the biggest are public and party discontent. A lapse of judgment is another. Failure to meet expectations, too.
But even with the signs, it can be hard to quit. History is littered with examples of those who have hung on past their expiration date.
"It's really, really tough for anyone who's at the top of their game to give it up," Blake said. "Walking away is tough."
But why?
Psychologically speaking, our careers become deeply ingrained in who we are, according to Marie-Hélène Budworth, the director of York University's school of human resource management who has a PhD in organizational behaviour. At a dinner party, the answer to the question "Who are you?" is often occupation.
Quitting leaves the quitter searching for a new answer.
"It can be a real struggle to make the decision to leave it behind," Budworth said, "because you're leaving part of your identity."
This dilemma, of course, is only exaggerated for a role as public-facing as the prime minister of Canada.
Budworth also suggests social circle could play a role in Trudeau's decision. Often, the people around us agree with our views. It can create the notion of a false consensus - that most people are on my side because this small group is.
And there's also the public consequences of quitting.
"We live in a world where quitting is often seen as failure," Budworth explained. "Someone who quits couldn't hack it. They couldn't make it work."
It doesn't help people aren't good at remembering accomplishments, Budworth said. Instead, our nature is to simply remember how something ends. Thus, leaving in a cloud of controversy "does leave a lasting scar," Blake said.
It impacts legacy. Rehabilitating public opinion is a natural process for any public leader once they've left office. Trudeau's legacy will be damaged if he leaves now, but it will suffer even more if he sticks around and is crushed in the next election, Blake predicted.
Wynne has wrestled with these questions. She polled as low as 12 per cent the year prior to the 2018 election. She said she knew she would never ride to victory in the election, but as the campaign progressed, she learned just how bad it was. Door-knocking Liberal candidates were told: Love you, hate your leader.
By early June, the writing was on the wall. Five days before the election, Wynne admitted defeat.
"I don't know who voters will choose, but I am pretty sure that it won't be me," she told reporters. "After Thursday, I will no longer be Ontario's premier."
Her goal, she told the Star this week, was to get out of the way. She wanted voters to support her party with full knowledge she would no longer hold her office. Reaching that decision was brutal, she said, but she knew she had to go.
"I had become a lightning rod for anger among people in the province," Wynne said.
"People associated me with something they didn't want anymore ... I was the past and we needed the future to take over."
What Trudeau faces is different. Every situation is. It is different from his father, Pierre, who in 1984 decided to resign after a walk in the snow. That decision came amidst a period of unpopularity for the Liberals, with a struggling economy and divided country weighing the prime minister down. And it was certainly not a surprise, with Trudeau having committed to stepping down as leader in 1979 before backtracking.
For the elder Trudeau, his decision in 1984 seemed to come with breezing ease. "I had a good day yesterday," he said on making his decision, "and it seemed like a good day to have a last day."
For Justin Trudeau, this decision appears to be anything but easy.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is reflecting on his political future, according to sources who asked to remain anonymous.Ethan Cairns The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Mark Colley Toronto Star

Subject: Voters; Prime ministers; Emotions; Political leadership; Politics

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Why is it so difficult to quit?:   It seems everyone but the prime minister knows it's time for him to go

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication date: Dec 21, 2024

Document 301 of 500

Jewish girls school hit by gunfire again: Overnight shooting follows two similar incidents at same institution over the past seven months

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/jewish-girls-school-hit-gunfire-again/docview/3147640674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jewish+girls+school+hit+by+gunfire+again%3A+Overnight+shooting+follows+two+similar+incidents+at+same+institution+over+the+past+seven+months&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Jewish girls school in North York was struck by gunfire overnight for the third time this year, sparking outcry from community leaders as police in Toronto and areas north of the city plan to increase their presence at faith-based institutions.
At about 2:30 a.m. Friday, Toronto police said suspects fired shots at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School on Chesswood Drive near Sheppard Avenue West from inside a vehicle before fleeing the area.
There were no reported injuries. Police have not yet said if they're investigating the shooting as a hate crime, but noted investigators have support from the hate crime unit.
The overnight shooting, which comes just days before Hanukkah begins on Wednesday, is similar to two other incidents at the school over the last seven months. In May, police reported shots were fired at the building in the early hours of a Saturday morning. Then later in October, two men were arrested for another overnight shooting at the school on Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the Jewish year.
No injuries were reported in either of those incidents.
Rabbi Nochum Sosover, the executive director of the school, which has about 250 students from kindergarten to grade 8, said Friday that they found shattered glass and bullet holes on the front door.
Sosover briefly described the school's security footage where "you can see the car coming and doing the full shooting," adding that after the previous incident in October they hired security guards during schools hours, but don't have anyone working overnight.
"Students are afraid, staff are afraid, nobody should be afraid to come to school, nobody should be afraid sending their child to school," said Yaacov Vidal, principal of the school, noting that it was understandable some parents chose not to send their kids to class despite it being open.
"It is very, very hard to be woken up in the middle of the night to such news," Vidal said.
Vidal notified parents overnight and said the school looked into possibly hosting classes at alternative places while police investigated, but they were ultimately able to remain open. "We won't be deterred, we're here, even today we opened up right after, we're here to stay," Sosover said.
Sosover said the community feels supported by police, but urged that "a lot more has to be done."
Toronto police said residents should expect an increased presence of officers outside nearby schools and synagogues over the next few weeks. Meanwhile, York police said officers will be "actively patrolling" all faith-based institutions in response to the shooting.
Supt. Paul MacIntyre told reporters Friday that officers are canvasing the area and reviewing footage while they search for answers.
MacIntyre said the only connection between the three shootings right now is the location and they are "making great strides in the first one" while the suspects from the second shooting remain in custody.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow have all condemned the shooting on X, calling for an end to antisemitic attacks.
"Enough is enough. Antisemitism and antisemitic attacks have no place in Toronto," Chow wrote.
The Canadian government also announced Friday a new national forum to combat antisemitism that consists of members from all three levels of government, the judicial system, law enforcement and representatives of the Jewish community.
The Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs (CIJA) released a statement saying they hope the forum will "drive urgently needed change."
"Our community is frightened - but we're also determined. And we urge governments and authorities at all three levels to share that determination by taking real action against what has become a ticking time bomb in our country," said Richard Marceau, CIJA's vice president of external Affairs.
CREDIT: Kristjan Lautens Toronto Star

Document 302 of 500

Doug Ford pushing to meet with Donald Trump to talk 'businessman to businessman'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pushing-meet-with-donald-trump-talk/docview/3147721519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pushing+to+meet+with+Donald+Trump+to+talk+%27businessman+to+businessman%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Will there be dinner at Mar-a-Lago?
Premier Doug Ford says he's pressing U.S. contacts for a face-to-face meeting with Donald Trump to persuade the president-elect his threat of 25-per-cent tariffs is a bad idea on both sides of the border.
"I have a lot of pokers in the fire, a lot of people that know him. Talked to a senator, a very important senator yesterday, saying 'Hey, we'll set up a meeting with him,'" said Ford, who has been campaigning hard against the threatened levies that would devastate Canada's auto, manufacturing and energy exports and put jobs at risk.
He did not identify the particular senator.
The premier had several telephone conversations this week with senators, members of Congress, governors and other U.S. officials in the fight to pre-empt damaging tariffs when Trump returns to the White House on Jan. 20.
"So I just look forward to sitting down with him eye-to-eye, businessman to businessman, and saying, 'you know, we need to cut a deal that's going to be beneficial for Americans and Canadians,'" added the premier, who ran his family's printing, label and decal business for years.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and a small group of top advisers, including Dominic LeBlanc, who was named finance minister on Monday, flew south for a Nov. 29 dinner and three-hour meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida - a state where Ford has a winter home.
Within days, Ford launched a pre-planned, multimillion-dollar ad blitz in the United States highlighting Ontario's importance to the American economy. He noted that Ontario is America's third-largest trading partner and the top export destination for 17 states, which means the tariffs would put millions of American jobs on the line.
Ford said his message is getting through in the wake of Trump's Nov. 25 social media threat to impose the tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican products unless the two neighbouring countries tighten security at their borders to stem the tide of deadly fentanyl and illegal migrants.
"It's hitting the kitchen tables of Americans. I'm getting calls from people I know down there saying, 'boy ... your ads are working," said Ford, who has advocated for retaliatory Canadian tariffs and threatened to cut off electricity exports to New York state, Michigan and Minnesota if necessary.
"I've talked to senators and governors and congresspeople every single day and I'm not hearing the same story that's coming out of president-elect Trump ... they know how important Ontario and Canada is."
Ford took to social media regularly this week to reveal which U.S. officials and media he has been speaking with.
The list includes Sen. Kevin Cramer of North Dakota and Congressman Adrian Smith of Nebraska, chairman of the trade subcommittee of the House Ways and Means Committee. Both are Republicans.
"I emphasized the need to take a Fortress Can-Am approach that stops China from using Mexico as a back door for cheap auto parts and other products to enter our markets," said Ford.
He also spoke to outgoing U.S. Ambassador to Canada David Cohen, New York Gov. Kathy Hochul and Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer, both Democrats, as well as CNN, the Wall Street Journal, the New York Times, Bloomberg news service and the Associated Press.
As well, he met in person with former CIA director and retired General David Petraeus "to discuss Canada's critical military and economic partnership with the United States and the need for Canada to step up by meeting NATO's military spending target of at least two per cent of GDP."
Ford, who on Monday hosted a meeting of premiers and territorial leaders in Toronto to discuss the tariff threat, said they all pledged to keep the tariff fight front and centre.
"We have to get over this hurdle. We're their closest friend and ally and we need to be treated that way," he added.
"We're going to ramp it up in January."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford chairs the meeting at Canada's Premiers Conference in Toronto on Dec. 16, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; Social networks; Tariffs

Toronto hospital network expands cancer care with purchase of downtown building

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-hospital-network-expands-cancer-care-with/docview/3147721511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+hospital+network+expands+cancer+care+with+purchase+of+downtown+building&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The University Health Network, one of Canada's largest research hospital systems, will expand cancer care programs with the purchase of a 15-storey building in downtown Toronto.
The expansion comes as UHN's Princess Margaret Cancer Centre has reached capacity with increasing numbers of patients.
The hospital network made the announcement from the second floor of the newly acquired space, at 522 University Ave. CEO Kevin Smith and Dr. Keith Stewart, director at Princess Margaret Cancer Centre, addressed a room of reporters and health-care providers..
The Princess Margaret Cancer Centre sees almost 20,000 new cancer patients every year and has now reached the numbers that were anticipated for 2030. "But it's good news ... we're identifying cancer earlier, we're seeing better survivorship," said Smith.
Other necessary appointments equate to nearly 200,000 follow-up visits and 5,000 cancer surgeries, said Stewart. "We have simply outgrown our current space."
Premier Doug Ford and Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones also attended.
"UHN's expansion of care will ensure families in Toronto and indeed across Ontario can connect to the care they need when and where they need it for decades to come," said Jones.
The building, which cost $79 million, sits on the southwest corner of University Avenue and Elm Street. The space will support expanding programs in cancer care, research and education at Princess Margaret and Toronto General Hospital and will also serve as a new training ground for UHN's Michener Institute of Education.
With the additional space, UHN will have the ability to focus on new clinical research in cancer, develop innovative treatments and technologies, such as artificial intelligence, and educate the next generation of cancer experts.
Funds to support the new building's operation will be supported, in part, by the Princess Margaret Cancer Foundation.
When asked about the disparities in Black women's health when it comes to breast cancer, in particular, both Smith and Stewart told the Star the new space will provide opportunities for research and education helping to better understand gaps in cancer research, care and education.
"One of the major advantages we have in cancer research is living in Toronto because this is the most ethnically diverse city in the world," said Stewart. "We have a great opportunity to teach the world. Are we missing populations? Do we not to have the right genomic data on those populations? Are we culturally sensitive to their needs, such as skin tone. And of course, this building will accelerate that."
UHN plans to begin retrofitting the building and moving people in within the next few months and be fully operational in a matter of years, said Stewart.
The University Hospital Network also includes Toronto Western Hospital, the Toronto Rehabilitation Institute and West Park Healthcare Centre.
CAPTION: University Health Network announced the purchase of 522 University Ave. The new building will support continued growth for cancer care, research and education.The new location.University Health Network announced the purchase of 522 University Ave. The new building will support the continued growth for cancer care, research and education.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-reappoints-jim-warren-as-olg-chair/docview/3147461934/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+reappoints+Jim+Warren+as+OLG+chair&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has reappointed Jim Warren as chair of Ontario Lottery and Gaming.
Warren, once a top aide to former Liberal premier Dalton McGuinty, will serve another year in the role.
"As I step into this renewed mandate, I am excited to build on our collective achievements, drive continued innovation in Ontario's gaming and lottery sector, and uphold our commitment to public service," Warren said in a statement Friday.
He was named chair three years ago to keep close watch on the province's gaming agency following a controversy that saw executives awarded generous bonuses early in the pandemic despite the closure of casinos under public health measures to slow the spread of COVID-19.
At the time, Ford called the bonuses "unacceptable" and said "for all these smart guys, they aren't too frigging smart. You've got to be sitting around the table and think, is this prudent to do this?"
Warren is a former OLG vice-president and political commentator who runs his own consultancy firm, Riseley Strategies, which has performed work for the Toronto Star's parent company, Torstar.
He also worked in the office of late Toronto mayor Mel Lastman.

Liberal MPs should put the good of their country first

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-mps-should-put-good-their-country-first/docview/3147379079/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+MPs+should+put+the+good+of+their+country+first&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPs bickering in public is the real problem, Dec. 19
I am in total agreement that bickering in public is the death of any political party. It disturbs me that people of their intelligence and education stoop to undermining their leader rather than present to the public a solid front. The Liberal government has probably developed more programs to assist the Canadian people than any other in my lifetime. As a senior, I recognize that if the federal government had not developed the CERB and wage assistance programs during the pandemic, many Canadians would have lost their homes and businesses or been unable to feed their families. I recognize that this Liberal government has developed the child care, dental care and pharma care programs - all programs aimed at helping the middle class. This government has taken more children out of poverty than any other. What a legacy for a government to run an election on. But instead, we have people in cabinet who put themselves before the good of the country because they have hurt or angry feelings or they simply disagree with the Prime Minister on the path forward. We who have supported the Liberal party, with votes and finances over the years, are now left wishing you would stop the infighting and start fighting Pierre Poilievre. He wants to destroy all the progress that the Liberal government made for the middle class.
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
Freeland has what it takes to be the next PMInside Chrystia Freeland's surprising resignation - and the fallout that has Justin Trudeau fighting for his political life, Dec. 17
Accomplished journalist and author, former minister of foreign affairs, deputy prime minister and finance minister. M.P. Chrystia Freeland has demonstrated she has the respect, humility, energy, smarts, experience and courage to immediately replace Justin Trudeau as Canada's Prime Minister thus giving Canada and Trudeau an imperative break. Perhaps Trudeau can go into Canada's most contentious rest home - the Senate.
Joe Schwarz, Penticton B.C.
Don't tax the construction we want, tax the cost of increasing land speculation
I am a long-time housing advocate. Here is why Bonnie Crombie's housing plan may actually work, Dec. 15
Eric Lombardi's list to improve housing affordability happily included enforcing rent guidelines which were exceeded over 200 per cent thanks to Premier Doug Ford's loopholes. (The Centre For Policy Alternatives: Rent Control In Ontario). However, Lombardi's analysis of development charges contradicts many long-time experts who argue the fee, if reasonable, does not increase the price of homes, because the vendor is already charging what the market will bear, and will not pass on the savings. Similarly, the Land Transfer Tax savings will mean more for the vendor. However, these cuts would work if we adopt the Land Value Tax advocated by experts and the Wall Street Journal in a video The Invisible Role Taxes Play in America's Housing Shortage. Experts say: Don't tax the construction we want, tax the cost of increasing land speculation. Lombardi, like many knowledgeable advocates, has previously supported this strategy because it will increase affordability and housing starts.
Joseph Polito, Toronto
They are called 'sick days' for a reason
School boards should spend 'spying' money on support staff for teachers, Dec. 17
It has been interesting to read the responses to teacher use of sick days from readers. One in particular said that teachers are reacting to not being able to stockpile their sick days and get a payout at retirement. The mentality therefore has become "use it or lose it." What happened to work ethic and common sense? If you are not sick go to work. If you are sick use a sick day. If the year ends and you have enjoyed a healthy year with no illness, good on you. The next year begins again with whatever number of days the employer provides. Such days are named "sick days" for a reason. They are to prevent loss of pay if sick. Teachers deserve a good sick benefits package. They have one. Eleven days at full pay and extensive coverage at 90 per cent is envied by the majority of employees in all sectors across Ontario.
Mike Faye, Toronto
Canadians have names for Trump, tooDonald Trump calls Justin Trudeau 'Governor' of the 'Great State of Canada, Dec. 11
While President-elect Donald Trump exercises what he thinks is clever humour by calling our Prime Minister "Gov.," I am sure he would find himself rolling in the aisles over what Canadians call him.
J. Richard Wright, Niagara-on-the-Lake
The hijab is a public display of my personal boundaries
The hijab has become quite a topic of discussion in the western world. For some, it is a symbol of religion and should not be allowed in public places. For others it appears to curtail women rights and should therefore be opposed. The perspective linking hijab with oppression and curtailing women rights needs to be looked into closely. Have hijab wearing women been asked if they feel suppressed or if their rights are endangered wearing a piece of cloth? For those who find it a point of contention , there is a need to examine why an object as innocuous as a piece of cloth is offensive. I am a Muslim woman, and for me, the hijab symbolizes both privacy and freedom at the same time. It is a means of silently but proudly announcing that I prefer to set boundaries for myself, boundaries that raise my self-esteem and worth because I am precious and my beauty shall stay hidden, just like a pearl in its shell, not visible to all. Having my boundaries set, the hijab gives me freedom. I am free to explore the world and excel in my abilities. There is absolutely no hindrance caused by the hijab. The silent display of seclusion in the public eye gives me inner strength and courage to comfortably be where I want to be. The hijab reflects a concept of privacy and freedom in all public places. It is not meant to curb women's rights but to grant them privilege and a sense of privacy. It is therefore imperative to take into account facts before concluding the hijab is a symbol of suppression and a means of curbing women's rights.
Sarah Nasir, North York
Different spelling, same nameDid you choose one of Ontario's top 10 names for your baby? Dec. 18
I have now seen this list published a few times and continue to see Sophia and Sofia as separate names. These ARE the same name and should be shown as Sophia/Sofia on the lists like any name that is spelled differently. I know it is not the Star's list but really? I guess the name Darryl will never make the list (or is it Daryl, Darryll or Darrell).
Jeff Gibson, Oakville

Document 306 of 500

Trudeau to shuffle a dwindling deck: Erskine-Smith, McGuinty expected to join cabinet hit by resignations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-shuffle-dwindling-deck/docview/3147379048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+to+shuffle+a+dwindling+deck%3A+Erskine-Smith%2C+McGuinty+expected+to+join+cabinet+hit+by+resignations&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid continued questions about his leadership, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will shuffle his cabinet Friday.
A senior government source, who was not authorized to speak publicly, confirmed that Trudeau will significantly shuffle his front bench on Friday at 11:30 a.m.
The Star has learned that Ottawa MP David McGuinty will become the public safety minister and Toronto MP Nate Erskine-Smith will take on the housing portfolio.
Erskine-Smith has been an MP since 2015, but has never served as a cabinet minister. He has voted against his own party on several occasions, although he has supported the government on any platform promises and in all confidence votes. He announced publicly that he would not run again.
Last year, he ran for the leadership of the Ontario Liberal Party, coming second to Bonnie Crombie.
The source said the shuffle is expected to address the fallout from several resignations this fall, as well as recent announcements by ministers who have said they will not be running in next year's election.
Trudeau has already moved Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc into the finance role after Chrystia Freeland's resignation from cabinet on Monday. Freeland surprised Ottawa with her decision to step down just hours before she was set to deliver the fall fiscal update.
McGuinty has been an MP since 2004, and held onto his seat even in the 2011 election when the Liberals were reduced to just 34 seats in the House of Commons. He currently serves as chair of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, a committee that has been critical of several of the agencies he will now oversee, including the RCMP and CSIS. His brother, Dalton, was premier of Ontario from 2003 to 2013.
When asked, following Freeland's departure, if he believed Trudeau was harming the Liberal party's reputation, McGuinty said it would be a "mistake to underestimate" both the party and the prime minister.
Freeland left Trudeau's cabinet Monday with a scathing resignation letter, in which she argued the government was overspending and not keeping money available for a widely expected trade war when U.S. president-elect Donald Trump is sworn in on Jan. 20.
"We need to take that threat extremely seriously," Freeland wrote in the letter, which she posted on social media. "That means eschewing costly political gimmicks, which we can ill afford and which make Canadians doubt that we recognize the gravity of the moment."
Speaking on the political podcast "The Herle Burly," LeBlanc said he recognizes the impact Trump's proposed 25 per cent tariffs would have on Canada, but is confident the government has what it needs to respond.
"I'm reassured that the government has the fiscal room, if there's a decision that has to be made, to intervene significantly," he said.
The shuffle will have to deal with much more than the fallout from Freeland's departure, as several of Trudeau's ministers have resigned or said they would not seek re-election since his last major cabinet shuffle in July 2023.
In September, Pablo Rodriguez stepped down as transport minister to seek the leadership of the Quebec Liberal party. The transport portfolio was given to Treasury Board President Anita Anand, who has been overseeing both files since then.
Last month, Randy Boissonnault gave up his employment, workforce development and official languages portfolios amid questions about whether he had improperly claimed Indigenous heritage while a business he co-owned sought government contracts. His files were added to Veterans Affairs Minister Ginette Petitpas Taylor's workload.
On Monday, just prior to Freeland's resignation, Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced he would not run again. Earlier this fall, National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Sport Minister Carla Qualtrough, Southern Ontario Economic Development Minister Filomena Tassi and Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal all said they would not seek re-election.
Friday's shuffle comes amid growing concerns about Trudeau's leadership. As of Thursday afternoon, 18 Liberal MPs had publicly called on him to resign.
Other than brief remarks at two Liberal holiday parties this week, Trudeau has not addressed those calls and has not spoken to reporters since Freeland's resignation.
With files from Raisa Patel
and Susan Delacourt
CREDIT: Ryan Tumilty Toronto Star With files from Raisa Patel and Susan Delacourt

Subject: Resignations; Public safety; Prime ministers; Cabinet; Political dissent; Politics; Political leadership

Document 307 of 500

Justin Trudeau avoids questions about his future after cabinet overhaul

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-avoids-questions-about-his-future/docview/3147378753/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+avoids+questions+about+his+future+after+cabinet+overhaul&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-An embattled Prime Minister Justin Trudeau made a significant overhaul to his cabinet Friday, while his leadership continues to be in question.
However, Trudeau has still not answered questions about his future, following Chrystia Freeland's surprise resignation as finance minister on Monday and calls from Liberal MPs to step down.
After the first meeting of his new cabinet, Trudeau briefly spoke with reporters, but did not address questions about whether he will stay or whether he will prorogue Parliament.
He said the first cabinet meeting was focused on the incoming Donald Trump administration and the president-elect's threat to impose 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian exports to the U.S.
"We know how important it is for Canadians that we be there to protect not just the Canadian economy, but their jobs. The cost of living challenges they're facing, they could get worse. We have a lot of work to do," he said.
Trudeau brought in eight new people in the cabinet shuffle and moved several others.
Nate Erskine-Smith, a Toronto MP known for being outspoken and sometimes voting against his own party, will join cabinet as housing minister, replacing Sean Fraser who announced he would not run again on Monday.
Ottawa MP David McGuinty will be the new public safety minister, taking over the role from Dominic LeBlanc, who became finance minister on Monday after Chrystia Freeland's shocking resignation.
Also among the new faces, Montreal MP Rachel Bendayan will become the official languages minister and associate minister of public safety.
Nova Scotia MP Darren Fisher will be the veterans' affairs minister and associate minister of defence. Winnipeg MP Terry Duguid will be the minister for sport and responsible for Prairies economic development.
Newfoundland MP Joanne Thompson will take on the seniors file, Quebec MP Élisabeth Brière will take on the post of national revenue and Ruby Sahota will be responsible for southern Ontario economic development and democratic institutions.
There are also changes for current cabinet ministers. Oakville MP Anita Anand who has been doing double duty as transport minister and Treasury Board president, will stay on as transport minister and pick up a new portfolio focused on internal trade.
New Brunswick MP Ginette Petitpas Taylor will take on the Treasury Board post, dropping her position as veterans' affairs and employment minister. The employment portfolio will be taken on by Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon.
Gary Anandasangaree, the minister of Indigenous Crown Relations, will have northern affairs and northern economic development added to his responsibilities.
Just before the shuffle, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh made clear he will vote to bring down the government at the first opportunity when the House of Commons returns in late January, this could mean the new ministers will only serve in their portfolio for a matter of weeks.
Erskine-Smith who had indicated he would not be running for reelection said he will now stand in the next campaign. He said ultimately, however long he has the portfolio, he believes he has a chance to make real change.
"I understand there's gonna be a short runway. I'm not blind to that. But if I can make a small difference, if I can make a big difference, I will make the biggest difference that I can. That's why I joined politics in the first place," he said.
Bendayan said the prime minister is still reflecting on his next steps, but she took him up on the offer because she felt called to serve.
"The prime minister was quite clear that he has a choice that he's reflecting on. I think each one of us, and certainly I'll speak for myself. I made a choice today," she said. "The decision that I made is based on the intention that I always had and that was to enter politics not worrying about the next election or the partisan nature of this job, but in order to serve Canadians."
Ahead of his first cabinet meeting, McGuinty said he believes the government can still find a way to negotiate with the other parties, despite Singh's promise to bring down the government.
"Everything is negotiable in the House of Commons. That has always been the case. There is always a pathway forward," he said.
Trudeau has continued to face questions about his leadership since Freeland announced her resignation as finance minister and deputy prime minister on Monday. There are currently 20 Liberal MPs calling on the prime minister to resign, with Toronto MP Rob Oliphant the latest to add his name to this list.
Freeland quit just hours before she was set to deliver the fall fiscal update, and was critical of the government's spending plans in her resignation letter, arguing the prime minister was not taking U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's trade threats seriously.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau delivers remarks during a national caucus holiday event in Ottawa on Dec. 17, 2024.

Full text: MPs should put the good of their country first
MPs bickering in public is the real problem, Letters, Dec. 19
I am in total agreement that bickering in public is the death of any political party. It disturbs me that people of their intelligence and education stoop to undermining their leader rather than present to the public a solid front. The Liberal government has probably developed more programs to assist the Canadian people than any other in my lifetime. As a senior, I recognize that if the government had not developed wage assistance programs during the pandemic, many Canadians would have lost their homes and businesses or been unable to feed their families. I recognize that this government has developed child care, dental care and pharma care programs - all programs aimed at helping the middle class. This government has taken more children out of poverty than any other. What a legacy to run an election on. But instead, we have people in cabinet who put themselves before the good of the country because they have hurt or angry feelings or they simply disagree with the Prime Minister on the path forward. We who have supported the Liberal party, with votes and finances over the years, are now left wishing you would stop the infighting and start fighting Pierre Poilievre. He wants to destroy all the progress that the Liberal government has made for the middle class.
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
Freeland has what it takes to be the next PM
Inside Chrystia Freeland's surprising resignation - and the fallout that has Justin Trudeau fighting for his political life, Dec. 17
Accomplished journalist and author, former minister of foreign affairs, deputy prime minister and finance minister. Chrystia Freeland has demonstrated she has the respect, humility, energy, smarts, experience and courage to immediately replace Justin Trudeau as Canada's Prime Minister, thus giving Canada and Trudeau an imperative break. Perhaps Trudeau can go into Canada's most contentious rest home - the Senate.
Joe Schwarz, Penticton, B.C.
Tax the land speculators,
not the builders
Here is why Bonnie Crombie's housing plan may actually work, Opinion, Dec. 15
Eric Lombardi's list to improve housing affordability happily included enforcing rent guidelines, which were exceeded over 200 per cent thanks to Premier Doug Ford's loopholes.
However, his analysis of development charges contradicts many long-time experts who argue the fee, if reasonable, does not increase the price of homes, because the vendor is already charging what the market will bear and will not pass on the savings.
Similarly, the land transfer tax savings will mean more for the vendor.
However, these cuts would work if we adopt the land value tax advocated by experts and the Wall Street Journal. Experts say: Don't tax the construction we want, tax the cost of increasing land speculation.
Lombardi, like many knowledgeable advocates, has previously supported this strategy because it will increase affordability and housing starts.
Joseph Polito, Toronto
They are called 'sick days' for
a good reason
School boards should spend 'spying' money on support staff for teachers, Letters, Dec. 17
It has been interesting to read the responses to teachers' use of sick days from readers. One in particular said teachers are reacting to not being able to stockpile their sick days and get a payout at retirement. The mentality therefore has become "use it or lose it."
What happened to work ethic and common sense? If you are not sick, go to work. If you are sick, use a sick day. If the year ends and you have enjoyed a healthy year with no illness, good on you. The next year begins again with whatever number of days the employer provides. They are named "sick days" for a reason. They are to prevent loss of pay if sick.
Teachers deserve a good sick benefits package. They have one. Eleven days at full pay and extensive coverage at 90 per cent is envied by the majority of employees in all sectors across Ontario.
Mike Faye, Toronto
Canadians have their own names
for Trump
Donald Trump calls Justin Trudeau 'Governor' of the 'Great State of Canada, Dec. 11
While president-elect Donald Trump exercises what he thinks is clever humour by calling our Prime Minister "Gov.," I am sure he would find himself rolling in the aisles over what Canadians call him.
J. Richard Wright,
Niagara-on-the-Lake, Ont.
Hijab symbolizes both privacy
and freedom
The hijab has become quite a topic of discussion in the Western world. For some, it is a symbol of religion and should not be allowed in public places. For others it appears to curtail women's rights and should therefore be opposed.
The perspective linking the hijab with oppression and curtailing women's rights needs to be looked into closely. Have hijab-wearing women been asked if they feel suppressed or if their rights are endangered wearing a piece of cloth?
For those who find it a point of contention , there is a need to examine why an object as innocuous as a piece of cloth is offensive.
I am a Muslim woman and, for me, the hijab symbolizes both privacy and freedom at the same time. It is a means of silently but proudly announcing that I prefer to set boundaries for myself, boundaries that raise my self-esteem and worth because I am precious and my beauty shall stay hidden, just like a pearl in its shell, not visible to all.
Having my boundaries set, the hijab gives me freedom. I am free to explore the world and excel in my abilities. There is absolutely no hindrance caused by the hijab. The silent display of seclusion in the public eye gives me inner strength and courage to comfortably be where I want to be.
The hijab reflects a concept of privacy and freedom in all public places. It is not meant to curb women's rights but to grant them privilege and a sense of privacy. It is therefore imperative to take into account facts before concluding the hijab is a symbol of suppression and a means of curbing women's rights.
Sarah Nasir, North York

Document 309 of 500

Health worker guilty of helping suspect: Accessory after Leslieville shooting that killed mom outside injection site

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-worker-guilty-helping-suspect/docview/3147378722/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+worker+guilty+of+helping+suspect%3A+Accessory+after+Leslieville+shooting+that+killed+mom+outside+injection+site&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A community harm-reduction health worker has pleaded guilty to being an accessory to manslaughter in connection to the high-profile shooting death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat outside a Leslieville safe injection site two summers ago.
In doing so, Khalila Mohammed has admitted to developing a relationship with a man she helped flee the scene - by treating his wounds, hiding his clothing, lying to police, and sending him a chain of texts revealing a "romance" that began immediately after Huebner-Makurat's death.
"I wanna be there for you and by your side," Mohammed wrote in one text message, dated July 9, 2023 - two days after a stray bullet struck and killed the 44-year-old mother of two, sparking intense local outrage in the bustling area near the South Riverdale Community Health Centre at 955 Queen St. E.
Huebner-Makurat was on her way to lunch with a friend when alleged drug dealers exchanged bullets on the busy street.
The furor over her killing - in a neighbourhood that had warned about open drug use and increased crime around the facility - contributed to Premier Doug Ford's move earlier this year to ban safe injection sites near schools and daycares.
The health centre's site - one of the city's first when it opened in November 2017 - is being forced to close.
On Wednesday, Mohammed, 25, who supervised users injecting drugs at the site since 2021, stood in a downtown Toronto courtroom with her lawyer Brian Greenspan and admitted she assisted Ahmed Ibrahim - with whom she developed a romantic relationship after the killing. She was supposed to have a preliminary hearing earlier this month; her guilty plea eliminates the need for a trial.
Ontario Court Justice Russell Silverstein asked Mohammed if she had read a 28-page synopsis of the case and understood her decision.
"Yes, Your Honour," the Pickering resident said in a soft voice. She had no criminal record before this offence, and acknowledged she will receive a conviction and criminal record.
Damian Hudson, who is in his early 30s, is set to stand trial for second-degree murder for allegedly firing the shot that killed Huebner-Makurat. Ibrahim, 21, has meanwhile been ordered to stand trial for robbery and manslaughter. He is not alleged to have been armed that day.
Police believe a third suspect - Ahmed Ali, alleged to be the shooter who exchanged fire with Hudson - has fled to Somalia.
'Get out of the city'
The prosecution alleges that Hudson, Ibrahim and Ali were drug dealers who frequented the parkette next to the health centre.
According to the court's summary of the case, Mohammed spent a lot of time there talking to Ibrahim, whom she referred to as Ben.
On July 7, surveillance cameras captured a man alleged to be Ali attacking Hudson, while Ibrahim grabbed his satchel. Pandemonium erupted when the guns came out; in all, six cartridge casings were found, four fired from a .40-calibre handgun and two from a .22-calibre pistol.
While Huebner-Makurat lay dying on Queen Street, Mohammed tended to Ibrahim, who was injured and bloodied, inside the health centre.
She then provided him with a change of clothing, escorted him off the premises and ordered him a ride-sharing vehicle with her personal account.
Text messages between the pair, later obtained by police, established that their relationship "blossomed into a romance" immediately after the shooting, the court's summary reads.
"From her messages, it is clear that Khalila Mohammed is aware that Ibrahim was directly involved in the shooting of Karoline Huebner-Makurat. She suggests to him that he 'stay away for a while' to avoid being arrested by police. She offers to provide him with Percocet pills. She offers to check the news media releases regarding the shooting. She says she is going to check surveillance footage to see if he is visible," the 28-page document states. It adds there is no evidence that she actually checked footage or had access to it.
In the same July 9 text conversation where she wrote she wanted to be "by your side," Mohammed told Ibrahim about her knowledge of how the investigation is unfolding, and informed him that police want to speak to her.
That night, they discussed the quality of three suspect images released by police.
She told him to "get out of the city" and "lay low," and said she had his jeans and belt tucked away and that his hooded sweatshirt is "gone."
A response back: "Make sure your deleting all your texts, trust me."
The next day, Mohammed also assured Ibrahim that the way the cameras are positioned at the centre, they would not have captured the shooting. This showed "an awareness on her part of Ibrahim's involvement in the shooting," the synopsis says.
"Big breath babe," she texted Ibrahim. "I can't see s--- from that video."
On July 12, Mohammed got a visit from two detectives. "Yoooooo," she texted. "Homicide detective are here ... 2 of them."
She then lied to them, telling the officers she did not know the "guy who came in for wound care." At the same time, she reassured Ibrahim in text messages that she would tell police she knew nothing, and that he could not be identified in a billboard about the case.
"No face, no case," she wrote to him.
Their relationship continued in the weeks that followed, primarily via text messages. On July 20, she rented an Airbnb in Pickering where they spent the night together.
When Mohammed was arrested Aug. 14, 2023, she continued to deny any knowledge of Ibrahim.
Her sentencing hearing is set for March 28.
The prosecution and defence did not indicate their positions on punishment, except to note that it will be a contested hearing.
Once Mohammed is sentenced, the Crown will withdraw three other charges: two counts of attempting to obstruct justice and accessory after the fact to robbery.
The ongoing fallout from the shooting
After Huebner-Makurat's death, the Ontario government passed the Community Care and Recovery Act. It is set to go into effect at the end of March. It introduces new zoning restrictions that require the safe injection sites to be located at least 200 metres from schools and daycare centres, forcing the closure of 10 Ontario sites, including the South Riverdale site.
The premier has called safe injection sites a "failed policy" experiment that has created dangerous conditions near homes and schools.
Last week, a Toronto social service agency announced it is suing the Ontario government, claiming its new legislation violates the Charter and that removing the sites will "deprive people of lifesaving care."
CREDIT: Betsy Powell Courts Reporter

Document 310 of 500

Veteran police officer dies while on duty: Medical episode suspected as detective dies after executing a search warrant in North York

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/veteran-police-officer-dies-while-on-duty/docview/3147378527/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Veteran+police+officer+dies+while+on+duty%3A+Medical+episode+suspected+as+detective+dies+after+executing+a+search+warrant+in+North+York&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police officers are in shock and a family is in mourning after a veteran detective with an elite unit died in what appears to have been a medical episode following the execution of a search warrant in North York on Thursday.
Det. John Park, a 40-year-old father of two and 14-year veteran of the force, died shortly after participating in a hold-up squad investigation into a recent robbery. Although his death is under investigation, police say preliminary information suggests he died from a medical event unrelated to his line-of-duty work on Thursday morning.
The unexpected loss of a colleague while at work prompted waves of grief throughout the force, summoning senior police brass to the hospital where the officer was pronounced dead and inspiring an impromptu processional, attended by Park's grieving loved ones and first responders from across emergency services.
"There's no words to express the sorrow that we feel right now," Toronto police Deputy Chief Lauren Pogue said through tears Thursday afternoon, outside Humber River Hospital, stopping at one point to regain her composure.
"This is every family's worst fear when a loved one works as a police officer and does not come home safely after their shift," she said.
Park leaves behind his wife, Tina, and two children, who are nine and five years old.
Pogue described Park as a "wonderful and loving father" who was appreciated by everyone who knew him at the service, calling his death "heartbreaking news just before Christmas."
"It's devastating. It's just such a sad situation in the hospital right now, but this is the time we stand together and support the family," said Clayton Campbell, president of the Toronto Police Association.
He noted the hold-up squad is "one of the most elite units" in the service, responsible for investigating carjackings, home invasions and robberies. Park had just recently been promoted. Before joining the hold-up squad, he served as a constable at 22 Division in Etobicoke.
"We are just so proud of his career," Campbell said.
News of a police officer's on-duty death initially sparked concerns that Park may have come into contact with a toxic substance, such as narcotics, during the search warrant execution. But Pogue said there is currently "nothing to believe that it is related to narcotics exposure" but was rather a "medical episode."
Pogue said little Thursday about the circumstances of Park's death, saying only that he was found alone.
Just after 2 p.m. on Thursday, a procession escorted Park's loved ones, some visibly distraught, from Humber River Hospital to the nearby Ontario Forensic Pathology Office. Scores of police vehicles, along with uniformed and plain clothes officers, were joined by members of Toronto police's Mounted Unit, the Toronto Fire Services and Emergency Medical Services in offering a salute to Park in the procession. Ontario Provincial Police leadership, along with dozens of officers from the nearby Keele Street detachment, were also present to pay their respects.
Mayor Olivia Chow also attended, greeted briefly by Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw before joining the procession as it entered the coroner's facility.
Earlier in the day, members of the service's top brass could be seen huddled outside a room in the hospital's emergency department.
The search warrant execution took place at a building near Jane Street and Steeles Avenue West. On Thursday morning, forensic vehicles, police cars, fire trucks and an ambulance were stationed in the apartment parking lot. Uniformed officers were later seen patrolling and taking photos of the vehicles in the lot.
In a statement posted on X, Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he was "deeply saddened" by Park's death.
"My condolences go out to the family and loved ones of the officer, as well as all members of the @TorontoPolice," the premier wrote.
Chow also issued a statement, saying her "heartfelt condolences go out to the officer's family, friends, and colleagues during this painful time."
On Thursday night, the CN Tower announced on X it would dim its lights for five minutes at the top of each hour in honour of Park.
CREDIT: Wendy Gillis, AbO'Brien and Estella Ren Toronto Star

Document 311 of 500

New Science Centre opening delayed: Timeline for facility at Ontario Place pushed to 2029

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-science-centre-opening-delayed/docview/3147378336/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Science+Centre+opening+delayed%3A+Timeline+for+facility+at+Ontario+Place+pushed+to+2029&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The timeline for opening a permanent new Ontario Science Centre appears to have been delayed, with infrastructure officials telling the auditor general's office the centre is expected to be up and running in 2029.
When Premier Doug Ford announced last year the planned move for the attraction, from its east Toronto location to a redeveloped Ontario Place on the city's waterfront, the science centre said the new facility would open in 2028.
In June, when the government announced the abrupt closure of the science centre over structural concerns with the roof, its press release said the Ontario Place facility would open "as early as" 2028.
Now, according to a report this month from Ontario's auditor general Shelley Spence on the Ontario Place redevelopment, "the new building is expected to open in 2029."
That information was provided to the auditor general's office by Infrastructure Ontario, a spokesperson for the auditor said.
Jason Ash, co-chair of the group Save Ontario's Science Centre, said it's a shame that there will be no permanent Ontario Science Centre for at least an extra year.
"It's really a whole generation of Ontario children who are not going to have access to world-class science education that the Ontario Science Centre has provided for 55 years," he said.
"Simply put, the Ontario Science Centre was unique in that the majority of its business actually came from Ontarians, with tourism coming in second place. So Ontario kids aren't being served and tourists are not going to get a great experience either in the meantime."
Two requests for comment from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma went unanswered, with a spokesperson ultimately replying to a third by saying she would not be responding.
"Due to the request submitted by the Leader of the Official Opposition to the Integrity Commissioner on October 16th, 2024, Minister Kinga Surma has been asked not to comment and will respect the process at this time," Ash Milton wrote.
Milton's reply came after Surma answered at least a dozen questions in question period on the auditor general's Ontario Place redevelopment findings.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles' integrity complaint focused largely on terms and negotiations that led to a 95-year lease for Therme to build and operate a spa and waterpark at Ontario Place, but also alleges "irregularities" with the plan to relocate the science centre.
Stiles suggests, based on information previously uncovered last year by the auditor general, that the government wanted to have the science centre at Ontario Place and integrate parking promised to Therme with the science centre building in order to dispel public concerns over the project.
"This is yet another expensive project that nobody asked for - with a plan that no one is buying," Stiles wrote in a statement this week.
"We could have revitalized the existing Science Centre for a fraction the cost. Ford and Surma boasted about the new Science Centre opening in 2028, but that sounds like more false promises from a flailing government."
A spokesperson for Infrastructure Ontario said the procurement process for the new centre continues, with the request for proposals stage expected to wrap up next fall.
A firm date for the completion of construction will be posted once a contract is awarded, and the opening date will depend on the centre's timeline for moving into the new building, Karen Evans said in an email.
"The overall plan for delivering the vision remains unchanged," she said.
That audit found that it will now cost more for the government to build a new Ontario Science Centre at Ontario Place than it would have to maintain the site it abruptly closed earlier this year.
The cost estimate for building and maintaining a new science centre at Ontario Place has increased by nearly $400 million from the government's spring 2023 business case for relocating it, the auditor said, meaning it will cost approximately $1.4 billion - higher than the $1.3-billion estimate for maintaining the attraction at its east Toronto location.
The increase is due to higher design and construction costs, life cycle and maintenance costs, and ancillary costs that have added up because of changes to the scope of the planned building and about $61 million in cost escalations, Spence wrote.
Michael Lindsay, at the time the president and CEO of Infrastructure Ontario, said he didn't agree that building a new science centre will cost more than rehabilitating the old one, noting that project would also be facing inflationary price increases.
Lindsay provided some reasoning for possible delays in a briefing on the day the science centre was closed.
"The language ... (of "as early as" 2028) probably just reflects the realities of procurement and construction," he said.
"We're going to be talking to our counterparties through the procurement about what it would take to construct a new science centre, how long that might be, and so I think the plan remains the plan. That language probably just points at the reality that further conversation with our market is coming about what it's going to take to both procure and then build the new science centre."
Ontario is planning to have a temporary science centre open in the interim, but has not provided any public updates on that process since it issued a request for proposals in June.
That RFP showed the province is looking for retail or commercial space of about 50,000 to 100,000 square feet - much smaller than the original building's 568,000 square feet - with a start date of "no later than" Jan. 1, 2026.
The science centre is currently operating two pop-up exhibits at Harbourfront Centre and the Sherway Gardens mall in Toronto.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A temporary pop-up Ontario Science Centre is seen at Sherway Gardens. After the province said last year that the Science Centre would open at Ontario Place in 2028, an auditor general's report this month said "the new building is expected to open in 2029."Nathan Denette THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Document 312 of 500

'I wanna be there for you and by your side': Safe-injection worker pleads guilty to aiding suspect in killing of Leslieville mom Karolina Huebner-Makurat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-wanna-be-there-you-your-side-safe-injection/docview/3147526593/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+wanna+be+there+for+you+and+by+your+side%27%3A+Safe-injection+worker+pleads+guilty+to+aiding+suspect+in+killing+of+Leslieville+mom+Karolina+Huebner-Makurat&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A community harm-reduction health worker has pleaded guilty to being an accessory to manslaughter in connection to the high-profile shooting death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat outside a Leslieville safe injection site two summers ago.
In doing so, Khalila Mohammed has admitted to developing a relationship with a man she helped flee the scene - by treating his wounds, hiding his clothing, lying to police, and sending him a chain of texts revealing a "romance" that began immediately after Huebner-Makurat's death.
"I wanna be there for you and by your side," Mohammed wrote in one text message, dated, July 9, 2023 - two days after a stray bullet struck and killed the 44-year-old mother of two, sparking intense local outrage in the bustling area near the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, at 955 Queen St. E.
Huebner-Makurat was on her way to lunch with a friend when alleged drug dealers exchanged bullets on the busy street.
The furore over her killing - in a neighbourhood that had warned about open drug use and increased crime around the facility - contributed to Premier Doug Ford's move earlier this year to ban safe injection sites near schools and daycares.
The health centre's site - one of the city's first when it opened in November 2017 - is being forced to close.
On Wednesday, Khalila Mohammed, 25, who supervised users injecting drugs at the site since 2021, stood in a downtown Toronto courtroom with her lawyer Brian Greenspan and admitted she assisted Ahmed Ibrahim - with whom she developed a romantic relationship after the killing. She was supposed to have a preliminary hearing earlier this month; her guilty plea eliminates the need for a trial.
Ontario Court Justice Russell Silverstein asked Mohammed if she had read a 28-page synopsis of the case and understood her decision.
"Yes, Your Honour," the Pickering resident said in a soft voice. She had no criminal record before this offence, and acknowledged she will receive a conviction and criminal record.
Damian Hudson, who is in his early 30s, is set to stand trial for second-degree murder for allegedly firing the shot that killed Huebner-Makurat. Ibrahim, 21, has meanwhile been ordered to stand trial for robbery and manslaughter. He is not alleged to have been armed that day.
Police believe a third suspect - Ahmed Ali, alleged to be the shooter who exchanged fire with Hudson - has fled to Somalia.
'Get out of the city' The prosecution alleges that Hudson, Ibrahim and Ali were drug dealers who frequented the parkette next to the health centre.
According to the court's summary of the case, Mohammed spent a lot of time there talking to Ibrahim, whom she referred to as Ben.
On July 7, surveillance cameras captured a man alleged to be Ali attacking Hudson, while Ibrahim grabbed his satchel. Pandemonium erupted when the guns came out; in all, six cartridge casings were found, four fired from a .40-calibre handgun and two from a .22-calibre pistol.
While Huebner-Makurat lay dying on Queen Street, Mohammed tended to Ibrahim, who was injured and bloodied, inside the health centre. She then provided him with a change of clothing, escorted him off the premises and ordered him a ride-sharing vehicle with her personal account.
Text messages between the pair, later obtained by police, established that their relationship "blossomed into a romance" immediately after the shooting, the court's summary reads.
"From her messages, it is clear that Khalila Mohammed is aware that Ibrahim was directly involved in the shooting of Karoline Huebner-Makurat. She suggests to him that he 'stay away for a while' to avoid being arrested by police. She offers to provide him with Percocet pills. She offers to check the news media releases regarding the shooting. She says she is going to check surveillance footage to see if he is visible," the 28-page document states. It adds there is no evidence that she actually checked footage or had access to it.
In the same July 9 text conversation where she wrote she wanted to be "by your side," Mohammed told Ibrahim about her knowledge of how the investigation is unfolding, and informed him that police want to speak to her.
That night, they discussed the quality of three suspect images released by police. She told him to "get out of the city" and "lay low," and said she had his jeans and belt tucked away and that his hooded sweatshirt is "gone."
A response back: "Make sure your deleting all your texts, trust me."
The next day, Mohammed also assured Ibrahim that the way the cameras are positioned at the centre, they would not have captured the shooting. This showed "an awareness on her part of Ibrahim's involvement in the shooting," the synopsis says.
"Big breath babe," she texted Ibrahim. "I can't see s--- from that video."
On July 12, Mohammed got a visit from two detectives. "Yoooooo," she texted. "Homicide detective are here ... 2 of them."
She then lied to them, telling the officers she did not know the "guy who came in for wound care." At the same time, she reassured Ibrahim in text messages that she would tell police she knew nothing, and that he could not be identified in a billboard about the case.
"No face, no case," she wrote to him.
Their relationship continued in the weeks that followed, primarily via text messages. On July 20, she rented an Airbnb in Pickering where they spent the night together.
When Mohammed was arrested Aug. 14, 2023, she continued to deny any knowledge of Ibrahim.
Her sentencing hearing is set for March 28.
The prosecution and defence did not indicate their positions on punishment, except to note that it will be a contested hearing.
Once Mohammed is sentenced, the Crown will withdraw three other charges: two counts of attempting to obstruct justice and accessory after the fact to robbery.
The ongoing fallout from the shootingAfter Huebner-Makurat's death, the Ontario government passed the Community Care and Recovery Act. It is set to go into effect at the end of March. It introduces new zoning restrictions that require the safe injection sites to be located at least 200 metres from schools and daycare centres, forcing the closure of 10 Ontario sites, including the South Riverdale site.
The premier has called safe injection sites a "failed policy" experiment that has created dangerous conditions near homes and schools.
Last week, a Toronto social service agency announced it is suing the Ontario government, claiming its new legislation violates the Charter and that removing the sites will "deprive people of lifesaving care."
CAPTION: A still from surveillance footage at the South Riverdale Community Health Centre shows Ahmed Ibrahim, in red, being escorted out of the front of the building by Khalila Mohammed.The outrage over the shooting of Karolina Huebner-Makurat, 44, contributed to Doug Ford'smove to close of several safe-injections sites across the province.

Document 313 of 500

Justin Trudeau to make Toronto MP Nate Erskine-Smith housing minister, Ottawa MP goes to public safety

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-make-toronto-mp-nate-erskine-smith/docview/3147526391/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+to+make+Toronto+MP+Nate+Erskine-Smith+housing+minister%2C+Ottawa+MP+goes+to+public+safety&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Amid continued questions about his leadership, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will shuffle his cabinet Friday.
A senior government source, who was not authorized to speak publicly, confirmed that Trudeau will significantly shuffle his front bench on Friday at 11:30 a.m.
The Star has learned that Ottawa MP David McGuinty will become the public safety minister and Toronto MP Nate Erskine-Smith will take on the housing portfolio.
Erskine-Smith has been an MP since 2015, but has never served as a cabinet minister. He has voted against his own party on several occasions, although he has supported the government on any platform promises and in all confidence votes. He announced publicly that he would not run again.
Last year, he ran for the leadership of the Ontario Liberal Party, coming second to Bonnie Crombie.
The source said the shuffle is expected to address the fallout from several cabinet resignations this fall, as well as recent announcements by ministers who have said they will not be running in next year's election.
Trudeau has already moved Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc into the finance role after Chrystia Freeland's resignation from cabinet on Monday. Freeland surprised Ottawa with her decision to step down just hours before she was set to deliver the fall fiscal update.
McGuinty has been an MP since 2004, and held onto his seat even in the 2011 election when the Liberals were reduced to just 34 seats in the House of Commons. He currently serves as chair of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, a committee which has been critical of several of the agencies he will now oversee, including the RCMP, CSIS and CSE. His brother, Dalton, was premier of Ontario from 2003 to 2013.
When asked, following Freeland's departure, if he believed Trudeau was harming the Liberal party's reputation, McGuinty said it would be a "mistake to underestimate" both the party and the prime minister.
Freeland left Trudeau's cabinet Monday with a scathing resignation letter, in which she argued the government was overspending and not keeping money available for a widely expected trade war when U.S. president-elect Donald Trump is sworn in on Jan. 20.
"We need to take that threat extremely seriously," Freeland wrote in the letter, which she posted on social media. "That means eschewing costly political gimmicks, which we can ill afford and which make Canadians doubt that we recognize the gravity of the moment."
Speaking on the political podcast "The Herle Burly," LeBlanc said he recognizes the impact Trump's proposed 25 per cent tariffs would have on Canada, but is confident the government has what it needs to respond.
"I'm reassured that the government has the fiscal room, if there's a decision that has to be made, to intervene significantly," he said.
The shuffle will have to deal with much more than the fallout from Freeland's departure, as several of Trudeau's ministers have resigned or said they would not seek re-election since his last major cabinet shuffle in July 2023.
In September, Pablo Rodriguez stepped down as transport minister to seek the leadership of the Quebec Liberal party. The transport portfolio was given to Treasury Board President Anita Anand, who has been overseeing both files since then.
Last month, Randy Boissonnault gave up his employment, workforce development and official languages portfolios amid questions about whether he had improperly claimed Indigenous heritage while a business he co-owned sought government contracts. His files were added to Veterans Affairs Minister Ginette Petitpas Taylor's workload.
On Monday, just prior to Freeland's resignation, Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced that he would not run again. Earlier this fall, National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Sport Minister Carla Qualtrough, Southern Ontario Economic Development Minister Filomena Tassi and Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal all said they would not seek reelection.
Friday's shuffle comes amid growing concerns about Trudeau's leadership. As of Thursday afternoon, 18 Liberal MPs had publicly called on him to resign.
Other than brief remarks at two Liberal holiday parties this week, Trudeau has not addressed those calls and has not spoken to reporters since Freeland's resignation.
With files from Raisa Patel and Susan Delacourt
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau delivers remarks during a National Caucus holiday event in Ottawa, on Dec. 17, 2024.
