A revolt brewing against Justin Trudeau + a Toronto mom helping deliver letters to Santa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/revolt-brewing-against-justin-trudeau-toronto-mom/docview/3147091342/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+revolt+brewing+against+Justin+Trudeau+%2B+a+Toronto+mom+helping+deliver+letters+to+Santa&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, December 19 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
We're making changes to First Up. Now, only Star subscribers get the full version of First Up, with more context on the day's news right in their inbox - and they get sent it before anyone else, too. Not a subscriber yet? Join the community supporting our trusted journalism: subscribe today for only $1.
Here's the latest on Liberal MPs calling for Justin Trudeau's resignation, a Toronto mom delivering letters to Santa and another turn in Olivia Chow's bumpy ride with Uber.
DON'T MISSA revolt is brewing against Justin Trudeau
More Liberal MPs have begun openly calling for the prime minister to step down.
Loonie dips below 70 cents U.S. for first time since start of pandemic as Freeland resignation shakes marketsMatt Gurney: Chrystia Freeland is not a heroScott Stinson: Jagmeet Singh is embarrassing himselfThe Canada Post strike jeopardized letters to Santa - so this Toronto mom helped deliver
While Canada Post was on strike, Caroline Tolton responded to neighbourhood kids' letters to Santa.

City council approved a new ad campaign calling for higher wages for drivers.

Toronto unanimously approved down payment help for higher-income earners. Here's how Olivia Chow can fix that.The Googly Eye Bandit isn't just putting peepers on public art, Vinay Menon writes. They're providing a balm for the soul in these gloomy times."I'm absolutely happy." After tangling with bylaws, this curbside coffee seller got a reprieve from Toronto council.A bullet was found under a child's bed after several Oshawa homes were hit by gunfire yesterday.There's one good reason Trump is happy to see Chrystia Freeland go, writes Carol Off.Olivia or Noah? Document 316 of 500

'There's no words': Veteran Toronto police officer dies while on duty in a suspected medical event

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-no-words-veteran-toronto-police-officer/docview/3147091142/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There%27s+no+words%27%3A+Veteran+Toronto+police+officer+dies+while+on+duty+in+a+suspected+medical+event&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A veteran detective died after executing a search warrant in North York Thursday morning in what Toronto police say appears to be a medical episode.
"There's no words to express the sorrow that we feel right now," an emotional Toronto police Deputy Chief Lauren Pogue told reporters Thursday afternoon, outside Humber River Hospital.
"Losing a friend and colleague in the line of duty always has a profound impact on our service, on our city, on our members and our policing community,"
Pogue identified the officer as Det. John Park, a 14-year veteran of the Toronto police who leaves behind a wife and two children, who are nine and five years old.
"Det. Park was a wonderful and loving father, and he was appreciated by everyone who knew him at the Toronto Police Service," Pogue said.
Earlier Thursday, Toronto police said in a statement that preliminary information suggested the cause of death was medical.
Pogue later reiterated that message, telling reporters that it appeared Park experienced a "medical episode" after executing a search warrant connected to an ongoing robbery investigation. He was found alone, she said, providing no further details.
Park was a member of the Toronto police hold-up squad and had just been promoted to sergeant last week, according to a police source who spoke on condition of anonymity.
Earlier Thursday, senior police leadership gathered at Humber River Hospital, where the officer had been rushed. Inside, a handful of officers could be seen huddled outside a room in the emergency department.
On Thursday afternoon, dozens of emergency vehicles, including fire, EMS and the police mounted unit, launched a procession on Sir William Hearst Drive to accompany the officer's body to the nearby Ontario Forensic Pathology office. Scores of uniformed police officers joined the procession by foot, alongside police leaders and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow.
The robbery search warrant execution took place at a building near Jane Street and Steeles Avenue West, in North York's 31 division just before his death.
News of a police officer's on duty death initially sparked concerns that Park may have come into contact with a toxic substance, such as narcotics, but Pogue stressed there is currently "nothing to believe that it is related to narcotics exposure."
Two forensic vehicles, 10 police cars and an ambulance were stationed in the lot, alongside three fire trucks. Uniformed officers were later seen patrolling and taking photos of the vehicles in the lot.
In a statement posted on X, formerly Twitter, Ontario premier Doug Ford said he was "deeply saddened" by the death.
"My condolences go out to the family and loved ones of the officer, as well as all members of the @TorontoPolice," the premier wrote.
Mayor Olivia Chow also issued a statement, saying her "heartfelt condolences go out to the officer's family, friends, and colleagues during this painful time."
More to come.

John Park, who died in the line of duty in North York Thursday morning.Officers prepare for a procession to honour Det. John Park, a 14-year veteran of the Toronto police who died in the line of duty Thursday.Emergency workers gathered outside Humber River Hospital after a Toronto police officer died in the line of duty in North York Thursday morning.According to an internal memo sent to officers Thursday morning, the officer had just executed a search warrant in North York's 31 division just before his death."It is with profound sadness that we confirm the passing of one of our officers while on duty this morning," Toronto police tweeted Thursday."It is with profound sadness that we confirm the passing of one of our officers while on duty this morning," Toronto police tweeted Thursday."It is with profound sadness that we confirm the passing of one of our officers while on duty this morning," Toronto police tweeted."It is with profound sadness that we confirm the passing of one of our officers while on duty this morning," Toronto police tweeted.

Document 317 of 500

City OKs changes to housing program: Council unanimously approves down payment assistance for higher-income earners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-oks-changes-housing-program/docview/3146873776/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+OKs+changes+to+housing+program%3A+Council+unanimously+approves+down+payment+assistance+for+higher-income+earners&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto councillors have greenlit changes to their affordable home ownership strategy that will allow assistance for higher-income earners, in recognition that city hall was continually falling short of its own targets.
The city's program previously operated by waiving fees for housing organizations such as Habitat for Humanity and Options for Homes, with the idea that savings would be passed on as down payment assistance loans for low-to-mid-income earners.
The revamp approved in a unanimous vote Wednesday adds new fee waivers and options for non-profits to partner with private developers, while raising the income levels eligible for assistance.
Where the program to date has capped prospective homebuyers' incomes at the 60th percentile, or around $102,000 this year, council has now approved assistance for households as high as the 80th percentile of earnings, or around $160,000.
While staff faced a handful of questions on the strategy's details from councillors Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre) and Stephen Holyday (Ward 2, Etobicoke Centre) during the council meeting, including questions about how participants gained equity, the changes were approved without heavy debate. Housing committee chair Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park) offered just four words of commentary before the vote was held: "Vote yes, thank you."
The overhaul of Toronto's affordable home ownership strategy was sparked by limited uptake, with city hall acknowledging it was "typically" falling short of its target of helping 400 households per year - as well as the province recently mandating that cities provide fee waivers to non-profits. While staff blamed a number of factors, from higher interest rates to a relatively limited number of interested builders, a major challenge has been bridging the widening gap between Torontonians' incomes and housing costs.
The city considers home ownership affordable when housing bills take up no more than 30 per cent of a household's earnings. City housing development director Noah Slater, in a previous interview with the Star, raised the example of a $750,000 condo.
For a family at the 80th percentile of earnings, they might be within tens of thousands of dollars of being able to buy it, versus the hundreds of thousands of dollars a family in the 60th percentile of earnings would need, Slater said.
An analysis by Beam Group and BGM Strategy Group also pointed to other problems with the old rule system, such as restrictive eligibility criteria.
In Alexandra Park, the Star recently reported on an affordable ownership program aimed at tenants of the area's subsidized and co-op housing.
Run by Habitat for Humanity, that program struggled this year to find takers, which some tenants blamed on eligibility rules. While the average income in 2023 among Toronto's subsidized housing tenants was roughly $19,200, the income required to qualify for one of the available units was $63,000 per year.
Under the new program, assistance offered to the highest-income earners would be formally counted as "attainable" rather than "affordable" housing. A focus on "attainable" housing was one requirement of a deal between Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford last year, which saw the province assume responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway.
But even with the changes now approved by city council, Slater had warned that Toronto won't likely see "substantial gains" on its home ownership goals - with a big-picture target of approving 4,000 new affordable owned homes by 2030 - unless Ottawa and Queen's Park also offer more assistance.
CAPTION: City council has approved new rules that make households in the 80th percentile of earners eligible for down payment assistance. The revamp also adds new fee waivers and options for non-profits to partner with private developers.Lance McMillan Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Victoria Gibson Toronto Star

Subject: Down payments; Waivers; Profits; Home ownership; Low income housing credit; Affordable housing; Housing policy

Document 318 of 500

Two Liberals, two reasons for keeping their distance from PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-liberals-reasons-keeping-their-distance-pm/docview/3146873129/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+Liberals%2C+two+reasons+for+keeping+their+distance+from+PM&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bonnie Crombie, meet Chrystia Freeland.
You have a common frenemy. But you each have a very different political strategy and personal reality.
Crombie, of course, is the Ontario Liberal leader who aspires to be the province's next premier. Freeland, before we forget, is the former federal finance minister who dreams of being Canada's next Liberal prime minister.
Both politicians are feverishly distancing themselves from Justin Trudeau's political dead weight: Crombie doesn't want an albatross around her neck; Freeland didn't want Trudeau chopping her political neck.
But they are wary of the PM in very different ways.
This week, Freeland offered a furious denunciation of Trudeau's latest tax cuts and rebates as ill-timed "gimmicks." Her headline-grabbing critique on Parliament Hill was economically instructive, if perhaps politically destructive and personally self-serving.
In recent weeks, Crombie has proffered her own public objections to federal government tax policies. In search of her own headlines (harder to garner at Queen's Park), Crombie dissociated herself from the shared Freeland-Trudeau legacy, notably the carbon levy.
In a flurry of pre-election platform announcements, Crombie has promised to cut income taxes and slash government fees for eligible first-time homebuyers, as she remakes herself into a tax-fighter for the times. For we live in increasingly anti-tax times.
The timing and tactics of these two public figures, Crombie and Freeland, serve as reminders of how hard it is, in today's increasingly volatile climate, to discern serious policy from self-serving politicking. In her belated cri de coeur - made public, conveniently, only after she lost her job - Freeland decried Trudeau's Christmas tax cuts as pointless politics. Better to "keep our powder dry," she argued, in case all those billions are needed to put out fires in any future trade war with Donald Trump's America.
Fair enough. But what if the president-elect ultimately forgoes those threatened 25 per cent tariffs, would Freeland still condemn the Liberal government's tax breaks down the road?
One suspects that if she were still in cabinet, keen to save the collective political skin of her fellow Liberals, she'd be fully on board for a precampaign tax giveaway - not today, just later in the day, before election day. Which makes Freeland's clear-eyed critique less thoughtful and more situational.
The point is that Freeland, too, is a product of our political times, even if she purports to be different. Tax cuts are now the order of the day - the only way for any politician to cut through the noise and collect votes.
Tories aren't the only tax-fighters. New Democrats are also leading the way, from federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's latest demands to Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew's early tax cuts when his NDP government took power.
The difference, perhaps, is that Freeland felt more guilt than the others because she backed the expansion of child care, pharmacare and dental care programs. It's hard to call for more spending in one breath while calling for tax cuts in the next, although that hasn't stopped some politicians (Trudeau and Ford, for example) from sucking and blowing at the same time.
It's worth noting that various provincial premiers, led by Ontario's Doug Ford and Manitoba's Kinew, offered Freeland heartfelt praise when her resignation became public Monday. Never mind that her impassioned critique of gimmicky tax cuts applies just as much to the tax-cutting sprees of these two like-minded premiers from two opposing parties.
Which is where Crombie finds herself as she tries to stand out in a crowded political field. Personally, my heart sinks whenever I hear politicians play the anti-tax card, because I believe in the social programs that make government an essential force for good in increasingly bad times.
I hear people complain bitterly about "hallway health care" (as Ford has since before taking power six years ago); I listen to people worry about the impact of climate change on weather patterns (as they lambaste the refundable carbon levy); I watch families fret about the scourge of rising homelessness and drug addiction on our streets; and I heed the need for pharmacare and dental care as part of medicare.
You can't have it both ways, calling for more funding and fewer taxes in the same breath, because de-taxing ultimately leads to defunding. On the other hand, Crombie (and Freeland, for that matter) can't get elected today when they are flanked by new-found tax-fighters to the left and right.
Which is why Freeland's critique felt fatuous after the fact. Her gratuitous rhetoric about gimmicky tax cuts might have more credibility were she not guilty of her own inconstancy and inconsistency on fiscal policy.
Against that backdrop of political contradictions, what are we to make of Crombie's new-found tax-cutting persona as she tries to court anti-tax voters, which is to say most of the electorate? Should we reproach her for telling voters what they like to hear - and are hearing in full force from Tories and New Democrats as elections loom?
It turns out that Crombie's housing plan has won plaudits from some experts and advocates as good policy - a way to break down barriers for first-time buyers. Mostly, though, it may be good politics for first-time voters.
The challenge for any politician - friend, foe or frenemy - is to find a way.
CAPTION: Contrary to Chrystia Freeland's recent denunciation of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's latest tax cuts and rebates, Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, right, has chosen to remake herself into a tax-fighter for the times, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Chris YoungThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Dental care; Tax cuts; Voters; Prime ministers; Politicians; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax cuts

Location: Manitoba Canada; Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin; Freeland, Chrystia; Crombie, Bonnie

Company / organization: Name: Liberal Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Title: Two Liberals, two reasons for keeping their distance from PM

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.9

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 19, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: Opinions, Commentary

ProQuest document ID: 3146873129

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-liberals-reasons-keeping-their-distance-pm/docview/3146873129/se-2?accountid=15090

Copyright: Copyright 2024 Toronto Star Newspapers Limited. All Rights Reserved.

Last updated: 2024-12-27

Document 319 of 500

How Bonnie Crombie fits into Chrystia Freeland's critique

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-bonnie-crombie-fits-into-chrystia-freelands/docview/3147191768/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Bonnie+Crombie+fits+into+Chrystia+Freeland%27s+critique&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 320 of 500

'Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl' review: As the British cut-ups return, Tom Cruise and 007 aren't safe from their spoofing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wallace-amp-gromit-vengeance-most-fowl-review-as/docview/3147191765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Wallace+%26amp%3B+Gromit%3A+Vengeance+Most+Fowl%27+review%3A+As+the+British+cut-ups+return%2C+Tom+Cruise+and+007+aren%27t+safe+from+their+spoofing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Butter me crumpets! Getting a new "Wallace & Gromit" movie from Aardman Animations at Christmas is like receiving a tin of home-baked goodies from a beloved relative across the pond.
"Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl" continues the handmade quality and stop-motion whimsy of cheese-gobbling inventor Wallace (voiced by Ben Whitehead, replacing the late Peter Sallis) and his silently faithful canine pal, Gromit, in their quintessentially British exploits.
A bonus this time is the return of adorably evil nemesis Feathers McGraw, a mute and menacing penguin who hasn't had a major role in an Aardman movie for three decades, although he's made cameo appearances.
It's been 31 years since "The Wrong Trousers," Nick Park's stop-motion short that introduced us to the beady-eyed Feathers, a resourceful but delusional felon whose red rubber-glove headpiece fools only the foolish into thinking he's a chicken.
McGraw is now also a jailbird, ignominiously incarcerated in a zoo after being thwarted by Wallace and his pooch for attempting to steal the much-prized Blue Diamond, a rare item of serious value in the blue-collar world of Park's creation.
"Vengeance Most Fowl," the second "Wallace & Gromit" feature (it follows 2005's Oscar-winning "The Curse of the Were-Rabbit"), picks up after the events from McGraw's earlier heist "many years ago," as an opening text note accurately states.
Ever the tinkerer, Wallace has moved on from inventing fancy pants that can scale walls and pivoted into "smart gnome" technology, much to the chagrin of Gromit, who feels his pal has gone overboard in replacing humans with machines.
Gnome rhymes with "home" and "roam," which leads to more of the outrageous puns that Aardman favours. Canine and cheese riffs abound, as do visual and sonic hat tips to classic movies - James Bond and Tom Cruise flicks among them. Feathers has an evil lair like 007 villain Ernst Stavro Blofeld's, but he strokes a pet baby seal instead of a Persian cat.
The bookish Gromit goes to bed reading such English Lit faves as "A Room of One's Own" by Virginia "Woof" and "Paradise Lost" by John "Stilton," sight gags that might baffle kids but should keep their parents amused.
Wallace's faux-friendly robot gnome, Norbot (voiced by Reece Shearsmith), is a relentlessly cheerful and helpful pal that just might be the solution to the pair's growing pile of past-due bills - although anti-tech Gromit's understandable instinct is to want to switch it off.
The voice-activated assistant is remarkably capable - it can trim a hedge in seconds flat - but has made Gromit feel as if he's become man's second-best friend.
Even with a factory setting of "good," Norbot's wide-eyed cheer is, admittedly, a little unsettling. It makes me think of the malevolent ventriloquist's dummy Fats in "Magic," the 1978 psychological horror film starring Anthony Hopkins.
But when Norbot's personality is hacked and dialed to "evil" by Feathers, who proceeds to create a veritable gnome army, the machine's eyes go dark and soulless, a sight some younger viewers might find scarier than intended.
Like a proud papa, Wallace fails to see Norbot's flaws, but at least some characters - Gromit, for one - trust their instincts. As the gnome invasion rises faster than a New Jersey mystery drone, and with the Blue Diamond once again threatened, the cops are forced to step in.
Bumbling Chief Inspector Albert Mackintosh (Peter Kay) and his competent new sidekick PC Mukherjee (Lauren Patel) are on the case, determined to find the culprit behind a corrupted Norbot army crime wave.
The question of automation (and automatons) in today's society is relevant, and "Vengeance Most Fowl" has mischievous fun with the premise, even if this inventive tale - with a running time of 79 minutes and a script trimmed to Norbot-approved neatness - isn't reinventing the wheel of cheese (ouch!).
Still, the gentle touch of the stop-motion animation feels distinctly human. That's welcome in these hard digital times since, as Wallace learns, some things, like patting his old pal Gromit, are simply better when done by hand.
Naive Wallace learns the hard way that machines don't want to be our friends, they want to replace us: "I never imagined they could be used for wrongdoing."
"Vengeance Most Foul" is a cracking good yarn, but Park and his team missed at least one opportunity for groan-worthy punnery. Could they not have worked in a reference to "Diamonds Are Furr-ever" in their Bond riffs?
Next time, maybe. We'll be cheesed to meet the gang once again.
CAPTION: Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Fowl | Official Trailer | NetflixGromit, robot gnome Norbot and Wallace star in "Wallace & Gromit: Vengeance Most Foul," now screening at TIFF Lightbox.

Subject: Diamonds; Motion pictures

People: Cruise, Tom

Document 321 of 500

Toronto unanimously approves down payment help for higher-income earners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-unanimously-approves-down-payment-help/docview/3146504854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+unanimously+approves+down+payment+help+for+higher-income+earners&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto councillors have greenlit changes to their affordable home ownership strategy that will allow assistance for higher-income earners, in recognition that city hall was continually falling short of its own targets.
The city's program previously operated by waiving fees for housing organizations such as Habitat for Humanity and Options for Homes, with the idea that savings would be passed on as down payment assistance loans for low-to-mid-income earners.
The revamp approved in a unanimous vote Wednesday adds new fee waivers and options for non-profits to partner with private developers, while raising the income levels eligible for assistance.
Where the program to date has capped prospective homebuyers' incomes at the 60th percentile, or around $102,000 this year, council has now approved assistance for households as high as the 80th percentile of earnings, or around $160,000.
While staff faced a handful of questions on the strategy's details from councillors Chris Moise (Ward 13, Toronto Centre) and Stephen Holyday (Ward 2, Etobicoke Centre) during the council meeting, including questions about how participants gained equity, the changes were approved without heavy debate. Housing committee chair Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park) offered just four words of commentary before the vote was held: "Vote yes, thank you."
The overhaul of Toronto's affordable home ownership strategy was sparked by limited uptake, with city hall acknowledging it was "typically" falling short of its target of helping 400 households per year - as well as the province recently mandating that cities provide fee waivers to non-profits. While staff blamed a number of factors, from higher interest rates to a relatively limited number of interested builders, a major challenge has been bridging the widening gap between Torontonians' incomes and housing costs.
The city considers home ownership affordable when housing bills take up no more than 30 per cent of a household's earnings. City housing development director Noah Slater, in a previous interview with the Star, raised the example of a $750,000 condo. For a family at the 80th percentile of earnings, they might be within tens of thousands of dollars of being able to buy it, versus the hundreds of thousands of dollars a family in the 60th percentile of earnings would need, Slater said.
An analysis by Beam Group and BGM Strategy Group also pointed to other problems with the old rule system, such as restrictive eligibility criteria.
In Alexandra Park, the Star recently reported on an affordable ownership program aimed at tenants of the area's subsidized and co-op housing. Run by Habitat for Humanity, that program struggled this year to find takers, which some tenants blamed on eligibility rules. While the average income in 2023 among Toronto's subsidized housing tenants was roughly $19,200, the income required to qualify for one of the available units was $63,000 per year.
Under the new program, assistance offered to the highest-income earners would be formally counted as "attainable" rather than "affordable" housing. A focus on "attainable" housing was one requirement of a deal between Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford last year, which saw the province assume responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway.
But even with the changes now approved by city council, Slater had warned that Toronto won't likely see "substantial gains" on its home ownership goals - with a big-picture target of approving 4,000 new affordable owned homes by 2030 - unless Ottawa and Queen's Park also offer more assistance.
CAPTION: Toronto City Hall just approved new rules that make it so households in the 80th percentile of earners are eligible for down payment assistance.

Document 322 of 500

Olivia Chow has been ducking fights since she took office. It's time to talk about why

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/olivia-chow-has-been-ducking-fights-since-she/docview/3146162544/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Olivia+Chow+has+been+ducking+fights+since+she+took+office.+It%27s+time+to+talk+about+why&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee surprised observers when it decided to send the issue of rideshare services such as Uber back for more study rather than recommending a cap on drivers.
Afterwards, Coun. Brad Bradford said that her "punting" the issue was part of a pattern. "Like so many of her other priorities - tearing down the Gardiner, saving Ontario Place, shutting down the island airport, or delivering a 'modest' property tax increase - she has failed to deliver." This was not surprising stuff from Bradford, who has often presented himself as council's leader of the opposition to Chow.
But it's interesting that, if you subtract the mention of the tax increase, it's a list of items where many voters who might be inclined to support Chow would agree she's disappointed them. Rather than tear down the Gardiner, she arranged to give control over it (and its budget, of course) to Doug Ford. Rather than chain herself to a tree on the waterfront, she agreed to be silent on Ontario Place. Rather than try to shut down the island airport, she insisted she had nothing against it and engineered a vote that would see it stay open.
And there were two other items in the past couple of weeks where she ducked a fight when both her opponents and her potential supporters expected her to come out swinging: Doug Ford's bike lane removals (where activists, not the mayor, are leading a Charter challenge), and her announcement that she's supporting the police department's requested budget increase.
In many cases, the Toronto island airport being a prime example, these are instances where Chow has not made any move to do something. Her opponents insist she must be doing something - it's a secret agenda! - and progressives join the fight, hoping she has a secret agenda because they think she should do that thing.
But why she is not choosing fights that seemingly everyone on all sides expects her to want?
The answer might be that everyone understands her political program wrong. As a longtime prominent NDP member and activist, she is typecast as a wild-eyed radical looking to overhaul government to look like a progressive wish list.
Is it possible that instead she's a pragmatist? I think she thinks she is.
Listening to her talk about political change over the past few years, it seems clear she thinks real transformation often happens in increments, with policy changes that are broadly supported - which means, she has said, they are less likely to be reversed by whoever comes along next. When you do something popular, it stays done.
This is different than the kind of "wedge" politics practised by others - including, notably, Premier Doug Ford. Theirs is an electoral strategy in which you intentionally choose to highlight controversial issues that fire up your base, especially if it can split the opposition into factions that leave you with the largest wedge of the electorate.
In that kind of politics, picking bitter fights is an important part of the job.
I asked Chow about that recently during an interview for the This Matters podcast that will be aired later this week.
"You could alienate a whole pile of people," she said, "and win the fight. Fine. Feels good ... I know how it's done. I've been in politics a long time. (But) it's divisive, and it just gets everybody cynical. And I don't want to see that. I want to see a city where people collaborate and work together, even if they don't totally agree with each other."
"I'm a very practical person," she said. "When I see there's something that is wrong, I want to go and fix it. I want people to collaborate rather than fight, because we're always stronger together."
That's her political slogan, and she understandably expresses it as a matter of principle. But I think she also thinks it's a matter of strategy.
She has some core things she's working on: getting affordable and non-profit housing built (especially government housing), making student nutrition programs universal and improving TTC service are three she frequently mentions. And in each case, her approach appears to be chipping away incrementally: she talks of her main "public builder" housing programs as small demonstrations to get other levels of government to see success and get involved in order to share in it.
And maybe it's also a demonstration to voters - that the things she's talking about and building capacity for inside the government aren't necessarily radical, but instead practical.
Avoiding fights she's likely to lose means she doesn't expend political capital on failures that weaken her ability to do the things that are core to her agenda.
The only thing is, if people who think they're on your side see you avoiding the fights they care about, you're not putting wins on the board that they feel proud of and inspired by, either. If your political approach is to do things that are broadly popular when you do them, it will always look like anyone could have done it.
And if the most noteworthy fight you've won is to raise taxes higher than ever before, people on all sides might wonder what that tax increase is buying that they couldn't get from any other leader.
CAPTION: Toronto Mayor says she's worried that political instability triggered by the resignation of the former finance minister could see the Trudeau government toppled before some funding deals with the city are finalized.

Document 323 of 500

Why Trudeau must go - and now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-trudeau-must-go-now/docview/3146138028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Trudeau+must+go+-+and+now&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As of press time, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was still running the country, but it will be a surprise if he doesn't soon resign, something he should have done months ago.
Trudeau long ago lost the confidence of Canadians, and now lacks the support of top cabinet officers who have quit, as well as much of his own caucus.
There are several able potential successors to Trudeau in a Liberal leadership campaign that can't begin soon enough.
They include Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister whose stunning resignation on Monday brought matters to a head.
Christy Clark, the former B.C. premier, has declared her interest in the job.
And while Mark Carney, former governor of the Bank of Canada, has been too coy by half about his ambitions, he has the credentials to be PM.
And there are others, including cabinet officers François-Philippe Champagne and Mélanie Joly.
But first a recap of this week's turning point in history, when the nine-year-old Trudeau government effectively collapsed.
Chrystia Freeland has long been the keystone of the Trudeau government. Remove her as finance minister and deputy PM and what remains of the dilapidated Trudeau government crumbles.
Freeland, far more than Trudeau, commands the respect - and in the case of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, the affection - of the premiers, the Liberal caucus, the business community, international leaders and the non-profit sector.
She shepherded Canada through the economic crisis of the pandemic, and financed the feds' national daycare, pharmacare and denticare programs. She is a gifted diplomat who worked to make those initiatives consensual among the premiers.
On Friday, Trudeau tried to demote Freeland. She wisely chose instead to quit a madhouse government altogether, though she is keeping her Toronto seat in Parliament, a hopeful sign that she might contest the Liberal leadership.
This space doesn't endorse politicians. The Trudeau government has effectively met a fiery end, and a Liberal leadership contest cannot get underway soon enough. The economic and social stability of the country requires it.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, a man noted for his calm demeanour, demanded Monday that Trudeau resign. In October, a letter signed by about 25 Liberal MPs began circulating in Parliament calling on Trudeau to resign. And that was prior to Monday's catastrophic loss of Freeland.
For once, Pierre Poilievre, the Tory leader, wasn't exaggerating on Monday when he said of Freeland's non-confidence in Trudeau that the Liberal government is "spiralling out of control before our very eyes."
There's not much left of the Trudeau government, with its eight cabinet vacancies.
Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced Monday he is leaving politics.
Fraser was counted on to spearhead one of the biggest affordable housing projects in history.
But most conspicuous among the cabinet defectors is Freeland, aptly dubbed "Minister of Everything."
Freeland secured Canada's free-trade deal with the European Union (EU). And she stood up to then-U.S. president Donald Trump in renegotiating a NAFTA that Trump vowed to rip up.
NAFTA's replacement is a Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) barely distinguishable from NAFTA.
Freeland has delivered several sensible budgets, trying if not always succeeding in squaring the circle of her fiscal probity with Trudeau's big-spending agenda.
Trudeau's dubious GST holiday and promise of $250 cheques for everyday Canadians marked the final rift between Trudeau and his finance minister.
That measure will briefly deliver only a bit more pocket change for Canadians.
But in total, it is an estimated $6 billion to $8 billion expense for Ottawa.
In its fall economic statement issued Monday, Ottawa revealed that the deficit in the current year has ballooned to $61.9 billion from a forecast $40 billion in the latest budget.
That includes a one-time expense of about $16.4 billion in Indigenous claims now making their way through the courts.
Given the danger of U.S. president-elect Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian exports, Ottawa should be bulking up its financial reserves, Freeland says, not squandering them as she believes Trudeau has done.
Protecting Canada from the Trump threat "means keeping our fiscal powder dry today, so we have the reserves we may need for a coming tariff war," Freeland wrote in her resignation letter.
She disparaged Trudeau's "costly political gimmicks."
Every hour Trudeau remains in office diminishes the laudable elements of his legacy. They include some success in fighting poverty, using an affordable national daycare program to help liberate women to join the paid workforce, and, with Freeland's help, forging free-trade deals with the EU and Asia-Pacific and negotiating an acceptable replacement to NAFTA.
The guidance for Liberals today was laid out by Winston Churchill. The emphasized word below is Churchill's:
"If (the leader) trips, he must be sustained. If he makes mistakes, they must be covered. If he sleeps, he must not be wantonly disturbed. If he is no good, he must be pole-axed."
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Document 324 of 500

Fiscal watchdog warns of $12.7-billion shortfall

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fiscal-watchdog-warns-12-7-billion-shortfall/docview/3146137831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fiscal+watchdog+warns+of+%2412.7-billion+shortfall&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It would cost Ontario $31.4 billion over 10 years to clear a backlog of school repair needs, build enough new spaces to address growth and maintain all schools in a state of good repair, but the government's current plan falls far short of that, according to a report Tuesday from the province's fiscal watchdog.
The Financial Accountability Office wrote in a report that the provincial government's 10-year capital plan allocates $18.7 billion for school buildings, resulting in a shortfall of $12.7 billion.
Education Minister Jill Dunlop wrote in a statement that the province has doubled the funding to build and expand schools and cut construction timelines in half.
"We have done our part to invest and cut red tape for school boards to build and we need them to do their part and use the public tax dollars to get shovels in the ground faster," she wrote.
"We are meeting the needs of growing communities with 240 new schools under construction that will create 81,000 pupil spaces in the coming years."
Financial accountability officer Jeffrey Novak's report estimated that about 37 per cent of schools in the province are below a state of good repair, and 32 schools should be replaced entirely.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said when school buildings fall into disrepair, it leads to negative impacts on students.
"It means leaky roofs," she said. "It means classrooms that hit freezing temperatures in the winter and stifling heat in the summer. It means asbestos in our walls and lead in our drinking water, and students learning in portables that should have been replaced decades ago. It is unacceptable."
The FAO report said most schools are under full capacity, with hundreds of schools operating with fewer than 60 per cent of the maximum number of students.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said a $12.7-billion shortfall is a "complete failure."
"This funding isn't even about improving our schools - it's just the bare minimum to keep them safe and functional," she wrote in a statement.
"That's $12.7 billion less for our kids' learning environments. Parents trust our schools to deliver for their children, and we need to invest in them to ensure that trust isn't misplaced."
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Students; School boards; Accountability

Document 325 of 500

LeBlanc is Ford's new best friend in Ottawa: Premier 'a great ally for Canada, and for me,' minister says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leblanc-is-fords-new-best-friend-ottawa/docview/3146137761/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LeBlanc+is+Ford%27s+new+best+friend+in+Ottawa%3A+Premier+%27a+great+ally+for+Canada%2C+and+for+me%2C%27+minister+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Dominic LeBlanc once confided to Doug Ford that he used to babysit Justin Trudeau as a child, the Ontario premier quipped: "Dom, you still do."
LeBlanc - now the embattled Trudeau's minister of everything with responsibilities for finance, intergovernmental affairs and border security - enjoys an especially warm rapport with Ford.
"Premier Ford, as chair of the Council of the Federation, will be a great ally for Canada, and for me," the new omni-minister said after being sworn in at Rideau Hall on Monday eight hours after Chrystia Freeland's shocking resignation plunged Ottawa into disarray.
The treasurer - whose father, Romeo LeBlanc, was press secretary to Trudeau's father, former prime minister Pierre Trudeau, which led to the babysitting gig more than 40 years ago - has worked closely with Ford in recent years.
He is counting on doing so even more in the weeks ahead with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump set to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to reduce illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
"It's been a privilege to work with him in the past," LeBlanc said of Ford.
"I look forward to using the very effective advocacy (of) premiers like Doug Ford (and Alberta) Premier (Danielle) Smith ... as we try and work together to convince the American administration that this isn't the right way to proceed," he said on the front steps of Rideau Hall, where his late father had resided as governor general from 1995 to 1999.
At an Empire Club luncheon Tuesday in Toronto, both Smith and Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey touted LeBlanc's communications skills, saying he would be a constructive partner they need to present a united front against Trump.
Later Tuesday, LeBlanc said he "had an encouraging exchange with Premier Ford this morning" about the $1.3-billion border-security plan.
"We're very much going to rely on Premier Ford (and) his colleague premiers. That he's chairing the Council of the Federation at this moment we think is important for Canada," the minister said in Ottawa. "He and other premiers have a very important role in talking to Americans about the impact of these tariffs on affordability issues in the United States."
The existential threat posed by the next president's tariffs, which would cripple the North American economy by sparking inflation and hindering trade, underscores the importance of a federal Liberal like LeBlanc and a provincial Progressive Conservative like Ford working with one another.
The two men, both gregarious and outgoing, genuinely like one another and have spent private time together in each other's homes even though they live 1,600 kilometres apart.
It was on sunny day in August 2022 - as they puffed cigars on LeBlanc's cottage porch in Grande-Digue, N.B. - that the pair began working toward a revamped health-care funding accord that would be unveiled seven months later.
That mutual respect was again on display last Friday when LeBlanc praised Ford for his hawkish stance in dealing with Trump's tariffs.
"I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years," he said in Fredericton when asked about the premier's threat to withhold Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. in retaliation for any 25 per cent levies. "He's certainly able to speak for himself."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 326 of 500

Chow strategic about when to fight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-strategic-about-when-fight/docview/3146137684/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+strategic+about+when+to+fight&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last week, Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee surprised observers when it decided to send the issue of rideshare services such as Uber back for more study rather than recommending a cap on drivers.
Afterwards, Coun. Brad Bradford said that her "punting" the issue was part of a pattern. "Like so many of her other priorities - tearing down the Gardiner, saving Ontario Place, shutting down the island airport, or delivering a 'modest' property tax increase - she has failed to deliver." This was not surprising stuff from Bradford, who has often presented himself as council's leader of the opposition to Chow.
But it's interesting that, if you subtract the mention of the tax increase, it's a list of items where many voters who might be inclined to support Chow would agree she's disappointed them. Rather than tear down the Gardiner, she arranged to give control over it (and its budget, of course) to Doug Ford. Rather than chain herself to a tree on the waterfront, she agreed to be silent on Ontario Place. Rather than try to shut down the island airport, she insisted she had nothing against it and engineered a vote that would see it stay open.
And there were two other items in the past couple of weeks where she ducked a fight when both her opponents and her potential supporters expected her to come out swinging: Doug Ford's bike lane removals (where activists, not the mayor, are leading a Charter challenge), and her announcement that she's supporting the police department's requested budget increase.
In many cases, the Toronto island airport being a prime example, these are instances where Chow has not made any move to do something. Her opponents insist she must be doing something - it's a secret agenda! - and progressives join the fight, hoping she has a secret agenda because they think she should do that thing.
But why she is not choosing fights that seemingly everyone on all sides expects her to want?
The answer might be that everyone understands her political program wrong. As a longtime prominent NDP member and activist, she is typecast as a wild-eyed radical looking to overhaul government to look like a progressive wish list.
Is it possible that instead she's a pragmatist? I think she thinks she is.
Listening to her talk about political change over the past few years, it seems clear she thinks real transformation often happens in increments, with policy changes that are broadly supported - which means, she has said, they are less likely to be reversed by whoever comes along next. When you do something popular, it stays done.
This is different than the kind of "wedge" politics practised by others - including, notably, Premier Doug Ford. Theirs is an electoral strategy in which you intentionally choose to highlight controversial issues that fire up your base, especially if it can split the opposition into factions that leave you with the largest wedge of the electorate.
In that kind of politics, picking bitter fights is an important part of the job.
I asked Chow about that recently during an interview for the "This Matters" podcast that will be aired later this week.
"You could alienate a whole pile of people," she said, "and win the fight. Fine. Feels good ... I know how it's done. I've been in politics a long time. (But) it's divisive, and it just gets everybody cynical. And I don't want to see that. I want to see a city where people collaborate and work together, even if they don't totally agree with each other.
"I'm a very practical person," she said.
"When I see there's something that is wrong, I want to go and fix it. I want people to collaborate rather than fight, because we're always stronger together."
That's her political slogan, and she understandably expresses it as a matter of principle. But I think she also thinks it's a matter of strategy.
She has some core things she's working on: getting affordable and non-profit housing built (especially government housing), making student nutrition programs universal and improving TTC service are three she frequently mentions. And in each case, her approach appears to be chipping away incrementally: she talks of her main "public builder" housing programs as small demonstrations to get other levels of government to see success and get involved in order to share in it.
And maybe it's also a demonstration to voters - that the things she's talking about and building capacity for inside the government aren't necessarily radical, but instead practical.
Avoiding fights she's likely to lose means she doesn't expend political capital on failures that weaken her ability to do the things that are core to her agenda.
The only thing is, if people who think they're on your side see you avoiding the fights they care about, you're not putting wins on the board that they feel proud of and inspired by, either. If your political approach is to do things that are broadly popular when you do them, it will always look like anyone could have done it.
And if the most noteworthy fight you've won is to raise taxes higher than ever before, people on all sides might wonder what that tax increase is buying that they couldn't get from any other leader.
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Subject: Collaboration; Political activism; Voters; Tax increases

Document 327 of 500

Chrystia Freeland lost confidence in Justin Trudeau - and she showed it in the most damaging way possible

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-lost-confidence-justin-trudeau/docview/3145853775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+lost+confidence+in+Justin+Trudeau+-+and+she+showed+it+in+the+most+damaging+way+possible&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It wasn't Chrystia Freeland's announcement Monday that she was quitting as finance minister that marked the third and final act of Justin Trudeau's government.
It was how she quit. How she tried to define what siding with the prime minister says about Liberals - MPs and cabinet ministers alike - and in doing so paved the way for deeper internal divisions and the impending showdown waiting in the wings.
In a bombshell letter posted to social media Monday morning - the very day she was scheduled to table the federal government's fall economic update, and a federal byelection day in B.C. - Freeland wrote that the prime minister intended to shuffle her out of the finance portfolio and offered her another position. What she didn't say was that it was to become a minister without a department, charged with Canada-U.S. relations - and the same job, say sources who spoke on condition they not be named, that former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney had passed on.
Freeland wrote that the move meant Trudeau no longer had confidence in her. But what she really told Canadians was that she no longer had confidence in him.
Freeland suggested the prime minister is incompetent, that he is a bad fiscal manager, that he cares more about "costly political gimmicks" than preparing the country for the potential catastrophe that Donald Trump's return to the White House signals for Canada.
She suggested Trudeau had failed to adequately respond to the threat so far, that he wasn't working "in good faith and humility with the premiers ... and building a true Team Canada response." While much ink was spilled about the premiers going in their own direction following their call with the prime minister, some on the line felt Trudeau hadn't laid out a fully baked strategy for dealing with another Trump administration, and many were surprised to find their crucial exports to the U.S. - oil, potash, uranium - on the list of targeted products, without any prior consultation.
Freeland suggested that on the key matters facing the country, Trudeau was not only not leading, but that he is doing more harm than good.
By doing so, she threw down the gauntlet: anyone stepping into her old shoes would be endorsing an economic plan she had just demolished.
No wonder rumours flew Monday that Carney had not only rejected the finance portfolio - meaning Freeland was dumped and then expected to read a deficit-crashing, reputation-tarnishing fiscal update with no solid replacement locked-in - but that he would not figure in Trudeau's upcoming cabinet shuffle at all.
It wasn't the only piece of hot gossip. Rumours spread that Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne had refused to read the economic update in the House and was considering quitting; so were Treasury Board President and Transport Minister Anita Anand, and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly (none of whom returned messages before deadline Monday night requesting comment). If Freeland was launching her leadership bid from the backbench, perhaps other ministers were tempted to follow. Several ministers were asked Monday whether they still had confidence in the prime minister; an uncomfortable number declined to comment.
Meanwhile, caucus was abuzz about Freeland joining the dissenters rank. "They try to minimize and dismiss me," New Brunswick MP Wayne Long said of the Prime Minister's Office. "But you can't dismiss her. Not a chance."
More than half a dozen Liberal MPs called publicly on Trudeau to resign Monday. Some suggested the number of MPs now willing to sign a letter calling on the prime minister to step down had swelled to 45.
"I think the only path forward for us is to choose a new leader and to present a new plan to Canadians with a different vision," Hamilton MP Chad Collins told reporters.
It didn't help that earlier in the day, Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced he was stepping aside, and tongues wagged that Toronto MP and former Ontario Liberal leadership candidate Nathaniel Erskine-Smith was in line for the job, after telling the PMO he would run again as an MP if he was giving Fraser's portfolio. Many loyal MPs patiently waiting for their turn in cabinet were dismayed by the news, as were those who appeared to have been passed over for promotions, waiting by the phone all weekend only to never hear it ring.
When Freeland arrived at a hastily called caucus meeting Monday evening, she was greeted with a standing ovation. Her fellow MPs hugged her. She was in the room when Trudeau tried to explain what happened over the weekend, and how much he still respected her.
But the mood in the room was tense. "People are really pissed off," said one Liberal MP, speaking confidentially. They wanted to know why Freeland had been thrown under the bus. The prime minister tried to distance himself from a mess much like the messes he seems to frequently make, as witnessed by his relationship breakdowns with cabinet ministers Bill Morneau, Jody Wilson-Raybould, and David Lametti, just to name just a few.
Some MPs suggested the only way out was prorogation. Others suggested it was only a matter of time before Trudeau realizes he can't hang on and triggers a leadership race.
Complicating matters further, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called on the prime minister to step aside.
If Trudeau didn't have the confidence of his former finance minister, a significant chunk of his caucus, and now the leader of the only party willing to prop him up in the House of Commons, how much longer could his tenure last?
That's the conversation Freeland reignited Monday with her decision to step aside in the most publicly damaging way possible.
CAPTION: Liberal MP Chrystia Freeland arrives for a national caucus meeting in Ottawa on Dec. 16, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Scandals; Prime ministers

Document 328 of 500

Dominic LeBlanc is Doug Ford's new ally in Justin Trudeau's cabinet

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dominic-leblanc-is-doug-fords-new-ally-justin/docview/3145730896/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dominic+LeBlanc+is+Doug+Ford%27s+new+ally+in+Justin+Trudeau%27s+cabinet&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Dominic LeBlanc once confided to Doug Ford that he used to babysit Justin Trudeau as a child, the Ontario premier quipped: "Dom, you still do."
LeBlanc - now the embattled Trudeau's minister of everything with responsibilities for finance, intergovernmental affairs and border security - enjoys an especially warm rapport with Ford.
"Premier Ford, as chair of the Council of the Federation, will be a great ally for Canada, and for me," the new omni-minister said after being sworn in at Rideau Hall on Monday eight hours after Chrystia Freeland's shocking resignation plunged Ottawa into disarray.
The treasurer - whose father, Romeo LeBlanc, was press secretary to Trudeau's father, former prime minister Pierre Trudeau, which led to the babysitting gig more than 40 years ago - has worked closely with Ford in recent years.
He is counting on doing so even more in the weeks ahead with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump set to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to reduce illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
"It's been a privilege to work with him in the past," LeBlanc said of Ford.
"I look forward to using the very effective advocacy (of) premiers like Doug Ford (and Alberta) Premier (Danielle) Smith ... as we try and work together to convince the American administration that this isn't the right way to proceed," he said on the front steps of Rideau Hall, where his late father had resided as governor general from 1995 to 1999.
At an Empire Club luncheon Tuesday in Toronto, both Smith and Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey touted LeBlanc's communications skills, saying he would be a constructive partner they need to present a united front against Trump.
Later Tuesday, LeBlanc said he "had an encouraging exchange with Premier Ford this morning" about the $1.3 billion border-security plan.
"We're very much going to rely on Premier Ford (and) his colleague premiers. That he's chairing the Council of the Federation at this moment we think is important for Canada," the minister said in Ottawa.
"He and other premiers have a very important role in talking to Americans about the impact of these tariffs on affordability issues in the United States," he said.
The existential threat posed by the next president's tariffs, which would cripple the North American economy by sparking inflation and hindering trade, underscores the importance of a federal Liberal like LeBlanc and a provincial Progressive Conservative like Ford working with one another.
While Trump took to social media late Monday night to deride Freeland, whom he dealt with during a 2019 trade war, as a "totally toxic" person "who will not be missed," Ford thinks the world of the Liberal MP for University-Rosedale.
As the Star's Susan Delacourt reported in April 2020, theirs is a friendship forged during the darkest depths of the COVID-19 pandemic.
"He and I have actually come to describe one another as each other's therapists," Freeland said at the time. "Some of the daily struggles that we go through are quite similar and so sometimes when we talk we'll just say, 'This is our therapy session.'"
Ford told Delacourt his nightly hours-long conversations with the prime minister's pandemic point person were therapeutic for them - and productive for a nation gripped by a health emergency.
"We'll talk for God knows how long at nighttime, and discuss every issue you can possibly think of, and we laugh at the end. We just feel like a thousand pounds are off our shoulders," he said at the time.
After the COVID-19 crisis was over, their closeness endured, a relationship that was instrumental in securing billions of dollars in federal and provincial subsidies for Ontario's electric-vehicle manufacturing sector and a national deal on $10-a-day child care, among other initiatives.
Whenever there was an impasse between Ottawa and Queen's Park over funding or policy differences, a phone call or text message between Ford and Freeland could resolve things without meddlesome officials or gatekeepers.
That informality was on display Monday when she phoned her friend during the closed-door Council of the Federation meeting at the Hilton Hotel near Pearson International Airport.
"She gave me a call. I did put it on speaker for the rest of the premiers. We have a good relationship with Chrystia over the years. I just want to wish her all the best," Ford told reporters Monday afternoon following the confab.
But next to Freeland, LeBlanc, a Harvard-educated lawyer, has been the premier's favourite minister in Trudeau's cabinet.
The two men, both gregarious and outgoing, genuinely like one another and have spent private time together in each other's homes even though they live 1,600 km apart.
It was on sunny day in August 2022 - as they puffed cigars on LeBlanc's cottage porch in Grande-Digue, New Brunswick - that the pair began working toward a revamped health-care funding accord that would be unveiled seven months later.
LeBlanc was so appreciative of Ford's instrumental role in securing the health deal that when the premier appealed for help in clearing federal hurdles to building Highway 413, the controversial 52-km Milton-to-Vaughan freeway, the minister assuaged project skeptics like Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault.
That mutual respect was again on display last Friday when LeBlanc praised Ford for his hawkish stance in dealing with Trump's tariffs.
"I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years," he said in Fredericton when asked about the premier's threat to withhold Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. in retaliation for any 25 per cent levies.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself."
Over the coming tumultuous weeks, the two men will be speaking a lot more to one another.
CAPTION: Dominic LeBlanc participates in a news conference after a swearing in ceremony at Rideau Hall in Ottawa on Monday.

Subject: Prime ministers; Pandemics; COVID-19; Tariffs

Document 329 of 500

Why Trudeau must go - and now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-trudeau-must-go-now/docview/3145730565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Trudeau+must+go+-+and+now&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It will be a surprise if Prime Minister Justin Trudeau doesn't soon resign, something he should have done months ago.
Trudeau long ago lost the confidence of Canadians, and now lacks the support of top cabinet officers who have quit, as well as much of his own caucus.
There are several able potential successors to Trudeau in a Liberal leadership campaign that can't begin soon enough.
They include Chrystia Freeland, the former finance minister whose stunning resignation on Monday brought matters to a head.
Christy Clark, the former B.C. premier, has declared her interest in the job.
And while Mark Carney, former governor of the Bank of Canada, has been too coy by half about his ambitions, he has the credentials to be PM.
And there are others, including cabinet officers François-Philippe Champagne and Mélanie Joly.
But first a recap of this week's turning point in history, when the nine-year-old Trudeau government effectively collapsed.
Chrystia Freeland has long been the keystone of the Trudeau government. Remove her as finance minister and deputy PM and what remains of the dilapidated Trudeau government crumbles.
Freeland, far more than Trudeau, commands the respect - and in the case of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, the affection - of the premiers, the Liberal caucus, the business community, international leaders and the non-profit sector.
Freeland shepherded Canada through the economic crisis of the pandemic, and financed the feds' national daycare, pharmacare and denticare programs. She is a gifted diplomat who worked to make those initiatives consensual among the premiers.
On Friday, Trudeau tried to demote Freeland. She wisely chose instead to quit a madhouse government altogether, though she is keeping her Toronto seat in Parliament, a hopeful sign that she might contest the Liberal leadership.
This space doesn't endorse politicians. The Trudeau government has effectively met a fiery end, and a Liberal leadership contest cannot get underway soon enough. The economic and social stability of the country requires it.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, a man noted for his calm demeanour, demanded Monday that Trudeau resign.
In October, a letter signed by about 25 Liberal MPs began circulating in Parliament calling on Trudeau to resign. And that was prior to Monday's catastrophic loss of Freeland.
For once, Pierre Poilievre, the Tory leader, wasn't exaggerating on Monday when he said of Freeland's non-confidence in Trudeau that the Liberal government is "spiralling out of control before our very eyes."
There's not much left of the Trudeau government, with its eight cabinet vacancies.
Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced Monday he is leaving politics. Fraser was counted on to spearhead one of the biggest affordable housing projects in history.
But most conspicuous among the cabinet defectors is Freeland, aptly dubbed "Minister of Everything."
Freeland secured Canada's free-trade deal with the European Union (EU). And she stood up to then-U.S. president Donald Trump in renegotiating a NAFTA that Trump vowed to rip up.
NAFTA's replacement is a Canada-U.S.-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) barely distinguishable from NAFTA.
Freeland has delivered several sensible budgets, trying if not always succeeding in squaring the circle of her fiscal probity with Trudeau's big-spending agenda.
Trudeau's dubious GST holiday and promise of $250 cheques for everyday Canadians marked the final rift between Trudeau and his finance minister.
That measure will briefly deliver only a bit more pocket change for Canadians.
But in total, it is an estimated $6 billion to $8 billion expense for Ottawa.
In its fall economic statement issued Monday, Ottawa revealed that the deficit in the current year has ballooned to $61.9 billion from a forecast $40 billion in the latest budget.
That includes a one-time expense of about $16.4 billion in Indigenous claims now making their way through the courts.
Given the danger of U.S. president-elect Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian exports, Ottawa should be bulking up its financial reserves, Freeland says, not squandering them as she believes Trudeau has done.
Protecting Canada from the Trump threat "means keeping our fiscal powder dry today, so we have the reserves we may need for a coming tariff war," Freeland wrote in her resignation letter.
She disparaged Trudeau's "costly political gimmicks."
Every hour Trudeau remains in office diminishes the laudable elements of his legacy. They include some success in fighting poverty, using an affordable national daycare program to help liberate women to join the paid workforce, and, with Freeland's help, forging free-trade deals with the EU and Asia-Pacific and negotiating an acceptable replacement to NAFTA.
The guidance for Liberals today was laid out by Winston Churchill. The emphasized word below is Churchill's:
"If (the leader) trips, he must be sustained. If he makes mistakes, they must be covered. If he sleeps, he must not be wantonly disturbed. If he is no good, he must be pole-axed."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau long ago lost the confidence of Canadians, and nowlacks the supportof top cabinet officers who have quit, as well as much of his own caucus, David Olive writes.

Subject: Political campaigns; Leadership; Canadians; North American Free Trade Agreement; Decades; Prime ministers

Title: Why Trudeau must go - and now
