Document 330 of 500

Ford reaches for his Captain Canada cape

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-reaches-his-captain-canada-cape/docview/3145482260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+reaches+for+his+Captain+Canada+cape&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fear not, fellow Ontarians and Canadians.
In this hour of political chaos and economic crisis, Doug Ford has a message for all of you - across the province and the country:
The prime minister may be incapacitated for the moment. But the premiers are empowered at full capacity for our future confrontations with the United States.
With the federal government seemingly sidelined, provincial governments are moving into the bilateral breach. Chaos theory is our new Canadian reality, but stability is today's provincial priority as upheaval looms in America.
"No matter what's happening in Ottawa, all of us premiers will remain united as Team Canada to provide steady and stable leadership during these uncertain times in Ottawa," proclaimed Ford, flanked by his fellow premiers at an airport hotel Monday.
At which point he helpfully handed the microphone to his Quebec counterpart, François Legault, to "translate that into French." It was an unmistakable - and unprecedented - message and missive from Ford as he makes the most of his rotating chairmanship of the premiers' council to reincarnate himself as Captain Canada.
Summoned to a summit in Toronto to deal with Donald Trump's vortex of power in Washington, the premiers pivoted to Justin Trudeau's vacuum of power in Ottawa. While focused on the perils of tariffs from the incoming U.S. president, they were blindsided by the outgoing Canadian finance minister, Chrystia Freeland, quitting cabinet.
On the very day they were trying to fathom Trump's next move, they were transfixed by Trudeau's latest moves in removing the minister who had long been their point person in Ottawa. When Freeland called Ford to bid him farewell Monday, he pointedly put her on speakerphone so that this fallen federal politician could have the final word with the premiers mid-meeting.
And so what should have been a day dedicated to preparing for the president-elect's scorched earth tariff strategy turned into a day of earnest, if not pretentious promises to steady the ship of state in turbulent waters with the prime minister missing in action. Listening to their orations, it was sometimes hard to tell if the premiers were auditioning for a caretaker government or a government takeover.
All the drama in Ottawa and Washington allowed the premiers to profess great unity, despite the undeniable diversity of provincial views about precisely how to handle the threat of 25 per cent tariffs from Trump. For all their amity at Monday's closing news conference before they boarded their flights home, the premiers could not undo their contradictory jumble of remarks in recent days about how to co-ordinate their responses.
When everyone is talking over each other it is even harder to triangulate about bilateral, interprovincial and federal-provincial relations.
Which is precisely what happened last week as Ford got ahead of his skis by vowing to cut off energy supplies to neighbouring Michigan and other U.S. customers if Trump's tariffs take effect. Ford's pronouncement was promptly repudiated by the premiers of Alberta, Quebec and Newfoundland, who restated their objections again Monday.
Unity of purpose doesn't go far when the premiers are working at cross-purposes.
Turns out that there's a reason we have a federal government representing the national interest. Unlike the so-called Council of the Federation, as the premiers like to call their ad hoc collective, which traditionally soars to aspirational rhetoric before predictably coming undone when the premiers go their separate ways.
It is tough enough coping with Trump trolling us on tariffs. It is taxing enough trying to cobble together a united front among 13 premiers going in different directions. It is especially trying to make it all come together when the federal government - and its scheduled fall economic statement - are left in limbo for half a day.
"It's chaos right now in Ottawa, and it's time that we ... step up and we'll make sure that we tell the world that there's stability here," Ford insisted. The premiers will "protect our provinces and our territories and our entire country from coast to coast to coast - that's our job, we're ready, and we'll rise up to the occasion."
Actually, that's not in the job description of premiers. That's what a national government does, with the premiers invited to offer their words of wisdom without having the last word - which is reserved for the prime minister of the day.
No one can deny that Monday was a chaotic day in the national capital. But every day is chaos in the U.S. capital, and no one looks to the 50 governors to speak on behalf of America.
"Collectively, we represent all Canadians," Saskatchewan's Scott Moe mused on Monday, a little drunk on power amid the momentary vacuum of power in Ottawa.
Asked whether the prime minister should surrender power, Ontario's premier didn't quite make a power grab but cheerfully claimed the spotlight:
"I have faith in the premiers, that's who I have faith in," Ford asserted, while proclaiming a special message for Canadians: "You have a strong group of premiers representing you ... Folks, you're in good hands with these premiers."
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Full text: Offer teachers more support
Toronto school boards are firing teachers who lie about sick days - and using private investigators to catch them, Dec. 14
I was an elementary teacher for many years in the Toronto District School Board and I suggest that if class sizes were reduced and teachers weren't given more and more administrative tasks, perhaps absenteeism would not be such a problem.
Obviously, I don't approve of taking a sick day to attend a casino, (as some teachers apparently did) but in my experience that is rare. Teaching in unventilated classrooms with 35 coughing, sneezing children is bound to make teachers sick.
Also, the stress of teaching with fewer and fewer supports for children with learning disabilities and ESL means mental health days are increasingly essential.
Money should be spent on supports for special education, teaching assistants and on hiring more teachers to reduce class sizes, not on investigators to spy on overworked, stressed-out teachers.
Marian Lydbrooke, Toronto
Absenteeism an issue. But why?
Almost lost in your report on teacher absenteeism is Toronto Catholic Teachers' president Deborah Karam's comment that no one is addressing why. Do teachers feel supported by principals, principals by supervisory officers, and any of these by trustees?" Even in families, fatigue and frustration result when the misbehaving child is always right.
Ab Dukacz, Mississauga
Hire teachers to prevent burnout
To our school boards. Instead of hiring private investigators, how about hiring more teachers? Preventing burnout will decrease the sick days used.
Kate Chung, Toronto
Ministry's self-created crisis
Blatant abuse of sick days is obviously wrong but that is not the real issue. This is largely a self-created crisis caused by short-sighted number-crunchers. Teachers, like those in many professions, used to bank their sick days over their careers. Now, they get 11 sick days a year - use them or lose them. So, staff use them, often for mental health and minor ills, where in the past they would have tried to make it to a break to recharge. This has created a monstrous increase in supply costs and there are simply not enough supply staff to meet the need.
The government's advertised savings never occurred because the use of days went from five to six per cent average to more than 16 per cent at some boards. The reward for not using the days used to be a gratuity upon retirement.
It would be far more cost effective over time to reward staff with a gratuity than to try to get them to not use their contracted sick days.
Dale Adams, Mississauga
Tariff threat will hurt Americans
Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11
Although I disagree with Premier Doug Ford on most things, I like his stance on fighting back against Donald Trump's tariff threats. Why suffer in silence? Trump doesn't realize he's hurting everyone, Americans and Canadians alike. Most of Canada's trade surplus with the U.S. comes from oil exports. Trump really needs to learn how to do the math. Even worse, many of Alberta's oil companies are American owned, so the surplus money goes back to Texas anyway. It's the same with Canadian lumber and minerals. In his fight against Canada's trade surplus, Trump wants to impose tariffs on Canadian goods that are mostly owned by American companies.
Max Moore, Toronto
What about our best interests?
Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11
The question that needs to be asked before any action on Donald Trump's tariffs can be formulated is: Why does it take a politician from a different country to make our government meet its responsibilities to Canadian citizens? Then we need to ask ourselves if our politicians are the people we want to deal with Trump's looming tariffs.
Raymond Clarke,
Lloydminster, Sask.
Gig economy an inhumane model
I went undercover as an Uber Eats courier and made just $1.74 per hour online. Here's what I learned about the troubling cost of convenience, Dec. 15
Ghada Alsharif has exposed an important, hidden cost of our gig economy. Can we workshop some names for this cost? I'd like to propose two possible titles: "The Bezos Effect" and "Amazonification." Why these? Because I remember pizza delivery long before the arrival of Jeff Bezos. It was a phone call, then a cash transaction, with accompanying tip. The advent of debit/credit cards didn't really change the model. There were long-distance book sellers, LP records and tapes, etc. What changed is the philosophy of convenience: Amazon taught us that we can have it now. Immediately. The people paying the cost of this convenience are those fulfilling the orders, working in "fulfilment centres," delivering for Uber or Skip or DoorDash. A dangerous, inhuman economic model.
Peter Giaschi, Penticton, B.C.
The irony of Jordan Peterson
'It's become uncomfortable for me': Jordan Peterson, the controversial psychologist, moves from Toronto to the U.S., Dec. 13
I find one of Jordan Peterson's main reasons for moving from Toronto to the United States to be full of irony; namely that "it's become uncomfortable for me" to live here. This is the man who was investigated and found to have made numerous transphobic, sexist and other comments by the regulatory body of his own profession and ordered to undergo remedial training. Peterson's own words have made it more than just uncomfortable for the many he degraded to live their own personal lives safely and openly.
Isabella Tatar, Toronto
Food banks could also use money
Toronto food banks are seeing skyrocketing use. Here's a guide to what they really need - and what you shouldn't donate, Dec. 15
Many good suggestions were given in this article. But, you forgot to mention probably the best donation possible: Money - cash, cheque, or credit card.
Raymond Rea, Toronto

Document 332 of 500

Freeland has lost confidence in Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/freeland-has-lost-confidence-trudeau/docview/3145482093/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Freeland+has+lost+confidence+in+Trudeau&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It wasn't Chrystia Freeland's announcement Monday that she was quitting as finance minister that marked the third and final act of Justin Trudeau's government.
It was how she quit. How she tried to define what siding with the prime minister says about Liberals - MPs and cabinet ministers alike - and in doing so paved the way for deeper internal divisions and the impending showdown waiting in the wings.
In a bombshell letter posted to social media Monday morning - the very day she was scheduled to table the federal government's fall economic update, and a federal byelection day in B.C. - Freeland wrote that the prime minister intended to shuffle her out of the finance portfolio and offered her another position. What she didn't say was that it was to become a minister without a department, charged with Canada-U.S. relations - and the same job, say sources who spoke on condition they not be named, that former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney had passed on.
Freeland wrote that the move meant Trudeau no longer had confidence in her. But what she really told Canadians was that she no longer had confidence in him.
Freeland suggested the prime minister is incompetent, that he is a bad fiscal manager, that he cares more about "costly political gimmicks" than preparing the country for the potential catastrophe that Donald Trump's return to the White House signals for Canada.
She suggested Trudeau had failed to adequately respond to the threat so far, that he wasn't working "in good faith and humility with the premiers ... and building a true Team Canada response." While much ink was spilled about the premiers going in their own direction following their call with the prime minister, some on the line felt Trudeau hadn't laid out a fully baked strategy for dealing with another Trump administration, and many were surprised to find their crucial exports to the U.S. - oil, potash, uranium - on the list of targeted products, without any prior consultation.
Freeland suggested that on the key matters facing the country, Trudeau was not only not leading, but that he is doing more harm than good.
By doing so, she threw down the gauntlet: anyone stepping into her old shoes would be endorsing an economic plan she had just demolished.
No wonder rumours flew Monday that Carney had not only rejected the finance portfolio - meaning Freeland was dumped and then expected to read a deficit-crashing, reputation-tarnishing fiscal update with no solid replacement locked-in - but that he would not figure in Trudeau's upcoming cabinet shuffle at all.
It wasn't the only piece of hot gossip. Rumours spread that Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne had refused to read the economic update in the House and was considering quitting; so were Treasury Board President and Transport Minister Anita Anand, and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly (none of whom returned messages before deadline Monday night requesting comment). If Freeland was launching her leadership bid from the backbench, perhaps other ministers were tempted to follow. Several ministers were asked Monday whether they still had confidence in the prime minister; an uncomfortable number declined to comment.
Meanwhile, caucus was abuzz about Freeland joining the dissenters rank. "They try to minimize and dismiss me," New Brunswick MP Wayne Long said of the Prime Minister's Office. "But you can't dismiss her. Not a chance."
More than half a dozen Liberal MPs called publicly on Trudeau to resign Monday. Some suggested the number of MPs now willing to sign a letter calling on the prime minister to step down had swelled to 45.
"I think the only path forward for us is to choose a new leader and to present a new plan to Canadians with a different vision," Hamilton MP Chad Collins told reporters.
It didn't help that earlier in the day, Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced he was stepping aside, and tongues wagged that Toronto MP and former Ontario Liberal leadership candidate Nathaniel Erskine-Smith was in line for the job, after telling the PMO he would run again as an MP if he was given Fraser's portfolio. Many loyal MPs patiently waiting for their turn in cabinet were dismayed by the news, as were those who appeared to have been passed over for promotions, waiting by the phone all weekend only to never hear it ring.
When Freeland arrived at a hastily called caucus meeting Monday evening, she was greeted with a standing ovation. Her fellow MPs hugged her. She was in the room when Trudeau tried to explain what happened over the weekend, and how much he still respected her.
But the mood in the room was tense. "People are really pissed off," said one Liberal MP, speaking confidentially. They wanted to know why Freeland had been thrown under the bus. The prime minister tried to distance himself from a mess much like the messes he seems to frequently make, as witnessed by his relationship breakdowns with cabinet ministers Bill Morneau, Jody Wilson-Raybould and David Lametti, just to name a few.
Some MPs suggested the only way out was prorogation. Others suggested it was only a matter of time before Trudeau realizes he can't hang on and triggers a leadership race.
Complicating matters further, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh called on the prime minister to step aside.
If Trudeau didn't have the confidence of his former finance minister, a significant chunk of his caucus and now the leader of the only party willing to prop him up in the House of Commons, how much longer could his tenure last?
That's the conversation Freeland reignited Monday with her decision to step aside in the most publicly damaging way possible.
CAPTION: In a letter posted online, Liberal MP Chrystia Freeland suggested that on the key matters facing the country, the prime minister was doing more harm than good, Althia Raj writes.Spencer ColbyThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 333 of 500

Provincial leaders pledge stability: Council of the Federation members focus on U.S. trade threat amid political chaos in Ottawa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-leaders-pledge-stability/docview/3145482045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+leaders+pledge+stability%3A+Council+of+the+Federation+members+focus+on+U.S.+trade+threat+amid+political+chaos+in+Ottawa&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders are expressing alarm at the political chaos in Ottawa as they gird for U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canadian products.
The premiers, meeting in the GTA Monday, said the stunning resignation of Chrystia Freeland from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's cabinet could not have come at a worst time - and they vowed to provide stability for the nation.
"It's chaos right now up in Ottawa," Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, said after a conference at the Hilton Hotel near Pearson International Airport.
Ford, a close personal friend and political ally of Freeland, put her on speakerphone so she could address the other premiers when she called him during their meeting.
"We have a good relationship with Chrystia over the years. I just want to wish her all the best," he said, emphasizing the uncertainty is not helpful.
"As a country, we have to project strength and unity."
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew praised Freeland for being "very positive and collaborative" and said the provinces and territories are focused on dealing with Trump.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith pointed out that she and her colleagues "have a secure mandate" from voters.
"This is a stable table and I think it's up to us to continue offering that continuity if there is that uncertainty happening at the federal level," said Smith.
Dominic LeBlanc was sworn in as finance minister in a hasty ceremony at Rideau Hall later Monday afternoon.
Amid the disarray in federal politics, the premiers will travel to Washington in February on a Team Canada mission to promote trade to senators, congresspeople, governors and other American officials.
Behind closed doors, the premiers heard for two hours from former Canadian ambassadors to Washington, David McNaughton, Gary Doer and Frank McKenna, who shared strategies for coping with Trump.
"That was ... a very engaging discussion and some very astute advice provided to each of us here," said Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe.
The premiers were so impressed with the presentation that their federation council may retain one of the former ambassadors as an emissary in Washington in the coming months.
The premiers' in-person confab was held after two recent virtual first ministers' meetings with Trudeau in the wake of Trump's plan to impose levies on Canadian and Mexican goods unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Ford made headlines stateside last Wednesday with his threat to "cut off" Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. in retaliation for any tariffs.
"Americans are going to feel the pain as well and isn't that unfortunate," he warned.
The premier's gambit forced Trump to respond the next day.
"That's OK if he that does that. That's fine," the president-elect said Thursday, complaining the U.S. "is subsidizing Canada" by spending so much on defence.
"We shouldn't have to do that. And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than $100 billion a year," maintained Trump.
Ford was encouraged that the incoming president took notice, and Freeland and LeBlanc appeared to endorse his hawkish stance even though some other premiers from energy-exporting provinces disagree.
"We have to be candid about the reality of the incoming U.S. administration. This is an administration that believes in America first. This is an administration which is proudly an economic nationalist administration," Freeland said Friday in her last public event as finance minister.
At a separate event that day, LeBlanc said pointedly, "I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself," he said, conceding "other premiers have offered views on what might be the appropriate response."
Indeed, Alberta's Smith, whose province supplies 60 per cent of oil used in the U.S., and Quebec Premier François Legault, whose province is a major exporter of hydro electricity, have rejected Ford's musings about curbing energy exports.
Ontario sells electricity to Michigan, New York and Minnesota, powering the homes of 1.5 million Americans.
Ford said the premiers will continue to pressure Ottawa to boost Canada's military spending, a push being led by Manitoba's Kinew.
"The reason why we have that unity on these priorities is because if we as Canadians come together to invest in the Canadian Armed Forces, to protect our border, to rid the scourge of toxic drugs in our communities, what's the downside we take action on those things?" said the Manitoban.
"We're unified in investing in the military, we're unified in cracking down on drug trafficking, we're unified on having a secure border," added Kinew.
So far, Canada is falling short of NATO's demand that its 32-member nations spend at least two per cent of gross domestic product on defence.
NATO estimates the Trudeau government will spend 1.37 per cent of GDP on the military this year.
The prime minister has previously pledged to meet the two per cent target by 2032, including increased spending on defence infrastructure totalling $8.1 billion over the next five years.

School boards should spend 'spying' money on support staff for teachers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/school-boards-should-spend-spying-money-on/docview/3145481998/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+boards+should+spend+%27spying%27+money+on+support+staff+for+teachers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto school boards are firing teachers who lie about sick days - and using private investigators to catch them, Dec. 14
I was an elementary teacher for many years in the Toronto District School Board and I suggest that if class sizes were reduced and teachers weren't given more and more administrative tasks, perhaps absenteeism would not be such a problem. Obviously, I don't approve of taking a sick day to attend a casino, (as some teachers apparently did) but in my experience that is rare. Teaching in unventilated classrooms with 35 coughing, sneezing children is bound to make teachers sick. Also, the stress of teaching with fewer and fewer supports for children with learning disabilities and ESL means mental health days are increasingly essential. Money should be spent on supports for special education, teaching assistants and on hiring more teachers to reduce class sizes, not on investigators to spy on overworked, stressed-out teachers.
Marian Lydbrooke, Toronto
No one is addressing why teachers are absent
Almost lost in your report on teacher absenteeism is Toronto Catholic Teachers' president Deborah Karam's comment that no one is addressing why. Do teachers feel supported by principals, principals by supervisory officers, and any of these by trustees?" Even in families, fatigue and frustration result when the misbehaving child is always right.
Ab Dukacz, Mississauga
Want to prevent burnout? Hire more teachers
To our school boards. Instead of hiring private investigators, how about hiring more teachers? Preventing burnout will decrease the sick days used.
Kate Chung, Toronto
The Ministry of Education created this - use a carrot not a stick!
Blatant abuse of sick days is obviously wrong but that is not the real issue. This is largely a self-created crisis caused by short-sighted number crunchers. Teachers, like those in many professions, used to bank their sick days over their careers. Now, they get 11 sick days a year - use them or lose them. So, staff use them, often for mental health and minor ills, where in the past they would have tried to make it to a break to recharge. This has created a monstrous increase in supply costs and there are simply not enough supply staff to meet the need. The government's advertised savings never occurred because the use of days went from five to six per cent average to more than 16 per cent at some boards. The reward for not using the days used to be a gratuity upon retirement. It would be far more cost effective over time to reward staff with a gratuity than to try to get them to not use their contracted sick days.
Dale Adams, Mississauga
Trumps stand on tariffs will hurt Americans
Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11
Although I disagree with Premier Doug Ford on most things, I like his stance on fighting back against Donald Trump's tariff threats. Why suffer in silence? Trump doesn't realize he's hurting everyone, Americans and Canadians alike. Most of Canada's trade surplus with the U.S. comes from oil exports . Trump really needs to learn how to do the math. Even worse, many of Alberta's oil companies are American owned, so the surplus money goes back to Texas anyway. It's the same with Canadian lumber and minerals. In his fight against Canada's trade surplus, Trump wants to impose tariffs on Canadian goods that are mostly owned by American companies.
Max Moore, Toronto
Why can't our government look after our best interests?
Insider says Justin Trudeau has a $1B border plan designed to counter Trump's tariff threat, Dec. 11
The question that needs to be asked before any action on Donald Trump's tariffs can be formulated is; Why does it take a politician from a different country to make our government meet its responsibilities to Canadian citizens? Then we need to ask ourselves if our politicians are the people we want to deal with Trump's looming tariffs.
Raymond Clarke, Lloydminster, SK
The gig economy is an inhuman economic model
I went undercover as an Uber Eats courier and made just $1.74 per hour online. Here's what I learned about the troubling cost of convenience, Dec. 15
Ghada Alsharif has exposed an important, hidden cost of our gig economy. Can we workshop some names for this cost? I'd like to propose two possible titles: "The Bezos Effect" and "Amazonification." Why these? Because I remember pizza delivery long before the arrival of Jeff Bezos. It was a phone call, then a cash transaction, with accompanying tip. The advent of debit/credit cards didn't really change the model. There were long-distance book sellers, LP records and tapes, etc. What changed is the philosophy of convenience: Amazon taught us that we can have it now. Immediately. The people paying the cost of this convenience are those fulfilling the orders, working in "fulfilment centres," delivering for Uber or Skip or DoorDash. A dangerous, inhuman economic model.
Peter Giaschi, Penticton
The irony of Jordan Peterson'It's become uncomfortable for me': Jordan Peterson, the controversial psychologist, moves from Toronto to the U.S., Dec. 13
I find one of Jordan Peterson's main reasons for moving from Toronto to the United States to be full of irony; namely that "it's become uncomfortable for me" to live here. This is the man who was investigated and found to have made numerous transphobic, sexist and other comments by the regulatory body of his own profession and ordered to undergo remedial training. Peterson's own words have made it more than just uncomfortable for the many he degraded to live their own personal lives safely and openly.
Isabella Tatar, Toronto
Or you could give moneyToronto food banks are seeing skyrocketing use. Here's a guide to what they really need - and what you shouldn't donate, Dec. 15
Many good suggestions were given in this article. But, you forgot to mention probably the best donation possible. Give money - cash, cheque, or credit card.
Raymond Rea, Toronto
CAPTION: President of Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers Deborah Karam in her office, on Dec. 13, 2024.

Document 335 of 500

Chaos in Ottawa has Doug Ford reaching for his Captain Canada cape

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chaos-ottawa-has-doug-ford-reaching-his-captain/docview/3145853903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chaos+in+Ottawa+has+Doug+Ford+reaching+for+his+Captain+Canada+cape&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fear not, fellow Ontarians and Canadians.
In this hour of political chaos and economic crisis, Doug Ford has a message for all of you - across the province and the country:
The prime minister may be incapacitated for the moment. But the premiers are empowered at full capacity for our future confrontations with the United States.
With the federal government seemingly sidelined, provincial governments are moving into the bilateral breach. Chaos theory is our new Canadian reality, but stability is today's provincial priority as upheaval looms in America.
"No matter what's happening in Ottawa, all of us premiers will remain united as Team Canada to provide steady and stable leadership during these uncertain times in Ottawa," proclaimed Ford, flanked by his fellow premiers at an airport hotel Monday.
At which point he helpfully handed the microphone to his Quebec counterpart, François Legault, to "translate that into French." It was an unmistakable - and unprecedented - message and missive from Ford as he makes the most of his rotating chairmanship of the premiers' council to reincarnate himself as Captain Canada.
Summoned to a summit in Toronto to deal with Donald Trump's vortex of power in Washington, the premiers pivoted to Justin Trudeau's vacuum of power in Ottawa. While focused on the perils of tariffs from the incoming U.S. president, they were blindsided by the outgoing Canadian finance minister, Chrystia Freeland, quitting cabinet.
On the very day they were trying to fathom Trump's next move, they were transfixed by Trudeau's latest moves in removing the minister who had long been their point person in Ottawa. When Freeland called Ford to bid him farewell Monday, he pointedly put her on speakerphone so that this fallen federal politician could have the final word with the premiers mid-meeting.
And so what should have been a day dedicated to preparing for the president-elect's scorched earth tariff strategy turned into a day of earnest, if not pretentious promises to steady the ship of state in turbulent waters with the prime minister missing in action. Listening to their orations, it was sometimes hard to tell if the premiers were auditioning for a caretaker government or a government takeover.
All the drama in Ottawa and Washington allowed the premiers to profess great unity, despite the undeniable diversity of provincial views about precisely how to handle the threat of 25-per-cent tariffs from Trump. For all their amity at Monday's closing news conference before they boarded their flights home, the premiers could not undo their contradictory jumble of remarks in recent days about how to co-ordinate their responses.
When everyone is talking over each other it is even harder to triangulate about bilateral, interprovincial and federal-provincial relations.
Which is precisely what happened last week as Ford got ahead of his skis by vowing to cut off energy supplies to neighbouring Michigan and other U.S. customers if Trump's tariffs take effect. Ford's pronouncement was promptly repudiated by the premiers of Alberta, Quebec and Newfoundland, who restated their objections again Monday.
Unity of purpose doesn't go far when the premiers are working at cross-purposes.
Turns out that there's a reason we have a federal government representing the national interest. Unlike the so-called Council of the Federation, as the premiers like to call their ad hoc collective, which traditionally soars to aspirational rhetoric before predictably coming undone when the premiers go their separate ways.
It is tough enough coping with Trump trolling us on tariffs. It is taxing enough trying to cobble together a united front among 13 premiers going in different directions. It is especially trying to make it all come together when the federal government - and its scheduled fall economic statement - are left in limbo for half a day.
"It's chaos right now in Ottawa, and it's time that we ... step up and we'll make sure that we tell the world that there's stability here," Ford insisted. The premiers will "protect our provinces and our territories and our entire country from coast to coast to coast - that's our job, we're ready, and we'll rise up to the occasion."
Actually, that's not in the job description of premiers. That's what a national government does, with the premiers invited to offer their words of wisdom without having the last word - which is reserved for the prime minister of the day.
No one can deny that Monday was a chaotic day in the national capital. But every day is chaos in the U.S. capital, and no one looks to the 50 governors to speak on behalf of America.
"Collectively, we represent all Canadians," Saskatchewan's Scott Moe mused on Monday, a little drunk on power amid the momentary vacuum of power in Ottawa.
Asked whether the prime minister should surrender power, Ontario's premier didn't quite make a power grab but cheerfully claimed the spotlight:
"I have faith in the premiers, that's who I have faith in," Ford asserted, while proclaiming a special message for Canadians: "You have a strong group of premiers representing you ... Folks, you're in good hands with these premiers."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media during a meetings of Canada's premiers hosted by Ontario on Dec. 16, 2024.

Document 337 of 500

Chrystia Freeland is out of cabinet for the first time in the Trudeau-era. A brief history of her rise in Canadian politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-is-out-cabinet-first-time/docview/3145189053/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland+is+out+of+cabinet+for+the+first+time+in+the+Trudeau-era.+A+brief+history+of+her+rise+in+Canadian+politics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the first time during Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's time in office, he won't have Chrystia Freeland in his cabinet.
Trudeau's deputy prime minister and minister of finance resigned on Monday morning, revealing that Trudeau had offered her another cabinet position, which she declined.
"For the past number of weeks, you and I have found ourselves at odds about the best path forward for Canada," she said.
That early-morning bombshell has thrown the government into disarray, as it was set to release its fiscal update later on Monday afternoon.
Freeland, who has long been Trudeau's right hand, has spent more than a decade as a Liberal MP, the majority of that spent as a key cabinet minister overlooking everything from international trade agreements to the government's COVID-19 response.
Before entering politics, Freeland attended Harvard University, during which she went on exchange at the University of Kyiv. She went on to Oxford University in the U.K. as a Rhodes scholar after graduating from Harvard. A year later, Freeland worked for the Financial Times as its Moscow bureau chief before moving back to Canada to work as the deputy editor for the Globe and Mail. In 2010, Freeland joined Thomson Reuters, and later became the managing director and editor of consumer news. In 2012, during a signing session of her second book, Freeland met Trudeau, who was a Liberal leader hopeful at the time, and convinced her to run for office in a byelection for Bob Rae's vacant seat.
2015-2017: Minister of International Trade
Freeland joined Trudeau's very first cabinet in 2015 as the minister of international trade. She helped close the Canada-European Union Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement after a nearly decade-long negotiation that began under former Prime Minister Stephen Harper.
She also led Canada through the negotiations of the Trans-Pacific Partnership, which she signed Canada onto in February 2016. The multinational Pacific Rim trade agreement was tanked when then-U.S. president Donald Trump, in his first year in office, withdrew the U.S. from the agreement.
2017-2019: Minister of Foreign Affairs
In a cabinet shuffle at the beginning of the year in 2017, Freeland was appointed minister of foreign affairs, replacing Stéphane Dion.
Right out the gate, Freeland backed the creation of a Canadian Magnitsky Act, meant to expand international sanctions law to target individuals behind gross human rights violations.
The act, inspired by a equivalent law in the U.S., was named after Russian whistleblower Sergei Magnitsky, who died in a Moscow prison in 2009 after accusing officials of a $230-million tax fraud.
Freeland then oversaw Canada's side of the renegotiation of the North American Free Trade deal with the U.S. and Mexico after Trump insisted on a new deal after taking office. After 13 months of negotiations, another year of revisions and several threats from Trump to tear up a North American free trade deal entirely, the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) was born.
It was during USMCA negotiations when the first tariff war between Canada and the U.S. took place, after Trump slapped 25 per cent steel tariffs on Canada and Freeland, on Canada's behalf, responded "dollar-for-dollar" in kind.
Freeland spent much of her time as foreign affairs minister on human rights causes. In 2017, she called out Myanmar's oppression of Rohingya Muslims as an "ethnic cleansing." She also condemned violence in Hong Kong against pro-democracy protesters, for which the Chinese officials accused the minister of "meddling" in China's domestic affairs.
She also oversaw Canadian arms exports to Saudi Arabia during the war in Yemen. A 2018 Star investigation found that Canada had exported $258 million worth of weapons and military goods to the countries bombing Yemen, while only supplying $65 million in humanitarian aid.
2019-2024: Deputy Prime Minister & Minister of Finance
At Trudeau's November 2019 cabinet unveiling, Freeland became the first deputy prime minister in 13 years, while also being appointed minister of intergovernmental affairs.
When she was appointed, Trudeau said he would lean on Freeland to navigate the "large issues" like energy and the environment that require the Liberal government "to engage in a strong and positive way with different orders of government right across the country." In front of all his other newly-sworn ministers, he heaped praise on Freeland as someone he worked well with during the NAFTA renegotiation.
As the COVID-19 pandemic ground the country to a halt, Freeland was part of Trudeau's special cabinet committee that focused on the coronavirus outbreak.
Her role quickly shifted, and just months into the pandemic when Bill Morneau resigned as finance minister in August 2020 as a result of the WE Charity scandal, Trudeau appointed Freeland as the finance minister, the first woman to ever hold the position. Trudeau-confidant Dominic LeBlanc took over her duties as intergovernmental affairs minister.
Just months later, in November, Freeland unveiled a $25.1 billion aid package to boost the Canadian economy during the pandemic and oversaw the rollout of billions in major pandemic supports, like the Canada Emergency Response Benefit and the Canada Emergency Wage Subsidy programs.
It was during this time that Freeland developed a friendship with Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
"He and I have actually come to describe one another as each other's therapists," Freeland said in an interview in 2020. "Some of the daily struggles that we go through are quite similar and so sometimes when we talk we'll just say, 'This is our therapy session,'" Freeland told the Star's Susan Delacourt.
Ford, in turn, laid praise on Freeland, "Chrystia is just tough as nails. The iron lady. I'm telling you, just a wonderful person. Has a big heart but she's no nonsense as well," he said.
During the "Freedom Convoy" protests in Ottawa, as Trudeau invoked the use of the Emergencies Act to end the protest, Freeland gave banks the authority to freeze nearly 280 bank accounts of convoy participants, totalling $8 million. In January, a federal court found the use of the Emergencies Act violated Charter rights. The federal government said it would appeal the decision.
Freeland was also at the forefront of the federal government's response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, after her years of academic and media experience in Kyiv and Moscow.
In her resignation letter, Freeland said she would stay on as a Liberal MP and run for re-election.
CAPTION: Chrystia Freeland at Toronto Reference Library in Toronto after she won the Liberal nomination Sept. 15, 2013.

Document 338 of 500

Chrystia Freeland's resignation triggers chaos and Liberal calls for Justin Trudeau to step down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freelands-resignation-triggers-chaos/docview/3145189047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland%27s+resignation+triggers+chaos+and+Liberal+calls+for+Justin+Trudeau+to+step+down&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The sudden resignation of Justin Trudeau's finance minister threw his leadership into doubt and his minority government into chaos Monday after Chrystia Freeland quit just hours before she was set to deliver a key fiscal update in the House of Commons.
Instead, Freeland's stunning resignation, her revelations that Trudeau wanted to yank her from the finance portfolio, that she and Trudeau had argued for weeks over "political gimmicks" in the fall economic update, and clashed over how best to ready Canada for the Trump administration's threatened tariff war set the stage for a day of intense political drama on Parliament Hill.
It stunned her cabinet and caucus colleagues and caused chaos among the Liberal ranks, with some like Liberal MP Chad Collins publicly urging Trudeau to resign for the first time.
"It's obvious that Canadians across the country feel the same and want him to leave," he said.
Freeland dropped the announcement on "X" - formerly Twitter - via a missive just as Housing Minister Sean Fraser announced he was quitting for "family reasons," he said. It took him, too, by surprise.
Finance officials had almost no notice of their minister's plans which immediately threw the launch of the fall fiscal economic statement into disarray.
The prime minister huddled with his top officials and cabinet ministers all morning. By midday, it was not clear whether the prime minister would address Canadians, or would have some other minister step in to deliver the speech about the fall statement after markets close at 4 p.m. In the end, House Leader Karina Gould presented the document in the Commons, as Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc was being sworn in as the new finance minister at Rideau Hall.
Immediately upon Freeland's news, the Toronto stock exchange and the Canadian dollar dipped and hadn't recovered by end of day before the fiscal update landed.
When the document was finally tabled, it revealed a whopping $61.9 billion deficit for last year and an unfunded promise to deliver $250 cheques to Canadians.
Freeland and Fraser's departures from Justin Trudeau's cabinet are a blow to the Liberal government and sowed more confusion in an unstable minority Parliament and reignited questions about the prime minister's political future.
Several of his most loyal ministers left cabinet without taking any questions. An emergency Liberal caucus meeting was to be held at 5 p.m.
Freeland's statement said Trudeau told her Friday he no longer wants her to serve as finance minister and offered her another unspecified position in cabinet, signalling she no longer had his confidence and leading to her resignation. Freeland, who was in Ottawa on Monday, said she intends to remain sitting as an MP and to contest her seat in the next election.
The prospect of a snap election - which the Conservatives called for - could not be ruled out, given the explosive reaction to Freeland's exit. The NDP, which has propped up the Trudeau minority government, did not pronounce on what comes next, only issuing a statement that accused the Liberals of being obsessed with infighting.
"For the past number of weeks, you and I have found ourselves at odds about the best path forward for Canada," Freeland said, adding that Canadians "know when we are working for them," and "when we are focused on ourselves."
Citing the looming threat of U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's promised tariffs, she said Canada faces a "grave challenge" from his policy of "economic nationalism" that requires fiscal discipline instead of "political gimmicks."
"That means keeping our fiscal powder dry today, so we have the reserves we may need for a coming tariff war. That means eschewing costly political gimmicks we can ill afford and which make Canadians doubt that we recognize the gravity of the moment."
The Trudeau government's fall fiscal update - which was expected to unveil more than $1 billion in border security measures - comes amid heavy scrutiny over the federal government's spending and its inability to stick to its own fiscal "guardrails" - the self-imposed spending guidelines Freeland had once set.
The Trudeau government has been scrambling for three weeks to allay fears of tariff threats from the incoming Trump administration, and has promised to spend more on helicopters, drones, surveillance technology and manpower at the border. Freeland was offered a ministerial role to deal with Canada-U.S. relations, but the position would have given her none of the tools she had when she renegotiated NAFTA with the previous Trump administration, according to a senior Liberal source.
Ministers emerged grim-faced from the cabinet meeting with most offering no comment. Some gamely asserted they continue to have confidence in the government and said Canadians should too.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne told reporters "it is a serious moment" for the government.
Asked if he still has confidence in the prime minister, Champagne said "the moment now is for us to focus on the best interest of Canadians, that's what we're doing."
Only a few others offered kind words about Freeland, with Fraser, who said he intends to step down on Monday due to family reasons, and Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon describing her as a respected friend.
Before the meeting, just minutes after Freeland's letter was put out, Treasury Board President and Transport Minister Anita Anand appeared on the verge of tears when asked to comment.
"I'll just say that Chrystia Freeland is a good friend, someone I work with very closely," Anand said. "This news has hit me really hard, and I'll reserve further comment until I have time to process it."
But among the Liberal ranks, calls for Trudeau to resign resurfaced.
Wayne Long, one of the MPs who has called on Trudeau to resign before, told the Star he hopes the resignation "opens the door to some cabinet ministers speaking up."
"How much more can the PM endure before doing the right thing and stepping down?" he said.
Hamilton MP Chad Collins, who publicly called for the government's cheque plan to be expanded this fall, also revealed for the first time on Monday that he was among 24 MPs who signed a letter to the prime minister calling on him to step down.
Several Liberal MPs, speaking to the Star on the condition they not be named, expressed surprise and disappointment at the news, with one characterizing Freeland's departure as a "devastating blow" for Trudeau, who earlier this fall survived a challenge from dozens of MPs who called for his resignation.
That MP said conversations will be renewed within caucus about the prime minister's future, and that there will be "reflecting" during the holiday period over next steps. The MP added that they have become increasingly "disillusioned" with running again under the Liberal banner, a feeling they said was prompted by the government's GST holiday and $250 rebate proposal for working Canadians, a multibillion dollar affordability plan that threatens Ottawa's fiscal guardrails promised by Freeland.
"We're going to spend between the two (measures) in excess of $6 billion to what, save Justin Trudeau's career?" The MP said, noting that Freeland's reference to "political gimmicks" in her resignation letter was particularly damning.
Two other MPs who have not previously called on Trudeau to resign, said now is the time for him to go. One told the Star the best course for the Liberals now is for Trudeau to step down and then table a new throne speech under a new prime minister for a fresh start ahead of the next federal election. The other said it was understandable that Freeland would walk away, in light of how the Liberals are struggling and that their predicament is mainly because of Trudeau.
The Star reported this summer that some Liberal MPs wanted Freeland out of cabinet. Freeland has long been rumoured to be a potential successor to Trudeau.
Meanwhile, Trudeau's political opponents in Parliament pounced on the shock resignation, with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre saying it is evidence that the "government of Canada itself is spiralling out of control."
He said it is now up to NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh to decide whether or not the government falls - and when. While Singh exited his parliamentary accord with the minority Liberals in September, his party has yet to declare a loss of confidence in the Trudeau government.
Singh, for the first time, demanded Trudeau resign on Monday, but refused to say if he was prepared to help bring down the government.
"All tools, all options are on the table. People are hurting, people are struggling, and so all options are on the table," the NDP leader said.
In Montreal, Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet declared the days of the Trudeau government were "over" and called for the prime minister to ask the Governor General to dissolve Parliament by the start of the new year.
The chaos extended into question period in the House of Commons, where the Conservatives taunted the Liberals and Poilievre repeatedly asked "who is the Finance Minister?"
In Toronto, asked if the premiers were concerned about Freeland's stunning resignation Monday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford said: "We all are."
Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, emphasized "this is a time for the premiers to step up, which we're going to, and project unity across the country."
Fraser, in his own resignation press conference, said he only learned of Freeland stepping down following a reporter's question.
"This is news for me," he said in French.
The Nova Scotia MP, who held the immigration file before moving to housing, said he was not seeking re-election due to family reasons, but described his time in government as "the best job I can imagine having." He said he made his decision earlier this fall following a surgery.
"Our path to creating a family was a challenging one. We've experienced the highest of highs and the lowest of lows when it comes to our kids. We have had micro-preemies, lengthy hospitalizations, medical interventions, and know the pain of losing our one-day-old daughter, Ruth, who I miss every day and who I love very much," he said.
Freeland and Fraser's resignations come as Trudeau prepares to shake up his cabinet after several ministers announced they won't be seeking re-election this fall. Their resignations make it nine ministers in Trudeau's cabinet who have stepped down or announced their intention to step down in recent months.
The Trudeau government is also facing a byelection in B.C. Monday night where polls suggest they will lose the seat they previously held.
With files from Robert Benzie.
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland speaks with reporters in Ottawa on Tuesday.

Document 339 of 500

Call it the Ford Effect: How lashing out at the city can spark an unintended reaction - and make for a better Toronto

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/call-ford-effect-how-lashing-out-at-city-can/docview/3145189032/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Call+it+the+Ford+Effect%3A+How+lashing+out+at+the+city+can+spark+an+unintended+reaction+-+and+make+for+a+better+Toronto&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's war on bike lanes is having some unintended consequences - a phenomenon I have seen before and mentally dubbed "the Ford Effect."
My definition of the Ford Effect: To strike forcefully to achieve a political aim and, in doing so, cause unintended emotions or enlightenment about something the public previously knew little about.
I first noticed it at city hall during the 2010-14 term, when Ford was a city councillor and chief adviser to his late brother Rob Ford, the populist mayor who rode to power vowing to shake up Toronto politics and end the "gravy train" of wasteful spending.
They wanted to slash the city budget by closing libraries and Riverdale Farm and reduce funding for transit, heritage, student nutrition, arts and school crossing guards. Rob Ford's executive committee held a meeting overnight in 2011 to move as quickly as possible through responses from the many outraged Torontonians who told the Fords they valued the threatened services.
"You have galvanized a giant machine," determined to protect city services, one resident told them. Fourteen-year-old Scarborough student Anika Tabovaradan, who had never before made a political presentation and said she hated public speaking, bawled as she spoke at 2 a.m. about her love for her local library branch, my then-colleague Daniel Dale reported from the meeting.
Torontonians, many of whom rarely if ever think of city hall, gave the Fords and their councillors an earful. Instead of cutting the budget, council cut the Fords' consultant-suggested cuts.
The same year, Doug Ford led a bid to wrest control of Toronto's east waterfront from city-provincial-federal agency Waterfront Toronto, triggering a groundswell of interest in, and defence of, the agency's existing Port Lands redevelopment plan that was on almost nobody's radar.
The Etobicoke councillor's secret solicitation of an Australian developer to remake the plan with a Ferris wheel, megamall and glitzy boat-in hotel, raised the profile of Waterfront Toronto's plan to remake the mouth of the Don River into a lush valley with new parks and homes. Council voted to accelerate, not kill, that vision, which is expected to fully open to the public next year.
Now to bike lanes. Doug Ford is infinitely more powerful as premier than he ever was at city hall. There appears to be little to stop his majority PC government from removing cycleways on Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street.
A cycling advocacy group has launched a court challenge to try to save the lanes. Even if that is successful, Ford could overrule a court decision with the notwithstanding clause, an opt-out of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms that he seems eager to deploy.
But the Ford Effect is exposing a host of factors contributing to traffic gridlock that have nothing to do with cyclists.
They include the closure of vehicle traffic lanes for the PC government's Ontario Line transit construction, as well as for condo construction projects, city maintenance work and utility repairs, as I recently reported with my colleague, Kate Allen.
In a recent edition of the City Hall Watcher newsletter, engineer Damien Houle argued persuasively that traffic signals dramatically reduce road capacity and slow traffic, a result that some people welcome given the six Toronto cyclists who have died so far this year after being hit by drivers. Those tragedies, and resulting white-painted "ghost bike" memorials, are cited in arguments to save the bike lanes.
I can vouch for the traffic signal issue. Driving the Bloor Street West bike lane extension that apparently sparked Ford's ire, I encountered six traffic lights over 1.7 kilometres between The Kingsway and Aberfoyle Crescent, including a signal near Grenview Boulevard that doesn't actually connect any streets. They enhance the Kingsway's pedestrian-friendly village-y feel, but drivers pay the price.
Ford and his transportation minister aimed a spotlight at the question of how many people actually commute by bike by citing a misleading figure of just over one per cent. That triggered reporting that revealed an unreleased provincial report citing higher figures.
While enlightening the public on issues around bike lanes and gridlock, and spurring more informed debate, the Ford Effect has not, to date, triggered the kind of public backlash seen with the proposed cuts to the 2011 city budget or the vision of a megamall on the expensively reclaimed waterfront. Ford's renewed lakefront focus, with a controversial Ontario Place redevelopment, also appears to be moving toward fruition.
Still, when we eventually see the results of Ford's characteristically hard-charging way of attacking issues, Torontonians will at least have a clearer picture of the facts, including whether removing bike lanes speed up traffic at all.
Unlike Toronto traffic, the Ford Effect is always in motion.
CAPTION: When Doug Ford and his brother, Rob, wanted to slash the city budget by closing libraries and Riverdale Farm, outraged Torontonians gave them and city councillors an earful, leading to a climbdown on drastic cuts.

Document 340 of 500

Premiers express alarm at political 'chaos' in Ottawa amid looming Trump trade threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premiers-express-alarm-at-political-chaos-ottawa/docview/3144879538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers+express+alarm+at+political+%27chaos%27+in+Ottawa+amid+looming+Trump+trade+threat&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders are expressing alarm at the political chaos in Ottawa as they gird for U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threatened 25-per-cent tariffs against Canadian products.
The premiers, meeting in the GTA Monday, said the stunning resignation of Chrystia Freeland from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's cabinet could not have come at a worst time - and they vowed to provide stability for the nation.
"It's chaos right now up in Ottawa," Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, said after a conference at the Hilton Hotel near Pearson International Airport.
Ford, a close personal friend and political ally of Freeland, put her on speakerphone so she could address the other premiers when she called him during their meeting.
"We have a good relationship with Chrystia over the years. I just want to wish her all the best," he said, emphasizing the uncertainty is not helpful.
"As a country, we have to project strength and unity."
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew praised Freeland for being "very positive and collaborative" and said the provinces and territories are focused on dealing with Trump.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith pointed out that she and her colleagues "have a secure mandate" from voters.
"This is a stable table and I think it's up to us to continue offering that continuity if there is that uncertainty happening at the federal level," said Smith.
Amid the disarray in federal politics, the premiers will travel to Washington in February on a Team Canada mission to promote trade to senators, congresspeople, governors and other American officials.
Behind closed doors, the premiers heard for two hours from former Canadian ambassadors to Washington David McNaughton, Gary Doer and Frank McKenna, who shared strategies for coping with Trump.
"That was ... a very engaging discussion and some very astute advice provided to each of us here," said Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe.
The premiers were so impressed with the presentation that their federation council may retain one of the former ambassadors as an emissary in Washington in the coming months.
Freeland's departure as finance minister and deputy prime minister came hours before she was supposed to deliver a fall economic statement that outlined $1.3 billion in new spending on border security measures, including increased personnel and new equipment such as helicopters and drones.
House Leader Karina Gould ended up having to deliver the statement in the Commons.
Less than an hour after that, Dominic LeBlanc was sworn in as finance minister in a hasty ceremony at Rideau Hall. LeBlanc also remains intergovernmental affairs and public safety minister.
The premiers' in-person confab was held after two recent virtual first ministers' meetings with Trudeau in the wake of Trump's plan to impose levies on Canadian and Mexican goods unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Ford made headlines stateside last Wednesday with his threat to "cut off" Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. in retaliation for any tariffs.
"Americans are going to feel the pain as well and isn't that unfortunate," he warned.
The premier's gambit forced Trump to respond the next day.
"That's OK if he that does that. That's fine," the president-elect said Thursday, complaining the U.S. "is subsidizing Canada" by spending so much on defence.
"We shouldn't have to do that. And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than $100 billion a year," maintained Trump.
Ford was encouraged that the incoming president took notice, and Freeland and LeBlanc appeared to endorse his hawkish stance even though some other premiers from energy-exporting provinces disagree.
"We have to be candid about the reality of the incoming U.S. administration. This is an administration that believes in America first. This is an administration which is proudly an economic nationalist administration," Freeland said Friday in her last public event as finance minister.
At a separate event that day, LeBlanc said pointedly, "I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself," he said, conceding "other premiers have offered views on what might be the appropriate response."
Indeed, Alberta's Smith, whose province supplies 60 per cent of oil used in the U.S., and Quebec Premier François Legault, whose province is a major exporter of hydro electricity, have rejected Ford's musings about curbing energy exports.
Ontario sells electricity to Michigan, New York and Minnesota, powering the homes of 1.5 million Americans.
Ford said Monday the premiers will continue to pressure Ottawa to boost Canada's military spending, a push being led by Manitoba's Kinew.
"The reason why we have that unity on these priorities is because if we as Canadians come together to invest in the Canadian Armed Forces, to protect our border, to rid the scourge of toxic drugs in our communities, what's the downside we take action on those things?" said the Manitoban.
"We're unified in investing in the military, we're unified in cracking down on drug trafficking, we're unified on having a secure border," added Kinew.
So far, Canada is falling short of NATO's demand that its 32 member nations spend at least two per cent of gross domestic product on defence.
NATO estimates the Trudeau government will spend 1.37 per cent of GDP on the military this year.
The prime minister has previously pledged to meet the two-per-cent target by 2032, including increased spending on defence infrastructure totalling $8.1 billion over the next five years.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media during the fall meetings of Canada's premiers hosted by Ontario on Monday.

Letters: Federal government orders end to Canada Post strike while commission tries to reach new contract, Dec. 13 Our leaders rarely acknowledge the grip Big Oil has on Canada. That's why what Catherine McKenna did was so striking, Dec. 12 Doug Ford warns of 'cutting off their energy' to retaliate against tariffs by Donald Trump, Dec. 11 How Toronto plans to add 56,000 residents in the Golden Mile, Dec. 6 Tax the snowbirds in response to Trumps threats, Dec. 12 From bike lanes to carbon tax to guns - these initiatives don't stand up to critical analysis, Dec. 12

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3144878801/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Federal+government+orders+end+to+Canada+Post+strike+while+commission+tries+to+reach+new+contract%2C+Dec.+13+Our+leaders+rarely+acknowledge+the+grip+Big+Oil+has+on+Canada.+That%27s+why+what+Catherine+McKenna+did+was+so+striking%2C+Dec.+12+Doug+Ford+warns+of+%27cutting+off+their+energy%27+to+retaliate+against+tariffs+by+Donald+Trump%2C+Dec.+11+How+Toronto+plans+to+add+56%2C000+residents+in+the+Golden+Mile%2C+Dec.+6+Tax+the+snowbirds+in+response+to+Trumps+threats%2C+Dec.+12+From+bike+lanes+to+carbon+tax+to+guns+-+these+initiatives+don%27t+stand+up+to+critical+analysis%2C+Dec.+12&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada Post
runs on obsolete business model
Federal government orders end to Canada Post strike while commission tries to reach new contract, Dec. 13
Well, there we have it, another toxic chapter writ in the book of the labour relationship at Canada Post. What is really telling is the lack of sympathy for workers who voluntarily gave up their contract negotiations during the pandemic to keep the mail and parcels moving.
This echoes our current apathy toward the work environment for our "hero" nurses. Capitalism's race to the bottom for workers has created an environment where having benefits and pensions is seen as elitist and worthy of contempt and scorn. The definition of stupidity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting a different result. And so it is with Canada Post. An obsolete business model and Dickensian labour relations. What is really needed is a business model that focuses on providing letter mail and parcel delivery to rural communities that breaks even. Management seems incapable of taking this on.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Canada has become a petrostate
Our leaders rarely acknowledge the grip Big Oil has on Canada. That's why what Catherine McKenna did was so striking, Dec. 12
In the summer of 2017, when I was 10 years old, the Honourable Catherine McKenna and I made a pinky promise to protect the climate, and I felt empowered. I've led more than 200 Fridays for Future strikes since November 2018, and I'm suing the government of Ontario for violating our human rights by weakening climate laws.
At the time, it seemed like a simple promise, but in hindsight, it now feels somewhat naive. The truth is, Canada has become a captured petrostate (a country where the fossil fuel industry has such a strong grip on politics, the economy, and even social systems that it can block meaningful climate action) largely due to the misinformation flooding social media.
It's time to turn to accountable sources like the Toronto Star, not platforms controlled by Mark Zuckerberg and Elon Musk. Another truth is that McKenna will be remembered as a good ancestor, a leader who spoke out when it mattered most.
Sophia Mathur,
Fridays For Future Greater Sudbury, Sudbury
Ford should not lead Council of the Federation
Doug Ford warns of 'cutting off their energy' to retaliate against tariffs by Donald Trump, Dec. 11
No sooner had Premier Doug Ford called for a united approach from all the premiers and Ottawa, than he does a complete turnaround and threatens to go to economic war with the United States. While this is faithful to the premier's penchant to "act first, think later," it's enough of a departure from the collective course the other premiers and the federal government have decided on that Ford should immediately step down as chair of the Council of the Federation. Surely there must be another premier who doesn't panic at the first sign of potential trouble and can respond in a more diplomatic manner.
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
A golden chance for community development
How Toronto plans to add 56,000 residents in the
Golden Mile, Dec. 6
Since its inception in 2018, the Inclusive Local Economic Opportunity (ILEO) - established by United Way Greater Toronto and BMO Financial Group - has worked to ensure that the Golden Mile's redevelopment avoids the missteps of the past. By fostering collaboration between front-line social service agencies like ours, developers, corporations, and the city, ILEO has created projects that deliver real value to local residents.
From training and placing residents in construction jobs to supporting local entrepreneurs and encouraging developers to buy local products, ILEO has already put more than $12 million into the pockets of residents and created nearly 175 jobs, even before construction began.
As an agency embedded in the community, we understand both the challenges and opportunities that come with redevelopment. By being part of this process from the start, we're optimistic about what's ahead. With a commitment to collaboration and putting residents at the heart of decision-making, we're confident the Golden Mile can thrive as it undergoes these profound changes. Thank you for bringing much-needed attention to this project.
Your article not only sheds light on the immense potential of the Golden Mile but also inspires hope that meaningful and inclusive progress is possible.
Vanda Henriques,
Working Women
Community Centre, Toronto
Exchange rate will deter snowbirds
Tax the snowbirds in response to Trumps threats, Dec. 12
Snowbirds pay their taxes to Canada and at the same time pay state and local city taxes on consumables purchased in the U.S. Canadians cannot work legally in the U.S. without proper documentation so tax on income is out of the question. The actual deterrent to snowbirds is the unconscionable exchange rate on the U.S.-Canadian dollar. To some retirees, it's better to enjoy the later years by golfing, playing tennis or pickleball, or just sitting in the sunshine for five or six months rather than sitting idly by in the cold winter.
James Rae, Burlington
No expert on road safety
From bike lanes to carbon tax to guns - these initiatives don't stand up to critical
analysis, Dec. 12
Mark McQueen, whose biography includes his role as a former adviser to former prime minister Brian Mulroney, clearly doesn't have any expertise in road safety. In suggesting that bike lanes are not a good investment, he points to the six cyclist deaths in the city in 2024, while missing the obvious point that five of those six deaths were on roads without bike lanes. The other death was on a road with a blocked bike lane.
McQueen's figures for new bike lanes in Toronto are also wildly exaggerated, while his comments on emergency response times misstate his cited source.
I'm sure McQueen is an expert in various fields, but none of those fields include cycling safety.
Albert Koehl, co-ordinator
Community Bikeways, Toronto
