Document 342 of 500

Leaders to discuss response to Trump tariff threat: Meeting comes days after Ford suggested he would 'cut off' Ontario's electricity exports to U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/leaders-discuss-response-trump-tariff-threat/docview/3144878799/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Leaders+to+discuss+response+to+Trump+tariff+threat%3A+Meeting+comes+days+after+Ford+suggested+he+would+%27cut+off%27+Ontario%27s+electricity+exports+to+U.S.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's provincial and territorial leaders are meeting Monday to discuss U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canadian products.
Premier Doug Ford, chair of the Council of the Federation, is hosting the meeting at the Hilton Hotel near Pearson International Airport.
The in-person confab comes after two recent virtual first ministers' meetings with the premiers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in the wake of Trump's plan to impose levies on Canadian and Mexican goods unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Ford made headlines stateside last Wednesday with his threat to "cut off" Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. in retaliation for any tariffs.
"Americans are going to feel the pain as well and isn't that unfortunate," he warned.
The premier's gambit forced Trump to respond the next day.
"That's OK if he that does that. That's fine," the president-elect said Thursday, complaining the U.S. "is subsidizing Canada" by spending so much on defence.
"We shouldn't have to do that. And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than $100 billion a year," maintained Trump.
Ford was encouraged that the incoming president took notice.
"It was the first time president-elect Trump said, 'I want to make a deal with Canada.' That's good news," he told reporters Friday.
Both Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc appeared to endorse Ford's hawkish stance even though some other premiers from energy-exporting provinces disagree.
"We have to be candid about the reality of the incoming U.S. administration. This is an administration that believes in America first. This is an administration which is proudly an economic nationalist administration," said Freeland.
LeBlanc, for his part, said pointedly: "I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself," he said, conceding "other premiers have offered views on what might be the appropriate response."
Indeed, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, whose province supplies 60 per cent of oil used in the U.S., and Quebec Premier François Legault, whose province is a major exporter of hydro electricity, rejected Ford's musings about curbing energy exports.
Ontario sells electricity to Michigan, New York and Minnesota, powering the homes of 1.5 million Americans.
Ford, insisting he doesn't want a trade war, made no apologies for his willingness to up the ante against Trump. "If it comes to Alberta or Quebec, that's their choice. They believe in diplomacy - good luck," the premier said.
Freeland, who delivers a fall economic statement Monday outlining $1 billion in new spending on border security, agreed with the Ontarian, noting "Canada will not escalate and Canada will not back down."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

An obsolete business model and Dickensian labour relations mar Canada Post

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/obsolete-business-model-dickensian-labour/docview/3144878718/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+obsolete+business+model+and+Dickensian+labour+relations+mar+Canada+Post&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Federal government orders end to Canada Post strike while commission tries to reach new contract, Dec. 13
Well, there we have it, another toxic chapter writ in the book of the labour relationship at Canada Post. What is really telling is the lack of sympathy for workers who voluntarily gave up their contract negotiations during the pandemic to keep the mail and parcels moving.
This echoes our current apathy toward the work environment for our "hero" nurses. Capitalism's race to the bottom for workers has created an environment where having benefits and pensions is seen as elitist and worthy of contempt and scorn. The definition of stupidity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting a different result. And so it is with Canada Post. An obsolete business model and Dickensian labour relations. What is really needed is a business model that focuses on providing letter mail and parcel delivery to rural communities that breaks even. Management seems incapable of taking this on.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga

Document 344 of 500

Doug Ford's finance minister touts improved credit ratings: 'That's $1 billion you have'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-finance-minister-touts-improved-credit/docview/3144673172/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+finance+minister+touts+improved+credit+ratings%3A+%27That%27s+%241+billion+you+have%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Even in a time of economic uncertainty, they have to give credit where credit is due.
Despite the threat of a trade war with the U.S. and rising unemployment, Ontario got some very positive fiscal news this year.
The province's credit rating was upgraded to "AA-" from "A+(P)" by S&P Global last week, the first such improvement from that agency since 2001.
That followed a credit upgrade in June to "AA" from "AA (low) (P)" by Morningstar DBRS, which had last boosted Ontario's ranking in 2006.
For Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy that means much more than just an alphabet soup of ratings decipherable only to those on Bay Street and Wall Street.
"Bond holders are looking at who is managing their credit most effectively. It leads to lower interest costs," Bethlenfalvy said in an interview with the Star this week.
"Ultimately, what that means is that your borrowing costs ... are lower," said the treasurer.
According to the most recent public accounts, Ontario's interest expenditure dropped from $12.4 billion in 2022-23 to $11.4 billion last fiscal year - a savings of $1 billion.
"That's $1 billion you have to pay down debt, you can invest in health care, you've got options for your priorities," he said.
"When you have to borrow in the bond markets - which we continue to do, primarily for infrastructure because we've got an ambitious plan - investors have choices," said Bethlenfalvy, referring to the billions the province is borrowing to fund subways, highways, hospitals and other projects.
"So how do you stand out amongst all those that are issuing bonds and get the lowest rate? They look at your fiscal track record, they look at your credit ratings, they look at a host of factors. This is a vote of confidence in Ontario."
That's especially important to Bethlenfalvy, who was co-president of DBRS when the rating agency downgraded Ontario to "AA (low)" from "AA" in 2009 after the global financial crash.
"I was there, of course, and now, whoops, the shoe is on the other foot," the treasurer mused.
"Having worked in the capital markets and at a credit-rating agency - working both in New York and Toronto - I've seen capital markets, I've seen sovereigns and how they manage their debt. And I think it still matters," he said.
"It matters because you don't know what's around the corner, whether it's a pandemic or ... a financial crisis (like) 2008. We're dealing with economic uncertainty all the time so I think it's wise to be prudent on the fiscal side and to have targets - and that's what rating agencies like."
Credit raters also like fiscal capacity so Premier Doug Ford's frequent boast that he has "never raised a tax" is not unhelpful because it suggests the province has untapped revenue streams if need be.
Bethlenfalvy, who has no plans to raise any taxes, counters that by noting rating agencies "like it when you grow your economy, because that increases your tax revenues."
He emphasized that S&P upgraded Ontario's rating after he announced the province would spend $3 billion to send $200 "rebate" cheques to 15 million Ontarians in the new year despite running a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
"If I can point out, under the previous government - 15 years - taxes were raised and credit ratings fell," said Bethlenfalvy, referring to the Liberals of premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne who governed from 2003 to 2018 and suffered two S&P downgrades, one from DBRS, two from Moody's and one from Fitch.
"Obviously S&P and other rating agencies have confidence that we're very committed to our fiscal track."
CAPTION: Ontario's Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy speaks at a TTC press conference on Nov. 29, 2024.

Subject: Uncertainty; Rating services; Borrowing; International finance; Ratings & rankings; Cost control; Credit ratings; Capital markets

Document 345 of 500

I am a long-time housing advocate. Here is why Bonnie Crombie's housing plan may actually work

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-am-long-time-housing-advocate-here-is-why/docview/3144671785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+am+a+long-time+housing+advocate.+Here+is+why+Bonnie+Crombie%27s+housing+plan+may+actually+work&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've spent years advocating for bold reforms to tackle Ontario's housing affordability crisis at the volunteer organization More Neighbours Toronto. We've called for modernizing outdated planning rules, loosening restrictive land-use policies, and streamlining sluggish permitting - long-term measures that will help stabilize prices and boost supply. But these deeper changes take years to bear fruit, and right now Ontario's housing market is on the brink. Housing starts are collapsing, construction workers face mounting job losses, and families are watching their dreams of owning a home slip away.
One major reason housing is so expensive is that governments have quietly piled massive taxes and fees onto new homes. Consider this: in 2000, a benchmark two-bedroom Toronto condo cost about $145,000 (around $244,000 in today's dollars). Now, the taxes and fees slapped onto a similar new unit exceed $240,000 - essentially more than the condo once cost. The Residential Construction Council of Ontario (RESCON) says these charges now eat up 36 per cent of a new home's price - an even higher rate than the sin taxes on alcohol or tobacco. It's an incredible distortion that drives prices up for everyone, both renters and aspiring owners.
More Neighbours Toronto is a non-partisan group committed to working with any government, at any level, that takes the affordability crisis seriously. We've consulted with every major party at the provincial and federal levels, and we were a cited stakeholder in the Ford government's Housing Affordability Task Force. We've maintained open lines with the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing (MMAH) and also engaged with the Ontario NDP on its Homes Ontario plan. So, when Bonnie Crombie's team asked me to join their housing platform task force to provide input to their plans, it signalled they weren't just paying lip service - they were serious. Now, seeing their commitment in action, I believe the Ontario Liberals have nailed a key piece of housing policy that Doug Ford should have adopted long ago - and still could.
At the heart of the Ontario Liberal's new proposal - More Homes You Can Afford - is a direct assault on these runaway fees. They plan to scrap development charges - hidden levies that can add six figures to a home's cost - for all new homes under 3,000 square feet, large enough for a four-bedroom family home. To emphasize how important this proposal is, consider that in 1999, development charges on a two-bedroom Toronto condo were about $1,348 (around $2,265 today). Now that same unit faces more than $80,000 in development charges - a 26-fold increase after inflation. Across Ontario, development charges can top $170,000 per home.
This represents a radical break from the old "growth pays for growth" model that burdened one generation with staggering debts to fund citywide infrastructure just to keep property taxes low for another. Crombie's plan suggests the province would step in with a "better communities fund," which pledges to make municipalities whole and provide stable, long-term infrastructure funding they can count on.
Some might dismiss this as a developer giveaway, but that misunderstands how markets work. Since all developers pay these fees, they must pass them on to buyers, pushing prices even higher and ironically encouraging speculative demand. Cutting development charges and other housing-related taxes lowers the price floor, making it easier to launch projects, get housing built, and easing price pressures market-wide.
The Ontario Liberals proposal doesn't stop at development charges. It would also eliminate the provincial Land Transfer Tax for first-time buyers and seniors, instantly saving young families about $9,500 and downsizing seniors about $13,500. Critics may point to Crombie's housing record as Mississauga's mayor, where development charges rose dramatically in parallel with other GTA municipalities, but no single municipal leader can fix a province-wide systemic failure alone. Her experience as mayor is precisely the reason the Ontario Liberals want a provincial solution for infrastructure funding to replace these taxes.
The rental market gets attention too. The Ontario Liberal's plan suggests implementing phased-in rent control - like in Manitoba, Oregon, and British Columbia - that protects tenants without deterring new construction. They would remove community benefit charges and Land Transfer Tax for nonmarket providers such as co-ops, community land trusts, and not-for-profits like Habitat for Humanity, allowing them to build affordable homes unencumbered by red tape. Clearing the Landlord-Tenant Board backlog within two months, adding anti-renoviction protections, and creating a Rental Emergency Support Fund would finally bring much-needed stability to renters struggling in today's chaotic market.
As an advocate of housing abundance as a solution to our housing crisis, I consider this proposal among the boldest I've seen proposed by a political leader in years. No single measure will fix everything overnight - we still need modernized building codes, faster approvals, and more flexible land use - but More Homes You Can Afford signals that our politics is finally willing to confront the hard truths at the root of our problem.
CAPTION: Members of thevolunteer-run pro-housing group More Neighbours Toronto gather in the Distillery District in February 2022. FounderEric Lombardi is in the centre.
CREDIT: Eric Lombardi, Contributor

Subject: Prices; Task forces; Infrastructure; Affordable housing; Transfer taxes; Housing starts; Fees & charges

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure Affordable housing Transfer taxes Housing starts

Document 346 of 500

Crombie is stuck in a shrinking middle ground

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-is-stuck-shrinking-middle-ground/docview/3144528416/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+is+stuck+in+a+shrinking+middle+ground&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've been writing about the Ford family for more than 15 years and I've learned you can't "out Ford a Ford."
It's a lesson Ontario Liberals seemingly haven't grasped.
Ontario politics are experiencing a bit of political déjà vu. Think back to the 2010 Toronto mayoral election, when Rob Ford, an irascible councillor from north Etobicoke, was running an angry man's campaign, ranting against waste and things like the guy who watered the plants at city hall.
That's all Ford lore now, but George Smitherman, former Liberal MPP and high-profile minister in the McGuinty government, was his main rival. Smitherman leaned into his own angry reputation and "Furious George" nickname, trying to out Ford a Ford.
At the time, I wrote a "Dear George" column aimed at Smitherman in Eye Weekly magazine, saying it felt like he was running the same campaign as Rob Ford. There was a void in the race waiting to be filled with big and positive ideas about what Toronto could be instead of just focusing on the problems in a race to the bottom. Problems must be faced, but not at the expense of offering positive visions for the future, too. Rob Ford beat Smitherman by a large margin.
A similar thing occurred in 2017. Even then, Toronto was besieged by traffic woes, and John Tory, to his great credit, proposed road tolls on the Gardiner and DVP that, as proven in other cities, would reduce congestion by encouraging drivers to take transit, all while raising funds for its expansion.
With an election looming, Kathleen Wynne's unpopular Liberal government snuffed out the idea, fearing it was political self-sabotage, a turn away from the somewhat progressive platform she won a majority with in 2014. While Doug Ford was not yet leader of the PCs in 2017, the lesson here is since Wynne was polling so low, why not try something big and progressive? However, the Liberal backroom establishment pushed Wynne away from policies that, I would argue, were more true to her own politics.
Of late, Bonnie Crombie's tenure as leader of the Ontario Liberal party has not been as smooth nor as easy as expected. As the Star reported last week, after a year leading the party, Crombie hasn't been able to close the gap with Doug Ford or create a buzz around her leadership.
Having just nine sitting MPPs in her caucus and not yet having a seat herself surely has something to do with this. But other new party leaders have been able to create needed buzz, especially when up against a government that should, based on the sheer number of scandals and investigations alone, be quite vulnerable.
A criticism lobbed at Crombie is that she's "Doug Ford lite," and if you consider some of her statements and platform, she's often more like - or moving toward - Ford than not. She recently came out against the federal carbon tax, said she would cut other taxes, used specific "fund the police" language and in a Trillium interview last week said identity politics are dead.
This isn't to say all of her platform is unworthy of consideration. Crombie's plan to axe the land transfer tax for first-time home buyers is quite compelling, and needing to distance herself from the unpopular federal Liberals is understandable, too. But how to do that is tricky. Move further left or get closer to Ford? The question Ontario voters must ask themselves is how different, on the whole, is Crombie from Ford?
As for identity politics, reports of its death have been greatly exaggerated. Being a centrist (even if actually tacking conservative) is absolutely a political identity, just as campaigning on a record as a moderate suburban mayor is.
However, you can't "out suburb" a Ford either, but even this is complicated. As Mississauga mayor, Crombie saw kilometres of bike lanes installed, used her "strong mayor" powers to push through fourplexes while Ford balked on doing the same across the province, and even saw a stormwater fee implemented, a good thing but falsely derided as a "rain tax" when Toronto later proposed it.
At the same time, Crombie has referred to the NDP as "radical," an amusing term considering the Ontario NDP are a fairly standard centre-left party, much to the chagrin of more ardent lefties. Political space around the centre is quite crowded, then.
All of this puts Crombie in a difficult place. Can't "out Ford a Ford" on the right, but moving left means quickly bumping into NDP territory. If she continues as she has though, there's an incredible opportunity for the NDP to fill the void. Maybe even some room to be a little radical.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is in a tricky position, Shawn Micallef writes. She can't "out Ford a Ford" on the right, but moving left means quickly bumping into NDP territory.Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Document 347 of 500

Bending the rules: How Canada opened its doors to an unknown number of Nazi war criminals and collaborators to live out their lives in peace

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bending-rules/docview/3144528317/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bending+the+rules%3A+How+Canada+opened+its+doors+to+an+unknown+number+of+Nazi+war+criminals+and+collaborators+to+live+out+their+lives+in+peace&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unknown number of Nazi war criminals and collaborators lived out their lives in peace in Canada, in places such as the Mennonite farms and villages of Waterloo Region.
They were among thousands of ethnic Germans brought into Canada after the Second World War - many of them members of the most reprehensible units of the Nazi war machine - by a group called the Canadian Christian Council for the Resettlement of Refugees (CCCRR), which included the Mennonite Central Committee (MCC).
How this happened is detailed in a Government of Canada report, "Nazi War Criminals in Canada: The Historical And Policy Setting From the 1940s to the Present." Known as The Rodal Report, it is a 600-page study prepared by Oxford-trained historian Alti Rodal for the Deschênes Commission on Nazi War Criminals in Canada, which released its findings in 1986.
For decades, Ottawa refused to release the entirety of the Rodal report. The Waterloo Region Record filed an Access to Information request for a complete copy six years ago.
The Deschênes Commission said there were 29 cases of suspected Nazi war criminals in Canada that warranted special consideration because of the seriousness of the allegations and the availability of evidence against the men.
Among those priority cases was Waterloo resident Helmut Oberlander. The Record filed an Access to Information Act request for the files on the other 28 cases, but Library and Archives Canada has refused to release any of the documents.
Before he died in September 2021, Oberlander was front page news in Waterloo Region, fighting a deportation order on and off since 1995. That's when Ottawa announced it was going to strip Oberlander of his citizenship and deport him for failing to disclose his voluntary service with Einsatzgruppe D, one of the Nazi's mobile death squads that operated in Eastern Europe, Soviet Ukraine and the Caucuses.
Oberlander and his wife came to Canada in May 1954, sponsored by a German relative in Waterloo. He was "Volksdeutsche" - a racial term coined by the Nazis for ethnic Germans who didn't hold German citizenship - from one of the oldest Mennonite communities in southern Russia. Oberlander was not brought to Waterloo Region by the MCC, but others were.
The Rodal report makes it clear there were many more - in fact it was easier for Nazi war criminals to come to Canada after the Second World War than Jews who wanted to rebuild their lives in Canada following the Holocaust.
Refugee aid groups and immigration officials preferred Germans and Baltic people, deemed to be highly attractive and desirable immigrants, "who were likely to have included - and indeed have been shown to have included in their midst persons who were Nazi collaborators and alleged war criminals," says Rodal.
During 1947-1949, the MCC and the CCCRR helped bring 6,005 Mennonites to Canada, and 15,000 Volksdeutsche - primarily from the western and southwestern areas of the former Soviet Union that became Ukraine in 1991.
About 807 of them settled in Ontario, primarily Kitchener-Waterloo, St. Catharines and Leamington. Many who first settled in other parts of Canada later moved to this region, say Mennonite historians.
Some Mennonites who were former SS and Nazi collaborators came to this region, but not many, said Troy Osborne, a history professor and dean of Mennonite studies at Conrad Grebel University in Waterloo.
"I don't think there were a lot in this area," said Osborne.
But researchers are reluctant to dig into this chapter of Mennonite history in the region.
"Part of the reason I think it is frosted over around here, you are talking about people's grandparents, it is not just about the MCC, it is about people's own family histories too," said Osborne. "That's what I've discovered."
Library and Archives Canada released the most complete version of the Rodal report in February 2024 following an international scandal six months earlier. The House of Commons gave two standing ovations to a former member of the Ukrainian-SS in September 2023.
That's when Ukraine president Volodymyr Zelenskyy was a guest of honour in Ottawa and spoke in Parliament to thank Canada for help in the war against Russia.
In the audience was 98-year-old Yaroslav Hunka, a former member of the Ukrainian SS - part of the Waffen-SS largely made up of non-German volunteers.
Federal politicians and their guests gave Hunka two standing ovations after he was introduced as a true hero of Ukraine by the Speaker of the House, Anthony Rota, a Liberal MP from North Bay.
When Hunka's background became public, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau apologized, and Rota resigned as Speaker of the House. Immigration Minister Marc Miller acknowledged Canada has a dark history when it comes to Nazis, and said requests to release more classified information about it would be revisited.
In February 2024, Library and Archives Canada released almost everything from the Rodal Report, minus one page and a handful of redactions.
The report lays out in detail a seeming indifference to war crimes by officials, openly discriminatory immigration policies, antisemitism, and security screening that was lax to non-existent.
"It would be rash to assume that significant numbers of war criminals and Nazi collaborators did not enter Canada," Rodal says in her report.
The original SS were early, enthusiastic supporters of Adolf Hitler, pledging loyalty to him alone. Later, the Nazis easily found enough volunteers in the Baltics, Eastern Europe, the Balkans and Soviet Ukraine to form divisions called the Waffen-SS.
The SS and Waffen-SS ran the concentration camps and death camps. They were largely responsible for carrying out the Holocaust.
The Nuremberg War Crimes Tribunal ruled the SS as a whole, including Waffen-SS divisions, a criminal organization. From 1945-1950 they were officially banned from coming to Canada, along with members of the SD (the brutal SS intelligence agency that operated in tandem with the SS), the Brown Shirts, or Storm Troopers, known as the SA, and members of the Einsatzgruppen mobile death squads that killed 1.5 million people, mostly Jews, in 1941-1943.
After the war, the Allies suspected many men from Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine had volunteered for some of the Nazi's worst units, says Rodal. Among that group were the Volksdeutsche living in Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine.
By 1950, Canada had increased its immigration quotas and was relaxing its policies on the banned groups and favouring 'desirable' ethnic German immigrants.
Rodal ties the CCCRR - an umbrella organization that represented Baptist, Lutheran, Mennonite and Catholic Church groups mainly of German origin - with the changes in government policy.
The group lobbied Ottawa tirelessly to drop the immigration bans on former SS members, and senior bureaucrats did, without telling the House of Commons or the Canadian public, says Rodal, setting the stage for Germany to become the biggest source of immigrants to Canada in the 1950s.
The church group "is closely linked to changes in government policy with regard to admitting enemy aliens and former members of the Nazi party and the Waffen SS, and other Nazi organizations," says Rodal.
In fact, Helmut Rauca, a former member of Einsatzgruppe A, one of the Nazis' mobile death squads responsible for killing thousands of Jews in Latvia, came to Canada in the late 1940s as a CCCRR sponsored refugee, under his own name, settling in Toronto.
West Germany issued an arrest warrant for Rauca in 1963, and Canada deported him in 1983 to face charges of crimes against humanity. He died in a West German jail awaiting trial.
By an order-in-council, Ottawa provided funds to the CCCRR, says Rodal, and it enjoyed direct access to ministers of immigration, deputy-ministers of immigration, and the prime minister.
It also had access to a powerful group of senior bureaucrats that screened certain visa applications, the civil service and the military looking for communists, fellow travellers and gays. It was called the Security Panel, and it always met behind closed doors.
In 1950 the Security Panel ranked communists as the most dangerous security threat facing Canada, and former members of the SS, the Nazi Party or Nazi collaborators were ranked lower, says Rodal.
At the same time, Jews faced widespread discrimination when applying for visas to Canada and were often rejected by screening officials as communists or communist sympathizers, perpetuating Third Reich propaganda that equated Jews with communism, says Rodal.
The Rodal report is supported by many Mennonite historians.
The Mennonite Central Committee has released reports by 12 historians with access to MCC archives. Their research was summarized in a special, 68-page special edition of the MCC newsletter "Intersections" in the fall of 2021.
To help Mennonites get out of Europe after the war, the MCC publicly and systemically downplayed the collaboration of tens of thousands of Mennonites with the Nazis, says that special edition. Many of them were coached on what to say during interviews with immigration and security officers: You are not German, but Mennonite, maybe Dutch; sorry but all my ID was lost; and most importantly you were forced to join any Nazi military unit, you never volunteered.
Mennonites had enjoyed a privileged status under German occupation, notes the Intersections special edition.
"MCC partnered with or directly hired multiple former Nazi officials and even SS agents because of the expertise they had built promoting fascist schemes to resettle Mennonites during WWII," says the publication.
One of the former Nazi officials hired by MCC to help Mennonite refugees was Heinrich Wiebe. The Nazis had installed Wiebe as mayor of the Ukrainian city of Zaporizhzhia. He implemented Nazi racial policies, confiscated the property of Jews and moved them into a ghetto. Wiebe fled to the west when the Soviet Red Army began pushing the German military out of Russian territory.
With MCC help, Wiebe and his wife Olga came to Canada, and settled in Vancouver after the war.
MCC Canada director Rick Cober Bauman supported the decision to bring in independent historians to review the archives. It was not a unanimous decision, and a minority was opposed to digging into old relationships with Nazis, he said.
"All things considered I am pleased that we did it," said Bauman, who grew up in a rural part of Waterloo Region, the son of a preacher. "I think it was a good decision."
Aileen Friesen is among the new generation of scholars asking questions about what Mennonites did before, during and after the Second World War. She wrote about Wiebe in the special edition of Intersections.
She is a University of Winnipeg historian, executive director of the Plett Foundation, which researches the history of Mennonites who came from Russia in the 1870s and settled in Western Canada, and co-director of the centre for transnational Mennonite studies. For years she's researched the Holocaust, Soviet Ukraine and Mennonites.
Friesen stresses it was a complicated time for millions of desperate and traumatized people on the move after the war, with thousands trying to hide their Nazi ties.
The MCC had about 100 people on the ground in postwar Europe to sort through everything and decide who to help.
The MCC was founded in 1920 to help Mennonites get out of the newly formed Soviet Union and come to Canada. In the early 1930s Soviet leader Josef Stalin engineered a famine in Ukraine that killed millions, including many Mennonites. Some Mennonite farmers were killed by the Soviets during an anti-Kulak campaign that targeted prosperous peasants in Soviet Ukraine. Then Stalin had 750,000 people executed during a bloody purge of political opponents called "the Great Terror."
"You look at the 1930s in Ukraine, just indescribable, but then you look at what happened next in the Holocaust, also indescribable," said Friesen.
The MCC helped some Mennonites and Volksdeutsche come to Canada who were otherwise ineligible to come because of their war records, she said, but it is difficult to know how many. Some of the answers may be in archives in Ukraine that are now threatened by Russian drones and missiles.
"It is heartbreaking," said Friesen. "We are finally at the stage to tell the story more fully and these archives might not be there to do it."
In 1948, the MCC helped Jacob Loitjens emigrate to Paraguay from the Netherlands. In 1950, a Dutch court sentenced Loitjens to life in prison for war crimes. In 1962, Loitjens, his wife and three children came to Canada from Paraguay with MCC help and settled in Vancouver where he taught biology at UBC. He joined the First Mennonite Church in Vancouver and, in 1972, he became a Canadian citizen.
In the early 1990s, he was stripped of his citizenship and deported, but not before the MCC and the Mennonite church publicly defended Loitjens and spoke against his deportation.
This is not just Mennonite history but Canadian history and everyone should care about it because many different immigrant groups are implicated in the very difficult story of the Holocaust, said Friesen in an interview from Winnipeg.
"The antisemitism in the Christian churches within the broader society still needs to be addressed, we can't ignore that element of it," said Friesen. "Because it is important to be very honest about ways in which prejudice has encouraged people to take violent actions."
After the publication of the special newsletter, MCC leadership went through training and workshops on antisemitism.
"We take a firm and clear stand against antisemitism as an organization," said Bauman. "We have ongoing anti-racism work both internationally and, in the U.S., and Canada."
In the summer of 1949, the UN's International Refugee Organization ordered a pause in the MCC's immigration work, says Rodal. American intelligence had screened some Mennonite refugees preparing to board a ship in Germany bound for the U.S. and Canada.
The Americans found many former members of the Ukrainian-SS and former members of the Nazi's mobile death squads - the Einsatzgruppen - and German citizens among the Mennonite refugees selected by the MCC, says Rodal. The UN refugee agency passed on the warning to Ottawa that many Mennonites had volunteered for the Nazi's "most reprehensible units," says Rodal.
Cornelius Klassen, who played a key role as the European director of refugee and resettlement services for the MCC from 1945-1954, communicated directly with A.L. Jolliffe, the Director of the Immigration Branch and the federal Department of Mines and Resources, to explain.
"He presented the argument the Mennonites were tricked into German citizenship by being asked to sign blank forms when they were in the camps in Germany, forms which turned out to be applications for German citizenship," says Rodal.
Jolliffe was skeptical, but there was pressure to bring more immigrants to Canada as labour shortages were holding back economic growth, says Rodal.
The International Refugee Organization came up with a compromise - allow this ship load of Mennonites to sail, and those who were now barred from the U.S. could go to either Canada or Paraguay. The names of future immigrants would be screened with name checks at the Berlin Document Centre - a huge repository of all German military records.
They were allowed to come to Canada, and about a year later in April 1950, Prime Minister Louis St. Laurent, the Security Panel and the cabinet decided to drop the blanket ban on German nationals and Volksdeutsche, says Rodal.
The change was made quietly, by orders-in-council, says Rodal. They did that knowing it was impossible to get reliable information on war records for those from Eastern Europe, says Rodal. They were allowed to come if they had relatives of friends in Canada willing to sponsor them, and if they passed the medical examination, says Rodal.
When a Toronto couple sponsored their two sons, both former members of the Waffen-SS, the Security Panel cleared them because the parents were "reliable and respectable people," and the West German government had asked Canadian Immigration officials in Germany to approve the visa requests and reunite the family in Toronto, says Rodal.
The director of immigration in Ottawa cabled the head of the Canadian Immigration Mission in Germany about the approval of visas for the two brothers - "strictly secret information."
In the spring of 1950, the British wanted Canada to take in 8,500 Ukrainians as immigrants. British intelligence claimed the men were from the regular German army - the Wehrmacht. But they were in fact Ukrainian SS troops who fled west to escape the Soviet Red Army, says Rodal.
The plan was delayed when Samuel Bronfman, head of the Canadian Jewish Congress, warned Ottawa it was about to allow a division of Waffen-SS into the country.
The story of the Ukrainian-SS division is not well known in Canada, which is why a former member received two standing ovations in Parliament. Rodal is clear in her report - the SS did not have to force people in Soviet Ukraine to take up arms against their Jewish neighbours or the Soviet Red Army.
"That members joined the division voluntarily is an established fact," says Rodal. "The response to the call for volunteers was overwhelming."
According to German records cited by Rodal, 80,000 Ukrainian men volunteered for the SS, and 50,000 were accepted. "The quota set for the unit was overfilled many times over," says the Rodal report, "and tens of thousands of volunteers had to be rejected."
By the summer of 1943, the Nazi's formed a new Ukrainian Division with 13,000 men. Many of the other volunteers went into Nazi-organized police formations.
The Germans were astonished, says Rodal, even after so many Ukrainians helped the mobile Nazi death squads - the Einsatzgruppen units that massacred 1.5 million people, mostly Jews in 1941 and 1942 in Soviet Ukraine, Eastern Europe, and the Baltics.
The Ukrainian-SS Division fought against the Red Army at the Battle of Brody in July 1943 and 10,000 Waffen-SS were killed or captured, says Rodal. The Germans had no trouble in re-filling the ranks with Nazi police formations and concentration camp guards, says Rodal.
"It is likely that at least some persons who had served with Nazi sponsored Ukrainian police/militia units used in killing actions in 1941-1942 found their way into the ranks of the division either before or after the Battle of Brody," says the Rodal report.
The Nazis called it the 14th Galician Waffen Grenadier Division. It is sometimes called the Galician SS, the Ukrainian SS, the First Galician or the 14th Grenadiers. Different names for the same unit. Ukrainian-SS members massacred people in Delatyn, Nadvirna, Lawoczne, Obertyn and Kossów.
After the Battle of Brody, the Ukrainian-SS Division headed west and surrendered to the British. Less than two weeks before the end of the war, the Ukrainian-SS was rebranded as the Ukrainian Army of Liberation, or the OUN. The British were suspicious and kept 8,500 of them in prisoner of war camps for years, first in Italy and then in the U.K.
When they were finally allowed to come to Canada, the RCMP was supposed to screen them, but the Mounties relied on the British, who didn't look too deeply into the backgrounds of the disciplined troops with no badges on their uniforms and no identity papers in their pockets.
The outrage of the Canadian Jewish Congress slowed, but did not stop the immigration of the former Ukrainian-SS members to Canada, says Rodal.
The international embarrassment following the standing ovations for Hunka in the House of Commons is not the only time members of the Ukrainian-SS have given Canada an international black eye.
There is a cenotaph in an Oakville cemetery topped with the symbol of the Ukrainian-SS, and the 200 or so grave monuments around it also have the symbol - a stylized silhouette of a lion, standing on its hind legs and surround by three crowns. It is a Nazi symbol banned in Germany.
St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Cemetery in Oakville is on land owned by St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Orthodox Cathedral on Bathurst Street in Toronto.
In Edmonton there is a bust of Roman Shukhevych in front of a Ukrainian youth centre. Shukhevych was the commander of the Nachtigall Battalion, made up of 650 Ukrainian volunteers who perpetrated massacres of ethnic Poles in Galicia-Volhynia and Jews in Lviv.
In the spring of 1951, again by order-in-council, the blanket immigration ban on former SS and Waffen-SS members was relaxed and replaced with a case-by-case approach which focused on voluntary service versus forced conscription, says Rodal.
RCMP war crimes investigators found 15 members of the Ukrainian-SS had entered Canada before the immigration rules were relaxed, says Rodal.
In October 1955, the immigration restrictions put in place after the war were completely removed. Former members of the SS, the Waffen-SS and SA became admissible to Canada, says Rodal.
That decision was made by a subgroup of the Security Panel, and it did not seek the approval of the full Security Panel or the federal cabinet, says Rodal.
It was treated as an administrative procedure within the discretionary authority of the responsible minister, says Rodal.
At that time, the federal Minister of Immigration and Citizenship was Jack Pickersgill. As the immigration restrictions for former members of the SS were relaxed, he gave the opposite impression to a reporter.
In 1955, Pickersgill said in an interview that German immigrants were carefully screened for communist sympathies, or membership in the Nazi Party and the SS.
"The best that can be said for this public stance is that it is untruthful," says Rodal, adding the government continued to imply that mere membership in the SS or collaboration with the enemy was an absolute bar to entering Canada.
The changes were kept quiet.
Millions of Europeans had fresh memories of SS atrocities, and Canada did not want its lax immigration policies for former SS members to become a public scandal among European allies, says Rodal.
CREDIT: Terry Pender waterloo region record

Subject: Bans; Holocaust; Historians; War crimes; Immigrants; Communism; World War II; Bureaucrats; Archives & records; Citizenship; Access to information; Nazi era; Libraries; Speakers of the House; Criminals; Arrest warrants; Prime ministers; Immigration

Location: Russia; Canada; Eastern Europe; Ukraine

Title: Bending the rules:   How Canada opened its doors to an unknown number of Nazi war criminals and collaborators to live out their lives in peace

Document 348 of 500

Ford's encampment crackdown will keep people in institutions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-encampment-crackdown-will-keep-people/docview/3144528293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+encampment+crackdown+will+keep+people+in+institutions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Homeless encampments have become a regular fixture in cities across Canada. Unsheltered homelessness, including people staying in encampments, rose 88 per cent across the country from 2018 to 2022, according to the last national point-in-time count.
In Ontario, advocates, clinicians, researchers, mayors and councillors, in addition to people experiencing homelessness, have been sounding the alarm, calling on the provincial government to act. Last week, Premier Doug Ford responded to those calls by signalling that he would introduce legislation to provide municipalities with "enhanced legislative powers" to dismantle encampments.
Then on Thursday, the government introduced new legislation giving police more power to punish drug use in public parks, and penalties for those who continually "trespass" in parks and other properties. These changes are all part of the Ford government's plans to address encampments.
Ford also pledged $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing across the province. While this is a much-needed investment, building sufficient affordable housing will take time and additional resources. Where will encampment residents go in the meantime?
The government's approach to dismantling encampments in effect puts forward three potential paths for residents: shelter, jail or hospital. These options, known for decades as the institutional circuit, do not resolve the primary unmet need of encampment residents: rapid access to housing with supports. Moreover, shelter, jail and hospitals represent the most expensive types of services where you can find people experiencing homelessness.
Dismantling encampments may also cause further harm to those residing there. A U.S. simulation study found that involuntary displacement, such as encampment dismantlement, produces additional overdose deaths. Involuntary displacement also results in more hospitalizations, as well as reduced initiation of medications for opioid use disorder. This research strongly suggests that dismantling encampments in Ontario would have a similar result: increased overdose fatalities and reduced access to addiction treatment.
As researchers and clinicians working with people who are homeless, we know there is a better and more efficient way to reduce the number of encampments: a housing-first intervention. That involves the provision of a rent supplement, along with community-based mental health and addiction supports to help those with serious mental illness, substance use disorders and chronic medical conditions to immediately access permanent housing.
Research from the Canadian At Home/Chez Soi demonstration project - the largest randomized controlled trial of the housing-first approach conducted anywhere in the world - found that the intervention is effective in housing 80 to 90 per cent of people experiencing long-term homelessness. Followup research conducted in Toronto showed these outcomes were sustained as many as six years later.
Housing-first intervention is smart and cost-effective. The cost is partially offset by a reduction in the use of health care, shelter and criminal justice services. Many hospital visits by people experiencing homelessness are preventable with housing and the right supports, giving the housing-first approach a role to play in reducing hallway medicine in the province.
It is not just in research where we see the effectiveness. Houston has become a leader in the U.S. when it comes to ending homelessness. In 2011, the city had about 8,500 people who were homeless. A decade later, it had reduced that by 61 per cent. Unsheltered homelessness is also down 33 per cent in the last four years. Houston's approach? Housing first.
We agree with the premier that enough is enough: It's time to end unsheltered homelessness in Ontario. But, let's not waste more money shuffling people experiencing homelessness around the institutional circuit. Instead, let's leverage a housing-first intervention, a proven way to get people the housing and support they need.
This approach is not only more fiscally prudent for taxpayers, it is a more sustainable solution for improving access and safety in our public spaces.
Dr. Nick Kerman is a psychologist and scientific associate at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH). Dr. Vicky Stergiopoulos is a psychiatrist and senior
scientist at the centre.

'Uncomfortable' Peterson leaves city for U.S.: Controversial psychologist says anti-hate bill's passage could turn Canada into 'totalitarian hellhole'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/uncomfortable-peterson-leaves-city-u-s/docview/3144528286/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Uncomfortable%27+Peterson+leaves+city+for+U.S.%3A+Controversial+psychologist+says+anti-hate+bill%27s+passage+could+turn+Canada+into+%27totalitarian+hellhole%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jordan Peterson is leaving Toronto.
Peterson, appearing on his daughter Mikhaila's podcast in an episode posted Dec. 6, officially announced his move to a sunnier home in the United States, where there "are decided advantages" over living in Toronto.
A psychologist who rose to prominence rallying against preferred pronoun use at the University of Toronto, Peterson has become a popular talking head among conservative and right-wing media in recent years.
Why Peterson is moving
"The issue with the College (of Psychologists of Ontario) ... is very annoying, to say the least," Peterson said, citing reasons why he decided to leave Toronto.
"And the new legislation that the (federal) Liberals are attempting to push through, Bill C-63 (Online Harms Act) - we'd all be living in a totalitarian hellhole if it passes."
"The tax situation is out of hand, the government in Canada at the federal level is incompetent beyond belief. And it's become uncomfortable for me in my neighbourhood in Toronto."
Peterson's spat with the College of Psychologists of Ontario (CPO) stems from complaints over social media posts about, among other things, a "not beautiful" plus-size model. The college ordered Peterson to undergo a coaching program on professionalism in public statements.
In August, three Superior Court judges comprising a panel of Divisional Court, ruled it was reasonable for the CPO to order Peterson to take a course on professionalism in communications. The governing body for psychologists made the order after receiving numerous complaints over Peterson's commentary, which has been criticized for attacking feminism and racial diversity.
Peterson has repeatedly taken aim at the Online Harms Act, or Bill C-63, which has sparked debate over how Ottawa should best strike the balance between protecting its citizens and their right to express themselves freely.
There are seven categories of harm laid out in the bill - three deal with protecting children from exploitative or harmful content, another category covers the sharing of intimate images without consent, and the remaining categories include content that promotes hatred, incites violence, and incites violent extremism or terrorism. The bill was eventually split in two to ensure the child safety guidelines could be passed into law.
Where is he relocating to?
Peterson didn't specify where in the U.S. he has moved to, but mentioned that he was near his daughter, who lives in Arizona.
Peterson didn't say exactly where he lived in Toronto, but a Toronto Life profile from 2021 noted that he had "modest semi in Seaton Village." On the podcast episode, he said he lived in a "kind of champagne socialist neighbourhood" and was "less popular in my own neighbourhood than I am anywhere else in the world, literally."
He called the Toronto District School Board "probably the most woke institution in North America, which is really saying something," over its handling of the COVID-19 pandemic, and said Toronto is "run by the wife (Olivia Chow) of the last leader of the socialist party in Canada (Jack Layton), and so the goal in Toronto was to turn it into Portland or San Francisco. And that's happening."
He said he moved once he and his son, whom he lived close to, became uncomfortable living in Toronto.
"I didn't ever expect that to be something that happened in Canada. But under Justin (Trudeau) and the woke mob, that's definitely something that's happened," Peterson said.
How did he get famous?
It's been almost a decade since Peterson rocketed to national consciousness - and controversy - by arguing that being forced to use pronouns such as "ze" and "zir" was a violation of free speech while lecturing at the University of Toronto. He is also the author of the bestselling book, "12 Rules for Life: Antidote to Chaos," which urged readers to seize responsibility for their own lives, sparking speaking tours, lucrative crowdfunding and official merchandise and launched him into global fame.
Peterson also parted ways with U of T, writing in the National Post in 2022 that he'd resigned from the full-time, tenured position. There were a few reasons, he wrote. Chief among them was the "appalling ideology currently demolishing the universities and, downstream, the general culture."
He has since launched his own online school and regularly appears on conservative media, rallying against climate change activists and trans rights activists.
Peterson vs. Trudeau
Testifying under oath at a public inquiry into foreign interference in October, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appeared to reference recent American allegations that RT - a media mouthpiece for the Russian government - had been covertly bankrolling right-wing influencers, allegedly including Peterson. Peterson denied those claims, telling the National Post, "I don't think it's reasonable for the prime minister of this country to basically label me a traitor and I don't find it amusing."
CAPTION: Jordan Peterson, shown in 2018, appeared on his daughter's podcast recently, where he cited his feud with the College of Psychologists of Ontario and concerns about the Online Harms Act bill as among the key reasons for his decision to leave Canada.Chris WilliamsonGetty Images file photo

Document 350 of 500

Crombie's housing plan may work

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombies-housing-plan-may-work/docview/3144528271/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%27s+housing+plan+may+work&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've spent years advocating for bold reforms to tackle Ontario's housing affordability crisis at the volunteer organization More Neighbours Toronto. We've called for modernizing outdated planning rules, loosening restrictive land-use policies, and streamlining sluggish permitting - long-term measures that will help stabilize prices and boost supply. But these deeper changes take years to bear fruit, and right now Ontario's housing market is on the brink. Housing starts are collapsing, construction workers face mounting job losses and families are watching their dreams of owning a home slip away.
One major reason housing is so expensive is that governments have quietly piled massive taxes and fees onto new homes. Consider this: in 2000, a benchmark two-bedroom Toronto condo cost about $145,000 (around $244,000 in today's dollars). Now, the taxes and fees slapped onto a similar new unit exceed $240,000 - essentially more than the condo once cost.
More Neighbours Toronto is a non-partisan group committed to working with any government, at any level, that takes the affordability crisis seriously. We've consulted with every major party at the provincial and federal levels, and we were a cited stakeholder in the Ford government's Housing Affordability Task Force. We've maintained open lines with the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing (MMAH) and also engaged with the Ontario NDP on its Homes Ontario plan.
So, when Bonnie Crombie's team asked me to join their housing platform task force to provide input to their plans, it signalled they weren't just paying lip service - they were serious. Now, seeing their commitment in action, I believe the Ontario Liberals have nailed a key piece of housing policy that Doug Ford should have adopted long ago - and still could.
At the heart of the Ontario Liberal's new proposal - More Homes You Can Afford - is a direct assault on these runaway fees. They plan to scrap development charges - hidden levies that can add six figures to a home's cost - for all new homes under 3,000 square feet, large enough for a four-bedroom family home.
To emphasize how important this proposal is, consider that in 1999, development charges on a two-bedroom Toronto condo were about $1,348 (around $2,265 today). Now that same unit faces more than $80,000 in development charges - a 26-fold increase after inflation. Across Ontario, development charges can top $170,000 per home.
This represents a radical break from the old "growth pays for growth" model that burdened one generation with staggering debts to fund citywide infrastructure just to keep property taxes low for another. Crombie's plan suggests the province would step in with a "better communities fund," which pledges to make municipalities whole and provide stable, long-term infrastructure funding they can count on.
Some might dismiss this as a developer giveaway, but that misunderstands how markets work. Since all developers pay these fees, they must pass them on to buyers, pushing prices even higher and ironically encouraging speculative demand.
Cutting development charges and other housing-related taxes lowers the price floor, making it easier to launch projects, get housing built, and easing price pressures market-wide.
As an advocate of housing abundance as a solution to our housing crisis, I consider this proposal among the boldest I've seen proposed by a political leader in years. No single measure will fix everything overnight - we still need modernized building codes, faster approvals and more flexible land use - but More Homes You Can Afford signals that our politics is finally willing to confront the hard truths at the root of our problem.
Eric Lombardi is the founder and president of More
Neighbours Toronto, a
volunteer organization committed to ending the housing crisis. Follow him on X: @EricDLombardi
CREDIT: Eric Lombardi Contributor Eric Lombardi is the founder and president of More Neighbours Toronto, a volunteer organization committed to ending the housing crisis. Follow him on X: @EricDLombardi;

Document 351 of 500

Toronto school boards are firing teachers who lie about sick days - and using private investigators to catch them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-school-boards-are-firing-teachers-who-lie/docview/3144364604/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+school+boards+are+firing+teachers+who+lie+about+sick+days+-+and+using+private+investigators+to+catch+them&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School boards, struggling with skyrocketing costs of absenteeism, are turning to private investigators to help uncover sick leave abuse - and, in some cases, firing educators.
One union local in Toronto recently warned members to only book off when ill because they could be under surveillance.
"It is essential to use sick days only as intended under the collective agreement," says a memo from Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers sent to its more than 4,000 members.
"The board has hired private investigators to observe members, attempting to identify what they deem as misuse of sick days," says the memo, obtained by the Star. "This has led to disciplinary actions, up to and including termination. Protect yourself by adhering to the (collective agreement) guidelines."
The Catholic board employs two such investigators. Sources told the Star that earlier this year, up to five teachers at one school were let go after calling in sick and spending the day at a Niagara casino. The school's vice-principal, who reportedly knew of the outing, was also fired, sources say. (The sources were not authorized to speak publicly.)
The Toronto public board also has an investigations unit and says it has, on occasion, hired outside private investigators to look into claims of misconduct, including sick leave abuse, and that employees have lost their job as a result.
Education unions have called the use of investigators "disturbing," but school boards across the province say that while the vast majority of teachers use the days as allowed - for illness, injury or medical appointments - they can't keep up with the escalating costs.
Absenteeism has also exacerbated the province's already dire shortage of educators, leaving schools scrambling to find fill-ins, and students without a permanent teacher.
Last June, the province sent boards guidelines for implementing attendance support programs, noting that "absenteeism not only creates operational challenges for school boards to meet educational priorities, but it also adds financial pressure on school boards."
Last week, the annual provincial auditor general's report noted that the Toronto District School Board - the largest in the country - could save $24 million if it better managed staff absences.
At the Toronto Catholic District School Board, unfunded sick-leave costs account for about two-thirds of its $66-million budget deficit.
Chair Markus de Domenico said the board must "keep the integrity of the system and ... deal with the funding issue we have."
Staff "are very professional and dedicated ... but we are also responsible to the public to be good stewards of our finances and we just have to ensure that the system is being used for what it's designed - to assist people that are ill or have a reason to be off," he told the Star.
"There are, as there are in any large organizations, occasionally people that choose to abuse that. But they don't represent the very vast majority of our staff."
In recent years, some cases where teachers were on sick leave but posting vacation photos on social media have been uncovered, or teachers spotted shopping at Yorkdale Mall on a professional development day.
Roseline Dorcin, a Toronto public board teacher, was placed on medical leave in September 2019, but after parents spotted social media posts showing her living in Montreal and campaigning for the federal election, the board hired private investigators.
Dorcin, who was found to be working at a Montreal school while collecting sick pay, was later fired from the board and stripped of her teaching certificate by the Ontario College of Teachers.
The Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers' union has called hiring investigators "heavy-handed" and "deeply concerning," but the board says sick leave is an "enormous contributor" to its deficit - accounting for $44 million of its $66-million budget shortfall.
The Toronto Catholic board is "committed to ensuring staff benefits are used appropriately," said Shazia Vlahos, the board's chief communications officer of policy, government relations and strategy.
"We take allegations of sick leave misuse seriously and are implementing reasonable measures to address such concerns," including labour relations investigators looking into allegations of professional misconduct by employees, she said. She added that "we hope this approach will allow us to use the anticipated savings from these unfunded costs in a prudent manner to further support student learning and address current classroom instructional needs."
Deborah Karam, president of Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers, said her members want to be in the classroom and "should not be vilified for accessing the sick leave that allows them to recover and return to work healthy."
"If the (board) truly cared about the quality of student education and the welfare of school staff, it would focus on understanding and addressing why teachers are reporting higher levels of stress and burnout than ever," said Karam.
Karam called the board's hiring of private investigators a "heavy-handed and disturbing approach to employee relations." The union has heard that the board is monitoring teachers' social media accounts and that some have been followed by investigators - actions she called "dramatic" and designed to "intimidate."
Antonella Di Carlo, president of the 2,500-member Toronto Catholic teachers' secondary unit, said the board is getting tough on attendance monitoring and that she has recently heard from teachers who've received letters asking them to justify their use of sick days.
Di Carlo says these teachers' use of the sick days was legitimate.
"If it's a problem in the minority, then identify it as such," said Di Carlo, adding that teachers feel like they're being surveilled by their employer. "So you're terrorizing a profession right now ... for the minority?"
The Star asked both unions about disciplinary actions that their members have faced, or potential grievances launched by the union over sick-day cases, but they declined to provide details.
One board insider, who was not authorized to speak publicly, told the Star the use of private investigators "sends a message" to the few who misuse the system.
"The party's over," the insider said.
In 2012, the then-Liberal government cut the number of paid sick days and ended the practice of allowing teachers to bank unused days for a payout at retirement. Now all permanent school staff in Ontario are entitled to 11 sick days at full pay, and 120 days of short-term leave at 90 per cent of their salary.
When teachers are sick or on medical leave, a supply or long-term occasional covers for them. When none are available, school staff may have to give up preparation time, be reassigned, or combine classes. Principals can also use uncertified emergency replacements, such as the lunchroom monitor.
According to 2022-23 data compiled by the not-for-profit School Boards' Co-Operative Inc., the average number of total paid sick days at the Toronto Catholic board was 16.16 for elementary and 13.92 for high school teachers. At the Toronto District School Board, it was 20.04 and 17.31, respectively.
The provincial average number of paid sick days for permanent board employees was 15.5 days a year, according to the data obtained by the Star.
At the Toronto public board, total sick leave costs for 2022-23 were $213 million, which includes paying for replacement staff. The board has eight staff members working in accountability and investigations, which includes six investigators. They look into allegations of professional misconduct, including sick leave abuse. It also uses external private investigation firms to carry out workplace investigations.
"While we don't currently have data readily available on terminations due to misuse of sick leave, we have terminated staff for this reason in the past," a board spokesperson told the Star.
A recent report from the province's auditor general "found that the lack of a formal attendance management program, an insufficient number of disability caseworkers and the (Toronto District School Board's) lax processes around the collection and review of medical notes have all contributed to the increase in sick leave use and related costs," adding with 40,000 employees this is a glaring gap. (A disability case worker administers the board's short-term leave plan.)
The auditor noted that the board only conducted about six investigations a year on suspected sick-day abuse over a 10-year period, "all of which were initiated in response to complaints it received; none were initiated as a result of its own analysis."
The York Region District School Board does have a plan in place that identifies and speaks to employees who are off more than 11 days during a school year,and it has a slightly lower average sick-day count than the TDSB.
In Toronto's Catholic board, a disability case administrator is called as soon as an employee is off for five days in a row, and then emailed a medical form they must complete, with a follow-up afterward.
The auditor says the Toronto public board could save $24 million a year if its average sick days fell in line with the provincial average.
The board says it is developing an attendance support program, but that it would require funding from the province to add more disability case workers.
CAPTION: Toronto Catholic District School Board Chair Markus de Domenico said "we just have to ensure that the system is being used for what it's designed - to assist people that are ill or have a reason to be off."Deborah Karam, president of Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers, said her members "should not be vilified for accessing the sick leave that allows them to recover and return to work healthy."

Subject: Absenteeism; Investigations; Cost control; Professional misconduct; Sick leave; Employees; Teachers; Budget deficits; Social networks; School boards

Document 352 of 500

Doug Ford lite? Why Bonnie Crombie may be the best thing to happen to Ontario's NDP

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-lite-why-bonnie-crombie-may-be-best/docview/3144364539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+lite%3F+Why+Bonnie+Crombie+may+be+the+best+thing+to+happen+to+Ontario%27s+NDP&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I've been writing about the Ford family for more than 15 years and I've learned you can't "out Ford a Ford."
It's a lesson Ontario Liberals seemingly haven't grasped.
Ontario politics are experiencing a bit of political déjà vu. Think back to the 2010 Toronto mayoral election, when Rob Ford, an irascible councillor from north Etobicoke, was running an angry man's campaign, ranting against waste and things like the guy who watered the plants at city hall.
That's all Ford lore now, but George Smitherman, former Liberal MPP and high-profile minister in the McGuinty government, was his main rival. Smitherman leaned into his own angry reputation and "Furious George" nickname, trying to out Ford a Ford.
At the time I wrote a "Dear George" column aimed at Smitherman in Eye Weekly magazine, saying it felt like he was running the same campaign as Rob Ford. There was a void in the race waiting to be filled with big and positive ideas about what Toronto could be instead of just focusing on the problems in a race to the bottom. Problems must be faced, but not at the expense of offering positive visions for the future, too. Rob Ford beat Smitherman by a large margin.
A similar thing occurred in 2017. Even then Toronto was besieged by traffic woes, and John Tory, to his great credit, proposed road tolls on the Gardiner and DVP that, as proven in other cities, would reduce congestion by encouraging drivers to take transit all while raising funds for its expansion.
With an election looming, Kathleen Wynne's unpopular Liberal government snuffed out the idea, fearing it was political self-sabotage, a turn away from the somewhat progressive platform she won a majority with in 2014. While Doug Ford was not yet leader of the PCs in 2017, the lesson here is since Wynne was polling so low, why not try something big and progressive? However, the Liberal backroom establishment pushed Wynne away from policies that, I would argue, were more true to her own politics.
Of late, Bonnie Crombie's tenure as leader of the Ontario Liberal party has not been as smooth nor as easy as expected. As the Star reported last week, after a year leading the party, Crombie hasn't been able to close the gap with Doug Ford or create a buzz around her leadership.
Having just nine sitting MPPs in her caucus and not yet having a seat herself surely has something to do with this. But other new party leaders have been able to create needed buzz, especially when up against a government that should, based on the sheer number of scandals and investigations alone, be quite vulnerable.
A criticism lobbed at Crombie is that she's "Doug Ford lite," and if you consider some of her statements and platform, she's often more like - or moving toward - Ford than not. She recently came out against the federal carbon tax, said she would cut other taxes, used specific "fund the police" language and in a Trillium interview last week said identity politics are dead.
This isn't to say all of her platform is unworthy of consideration. Crombie's plan to axe the land transfer tax for first-time home buyers is quite compelling, and needing to distance herself from the unpopular federal Liberals is understandable, too. But how to do that is tricky. Move further left or get closer to Ford? The question Ontario voters must ask themselves is how different, on the whole, is Crombie from Ford?
As for identity politics, reports of its death have been greatly exaggerated. Being a centrist (even if actually tacking conservative) is absolutely a political identity, just as campaigning on a record as a moderate suburban mayor is.
However, you can't "out suburb" a Ford either, but even this is complicated. As Mississauga mayor, Crombie saw kilometres of bike lanes installed, used her "strong mayor" powers to push through fourplexes while Ford balked on doing the same across the province, and even saw a stormwater fee implemented, a good thing but falsely derided as a "rain tax" when Toronto later proposed it.
At the same time, Crombie has referred to the NDP as "radical," an amusing term considering the Ontario NDP are a fairly standard centre-left party, much to the chagrin of more ardent lefties. Political space around the centre is quite crowded, then.
All of this puts Crombie in a difficult place. Can't "out Ford a Ford" on the right, but moving left means quickly bumping into NDP territory. If she continues as she has though, there's an incredible opportunity for the NDP to fill the void. Maybe even some room to be a little radical.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie has just nine sitting MPPs in her caucus and does not yet have a seat herself at Queen's Park.

Subject: Local elections; Mayors; Identity politics; Political leadership

People: Ford, Rob
