Document 353 of 500

Rob Ford was a terrible mayor. He was also my friend: Ford left office 10 years ago. John Filion is still grappling with the legacy he left behind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rob-ford-was-terrible-mayor-he-also-my-friend/docview/3144297411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rob+Ford+was+a+terrible+mayor.+He+was+also+my+friend%3A+Ford+left+office+10+years+ago.+John+Filion+is+still+grappling+with+the+legacy+he+left+behind&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was a time, when he would raise his giant body to roar against the left and the elite, thick neck turning red, his rage all too real, when Rob Ford was the defining figure, not only in Toronto politics, but of the city itself. He was simultaneously praised and ridiculed, admired and loathed, revered and scorned. But never, I believe, fully understood. Not as a politician, and certainly not as the very raw, very real and essentially very broken human being that he was.
Rob's mayoralty ended 10 years ago this month. His final years in power were lost in a cascade of surrealistic scandal, including a crack video, police surveillance, denials, admissions, talk show jokes, and a jaw-dropping declaration about having "more than enough to eat at home." By the time John Tory succeeded him as mayor on Dec. 1, 2014, Rob was a diminished force, stripped of most of his political power, battling addictions and cancer. It was difficult to watch as it unfolded, and it's painful to reflect on it now.
Rob introduced this city to a style of polarizing politics that seemed shocking at the time. He separated us by geography and culture, education and income - but more than anything else by the depth of our personal grievances and the anger that fuels them. Today, Rob seems less like an aberration than an early warning sign of the politics of blind resentment and the global unravelling of the political order we thought we knew.
Politically, Rob was my ideological opposite. Personally, he was a friend. Ten years on, that conflict remains.
Rob and I spent 15 years together on Toronto Council. I orchestrated the vote to remove most of his powers as mayor, when I believed his wildly out-of-control behaviour left no other choice. But he didn't hold it against me - at least not for long. By the time he was mayor we had become the unlikeliest of friends. And when my friend became internationally notorious as "Toronto's crack-smoking mayor," I wrote a book about him.
Others had chronicled his chronically bad behaviour; I wanted to explain what caused it. The book's title, "The Only Average Guy," was how Rob described himself to me. He wanted to be seen as a man so supremely common that it rendered him uniquely exceptional.
It was an inherently incongruous notion, much like Rob himself. As I wrote at the time, he was "a jumble of contradictions: attention seeker and shy loner; bully and kind-hearted friend; a savvy politician who can't comprehend much of what happens around him; the self-proclaimed 'best mayor the city has ever had' and the guy with such low self-esteem that he'll stare at the floor to avoid eye contact."
His brother Doug, now the premier of Ontario, puzzled over our friendship. "I don't f--king understand it," he told me several times. Neither did I, until I realized that there were few people in Rob's life who didn't want something from him. During long council meetings, I engaged him in the only two subjects he could comfortably discuss: politics and football. Those were my entry into his heavily guarded world.
Because Rob so conspicuously carried his personal demons into the mayor's office, people always had trouble separating the person from the politician. I knew them as two different men.
The better I got to know Rob, the more I believed he wasn't in charge of his own destiny. He was born into an affluent but emotionally destitute family, ruled by a tougher-than-nails Dad who taught four children to crave his approval - and then withheld it. Three of the four siblings have had issues with drugs and alcohol. The fourth is the premier of Ontario.
Doug Ford Sr., a bumptious backbencher in the Conservative government of Mike Harris, instructed his sons to "take it to the next level," according to Doug Jr. Whatever your father accomplished, you and Rob were required to take further? I asked him in 2014.
"We have to," Doug replied.
Rob put it in more Biblical terms. "Like my Dad said, 'Many are called, few are chosen.' It's on his tombstone. The Ford family was chosen."
Unable to meet Dad's expectations in business or football, Rob felt compelled to enter politics. As a councillor, his early scandals were small stuff: He called fellow councillor Giorgio Mammoliti a "Gino boy.' He got into a drunken argument at a Leafs game, then lied about it. The controversies made good headlines but didn't earn him a following. That didn't come until his colleagues tried to sanction him for not spending his taxpayer-funded office budget, taking it instead from his own pocket.
By the time he launched a longshot bid for mayor in 2010, Rob was able to intuit that attacks from mainstream media and other politicians gained him support. Conventional political wisdom got turned upside down. Rob was overweight and dishevelled. He lacked charm, eloquence, analytical ability or education. To the astonishment of the high and mighty, all of these became political assets.
The demands of the mayoralty were always beyond Rob. Not because he was stupid - he wasn't - but because the world he could understand was incredibly small. He coped with that by making the job small too.
People who revere Rob still praise him for fielding residents' complaints until past midnight and following up with home visits. Rob saw nothing odd about a mayor who represented millions of people trying to solve their problems one person at a time. He loved greeting supporters and posing for selfies. The legend that grew around his brand of customer service made him feel capable, confident and appreciated. For a short time, that felt like enough.
But a year into his term as mayor, Rob began to lose support on council. The darkness of failure closed in on him again. Belligerence couldn't hide the pain that sent him on late night visits to his father, wailing beside his grave in a drunken stupor.
First came the public displays of stupid drunkenness. Then he found a better pain killer: crack cocaine. That led to an undercover police investigation and a blackmail attempt by the gang that sold him the crack and peddled a video of him smoking it. The three-ring circus made international headlines.
By the time Rob publicly admitted to smoking crack, "probably in one of my drunken stupors", most members of the public had had enough. So too had Toronto Council. On November 15, 2013, I introduced a motion to shift most of his powers to the deputy mayor. It passed easily.
Humiliated and alone, Rob spiralled further downwards. Eventually, in the spring of 2014, his family finally convinced him to go into rehab. He emerged eight weeks later, resuming an uphill effort to convince voters that he was a new man. But then, two months before the election, he was diagnosed with a rare form of terminal cancer, unrelated to his addictions or his weight. It was just bad luck. Rob abandoned his campaign for mayor. His brother Doug could run instead. Rob went back on the ballot for his old council seat.
The last time I saw Rob was October 2015. The cancer's progression had slowed enough for him to be back at city hall. I dropped by his office, expecting the usual gruff exterior. But the Rob who greeted me that day was soft-spoken, almost reflective.
"Rob, you seem really different," I observed.
"I feel different," he replied.
He asked me to help him choose between two U.S. networks competing to interview him. I suggested the show where a friend of his worked.
"Friends," he scoffed. "There aren't many people you can trust."
"Well, there's your family," I ventured.
"They are sometimes the worst," he replied.
He asked if I would go with him to New York for an interview. I had turned down similar invitations in the past, not knowing what scene I might find myself in. This time I decided to chance it.
"Sure, I'll go with you. We can take in a football game. Jets or Giants?"
"The Only Average Guy" was about to be published. Rob already expected it to contain unflattering scenes. Still, I worried he'd feel betrayed. "You can't write about me fairly because you're not me," he told me when I first mentioned the book.
"I'd like to read you parts of it," I offered, knowing that Rob didn't read books, not even ones about him. "I want you to know I tried to treat you fairly."
"That's OK, buddy," he said. "There are no hard feelings. I hope you sell a million copies."
He was as worried about my feelings as I was about his.
We talked about second chances and how he wanted to make the most of his, especially for his kids' sake.
Had Rob stayed healthy, would his recovery have lasted? Sadly, we'll never know.
A few days after my visit, Rob's cancer put him back in the hospital. We texted for a while, but that trailed off as he became weaker. On March 22, 2016, he died. He was 46.
I thought back to his 45th birthday, when I had compiled a CD of songs from 1969, the year he was born, and couriered it to him in rehab. The song list began with "Gimme Shelter" and ended optimistically with "Here Comes the Sun." In between, selections included "Dazed and Confused," "Fortunate Son," "Everyday People," Suspicious Minds," "Everybody's Talkin'," and the one I thought summed up Rob best, "The Boxer."
"In the clearing stands a boxer
And a fighter by his trade
And he carries the reminders
Of every glove that laid him down
Or cut him till he cried out
In his anger and his shame
'I am leaving, I am leaving'
But the fighter still remains."
Rob was touched. "John, that's the nicest thing anyone has ever done for me."
Then he asked if I would make him another playlist, and one for his Mom.
"Sure," I agreed. "What's your favourite song?"
"The same as everybody else," he answered. " 'Stairway to Heaven.' "
In the spring of 2015, when Rob was still alive but no longer mayor, and his brother was between political jobs, Doug granted me a final interview for my book. What he told me then now seems like a prophecy.
"We're only on Chapter 18 in a 30-chapter story about Rob or our family," he said. "Or myself ... I'm just at the beginning of my career. And we've got a great future ahead of us!"
It's hard now to separate Rob's legacy from Doug's. Doug entered politics in 2010, arriving at city hall as councillor as Rob became mayor. Once there, Doug asserted himself as a sort of co-mayor, the comparatively more controlled older brother.
I've learned not to underestimate Doug Ford, but without that profile, and without the roadmap of Rob's folksy pathway to success, it's unlikely he would be looking towards a third term as premier - or, I suspect, already eyeing what comes next: Prime Minister Ford? Mayor Ford II?
The more interesting question about Rob's legacy is how much he foreshadowed Donald Trump. I have found no evidence that Trump patterned himself after Rob, but the similarities of their appeal startled me so much in 2016 that I travelled to the U.S., my book on Rob in hand, to try to warn Democrats that I had seen this show before.
Still, it does Rob a disservice to compare him to a demagogue like Trump. With Trump, everything from the politics to the hair is contrived and fake. Rob's appeal was genuine. He attracted low-information voters, not by pandering to them, but because he was one himself.
There was a time when Toronto beheld a raging Rob Ford - in all his furious bluster and rumpled suit - and liked what it saw. And despite how badly it all turned out, what it saw was real.
I sure don't miss him as mayor. But I do miss him.
John Filion was a Toronto city counciLlor for 24 years. He is
the author of "The Only Average Guy: Inside the Uncommon World of Rob Ford."
CAPTION: Right Ford stands next to brother Doug as he scrums with media in late 2014.John Filion spent 15 years as Ford's political adversary and personal friend.Chris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Our health-care system doesn't need defending, it needs a major overhaul

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/our-health-care-system-doesnt-need-defending/docview/3144297052/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Our+health-care+system+doesn%27t+need+defending%2C+it+needs+a+major+overhaul&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's public health care must be defended, Dec. 6
The author of this article falls in the same trap as most Canadians do: when it comes to health care, there are only two countries in the world, Canada and the U.S. The U.S. does it all wrong and Canada does it all right. And the insurance companies are bad guys too. Why don't we look at countries where private and public health care cohabit happily and often with involvement of insurance companies? Countries like France, Germany, Switzerland, Singapore to name a few, have far better health care than Canada and the U.S. and insurance companies play a role in the quality of the health care offered to citizens of those countries. The author of this article goes on to say that Canadians have access to health care "regardless of wealth and social position." How can we reconcile this statement with the fact that more than 2 1/2 million Canadians don't have a family doctor,wait times in our emergency rooms are measured in hours, if not days, when it should be measured in minutes and sick people have to wait months for necessary tests and procedures? Our health-care system doesn't need to be defended, it needs a major overhaul to have a chance to survive.
Claude Gannon, Markham
What do the French know that Metrolinx doesn't?Notre Dame Cathedral unveils its new interior 5 years after devastating fire, Nov. 30
How is it possible for Notre Dame to be rebuilt in the scheduled five years while Metrolinx work enters its teenage years of "incompletemess?"
Dale Nicol, Trenton
Ask teachers how you can helpIn my next teacher-parent meeting, my focus will be the teacher. Here's why, Dec. 8
Ricardo Tranjan suggests that parents should ask their children's teacher how she or he is coping with the stresses in the Ontario educational system right now. The next question should be, "How can I help?" Parents can talk to their kids about how they are behaving in class and they can also write to their MMP about the need for better funding in schools.
Katy Austin, Midland, ON
What does Poilievre have to hide?Pierre Poilievre could receive and make public the names of Conservatives allegedly tied to foreign meddling, former top spy says, Dec 9
Making decisions in the best interests of Canadians as a mature and professional adult, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau steps in and provides a solution for his rival's obstinance at his own political expense. Trudeau is a selfless, gracious, and thoughtful man. He displays the characteristics of a leader often. But if a pig farts in Woodstock, it's Trudeau's fault to some. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre refuses to get his security clearance because he is playing childish games and is completely at ease with risking the safety of MPs and Canadians. He places his interests above those of the country. Why has refused to get this clearance? What does he have to hide? Why would anyone have confidence in him as a leader? I am not suggesting for anyone to necessarily vote red. However, I do think Canadians should thoughtfully consider "anything but blue" as a guiding light.
Timothy Kwiatkowski, London, ON
Latest prohibitions put our military and police in more dangerTrudeau government adds hundreds more assault-style weapons to its gun ban, Dec. 6
Some Canadians, who believe only the military and police should have access to weapons, are pleased with this latest gesture toward civilian disarmament. There's a big disconnect with that though. As a firearms advocate and volunteer coach, I've talked to dozens of police over the years and a common challenge I hear is how today's recruits need more time and training just to get comfortable handling firearms. Learning to shoot isn't something you just pick up - it takes time and starting young, in a safe and controlled environment, is the ideal way. The Liberals love to say they support our police and military, but the reality is - their continued actions, over the past 35 years, just puts our troops and law enforcement into more danger.
Fred Hoenisch, Central Saanich, B.C.
Sidewalk cyclist paid no penalty, but I am still recovering
You make a lot of fuss about bikers who are hit, but what about pedestrians who are hit by bikes? The police don't even separate pedestrian casualties from bike casualties, and they never worry about whether a pedestrian has been injured by a cyclist on the sidewalk. This is a serious issue for me, because my hip was broken by an adult cyclist who was afraid of the road and so rode his e-bike on the sidewalk. He paid no penalty, but I spent a month and a half in (they called it a hospital) and I have still not recovered.
Andy Turnbull, Toronto
Using the notwithstanding clause is inhumanCompassion takes a back seat in Ford's clear out the encampments strategy, Dec. 9
The homeless encampments need to be improved. Forcing these homeless humans out to another place is not a solution. I would suggest a large tent with showers, washrooms, a place to do laundry, a place where they can get a meal and where people can drop off food, clothing and some cash. Using the notwithstanding clause is inhuman. These are people not garbage.
Brian Mellor, Midland

Document 355 of 500

The Nobel-winning economist who's reclaiming 'freedom' from the right

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nobel-winning-economist-whos-reclaiming-freedom/docview/3144296991/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Nobel-winning+economist+who%27s+reclaiming+%27freedom%27+from+the+right&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Joseph Stiglitz seeks to redefine "freedom" to mean liberty for all to enjoy a good life.
For the past few decades, freedom has been perverted to mean liberty for free markets to do as they please with minimal control by the governments that represent those affected, and afflicted, by such liberties.
"The current conservative reading of what freedom means," Stiglitz writes in his new book, "The Road to Freedom: Economics and the Good Society," "is superficial, misguided, and ideologically motivated."
Mainstream politics continues to subscribe to the "trickle-down" theory of how wealth eventually makes its way to the masses, though that notion has been thoroughly debunked.
Dating from the era of Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher, political leaders in liberal democracies have persisted in their belief that free markets know best.
The idea - and it is a mere idea - that unfettered markets will self-correct their harmful excesses is widely held in mainstream politics and the media.
That way of governing mostly benefits the advantaged, a minority in society. And it conflicts with the moral sentiments of Adam Smith, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Franklin Roosevelt and Tommy Douglas.
"The Right claims to be the defender of freedom," writes Stiglitz, a Nobel laureate economist. But in practice that turns out to mean "the freedom to exploit," Stiglitz says, "reducing the freedoms of most citizens."
"A person facing extremes of want and fear is not free," Stiglitz writes.
Freedom as neo-conservatives define it is a means by which the privileged escape taxation and regulation.
It is a bitter freedom for most, manifesting itself in societal failure to provide economic opportunity for all, and to protect against job loss from globalization, industrial pollution, oligopoly control of prices, Big Tech intrusions on personal privacy, and underfunded health care and education for lack of tax revenue.
Stiglitz proposes a "progressive capitalism" that remedies income inequality, makes high-quality education and health care accessible to all, restrains corporate power, and sees a larger role in society for co-operatives and non-profit groups.
Stiglitz has long been engaged in this egalitarian fight.
His latest book is among his best. It summarizes Stiglitz's decades of first-hand experience at the highest levels of government and commerce. And it draws on both historical and recent events, including the pandemic and the resentment that sustains Trumpism.
Stiglitz chronicles the relaxation of financial-industry regulations that caused the Wall Street meltdown and resulting Great Recession, in which the markets did not correct themselves. They had to be bailed out with $800 billion of taxpayer funds in the U.S. alone, after destroying 400,000 jobs in Canada and nine million in the U.S.
What Stiglitz calls for, a genuine freedom for all, can be called revolutionary, though the man himself, now 81, is no renegade.
Stiglitz is a former chair of the Council of Economic Advisers in the Clinton administration and was later chief economist at the World Bank.
Stiglitz despairs that even centre-left leaders like Clinton and former British prime minister Tony Blair bought into the neo-con mantra that unrestrained free markets alone can deliver mass prosperity.
Instances of trying to correct imbalances between rich and poor have been infrequent. And they generally meet with a chorus of disapproval from what John Kenneth Galbraith called the "culture of contentment" - the affluent minority satisfied with things as they are.
The Trudeau government was criticized this year for increasing the capital-gains inclusion taxation rate, a measure affecting the contented class.
"We just don't think it's right," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said, "that a student, or an electrician or a teacher be paying taxes on 100 per cent of their income while others have the opportunities to use accountants and pay taxes on only 50 per cent of that income."
In our society of relatively unfettered free markets, employers have scrapped defined-benefit pensions. With weak competition protections, Big Business merges into oligopolies whose power rivals that of governments. And business lobbyists extract billions of dollars from taxpayers in corporate welfare.
Language is powerful. Whoever controls the conversation controls the society.
What's needed to ensure a wider and more comprehensive well-being in society, Stiglitz argues, is for progressives to take back "freedom" and restore its real meaning.
"The true advocates of deep, meaningful freedom are aligned with the progressive movement both in the U.S. and abroad," Stiglitz writes.
"They and the centre-left parties that represent them need to reclaim the freedom agenda."
Neo-liberalism is based on fear of subjugation by government and not a little greed. Yet today's prevailing free-market, anti-government doctrine has "led to massive inequality and provided fertile ground for populists," Stiglitz writes.
Political conditions being what they now are, the next few years portend overcast skies for progressives.
"The Road to Freedom" is a balm and a reform blueprint for progressives as they regroup.
They can also take some comfort from the adage of economist Abraham Rotstein of the University of Toronto that "All dogmas have their day."
CAPTION: U.S. economist Joseph Stiglitz is seen in a file photo taken on Jan. 31, 2017. What's needed to ensure a wider and more comprehensive well-being in society, Stiglitz argues in his new book, "The Road to Freedom: Economics and the Good Society," is for progressives to take back "freedom" and restore its real meaning, David Olive writes.

Subject: Taxation; Economists; Free markets; Decades; Politics; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxation Economists Free markets

Location: United States--US

Title: The Nobel-winning economist who's reclaiming 'freedom' from the right

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 14, 2024

Section: business

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 356 of 500

Pierre Poilievre's love affair with labour hits a fork in the road

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-love-affair-with-labour-hits/docview/3144296790/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+love+affair+with+labour+hits+a+fork+in+the+road&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like teenage infatuation, it burned bright and fizzled quickly, with one party barely aware the love affair had begun. This week, the Conservatives appeared to mark the end of their outward courtship of organized labour.
For a while, it seemed Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was serious about standing up for workers' rights. He'd picked up the ball from former leader Erin O'Toole, reaching out to blue-collar workers, especially those in private-sector unions, hoping to mimic Ontario Progressive Conservative Premier Doug Ford's early success.
In May, the Conservative party helped unanimously pass the Liberal government's anti-scab legislation, Bill C-58. "Knock me over with a feather!" Unifor National President Lana Payne said in expressing her surprise. Poilievre's office pledged to keep the measures - which kick in next year - if the Tories form government.
This fall, in the lead up to a potentially disruptive Air Canada strike, Poilievre sided with the pilots, saying they were just looking for the same compensation as their American counterparts. "We are not going to support pre-empting those negotiations," he told reporters. It was a clear reversal from his 2012 position, when he supported then-prime minister Stephen Harper's pre-emptive use of back-to-work legislation for those same pilots.
In the summer, Poilievre declined to weigh in on the lockouts affecting employees of the National Railway Company (CN) and Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CPKC), even after Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon asked the Canada Industrial Relations Board (CIRB) to impose binding arbitration and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked the Liberal government for caving to "corporate greed." Conservative MPs were told to keep their mouths shut.
Then in September, Poilievre tried to distance himself from his anti-union record. In a statement sent to the National Post, he promised not to introduce legislation he'd supported while in Harper's cabinet. "Hundreds of visits to workshops, factories, union halls and training centres have taught me a lot and helped me evolve my thinking," Poilievre is quoted telling the paper. "A common sense Conservative government will not introduce or pass bills C-377, C-525 or right to work laws. Period. This commitment will be written in my election platform."
Those bills were sponsored by Conservative MPs and were an attack against organized labour. Bill C-377 placed onerous, overarching, expensive reporting requirements on unions, drowning them in red tape, forcing not just transparency disclosures on union leadership but on union suppliers, too. Bill C-525 made it more difficult for workers to unionize by replacing the card-check system with a secret-ballot vote. A move that sounds good but studies show opens the door to more threats and intimidation from employers, and leads to declines in union membership. When the Liberals were elected in 2015, they repealed the laws - and Poilievre spoke against it in the Commons.
The Liberals, the NDP, the Bloc Québécois, the Greens, and many labour groups never believed Poilievre's support for workers was anything more than an electoral ploy. They frequently suggested the Conservative leader wasn't as committed to workers' rights as he portrays himself to be.
This week, Poilievre and his Conservative MP Blaine Calkins showed the skepticism is valid.
It wasn't just that on Wednesday Poilievre called on the prime minister to end the mail strike by forcing Canada Post and the Canadian Union of Postal Workers to sit down and "get a deal." (The Liberals initially described the move as "hostile" to workers but on Friday, MacKinnon declared the parties were at an impasse and asked the Canada Industrial Relations Board to order workers back to work until May 22, during which time a commission would be established to help resolve the labour dispute.)
It wasn't just that Poilievre decried the large number of strikes this year, portraying worker action in a negative light. Despite the tension, Payne counts 98 disputes since August 2022, Unifor's president said she's tried to stress to "every politician who wants to listen" that strikes have "a very positive impact overall in terms of progress for working people.
"These companies don't come to the table wanting to hand over things," she said. Workers took advantage of the moment before them - with high inflation and low unemployment, she said, and helped push the envelope on pensions, wages, and benefits.
The next telling moment will be how Poilievre reacts to a quip by Calkins in the House of Commons.
As MacKinnon slammed Poilievre on Monday for supporting C-525 and C-377, and erroneously accusing him of being the "author" of the most oppressive pieces of anti-labour legislation, Calkins piped up from his seat on the far south corner of the chamber to proudly claim ownership and defend the bill's content. "That was my bill," he yelled at MacKinnon, about C-525. "Wanting more democracy?" he said, smiling. "How draconian," he mocked.
Far from distancing himself, here was a Poilievre shadow minister praising the controversial legislation. Calkins's thinking on labour, and those of the Conservative MPs laughing around him, doesn't appear to have "evolved."
Poilievre takes great pains to muzzle his MPs. They are told to avoid commenting, ignore media requests, and rat on colleagues who speak to reporters. Whether he now responds to Calkins's outburst by remaining silent or disciplining him will be a strong indicator of whether the Conservative leader still has any love for organized labour, or if the honeymoon is truly over.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Dec. 11, 2024.

Document 357 of 500

Minister touts credit rating: S&P Global upgrades the province's status for the first time since 2001

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/minister-touts-credit-rating/docview/3144296783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Minister+touts+credit+rating%3A+S%26amp%3BP+Global+upgrades+the+province%27s+status+for+the+first+time+since+2001&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Even in a time of economic uncertainty, you have to give credit where credit is due.
Despite the threat of a trade war with the U.S. and rising unemployment, Ontario got some very positive fiscal news this year. The province's credit rating was upgraded to "AA-" from "A+(P)" by S&P Global last week, the first such improvement from that agency since 2001.
That followed a credit upgrade in June to "AA" from "AA (low) (P)" by Morningstar DBRS, which had last boosted Ontario's ranking in 2006.
For Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy that means much more than just an alphabet soup of ratings decipherable only to those on Bay Street and Wall Street.
"Bond holders are looking at who is managing their credit most effectively. It leads to lower interest costs," Bethlenfalvy said in an interview with the Star this week.
"Ultimately, what that means is that your borrowing costs ... are lower," said the treasurer.
According to the most recent public accounts, Ontario's interest expenditure dropped from $12.4 billion in 2022-23 to $11.4 billion last fiscal year - a savings of $1 billion.
"That's $1 billion you have to pay down debt, you can invest in health care, you've got options for your priorities," he said.
"When you have to borrow in the bond markets - which we continue to do, primarily for infrastructure because we've got an ambitious plan - investors have choices," said Bethlenfalvy, referring to the billions the province is borrowing to fund subways, highways, hospitals and other projects.
"So how do you stand out amongst all those that are issuing bonds and get the lowest rate? They look at your fiscal track record, they look at your credit ratings, they look at a host of factors. This is a vote of confidence in Ontario."
That's especially important to Bethlenfalvy, who was co-president of DBRS when the rating agency downgraded Ontario to "AA (low)" from "AA" in 2009 after the global financial crash.
"I was there, of course, and now, whoops, the shoe is on the other foot," the treasurer mused.
"Having worked in the capital markets and at a credit-rating agency - working both in New York and Toronto - I've seen capital markets, I've seen sovereigns and how they manage their debt. And I think it still matters," he said.
"It matters because you don't know what's around the corner, whether it's a pandemic or ... a financial crisis (like) 2008. We're dealing with economic uncertainty all the time so I think it's wise to be prudent on the fiscal side and to have targets - and that's what rating agencies like."
Credit raters also like fiscal capacity so Premier Doug Ford's frequent boast that he has "never raised a tax" is not unhelpful because it suggests the province has untapped revenue streams if need be.
Bethlenfalvy, who has no plans to raise any taxes, counters that by noting rating agencies "like it when you grow your economy, because that increases your tax revenues."
He emphasized that S&P upgraded Ontario's rating after he announced the province would spend $3 billion to send $200 "rebate" cheques to 15 million Ontarians in the new year despite running a $6.6-billion budget deficit.
"If I can point out, under the previous government - 15 years - taxes were raised and credit ratings fell," said Bethlenfalvy, referring to the Liberals of premiers Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Uncertainty; Rating services; Borrowing; Ratings & rankings; Cost control; Credit ratings; Capital markets

Document 358 of 500

Toronto mayor still believes housing crisis can be solved: Progress is happening, she says, even if it's not visible to most Torontonians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/toronto-mayor-still-believes-housing-crisis-can/docview/3144285339/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+mayor+still+believes+housing+crisis+can+be+solved%3A+Progress+is+happening%2C+she+says%2C+even+if+it%27s+not+visible+to+most+Torontonians&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To read up on the recent history of housing in Toronto is to feel an overwhelming sense of déjà vu. Consider a few headlines from this newspaper: "It's Time for Meaningful Action on Housing Crisis," "City's Housing Crisis Has Roots Decades Deep," and "Housing Crisis Damages Children." All of these articles appeared between 2000 and 2002, when Mel Lastman was mayor. But if the problem was bad in Lastman's day, it's exponentially worse today. When Olivia Chow took office in July 2023 as the amalgamated city's fifth mayor, she promised to tackle housing and traffic - and to succeed where her predecessors had failed. In an interview, edited for clarity and length, she reflected on what success will look like, and what it will take to get there.
There are problems to be fixed and problems to be managed. Which one is housing?
We can end the housing crisis. I remain optimistic about that. For three decades, governments didn't build housing. Now, we're fixing the old ideology that says the government has no role to play in housing.
Since becoming mayor, you've done a lot on the housing file, from streamlining the permitting and approvals process to beefing up tenant protection programs. How do you reconcile the fact that, despite everything you've done, we're still in this crisis?
International financial markets have high interest rates, so builders are saying, "Financially, we can't move forward." That's why the City of Toronto had said, "There will be no development charges for purpose-built rental housing and a discount on property taxes if 20 per cent of your development comprises affordable housing." Yes, there is a crisis. But each building we open, each tenant that moves in, provides a source of hope.
The Wilson Heights project, a 1,5000-unit development on a parking lot by Wilson Subway Station, had been stuck for five years. But we're starting to build.
In two years, our interest rates will likely be a lot lower. You will have lots of pent up demand. The City of Toronto has said, "Here are parking lots. Here is land you can build on." There's a wave coming.
When you talk about building on parking lots you're surely referring to Housing Now, a program introduced in 2019. Under Housing Now, city properties would be made available to developers, who, in return, would ensure that a third of their units would be priced affordably. Less than two months before you took office as mayor, CBCreported that Housing Now had created zero new affordable units. What went wrong?
No one was really in charge of approving things.
Is someone in charge now?
There's one person now, at the Development Review Division. It used to be that you had to get separate approvals for planning, legal, building, engineers, real estate, etc. Now you can do everything in one stop.
Was bureaucratic red tape the fatal flaw in Housing Now?
The other piece was money. Without federal and provincial money, there aren't enough financial incentives for developers to build.
You recently requested, along with other GTA mayors, that all promised grants from the federal Housing Accelerator Fund be paid out right away. There may soon be a change of power in Ottawa. Is that your reason for requesting the funding now?
We're doing the building. We're providing incentives for builders. We've got land. We've got developers. Why wait?
So this isn't about insulating the city against a potential change in government? What happens if there's a change in government and the commitments get changed?
I won't speculate. We have a conservative provincial government. They're partnering with us on any number of projects, like Na-Me-Res, which offers supportive housing for Indigenous men.
But do you expect that a Poilievre government will be as amenable to co-operation as Doug Ford has been?
It doesn't matter what party you're in or whether you are Prime Minister Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre. Everyone's now saying that the government has a role to play in housing.
So, when Poilievre talks about axing the Liberals' housing grants is that just idle rhetoric?
They might not keep the Liberals' exact program. They might have another program.
You're saying that, given that housing is a major issue, Poilievre is going to have to do something big?
Absolutely. Pierre Poilievre understands the need for housing.
In the recent past, when governments got involved in housing, it was at the policy level. They might change zoning or permitting rules. But you want governments to step up and actually build homes. Is this happening?
We have five projects that we're building. Historically, in Toronto, the city government had always built homes, until the federal and provincial governments cancelled these programs. We built over 30,000 units of housing between the late '70s and the '90s.
You've seen this phenomenon in the U.S. where, as soon as a city decides to start building, there are a bunch of regulations that kick in. Councillors say things like, "All the work needs to be done by unionized builders," or "We're going to hold this building to high environmental standards." Predictably, the price of city-built units goes up and the number of units that gets built goes down. Is this something you worry about?
No, because we've had a good track record of building in the '80s and '90s. If anyone is saying the city doesn't know how to build, they should go to the Regent Park building at 175 Oak St. We just opened it. It's beautiful. And it didn't blow up anybody's piggy bank.
Let's talk about traffic. I have a similar question to the one I asked about housing. Are we looking to fix the problem or manage it?
We're looking to fix it. We just got the funds to order 55 new subway cars for the Bloor-Danforth Line. With new cars, we will put in advanced control, using GPS so that trains can come more frequently. What does this have to do with traffic? A lot. The Bloor-Danforth Line can carry three times the number of people as the Gardiner Expressway.
Some people argue that transit doesn't help with congestion as much as it should because of the problem of "induced demand." When subway services improve, some commuters will switch from driving to taking transit, thereby freeing up road space. But then, other people, seeing that the roads are now freer, will get in their car and drive. Will better transit just encourage other people to drive?
Not if it's faster for them to take public transit.
So is the phenomenon of induced demand not a real thing?
Look at Hong Kong; it's a dense place. People say, "If I can save 20 minutes by taking public transit, I'll do that."
London, England, has successfully tackled congestion. But that city has congestion pricing to disincentivize road use. Surely, part of London's success is due not only to quality transit but also to the fact that there's a financial cost to driving in the city.Do you see a possibility of having congestion pricing in the future?
You'd have to talk to Premier Ford.
Could I get your opinion on congestion pricing, though?
As mayor, I can help fund more frequent bus services. I have control over that. But I have no control whatsoever on road pricing. If you look at our congestion management plan, we're hiring a lot more of those wonderful traffic agents. There are traffic wardens standing in the road, moving traffic along. We upped the fine for obstructing the flow of traffic to $450 and upped enforcement too. We are requesting to use more video cameras to charge people who are breaking the law. We are co-ordinating on road construction so you don't see people coming in and digging up utilities and then digging them up all over again when there's more work to be done.
Do you think these solutions can, collectively, fix the traffic problem in the absence of congestion pricing?
It's hard to say. But we can only do what we have control over.
Let's say, a decade from now, you're in your 12th year as mayor. In this hypothetical future, are the housing and traffic crises just distant unhappy memories?
In 2034, people will find housing more affordable. The neighbourhoods will be more complete with community centres, greenspaces and libraries. There'll be more alternatives to driving. Energy bills won't be as high because we're more resilient in terms of climate. Fewer people will use the food bank. And kids will have a really good lunch program in school.
I find this future hard to envision because we've been dealing with these problems for so, so long.
But I've only been mayor for a year and a half.
Simon Lewsen is an Ontario-based freelance journalist.
CREDIT: Simon Lewsen contributor;

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-care-system-is-need-major-overhaul-derived/docview/3144285307/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+care+system+is+in+need+of+a+major+overhaul+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Canada%27s+public+health+care+must+be+defended%2C+Dec.+6+Notre+Dame+Cathedral+unveils+its+new+interior+5+years+after+devastating+fire%2C+Nov.+30+Trudeau+government+adds+hundreds+more+assault-style+weapons+to+its+gun+ban%2C+Dec.+6+In+my+next+teacher-parent+meeting%2C+my+focus+will+be+the+teacher.+Here%27s+why%2C+Dec.+8+Pierre+Poilievre+could+receive+and+make+public+the+names+of+Conservatives+allegedly+tied+to+foreign+meddling%2C+former+top+spy+says%2C+Dec.+9&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care system is in need of a major overhaul
Canada's public health care must be defended, Dec. 6
The author of this article falls in the same trap as most Canadians do: when it comes to health care, there are only two countries in the world, Canada and the U.S. The U.S. does it all wrong and Canada does it all right. And the insurance companies are bad guys too. Why don't we look at countries where private and public health care cohabit happily and often with involvement of insurance companies? Countries like France, Germany, Switzerland, Singapore, to name a few, have far better health care than Canada and the U.S., and insurance companies play a role in the quality of the health care offered to citizens of those countries. The author of this article goes on to say that Canadians have access to health care "regardless of wealth and social position." How can we reconcile this statement with the fact that more than 21/2 million Canadians don't have a family doctor,wait times in our emergency rooms are measured in hours, if not days, when it should be measured in minutes, and sick people have to wait months for necessary tests and procedures? Our health care system doesn't need to be defended, it needs a major overhaul to have a chance to survive.
Claude Gannon, Markham
What do the French know that Metrolinx doesn't?
Notre Dame Cathedral unveils its new interior 5 years after devastating fire, Nov. 30
How is it possible for Notre Dame to be rebuilt in the scheduled five years while Metrolinx work enters its teenage years of "incompletemess?"
Dale Nicol, Trenton, Ont.
Latest prohibitions put military, police in more danger
Trudeau government adds hundreds more assault-style weapons to its gun ban, Dec. 6
Some Canadians, who believe only the military and police should have access to weapons, are pleased with this latest gesture toward civilian disarmament. There's a big disconnect with that though. As a firearms advocate and volunteer coach, I've talked to dozens of police over the years and a common challenge I hear is how today's recruits need more time and training just to get comfortable handling firearms. Learning to shoot isn't something you just pick up - it takes time and starting young, in a safe and controlled environment, is the ideal way. The Liberals love to say they support our police and military, but the reality is - their continued actions, over the past 35 years, just puts our troops and law enforcement into more danger.
Fred Hoenisch, Central Saanich, B.C.
Ask teachers how you can help
In my next teacher-parent meeting, my focus will be the teacher. Here's why, Dec. 8
Ricardo Tranjan suggests that parents should ask their children's teacher how she or he is coping with the stresses in the Ontario educational system right now. The next question should be, "How can I help?" Parents can talk to their kids about how they are behaving in class and they can also write to their MPP about the need for better funding in schools.
Katy Austin, Midland, Ont.
Poilievre must be hiding something
Pierre Poilievre could receive and make public the names of Conservatives allegedly tied to foreign meddling, former top spy says, Dec. 9
Making decisions in the best interests of Canadians as a mature and professional adult, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau steps in and provides a solution for his rival's obstinance at his own political expense. Trudeau is a selfless, gracious and thoughtful man. He displays the characteristics of a leader often. But if a pig farts in Woodstock, it's Trudeau's fault to some. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre refuses to get his security clearance because he is playing childish games and is completely at ease with risking the safety of MPs and Canadians. He places his interests above those of the country. Why has refused to get this clearance? What does he have to hide? Why would anyone have confidence in him as a leader? I am not suggesting for anyone to necessarily vote red. However, I do think Canadians should thoughtfully consider "anything but blue" as a guiding light.
Timothy Kwiatkowski, London, Ont.
Sidewalk e-bike rider hit me and paid no penalty
You make a lot of fuss about bikers who are hit, but what about pedestrians who are hit by bikes? The police don't even separate pedestrian casualties from bike casualties, and they never worry about whether a pedestrian has been injured by a cyclist on the sidewalk. This is a serious issue for me, because my hip was broken by an adult cyclist who was afraid of the road and so rode his e-bike on the sidewalk. He paid no penalty, but I spent a month and a half in (they called it a hospital) and I have still not recovered.
Andy Turnbull, Toronto

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Health care system is in need of a major overhaul ...[Derived Headline ] :   Canada's public health care must be defended, Dec. 6 Notre Dame Cathedral unveils its new interior 5 years after devastating fire, Nov. 30 Trudeau government adds hundreds more assault-style weapons to its gun ban, Dec. 6 In my next teacher-parent meeting, my focus will be the teacher. Ford says energy threat got Trump's attention: Deputy PM says government needs to 'be candid' with U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-says-energy-threat-got-trumps-attention/docview/3144285297/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+says+energy+threat+got+Trump%27s+attention%3A+Deputy+PM+says+government+needs+to+%27be+candid%27+with+U.S.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says his threat to "cut off" Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. has forced president-elect Donald Trump to sit up and pay attention.
In response to Trump's plan to slap 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian goods next month, Ford warned the country's most populous province was prepared to retaliate, provoking a response from the incoming president.
"It was the first time president-elect Trump said, 'I want to make a deal with Canada.' That's good news," the premier said Friday at the province's emergency operations centre in North York.
In separate news conferences, both Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc echoed Ford in the need to be hawkish with Trump.
"We have to be candid about the reality of the incoming U.S. administration. This is an administration that believes in America first. This is an administration which is proudly an economic nationalist administration," said Freeland, who conferred with Ford Thursday night, at the Toronto Stock Exchange.
"This is an administration which openly has a strategy of creating economic uncertainty outside the United States as a strategy to discourage investment anywhere other than the United States. We're realistic. We recognize that that is the case."
LeBlanc, speaking in Fredericton, said "I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself," the public safety minister said, acknowledging "other premiers have offered views on what might be the appropriate response."
While some premiers have rejected Ford's tough talk about restricting energy exports - notably Alberta's Danielle Smith and Quebec's François Legault - the Ontarian shrugged off their stance.
"If it comes to Alberta or Quebec, that's their choice. They believe in diplomacy - good luck," the premier said.
Freeland, who will deliver a fall economic statement Monday detailing $1 billion in new spending on border security, stressed that "Canada will not escalate and Canada will not back down."
On the floor of the New York Stock Exchange on Thursday, Trump was asked about Ford's comments.
"That's OK if he does that. That's fine," said the president-elect, who has vowed to impose the 25 per cent levies on Canadian and Mexican goods upon his Jan. 20 inauguration.
"The United States is subsidizing Canada and we shouldn't have to do that," added Trump, who wants Canada and Mexico to tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling. "And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than $100 billion a year. We shouldn't have to be doing that."
That appeared to be a reference to the amount Trump feels Ottawa needs to boost military spending in order to achieve its NATO target.
Asked whether the LCBO should stop selling American wine, beer and spirits if Trump imposes tariffs, the premier said, "we'll use every tool in our tool box" as in the 2019 trade war with Trump.
"We put a tariff on bourbon last time. The LCBO is one of the largest purchasers of alcohol in the entire world, but I'd prefer not to do any of this. I'd prefer to make a deal," Ford said.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 361 of 500

Poilievre's love affair with labour tested

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievres-love-affair-with-labour-tested/docview/3144285186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+love+affair+with+labour+tested&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like teenage infatuation, it burned bright and fizzled quickly, with one party barely aware the love affair had begun. This week, the Conservatives appeared to mark the end of their outward courtship of organized labour.
For a while, it seemed Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was serious about standing up for workers' rights. He'd picked up the ball from former leader Erin O'Toole, reaching out to blue-collar workers, especially those in private-sector unions, hoping to mimic Ontario Progressive Conservative Premier Doug Ford's early success.
In May, the Conservative party helped unanimously pass the Liberal government's anti-scab legislation, Bill C-58.
"Knock me over with a feather!" Unifor National President Lana Payne said in expressing her surprise. Poilievre's office pledged to keep the measures - which kick in next year - if the Tories form government.
This fall, in the lead up to a potentially disruptive Air Canada strike, Poilievre sided with the pilots, saying they were just looking for the same compensation as their American counterparts. "We are not going to support pre-empting those negotiations," he told reporters. It was a clear reversal from his 2012 position, when he supported then-prime minister Stephen Harper's pre-emptive use of back-to-work legislation for those same pilots.
In the summer, Poilievre declined to weigh in on the lockouts affecting employees of the National Railway Company (CN) and Canadian Pacific Kansas City Limited (CPKC), even after Labour Minister Steve MacKinnon asked the Canada Industrial Relations Board (CIRB) to impose binding arbitration and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked the Liberal government for caving to "corporate greed." Conservative MPs were told to keep their mouths shut.
Then in September, Poilievre tried to distance himself from his anti-union record. In a statement sent to the National Post, he promised not to introduce legislation he'd supported while in Harper's cabinet. "Hundreds of visits to workshops, factories, union halls and training centres have taught me a lot and helped me evolve my thinking," Poilievre is quoted telling the paper. "A common sense Conservative government will not introduce or pass bills C-377, C-525 or right to work laws. Period. This commitment will be written in my election platform."
Those bills were sponsored by Conservative MPs and were an attack against organized labour. Bill C-377 placed onerous, overarching, expensive reporting requirements on unions, drowning them in red tape, forcing not just transparency disclosures on union leadership but on union suppliers, too. Bill C-525 made it more difficult for workers to unionize by replacing the card-check system with a secret-ballot vote. A move that sounds good, but studies show opens the door to more threats and intimidation from employers, and leads to declines in union membership. When the Liberals were elected in 2015, they repealed the laws - and Poilievre spoke against it in the Commons.
The Liberals, the NDP, the Bloc Québécois, the Greens, and many labour groups never believed Poilievre's support for workers was anything more than an electoral ploy. They frequently suggested the Conservative leader wasn't as committed to workers' rights as he portrays himself to be.
This week, Poilievre and his Conservative MP Blaine Calkins showed the skepticism is valid.
It wasn't just that on Wednesday Poilievre called on the prime minister to end the mail strike by forcing Canada Post and the Canadian Union of Postal Workers to sit down and "get a deal." (The Liberals initially described the move as "hostile" to workers, but on Friday, MacKinnon declared the parties were at an impasse and asked the Canada Industrial Relations Board to order workers back to work until May 22, during which time a commission would be established to help resolve the labour dispute.)
It wasn't just that Poilievre decried the large number of strikes this year, portraying worker action in a negative light. Despite the tension, Payne counts 98 disputes since August 2022, Unifor's president said she's tried to stress to "every politician who wants to listen" that strikes have "a very positive impact overall in terms of progress for working people.
"These companies don't come to the table wanting to hand over things," she said. Workers took advantage of the moment before them - with high inflation and low unemployment, she said, and helped push the envelope on pensions, wages, and benefits.
The next telling moment will be how Poilievre reacts to a quip by Calkins in the House of Commons.
As MacKinnon slammed Poilievre on Monday for supporting C-525 and C-377, and erroneously accusing him of being the "author" of the most oppressive pieces of anti-labour legislation, Calkins piped up from his seat on the far south corner of the chamber to proudly claim ownership and defend the bill's content. "That was my bill," he yelled at MacKinnon, about C-525. "Wanting more democracy?" he said, smiling. "How draconian," he mocked.
Far from distancing himself, here was a Poilievre shadow minister praising the controversial legislation. Calkins's thinking on labour, and those of the Conservative MPs laughing around him, doesn't appear to have "evolved."
Poilievre takes great pains to muzzle his MPs. They are told to avoid commenting, ignore media requests, and rat on colleagues who speak to reporters. Whether he now responds to Calkins's outburst by remaining silent or disciplining him will be a strong indicator of whether the Conservative leader still has any love for organized labour, or if the honeymoon is truly over.
CAPTION: This week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre appeared to mark the end of his courtship of organized labour, Althia Raj writes.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 362 of 500

Boards hire PIs to probe misuse of sick days: Teachers warned of surveillance as schools struggle with rising absenteeism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/boards-hire-pis-probe-misuse-sick-days/docview/3144285056/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Boards+hire+PIs+to+probe+misuse+of+sick+days%3A+Teachers+warned+of+surveillance+as+schools+struggle+with+rising+absenteeism&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School boards, struggling with skyrocketing costs of absenteeism, are turning to private investigators to help uncover sick leave abuse - and, in some cases, firing educators.
One union local in Toronto recently warned members to only book off when ill because they could be under surveillance.
"It is essential to use sick days only as intended under the collective agreement," says a memo from Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers to its more than 4,000 members.
"The board has hired private investigators to observe members, attempting to identify what they deem as misuse of sick days," says the memo, obtained by the Star. "This has led to disciplinary actions, up to and including termination. Protect yourself by adhering to the (collective agreement) guidelines."
The Catholic board employs two such investigators. Sources told the Star that earlier this year, up to five teachers at one school were let go after calling in sick and spending the day at a Niagara casino. The school's vice-principal, who reportedly knew of the outing, was also fired, sources say. (The sources were not authorized to speak publicly.)
The Toronto public board also has an investigations unit, and says it has, on occasion, hired outside private investigators to look into claims of misconduct, including sick leave abuse, and that employees have lost their job as a result.
Education unions have called the use of investigators "disturbing," but school boards across the province say that while the vast majority of teachers use the days as allowed - for illness, injury or medical appointments - they can't keep up with the escalating costs.
Absenteeism has also exacerbated the province's already dire shortage of educators, leaving schools scrambling to find fill-ins, and students without a permanent teacher.
Last June, the province sent boards guidelines for implementing attendance support programs, noting that "absenteeism not only creates operational challenges for school boards to meet educational priorities, but it also adds financial pressure on school boards."
Last week, the provincial auditor general's annual report noted that the Toronto District School Board - the largest in the country - could save $24 million if it better managed staff absences.
At the Toronto Catholic District School Board, unfunded sick-leave costs account for about two-thirds of its $66-million budget deficit.
Chair Markus de Domenico said the board must "keep the integrity of the system and ... deal with the funding issue we have."
Staff "are very professional and dedicated ... but we are also responsible to the public to be good stewards of our finances and we just have to ensure that the system is being used for what it's designed - to assist people that are ill or have a reason to be off," he told the Star.
"There are, as there are in any large organizations, occasionally people that choose to abuse that. But they don't represent the very vast majority of our staff."
In recent years, some cases where teachers were on sick leave but posting vacation photos on social media have been uncovered, or teachers spotted shopping at Yorkdale mall on a professional development day.
Roseline Dorcin, a Toronto public board teacher, was placed on medical leave in September 2019, but after parents spotted social media posts showing her living in Montreal and campaigning for the federal election, the board hired private investigators.
Dorcin, who was found to be working at a Montreal school while collecting sick pay, was later fired from the board and stripped of her teaching certificate by the Ontario College of Teachers.
The Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers union has called hiring investigators "heavy-handed" and "deeply concerning," but the board says sick leave is an "enormous contributor" to its deficit - accounting for $44 million of its $66-million budget shortfall.
The Toronto Catholic board is "committed to ensuring staff benefits are used appropriately," said Shazia Vlahos, the board's chief communications officer of policy, government relations and strategy.
"We take allegations of sick leave misuse seriously and are implementing reasonable measures to address such concerns," including labour relations investigators looking into allegations of professional misconduct by employees, she said. She added that "we hope this approach will allow us to use the anticipated savings from these unfunded costs in a prudent manner to further support student learning and address current classroom instructional needs."
Deborah Karam, president of Toronto Elementary Catholic Teachers, said her members want to be in the classroom and "should not be vilified for accessing the sick leave that allows them to recover and return to work healthy."
"If the (board) truly cared about the quality of student education and the welfare of school staff, it would focus on understanding and addressing why teachers are reporting higher levels of stress and burnout than ever," said Karam.
Karam called the board's hiring of private investigators a "heavy-handed and disturbing approach to employee relations." The union has heard that the board is monitoring teachers' social media accounts and that some have been followed by investigators - actions she called "dramatic" and designed to "intimidate."
Antonella Di Carlo, president of the 2,500-member Toronto Catholic teachers secondary unit, said the board is getting tough on attendance monitoring and that she has recently heard from teachers who've received letters asking them to justify their use of sick days.
Di Carlo says these teachers' use of the sick days was legitimate.
"If it's a problem in the minority, then identify it as such," said Di Carlo, adding that teachers feel like they're being surveilled by their employer. "So you're terrorizing a profession right now ... for the minority?"
The Star asked both unions about disciplinary actions that their members have faced, or potential grievances launched by the union over sick-day cases, but they declined to provide details.
One board insider, who was not authorized to speak publicly, told the Star the use of private investigators "sends a message" to the few who misuse the system.
"The party's over," the insider said.
In 2012, the then Liberal government cut the number of paid sick days and ended the practice of allowing teachers to bank unused days for a payout at retirement. Now all permanent school staff in Ontario are entitled to 11 sick days a year at full pay, and 120 days of short-term leave at 90 per cent of their salary.
When teachers are sick or on medical leave, a supply or long-term occasional covers for them. When none are available, school staff may have to give up preparation time, be reassigned, or combine classes. Principals can also use uncertified emergency replacements, such as the lunchroom monitor.
According to 2022-23 data compiled by the not-for-profit School Boards' Co-Operative Inc., the average number of total paid sick days at the Toronto Catholic board was 16.16 for elementary and 13.92 for high school teachers. At the Toronto District School Board, it was 20.04 and 17.31, respectively.
The provincial average number of paid sick days for permanent board employees was 15.5 days a year, according to the data obtained by the Star.
At the Toronto public board, total sick leave costs for 2022-23 were $213 million, which includes paying for replacement staff. The board has eight staff members working in accountability and investigations, which includes six investigators. They look into allegations of professional misconduct, including sick leave abuse. It also uses external private investigation firms to carry out workplace investigations.
"While we don't currently have data readily available on terminations due to misuse of sick leave, we have terminated staff for this reason in the past," a board spokesperson told the Star.
A recent report from the province's auditor general "found that the lack of a formal attendance management program, an insufficient number of disability caseworkers and the (Toronto District School Board's) lax processes around the collection and review of medical notes have all contributed to the increase in sick leave use and related costs," adding that with 40,000 employees this is a glaring gap. (A disability caseworker administers the board's short-term leave plan.)
The auditor noted that the board conducted only about six investigations a year on suspected sick-day abuse over a 10-year period, "all of which were initiated in response to complaints it received; none were initiated as a result of its own analysis."
The York Region District School Board does have a plan in place that identifies and speaks to employees who are off more than 11 days during a school year, and it has a slightly lower average sick-day count than the TDSB.
In Toronto's Catholic board, a disability case administrator is called as soon as an employee is off for five days in a row, and then emailed a medical form they must complete, with a followup afterward.
The auditor says the Toronto public board could save $24 million a year if its average sick days fell in line with the provincial average.
The board says it is developing an attendance support program, but that it would require funding from the province to add more disability caseworkers.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau Isabel Teotonio Toronto Star

Subject: Investigations; Sick leave; Employees; School boards; Absenteeism; Cost control; Professional misconduct; Teachers; Labor unions; Budget deficits; Economic impact

Business indexing term: Subject: Sick leave Employees Absenteeism Cost control Professional misconduct Labor unions Budget deficits Economic impact

Ford launches volunteer emergency response teams

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-launches-volunteer-emergency-response-teams/docview/3144284963/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+launches+volunteer+emergency+response+teams&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario needs your help.
The province is looking for volunteers to work in their communities when disaster strikes, Premier Doug Ford said Friday in launching Ontario Corps to better co-ordinate emergency preparedness and response.
Ontario Corps volunteers would work alongside skilled professionals and those from non-governmental organizations and First Nations to help "deliver critical on-the-ground support" during floods, heavy snowstorms and other disasters, Ford said at an announcement Friday in North York alongside MPP Trevor Jones, a former police officer who is now the associate minister of emergency preparedness and response.
"This includes providing services such as emergency shelter, debris management, food services and flood protection delivered by great partner organizations," added Ford, noting that Ontario Corps was piloted this month in the Gravenhurst area when heavy snowfall hit.
"The Salvation Army, Global Medic, Feed Ontario and other search-and-rescue volunteer associations were deployed to help the town conduct wellness checks on residents, provide food hampers and support a municipal warming centre," Ford said. "That's a great example of what Ontario Corps will do ... and it's going to be a game-changer for emergency management in Ontario."
Ontario Corps is similar to AmeriCorps in the U.S. Volunteers can sign up at ontario.ca/ontariocorps. Free training will be provided.
Ford also announced new headquarters where the province's emergency preparedness and response services will be co-ordinated, on Disco Road near Pearson airport.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
