Document 364 of 500

Toronto still lives in the shadow of Rob Ford: At once beloved and condemned, the contentious mayor is still very much with us today.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/toronto-still-lives-shadow-rob-ford/docview/3144284960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+still+lives+in+the+shadow+of+Rob+Ford%3A+At+once+beloved+and+condemned%2C+the+contentious+mayor+is+still+very+much+with+us+today.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, we're now a decade into the post-Rob Ford era of Toronto politics. It's been 10 years this week since he left the mayor's office - can that be true? I mean here we are fighting about a plan to rip out bike lanes. Doug Ford is scrapping with the downtown lefties, and is behind a controversial waterfront redevelopment scheme. Somehow, the Eglinton LRT is still under construction. It can seem at a glance that not much has changed. Rob Ford, may he rest in peace, died in 2016. But his politics haunts us still.
And yet, as you start considering it, the full surreal experience of his mayoralty seems to recede like a wild fever dream, something you can only half remember and you are certain can't have been real. Even before the whole crack scandal that made him an international celebrity, I mean.
There was the giant circus-style scale outside his office where his weekly weigh-ins were, for a time, the only time the press was allowed to question him. There were the all-night budget meetings where members of the public overflowed the rooms to plead with him to not shut down libraries or close school nutrition programs or end snow clearing, as he sipped diet coke and looked bored. There was so much time spent in courtrooms - one where a judge ordered him out of office before the decision was overturned by an appeals court, another where he fended off a libel suit. There were the giant public parties in his mom's backyard where Councillor Gary Crawford played the drums. There was the time he chased down and threatened a Star reporter in a park adjoining his house. There was the time city council overturned his transit plan. The two times he voted against his own budget. There was a lot of high school football, there was cursing at 911 operators, there was a strangely prominent role played by Councillor Giorgio Mammoliti's thumb.
And then, after all that, the long, drawn-out crack scandal, with all the seedy murder and jail connections surrounding the video and the people there the night it was shot, his appearance on Jimmy Kimmel and - yes this actually happened - his tackling of a fellow councillor in the chamber while his colleagues stripped him of most of his power because he'd made a crude joke about oral sex.
It was a lot.
Those who were around city hall then and are still there now can tell you it is a quieter place these days. In most ways that is a good thing. But a reporter can note there are a lot fewer members of the public hungry for city politics stories than there used to be. The public chambers are less full during meetings, and enraged civic engagement seems to have given way to resigned apathy. All that conflict and controversy at least got people paying attention.
The three-ring circus may have left with him, but Rob Ford also launched his brother's political career. With Doug in the premier's office we get to constantly revisit the Ford family vendettas against Toronto city council. If the subway extension to Scarborough Town Centre ever gets completed, it will forever be a memorial to his obsessions. Those riding the buses today out to U of T Scarborough might view the lack of Sheppard LRT line - fully funded by the province before Rob Ford cancelled it - as a legacy of those same obsessions.
The penny-pinching austerity that was his calling card continued to dominate Toronto politics for a long time after he left. It's a fear of tax increases, combined with an insistence that any government spending is suspect and probably shameful. You can see the effects of that, a decade later, in crumbling infrastructure and strangled service levels. It is a hole Toronto only began to dig itself out of last year, when new Mayor Olivia Chow raised taxes and started spending bigger again, talking not about what waste we could prevent but, instead, what things we could accomplish.
There's a further legacy: an angry us-versus-them resentment politics on the right wing of the spectrum, as petty as it is vicious, that views any level of sophistication or expertise or competence as not just undesirable but as worthy of contempt. It's an attitudinal politics that continues to dominate that side of the political spectrum, not just here but across North America. Rob Ford did not invent it or perfect it, but he did bring it to Toronto, and it has stayed.
His was a mayoralty of contradictions: all action and no progress, small-minded and larger than life, obsessed with customer service but slashing customer services. Out of office after one scandalous and largely unsuccessful term, he is still defining the city three elections later.
Rob Ford: such a unique character in Toronto's history. Ten years later, he'd probably be the consensus choice as the worst mayor in recent memory. But he's also probably the mayor who was most widely beloved. If you didn't witness him in action, you could hardly believe he existed. If you did, it can seem like he's never left.
Edward Keenan is a Toronto-based city columnist for the Star. Reach him via email:
ekeenan@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Edward Keenan City Columnist;

Document 365 of 500

Nobel winner wants to reclaim 'freedom' from the right wing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobel-winner-wants-reclaim-freedom-right-wing/docview/3144284883/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nobel+winner+wants+to+reclaim+%27freedom%27+from+the+right+wing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Joseph Stiglitz seeks to redefine "freedom" to mean liberty for all to enjoy a good life.
For the past few decades, freedom has been perverted to mean liberty for free markets to do as they please with minimal control by the governments that represent those affected, and afflicted, by such liberties.
"The current conservative reading of what freedom means," Stiglitz writes in his new book, "The Road to Freedom: Economics and the Good Society," "is superficial, misguided and ideologically motivated."
Mainstream politics continues to subscribe to the "trickle-down" theory of how wealth eventually makes its way to the masses, though that notion has been thoroughly debunked.
Dating from the era of Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher, political leaders in liberal democracies have persisted in their belief that free markets know best.
The idea - and it is a mere idea - that unfettered markets will self-correct their harmful excesses is widely held in mainstream politics and the media.
That way of governing mostly benefits the advantaged, a minority in society. And it conflicts with the moral sentiments of Adam Smith, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Franklin Roosevelt and Tommy Douglas.
"The Right claims to be the defender of freedom," writes Stiglitz, a Nobel laureate economist. But in practice that turns out to mean "the freedom to exploit," Stiglitz says, "reducing the freedoms of most citizens."
"A person facing extremes of want and fear is not free," Stiglitz writes.
Freedom as neo-conservatives define it is a means by which the privileged escape taxation and regulation.
It is a bitter freedom for most, manifesting itself in societal failure to provide economic opportunity for all, and to protect against job loss from globalization, industrial pollution, oligopoly control of prices, Big Tech intrusions on personal privacy, and underfunded health care and education for lack of tax revenue.
Stiglitz proposes a "progressive capitalism" that remedies income inequality, makes high-quality education and health care accessible to all, restrains corporate power, and sees a larger role in society for co-operatives and non-profit groups.
Stiglitz has long been engaged in this egalitarian fight.
His latest book is among his best. It summarizes Stiglitz's decades of first-hand experience at the highest levels of government and commerce. And it draws on both historical and recent events, including the pandemic and the resentment that sustains Trumpism.
Stiglitz chronicles the relaxation of financial-industry regulations that caused the Wall Street meltdown and resulting Great Recession, in which the markets did not correct themselves. They had to be bailed out with $800 billion of taxpayer funds in the U.S. alone, after destroying 400,000 jobs in Canada and nine million in the U.S.
What Stiglitz calls for, a genuine freedom for all, can be called revolutionary, though the man himself, now 81, is no renegade.
Stiglitz is a former chair of the Council of Economic Advisers in the Clinton administration and was later chief economist at the World Bank.
Stiglitz despairs that even centre-left leaders like Clinton and former British prime minister Tony Blair bought into the neo-con mantra that unrestrained free markets alone can deliver mass prosperity.
Instances of trying to correct imbalances between rich and poor have been infrequent. And they generally meet with a chorus of disapproval from what John Kenneth Galbraith called the "culture of contentment" - the affluent minority satisfied with things as they are.
The Trudeau government was criticized this year for increasing the capital-gains inclusion taxation rate, a measure affecting the contented class.
"We just don't think it's right," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said, "that a student, or an electrician or a teacher be paying taxes on 100 per cent of their income while others have the opportunities to use accountants and pay taxes on only 50 per cent of that income."
In our society of relatively unfettered free markets, employers have scrapped defined-benefit pensions. With weak competition protections, Big Business merges into oligopolies whose power rivals that of governments. And business lobbyists extract billions of dollars from taxpayers in corporate welfare.
Language is powerful. Whoever controls the conversation controls the society.
What's needed to ensure a wider and more comprehensive well-being in society, Stiglitz argues, is for progressives to take back "freedom" and restore its real meaning.
"The true advocates of deep, meaningful freedom are aligned with the progressive movement both in the U.S. and abroad," Stiglitz writes.
"They and the centre-left parties that represent them need to reclaim the freedom agenda."
Neo-liberalism is based on fear of subjugation by government and not a little greed. Yet today's prevailing free-market, anti-government doctrine has "led to massive inequality and provided fertile ground for populists," Stiglitz writes.
Political conditions being what they now are, the next few years portend overcast skies for progressives. "The Road to Freedom" is a balm and a reform blueprint for progressives as they regroup.
They can also take some comfort from the adage of economist Abraham Rotstein of the University of Toronto that "all dogmas have their day."
CAPTION: What's needed to ensure a wider and more comprehensive well-being in society, U.S. economist Joseph Stiglitz argues in his new book, is for progressives to take back "freedom" and restore its real meaning.ERIC PIERMONT AFP via Getty Images file photo
CREDIT: David Olive Toronto Star OPINION

Document 366 of 500

The Ford Effect is always in motion: Premier's war on bikes latest example of how a forceful political move can have unintended results

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-effect-is-always-motion/docview/3144284695/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ford+Effect+is+always+in+motion%3A+Premier%27s+war+on+bikes+latest+example+of+how+a+forceful+political+move+can+have+unintended+results&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's war on bike lanes is having some unintended consequences - a phenomenon I have seen before and mentally dubbed "the Ford Effect."
My definition of the Ford Effect: To strike forcefully to achieve a political aim and, in doing so, cause unintended emotions or enlightenment about something the public previously knew little about.
I first noticed it at city hall during the 2010-14 term, when Ford was a city councillor and chief adviser to his late brother Rob Ford, the populist mayor who rode to power vowing to shake up Toronto politics and end the "gravy train" of wasteful spending.
They wanted to slash the city budget by closing libraries and Riverdale Farm and reduce funding for transit, heritage, student nutrition, arts and school crossing guards. Rob Ford's executive committee held a meeting overnight in 2011 to move as quickly as possible through responses from the many outraged Torontonians who told the Fords they valued the threatened services.
"You have galvanized a giant machine," determined to protect city services, one resident told them. Fourteen-year-old Scarborough student Anika Tabovaradan, who had never before made a political presentation and said she hated public speaking, bawled as she spoke at 2 a.m. about her love for her local library branch, my then-colleague Daniel Dale reported from the meeting.
Torontonians, many of whom rarely if ever think of city hall, gave the Fords and their councillors an earful. Instead of cutting the budget, council cut the Fords' consultant-suggested cuts.
The same year, Doug Ford led a bid to wrest control of Toronto's east waterfront from city-provincial-federal agency Waterfront Toronto, triggering a groundswell of interest in, and defence of, the agency's existing Port Lands redevelopment plan that was on almost nobody's radar.
The Etobicoke councillor's secret solicitation of an Australian developer to remake the plan with a Ferris wheel, megamall and glitzy boat-in hotel, raised the profile of Waterfront Toronto's plan to remake the mouth of the Don River into a lush valley with new parks and homes. Council voted to accelerate, not kill, that vision, which is expected to fully open to the public next year.
Now to bike lanes. Doug Ford is infinitely more powerful as premier than he ever was at city hall. There appears to be little to stop his majority PC government from removing cycleways on Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street.
A cycling advocacy group has launched a court challenge to try to save the lanes. Even if that is successful, Ford could overrule a court decision with the notwithstanding clause, an opt-out of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms that he seems eager to deploy.
But the Ford Effect is exposing a host of factors contributing to traffic gridlock that have nothing to do with cyclists. They include the closure of vehicle traffic lanes for the PC government's Ontario Line transit construction, as well as for condo construction projects, city maintenance work and utility repairs, as I recently reported with my colleague, Kate Allen.
In a recent edition of the City Hall Watcher newsletter, engineer Damien Houle argued persuasively that traffic signals dramatically reduce road capacity and slow traffic, a result that some people welcome given the six Toronto cyclists who have died so far this year after being hit by drivers. Those tragedies, and resulting white-painted "ghost bike" memorials, are cited in arguments to save the bike lanes.
I can vouch for the traffic signal issue. Driving the Bloor Street West bike lane extension that apparently sparked Ford's ire, I encountered six traffic lights over 1.7 kilometres between The Kingsway and Aberfoyle Crescent, including a signal near Grenview Boulevard that doesn't actually connect any streets. They enhance the Kingsway's pedestrian-friendly village-y feel, but drivers pay the price.
Ford and his transportation minister aimed a spotlight at the question of how many people actually commute by bike by citing a misleading figure of just over one per cent. That triggered reporting that revealed an unreleased provincial report citing higher figures.
While enlightening the public on issues around bike lanes and gridlock, and spurring more informed debate, the Ford Effect has not, to date, triggered the kind of public backlash seen with the proposed cuts to the 2011 city budget or the vision of a megamall on the expensively reclaimed waterfront. Ford's renewed lakefront focus, with a controversial Ontario Place redevelopment, also appears to be moving toward fruition.
Still, when we eventually see the results of Ford's characteristically hard-charging way of attacking issues, Torontonians will at least have a clearer picture of the facts, including whether removing bike lanes speeds up traffic at all.
Unlike Toronto traffic, the Ford Effect is always in motion.
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 367 of 500

Doug Ford says he got Donald Trump's attention with energy threat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-he-got-donald-trumps-attention/docview/3144400472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+he+got+Donald+Trump%27s+attention+with+energy+threat&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says his threat to "cut off" Ontario electricity exports to the U.S. has forced president-elect Donald Trump to sit up and pay attention.
In response to Trump's plan to slap 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods next month, Ford warned the country's most populous province was prepared to retaliate, provoking a response from the incoming president.
"It was the first time president-elect Trump said, 'I want to make a deal with Canada.' That's good news," the premier said Friday at the province's emergency operations centre in North York.
In separate news conferences, both Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc echoed Ford in the need to be hawkish with Trump.
"We have to be candid about the reality of the incoming U.S. administration. This is an administration that believes in America first. This is an administration which is proudly an economic nationalist administration," Freeland, who conferred with Ford Thursday night, said at the Toronto Stock Exchange.
"This is an administration which openly has a strategy of creating economic uncertainty outside the United States as a strategy to discourage investment anywhere other than the United States. We're realistic. We recognize that that is the case," she said.
LeBlanc, speaking in Fredericton, said "I've gotten to know Doug Ford and like him over the last number of years.
"He's certainly able to speak for himself," the public safety minister said, acknowledging "other premiers have offered views on what might be the appropriate response."
While some premiers have rejected Ford's tough talk about restricting energy exports - notably Alberta's Danielle Smith and Quebec's François Legault - the Ontarian shrugged off their stance.
"If it comes to Alberta or Quebec, that's their choice. They believe in diplomacy - good luck," the premier said.
Freeland, who will deliver a fall economic statement Monday detailing $1 billion in new spending on border security, stressed that "Canada will not escalate and Canada will not back down."
After a first ministers' meeting Wednesday of the premiers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - along with Freeland and LeBlanc - Ford came out swinging.
"We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy," he said that night, referring to the electricity Ontario sells to Michigan, New York and Minnesota, which powers 1.5 million homes there.
"Americans are going to feel the pain as well and isn't that unfortunate."
The next day on the floor of the New York Stock Exchange, Trump was asked about Ford's comments.
"That's OK if he that does that. That's fine," said the president-elect, who has vowed to impose the 25 per cent levies on Canadian and Mexican goods upon his Jan. 20 inauguration.
"The United States is subsidizing Canada and we shouldn't have to do that," added Trump, who wants Canada and Mexico to tighten their borders to stem illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
"And we have a great relationship. I have so many friends in Canada, but we shouldn't have to subsidize a country. We're subsidizing more than $100 billion a year. We shouldn't have to be doing that."
That appeared to be a reference to the amount Trump feels Ottawa needs to boost military spending in order to achieve its NATO target of defence spending equal to two per cent of gross domestic product.
Ford said Friday the premiers agree "it's absolutely essential that we hit our two per cent commitment to NATO."
Asked whether the LCBO should stop selling American wine, beer and spirits if Trump imposes tariffs, the premier said, "we'll use every tool in our tool box" as in the 2019 trade war with Trump.
"We put a tariff on bourbon last time. The LCBO is one of the largest purchasers of alcohol in the entire world, but I'd prefer not to do any of this. I'd prefer to make a deal."
In that vein, Ford spoke Thursday with New York Gov. Kathy Hochul and Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer to assure them Canadians don't want a trade war.
"They fully understood where it's coming from," he said. "But again, my comment to the two governors yesterday - we want to sell you more energy."
On Monday, Ford will host a Council of the Federation meeting of the premiers in Toronto to discuss the trade crisis.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to members of the media at Queen's Park on Dec. 12, 2024.

Want to help when disaster strikes? Doug Ford announces volunteer emergency response team

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/want-help-when-disaster-strikes-doug-ford/docview/3144400452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Want+to+help+when+disaster+strikes%3F+Doug+Ford+announces+volunteer+emergency+response+team&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario needs your help.
The province is looking for volunteers to work in their communities when disaster strikes, Premier Doug Ford said Friday in launching Ontario Corps to better co-ordinate emergency preparedness and response.
Ontario Corps volunteers would work alongside skilled professionals and those from non-governmental organizations and First Nations to help "deliver critical on-the-ground support" during floods, heavy snowstorms and other disasters, Ford said at an announcement Friday in North York alongside MPP Trevor Jones, a former police officer who is now the associate minister of emergency preparedness and response.
"This includes providing services such as emergency shelter, debris management, food services and flood protection delivered by great partner organizations," added Ford, noting that Ontario Corps was piloted this month in the Gravenhurst area when heavy snowfall hit.
"The Salvation Army, Global Medic, Feed Ontario and other search-and-rescue volunteer associations were deployed to help the town conduct wellness checks on residents, provide food hampers and support a municipal warming centre," Ford said. "That's a great example of what Ontario Corps will do ... and it's going to be a game-changer for emergency management in Ontario."
Ontario Corps is similar to AmeriCorps in the U.S. Volunteers can sign up at ontario.ca/ontariocorps. Free training will be provided.
Ford also announced new headquarters where the province's emergency preparedness and response services will be co-ordinated, on Disco Road near Pearson airport.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends a news conference at Queen's Park on Dec. 12, 2024.

Document 369 of 500

'It's become uncomfortable for me': Jordan Peterson, the controversial psychologist, moves from Toronto to the U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/become-uncomfortable-me-jordan-peterson/docview/3144400447/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+become+uncomfortable+for+me%27%3A+Jordan+Peterson%2C+the+controversial+psychologist%2C+moves+from+Toronto+to+the+U.S.&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jordan Peterson is leaving Toronto.
Peterson, appearing on his daughter Mikhaila's podcast in an episode posted Dec. 6, officially announced his move to a sunnier home in the United States, where there "are decided advantages" over living in Toronto.
A psychologist who rose to prominence rallying against preferred pronoun use at the University of Toronto, Peterson has become a popular talking head among conservative and right-wing media in recent years.
Why Jordan Peterson is moving
"The issue with the College (of Psychologists of Ontario) ... is very annoying, to say the least," Peterson said, citing reasons why he decided to leave Toronto.
"And the new legislation that the (federal) Liberals are attempting to push through, Bill C-63 (Online Harms Act) - we'd all be living in a totalitarian hellhole if it passes."
"The tax situation is out of hand, the government in Canada at the federal level is incompetent beyond belief. And it's become uncomfortable for me in my neighbourhood in Toronto."
Peterson's spat with the College of Psychologists of Ontario (CPO) stems from complaints over social media posts about, among other things, a "not beautiful" plus-size model. The college ordered Peterson to undergo a coaching program on professionalism in public statements.
In August, three Superior Court judges comprising a panel of Divisional Court, ruled it was reasonable for the CPO to order Peterson to take a course on professionalism in communications. The governing body for psychologists made the order after receiving numerous complaints over Peterson's commentary, which has been criticized for attacking feminism and racial diversity.
Peterson has repeatedly taken aim at the Online Harms Act, or Bill C-63, which has sparked debate over how Ottawa should best strike the balance between protecting its citizens and their right to express themselves freely.
There are seven categories of harm are laid out in the bill - three deal with protecting children from exploitative or harmful content, another category covers the sharing of intimate images without consent, and the remaining categories include content that promotes hatred, content that incites violence, and content that incites violent extremism or terrorism. The bill was eventually split in two to ensure the child safety guidelines could be passed into law.
Where is he relocating to?Peterson didn't specify where in the U.S. he has moved to, but mentioned that he was near his daughter, who lives in Arizona.
Peterson didn't say exactly where he lived in Toronto, but a Toronto Life profile from 2021 noted that he had "modest semi in Seaton Village." On the podcast episode he said he lived in a "kind of champagne socialist neighbourhood" and was "less popular in my own neighbourhood than I am anywhere else in the world, literally."
He called the Toronto District School Board "probably the most woke institution in North America, which is really saying something," over its handling of the COVID pandemic, and said Toronto is "run by the wife (Olivia Chow) of the last leader of the socialist party in Canada (Jack Layton), and so the goal in Toronto was to turn it into Portland or San Francisco. And that's happening."
He said he moved once he and his son, whom he lived close to, became uncomfortable living in Toronto.
"I didn't ever expect that to be something that happened in Canada. But under Justin (Trudeau) and the woke mob, that's definitely something that's happened," Peterson said.
How did Jordan Peterson get famous?
It's been almost a decade since Peterson rocketed to national consciousness - and controversy - by arguing that being forced to use pronouns such as "ze" and "zir" was a violation of free speech while lecturing at the University of Toronto. He emerged as part public intellectual, part internet celebrity and a fighter against perceived political correctness. He was author of the bestselling book, "12 Rules for Life: Antidote to Chaos," which urged readers to seize responsibility for their own lives, sparking speaking tours, lucrative crowdfunding and official merchandise and launched him into global fame.
Peterson also parted ways with U of T, writing in the National Post in 2022 that he'd resigned from the full-time, tenured position. There were a few reasons, he wrote. Chief among them was the "appalling ideology currently demolishing the universities and, downstream, the general culture."
He has since launched his own online school and regularly appears on conservative media, rallying against climate change activists and trans rights activists.
Jordan Peterson vs. Justin Trudeau
Then, testifying under oath at a public inquiry into foreign interference in October, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appeared to reference recent American allegations that RT - a media mouthpiece for the Russian government - had been covertly bankrolling right-wing influencers, allegedly including Peterson, according to the prime minister.
Peterson denied those claims, telling the National Post, "I don't think it's reasonable for the prime minister of this country to basically label me a traitor and I don't find it amusing."
He has said he is considering legal action.
CAPTION: Jordan Peterson at his home in Toronto in December 2016. Peterson says he has moved to the U.S.

Document 370 of 500

The GST and HST holiday tax break starts on Saturday. Here are the items that will be covered

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gst-hst-holiday-tax-break-starts-on-saturday-here/docview/3143996270/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+GST+and+HST+holiday+tax+break+starts+on+Saturday.+Here+are+the+items+that+will+be+covered&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some last minute holiday shopping could be a bit cheaper than usual with the start of the two month tax holiday that kicks in on Saturday.
The temporary tax break will remove the federal and provincial tax from all groceries, restaurant meals, children's clothes, toys and diapers, as well as beer and wine, for a two-month period from Dec. 14 to Feb. 15, in a bid to ease the cost of living for Canadians.
In Ontario, for the products included in the tax break, that means the harmonized sales tax (HST), which is comprised of the federal GST portion (at five per cent) and the provincial portion (at eight per cent), won't apply.
The Liberals have also proposed sending $250 cheques to anyone who worked in 2023 and made less than $150,000, but have not yet introduced legislation on that measure. The New Democrats and Bloc - joined by some members of the Liberal caucus - want to see eligibility expanded to more Canadians.
What is covered under the GST/HST holiday break?Children's goods
Clothing, diapers, car seats and toys for kids are all covered under the new temporary tax break.
For clothing, that includes: baby clothes, including bibs and blankets; children's clothes up to girls' size 16 and boys' size 20; children's socks, hats, scarves and gloves; shoes smaller than 24.25 cm; and sports clothing like jerseys, leotards, ski jackets and recreational swimsuits.
What's not included: any specialized clothing or shoes designed exclusively for sports (like wetsuits, soccer cleats, skates, ski boots, etc.), adult clothing purchased for a child, costumes or makeup and jewelry.
Most diapers are included in the tax break, including cloth and disposable diapers, diaper inserts or training pants - however, adult diapers are not included (they are already exempt from GST/HST).
The same goes for children's car seats, which includes all boosters and seats that meet federal safety standards, but does not include car seats that are built as part of a stroller or carrier combination.
Children's toys covered under the tax break include most items intended for children under 14 years old (which mostly captures toys recommended for children aged 8 and up). That encompasses board games, card games, toys, dolls, plush toys and jigsaw puzzles (for all ages). Excluded are collectibles, like hockey cards or collectible dolls, and toys marketed for adults (like adult Lego sets).
Video games
Video game consoles, controllers and video games are included in the tax break as well, though only physical copies of games (like in CDs or cartridges) qualify.
Books
For the most part, published printed books (both hardcover and softcover editions), magazines and periodicals are covered under the tax break.
Ebooks and audio books are not included and magazines need to be supplied by a subscription or have less than five per cent of its printed space devoted to advertising in order to qualify. Agendas, calendars and notebooks do not qualify for the tax break.
Newspapers
Printed newspapers also qualify for the tax break, but excludes digital versions of those publications.
Christmas trees
Both natural and artificial Christmas trees, as well as "similar decorative trees," qualify for the tax break, according to the government.
Food and drinks
All restaurant meals - including dine-in, takeout and delivery - are covered by the tax holiday. That also covers prepared foods, pre-made meals and catering. The tax break also includes: snacks like chips, candy and baked goods; non-alcoholic drinks including coffee, tea, juices, soda and smoothies; beer; wine, cider and sake at 22.9 per cent ABV or less; and premixed alcoholic drinks at seven per cent ABV or less.
That exemption does not include food or drink from vending machines, dietary supplements and pet food.
Basic groceries - including fresh fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry and fish - are already excluded from both the provincial and federal portions of the HST in Ontario.
How about the provincial sales tax break?
The Ford government has pledged to match the federal government's two-month GST holiday, which overlaps with pre-existing tax breaks in the province.
Ontario already provides a "point-of-sale rebate" on the eight per cent portion of the HST on: books, children's clothes and shoes, children's car seats, diapers, some prepared food and beverages under $4 and some books and newspapers.
That will expand to cover the federal GST/HST tax holiday, meaning consumers will see a full 13 per cent tax break on the items above.
How much can I save?
The total savings you will see depends entirely on your spending habits. Stocking up on toys, books and processed foods? You could save a few dozen dollars. If you're sticking to the basic groceries and having a low-key holiday season, you'll be less likely to see any reduction in your expenses, as the HST already doesn't apply to basic groceries.
Still confused? Here are some examples.
Let's say you go all out and purchase a PlayStation 5 Slim, plus an extra controller and a physical copy of a game (for example, NHL 25). Using prices from Dec. 3 on BestBuy, your total would usually be $802.27. Remove the HST and that would bring your total down to $709.97, resulting in $92.30 in savings.
Maybe you stock up on diapers, toys and food at Costco. Assuming you get a box of 192 size 1 diapers (Kirkland brand), plus a kids Lego set and a box of assorted chips, your total would be around $199.47 pre-tax holiday (the point-of-sale rebate already refunds the provincial tax). Between December and February, that comes down to $189.97, or about $24.70 in savings.
And maybe you're buying Christmas presents for a book worm? A box set of the first five Game of Thrones books at Indigo (through its online store) would usually cost $67.74, but with the tax break the price comes down to $59.95.
CAPTION: The federal government announced the tax holiday in November, in an attempt to ease Canadians' cost-of-living woes.

Subject: Clothing; Meals; Computer & video games; Children & youth; Food; Diapers; Cost control; Sales taxes; Toys

Document 371 of 500

Open feuds just a start for Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/open-feuds-just-start-liberals/docview/3143676626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Open+feuds+just+a+start+for+Liberals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For those who enjoy indulging in a little Ottawa "Kremlinology," there's been a lot of interesting buzz, increasingly in the open, about rifts in the Liberal government. The most prominent of those differences includes two cabinet ministers, who have perhaps the highest profiles: Mélanie Joly and, just this week, Chrystia Freeland.
Other disagreements are less prominent, but no less instructive. Last week, the CBC reported that Chandra Arya, an Ontario-based Liberal MP, was accusing one of his fellow Liberal MPs, B.C.-based Sukh Dhaliwal, of threatening him and aggressively accosting him outside the House of Commons.
Their dispute is outside the scope of this column. What struck me as both interesting and germane is that, when your MPs are openly accusing each other of bad deeds in the media, that is a pretty big signal that the Liberal party, as a government and political institution, isn't doing so great.
Which brings us back to Freeland. Earlier this week, the Globe and Mail reported on tension between Freeland, the deputy prime minister and finance minister, and the PMO, citing Freeland's reluctance to engage in unwise spending - the GST holiday, the proposed rebate cheques - that is plainly political. The Globe cited sources who noted that the dispute seems similar to the one that eventually led Freeland's predecessor, Bill Morneau, to quit. It also noted that these kinds of tensions between a finance minister and a prime minister are nothing new or unusual.
True. Uncontroversial, even. But now let's consider that third story: Joly, foreign affairs minister, has been the subject of three recent articles. One of them was Justin Ling, here in the Star, where he called for her to be fired and replaced, based in large part on her underwhelming performance at the recent Halifax International Security Forum. Another was by yours truly; I agreed with Ling. The third was a profile piece in the New York Times, which included speculation that Joly might one day replace Justin Trudeau.
Though it ran recently, the Times interview took place in July and the profile was written in the summer, according to people familiar with the process. It's an unfortunate coincidence for Joly, given the negative reaction to her time in Halifax. But some of the reaction to it was interesting. I was surprised by the number of Liberals who either privately or publicly began mulling over the merit of a Joly leadership run.
Now, I absolutely see the argument for Joly for Liberal leader. It's just that, ahem, there already is a Liberal leader. And he's been pretty clear he's not going anywhere.
These three stories are, of course, unrelated. Almost unrelated, anyway. The common thread that runs through them is that the Liberals - again, as a government and also as a political party - are seemingly having a wee bit of internal discord. This isn't a shock, since we already knew about the internal rumblings in the caucus about Trudeau's continued leadership.
But none of what ails the Liberals is going to get better. In fact, most of it will get worse. Is there some good economic news? Sure. The rate-cut announcement this week will be happily received by the government. But it's only going to increase the tension between the PMO and Freeland, assuming the latter chooses to dig in and fight. Given that polling shows no "Trump Bump" and an overall negative reaction to the government tax reprieve and rebates - what I've been calling "the vibe bribe" - the pressure on Trudeau to leave is going to remain, while speculation about Joly's plans will keep swirling.
And as for the two squabbling backbench MPs, look around at the state of the world today. Can you think of anything else going around the globe that might possibly cause a rift in a caucus that can taste an oncoming freight train of electoral doom?
This government is already showing real signs of struggling to hold up under pressure. None of these pressures are going away, and starting in about five weeks, the pressure is actually going to increase, as president-elect Donald Trump makes his triumphant return to the Oval Office. Liberals today may feel like they've had a rotten few months. I can only invert a phrase once used by the prime minister and assure them that worse is always possible.
Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. Reach him via email: matt@readtheline.ca
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Finance Chrystia Freeland is reportedly at odds with the PMO, due to her reluctance to engage in unwise spending - the GST holiday, the proposed rebate cheques - that is plainly political, Matt Gurney writes.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photo

Document 372 of 500

Alberta kills cash cow known as photo radar

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-kills-cash-cow-known-as-photo-radar/docview/3143672242/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+kills+cash+cow+known+as+photo+radar&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One thing I love about Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's United Conservative Party is that it stays true to its school however indescribable.
Even Ontario Premier Doug Ford changes his mind occasionally. But the UCP sends out a stream of singular and ill-advised plans that puzzle the rest of Canada and possibly even Saskatchewan.
The latest is the move by "Transportation and Economic Corridors" Minister Devin Dreeshen to ban photo radar on Alberta's highways. Dreeshen, a farmer, announced his plan in a restaurant - see chalk "SMOKEHOUSE" sign - while wearing an apron.
Here's the thinking. Photo radar makes big money. Therefore, it's a "cash cow." Restaurants serve beef, hence the apron saying CASH COW with a red slash through the words.
Geddit? Maybe you didn't. "This is a historic day for Alberta drivers as we turn up the heat, we fire up the grill, and say goodbye to the photo radar cash cow," Dreeshen explained slowly and loudly.
When political visuals are this laboured, give up. I would have just gone with a cowboy hat.
At any rate, the CBC reports that highway photo radar sites will vanish April 1, except for those in school, playground and construction zones. At the moment, the province has about 2,200 sites in 24 municipalities.
Photo radar hauls in $145 million a year, an astonishing sum. I suppose Albertans speed to their privatized liquor stores after enduring crushingly expensive private health care and discovering former PM Stephen Harper is now in charge of their public service pensions. Will he go full bitcoin?
I'd drink too if I lived in Edmonton or Calgary, both NDP strongholds, imagine the loneliness. But perhaps rural Albertans speed because they enjoy the rush of freedom that risk brings. I know I did as a teenager. Several friends died.
Dreeshen said the cash mountain "came at the expense of public trust. With these changes, we are putting safety first."
He trusts the public not to speed. Hah!
Alberta's cops were livid, as are the towns that wouldn't get those speeding fines. Now police will have to find and chase speeders, which is time-consuming and dangerous for all. But of course, Albertans will justify the faith Dreeshen put in them to voluntarily stick to the speed limit.
But speed limits are a collar on individual liberty, just like public service pension plans. People's hard-earned money shouldn't cluster like that in communistic cash silos. It should range widely and roam free.
Ever-patient Globe and Mail health columnist André Picard went out on a limb. He opposes carnage on the roads, suggesting that carmakers install tech that warns drivers they are speeding or slows the car down automatically. The EU mandates this; why don't we? Well, you get what you vote for.
Why do Smith and her cabinet do these things? We look to MAGA Republicans for clues.
Perhaps it's all part of a cunning plan to make government smaller. After all, Alberta municipalities with less radar cash can offer fewer services to citizens, less road maintenance perhaps, or water quality testing. Fewer cops. The savings pile up.
Speaking of the government boot on the human face, I watch agog as President-to-be-Trump plans to dismantle government by appointing ragtag people who despise the agencies they will oversee.
Trump puts me in mind of Premier Smith. Neither of them has a coherent Thatcher-like strategy about lessening government power. Instead, they have a "cast of mind." Some stuff they like, some stuff they don't, and even they can't pin it down.
Killing photo radar has a vibe. What's next? Premier Ford has raised speed limits on Ontario highways. Should limits exist at all? Do they violate the public trust? Premier Smith is taking notes.
Heather Mallick is a Toronto-based columnist covering
current affairs for the Star. Follow her on Bluesky:
@hmallick.bsky.social

Document 373 of 500

Rob Ford humiliated Toronto, and himself. He was also my friend. I'm still struggling with the world he created, then left behind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rob-ford-humiliated-toronto-himself-he-was-also/docview/3143671934/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rob+Ford+humiliated+Toronto%2C+and+himself.+He+was+also+my+friend.+I%27m+still+struggling+with+the+world+he+created%2C+then+left+behind&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was a time, when he would raise his giant body to roar against the left and the elite, thick neck turning red, his rage all too real, when Rob Ford was the defining figure, not only in Toronto politics, but of the city itself. He was simultaneously praised and ridiculed, admired and loathed, revered and scorned. But never, I believe, fully understood. Not as a politician, and certainly not as the very raw, very real and essentially very broken human being that he was.
Rob's mayoralty ended 10 years ago this month. His final years in power were lost in a cascade of surrealistic scandal, including a crack video, police surveillance, denials, admissions, talk show jokes, and a jaw-dropping declaration about having "more than enough to eat at home." By the time John Tory succeeded him as mayor on Dec. 1, 2014, Rob was a diminished force, stripped of most of his political power, battling addictions and cancer. It was difficult to watch as it unfolded, and it's painful to reflect on it now.
Rob introduced this city to a style of polarizing politics that seemed shocking at the time. He separated us by geography and culture, education and income - but more than anything else by the depth of our personal grievances and the anger that fuels them. Today, Rob seems less like an aberration than an early warning sign of the politics of blind resentment and the global unravelling of the political order we thought we knew.
Politically, Rob was my ideological opposite. Personally, he was a friend. Ten years on, that conflict remains.
Rob and I spent 15 years together on Toronto Council. I orchestrated the vote to remove most of his powers as mayor, when I believed his wildly out-of-control behaviour left no other choice. But he didn't hold it against me - at least not for long. By the time he was mayor we had become the unlikeliest of friends. And when my friend became internationally notorious as "Toronto's crack-smoking mayor," I wrote a book about him.
Others had chronicled his chronically bad behaviour; I wanted to explain what caused it. The book's title, "The Only Average Guy," was how Rob described himself to me. He wanted to be seen as a man so supremely common that it rendered him uniquely exceptional.
It was an inherently incongruous notion, much like Rob himself. As I wrote at the time, he was "a jumble of contradictions: attention seeker and shy loner; bully and kind-hearted friend; a savvy politician who can't comprehend much of what happens around him; the self-proclaimed 'best mayor the city has ever had' and the guy with such low self-esteem that he'll stare at the floor to avoid eye contact."
His brother Doug, now the premier of Ontario, puzzled over our friendship. "I don't f---ing understand it," he told me several times. Neither did I, until I realized that there were few people in Rob's life who didn't want something from him. During long council meetings, I engaged him in the only two subjects he could comfortably discuss: politics and football. Those were my entry into his heavily guarded world.
Because Rob so conspicuously carried his personal demons into the mayor's office, people always had trouble separating the person from the politician. I knew them as two different men.
The better I got to know Rob, the more I believed he wasn't in charge of his own destiny. He was born into an affluent but emotionally destitute family, ruled by a tougher-than-nails Dad who taught four children to crave his approval - and then withheld it. Three of the four siblings have had issues with drugs and alcohol. The fourth is the premier of Ontario.
Doug Ford Sr., a bumptious backbencher in the Conservative government of Mike Harris, instructed his sons to "take it to the next level," according to Doug Jr. Whatever your father accomplished, you and Rob were required to take further? I asked him in 2014.
"We have to," Doug replied.
Rob put it in more Biblical terms. "Like my Dad said, 'Many are called, few are chosen.' It's on his tombstone. The Ford family was chosen."
Unable to meet Dad's expectations in business or football, Rob felt compelled to enter politics. As a councillor, his early scandals were small stuff: He called fellow councillor Giorgio Mammoliti a "Gino boy.' He got into a drunken argument at a Leafs game, then lied about it. The controversies made good headlines but didn't earn him a following. That didn't come until his colleagues tried to sanction him for not spending his taxpayer-funded office budget, taking it instead from his own pocket.
By the time he launched a longshot bid for mayor in 2010, Rob was able to intuit that attacks from mainstream media and other politicians gained him support. Conventional political wisdom got turned upside down. Rob was overweight and disheveled. He lacked charm, eloquence, analytical ability or education. To the astonishment of the high and mighty, all of these became political assets.
The demands of the mayoralty were always beyond Rob. Not because he was stupid - he wasn't - but because the world he could understand was incredibly small. He coped with that by making the job small too.
People who revere Rob still praise him for fielding residents' complaints until past midnight and following up with home visits. Rob saw nothing odd about a mayor who represented millions of people trying to solve their problems one person at a time. He loved greeting supporters and posing for selfies. The legend that grew around his brand of customer service made him feel capable, confident and appreciated. For a short time, that felt like enough.
But a year into his term as mayor, Rob began to lose support on council. The darkness of failure closed in on him again. Belligerence couldn't hide the pain that sent him on late night visits to his father, wailing beside his grave in a drunken stupor.
First came the public displays of stupid drunkenness. Then he found a better pain killer: crack cocaine. That led to an undercover police investigation and a blackmail attempt by the gang that sold him the crack and peddled a video of him smoking it. The three-ring circus made international headlines.
By the time Rob publicly admitted to smoking crack, "probably in one of my drunken stupors", most members of the public had had enough. So too had Toronto Council. On November 15, 2013, I introduced a motion to shift most of his powers to the deputy mayor. It passed easily.
Humiliated and alone, Rob spiraled further downwards. Eventually, in the spring of 2014, his family finally convinced him to go into rehab. He emerged eight weeks later, resuming an uphill effort to convince voters that he was a new man. But then, two months before the election, he was diagnosed with a rare form of terminal cancer, unrelated to his addictions or his weight. It was just bad luck. Rob abandoned his campaign for mayor. His brother Doug could run instead. Rob went back on the ballot for his old council seat.
The last time I saw Rob was October 2015. The cancer's progression had slowed enough for him to be back at city hall. I dropped by his office, expecting the usual gruff exterior. But the Rob who greeted me that day was soft-spoken, almost reflective.
"Rob, you seem really different," I observed.
"I feel different," he replied.
He asked me to help him choose between two U.S. networks competing to interview him. I suggested the show where a friend of his worked.
"Friends," he scoffed. "There aren't many people you can trust."
"Well, there's your family," I ventured.
"They are sometimes the worst," he replied.
He asked if I would go with him to New York for an interview. I had turned down similar invitations in the past, not knowing what scene I might find myself in. This time I decided to chance it.
"Sure, I'll go with you. We can take in a football game. Jets or Giants?"
"The Only Average Guy" was about to be published. Rob already expected it to contain unflattering scenes. Still, I worried he'd feel betrayed. "You can't write about me fairly because you're not me," he told me when I first mentioned the book.
"I'd like to read you parts of it," I offered, knowing that Rob didn't read books, not even ones about him. "I want you to know I tried to treat you fairly."
"That's ok, buddy," he said. "There are no hard feelings. I hope you sell a million copies."
He was as worried about my feelings as I was about his.
We talked about second chances and how he wanted to make the most of his, especially for his kids' sake.
Had Rob stayed healthy, would his recovery have lasted? Sadly, we'll never know.
A few days after my visit, Rob's cancer put him back in the hospital. We texted for a while, but that trailed off as he became weaker. On March 22, 2016, he died. He was 46.
I thought back to his 45th birthday, when I had compiled a CD of songs from 1969, the year he was born, and couriered it to him in rehab. The song list began with "Gimme Shelter" and ended optimistically with "Here Comes the Sun." In between, selections included "Dazed and Confused," "Fortunate Son," "Everyday People," Suspicious Minds," "Everybody's Talkin'," and the one I thought summed up Rob best, "The Boxer."
"In the clearing stands a boxer
And a fighter by his trade
And he carries the reminders
Of every glove that laid him down
Or cut him till he cried out
In his anger and his shame
'I am leaving, I am leaving'
But the fighter still remains."
Rob was touched. "John, that's the nicest thing anyone has ever done for me."
Then he asked if I would make him another playlist, and one for his Mom.
"Sure," I agreed. "What's your favourite song?"
"The same as everybody else," he answered. "'Stairway to Heaven'"
In the spring of 2015, when Rob was still alive but no longer mayor, and his brother was between political jobs, Doug granted me a final interview for my book. What he told me then now seems like a prophecy.
"We're only on Chapter 18 in a 30-chapter story about Rob or our family," he said. "Or myself...I'm just at the beginning of my career. And we've got a great future ahead of us!"
It's hard now to separate Rob's legacy from Doug's. Doug entered politics in 2010, arriving at city hall as councillor as Rob became mayor. Once there, Doug asserted himself as a sort of co-mayor, the comparatively more controlled older brother.
I've learned not to underestimate Doug Ford, but without that profile, and without the roadmap of Rob's folksy pathway to success, it's unlikely he would be looking towards a third term as Premier - or, I suspect, already eyeing what comes next: Prime Minister Ford? Mayor Ford II?
The more interesting question about Rob's legacy is how much he foreshadowed Donald Trump. I have found no evidence that Trump patterned himself after Rob, but the similarities of their appeal startled me so much in 2016 that I travelled to the U.S., my book on Rob in hand, to try to warn Democrats that I had seen this show before.
Still, it does Rob a disservice to compare him to a demagogue like Trump. With Trump, everything from the politics to the hair is contrived and fake. Rob's appeal was genuine. He attracted low-information voters, not by pandering to them, but because he was one himself.
There was a time when Toronto beheld a raging Rob Ford - in all his furious bluster and rumpled suit - and liked what it saw. And despite how badly it all turned out, what it saw was real.
I sure don't miss him as mayor. But I do miss him.
CAPTION: The Toronto Star's front page from Friday May 17, 2013, the morning after the Rob Ford crack scandal broke.A year into his term, Ford began to lose his grip on city council. With the darkness of failure closing in, his behaviour, always erratic, began to veer totally out of control.It's impossible to know what Doug Ford's career would have looked like had his brother not paved the way.On November 5, 2013, Ford shocked the world when he admitted, without prompting, that yes, he had smoked crack cocaine.To the end, Ford remained a hero to his base.John Filion spent 15 years as Rob Ford's political adversary and personal friend.Ford died on March 22, 2016 at Mount Sinai Hospital, in Toronto. He was 46 years old.Behind closed doors, the Rob Ford who returned to city council after his first round of chemotherapy was a different man, according to John Filion, his longtime adversary and friend.
CREDIT: John Filion

Full text: Despite all his failings, Ford leads in the polls
Our health care system is in crisis. Our schools desperately need repairs. Homelessness is the worst it's ever been. Our food banks have never been busier. Transit projects are years behind schedule and massively over budget. Yet, Premier Doug Ford is preoccupied with:
Rushing toward an election a year before it's scheduled and before the results of an RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal are published.
Ensuring that beer, wine and alcoholic cocktails are available everywhere, even though it cost more than $250 million in cancellation fees to the Beer Store.
Ripping out bike lanes that keep us safe. Contrary to the premier's rhetoric, removing the bike lanes will result in even more cars on the road and worse congestion.
Bribing the electorate with our own money to the tune of $3 billion, none of which would go to the poorest, most vulnerable people in our society who don't have a fixed address or bank accounts.
Threatening to use the notwithstanding clause to implement laws to rip out homeless encampments.
Getting rid of safe injection sites, which have saved the lives of hundreds of people suffering overdoses.
Granting a foreign company a sweetheart deal to build a giant spa and waterpark at Ontario Place that no one asked for, and that according to our auditor general was done without following normal procedures. The cost of this project has already ballooned by $1.8 billion.
Defending an incompetent Metrolinx organization whose mission seems to be overpaying for everything, and never delivering.
Spending hundreds of millions of tax dollars on advertising to promote his "accomplishments."
And yet, this premier is ahead in the polls. Ontario, can we not do better?
Giacomo Tonon, Willowdale
Ford is failing Ontarians
Ford's get-tough encampment plan coming, but Ontarians are divided over the approach, survey suggests, Dec. 9
Personally, I hold Premier Doug Ford responsible for any deaths from exposure to the elements, due to lack of housing this winter. Ford needs to spend our tax dollars more efficiently instead of wasting our money on his whims like a spa at Ontario Place. Ford needs to get moving and grow up and do the job he was elected to do. So far, he is failing miserably.
Lillian Shery, Toronto
What's the point in arresting the homeless?
Here in Peterborough, the police are starting a new policy to arrest the homeless. The problem with this is it will go nowhere. The courts may impose a fine, which they cannot pay, jail, which is not feasible as they are full, or probation. What is the answer? Is there one?
Barb Gabovic, Peterborough
Ontario will bear the brunt of Ford's retaliation
Doug Ford warns of 'cutting off their energy' to retaliate against tariffs by Donald Trump, Dec. 11
Sometimes I wonder if Premier Doug Ford thinks before he speaks. The much-disputed Enbridge Line 5 pipeline carries 500,000 barrels of western crude to Sarnia each day. It passes through Wisconsin and Michigan. Ontario also imports natural gas from the U.S. If there is one thing we know about Donald Trump, it's that he believes in getting even. Ontario will bear the brunt of his vengeance.
Michael Yaffe, Toronto
Conservatives are following Republican lead
One of us thinks we need a federal election ASAP. The other thinks that's a terrible idea, Opinion, Dec. 7
Susan Delacourt characterizes the Tories perfectly as an opposition with zero interest in making Parliament or the government work. There is no reason to believe that would change if they held power. This is exactly the way the Republican Party behaved during the Obama administration and look where they've gone since. The right has positioned itself as anti-government since the Reagan era. Delacourt also, sardonically I hope, asks if Premier Doug Ford should speak for Canada.
James Rooney, Toronto
Bullying Trump insults a key ally
As an American, I feel Canada deserves an apology for our president-elect's resent remarks. The diminution of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as "governor" and the "Great State of Canada" was not said in jest. It was an intentional insult by a bully, one without concern for one of America's most important allies.
David Warfel, Orchard Park, N.Y.
Hydrant a thing of beauty and joy
A fire hydrant located right in the middle of a busy Toronto sidewalk. What was the city thinking? Dec. 11
The brilliant yellow, blue and black hydrant square in the middle of a grey Eglinton Avenue sidewalk offers no harm to anyone paying attention and adds a flash of colour to the bland streetscape that surely most people appreciate. And I'll bet many dogs who are walked along that treeless stretch have come to appreciate it as well.
Harvey Starkman, Toronto
Investors come before residents
I volunteer at a busy food bank and I've learned something that should make everyone angry, Phillips, Dec. 10
This is what happens when a city prioritizes investors over residents. Toronto has a surplus of greed and poor planning.
Richard Kadziewicz,
Scarborough

Document 375 of 500

Human rights are a pain. And, with apologies to Toronto and Doug Ford, they're supposed to be

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/human-rights-are-pain-with-apologies-toronto-doug/docview/3143664552/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Human+rights+are+a+pain.+And%2C+with+apologies+to+Toronto+and+Doug+Ford%2C+they%27re+supposed+to+be&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Next week, when city council considers ombudsman Kwame Addo's report on the city denying shelter beds to refugee claimants in 2023, they'll have a lot to chew over. But for most of us, something that might jump out as particularly revealing is city manager Paul Johnson's response. Not just because he refused to accept the ombudsman's findings. But because he explains that the city of Toronto's recognition, enshrined in its Housing Charter, that people have a "right to adequate housing" is "aspirational."
This is something Addo is naively misunderstanding, Johnson suggests. When the city says - when it codifies in its laws - that people have a "right," that doesn't confer any obligation on authorities to respect that right. It just indicates an item tacked on the civic vision board. A stretch goal.
Now, as a member of the public service tasked with doing a job and then not being provided with the resources to do it fully, perhaps this is a necessary understanding for the city manager to adopt. Still, it is clarifying.
Because, typically, recognizing a right confers specific obligations on a government. Typically, when they come into conflict with a right, laws must be overturned, buildings must be rebuilt, arrests must be thrown out. In our system, human rights are not so much "aspirational" as "foundational."
Except, you know, not in our housing and shelter system. There, our city council has acknowledged what it says are human rights, but has not made adequate financial provision for those rights to be honoured.
In that, they are not alone.
Take the issue the ombudsman's report is examining: the housing of refugee claimants.
Addo's report says the city's staff improperly used immigration status in allocating shelter space, that in doing so they unintentionally introduced systemic racism into the system and that staff did not adjust these policies quickly enough after receiving council instruction.
But please note, the whole scenario was a response to the city scrambling to find homes for refugee claimants admitted to Canada by another level of government. Our federal government proudly admits those fleeing persecution, torture or war in other countries. It has an obligation under international law to admit them - one it not only recognizes but embraces and celebrates. In June, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau trumpeted our pride in accepting this commitment: "Let's help those who are most in need, because that's when Canada is at its best."
But once we welcome those people to Canada, our federal government leaves too many of them to sleep on the street.
Now, the federal government has stepped in since the incidents discussed in the ombudsman's report to increase its own funding for housing refugees. But in October, Mayor Olivia Chow said there were still 6,000 refugees in Toronto's shelter system, occupying half of its total capacity.
Or take the provincial government, whose leaders on Thursday stood up and said encampments of homeless people must be cleared. "Enough is enough. This has to stop," Premier Doug Ford said. In that he is stating the obvious: It is intolerable that public parks should be used as flophouses.
But there is the simple fact that the people using them that way, right now, have nowhere else to go. In Toronto, the emergency homeless shelter system turns away an average of 225 people seeking beds each day because it is full. The waiting list for subsidized housing ranges from eight to 14 years.
Ford's government announced $20 million in new shelter funding, alongside $5.5 million in rent supplements and $50 million in affordable housing money, that it says will solve this problem. That allocation of dollars is needed and welcome. But $75.5 million across the province seems unlikely to be adequate. The current budget for emergency homeless shelters in Toronto alone is $789 million.
Now, it is traditional, when considering the scope of the problem and our failure to deal with it, to say we just don't have the resources. But this is manifestly untrue. The provincial government is in the process of spending $3.2 billion to send every adult in Ontario a personal $200 cheque. The federal government has its own $4.68-billion scheme to send out $250 cheques. The city of Toronto is spending $380 million to host five World Cup games. We have resources, and we are making choices in how to allocate them.
Housing is a human right. Canada welcomes refugees. Encampments in parks are intolerable.
Our governments are good at saying the right words. But every time you see a person sleeping on a ventilation grate on the street, or camping in a park, or shivering under a pile of newspapers in a doorway, you're seeing evidence of their unwillingness to give their words meaning.
If we're speaking of aspirations, perhaps we should elect governments who aspire to mean what they say. Especially when the consequence of not doing so is to allow people to live through Canadian winters without shelter.
CAPTION: An ombudsman's report has sharply criticized the city's response to the refugee claimant crisis in the summer of 2023, during which the city denied shelter beds to migrants.

Document 376 of 500

Ford government's encampment crackdown will have people cycling through shelters and jails. There's a better way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-governments-encampment-crackdown-will-have/docview/3143664537/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government%27s+encampment+crackdown+will+have+people+cycling+through+shelters+and+jails.+There%27s+a+better+way&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Homeless encampments have become a regular fixture in cities across Canada. Unsheltered homelessness, including people staying in encampments, rose 88 per cent across the country from 2018 to 2022, according to the last national point-in-time count.
In Ontario, advocates, clinicians, researchers, mayors, and city councillors, in addition to people experiencing homelessness, have been sounding the alarm, calling on the provincial government to act. Last week, Premier Ford responded to those calls by signalling that he would introduce legislation to provide municipalities with "enhanced legislative powers" to dismantle encampments.
Then on Thursday, the government introduced new legislation giving police more power to punish drug use in public parks, and penalties for those who continually 'trespass' in parks and other properties. These changes are all part of the Ford government's plans to address encampments.
The premier also pledged $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing across the province. While this is a much-needed investment, building sufficient affordable housing will take time and additional resources. Where will encampment residents go in the meantime?
The government's approach to dismantling encampments in effect puts forward three potential paths for residents: shelter, jail, or hospital. These options, known for decades as the institutional circuit, do not resolve the primary unmet need of encampment residents: rapid access to housing with supports. Moreover, shelter, jail, and hospitals represent the most expensive types of services where you can find people experiencing homelessness.
Dismantling encampments may also cause further harm to those residing there. A U.S. simulation study found that involuntary displacement, such as encampment dismantlement, produces additional overdose deaths . Involuntary displacement also results in more hospitalizations as well as reduced initiation of medications for opioid use disorder, the first-line treatment for this condition. This research strongly suggests that dismantling encampments in Ontario would have a similar result: increased overdose fatalities and reduced access to addiction treatment.
As researchers and clinicians working with people who are homeless, we know there is a better and more efficient way to reduce the number of encampments in Ontario: a housing-first intervention. That involves the provision of a rent supplement along with community-based mental health and addiction supports to help those with serious mental illness, substance use disorders, and chronic medical conditions immediately access permanent housing. Research from the Canadian At Home/Chez Soi demonstration project - the largest randomized controlled trial of the housing-first approach conducted anywhere in the world - found that the intervention is effective in housing 80-90 per cent of people experiencing long-term homelessness. Followup research conducted in Toronto showed that these outcomes were sustained as many as six years later.
Housing-first intervention is smart and cost-effective. The cost is partially offset by a reduction in the use of health care, shelter, and criminal justice services. Many hospital visits by people experiencing homelessness are preventable with housing and the right supports, giving the housing-first approach a role to play in reducing hallway medicine in the province.
It is not just in research where we see housing-first's effectiveness. The City of Houston has become a leader in the U.S. when it comes to ending homelessness. In 2011, the city had approximately 8,500 people who were homeless. A decade later, they had reduced that by 61 per cent. Unsheltered homelessness is also down 33 per cent in the last four years. Houston's approach? Housing first.
We agree with the premier that enough is enough: It's time to end unsheltered homelessness in Ontario. But, let's not waste more money shuffling people experiencing homelessness around the institutional circuit. Instead, let's leverage a housing-first intervention, a proven way to get people the housing and support they need. This approach is not only more fiscally prudent for taxpayers, it is a more sustainable solution for improving access and safety in our parks and other public spaces.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media outside of his office in Queen's Park, in Toronto, on Dec. 11, 2024.
CREDIT: Nick Kerman andVicky Stergiopoulos

Subject: Affordable housing; Homeless people; Intervention

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Location: United States--US
