Document 391 of 500

'Americans are going to feel the pain,' Ford warns: Canada could cut off energy exports to states in retaliation for Trump tariffs, premier says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/americans-are-going-feel-pain-ford-warns/docview/3143190470/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Americans+are+going+to+feel+the+pain%2C%27+Ford+warns%3A+Canada+could+cut+off+energy+exports+to+states+in+retaliation+for+Trump+tariffs%2C+premier+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford warned Canada could cut off energy flowing to the United States in retaliation for Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.
The premier struck a much tougher tone in the face of what he said is a certainty: crippling 25 per cent U.S. tariffs on all products that the president-elect will impose on Canada and Mexico on his first day in office. He threatened that Canada, or at least Ontario, is prepared to hit back hard.
"We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy - going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin," the premier said following what he called a "very constructive" first ministers meeting Wednesday.
"Americans are going to feel the pain as well," Ford said, adding, "And isn't that sad."
During the virtual gathering of the 13 provincial and territorial leaders, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau laid out a $1-billion border security spending plan, including drones, helicopters and personnel. The Liberal government hopes the measures will persuade Trump that Canada is a secure economic partner and lead to a tariff exemption.
Ford called the federal plan "a good start" as he offered a more pessimistic outlook, saying Trump's vow is not a bluff and "we need to be ready to fight" with hefty Canadian import surcharges on U.S. goods that will punish Americans in return.
"I don't want this to happen. But we'll use every tool in our tool box, including cutting them off from energy that we're sending down there. This fight is coming 100 per cent on Jan. 20," he told reporters at Queen's Park.
In Ottawa, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland stuck to the Liberal government's line that it is reaching out to American counterparts to avoid tariffs, but she also said "the prime minister was clear in Halifax on Monday that Canada, of course, would respond to unjustified tariffs."
Freeland, the finance minister, admitted several premiers "proactively" identified key products their provinces ship to the U.S., including critical minerals and metals, that should be included in a "robust" retaliatory tariff list her office is working on.
But Freeland dodged repeated questions about whether Ottawa would list energy exports to the U.S. for counter-tariffs, saying only that the Liberal government knows "this is a serious moment for Canada."
While Ontario and Quebec export electricity to the U.S., about 60 per cent of oil used stateside comes from Alberta.
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said the government would share its border security plan with the Trump team before it would be publicly revealed to Canadians in the coming days, but said he welcomed provincial offers to augment border resources.
It was the second meeting of Canada's first ministers since Trump's Nov. 25 social media post warning he would impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican products as of his Jan. 20 inauguration unless America's neighbours tighten their borders to stop illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Prior to the first ministers meeting, Trudeau took an indirect dig at Trump, who has derided him as the "governor of the great state of Canada" on social media.
Despite urging lawmakers to back "Team Canada" and not undermine our national interest, the prime minister startled some at the Equal Voice gala on Tuesday night in Ottawa when he lamented the defeat of Kamala Harris in the U.S. election on Nov. 5.
At the event to boost political participation of women, Trudeau said in French: "There are politicians who fight and vote against women's right to choose (and) are regressive and reactionary forces that want us to go backwards and unfortunately succeed all too often."
"We were supposed to be on a steady, if difficult sometimes, march towards progress. And yet, just a few weeks ago, the United States voted for a second time to not elect its first woman president.
"Everywhere, women's rights and women's progress is under attack, overtly and subtly. I want you to know that I am and always will be a proud feminist. You will always have an ally in me and in my government."
Ford said several premiers chided the prime minister on their Wednesday night call, telling him his comments were "not helpful at all ...
"Donald Trump was elected democratically. If you like him or you don't like him, that's not our issue," said Ford, conceding Trump is "being a little more aggressive than he was in the past ... he's a different type of cat, to say the least."
In response to Trudeau's comments, Elon Musk, the billionaire tech mogul and key Trump adviser, fired back at the prime minister.
"He's such an insufferable tool. Won't be in power for much longer," Musk wrote on X, the social media platform he purchased for $44 billion (U.S.) in 2022 that's now worth around $4.2 billion.
Trudeau's comments were the first hint of public criticism of Trump, the Republican leader whose judicial and other appointments have sought to limit abortion rights in the U.S.
The prime minister has spent two weeks appealing across party and government lines to forge a common Canadian front to fight Trump's threatened tariffs, and urging lawmakers here not to "freak out" or "panic" in their responses.
In recent days, Canadian conservative politicians have met with Trump and his inner circle.
Quebec Premier François Legault met Trump and Musk in Paris at the reopening of Notre Dame cathedral last Friday.
Conservative MP Jamil Jivani met Trump's vice-president-elect, J.D. Vance, his close friend from their Yale Law School days. They met last weekend at a conference of the International Democratic Union - headed by Stephen Harper - in Washington.
Jivani and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre have been silent on what was discussed with Vance.
As well, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith was in Las Vegas to meet western U.S. governors earlier this week.
Next Monday in Toronto, Ford is hosting a Council of the Federation meeting in Toronto to further discuss the Trump tariff threat.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 392 of 500

Doug Ford to crack down on homeless encampments with fines for drug use and more funding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-crack-down-on-homeless-encampments-with/docview/3143190420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+to+crack+down+on+homeless+encampments+with+fines+for+drug+use+and+more+funding&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amid rising homelessness and an explosion of tent encampments in parks, Premier Doug Ford will earmark an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing - and toughen up trespassing laws.
"Enough is enough. This has to stop and it will stop. Families deserve to play in and enjoy their parks without fearing for the safety of their kids," Ford said Thursday as critics warned he's criminalizing homelessness, which has grown worse under his watch.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks for people and communities," said the premier.
His comments came on the final day of the legislative session as the Progressive Conservatives introduced changes that will immediately give municipalities more money for shelter and services for homeless people.
But the legislation to give police more power to punish scofflaws would not be in force until next the spring at the earliest.
Pressure has been mounting on Ford to act because encampments have spread throughout the province and many mayors - and constituents - have appealed to him for help.
Municipalities will have to start tracking how many homeless people are moved from encampments into new accommodations and report that to the province to justify how the funds are being spent.
"We want to help people, we don't want to punish people," the premier said at a Queen's Park press conference where he announced amendments to the Trespass to Property Act.
With the government extending the legislature's winter break by two weeks - meaning MPPs won't return until March 3 - the proposed legislation on new powers would not go to a final vote for weeks after that.
Liberal Leader Bonnie dismissed the plan as "political grandstanding."
"When we come back, we will likely be going to an election," said Crombie.
Andrew Boozary, a physician and professor at the University of Toronto's Dalla Lana School of Public Health, noted incarcerating someone costs triple the monthly amount of operating a single supportive housing unit.
"Housing is a matter of human dignity and sound health economics," said Boozary.
Harini Sivalingam, director of the equality program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association, expressed concern the new measures "will criminalize unhoused people living in encampments."
Under the trespass changes aimed at encampment dwellers, there would be a new offence of "continuous trespass" that judges could consider as an "aggravating factor" along with gauging the "likelihood to reoffend," said Attorney General Doug Downey.
"That will give greater tools to the judiciary and for others enforcing the law. We're quite confident this will be effective," said Downey.
The new laws also take aim at narcotics use in parks, empowering police and provincial offences officers to order people in encampments to stop using illegal drugs or face fines up to $10,000 or arrest and a maximum of six months in prison.
Opposition parties questioned the rationale and whether the extra funding is enough to make a difference, blaming Ford for the skyrocketing number of encampments since he took office in 2018.
"Look, if someone has $10,000 to pay a fine, I doubt they're living in a park," said New Democrat MPP Jessica Bell (University-Rosedale).
"It is cheaper to find a permanent home for someone than it is to send them to jail, and it's a lot more effective in getting them to rebuild their lives. This government's priorities are all wrong," said Bell.
Crombie added that Ford needs to show a detailed plan on "where we will house them other than in our jails, and give them the treatment they need."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner predicted the measures are "not going to work" because there is a chronic shortage of affordable housing.
"The Ford government has failed to build homes and now they want to throw people in jail," said Schreiner.
Included in the immediate $75.5 million cash infusion is $50 million for "ready-to-build affordable housing projects" across Ontario, $20 million to expand shelter capacity and a $5.5 million to the Canada-Ontario Housing Benefit to free up shelter spaces for encampment dwellers.
That's atop the annual provincial $700 million for homelessness prevention programs and the $378 million on the 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs that are replacing the 10 supervised drug injection sites the government is closing in March.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said local authorities would be held more accountable for the tent clusters in their cities and towns.
"Encampments are a public safety concern and not a solution to homelessness," said Calandra.
"We do not believe that somebody suffering from mental health and addiction is best treated in an encampment," he said.
In the wake of court rulings in Waterloo and Kingston, Ford threatened to use the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" to override any legal hurdles that would prevent the removal of tents from parks, though he believes the legislative changes could withstand a Charter challenge.
There are about 1,000 people living in Toronto parks despite existing city bylaws that prohibit camping or lighting fires and a flood of daily calls from concerned residents to the 311 customer service line.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who was not among the municipal leaders with Ford at the announcement, said while the city welcomed the extra provincial funding, "we cannot arrest or jail our way out of the homelessness crisis."
Chow said she would like to see thousands more supportive housing units, increased rent supplements, a rise in social assistance rates, and the reinstatement of rent controls.
The mayor said those measures would help homeless people "get back on their feet, rather than forcing them to move from space to space outside when they have nowhere to go."
Ford's political adversaries insist his approach is not enough to deal with something as complex as homelessness, which involves addiction, mental health as well as the soaring cost of rent.
His bill comes after a dozen mayors urged him to enact tougher laws to help them cope with encampments that have sprouted up across Ontario since the COVID-19 pandemic.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown, who was at Queen's Park for the announcement with more than a dozen other mayors, applauded the measures to clamp down on drug use in encampments because authorities have been "turning a blind eye" to it.
"This is helpful. We don't want to see fentanyl left behind in parks," said Brown, noting Brampton generally has enough space in its shelters but some homeless people don't want to stay in them because of a no-drugs policy.
With files from Ben Spurr and Victoria Gibson
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to the media outside of his office in the Queen's Park Legislature on Wednesday.

Document 393 of 500

Trudeau government's carbon price has had 'minimal' effect on inflation and food costs, study concludes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-carbon-price-has-had-minimal/docview/3143190195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+carbon+price+has+had+%27minimal%27+effect+on+inflation+and+food+costs%2C+study+concludes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Carbon pricing - including the Liberal government's carbon levy-and-rebate scheme - had a small impact on the rate of inflation over the past five years, when the climate policy was responsible for a "small fraction" of the surge in consumer prices, according to a new study.
Published Thursday, the report from University of Calgary economists Trevor Tombe and Jennifer Winter concluded emissions pricing had a "minimal impact" on inflation since 2019. During that time, emissions pricing has contributed to pushing up overall consumer prices by 0.5 per cent, the report found.
That's roughly 1/38th of the overall 19-per-cent increase in consumer prices over the past five years, with the effect on food prices being "even smaller," the report said.
Rising costs grew into a front-line political concern as inflation surged around the globe during the COVID-19 pandemic, peaking in Canada at 8.1 per cent in June 2022.
"Most of the price increases were driven by global factors, such as surging energy prices and disruptions in supply chains, rather than domestic climate policies," the authors wrote in their report, which was published by the Institute for Research on Public Policy and used Statistics Canada's data on household expenditures and modelling tools to measure the effects of tax policies on goods and services.
It also used government figures on energy consumption and emissions intensity, among other things.
"While emissions pricing does influence costs, its role in driving inflation is relatively small compared to other economic pressures," the study concluded.
The findings come as the opposition Conservatives continue to demand an immediate "carbon tax election" to unseat Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's governing Liberals. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has regularly blamed the federal carbon levy-and-rebate scheme for juicing inflation and promises to "axe the tax" if he takes power in the next federal election.
In recent months, Poilievre has claimed that planned increases in the federal carbon price will cause an economic "nuclear winter" by 2030, when "inflation would run rampant and people would not be able to leave their homes or drive anywhere."
Experts use the term "nuclear winter" to describe the global devastation that would follow a major nuclear war, with some projections predicting billions of people would die.
Poilievre also regularly cites the federal carbon price during parliamentary debates when lamenting the increase in food prices, accusing the government of starving Canadians with the policy.
"Before he starves more people, why can we not have a carbon-tax election and axe the tax?" Poilievre said in Parliament on Oct. 30.
Tombe and Winter's report included figures from Statistics Canada that show how much carbon pricing increased the average cost of a host of goods in 2023, including a 0.42 per cent increase for food and non-alcoholic drinks.
The federal fuel charge also adds almost 18 cents per litre to the cost of gasoline, according to government figures.
Asked about the debate around the carbon price, Tombe told the Star that "exaggerated claims by politicians are not new," and that voters should be mindful of rhetorical stretches.
But he said both the Liberals and Conservatives are guilty of exaggerating, with the Tories inflating the costs of carbon pricing, and the government downplaying impacts on affordability.
"The costs of carbon pricing are measurable. They're real, but they're small," Tombe said, noting the Bank of Canada has also pegged the policy's contribution to annual inflation at 0.15 percentage points.
"We shouldn't be under the illusion that if we eliminate the carbon tax that the affordability challenges that we're facing will disappear. That's simply not the case," he said.
Since 2019, the federal government has required all provinces and territories to have pricing systems to discourage greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change, and to reward businesses and consumers for reducing them. Each jurisdiction can design its own system or adopt a made-in-Ottawa scheme that includes a system for industrial carbon pricing and a "fuel charge" on consumer fuels like gasoline. This federal fuel charge, which applies in Ontario, comes with quarterly rebates that are set to deliver $1,120 to a household of four in the province in the current fiscal year.
The federal government also sets a minimum level for the carbon price in all jurisdictions, which sits at $80 per tonne this year and is set to increase annually until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030.
The rebates for the federal fuel charge are also set to increase each year.
Tombe and Winter said these rebates offset the cost increases from carbon pricing for most households.
"This means that many families, particularly those with lower incomes, are shielded from the negative financial impact of emissions pricing and some may end up with a net financial gain," their report said.
The Liberals and Conservatives have clashed over this claim, which the government has made for years. The argument intensified after the independent Parliamentary Budget Officer concluded most households ultimately lose money because of national carbon pricing when the broader economic impacts of the policy on employment and income are taken into account.
In their study, Tombe and Winter said their analysis does not consider "broader economic burdens or environmental benefits" of climate policies, noting that action to reduce emissions inevitably comes with costs.
"That said, considering only the costs of climate action presents an incomplete picture," they wrote. "There are tangible benefits to climate action that should not be overlooked," including lowering damages and cost of adaptation to a changing world.
The Canadian Climate Institute has estimated that national carbon pricing is set to drive much of the country's projected emissions reductions over the next five years, although the consumer "fuel charge" is expected to be responsible for a smaller chunk of those cuts - eight to 14 per cent - compared with industrial pricing systems.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responds to a question in the House of Commons on Dec. 11, 2024.

Subject: Climate policy; Costs; Emissions; Carbon; Environmental tax; Rebates; Inflation; Consumer Price Index; Food prices; Climate action; Nuclear winter; Energy consumption; Climate change; Households; Prime ministers; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Environmental tax Rebates Inflation Consumer Price Index Food prices Tax elections

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Statistics Canada; NAICS: 926110

Title: Trudeau government's carbon price has had 'minimal' effect on inflation and food costs, study concludes

Levy had 'minimal' effect on inflation or food costs: Climate policy has pushed up overall consumer prices by 0.5%, study finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/levy-had-minimal-effect-on-inflation-food-costs/docview/3143189900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Levy+had+%27minimal%27+effect+on+inflation+or+food+costs%3A+Climate+policy+has+pushed+up+overall+consumer+prices+by+0.5%25%2C+study+finds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Carbon pricing - including the Liberal government's carbon levy-and-rebate scheme - had a small impact on the rate of inflation over the past five years, when the climate policy was responsible for a "small fraction" of the surge in consumer prices, according to a new study.
Published Thursday, the report from University of Calgary economists Trevor Tombe and Jennifer Winter concluded emissions pricing had a "minimal impact" on inflation since 2019. During that time, emissions pricing has contributed to pushing up overall consumer prices by 0.5 per cent, the report found.
That's roughly 1/38th of the overall 19 per cent increase in consumer prices over the past five years, with the effect on food prices being "even smaller," the report said.
Rising costs grew into a front-line political concern as inflation surged around the globe during the COVID-19 pandemic, peaking in Canada at 8.1 per cent in June 2022.
"Most of the price increases were driven by global factors, such as surging energy prices and disruptions in supply chains, rather than domestic climate policies," the authors wrote in their report, which was published by the Institute for Research on Public Policy and used Statistics Canada's data on household expenditures and modelling tools to measure the effects of tax policies on goods and services.
It also used government figures on energy consumption and emissions intensity, among other things.
"While emissions pricing does influence costs, its role in driving inflation is relatively small compared to other economic pressures," the study concluded.
The findings come as the opposition Conservatives continue to demand an immediate "carbon tax election" to unseat Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's governing Liberals. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has regularly blamed the federal carbon levy-and-rebate scheme for juicing inflation and promises to "axe the tax" if he takes power in the next federal election.
In recent months, Poilievre has claimed that planned increases in the federal carbon price will cause an economic "nuclear winter" by 2030, when "inflation would run rampant and people would not be able to leave their homes or drive anywhere."
Experts use the term "nuclear winter" to describe the global devastation that would follow a major nuclear war, with some projections predicting billions of people would die.
Poilievre also regularly cites the federal carbon price during parliamentary debates when lamenting the increase in food prices, accusing the government of starving Canadians with the policy.
"Before he starves more people, why can we not have a carbon-tax election and axe the tax?" Poilievre said in Parliament on Oct. 30.
Tombe and Winter's report included figures from Statistics Canada that show how much carbon pricing increased the average cost of a host of goods in 2023, including a 0.42 per cent increase for food and non-alcoholic drinks.
The federal fuel charge also adds almost 18 cents per litre to the cost of gasoline, according to government figures.
Asked about the debate around the carbon price, Tombe told the Star that "exaggerated claims by politicians are not new," and that voters should be mindful of rhetorical stretches.
But he said both the Liberals and Conservatives are guilty of exaggerating, with the Tories inflating the costs of carbon pricing, and the government downplaying impacts on affordability.
"The costs of carbon pricing are measurable. They're real, but they're small," Tombe said, noting the Bank of Canada has also pegged the policy's contribution to annual inflation at 0.15 percentage points."We shouldn't be under the illusion that if we eliminate the carbon tax that the affordability challenges that we're facing will disappear. That's simply not the case," he said.
Since 2019, the federal government has required all provinces and territories to have pricing systems to discourage greenhouse gas emissions that cause climate change, and to reward businesses and consumers for reducing them. Each jurisdiction can design its own system or adopt a made-in-Ottawa scheme that includes a system for industrial carbon pricing and a "fuel charge" on consumer fuels like gasoline. This federal fuel charge, which applies in Ontario, comes with quarterly rebates that are set to deliver $1,120 to a household of four in the province in the current fiscal year.
The federal government also sets a minimum level for the carbon price in all jurisdictions, which sits at $80 per tonne this year and is set to increase annually until it hits $170 per tonne in 2030. The rebates for the federal fuel charge are also set to increase each year.
Tombe and Winter said these rebates offset the cost increases from carbon pricing for most households.
CAPTION: The University of Calgary study comes as the opposition Conservatives continue to demand an immediate "carbon tax election" to unseat Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's governing Liberals.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Climate policy; Costs; Emissions; Carbon; Environmental tax; Rebates; Inflation; Consumer Price Index; Food prices; Nuclear winter; Energy consumption; Prime ministers; Climate change; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Environmental tax Rebates Inflation Consumer Price Index Food prices Tax elections

People: Trudeau, Justin

PCs allow natural gas plants to bid on power plan: Grid expansion was supposed to use only green energy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-allow-natural-gas-plants-bid-on-power-plan/docview/3143189865/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+allow+natural+gas+plants+to+bid+on+power+plan%3A+Grid+expansion+was+supposed+to+use+only+green+energy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce supercharged the province's plan to generate more power Wednesday, increasing the public procurement by 50 per cent to meet surging demand for electricity.
But the Progressive Conservatives' project to expand the grid, which was originally supposed to be achieved exclusively with green energy, has quietly been altered to let new natural gas plants bid.
"Our province needs much more energy," Lecce said at a news conference in Brampton, where he announced that the previous electricity procurement of 5,000 megawatts will expand to 7,500, the equivalent of 20 per cent of all power generation currently available in Ontario.
Because the procurement is a competitive one, it could serve as a real-world demonstration of whether renewable power generation - such as wind and solar - is actually cheaper than natural gas, as many studies and international bodies have claimed.
"This will be a really interesting litmus test to watch these competitive forces at play," said Scott MacDougall, electricity program director at the Pembina Institute, a clean-energy think tank.
Critics, however, accuse the Ford government of putting its thumb on the scale to ensure new natural gas is built.
"We actually weren't worried initially because we thought that renewables were going to win most if not all of this, if it was a truly competitive process," said Aliénor Rougeot, climate and energy program manager at Environmental Defence.
"It's actually only looking at the fine print that we realized this process is not technology agnostic at all. That's why we're likely going to see way less renewable energy."
The Independent Electricity Systems Operator (IESO), the Crown corporation responsible for operating the electricity market, recently revised its projections for electricity demand upward from 60 per cent more power in 2050 to 75 per cent - the equivalent of adding four and a half cities the size of Toronto to the grid. This surge is being driven by efforts to combat climate change through electrification of heating and transport, and the new industrial production needed to build EVs and heat pumps.
Originally announced last year by then-energy minister Todd Smith, the green power procurement was an abrupt about-face for Premier Doug Ford's government, which had cancelled hundreds of renewable projects at a cost of $231 million shortly after being elected in 2018. But Smith was shuffled out of the energy portfolio last summer, and resigned from government shortly thereafter. In late August, Smith's replacement, Lecce, altered the terms of the energy procurement, removing the requirement that new generation be "non-emitting" and instead proclaiming that the process would be "technology agnostic."
Rougeot says the recently released Request for Proposals documents go further than that by "stacking the deck against renewables ... in practice, they're making it very hard for renewables to compete."
The RFPs include a points system that awards more points for things that gas can do but renewables cannot, such as guarantee 12 continuous hours of power. Solar and wind power are reliant on weather.
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Electricity; Alternative energy sources; Government purchasing; Climate change; Natural gas

Downtown construction projects didn't always block traffic lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/downtown-construction-projects-didnt-always-block/docview/3143189740/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Downtown+construction+projects+didn%27t+always+block+traffic+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's traffic is a nightmare. Here's what some experts say is the biggest culprit and what the city can do about it, Dec. 9
I was so glad to see the Star finally draw attention to the way Toronto streets are constantly being reduced by at least one lane when construction begins on major buildings. But I was disappointed that the writers did not include the history of the construction of First Canadian Place, the Bank of Montreal building at the northwest corner of King and Bay streets in 1975, and include mention of the methods of delivery created by Olympia and York.
To limit interference with traffic on the surrounding streets, builders Olympia and York constructed a large turntable in what would eventually be the basement of the building, so that multiple trucks could go off the street and onto the site to deliver their loads, instead of occupying a lane on the street while waiting to unload. I'm not aware of this method having been used anywhere else, though it may have been. This example shows that it might not be unreasonable for other companies starting construction of major buildings in Toronto to be told they cannot close off any street lanes for deliveries, and that all deliveries would have to be made on-site. I appreciate that the need for installation of additional water and power delivery to new buildings would probably require some lane closures, but surely the current level of closures has now reached unacceptable heights, and new delivery arrangements for construction materials could become mandatory.
Philip Wedge, Toronto
Tax the snowbirds in response to Trumps tariff threats
Donald Trump threatens 25% tariff on Canadian products; Doug Ford, premiers call for urgent action, Nov. 26
There is a better way to respond to President-elect Donald Trump's 25 per cent tariff threat that will be much more painful to the U.S. economy: tax snowbirds. Every year more than a million Canadians head south for up six months. They are supporting the U.S. economy at the expense of the Canadian economy. They support the U.S. housing market, entertainment industry (restaurants, golf and tennis clubs, etc.) and spend on daily living expenses. If they get sick they race home for free health care. Taxing senior citizens is not popular, but neither is seeing the cost of imported stuff go up by 25 per cent. Taxing Canadians who are away from the country for more than 30 days and want to keep their health care seems fair. Supporting Trump should come with consequences. Drastic times require drastic measures.
Michael Yaffe, Toronto
Trudeau is not governor
Donald Trump calls Justin Trudeau 'Governor' of the 'Great State of Canada,' Dec. 10
Donald Trump is wrong. Justin Trudeau is only the Prime Minister of this state. The true ruler of Canada, who is the representative of the monarch King Charles III, is Governor General Mary Simon. She is Commander-in-Chief of the Canadian Armed Forces and exercises virtually all the powers of the Crown. She is the one Trump should have dinner with next time.
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
Trump and Putin think alikeDonald Trump's comment to Justin Trudeau about Canada becoming '51st state' was a joke, minister who attended dinner says, Dec. 3
President-elect Donald Trump wants to absorb Canada and make it the 51st state. This is something that most Canadians would object to, but unfortunately our military would likely not be able to prevent it. This sounds like Russian President Vladimir Putin's attitude toward Ukraine. No wonder he and Trump are friends. They clearly think alike.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
Do we need Canada Post?
It is time to rethink Canada Post. Most of us do our transactions/business online. Why not reduce deliveries to twice per week and add more corner boxes. Reduce staff drastically. As far as packages, setup Purolator to compete with the big boys. We cannot continue with this inefficiency anymore.
Tom Steele, Peterborough
Without quality post-secondary education Canada will suffer
Where are the leaders of today? A cry commonly heard these days as people struggle , but the real cry should be: Where are the leaders of tomorrow? The lack of funding for colleges and universities is resulting in program cuts denying educational opportunities. Freezing tuition costs as Premier Doug Ford has done simply reduces the number of places available. No one wants to raise tuition, but if this was done it would at least provide spaces for those who can afford it; spaces currently being lost to funding cuts. In a country where we manufacture very little, our growth is relegated to financial services, innovation, technology and the service sector. Without high quality and broadly available post-secondary education that growth will be concentrated in the service sector alone, and we will all suffer for it in the future. To put it in terms the premier will understand, fewer people will be able to afford the new spa at Ontario Place.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Not a good look for a wannabe Prime MinisterPierre Poilievre says it shouldn't take Donald Trump to make the Liberals sort out a fentanyl crisis, Dec. 10
At a time when our federal government is dealing with Donald Trump and conflicts over trade and the border, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre does nothing but snipe and take cheap shots at Trudeau. His actions weaken and undermine our Prime Minister at a time when we need to show a strong united front. This is not a good look for someone who wants desperately to be the next Prime Minister.
Achim Krull, Pickering
Let's get on with decarbonizing our economyAs environment minister, I believed the oilsands sector would help us save the planet. I was wrong, Dec. 8
The oil and gas industry betrayed Catherine McKenna and they are misleading all of us. Zap the crap, and get on with decarbonizing our economy. Otherwise as McKenna concludes we will "leave our children a deadly and unsustainable future."
Doug Pritchard, Toronto
Ford's role is to be a custodian of the province not to destroy it
What a legacy Premier Doug Ford will leave this province. The auditor general's report provides a revealing overview of the long-term costs to the taxpayer of the Ontario Place lease, but it doesn't cover the emotional devastation caused by the loss of Toronto's most iconic cultural features: the Ontario Science Centre, Ontario Place and its lakeside forest as well as Casa Loma's heritage landscape. The role of government is to look after "the common good" not destroy it.
Barb Heidenreich, Toronto

Document 397 of 500

Is Ford Ontario's worst economic enemy?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-ford-ontarios-worst-economic-enemy/docview/3143189700/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Ford+Ontario%27s+worst+economic+enemy%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is braced for the worst from Donald Trump's looming tariffs, but consider a more bracing reality.
What if Ford is his own - and Ontario's - worst economic enemy? What if, instead of the incoming U.S. president, it is the long-serving Ontario premier who is the problem?
No one knows with any certainty whether Trump will impose his threatened 25 per cent tariffs.
But we do know with statistical certainty that Ontario is already facing serious economic bumps today - not from Trump's tariffs but Ford's fecklessness.
Unemployment in Ontario continues its relentless climb, leaving the province an outlier - exceeded nationally only by Newfoundland and Prince Edward Island. The news is not good, the outlook is not encouraging:
The rate jumped almost a full percentage point from 6.8 per cent to 7.6 per cent last month, according to the latest Statistics Canada data. That puts the province in pandemic territory, rather than a recovery trajectory.
In fact, if you exclude peak COVID, you'd have to go back a full decade to find unemployment at such high levels, as StatCan observed in an accompanying commentary. Back in 2014, Kathleen Wynne won her first election as the province's Liberal premier and presided over a decline in unemployment to 5.6 per cent through 2018, when Ford won power.
Which means that, far from helming the roaring economic engine of Canada, the premier is presiding over a period of decline. And has become a drag on the country.
"The decline in November was concentrated in Ontario, where manufacturing employment fell by 20,000 (-2.5%)," the non-partisan agency noted pointedly.
During Wynne's time as premier, the manufacturing sector increased by about 18,400 jobs. In the past 12 months under Ford, Ontario has lost nearly twice that many manufacturing jobs (33,000), the figures show.
Meanwhile, the overall unemployment rate is declining or flatlining in neighbouring provinces with large economies. Unemployment is a comparatively healthy 5.9 per cent in neighbouring Quebec and 5.8 per cent in Manitoba.
Let's admit, however, the limitations of having fun with numbers, no matter how unfunny. Let us not forget the old adage, in economics as in politics, that there are three kinds of lies:
Lies, damned lies and statistics.
By that measure, Ford is an expert practitioner of all three. For as long as he has been premier and even before, he has pilloried his opponents as economic charlatans and portrayed himself as a prudent steward of the province's industrial heartland.
Not a day goes by that he doesn't proclaim this truism to be self-evidently true - without any evidence beyond a statistical sleight of hand and a rhetorical trick. As for those annoying economic indicators, Ford insists they are merely a reflection of robust immigration numbers leading to more people chasing jobs.
Unfortunately for him, Statistics Canada notes that while the labour force rose by 0.4 per cent in November, it was "offsetting a similar-sized decline in October" - in other words, a wash - while the unemployment rate jumped.
To put this in perspective, Ontario's unemployment rate has soared by a full 1.5 percentage points over the past 12 months. Against that backdrop, our premier is not only unprepared but unrepentant: "We are the envy of North America, we're the envy of the world," Ford boasted lustily in the legislature.
"You wonder why president-elect Trump wants to put tariffs? Because we're a threat to the U.S., because we're a manufacturing powerhouse. We created more manufacturing jobs last year than all 50 U.S. states combined."
Never mind that the independent statistical agency says we've been losing those manufacturing jobs over the past year. That's an awfully bad baseline to boast about, before any Trump tariffs add more fuel to the fires of unemployment.
To his credit, Ford has recently been relatively restrained about bilateral boasting and domestic dissing. When America's president-elect publicly trolls Justin Trudeau, the premier rallies to the defence of the prime minister and downplays Trump's puerile mockery as merely humorous musings.
But it's fair to say that Ford got carried away with his own hyperbole this week by claiming that Trump's threats are proof he's feeling threatened. As illogical as those tariff threats might be in terms of economic theories of trade and comparative advantage, they have their own inexorable logic.
Given how intertwined our supply chains are, the tariffs may be empty threats that never come to pass, but we will still pay a price. For there is method to Trump's madness, because the more he imperils our trade horizons, the more he discourages future foreign or domestic investment in the manufacturing sector.
Even if Trump pulls back from his doomsday tariff scenarios, he still wins the day because future investors will be more inclined to locate their plants on the American side of the border to avoid future angst. Brace for the economy to get worse in 2025, but remember that all was not well in 2024.
What ever future tariffs Trump has in store for us, remember how we got to where we are today. For unemployment keeps rising inexorably, courtesy of Ontario's incumbent premier, not America's incoming president.
CAPTION: We can say with statistical certainty that Ontario is already facing serious economic bumps today, not from U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's tariff threats, but from Premier Doug Ford's fecklessness, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Presidents; Manufacturing; Unemployment; Economic conditions; Tariffs

Document 398 of 500

Bike lane bill faces Charter challenge: Cycling group says provincial bill 'puts lives at risk'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bike-lane-bill-faces-charter-challenge/docview/3143188364/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bike+lane+bill+faces+Charter+challenge%3A+Cycling+group+says+provincial+bill+%27puts+lives+at+risk%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A cycling advocacy group has launched a Charter challenge against the Ford government's recent legislation that takes aim at Toronto's bike lanes.
In a lawsuit filed in Superior Court, Cycle Toronto, along with bike lane users Eva Stanger-Ross and Narada Kiondo, argues that a section in the provincial government's Bill 212 "puts lives at risk."
The group's lawsuit seeks an immediate injunction to prevent the removal of bike lanes on Bloor Street, University Avenue and Yonge Street. It also asks the court to strike down the section of Bill 212 that allows for removal of the bike lanes - which the provincial government says is a step needed to improve traffic flow in the gridlocked city - if it finds it violates the Charter-protected rights to life and security of person.
Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto, was unavailable for an interview Wednesday because he is in hospital after "getting doored" in a painted bike lane on St. George Street earlier this week. Longfield sustained a serious break in his right leg that required immediate surgery, "an all too common reminder for the need for protected cycling infrastructure like on Bloor, Yonge and University," he said in a text.
In a press release, Longfield's group argued there is evidence separated bike lanes have little effect on congestion, but do prevent death and injury. "Without evidence, the Government of Ontario claims removal of the bike lanes addresses traffic concerns and that they could be replaced by bike lanes on secondary routes. Those routes do not exist," according to the release.
Bronwyn Roe, a lawyer at Ecojustice, who along with Paliare Roland LLP, is representing Longfield's group, said that Bill 212, the Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, is "an arbitrary and dangerous piece of legislation" and the province "cannot be allowed to put the lives of Toronto cyclists and Ontarians at risk."
Roe noted bike lanes are an "essential safety mechanism" in a city with people increasingly looking at alternative transportation methods. Removing them "locks in irresponsible car-dependence and is not in the best interest of communities," she added.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria's legislation was also amended recently to protect his government from lawsuits if cyclists are injured or killed on streets where the bike lanes are removed. Cycle Toronto called this amendment "a tacit acknowledgment of the safety risk the government is creating."
Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson for Sarkaria's office, did not comment on the lawsuit directly, instead pointing to a recent report about how gridlock costs Ontario's economy $56 billion and 88,000 jobs annually.
"Gridlock is at an all-time high and we need to deliver on our plan to keep people moving by bringing sanity back to bike lanes and building new roads, highways and transit," Brasier said.
Some legal experts say the Charter challenge may be the only viable legal strategy to reverse course before the bike lanes are removed in the coming weeks.
The Cycle Toronto lawsuit "is a very strong Charter challenge because the evidence shows bike lanes save lives and ... the (province) is proceeding with disregard for the available evidence," said Bruce Ryder, an associate professor at Osgoode Hall Law School who's not involved in the lawsuit.
Ryder added that the group only has to show "on the balance of probabilities" it's more likely than not that's the case.
Though a Charter challenge of Premier Doug Ford's decision to cut city council almost in half failed at the Supreme Court of Canada in 2021, according to Ryder that case was different because "there's no right to have a particular political structure."
David Schneiderman, professor of law and political science at the University of Toronto who's also not involved in the lawsuit, said Charter challenges are "iffy propositions at the best of times." But, he added, "I think there's a plausible argument to be made" if the strength of the evidence holds up in court.
"Also plausible is the argument that (this section in Bill 212) violates the principles of fundamental justice because it's considered to be arbitrary," said Schneiderman, but the legal team will have to show there's "no rational connection" between removing bike lanes and decreasing congestion.
CREDIT: Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Toronto%27s+traffic+is+a+nightmare.+Here%27s+what+some+experts+say+is+the+biggest+culprit+and+what+the+city+can+do+about+it%2C+Dec.+9+Donald+Trump+threatens+25%25+tariff+on+Canadian+products%3B+Doug+Ford%2C+premiers+call+for+urgent+action%2C+Nov.+26+Trump+calls+Trudeau+%27Governor%27+of+the+%27Great+State+of+Canada%2C%27+Dec.+10+Trump%27s+comment+about+Canada+becoming+51st+state+was+a+joke%2C+minister+who+attended+dinner+says%2C+Dec.+3+Pierre+Poilievre+says+it+shouldn%27t+take+Donald+Trump+to+make+the+Liberals+sort+out+a+fentanyl+crisis%2C+Dec.+10+As+environment+minister%2C+I+believed+the+oilsands+sector+would+help+us+save+the+planet.+I+was+wrong%2C+Dec.+8&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Construction crews don't need to block traffic
Toronto's traffic is a
nightmare. Here's what some
experts say is the biggest culprit and what the city can do about it, Dec. 9
I was so glad to see the Star finally draw attention to the way Toronto streets are constantly being reduced by at least one lane when construction begins on major buildings. But I was disappointed that the writers did not include the history of the construction of First Canadian Place, the Bank of Montreal building at the northwest corner of King and Bay streets in 1975.
To limit interference with traffic on the surrounding streets, builders Olympia and York constructed a large turntable in what would eventually be the basement of the building, so that multiple trucks could go off the street and onto the site to deliver their loads, instead of occupying a lane on the street while waiting to unload. I'm not aware of this method having been used anywhere else.
This example shows that it might not be unreasonable for other companies starting construction of major buildings in Toronto to be told they cannot close off any street lanes for deliveries, and that all deliveries would have to be made on-site. I appreciate that the need for installation of additional water and power delivery to new buildings would probably require some lane closures, but surely the current level of closures has reached unacceptable heights, and new delivery arrangements for construction materials could become mandatory.
Philip Wedge, Toronto
Tax snowbirds
in response to Trump's threats
Donald Trump threatens
25% tariff on Canadian
products; Doug Ford, premiers call for urgent action, Nov. 26
There is a better way to respond to president-elect Donald Trump's tariff threat that will be much more painful to the U.S. economy: tax snowbirds.
Every year, more than a million Canadians head south for up to six months. They are supporting the U.S. economy at the expense of the Canadian economy. They support the U.S. housing market, entertainment industry (restaurants, golf and tennis clubs, etc.) and spend on daily living expenses. If they get sick, they race home for free health care.
Taxing senior citizens is not popular, but neither is seeing the cost of imported stuff go up by 25 per cent. Taxing Canadians who are away from the country for more than 30 days and want to keep their health care seems fair. Supporting Trump should come with consequences. Drastic times require drastic measures.
Michael Yaffe, Toronto
Trump should wine and dine our true ruler
Trump calls Trudeau
'Governor' of the 'Great State of Canada,' Dec. 10
Donald Trump is wrong. Justin Trudeau is only the Prime Minister of this state. The true ruler of Canada, who is the representative of King Charles III, is Gov.-Gen. Mary Simon. She is commander-in-chief of the Canadian Armed Forces and exercises virtually all the powers of the Crown. She is the one Trump should have dinner with next time.
Sylvio Le Blanc, Montreal
Trump and Putin think alike
Trump's comment about
Canada becoming 51st state was a joke, minister who
attended dinner says, Dec. 3
President-elect Donald Trump wants to absorb Canada and make it the 51st state. This is something that most Canadians would object to, but unfortunately our military would likely not be able to prevent it. This sounds like Russian President Vladimir Putin's attitude toward Ukraine. No wonder he and Trump are friends. They clearly think alike.
Edward A. Collis, Burlington
We need quality post-secondary education
Where are the leaders of today? A cry commonly heard these days as people struggle, but the real cry should be: Where are the leaders of tomorrow?
The lack of funding for colleges and universities is resulting in program cuts, denying educational opportunities. Freezing tuition costs, as Premier Doug Ford has done, simply reduces the number of places available. No one wants to raise tuition, but if this was done, it would at least provide spaces for those who can afford it; spaces currently being lost to funding cuts.
In a country where we manufacture very little, our growth is relegated to financial services, innovation, technology and the service sector. Without high-quality and broadly available post-secondary education, that growth will be concentrated in the service sector alone, and we will all suffer for it in the future.
To put it in terms the premier will understand, fewer people will be able to afford the new spa at Ontario Place.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Do we need Canada Post?
It is time to rethink Canada Post. Most of us do our transactions/business online. Why not reduce deliveries to twice per week and add more corner boxes. Reduce staff drastically.
As for packages, set up Purolator to compete with the big boys.
We cannot continue with this inefficiency anymore.
TomSteele, Peterborough
Not a good look for a wannabe prime minister
Pierre Poilievre says it shouldn't take Donald Trump to make the Liberals sort out a fentanyl crisis, Dec. 10
At a time when our federal government is dealing with Donald Trump and conflicts over trade and the border, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre does nothing but snipe and take cheap shots at Trudeau.
His actions weaken and undermine our Prime Minister at a time when we need to show a strong united front.
This is not a good look for someone who wants desperately to be the next prime minister.
Achim Krull, Pickering
Let's get on with decarbonizing our economy
As environment minister, I believed the oilsands sector would help us save the planet.
I was wrong, Dec. 8
The oil and gas industry betrayed Catherine McKenna and they are misleading all of us.
Zap the crap, and get on with decarbonizing our economy. Otherwise as McKenna concludes we will "leave our children a deadly and unsustainable future."
Doug Pritchard, Toronto

Document 400 of 500

'There's a wave coming:' Why Toronto's mayor still believes the housing crisis can be solved

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-wave-coming-why-torontos-mayor-still/docview/3143188178/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27There%27s+a+wave+coming%3A%27+Why+Toronto%27s+mayor+still+believes+the+housing+crisis+can+be+solved&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To read up on the recent history of housing in Toronto is to feel an overwhelming sense of déjà vu. Consider a few headlines from this newspaper: "It's Time for Meaningful Action on Housing Crisis," "City's Housing Crisis Has Roots Decades Deep," and "Housing Crisis Damages Children." All of these articles appeared between 2000 and 2002, when Mel Lastman was mayor. But if the problem was bad in Lastman's day, it's exponentially worse today. When, in July 2023, Olivia Chow took office as the amalgamated city's fifth mayor, she promised to tackle housing and traffic - and to succeed where her predecessors had failed. In an interview, edited for clarity and length, she reflected on what success will look like, and what it will take to get there.
There are problems to be fixed and problems to be managed. Which one is housing?
We can end the housing crisis. I remain optimistic about that. For three decades, governments didn't build housing. Now, we're fixing the old ideology that says the government has no role to play in housing.
Since becoming mayor, you've done a lot on the housing file, from streamlining the permitting and approvals process to beefing up tenant protection programs. How do you reconcile the fact that, despite everything you've done, we're still in this crisis?
International financial markets have high interest rates, so builders are saying, "Financially, we can't move forward." That's why the City of Toronto had said, "There will be no development charges for purpose-built rental housing and a discount on property taxes if 20 per cent of your development comprises affordable housing." Yes, there is a crisis. But each building we open, each tenant that moves in, provides a source of hope.
The Wilson Heights project, a 1,5000-unit development on a parking lot by Wilson Subway Station, had been stuck for five years. But we're starting to build.
In two years, our interest rates will likely be a lot lower. You will have lots of pent up demand. The City of Toronto has said, "Here are parking lots. Here is land you can build on." There's a wave coming.
When you talk about building on parking lots you're surely referring to Housing Now, a program introduced in 2019. Under Housing Now, City properties would be made available to developers, who, in return, would ensure that a third of their units would be priced affordably. Less than two months before you took office as mayor, CBC reported that Housing Now had created zero new affordable units. What went wrong?
No one was really in charge of approving things.
Is someone in charge now?
There's one person now, at the Development Review Division. It used to be that you had to get separate approvals for planning, legal, building, engineers, real estate, etc. Now you can do everything in one stop.
Was bureaucratic red tape the fatal flaw in Housing Now?
The other piece was money. Without federal and provincial money, there aren't enough financial incentives for developers to build.
You recently requested, along with other GTA mayors, that all promised grants from the federal Housing Accelerator Fund be paid out right away. There may soon be a change of power in Ottawa. Is that your reason for requesting the funding now?
We're doing the building. We're providing incentives for builders. We've got land. We've got developers. Why wait?
So this isn't about insulating the city against a potential change in government? What happens if there's a change in government and the commitments get changed?
I won't speculate. We have a conservative provincial government. They're partnering with us on any number of projects, like Na-Me-Res, which offers supportive housing for Indigenous men.
But do you expect that a Poilievre government will be as amenable to cooperation as Doug Ford has been?
It doesn't matter what party you're in or whether you are Prime Minister Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre. Everyone's now saying that the government has a role to play in housing.
So, when Poilievre talks about axing the Liberals' housing grants is that just idle rhetoric?
They might not keep the Liberals' exact program. They might have another program.
You're saying that, given that housing is a major issue, Poilievre is going to have to do something big?
Absolutely. Pierre Poilievre understands the need for housing.
In the recent past, when governments got involved in housing, it was at the policy level. They might change zoning or permitting rules. But you want governments to step up and actually build homes. Is this happening?
We have five projects that we're building. Historically, in Toronto, the city government had always built homes, until the federal and provincial governments canceled these programs. We built over 30,000 units of housing between the late '70s and the '90s.
You've seen this phenomenon in the U.S. where, as soon as a city decides to start building, there are a bunch of regulations that kick in. Councillors say things like, "All the work needs to be done by unionized builders," or "We're going to hold this building to high environmental standards." Predictably, the price of city-built units goes up and the number of units that gets built goes down. Is this something you worry about?
No, because we've had a good track record of building in the '80s and '90s. If anyone is saying the City doesn't know how to build, they should go to the Regent Park building at 175 Oak Street. We just opened it. It's beautiful. And it didn't blow up anybody's piggy bank.
Let's talk about traffic. I have a similar question to the one I asked about housing. Are we looking to fix the problem or manage it?
We're looking to fix it. We just got the funds to order 55 new subway cars for the Bloor-Danforth Line. With new cars, we will put in advanced control, using GPS so that trains can come more frequently. What does this have to do with traffic? A lot. The Bloor-Danforth Line can carry three times the number of people as the Gardiner Expressway.
Some people argue that transit doesn't help with congestion as much as it should because of the problem of "induced demand." When subway services improve, some commuters will switch from driving to taking transit, thereby freeing up road space. But then, other people, seeing that the roads are now freer, will get in their car and drive. Will better transit just encourage other people to drive?
Not if it's faster for them to take public transit.
So is the phenomenon of induced demand not a real thing?
Look at Hong Kong. It's a dense place. People say, "If I can save 20 minutes by taking public transit, I'll do that."
London, England has successfully tackled congestion. But London has congestion pricing to disincentivize road use. Surely, part of London's success is due not only to quality transit but also to the fact that there's a financial cost to driving in the city. Do you see a possibility of having congestion pricing in the future?
You'd have to talk to Premier Ford.
Could I get your opinion on congestion pricing, though?
As mayor, I can help fund more frequent bus services. I have control over that. But I have no control whatsoever on road pricing. If you look at our congestion management plan, we're hiring a lot more of those wonderful traffic agents. There are traffic wardens standing in the road, moving traffic along. We upped the fine for obstructing the flow of traffic to $450 and upped enforcement too. We are requesting to use more video cameras to charge people who are breaking the law. We are coordinating on road construction so you don't see people coming in and digging up utilities and then digging them up all over again when there's more work to be done.
Do you think these solutions can, collectively, fix the traffic problem in the absence of congestion pricing?
It's hard to say. But we can only do what we have control over.
Let's say, a decade from now, you're in your 12th year as mayor. In this hypothetical future, are the housing and traffic crises just distant unhappy memories?
In 2034, people will find housing more affordable. The neighborhoods will be more complete with community centres, greenspaces, and libraries. There'll be more alternatives to driving. Energy bills won't be as high because we're more resilient in terms of climate. Fewer people will use the food bank. And kids will have a really good lunch program in school.
I find this future hard to envision because we've been dealing with these problems for so, so long.
But I've only been mayor for a year and a half.
CAPTION: Should Toronto start charging for access to downtown roads? Ask Doug Ford, says Olivia Chow.OLIVIA CHOW FOR OPINION WEEKLY Q&A TORONTO, ON - December 2 - Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow Q&A for Opinion Weekly with freelancer Simon Lewsen at Toronto City Hall, December 2, 2024. Andrew Francis Wallace/Toronto StarOLIVIA CHOW FOR OPINION WEEKLY Q&A TORONTO, ON - December 2 - Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow Q&A for Opinion Weekly with freelancer Simon Lewsen at Toronto City Hall, December 2, 2024. Andrew Francis Wallace/Toronto StarOLIVIA CHOW FOR OPINION WEEKLY Q&A TORONTO, ON - December 2 - Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow Q&A for Opinion Weekly with freelancer Simon Lewsen at Toronto City Hall, December 2, 2024. Title: 'There's a wave coming:' Why Toronto's mayor still believes the housing crisis can be solved

Document 401 of 500

Tories unveil anti-encampment law: Bill gives cities, police 'enhanced legislative powers' to clear tents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-unveil-anti-encampment-law/docview/3143188124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+unveil+anti-encampment+law%3A+Bill+gives+cities%2C+police+%27enhanced+legislative+powers%27+to+clear+tents&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives are clamping down on homeless encampments that have sprung up in parks and vacant lots across Ontario.
In a bill being introduced Thursday, the final day of sitting at Queen's Park before MPPs' holiday break, the Tories will give municipalities and police increased funding and "enhanced legislative powers" to deal with the crisis.
Mindful of judicial rulings in Waterloo and Kingston, Ford has threatened to use the Constitution's notwithstanding clause to override any court decisions that would thwart the removal of tents from parks.
"We are acting to put an end to the public disorder, drug use and trafficking, and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth in encampments," the premier said last week.
Ford assured municipal leaders that his "new legislation and supporting initiatives will achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra emphasized Wednesday that cities would be expected to use the new "tools on the table" to enforce bylaws against encampments on public spaces.
"Make no mistake about it, we're taking back our parks, we're taking back our streets," said Calandra.
"We're going to hold them (municipalities) accountable for clearing out those encampments, but at the same time understanding that we need resources and we need places for people to go," he said.
"If those investments don't give us the results that we are looking for ... the legislation that we are tabling (Thursday) ... will allow us to take even more aggressive steps."
But Ford's political opponents say a "heavy-handed" approach is wrong-headed for a complicated issue like homelessness, which encompasses mental health, addiction and housing affordability.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, whose Davenport riding includes Toronto's largest encampment in Dufferin Grove Park, said the premier's "legacy is homelessness and encampments in every community" since he was elected in 2018.
"They want to come forward with some bill that they're never even going to pass and some heavy-handed solutions that are actually not going to result in people (getting) in shelters or moving out of encampments," said Stiles.
But because the bill is being tabled on the last day of the legislative session, she said the measures might not even be enacted before an early election Ford has been considering calling this spring, one year before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"We're seeing a kind of an 11th-hour attempt to come forward with something that they have actually no intention of passing," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the increase of encampments across Ontario - especially in Toronto, where an estimated 1,000 people live in parks - happened on Ford's watch.
"The issue has exploded under the Conservatives over the past six years - it's much worse than we ever saw before," said Crombie, recounting her own personal experience.
"My biological dad was a homeless person who had major addiction issues and was in and out of shelters and lived his final years in the Seaton House and I really thank (them for) ... the counselling that he got, because he got some wraparound care, he got some resources that he needed for treatment," she said.
"We need a program that will give people shelter, but also the wraparound services that they need."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "the situation has reached a crisis point" because the Tories have neglected to build enough "deeply affordable housing" over the past six years.
"The solution to homelessness is housing," said Schreiner, predicting the legislation would be ineffective as long as there aren't enough shelter beds.
"So if the premier is going to take people's constitutional rights away from them, where are they going to go?" he said.
A recent Abacus Data poll paid for by the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness, a national umbrella group that works with charities, found 65 per cent were "concerned about the impact of encampments in their community" with 13 per cent unconcerned while 22 per cent were neutral or had no opinion.
However, Ontarians expressed concern with Ford's strategy for tackling homeless encampments, including his willingness to invoke the notwithstanding clause to override court decisions that thwart the removal of tents from parks.
Just 12 per cent of respondents favoured "stronger law enforcement measures to clear encampments" while 34 per cent backed "providing more affordable housing" and 16 per cent supported "creating safe spaces and shelters."
Another 16 per cent said they would prefer "increasing community-based support and outreach programs," and 14 per cent recommended expanding "access to mental health and addiction services." Seven per cent had no opinion.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from Nov. 29 to Dec. 4 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Ford is acting after a dozen mayors appealed to him to enact tougher laws to help them cope with encampments and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
The mayors of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor urged him in writing to intervene last month.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said municipal leaders are "very pleased with the response from the premier."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who did not sign the letter, has repeatedly stressed solutions are not easy.
"It takes a while to build extra shelters - we are building five; it takes a while to build supportive housing - we're building as many as we can afford to," Chow said last week.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Rob Ferguson

Subject: Homeless encampments; Legislation; Mayors; Affordable housing; Homeless people; Public safety; Housing policy

Title: Tories unveil anti-encampment law:   Bill gives cities, police 'enhanced legislative powers' to clear tents

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 12, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Se rvices, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: News
