Full text: Costly election would simply feed Ford's ego
Premier Doug Ford, who already has a majority in the Ontario legislature, is apparently planning an early election sometime in 2025.
If the Conservatives are supposed to be the stewards of our finances, why is Ford planning on spending close taxpayer dollars on an election we don't need? What possible use could there be, except to feed Ford's need for power?
Stephen Bloom, Toronto
Premier needs to address issues plaguing TDSB
The scathing auditor's report raises a lot of questions about Doug Ford's government - but here's the hardest one to answer, Dec. 7
Contributing columnist Shawn Micallef has accurately pointed out some important issues facing Ontario under Premier Doug Ford.
One issue is that the Toronto District School Board's infrastructure is in disrepair. Also, teacher availability and competency must be addressed; my granddaughter has been without a French teacher for more than three months.
Perhaps one of the reasons Ford is considering an early election is he hopes to be re-elected before the RCMP finalizes its damning report on the Greenbelt fiasco.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
Pragmatic
plan could hold Ford in check
Doug Ford's Tories leading as potential election looms, survey shows, Dec. 8
Ontario's opposition parties have every reason to consider a pragmatic new game plan when Doug Ford's headstrong government holds a commanding lead (according to the latest Abacus Data poll).
The opposition faces a daunting task despite the government's staggering mismanagement of a host of issues outlined by the auditor general. This includes a record amount of spending on advertising, much of it essentially partisan, to bolster the Progressive Conservative's re-election prospects.
There's a real danger the three opposition parties may well ensure another Tory majority if they split the vote. The 2022 election, when the Progressive Conservatives won 40.8 per cent of the popular vote while the NDP and Liberals had 23.7 and 23.8 per cent respectively, speaks volumes.
It's time the opposition leaders consider strategic co-operation, as envisioned by Steve Paikin, host of TVO's "The Agenda." Paikin's "non-aggression pact" as he calls it would see the parties divide ridings. They would choose candidates according to which person, party label aside, has the best chance of defeating a PC candidate in each constituency. While opposition co-ordination wouldn't necessarily end Tory rule, it would offer hope of holding the Ford government to a more accountable minority at least.
Gord McNulty, Hamilton
FIFA money better spent on helping homeless
Toronto can end tents in parks: Here's a city that solved street homelessness - and how we can do it too, Dec. 8
In one story, this article provides all the information we need to do away with homelessness in our city.
Instead of spending $380 million (according to a city of Toronto report released in February) to host World Cup games in 2026 that benefits only FIFA, imagine if we spent that $380 million to help end homelessness in our city.
There are solutions. Launch a non-profit organization to manage the housing of the currently homeless. That way, people can donate to the charity and get a tax receipt.
Tell FIFA that unless we raise $380 million through the charity by the end of 2025, they will get no financial aid from Toronto. Regardless of how much money we raise through the charity, start the new initiative in January 2026. Who knows? FIFA might even donate a big chunk of money just so their soccer tournament can go ahead.
William Robertson, Toronto
City's strategy
for High Park
is working
Recently you published a letter suggesting High Park is dead and deserted because of the limitations on access for cars, and suggesting this was the reason why the city has not continued to operate a Christmas event in the park.
Perhaps there are other reasons why this event was cancelled. There are seasonal events planned at Colborne Lodge, and I'm sure that if needed, the city could allow any special furnishings to be brought in on the five days per week, during which there are no limits on vehicular access, or even on weekends.
As for the park being "dead and deserted" I visit the park two or three times per week and I can assure you it is alive with people, animals and birds enjoying the tranquility of nature every day of the week. The city's mobility strategy is working well in terms of providing safe access for walking, cycling, and yes, even driving. Toronto is overrun with cars, and having a place free of drivers' aggressive behaviour is important now, and it will become even more essential as the city's density continues to increase.
It's time to put this issue to bed.
Jim Gough, Toronto
Trudeau knows exactly who Trump is
Bending the knee before a bully never works, Dec. 7 Letter to the Editor
It was disheartening to read the letter writer criticizing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's reaction to Trump's threats of 25 per cent tariffs.
What would the writer recommend instead? Ignore this existential threat? Cower in terror?
Trudeau recognizes what has been described as Trump's malignant narcissism.
Based on his previous experience, Trudeau recognized Trump's nature and did not bend the knee but made a clear-headed, realistic response to the dangers inherent in Trump's flexing his presidential muscles. Trudeau knows to take Trump seriously, to use whatever ploys are necessary to play to Trump's vanity for the sake of Canada's sovereignty. Trudeau's maturity in showing a quick and steady approach is heartening.
May Trudeau's calm but studied reaction attract voters to him and away from the perpetually whining Pierre Poilievre. The Conservative party leader's modus operandi is to constantly pander to citizens' most base anger.
Carolyn Brown, Toronto

Subject: Opposition parties; Charities; Soccer; Homeless people; Political advertising; Elections

Document 418 of 500

Voters divided over Ford's strategy: Premier's plan to clear encampments has just 12% support, poll finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-divided-over-fords-strategy/docview/3142493285/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+divided+over+Ford%27s+strategy%3A+Premier%27s+plan+to+clear+encampments+has+just+12%25+support%2C+poll+finds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians are deeply divided over Premier Doug Ford's strategy for tackling homeless encampments, including his threatened use of the "notwithstanding clause," a new poll suggests.
With Ford bringing forward measures Thursday to give police and municipalities "enhanced legislative powers" to remove the tent encampments from parks, the Abacus Data survey found little backing for a heavy-handed approach.
Only 12 per cent favoured "stronger law enforcement measures to clear encampments."
About one-third - 34 per cent - backed "providing more affordable housing" with 16 per cent supporting "creating safe spaces and shelters," another 16 per cent said "increasing community-based support and outreach programs," and 14 per cent recommending expanded "access to mental health and addiction services." Seven per cent had no opinion.
Still, 65 per cent said they were "concerned about the impact of encampments in their community" with 13 per cent unconcerned while 22 were neutral or had no opinion.
Abacus regularly polls for the Star, but this particular survey was paid for by the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness, an umbrella group that works with charities across the country.
"Politically, whether you're a mayor or the premier or an MPP, homelessness is now a visual representation of a crisis that ... people don't feel anybody's had a handle on," Abacus president David Coletto said Monday.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said there would be "additional resources and funding" for cities to find shelter for homeless people currently living outside.
"I just simply do not agree with those who believe that a park is a place where people with mental-health and addictions are best served. A park is for kids, a park is for communities to get together," said Calandra, warning municipalities would be expected to use the new powers and money.
"Look, the City of Toronto was able to clear encampments when Taylor Swift came. They have the ability to do so," he said, adding his legislation is in conjunction with Attorney General Doug Downey and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner.
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles said the Progressive Conservatives "have actually no intention of passing" the law before an early election Ford might call this spring, a year before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"They want to come forward with some bill that they're never even going to pass and some heavy-handed solutions that are actually not going to result in people (getting) in shelters or moving out of encampments," said Stiles.
"Six years in, (Ford's) legacy is homelessness and encampments in every community," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, recalling her own familial experience, said the moves are "too little too late" to help people living in parks and on the streets.
"My biological dad was a homeless person who had major addiction issues and was in and out of shelters and lived his final years in the Seaton House and I really thank (them for) ... the counselling that he got," said Crombie.
"But this issue has exploded under the Conservatives over the past six years ... We need a program that will give people shelter, but also the wraparound services that they need," she said.
While Ford said he is "fully prepared" to override the courts by using the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" to secure his legislation and "dismantle" encampments, 40 per cent of poll respondents said they would oppose that.
Of the 40 per cent that disagreed with using the "notwithstanding clause," about one-third - 32 per cent - said "police clearing encampments is not an effective solution to homelessness."
Another 26 per cent said "there are better, more compassionate solutions to the issue."
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians Nov. 29-Dec. 4 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Parks & recreation areas; Homeless people

Document 419 of 500

Supervised injection site faces layoffs: Union says 33 positions to be cut after controversial funding cut

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/supervised-injection-site-faces-layoffs/docview/3142492767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supervised+injection+site+faces+layoffs%3A+Union+says+33+positions+to+be+cut+after+controversial+funding+cut&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Employees at a prominent downtown harm reduction facility are facing layoffs in the wake of Premier Doug Ford's decision to shutter supervised drug consumption sites, according to their union.
CUPE Local 79 said it recently received layoff notices for its members employed at The Works, despite the board of health voting in September not to eliminate the positionsset to lose provincial funding next year under the Ontario PCs' controversial drug policy.
The union says the jobs of four community health officers and 29 temporary harm reduction councillors are on the line. The positions would be eliminated as of March 31, the date by which the Ontario government has ordered 10 of 23 sites across the province, including The Works, shut down.
At a news conference Monday, Local 79 president Nas Yadollahi described the layoff notices as "deeply troubling." Although she said the city has told the union final decisions on staffing levels won't be made until the 2025 budget is set early next year, she called on the municipality to rescind the notices and commit to redeploying affected employees to other jobs in the municipal workforce.
"These are the very people who have been providing critical life-saving harm reduction services for some of Toronto's most vulnerable residents," Yadollahi said.
"To the city we say: show the leadership that Toronto residents expect and deserve. Advocate for harm reduction services. Advocate for the workers who make these services possible. Recognize the service these workers have performed, and let them keep their jobs." In a statement, the city said that while it had "engaged" Local 79 about the staffing impact of the provincial policy changes, it "has not laid off any staff within 'The Works.' "
"Local 79 was notified earlier this month that any potential layoffs were paused pending further review," it said.
The Works has been providing harm reduction services since 1989. The facility at Yonge and Dundas streets is operated by Toronto Public Health with provincial funding, and on the list of five sites in the city that Ford's government announced in August would be closed.
The province said supervised consumption sites posed a danger to surrounding communities and they should be forced at least 200 metres from schools or daycares. The announcement, which followed the 2023 fatal shooting of Karolina Huebner-Makurat outside the site at the South Riverdale Community Health Centre, came with increased funding for addiction treatment.
But the closures have been heavily criticized by addiction experts, who say statistics show supervised consumption facilities reverse hundreds of overdoses a year and people will die if they're shut down.
The union says the city issued layoff notices despite The Works being "in the planning stages" of relocating to a site "that would still be able to operate under the legislation."
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Subject: Workforce planning; Funding; Layoffs; Harm reduction; Facilities; Shutdowns; Pharmacy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bank-canada-expected-cut-interest-rates-50-basis/docview/3142492705/se-2?accountid=15090

"The sharp increase in the unemployment rate is likely enough to drive a second consecutive 50 basis point cut from the Bank of Canada," said BMO's Reitzes.
"Despite that expectation, the risks to the Canadian dollar and housing from such a move should be considered and point to a more cautious path in 2025."
CAPTION: Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem is expected by most economists to announce a 50 basis points cut to the bank's key interest rate on Wednesday.

Document 421 of 500

Ontarians divided over Doug Ford's get-tough approach on homeless encampments, survey suggests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-divided-over-doug-fords-get-tough/docview/3142290617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+divided+over+Doug+Ford%27s+get-tough+approach+on+homeless+encampments%2C+survey+suggests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians are deeply divided over Premier Doug Ford's strategy for tackling homeless encampments, including his threatened use of the "notwithstanding clause," a new poll suggests.
With Ford bringing forward measures this week to give police and municipalities "enhanced legislative powers" to remove the tent encampments from parks, the Abacus Data survey found little backing for a heavy-handed approach.
Only 12 per cent favoured "stronger law enforcement measures to clear encampments."
About one-third - 34 per cent - backed "providing more affordable housing" with 16 per cent supporting "creating safe spaces and shelters," another 16 per cent said "increasing community-based support and outreach programs," and 14 per cent recommending expanded "access to mental health and addiction services." Seven per cent had no opinion.
Still, 65 per cent said they were "concerned about the impact of encampments in their community" with 13 per cent unconcerned while 22 were neutral or had no opinion.
Abacus regularly polls for the Star, but this particular survey was paid for by the Canadian Alliance to End Homelessness, an umbrella group that works with charities across the country.
"Politically, whether you're a mayor or the premier or an MPP, homelessness is now a visual representation of a crisis that ... people don't feel anybody's had a handle on," Abacus president David Coletto said Monday.
"It's a reflection of how the system isn't working," said Coletto.
While Ford said he is "fully prepared" to override the courts by using the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" to secure his legislation and "dismantle" encampments, 40 per cent of respondents said they would oppose that.
Just 30 per cent support the government using the Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to ensure its efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
Another 29 per cent would neither support nor oppose that or had no opinion.
Of the 40 per cent that disagreed with using the "notwithstanding clause," about one-third - 32 per cent - said "police clearing encampments is not an effective solution to homelessness."
Another 26 per cent said "there are better, more compassionate solutions to the issue" with 14 per cent expressing concern "it could set a dangerous precedent for overriding the human rights of other groups" and 11 per cent fearing "it undermines individual rights and protections."
Six per cent were "worried it will hurt people" and five per cent said Ford's government "has been too quick to invoke the 'notwithstanding clause' in recent years" and two per cent didn't like that it would override the courts.
"This is an issue that creates some vulnerability for Ford in that it reminds them of the version of him that they didn't like, which was the pre-pandemic version," said Coletto.
"The pressure he's feeling is real. People right across the province ... believe something has to happen, but it's not a slam dunk that they are going to embrace using the notwithstanding clause on something like this," he added.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians Nov. 29-Dec. 4 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Asked what they felt was the primary cause of encampments, which were far less prevalent before the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, 55 per cent of respondents blamed "the housing affordability crisis" with 45 per cent citing "addiction, mental health issues or personal choices."
But the issue isn't hurting Ford yet - Abacus found his Progressive Conservatives 43 per cent, ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 25 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
The premier is considering a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: A homeless tent encampment is seen in Dufferin Grove Park on Dec. 7, 2024.

Subject: Homeless encampments; Mental health; Affordable housing; Homeless people; Pandemics

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Title: Ontarians divided over Doug Ford's get-tough approach on homeless encampments, survey suggests

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication date: Dec 9, 2024

Premier Doug Ford's anti-encampment laws will be a death sentence for Canada's most vulnerable people

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premier-doug-fords-anti-encampment-laws-will-be/docview/3142290615/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Doug+Ford%27s+anti-encampment+laws+will+be+a+death+sentence+for+Canada%27s+most+vulnerable+people&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford preparing to override courts, give police and municipalities enhanced legislative powers to 'dismantle' homeless encampments, Dec. 5
Canada has not had a death penalty for almost 50 years, but Premier Doug Ford's proposed laws to get rid of encampments - in other words vulnerable people's homes - will be a death penalty. As someone who lives in Edmonton, a city with aggressive anti-homeless strategies, I can tell you these rules and practices don't eliminate homelessness. They increase deaths, upheaval, multi-generational trauma and substance abuse. Last year, when Edmonton's police adopted a more aggressive approach to homeless people, one in 10 of the city's homeless people died and drug overdoses reached record highs. The lack of long-term encampments means social services have no way of knowing exactly how many homeless people live in Edmonton (the latest estimate is one in about every 200 Edmonton residents is homeless) and are depending on amputations due to frostbite for estimates of homeless counts. At the same time, the Alberta government builds such poor quality shelter housing it makes refugee camps look comfortable. We need to end this over-policing of homeless people and provide a stable supply of quality housing outside our country's financialized housing market, or we risk destabilizing our whole society due to inequality and housing insecurity.
Adam Bentley, Edmonton
Mississauga has the fastest-growing rate of food bank users in Ontario
Big jump in number of Toronto-area residents who say they can't make ends meet, report says, Dec. 5
This phenomenon of food insecurity is Canada-wide. Reports from all areas of the country are showing a steep climb in use of food banks. Canada was instrumental in drafting the Universal Declaration of Human Rights 76 years ago, yet we have failed to uphold one of its fundamental rights: the right to food. On Nov. 13, Mississauga City Council declared food insecurity an emergency, underscoring a stark reality: Mississauga has the fastest-growing rate of food bank users in Ontario, surpassing the provincial average. Food insecurity stems from multiple factors, including high rent, inadequate government assistance, rising food and gas prices, and the prevalence of low-paying or part-time jobs. Food Banks Mississauga calls on all levels of government to address this crisis by:
 Improving Ontario's social assistance programs to reflect economic realities.
 Investing in affordable, supportive, and public housing.
 Strengthening labour laws to support workers and enhance worker assistance programs.
 Backing the Groceries and Essentials Benefit proposed by Food Banks Canada to help households afford basic necessities like food and shelter. To learn more about the Groceries and Essentials Benefit, visit www.foodbanksmississauga.ca/groceries-benefit.
Margaret Ramsay, The Compass Food Bank
Political decisions are failing society
Banks are making billions in profit every quarter, groceries are expected to cost $800 more for a family of four next year, record numbers of people are going to food banks. Something is very wrong!
John Morton, Toronto
Subway projects start-up process was ineptPoor budgeting and too many consultants behind soaring cost of Toronto subway projects, study reveals, Dec. 5
As a retired professional project management scheduling co-ordinator I am not surprised by the fiasco created because of the inept project start-up process that transpired early on. Projects of this magnitude cannot possibly be successful without the initial organizational requirements being performed in accordance with what I believe to be the only route to success: Every member of the team must contribute to the development of the schedule and be fully committed to the requirements to implement their tasks in a timely fashion. Designs cannot be tossed and redone every time a consulting engineer moves on to another commitment. Get a handle on it, Metrolinx: set objectives, set strategies based on those objectives and take the time to sit down as a team and GET IT DONE!
Gary Brewer, Kingston
Masters at the bait and switch
When it comes to taxpayer-funded projects these infrastructure companies are masters at the bait and switch. Low ball everything to get the job and once in the door, gradually create a situation where their overseers are in too deep to back out.
Richard Kadziewicz, Scarborough
Police budget ask won't even cover the overtime so how can it improve service?
Four hours of OT for a five-minute call: Crown attorneys told to stop calling Toronto police officers to help curb overtime 'insanity,' Dec. 4
The Crown office is being asked to help control rising police overtime due to the requirement to provide disclosure for trial. Disclosure is a basic and regular duty that police are required to perform. This overtime, called premium pay, is budgeted at $59 million this year, and was exceeded by $30 million in 2022. For 2025 budget, the police force is asking for an increase of $46 million. They claim the increase will improve service and 911 response times. In reality, this won't even cover the excessive overtime for what should be their regular job of providing disclosure.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Nurse practioners are key to solving our family doctor shortage
'Wait times have exploded': Bonnie Crombie takes aim at Ontario doctor shortage in campaign promise, Dec. 2
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's "guarantee" of family doctors won't solve Ontario's primary care crisis. Instead, Crombie should look at proven and tested solutions used across the world: nurse practitioners. While physicians are essential, they cannot address this crisis alone. Nurse practitioners with advanced clinical education and legal authority to diagnose, treat and prescribe provide high-quality, cost-effective care across all health settings. Dr. Jane Philpott, lead of Ontario's primary care action team, shared on X how utilizing nurse practitioners ensures all Ontarians' ongoing access to primary care. Nurses urge Crombie to revise her pledge and promise access to either a family doctor or nurse practitioners supported by interprofessional teams of nurses, allied health professionals and social workers to the 2.5 million Ontarians who currently lack a primary care provider.
Dr. Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario, Toronto

Share your favourite holiday memories
The Star invites readers to tell us about their favourite holiday memories, traditions, adventures or misadventures. Do you have a special family tradition passed down for generations, a funny childhood experience you'd like to share or certain event that has become family lore? We'd love to hear your favourite story. Please send your submissions - try to keep them to less than 350 words - to lettertoed@thestar.ca. If you have a photograph to accompany it, please send it as a jpeg file, no smaller than one mb. We'll run a selection of these letters later in December.
CAPTION: A homeless encampment is seen in Alexandra Park in the area of Bathurst and Dundas streets on Nov. 23.

Document 423 of 500

Compassion takes a back seat in Ford's clear out the encampments strategy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/compassion-takes-back-seat-fords-clear-out/docview/3142290591/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Compassion+takes+a+back+seat+in+Ford%27s+clear+out+the+encampments+strategy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How grimly fitting that Premier Doug Ford has chosen the season of want to threaten the use of the notwithstanding clause in the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms to clear the city's homeless encampments.
A Hollywood movie might track the evening snow settling on tents and over tarps as the provincial government moves to introduce clampdown legislation on the eve of the legislature's jollily generous holiday break. Yes, it sounds borderline Dickensian.
To parse the facts more carefully, the premier penned a letter, not to Santa Claus, but to 12 mayors (not including Olivia Chow), scantily outlining legislation to be introduced next week that will "achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Ford's main focus: "to put an end to public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth of the encampments."
A more thoughtful missive would have begun thusly:
We have failed.
We have collectively - municipalities and successive provincial governments alike - failed to provide long-promised solutions to chronic homelessness.
We have exacerbated the tragedy of want. We have inflamed the tragedy of ignorance.
Let's be clear. These encampments are occupying too much of the already limited green space in this city, spaces meant for the leisure of all residents, not to provide inadequate shelter for the most vulnerable. And public disorder and threats to safety can, and have, emerged from these tent cities.
They must be dealt with. But they cannot be meaningfully addressed until there is somewhere for those being evicted to go.
The premier's precis of the pending legislation does include additional funding to create more capacity in the shelter system, and "new tools and authorities" to assist police enforcing the prohibition of illicit drugs in public.
There's additionally the promise of new, unspecified approaches to drug treatment and rehabilitation that prioritize recovery over incarceration.
These promises are directionally right, though absent specific details, we'll wait to pass judgment.
But Ford did not put down his pen.
Instead, he prophesied.
Should the courts "interfere," as they have in the past, by upholding the Charter rights of individuals deprived of housing, his government is, he wrote, "fully prepared" to use the notwithstanding clause, Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, to override the court's decision. That includes being an intervener "in any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments."
The courts have done precisely that in the past. An Ontario Supreme Court ruling last year rejected an application from the Regional Municipality of Waterloo to be granted an injunction, under a local bylaw, to evict homeless people - approximately 70 shelters with an estimated 50 residents - from property owned by the municipality. The bylaw infringed Section 7 of the Charter, the court found. That is, that everyone has the right to "life, liberty and security of the person."
Keep in mind that Ford's notwithstanding threat, for that's what it is, comes on the heels of a letter addressed to the premier and signed by 450 members of Ontario's legal community urging him to stand down. "The notwithstanding clause was never intended to be used to deprive vulnerable groups of constitutional protection," the group wrote. Government policy should "promote not undermine the charter rights of disadvantaged groups."
Therein lies the heart of the matter.
Compassion takes a back seat in Ford's clear-'em-out strategy. The province should take its share of the responsibility for how we got here. As should municipalities. Have they, collectively, exhausted all reasonable means of assisting homeless people? No, they have not.
The encampments must be dealt with. But they are a blight of our collective making. And those living within them, as the courts have wisely concluded, have a right not to be moved until there is somewhere safe to move them to, not least as a cold and dreary winter descends upon us.
CAPTION: Homeless encampment at Dufferin Grove Park in Toronto, December 3, 2024.

Subject: Homeless encampments; Charter of Rights-Canada; Legislation; Evictions; Homeless people; Municipalities

Title: Compassion takes a back seat in Ford's clear out the encampments strategy

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 9, 2024

Document 424 of 500

A GST holiday could benefit struggling Canadians and the middle class. Just not the way Justin Trudeau's doing it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gst-holiday-could-benefit-struggling-canadians/docview/3142290552/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+GST+holiday+could+benefit+struggling+Canadians+and+the+middle+class.+Just+not+the+way+Justin+Trudeau%27s+doing+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Late last month, Canadians received what seemed like an early gift this holiday season: the federal government approved a two-month break from the GST on a select group of goods aimed at easing the financial strain of the festive season.
While the temporary move might offer some minor relief to Canadians facing a cost-of-living crisis, it's the wrong medicine for our economy and our ailing vision of an equitable society. Here's why.
The GST is a regressive tax and research shows that removing value-added taxes like the GST on essential items can be moderately effective for alleviating cost-of-living pressures for lower-income consumers.
That is the rationale behind GST exemptions already in place for basic food items like milk, bread and produce. However, extending exemptions to non-essentials, such as gifts, toys and restaurant meals, as adopted by the government, swings the aggregate benefit toward wealthier Canadians, who typically spend more on these items.
The temporary nature of this initiative combined with the basket of goods receiving a GST exemption implies that the real goal here is to increase consumer spending during a critical period for the retail sector.
The biggest beneficiaries of this big holiday spend are bound to be the biggest retailers of GST-exempted goods - companies like Walmart and Amazon.Boosting their profits won't help anyone but their shareholders.
Over the last two decades, studies have shown while profits for large publicly traded companies continue to soar, both wages and business investment have stagnated. These profits are funneled instead into wealth accumulation for a concentrated few, while further entrenching the market dominance of these corporations. Neither outcome helps build an affordable or equitable future for the majority of Canadians.
We also can't ignore what the GST holiday and the promised $250 rebate for the majority of workers set to follow early in 2025 will cost us. The $6.2 billion in unplanned spending will inevitably put pressure on government services to cut budgets.
Essential social infrastructure - such as education, health care and affordable housing - are already critically underfunded. These areas not only underpin Canada's quality of life but also drive long-term economic competitiveness.
What we need now is a significant influx of public investment in these sectors. A tax cut, especially one without compensatory revenue from other sources, risks taking us in the opposite direction.
A better approach would have been to adopt the NDP's proposed model, which combines GST relief with a tax the rich measure. Just days before the government's announcement, NDP leader Jagmeet Singh unveiled a plan to permanently remove GST from a focused basket of essentials, such as diapers, home heating and internet bills.
This proposal would be funded by an excess profits tax, which targets large corporations that leveraged market power during the pandemic, the war, and inflation to rake in outsized profits.
An even more impactful response to Canada's cost-of-living crisis would involve a comprehensive package of tax reforms designed to address income and wealth inequality. This could include measures such as an excess profits tax, a wealth tax and higher income taxes for the top 0.1 per cent. The revenue generated could be directed toward targeted, evidence-based initiatives that deliver tangible benefits to Canadians within months or years.
Why not help people escape debt through student loan forgiveness or accelerate the expansion of dental care coverage? Why not broaden pharmacare to cover more than just diabetes medications and contraceptives, enabling Canadians to prioritize their health without financial strain?
Long-term investments in critical areas - such as nonmarket affordable housing, public health care infrastructure and accessible, high-quality education - can create lasting affordability, benefiting not only today's Canadians but also future generations.
If we truly want to ease financial pressures for all Canadians - not just in the holiday season but year-round - it's time to move beyond superficial fixes and invest in structural changes that make life better for everyone.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Minister of Finance Chrystia Freeland visit Vince's Market, a grocery store in Sharon, Ontario, on Nov. 21. The government announced that week a sweeping promise to make goods like groceries, children's clothing, Christmas trees and restaurant meals free from GST/HST between Dec. 14 and Feb. 15.
CREDIT: Katrina Miller Contributor

Subject: Canadians; Affordable housing

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: A GST holiday could benefit struggling Canadians and the middle class. Just not the way Justin Trudeau's doing it

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Doug+Ford+preparing+to+override+courts%2C+give+police+and+municipalities+enhanced+legislative+powers+to+%27dismantle%27+homeless+encampments%2C+Dec.+5+Big+jump+in+number+of+Toronto-area+residents+who+say+they+can%27t+make+ends+meet%2C+report+says%2C+Dec.+5+Poor+budgeting+and+too+many+consultants+behind+soaring+cost+of+Toronto+subway+projects%2C+study+reveals%2C+Dec.+5+Four+hours+of+OT+for+a+five-minute+call%3A+Crown+attorneys+told+to+stop+calling+Toronto+police+officers+to+help+curb+overtime+%27insanity%2C%27+Dec.+4+%27Wait+times+have+exploded%27%3A+Bonnie+Crombie+takes+aim+at+Ontario+doctor+shortage+in+campaign+promise%2C+Dec.+2&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Encampment laws will be a death sentence
Doug Ford preparing to override courts, give police and municipalities enhanced legislative powers to 'dismantle' homeless encampments,
Dec. 5
Canada has not had a death penalty for almost 50 years, but Premier Doug Ford's proposed laws to get rid of encampments - in other words vulnerable people's homes - will be a death penalty. As someone who lives in Edmonton, a city with aggressive anti-homeless strategies, I can tell you these rules and practices dotn't eliminate homelessness. They increase deaths, upheaval, multi-generational trauma and substance abuse. Last year, when Edmonton's police adopted a more aggressive approach to homeless people, one in 10 of the city's homeless people died and drug overdoses reached record highs. The lack of long-term encampments means social services have no way of knowing exactly how many homeless people live in Edmonton and are depending on amputations due to frostbite for estimates of homeless counts.We need to end this over-policing of homeless people and provide a stable supply of quality housing outside our country's financialized housing market, or we risk destabilizing our whole society due to inequality and housing insecurity.
Adam Bentley, Edmonton
Mississauga
seeing rise in
food insecurity
Big jump in number of Toronto-area residents who say they can't make ends meet, report says, Dec. 5
This phenomenon of food insecurity is Canada-wide. Reports from all areas of the country are showing a steep climb in use of food banks. Canada was instrumental in drafting the Universal Declaration of Human Rights 76 years ago, yet we have failed to uphold one of its fundamental rights: the right to food. On Nov. 13, Mississauga City Council declared food insecurity an emergency, underscoring a stark reality: Mississauga has the fastest-growing rate of food bank users in Ontario, surpassing the provincial average. Food insecurity stems from multiple factors, including high rent, inadequate government assistance, rising food and gas prices, and the prevalence of low-paying or part-time jobs. Food Banks Mississauga calls on all levels of government to address this crisis by:
Improving Ontario's social assistance programs to reflect economic realities;
Investing in affordable, supportive, and public housing;
Strengthening labour laws to support workers and enhance worker assistance programs;
Backing the Groceries and Essentials Benefit proposed by Food Banks Canada to help households afford basic necessities like food and shelter.
Margaret Ramsay,
The Compass Food Bank
Subway projects start-up process was inept
Poor budgeting and too many consultants behind soaring cost of Toronto subway projects, study reveals, Dec. 5
As a retired professional project management scheduling co-ordinator I am not surprised by the fiasco created because of the inept project start-up process that transpired early on. Projects of this magnitude cannot possibly be successful without the initial organizational requirements being performed in accordance with what I believe to be the only route to success: Every member of the team must contribute to the development of the schedule and be fully committed to the requirements to implement their tasks in a timely fashion. Designs cannot be tossed and redone every time a consulting engineer moves on to another commitment. Get a handle on it, Metrolinx: set objectives, set strategies based on those objectives and take the time to sit down as a team and GET IT DONE!
Gary Brewer, Kingston
Police budget ask won't even cover overtime costs
Four hours of OT for a five-minute call: Crown attorneys told to stop calling Toronto police officers to help curb overtime 'insanity,' Dec. 4
The Crown office is being asked to help control rising police overtime due to the requirement to provide disclosure for trial. Disclosure is a basic and regular duty that police are required to perform. This overtime, called premium pay, is budgeted at $59 million this year, and was exceeded by $30 million in 2022. For 2025 budget, the police force is asking for an increase of $46 million. They claim the increase will improve service and 911 response times. In reality, this won't even cover the excessive overtime for what should be their regular job of providing disclosure.
Clyde McDonald,
Bracebridge, Ont.
Fixing this crisis requires nurse practitioners
'Wait times have exploded': Bonnie Crombie takes aim at Ontario doctor shortage in campaign promise, Dec. 2
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie's "guarantee" of family doctors won't solve Ontario's primary care crisis. Instead, Crombie should look at proven and tested solutions used across the world: nurse practitioners. While physicians are essential, they cannot address this crisis alone. Nurse practitioners with advanced clinical education and legal authority to diagnose, treat and prescribe provide high-quality, cost-effective care across all health settings. Dr. Jane Philpott, lead of Ontario's primary care action team, shared on X how utilizing nurse practitioners ensures all Ontarians' ongoing access to primary care. Nurses urge Crombie to revise her pledge and promise access to either a family doctor or nurse practitioners supported by interprofessional teams of nurses, allied health professionals and social workers to the 2.5 million Ontarians who currently lack a primary care provider.
Dr. Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses' Association
of Ontario, Toronto

Document 426 of 500

The GST/HST holiday tax break kicks in this week. Here is what is covered

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/gst-hst-holiday-tax-break-kicks-this-week-here-is/docview/3142182709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+GST%2FHST+holiday+tax+break+kicks+in+this+week.+Here+is+what+is+covered&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tax holiday for the holiday season will start this week, giving some Canadians relief on year-end shopping.
The temporary tax break will remove the federal and provincial tax from all groceries, restaurant meals, children's clothes, toys and diapers, as well as beer and wine, for a two-month period from Dec. 14 to Feb. 15, in a bid to ease the cost of living for Canadians.
In Ontario, for the products included in the tax break, that means the harmonized sales tax (HST), which is comprised of the federal GST portion (at five per cent) and the provincial portion (at eight per cent), won't apply.
The Liberals have also proposed sending $250 cheques to anyone who worked in 2023 and made less than $150,000, but have not yet introduced legislation on that measure. The New Democrats and Bloc - joined by some members of the Liberal caucus - want to see eligibility expanded to more Canadians.
What is covered under the GST/HST holiday break?Children's goods
Clothing, diapers, car seats and toys for kids are all covered under the new temporary tax break.
For clothing, that includes: baby clothes, including bibs and blankets; children's clothes up to girls' size 16 and boys' size 20; children's socks, hats, scarves and gloves; shoes smaller than 24.25 cm; and sports clothing like jerseys, leotards, ski jackets and recreational swimsuits.
What's not included: any specialized clothing or shoes designed exclusively for sports (like wetsuits, soccer cleats, skates, ski boots, etc.), adult clothing purchased for a child, costumes or makeup and jewelry.
Most diapers are included in the tax break, including cloth and disposable diapers, diaper inserts or training pants - however, adult diapers are not included (they are already exempt from GST/HST).
The same goes for children's car seats, which includes all boosters and seats that meet federal safety standards, but does not include car seats that are built as part of a stroller or carrier combination.
Children's toys covered under the tax break include most items intended for children under 14 years old (which mostly captures toys recommended for children aged 8 and up). That encompasses board games, card games, toys, dolls, plush toys and jigsaw puzzles (for all ages). Excluded are collectibles, like hockey cards or collectible dolls, and toys marketed for adults (like adult Lego sets).
Video games
Video game consoles, controllers and video games are included in the tax break as well, though only physical copies of games (like in CDs or cartridges) qualify.
Books
For the most part, published printed books (both hardcover and softcover editions), magazines and periodicals are covered under the tax break.
E-books and audio books are not included and magazines need to be supplied by a subscription or have less than five per cent of its printed space devoted to advertising in order to qualify. Agendas, calendars and notebooks do not qualify for the tax break.
Newspapers
Printed newspapers also qualify for the tax break, but excludes digital versions of those publications.
Christmas trees
Both natural and artificial Christmas trees, as well as "similar decorative trees," qualify for the tax break, according to the government.
Food and drinks
All restaurant meals - including dine-in, takeout and delivery - are covered by the tax holiday. That also covers prepared foods, pre-made meals and catering. The tax break also includes: snacks like chips, candy and baked goods; non-alcoholic drinks including coffee, tea, juices, soda and smoothies; beer; wine, cider and sake at 22.9 per cent ABV or less; and premixed alcoholic drinks at seven per cent ABV or less.
That exemption does not include food or drink from vending machines, dietary supplements and pet food.
Basic groceries - including fresh fruits, vegetables, meat, poultry and fish - are already excluded from both the provincial and federal portions of the HST in Ontario.
How about the provincial sales tax break?
The Ford government has pledged to match the federal government's two-month GST holiday, which overlaps with pre-existing tax breaks in the province.
Ontario already provides a "point-of-sale rebate" on the eight per cent portion of the HST on: books, children's clothes and shoes, children's car seats, diapers, some prepared food and beverages under $4 and some books and newspapers.
That will expand to cover the federal GST/HST tax holiday, meaning consumers will see a full 13 per cent tax break on the items above.
How much can I save?
The total savings you will see depends entirely on your spending habits. Stocking up on toys, books and processed foods? You could save a few dozen dollars. If you're sticking to the basic groceries and having a low-key holiday season, you'll be less likely to see any reduction in your expenses, as the HST already doesn't apply to basic groceries.
Still confused? Here are some examples.
Let's say you go all out and purchase a PlayStation 5 Slim, plus an extra controller and a physical copy of a game (for example, NHL 25). Using prices from Dec. 3 on BestBuy, your total would usually be $802.27. Remove the HST and that would bring your total down to $709.97, resulting in $92.30 in savings.
Maybe you stock up on diapers, toys and food at Costco. Assuming you get a box of 192 size 1 diapers (Kirkland brand), plus a kids Lego set and a box of assorted chips, your total would be around $214.67 pre-tax holiday. Between December and February, that comes down to $189.97, or about $24.70 in savings.
And maybe you're buying Christmas presents for a book worm? A box set of the first five Game of Thrones books at Indigo (through its online store) would usually cost $67.74, but with the tax break the price comes down to $59.95.
CAPTION: The federal government announced the tax holiday in November, in an attempt to ease Canadians' cost-of-living woes.

Subject: Clothing; Canadians; Meals; Computer & video games; Children & youth; Food; Diapers; Cost control; Sales taxes; Toys

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost control Sales taxes

Document 427 of 500

Compassion is taking a back seat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/compassion-is-taking-back-seat/docview/3142182681/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Compassion+is+taking+a+back+seat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How grimly fitting that Premier Doug Ford has chosen the season of want to threaten the use of the notwithstanding clause in the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms to clear the city's homeless encampments.
A Hollywood movie might track the evening snow settling on tents and over tarps as the provincial government moves to introduce clampdown legislation on the eve of the legislature's jollily generous holiday break. Yes, it sounds borderline Dickensian.
To parse the facts more carefully, the premier penned a letter, not to Santa Claus, but to 12 mayors (not including Olivia Chow), scantily outlining legislation to be introduced next week that will "achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Ford's main focus: "to put an end to public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth of the encampments."
A more thoughtful missive would have begun thusly:
We have failed.
We have collectively - municipalities and successive provincial governments alike - failed to provide long-promised solutions to chronic homelessness.
We have exacerbated the tragedy of want. We have inflamed the tragedy of ignorance.
Let's be clear. These encampments are occupying too much of the already limited green space in this city, spaces meant for the leisure of all residents, not to provide inadequate shelter for the most vulnerable. And public disorder and threats to safety can, and have, emerged from these tent cities.
They must be dealt with. But they cannot be meaningfully addressed until there is somewhere for those being evicted to go.
The premier's precis of the pending legislation does include additional funding to create more capacity in the shelter system, and "new tools and authorities" to assist police enforcing the prohibition of illicit drugs in public.
There's additionally the promise of new, unspecified approaches to drug treatment and rehabilitation that prioritize recovery over incarceration.
These promises are directionally right, though absent specific details, we'll wait to pass judgment.
But Ford did not put down his pen.
Instead, he prophesied.
Should the courts "interfere," as they have in the past, by upholding the Charter rights of individuals deprived of housing, his government is, he wrote, "fully prepared" to use the notwithstanding clause, Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, to override the court's decision. That includes being an intervener "in any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments."
The courts have done precisely that in the past. An Ontario Supreme Court ruling last year rejected an application from the Regional Municipality of Waterloo to be granted an injunction, under a local bylaw, to evict homeless people - approximately 70 shelters with an estimated 50 residents - from property owned by the municipality. The bylaw infringed Section 7 of the Charter, the court found. That is, that everyone has the right to "life, liberty and security of the person."
Keep in mind that Ford's notwithstanding threat, for that's what it is, comes on the heels of a letter addressed to the premier and signed by 450 members of Ontario's legal community urging him to stand down. "The notwithstanding clause was never intended to be used to deprive vulnerable groups of constitutional protection," the group wrote. Government policy should "promote not undermine the charter rights of disadvantaged groups."
Therein lies the heart of the matter.
Compassion takes a back seat in Ford's clear-'em-out strategy. The province should take its share of the responsibility for how we got here. As should municipalities. Have they, collectively, exhausted all reasonable means of assisting homeless people? No, they have not.
The encampments must be dealt with. But they are a blight of our collective making. And those living within them, as the courts have wisely concluded, have a right not to be moved until there is somewhere safe to move them to, not least as a cold and dreary winter descends upon us.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Charter of Rights-Canada; Legislation; Evictions; Homeless people; Municipalities; Homeless encampments

Celebrating the holidays with all your favourites just got more convenient thanks to Skip

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/celebrating-holidays-with-all-your-favourites/docview/3142182278/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Celebrating+the+holidays+with+all+your+favourites+just+got+more+convenient+thanks+to+Skip&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The holiday season is officially here, and with it comes the hustle and bustle of planning festive celebrations. But fret not, because Skip is here to give you the gift of unparalleled convenience and extensive delivery offerings!
Instead of running around the city to pick up all your holiday essentials, you can rely on Skip to deliver everything right to your doorstep - from foodie-approved dishes, snacks and groceries, to flowers, gifts and everything in between. Skip ensures you're ready to conquer all of the holiday happenings so you can get right to the good part, with less of the hassle.
Just in time for the holidays, Skip revealed its annual ordering trends in Canada for 2024 - and it's clear that Ontario's unique habits have set trends nationwide. From candy and coffee to butter chicken and sushi, our love for diverse culinary options runs deep. So, whether you're celebrating Christmas, Hanukkah, Eid, or New Year's Eve, there's something special for everyone during the holiday season, and getting it delivered to your door is as easy as a few taps on your phone.
For Christmas, charcuterie platters reign supreme in Ontario - and the Skip data proves it. Ontario made up 21 per cent of all grape orders, 31 per cent of olive orders, 30 per cent of cracker orders, and 28 per cent of cheese orders nationwide - we truly know how to throw a party! And for those who prefer to skip the lines at the grocery store, Skip has all the charcuterie must-haves right at your fingertips through Skip Express Lane and local partners like Fresh & Wild Food Market, so you can be sure to wow your crowd.
A true tradition for Jewish families on Christmas? Enjoying Chinese food, of course! But since many will crowd the local hot spots, it's the perfect opportunity to order it in for the whole family through Skip. Try Kendrick Lamar's go-to Chinese spot for fried rice with a dip sauce, New Ho King, or try other top Toronto restaurants like Rol San, Pearl Chinese, or Juicy Dumpling. With countless options readily available through Skip, choosing which restaurant to order from is the only hard part.
In 2024, the province's top ordered food items were savoury garlic naan and hearty butter chicken. To make the most of your time with family and friends over the holidays, you can order a gourmet Indian meal from Skip's restaurant partners like Bukhara Grill, Madras Masala, or Butter Chicken Factory. Oh, and don't forget the samosas when you do!
Whether you've had your holiday get-together with friends planned for two months or two hours, Skip's also got you covered with a variety of options for wine, beer and spirits. With LCBO, Wine Rack, Beer Store, and local Toronto favourites like Goldenfield Brewery. and Magnotta Winery readily available through Skip, you'll be sure to say "cheers!" to that.
Looking for some more recommendations? Here are some other trending food, alcohol and gifting destinations you can get delivered right to your doorstep through Skip for the holidays and beyond!
Gusto 101
For the ride-or-die Italian lovers, nothing compares to the best of Gusto 101. Picture this:dream-worthy focaccia, a vibrant kale salad and indulgent creamy mushroom pasta. Whether you choose one or all three, it's sure to be a meal to please.
PAI
Nothing compares to the authentic flavours of PAI's Thai cuisine, and we know your friends and family will agree. Treat your loved ones to the restaurant's legendary pad Thai, flavourful red and green curries and fresh shrimp rolls-it's a feast they'll savour and one that's sure to have them coming back for more next year.
Shake Shack
Get ready to be the hostess with the mostess because the hottest burgers from south of the border have finally made their way up north. There's only one way to get Shake Shack delivered - exclusively on Skip! Order all the classics like ShackBurgers, crispy crinkle-cut fries and a delicious milkshake to go with it, sure to make your next gathering truly memorable.
Henderson Brewing Company
Ready to up your beer and snack game for a night in? Henderson Brewing Company has you covered with its curated selection of its famous house beers and non-alcoholic beverages too. You can load up on frozen pizzas and Henderson's famous beer nuts while you're at it.
Simply Flowers
When it comes to last minute hostess gifts, Simply Flowers is anything but. From sculptural orchid arrangements to seasonal stems, they have an arrangement that's just right for any occasion, big or small.
Mac's Pizza
If you're looking for the ultimate indulgence for a movie night at home, Mac's Pizza is here to assist-exclusively through Skip.Whether you're craving a meat lovers or a loaded veggie pizza, you can grab whatever you're in the mood for (or one of each!) and keep the whole family happy and full.
Ready to Skip to the good part? All you have to do is download the Skip app on iOS or the Google Play Store to get all your holiday essentials delivered right to your doorstep! Delivery has never been more joyous, or more convenient.
CAPTION: New Ho King is available for delivery on Skip.Butter Chicken Hut is available for delivery on Skip.PAI Toronto is available for delivery on Skip.

Document 429 of 500

'We are in this legal limbo': Casa Loma residents opposing new development face a tougher fight after Doug Ford's housing law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-are-this-legal-limbo-casa-loma-residents/docview/3142072621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+are+in+this+legal+limbo%27%3A+Casa+Loma+residents+opposing+new+development+face+a+tougher+fight+after+Doug+Ford%27s+housing+law&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto-area residents group says it has no choice but to turn to the courts to have its voice heard on a condo project near Casa Loma that it fears will destroy a rare historic building and obstruct some of the famous view of the downtown core from the tourist attraction.
The Casa Loma Residents Association says its efforts to use the court system to challenge a municipal planning decision to approve a midrise condo project is slow and time-consuming, but it's the only recourse left to groups like them after the Doug Ford government passed Bill 185 Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act earlier this year, removing the ability of third-party groups to appeal development decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal.
"Community groups like ours have lost the right to appeal development decisions," said Robert Levy, the president of the residents association. "So now there's even less transparency around how planning decisions are being made."
The residents group says its case is unique because it isn't fighting the project, its density or its location. Its main ask has been for the developer to preserve part of a historic 1930s building that housed the Barker's Bread Bakery and to make sure the eight-floor condo doesn't obscure the view of downtown from Casa Loma.
"We were completely ignored," said Levy.
"There was willingness to address the issues - keep the facade, shift some of the density. It was never about stopping the project," he said. "It was really about citizens trying to make their city better."
Levy said after months of trying to find common ground with the city and Zinc Developments, which is building the eight-storey 143-unit condo project at 555 Davenport Rd., the group filed an appeal at the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT) last year.
But instead of hearing the case, the adjudicator dismissed the appeal in February - at the request of the developer - saying that the concerns of the residents association were "not based on a valid land use planning ground" and "had no reasonable prospect of success."
The group then appealed the dismissal to the court system, during which time Bill 185 was enacted, cancelling the ability of all third-party groups to appeal development decisions at the land tribunal.
Richard Macklin, the lawyer representing the group, said because it was appealing an OLT decision at divisional court, and not within the tribunal, it was unclear if Bill 185 applied to them - and so it has continued its appeal process.
"We are in this legal limbo," he said, arguing that the new law doesn't apply to cases outside the OLT. He said the group has filed a judicial review of the OLT decision. A hearing date has been scheduled for February. The city approved the development in May 2023.
Developer Ken Zuckerman, the president of Zinc Developments said he couldn't comment on the ongoing case.
"Due to the ongoing legal challenges by the CLRA we have been advised by our legal counsel not make any comments until the conclusion of the litigation," he said in an email.
Levy said the residents group appealed the city's approval of the condo after extensive efforts to have the unique property deemed heritage, which would give it special protections.
Levy said the city's heritage planning department had determined that the building - designed by the same architects who designed the ROM and Princess Gates - met the stringent criteria for full designation set out in the Ontario Heritage Act.
But Levy said that despite this, the city decided at the last minute to drop the endorsement for heritage designation and only preserve a small part of the building, according to documents the residents' group obtained through a freedom of information request.
"We never got an answer from the city about why they changed their mind," he said. "We are asking for two reasonable compromises that will make this project better: preserve some heritage, preserve views that are enjoyed by community. We don't have that many special areas - like Casa Loma - in Toronto."
In an email, Catherine Jung, an urban planner with the city, said the developer had "explored potential retention strategies, however, (they) learned that any retained façade would require substantial reconstruction."
Jung added that the "approved application proposes integrating part of the principal façade of the existing building as a commemorative element within the new development as part of a larger commemoration plan."
The residents' group also argues that the condo will block views of the downtown core from the Casa Loma area, which are to be protected under the city's official plan. It requires new developments to "maintain views to and from the Lake Iroquois Escarpment, including the Casa Loma/Spadina House complex."
The residents group says that the policy protects not only views from Casa Loma to the downtown core, but should also include nearby vantage points. It said it tried to work with the city and developer to adjust the plans for the condo so this view would be protected.
In an email, Jung said staff "examined the protected views of the area, finding the scale of the development would protect the distinctive characteristics and maintain views to and from the Casa Loma/Spadina House complex, in accordance with city policies."
She said the city "considered views to and from Casa Loma and Spadina House as well as from the Baldwin Steps during both summer and winter seasons. In addition, views from Casa Loma to the financial district skyline, and of Casa Loma and its towers from the east side intersection of Dupont Street and Spadina Road were considered."
Macklin said this discussion should have taken place at the OLT to understand the scope of the city policy, instead of an outright dismissal of the case.
Local city councillor Josh Matlow said he's tried to help the two sides find a solution outside of the courts:
"I've been telling both parties that a solution that respects heritage without losing a unit of new housing is possible," said Matlow. "It's unfortunate that so much time has been needlessly wasted."
Levy agreed that the legal fight has been a "complete waste of time" and said the group is still hoping to come to an out of court agreement with the developer.
Macklin concedes that the court process has bogged down the case, but said it's the only option available left for those who want to participate in the planning process:
"The Ford government passed a relatively draconian bill severely curtailing community involvement in municipal development projects," he said. "The government thinks that Bill 185 has fixed the housing crisis for them by silencing all the community groups. From our point of view, it's not that simple."
CAPTION: The historic bakery building at the southeast corner of Walmer and Davenport Roads with Art Deco facade.Robert Levy, president of the Casa Loma Residents Association, said his group has asked a condo builder to preserve part of a heritage building and to not obscure the historic view of downtown from the area. "We were completely ignored," he said.

Doug Ford's Tories leading as potential election looms, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-leading-as-potential-election/docview/3142072616/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+leading+as+potential+election+looms%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford mulls gambling on an early election, a new poll suggests that might be a safe bet.
The latest Abacus Data monthly tracking survey for the Star found Ford's Tories at 43 per cent - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 25 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at six per cent.
"Now is as good a time as any to go (to voters). I don't see how their numbers get better," said Abacus president David Coletto.
"He's still by far the favourite to win the election," said Coletto, warning that federal circumstances beyond Ford's control could transform the electoral landscape.
"Tim Houston benefited from the fact that Justin Trudeau is still the Liberal leader," he said, referring to last week's landslide re-election victory by the Nova Scotia Tories in which Trudeau's unpopularity hindered the provincial Grits there.
"Trudeau is pulling down Bonnie Crombie's numbers here. But what happens when - and I think it's a 'when' - the federal Liberals get defeated?"
Coletto said if, as every recent public-opinion poll suggests, Trudeau's minority Liberal government is toppled by Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives, the political dynamic at Queen's Park could change.
"Trudeau is the glue. But once he's gone, what happens? That's why Ford wants to go before Trudeau is gone."
While a provincial election is not scheduled until June 2026, the premier and his campaign advisers have been ruminating over facing voters this spring just after 15 million Ontarians receive $200 "rebate" cheques.
Three senior Tory insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, told the Star that Ford shrugged off Houston's massive win on Nov. 26.
"Ontario is not Nova Scotia," the premier told several people in the days after that election when aides insisted the PC success there portended good things for the Ontario party.
Sources say Ford is preoccupied by the crisis triggered by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian and Mexican goods unless both countries tighten their borders.
But that does not mean a snap election is necessarily on the backburner, they confide.
Mindful of the uncertainty that looms once Trump takes office Jan. 20, Ford's team is concerned Ontario could be plunged into a recession if tariffs spark a trade war.
Such economic upheaval could make re-election more challenging, they note.
Abacus surveyed 1,500 Ontarians from Nov. 28 through Wednesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.6 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
That means the poll was conducted in the wake of Trump's highly publicized tariff gambit.
In terms of opinions on the party leaders, 37 per cent had a positive impression of Ford while 43 per cent had a negative view with 18 per cent neutral and two per cent didn't know enough to say. That's a net impression rating for him of minus six per cent.
Crombie was 25 per cent positive, 32 per cent negative, 29 per cent neutral and 13 per cent unsure for a minus seven per cent net rating.
Stiles had a 28 per cent positive with a 25 per cent negative, a 30 per cent neutral and 18 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus three per cent.
Schreiner had a 22 per cent positive, 20 per cent negative, 37 per cent neutral and 23 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus two per cent.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends a signing of a memorandum of understanding with Governor of Illinois J.B. Pritzker, at the US-Canada Summit in Toronto on June 11, 2024.
