Canadians want a tough approach to Donald Trump - but some like him better than Justin Trudeau, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-want-tough-approach-donald-trump-some/docview/3141774889/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+want+a+tough+approach+to+Donald+Trump+-+but+some+like+him+better+than+Justin+Trudeau%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-As Canadian politicians jockey to position themselves as the best to deal with a mercurial incoming U.S. president Donald Trump, a wary Canadian public is cool to any compromises and supports a tough approach, according to a pair of new polls.
Many Canadians - 40 per cent - are prepared to cut Mexico loose in any trade talks Canada has with the United States, and most - 56 per cent - are not prepared to welcome any influx of migrants fleeing northward to escape Trump's threatened mass deportation orders, according to an Abacus Data poll over the last week shared with the Star.
An Angus Reid Institute poll similarly shows almost half of Canadians - 49 per cent - want the federal government to take a tough approach to Trump's tariff threat to slap a 25 per cent surcharge on Canadian imports. They agree with the suggestion Ottawa should "play hardball - even if the tariffs come, Canada shouldn't let itself be bullied." Another 33 per cent said the federal government should "try to negotiate for a lower tariff," while only 10 per cent said Canada should "concede and do whatever the U.S. demands to avoid the tariffs."
Economists say Trump's threatened 25 per cent across-the-board tariff on imports from Canada and Mexico, with an additional 10 per cent tariff on China (which Trump has previously threatened with 60 per cent tariffs) would slam Canada's economy and trigger a recession.
The Angus Reid poll found that pessimistic view is reflected in the expectations of most Canadians - about 75 per cent expect the Canadian dollar to suffer and the economy to worsen, and nearly half expect their own finances to take a hit. As a result, more than half - 54 per cent - believe Canada should increase border security and 55 per cent want Ottawa to increase its defence spending to the NATO target of two per cent of GDP or higher - and on a faster pace than is currently planned - reflecting a desire to see the federal government meet Trump's demands.
Taken together, the surveys suggest a high level of anxiety among Canadians vis-à-vis the incoming Trump administration that political leaders may seek to allay, amplify or aggravate.
In the past week, they've done all three.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has sought to assure Canadians that he had a productive face-to-face meeting with Trump last week at the Mar-a-Lago resort, saying his government will commit unspecified additional funding to securing the Canada-U.S. border with more technology and manpower to work with the U.S. to stem flows of illegal immigrants and fentanyl - all while arguing Canada is not the same headache for Washington on those two issues that Mexico is.
The Liberals are hopeful Canada can win a reprieve, but say they're not there yet.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has amplified Trump's concerns and blamed Trudeau for having "lost control of our border, lost control of immigration, lost control of our finances." In French, he said Trump is an "aggressive president," and said Canada needs tougher immigration and border policies to deal with "border disorder." Poilievre has echoed Trump's America first discourse, saying he'd bolster border security, increase police powers, cap the number of immigrants and "false refugees" Canada lets in, and slash taxes, calling his approach a "Canada first" plan.
The NDP has aligned with the effort to allay Trump's concerns, while talking tough. Leader Jagmeet Singh demands 1,100 more front-line border guards be hired (the border guards' union says up to 3,000 more are needed) and wants the prime minister to "stand up and fight like hell."
Curiously, despite Trump's threats, Coletto's latest survey also shows that some Canadians' impressions of Donald Trump have improved over the last two weeks, with his positive impressions slightly higher than Trudeau's.
Abacus found 26 per cent of Canadians held a positive view of Trump, up five points in a month; 14 per cent had a neutral impression, but most - 58 per cent - have a negative view (down seven points in a month).
Meanwhile, Trudeau's negatives outweigh his positives, with 23 per cent citing a positive impression, 15 per cent a neutral one, and 61 per cent a negative one, according to the same poll.
In fact, some Canadians' views of Trump have improved since November 2020, near the end of Trump's first term in office, while their view of Trudeau has worsened. Back then, 11 per cent of Canadians had a positive view of Trump, 11 per cent were neutral, while 77 per cent had a negative view. That was after Trump renegotiated NAFTA with Canada and Mexico, slapped steel and aluminum tariffs (later withdrawn) on Canada, and withheld exports of personal protective equipment to Canada during the COVID-19 pandemic. At the same time 42 per cent of Canadians had a positive impression of Trudeau, 22 per cent a neutral view, and 35 per cent a negative impression, says Coletto.
Trudeau, and his Liberal party, have since plunged in national polls, with Abacus' latest findings showing a 23-point lead for the Poilievre-led Conservatives over Trudeau's Liberals.
Coletto says it's too early to know who Canadians think is doing the best job in responding to Trump, but he doubts Poilievre is taking much of a risk in purposely not joining "Team Canada" - in part, he says, because "the prime minister is even less popular than Trump is in Canada. He is seen as so either disliked or ineffective that this is almost giving Poilievre a space to not have to be seen as collaborative and supportive of this effort."
Furthermore, many Canadians - 40 per cent according to Abacus - agree with two Conservative premiers who flatly said Canada should negotiate a separate free trade agreement with the United States that does not include Mexico, as Ontario's Doug Ford and Alberta's Danielle Smith stated.
Among those who identify as Liberal supporters, those who think it's a good idea to exclude Mexico is 42 per cent. And among Conservative supporters, it's higher still: 50 per cent agree that Mexico should be cut loose in future trade talks.
Trudeau has said his government is "leaving all doors open" on negotiating trade matters with Trump. "Ideally, we do that as a united North American market," he told reporters at press conference in Toronto two weeks ago. "But, pending decisions and choices that Mexico has made, we may have to look at other options."
Abacus Data surveyed 2,720 Canadian adults from Nov. 29 to Dec. 4, using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have a margin of error of plus or minus 1.9 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The Angus Reid Institute conducted an online survey from Nov. 29 to Dec. 2, using an online panel of 3,003 Canadian adults, and a random sample of this size would carry a margin of error of plus or minus 1.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
CAPTION: Then-U.S. President Donald Trump, left, and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talk before to a NATO roundtable meeting in Watford, Hertfordshire, England on Dec. 4, 2019.

Subject: International trade; Canadians; Prime ministers; Immigration; Border security; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Tariffs

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Brakes & Ladder: How construction sites are bringing the city to a crawl

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/brakes-amp-ladder/docview/3141774868/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brakes+%26amp%3B+Ladder%3A+How+construction+sites+are+bringing+the+city+to+a+crawl&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Toronto furiously debates bike lanes and their role in the city's notorious gridlock, mostly missing from the discussion is a culprit that at its peak occupied almost one-fifth of the city's road network.
Construction - for provincially managed transit projects, condo and office buildings, and utility work to support Toronto's booming growth - closes more kilometres of roadway than bike lanes, special events or anything else. City officials say construction closures are the biggest cause of the kind of traffic backups that are angering residents and the Ford government.
While nobody is debating Toronto's urgent need for transit, housing and critical infrastructure, even some of the offenders acknowledge that the current crush of closures could have been avoided. The province says it is playing catch-up after "decades of inaction" on transit, and the city is unveiling new fees to spur developers, whose equipment can occupy roadways for years as extensions are routinely granted, to speed up their work.
Roger Browne, the city's director of traffic management, said Toronto has about 5,600 kilometres of roadway that is not growing. "This summer we had points in time where as much as 18 per cent of road capacity was taken away by road construction ... Really, that's the issue today in terms of congestion."
In peak season, that translated to approximately 1,012 kilometres of roadway closed by construction during one week in July, according to estimates by city staff. During the month with the lowest construction activity, December, the city estimates that about two per cent of the total roadway is occupied for construction, or about 112 kilometres.
While Premier Doug Ford has focused his ire on Toronto's bikeways, the total on-street cycling network covered 286.3 kilometres at the end of last year, including lanes on small side streets. The bike lanes that Ford has targeted for removal to combat gridlock - on University Avenue, Yonge Street and Bloor Street - total 20.2 kilometres, or two per cent of the peak summertime construction-related lane closures.
As Jacquelyn Hayward, the city's director of planning, design and management, told a legislative committee in November: "Construction-related road closures are a primary factor contributing to congestion across the city of Toronto today - not bike lanes."
Pain points
The granddaddy of all construction closures is on Balmuto Street, near Yonge and Bloor. A short stretch of one northbound lane here has been closed since 2017, the longest that city staff are aware of. If this road closure were a child, it would be old enough to play soccer, read and make itself cereal in the morning.
The closure is tied to the troubled luxury skyscraper at 1 Bloor St. W., also known as The One, the Mizrahi Development Group project that went into receivership last year. If the lane closure lasts until the new expected finish date in 2027, it will be a decade old.
Another lengthy closure is the stretch of Queen Street downtown being torn up for work on the new Ontario Line subway, which includes closing the road between Yonge and Bay streets for a scheduled four years. That has resulted in a years-long detour of the 501 Queen streetcar via Richmond and Adelaide streets.
But perhaps a more routine example is the northbound curb-lane closure on busy Yonge Street at Soudan Avenue, just south of Eglinton Avenue, so that Tribute Communities can build a 38-storey tower with luxury condos and offices. City council approved the lane closure from July 16, 2021, to June 30, 2024, and later an extension to Dec. 31, 2025 - more than four years and five months.
On a recent weekday afternoon, a large truck occupied half the lane while on the other half a parade of pedestrians threaded a makeshift sidewalk past the site. Construction trucks, temporarily parked, blocked a live traffic lane on the other side of Yonge and another around the corner on Soudan. Tribute Communities did not respond to the Star's questions about the lane.
Coun. Josh Matlow, whose midtown ward of Toronto-St. Paul's sees a lot of construction, has urged council colleagues to take measures to curb construction lane-taking, including asking city staff to look at the feasibility of stopping it altogether or imposing dramatically escalating fees.
"The closure of lanes has one of the most significant impacts on traffic gridlock in Toronto, affecting drivers, bicyclists, transit riders and pedestrians alike," Matlow told the Star in an email. "It's time the city and the province restrict how often lanes are shut down for construction staging and return our roads back to commuters who are trying to get around the city."
Currently, developers' fees depend on what they are doing. Using a lane for a backhoe or tower crane, for example, costs $84.60 plus HST per day. To take up that lane for four years would cost just under $125,000 before tax. As part of its congestion management plan released in September, the city has proposed higher fees to try to incentivize developers to work faster; the new rates will be announced next year.
In contrast, Metrolinx does not pay fees for its closures since it has jurisdiction on the roadway, according to the city.
The public will be able to view more information about road closures, including who is responsible for each one, when the city unveils a "congestion management" dashboard, expected to launch soon and be updated in the new year with more detailed data, Browne said.
Waiting for transit
The biggest culprit when it comes to occupying the most rights-of-way is city-led construction work, according to staff. Runner-up is utility work. These types of construction can last anywhere from days to months, but very rarely longer than a year.
But measured by how long each project occupies a lane, the worst offender is transit expansion, followed by private development. These projects can last many years.
Spokespersons for Metrolinx and Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria both said they work closely with the city to minimize traffic disruption.
"After decades of inaction by the previous government, if we don't start building the infrastructure needed to support our growing population, gridlock will continue to get worse in the years ahead," said Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson for Sarkaria.
"We are building the largest transit expansion in North American history, and our investments in this much-needed rapid transit throughout the city will help relieve congestion on roads and the existing transit network."
David Wilkes, the president and CEO of BILD, a lobby group for developers, argues that these disruptions are minimal as a proportion of total roadway closures, and that the juice is worth the squeeze.
"Since typical highrise developments occupy only a small portion of road surface for staging versus entire road or lane closures for roadwork or transit projects, the overall impact on Toronto's road network is very small, especially considering the urgent need for housing."
Browne noted that Toronto is the busiest city in North America for construction projects, with 221 cranes in the air as of earlier this year - more than quadruple the city in second place, Los Angeles. In addition to the delays for housing, he noted many of the city-led construction projects are necessary: "This is critical infrastructure work that needs to get done."
However, critics say that while infrastructure work is necessary, we wouldn't have so many overlapping projects if the city had co-ordinated better. The city, meanwhile, says many construction projects were delayed by the pandemic, contributing to the current crush.
Cost of catch-up
Giles Gherson, chief executive of the Toronto Region Board of Trade, has joined the Ford government's calls for the city to stop building bike lanes on main streets, but, in an email to the Star, he acknowledged that construction lane closures are part of the problem.
"Unco-ordinated and highly concentrated construction projects are undeniably a major driver of congestion," he said.
"This is layered on top of an underdeveloped and under-resourced transit and transportation network that's been stretched to its limits by our region's unprecedented population growth. While disruptive, this construction is necessary as we play catch-up on decades of underinvestment in critical infrastructure."
As an example of poor co-ordination, Gherson pointed to two side-by-side projects. Soon after the city began rehabilitating the section of the Gardiner Expressway that crosses Dufferin Street, it also began streetcar track and water main replacement work on the section of King Street West that crosses Dufferin - "the logical parallel diversion route," Gherson said.
The water main that needed replacing was 146 years old. When local Coun. Ausma Malik announced King Street would reopen 10 weeks earlier than expected, she also noted that the work had been long overdue. "The timing of these necessary improvements was the unfortunate result of many years of neglect: a refusal to sufficiently invest in our city and its residents' safety," said Malik.
While reducing construction road closures and removing bike lanes might speed up traffic a little, Browne says gridlock's increasingly tight grip on Toronto commuters is a function of a booming population fighting for roughly the same amount of road space.
Toronto's population surged by about 126,000 people just between July 2022 and July 2023, according to Toronto Metropolitan University research. Population growth was smaller but still significant in surrounding municipalities, from which some drivers commute into Toronto.
"That incredible growth, in terms of population and demand for transportation infrastructure when road infrastructure is so limited, that is our real big problem," Browne said. "Traffic is sign of a growing, thriving city ...
"Where cycling or walking is viable, you want to make sure those options exist for them," as well as transit to encourage people to consider not driving and reduce the number of vehicles and therefore gridlock, he said.
"We need to provide options for everyone."

First Nations want courts to force Trudeau government to pay them a half billion in promised carbon cash

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/first-nations-want-courts-force-trudeau/docview/3141949983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=First+Nations+want+courts+to+force+Trudeau+government+to+pay+them+a+half+billion+in+promised+carbon+cash&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-First Nations groups challenging the Trudeau government's carbon levy in court are seeking an order that would force Ottawa to immediately dole out or guarantee hundreds of millions of dollars promised to them, saying they fear the money could be lost if there is an early election.
The federal government says a decision on how it plans to give around $530 million in carbon pricing proceeds promised since 2020 to Indigenous groups is coming soon. But the Chiefs of Ontario are worried following years of delays, a dispute over how the money should be divided and an unstable minority Parliament that has increased the chances of an early election.
"We don't want to litigate over this. We want Canada to work with us in good faith so we can end the litigation brought forward by Chiefs of Ontario," Ontario Regional Chief Abram Benedict said in a written statement. "The longer Canada takes to fix the carbon tax and the longer they're taking money from our Nations, and the more challenges they will face."
The Chiefs of Ontario filed for a judicial review of the Trudeau government's carbon pricing and rebate regime a year ago, arguing it was disproportionally impacted by the so-called carbon tax and were unable to reap the same benefits of the accompanying rebates as other Canadians.
The groups contended the carbon pricing system is discriminatory because First Nations face more difficulty transitioning away from fossil fuels due to infrastructure gaps and at the same time face a greater impact from climate change. Indigenous Peoples living and working on reserves are tax exempt so they are also less likely to qualify for the carbon rebates central to the Trudeau government's system.
"First Nations are not the cause of climate change and we should not bear the financial burden of fixing it," Benedict said.
Ottawa's carbon pricing system tries to incentivize consumers to forego fossil fuels by making polluting fuels more expensive, then collecting and returning the proceeds to Canadians through a revenue neutral quarterly rebate program.
A portion of the proceeds from the consumer levy are meant to go to small businesses and Indigenous groups - but the federal government has been slow to hand out that cash.
In its April budget, the federal government created a rebate system to give small businesses their portions of the proceeds, including $2.5 billion from previous years. But Indigenous groups have yet to receive any of the money owed to them and there is disagreement over which groups should qualify and how the cash should be divided. Earlier this year, Ottawa said it would increase the amount set aside for Indigenous groups from one to two per cent of the total carbon levy revenues. The money set aside includes $282.2 million for proceeds collected from 2020-21 to 2023-24, and $249.3 million for the 2024-25 fiscal year, according to Environment Canada.
The Trudeau government has said it's been engaging with First Nations, Inuit and Métis groups on the issue since 2021 and expects a resolution soon.
"We understand the concerns expressed and hope to be able to issue contributions in a very near future," a spokesperson for Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said in an email. "Our priority has always been and will continue to be returning these proceeds in a way that provides flexibility to address self-determined priorities, including climate action."
Lawyers for the Chiefs of Ontario said they were told the government hopes to start the process of giving out the promised cash early in the new year, but Indigenous groups are growing anxious with the chances of an early election now higher after the NDP pulled out of its supply-and-confidence agreement with the Liberals. The NDP, who are likely to still hold the balance of Parliament in a confidence vote, has not signalled a desire for an immediate election.
In a statement, the Conservative party, which leads all major polls and routinely attacks the carbon plan, did not say if it would hand out the money already set aside for First Nations if they form government before that's done.
"First Nations communities will not have to take our government to court over the carbon tax because we will not take the money from them in the first place as Justin Trudeau has been doing for years," said Jamie Schmale, the party's Crown-Indigenous Relations critic.
On Wednesday, another group of First Nations - the Confederacy of Treaty Six Nations in Alberta - said it is planning to challenge the system in court to seek an exemption from the carbon levy and is seeking the support of the Assembly of First Nations, which has already endorsed the case put forward by the Chiefs of Ontario.
"We have raised the issue of the carbon tax with the federal government before, as many chiefs and nations, and nothing has been done to address our concerns," said Stephen Buffalo, the president of the Indian Resource Council of Canada, an organization that represents First Nations that produce oil and gas. "Obviously this is a disrespect, an injustice, and a slap in the face to reconciliation."
CAPTION: Ontario Regional Chief Abram Benedict looks on during the AFN Special Chiefs Assembly in Ottawa on Dec. 4, 2024.

Document 453 of 500

Ontario Trillium Benefit payments go out on Tuesday. Here's how much you can expect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-trillium-benefit-payments-go-out-on/docview/3141551420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Trillium+Benefit+payments+go+out+on+Tuesday.+Here%27s+how+much+you+can+expect&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not the $200 cheque Doug Ford has promised, but some Ontarians will get some relief from this week's Ontario Trillium Benefit.
The benefit was first introduced in 2011 under then-premier Dalton McGuinty. It rolled pre-existing provincial credits into one benefit with consistent monthly payments as part of the province's poverty reduction strategy at the time.
This latest round of payments comes as Canadians continue to grapple with a heightened cost of living that has especially squeezed lower-income earners. A record number of Ontarians are turning to food banks for help. Rent - despite indications that average prices are cooling off - is still high for many. Add to that, wages have remained consistently stagnant in several industries. A Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives report from September found that minimum wage workers would need to earn double the current amount to afford Toronto rents.
The benefit - an umbrella for three separate credits, all with different eligibility requirements - can be difficult to navigate. Here's what you need to know about the program and whether you're eligible.
What is the Ontario Trillium Benefit?
The OTB is a combination of three separate credits - the Northern Ontario Energy Credit, Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit and the Ontario Sales Tax Credit - meant to help alleviate everything from energy costs to sales and property taxes.
Who is eligible?
You must be eligible for at least one of the three credits included in the OTB, and conditions for each differ, but generally you must be 18 years of age, or older, and an Ontario resident.
The Northern Ontario Energy Credit is meant to help higher home energy costs for Northern Ontarians and only applies to those living in Algoma, Cochrane, Kenora, Manitoulin, Nipissing, Parry Sound, Rainy River, Sudbury, Thunder Bay and Timiskaming.
For the Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit, you must meet several conditions, including being currently or previously married or in a common-law relationship and paying property taxes or renting, among other requirements.
Finally the Ontario Sales Tax Credit, meant to help Ontarians with the sales tax they pay, requires you to be 19 years of age or older, or currently or previously married or in a common-law relationship.
How much can I receive?
The amount you will receive depends on your income, household size and which credits you qualify for.
The Northern Ontario Energy Credit has a maximum amount of $180 for a single person, but you must live in northern Ontario.
The Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit, meant to offset property taxes and sales tax on energy costs provides a maximum of $1,248 if you're between 18 and 64 years old, $1,421 if you're 65 and older, $277 if you live on a reserve or in a public long-term-care home or $25 if you live in a designated college, university or private school residence.
For the Ontario Sales Tax Credit, individuals can receive up to $360 and an additional credit for spouses, common-law partners and dependants under 19.
To calculate how much you could get, the CRA has a child and family benefits calculator online.
When can I expect the benefit?
The OTB is usually divided into monthly payments and usually issued on the 10th day of each month.
If you qualify for more than $360 from any of the three credits included in the Ontario Trillium Benefit, you can choose to have your benefit paid out in monthly installments or all at once in a single payment in June.
For December, eligible Ontarians should see the payment in their bank account on the 10th if they chose to receive the benefit on a monthly basis.
If you are married or have a common-law partner, the OTB payments go out to the person whose tax return is assessed first, and the payment will be the same no matter who applies.
The Ontario government is also warning of text message scams that purport to be OTB payments, asking you to click on a link to receive your money.
The government will never send you texts asking for your personal or business information, to receive a tax credit or benefit, to receive a tax refund or to make a payment.
How can I apply?
To receive the benefit, you need to have filed your personal income tax and benefit return, and the Canada Revenue Agency automatically assess you for the OTB.
Payments may arrive later if you don't have direct deposit set-up with the CRA or if you filed your income tax return late.
You can contact the CRA if you qualified for the Ontario Trillium Benefit but didn't receive your payment, or received less than expected, by the 24th.
CAPTION: You only need to be eligible for one of the three credits rolled into the Ontario Trillium Benefit to receive funds.

Subject: Energy; Tax returns; Costs; Sales taxes; Tax credits; Tax refunds; Income taxes; Property taxes

Document 454 of 500

Trump stokes rivalry north of the border

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-stokes-rivalry-north-border/docview/3141357851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+stokes+rivalry+north+of+the+border&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a collective campaign to save Canada from Donald Trump's tariffs.
And then there's the competing political campaigns:
Justin Trudeau is campaigning to be re-elected as prime minister, boasting that he's practiced at placating the president-elect.
Pierre Poilievre is campaigning to displace him, promising to push back against America when empowered as a Conservative PM.
Doug Ford is campaigning to be re-elected premier, while still aspiring to be prime minister one day.
May the most patriotic (and persuasive) politician win the day.
Canadians traditionally unite against a common adversary. Hence the homage, of late, to the Team Canada spirit of the past - when political rivals rallied to protect our patrimony and economy.
The father of that Team Canada playbook, former Liberal PM Jean Chrétien, was invited to meet Ontario's Progressive Conservative premier the other day for a brainstorming photo-op. First ministers also held a video-conference last week in that same spirit.
It's always comforting to watch premiers and prime ministers, past and present, publicly pat each other on the back lest they stab one another in the front. But beyond the bonhomie, beware the rivalry.
As federal opposition leader, Poilievre has a more rambunctious playbook. It's part of his job description to criticize the government of the day, but nowhere is it written that he must uncritically amplify the hyperbolic rhetoric of an incoming American government.
Trump's misapprehensions about smuggling (of both drugs and humans) have become fresh ammunition for the Conservatives to portray the border as a hot mess. It's not - at least not now.
Which is why the Trudeau government has publicly countered the exaggerations, while also promising to counteract any existing border problems before they get out of hand. It's undeniably complicated to control the world's longest undefended frontier, but Ottawa gets credit for reaching a deal with Washington in early 2023 to stop the flow of asylum-seekers at Roxham Road.
Beyond the modalities of border crossings, however, it's the political boundaries that are being transgressed in interesting ways.
Poilievre taunts Trudeau as powerless, because he is unpopular at home and unloved by America's president-elect. It is conventional wisdom among Conservatives that only a new government in Ottawa, empowered by a new electoral mandate, can give Canada the reset it requires in the bilateral relationship.
Equally at Queen's Park, the presumption - or pretext, perhaps - is that a federal government on its last legs in Ottawa must be shored up by a more popular Ontario government. Hence the premier is auditioning for the role of Captain Canada by talking up bilateral issues.
But the posturing betrays a misunderstanding of how unpredictable power and popularity can be. Trump was toast just a few years ago and now he's a political titan again.
Relationships are no less ephemeral and situational. Trudeau proved that point by pre-empting much of the world with his impromptu flight to Florida where he dined at Mar-a-Lago, with both sides beaming.
Why would an ascendant Trump hobnob with Trudeau at his lowest moment? Didn't he disdain the prime minister in the past, accusing him of dishonesty and disrespect?
We can't read Trump's mind. But we can watch his body language and read his social media musings.
He cares about crowd size. But he also relishes star power.
Say what you will about Trudeau's declining aura at home, he is still a celebrity abroad. Trump was clearly chuffed that the world-famous prime minister came at a moment's notice to give him face.
The notion that Trump still holds a grudge against him, after a tiff at a summit years ago, is a misreading of his mercurial ways. The president-elect loves nothing more than supplicants who make amends for past slights.
Trump famously mocked Marco Rubio mercilessly when the Florida senator ran against him for the Republican nomination a decade ago. Now Rubio is his nominee for secretary of state.
Trump evidently derives greater satisfaction from former adversaries who pay homage than from fellow travellers seeking favours. The notion that a global unknown like Poilievre, eager to prove his bona fides as a fellow right-winger, would win Trump's affection or bend to his will remains untested.
Ford is certainly well placed to advocate for Canada's industrial heartland - his folksy style is a good fit for Americans. Ontario has produced a series of earnest ads for broadcast on the Fox TV network extolling our indispensability and amity for America, and the premier made his own appearance this week.
In fact, Ford is the country's most powerful Conservative politician, but like the federal opposition leader - who styles himself our prime minister in waiting - the premier remains a relative unknown in America. Unlike Poilievre, however, Ford is at least singing from the same Team Canada songbook as Trudeau.
Which leaves us with two different playbooks from our political leaders for saving Canada from Trump. And three distinct campaigns to save their own political skins in the elections to come.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 455 of 500

Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford have one eye on Donald Trump and another on their own futures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-pierre-poilievre-doug-ford-have/docview/3141356173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%2C+Pierre+Poilievre+and+Doug+Ford+have+one+eye+on+Donald+Trump+and+another+on+their+own+futures&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a collective campaign to save Canada from Donald Trump's tariffs.
And then there's the competing political campaigns:
 Justin Trudeau is campaigning to be re-elected as prime minister, boasting that he's practiced at placating the president-elect.
 Pierre Poilievre is campaigning to displace him, promising to push back against America when empowered as a Conservative PM.
 Doug Ford is campaigning to be re-elected premier, while still aspiring to be prime minister one day.
May the most patriotic (and persuasive) politician win the day.
Canadians traditionally unite against a common adversary. Hence the homage, of late, to the Team Canada spirit of the past - when political rivals rallied to protect our patrimony and economy.
The father of that Team Canada playbook, former Liberal PM Jean Chrétien, was invited to meet Ontario's Progressive Conservative premier the other day for a brainstorming photo-op. First ministers also held a video-conference last week in that same spirit.
It's always comforting to watch premiers and prime ministers, past and present, publicly pat each other on the back lest they stab one another in the front. But beyond the bonhomie, beware the rivalry.
As federal opposition leader, Poilievre has a more rambunctious playbook. It's part of his job description to criticize the government of the day, but nowhere is it written that he must uncritically amplify the hyperbolic rhetoric of an incoming American government.
Trump's misapprehensions about smuggling (of both drugs and humans) have become fresh ammunition for the Conservatives to portray the border as a hot mess. It's not - at least not now.
Which is why the Trudeau government has publicly countered the exaggerations, while also promising to counteract any existing border problems before they get out of hand. It's undeniably complicated to control the world's longest undefended frontier, but Ottawa gets credit for reaching a deal with Washington in early 2023 to stop the flow of asylum-seekers at Roxham Road.
Beyond the modalities of border crossings, however, it's the political boundaries that are being transgressed in interesting ways.
Poilievre taunts Trudeau as powerless, because he is unpopular at home and unloved by America's president-elect. It is conventional wisdom among Conservatives that only a new government in Ottawa, empowered by a new electoral mandate, can give Canada the reset it requires in the bilateral relationship.
Equally at Queen's Park, the presumption - or pretext, perhaps - is that a federal government on its last legs in Ottawa must be shored up by a more popular Ontario government. Hence the premier is auditioning for the role of Captain Canada by talking up bilateral issues.
But the posturing betrays a misunderstanding of how unpredictable power and popularity can be. Trump was toast just a few years ago and now he's a political titan again.
Relationships are no less ephemeral and situational. Trudeau proved that point by pre-empting much of the world with his impromptu flight to Florida where he dined at Mar-a-Lago, with both sides beaming.
Why would an ascendant Trump hobnob with Trudeau at his lowest moment? Didn't he disdain the prime minister in the past, accusing him of dishonesty and disrespect?
We can't read Trump's mind. But we can watch his body language and read his social media musings.
He cares about crowd size. But he also relishes star power.
Say what you will about Trudeau's declining aura at home, he is still a celebrity abroad. Trump was clearly chuffed that the world-famous prime minister came at a moment's notice to give him face.
The notion that Trump still holds a grudge against him, after a tiff at a summit years ago, is a misreading of his mercurial ways. The president-elect loves nothing more than supplicants who make amends for past slights.
Trump famously mocked Marco Rubio mercilessly when the Florida senator ran against him for the Republican nomination a decade ago. Now Rubio is his nominee for Secretary of State.
Trump evidently derives greater satisfaction from former adversaries who pay homage than from fellow travellers seeking favours. The notion that a global unknown like Poilievre, eager to prove his bona fides as a fellow right-winger, would win Trump's affection or bend to his will remains untested.
Ford is certainly well placed to advocate for Canada's industrial heartland - his folksy style is a good fit for Americans. Ontario has produced a series of earnest ads for broadcast on the Fox TV network extolling our indispensability and amity for America, and the premier made his own appearance this week.
In fact, Ford is the country's most powerful Conservative politician, but like the federal opposition leader - who styles himself our prime minister in waiting - the premier remains a relative unknown in America. Unlike Poilievre, however, Ford is at least singing from the same Team Canada songbook as Trudeau.
Which leaves us with two different playbooks from our political leaders for saving Canada from Trump. And three distinct campaigns to save their own political skins in the elections to come.
CAPTION: Donald Trump speaks at a campaign rally at PPG Paints Arena on Nov. 4, 2024, in Pittsburgh, Pa.

Subject: Conservatism; Political campaigns; Boundaries; Decades; Politics; Prime ministers

Document 456 of 500

Premier vows tough legislation to help take down encampments: Says he's willing to use notwithstanding clause if necessary

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-vows-tough-legislation-help-take-down/docview/3141355998/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+vows+tough+legislation+to+help+take+down+encampments%3A+Says+he%27s+willing+to+use+notwithstanding+clause+if+necessary&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is prepared to override the courts by giving municipalities and police "enhanced legislative powers" to "dismantle" homeless encampments across Ontario.
"We are acting to put an end to the public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth in encampments," Ford said Thursday.
His comments came in a response to a dozen mayors who appealed to Ford to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
"I am confident that the legislative authorities the province is preparing are common sense, practical and entirely aligned with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, especially for the law-abiding residents who simply want to enjoy the benefits of their local public spaces," the premier told them.
"For that reason, I do not expect the legislation will need to proactively invoke Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," he said. "However, should the courts interfere with our shared goal of effectively addressing and clearing out encampments using these enhanced tools, with your support, our government is fully prepared to use the notwithstanding clause."
His letter came after the mayors of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor appealed to him two weeks ago for help.
They urged his government to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments" and "strengthen the existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions."
Although there are more than 1,000 people living in tents in Toronto parks throughout the city, Mayor Olivia Chow did not sign the letter.
The premier agreed there is "an urgent need to do more and I am thrilled that you have shown considerable leadership in putting forward concrete and actionable solutions."
"Ontario is finalizing the details of new initiatives, including enhanced legislative powers, that will be responsive to your requests," he wrote.
"The new legislation and supporting initiatives will achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Legislation, which is expected before the house rises for the holiday break next Thursday, will include a funding boost "to create more capacity in our shelter systems, supported by the new accountability measures to ensure these funds support dismantling encampments."
As well, it will gives police "new tools and authorities" to further curb the use of illicit drugs in public.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he was "very pleased with the response from the premier."
"We don't permit illicit drugs in our Peel shelters and it's why we have seen encampments grow among those struggling with addictions," said Brown.
"We simply can't have dangerous drug use in our parks and public spaces. The commitment to clearly prohibit it and back it up legally is helpful," he said.
"I just hope the next shoe to fall is compassionate intervention for addictions."
In Waterloo last year, a judge ruled that evicting those living in homeless encampments would be a violation of their Charter rights if no shelter spaces are available. A similar judicial ruling was also handed down in Kingston.
Use of the notwithstanding clause - which Ford has invoked before, being the first Ontario premier to use the Constitution's nuclear option - allows a government to pass legislation overriding certain rights.
On Tuesday, the auditor general criticized him for his decision to close 10 of 17 Ontario's supervised consumption sites in favour of 19 Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment hubs.
Auditor general Shelley Spence said shuttering the facilities near schools and daycares was made "without proper planning."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Homeless encampments; Public spaces; Legislation; Mayors; Addictions; Homeless people; Drug use

Document 457 of 500

Ford too keen to override rights

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-too-keen-override-rights/docview/3141355995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+too+keen+to+override+rights&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No surprise here: after publicly asking Ontario mayors to ask him to pass legislation allowing them to clear encampments - overriding the Charter of Rights and Freedoms if necessary - Doug Ford announced Thursday he is bowing to the request they made in response to his request. Homeless people camping in parks are going to be forced to move along. And if the constitution is an obstacle, Ford will bulldoze through it.
The premier spoke a lot of niceties about how he didn't expect legal trouble, and wasn't going to pre-emptively invoke the constitutional nuclear option to override the Charter. But make no mistake, if the courts interfere, he'll go there: "Our government is fully prepared to use the notwithstanding clause."
This may be troubling to a lot of us who care about things like civil rights and the rule of law. Especially since Ford has developed a habit of invoking or threatening to invoke the clause that allows legislatures to ignore the Charter.
There was a time - for 36 years after the Charter came into effect - in which this provision was rarely used outside of Quebec, and never used in Ontario. Then came Ford, who in six years has invoked it three times, passed two of those into law (before repealing one and seeing another struck down by a court on different grounds). And now Ford is talking about doing it a fourth time.
For most of the Charter's history, we rightly saw use of the notwithstanding clause as extraordinary and generally shameful. After all, you're just not supposed to knowingly violate the constitutional principles that are the very foundation of our democratic order. But Ford, I think, is making a boastful habit of doing so because he thinks it shows he's really serious about something. He fancies himself a western movie sheriff who won't let the niceties of the law get in the way of administering his own brand of justice.
It appears not as though he does it reluctantly, but eagerly because he thinks it earns him a macho badge of honour. Strongman street cred. There's a lot of that going around lately.
But this is all even more distressing, obviously, from the point of view of homeless people. Because it seems unlikely their situation is going to improve as a result of being forcefully moved along from encampments on public property.
Those folks are not belligerently refusing to rent apartments or move into the emergency shelter system on some point of principle. They are in our parks because they literally have nowhere else to go.
If that were not the case, use of the notwithstanding clause would not be required. The text of the court decision saying clearing encampments is unconstitutional refers specifically to situations where there is no alternate shelter available. If these people had any other option, no new legislation would be required.
Homeless shelters are full. So much so that Toronto is in the process of building 20 new emergency shelters. But those won't be ready anytime soon. Apartment rents are sky-high. The wait-list for subsidized rental housing in Toronto is eight to 15 years long. There's just a shortage of places for poor people to go. Or even average-income people.
If you needed yet another indicator of how dire the housing situation has become in Toronto, it was right there on the city hall agenda Thursday, where the city was planning to change its home ownership subsidy guidelines to allow households earning up to $160,000 per year to qualify for public down payment assistance.
That's the 80th percentile of income earners who'll soon qualify for housing subsidies. Not because we're so ably helping everyone else that we need to seek out new beneficiaries. But because the people we were trying to help with this program before don't have enough money to be able to benefit.
A household earning an average income, my colleague Victoria Gibson reports, cannot pass a mortgage stress test, even if they are given a down payment.
Alongside the income thresholds, the city needs to change the name - and the aim - of the program. "Affordable" housing ownership has proven impossible. So now they're trying for "attainable" housing by helping those who are rich enough that it remains possible to help them.
So: the richest one-fifth of people may get housing subsidies. The poorest people may get pushed out of their tents into the winter cold.
Like using the notwithstanding clause, there was a time this might have been shocking. That it's no longer a surprise shouldn't make it any more acceptable.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford said he is prepared to override the courts to give municipalities and police the power to "dismantle" homeless encampments. He appears eager to use the notwithstanding clause because he thinks it earns him a macho badge of honour, Edward Keenan writes.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Subject: Down payments; Housing subsidies; Shelters; Homeless people

Title: Ford too keen to override rights

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.8

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 6, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Language of publication: English

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+seems+eager+to+toss+the+Charter+aside+-+again.+Here%27s+what+is+most+distressing+about+this+time&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 459 of 500

Behind the province's soaring transit costs: Four major Metrolinx projects have cost taxpayers more than similar jobs around the world. A study blames poor planning and too many consultants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/behind-provinces-soaring-transit-costs/docview/3141355547/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Behind+the+province%27s+soaring+transit+costs%3A+Four+major+Metrolinx+projects+have+cost+taxpayers+more+than+similar+jobs+around+the+world.+A+study+blames+poor+planning+and+too+many+consultants&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the cost to taxpayers of Ontario's massive transit expansion plan continued to soar, Metrolinx asked a team of researchers to find out why.
Driving up the projects' price tags, the researchers concluded, was a costly combination of poor planning, overdesigning and relying too much on external consultants.
In fact, more than half of the province's budgets for its four priority subway projects were for costs that have little to do with the construction itself, the researchers detail in a study released Thursday.
As a result, it costs taxpayers significantly more to build subway lines, light rail and other transit projects in the GTA than other jurisdictions around the world.
If this "cost crisis" continues, the researchers warn that the planned transit expansion would eventually grind to a halt, unable to meet the growing transportation demand in Canada.
The study focuses on Metrolinx's four priority subway projects - Ontario Line, Yonge North subway extension, Scarborough subway extension and Eglinton Crosstown West extension, all part of Premier Doug Ford's government's ambitious transit expansion plan unveiled in 2019.
The study found more than 55 per cent of the four projects' initial cumulative $26.7-billion budget was earmarked for things such as planning, external consultants, designing, and risk management. The overall costs for these projects have since reportedly ballooned beyond $40 billion.
The Star sought comment from Metrolinx about the study, co-authored by a senior transportation planner for the transit agency, but got no response by publication time.
In its most recent annual report, Metrolinx said it has taken steps to find efficiencies as it faces financial challenges from supply chain issues, labour disruption and rising inflation. Lead researcher Jedwin Mok said their findings show that poor budget planning and a lack of transparency have helped sustain higher spending not just at Metrolinx but across Canada and the English-speaking world.
"It's not a force of nature that costs have to go up. It's a result of the way we plan, the way we construct, the way we consult the community," said Mok, a researcher at University of Toronto's Transit Analytics Lab.
Mok said the study released this week is a preliminary version of a chapter in a U of T report about Canada's infrastructure from U of T's School of Cities.
The researchers studied publicly available budgets on Canadian projects and compared them with more than 1,000 rail transit projects across 60 countries completed or expected to complete between 1971 and 2036.
They found that developed nations such as Italy, Turkey and Spain can deliver comparable projects at as low as one-tenth the price per kilometre compared to contemporary transit projects in Canada. An at-grade tramway in Toronto cost significantly more per-kilometre than a mostly underground, automated express subway line in South Korea.
The researchers found that taxpayers in Toronto and other large Canadian cities have observed a dramatic increase in per-kilometre costs to deliver rapid transit projects since 2002, and they are now among the most expensive places in the world to build transit.
The 15.6 km-long Ontario Line is estimated to cost more than $1 billion per kilometre after adjusting for inflation and removing factors such as maintenance.
The project was announced in 2019 with an initial price tag of $10.9-billion. That cost estimate has reportedly grown to a whopping $27.2 billion, almost as much as what the Ford government had initially said all four projects would cost in total.
While the soaring price tag for transit construction is often blamed on high cost of living, housing and labour, the study found these factors only played a small part in the rising construction costs in Canada.
Rather, it was more of a result of a persistently "insular" pattern of delivery practices, policies and leadership perpetuated among the English-speaking world, the paper found.
Canada and other English-speaking countries tend to budget substantially more for soft costs and be less transparent about its spending for transit projects, said Marco Chitti, one of the paper's authors and a research fellow at Marron Institute of Urban Management at New York University.
Soft costs generally include planning, designing, risk management, external consultancy, in-house staff and land acquisitions and are not directly related to construction.
More than 55 per cent of initial budgets for Ontario's four priority subway projects went toward soft costs, leaving less than 45 per cent for the actual building of the infrastructure.
The agency was also found to have relied extensively on external consultants for project delivery.
In cities like Paris, Milan and Istanbul, a transit project generally enters bidding only when the public sector has developed 30 to 70 per cent of the design. In less efficient cities, the projects usually enter bidding with their design only 10 per cent done.
"We don't know what we want," Mok said. "Because we don't have a scope, and the consultants are also in the dark, we put a lot of extra money to cover for the inevitable inefficiency that comes from the unknown."
The unknown risks also in part lead to high "contingency" costs - a pool of money set aside to minimize the risk of project overruns - which accounted for more than 19 per cent of the total budgets of Metrolinx's subway projects.
The agency tends to leave room in its budget to guard against unforeseen challenges in the construction process. But the study finds Metrolinx appears to have consistently padded its budget for the "worst case scenario."
For example, Toronto's Scarborough subway extension is planned with thicker walls and larger tunnels than a similar project in Milan, which adheres to similar safety standards, according to the study.
"Excessively large contingencies can lead to 'budget laxism,' where the incentive to control costs over a project life cycle is low, and money allocated is unnecessarily spent," the study states.
Some factors that drive up the soft costs are beyond a public agency's control.
"There's so much politics that hurts the delivery of transit projects, especially from politicians who come in and impose technical decisions," Mok said.
It is important to have political leadership break away from the traditional way of building transit and execute the vision to deliver more with less, the researcher said.
"Clearly it's possible," he said. "We can fix it if we want it."
CREDIT: Sheila Wang Investigative Reporter

Subject: Construction costs; Researchers; Expansion; Light rail transportation; Budgets; Planning; Cost estimates

Doug Ford tells Fox News that Trump's tariff threat a bad idea between friends

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-tells-fox-news-that-trumps-tariff/docview/3141158268/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+tells+Fox+News+that+Trump%27s+tariff+threat+a+bad+idea+between+friends&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford went on Donald Trump's favourite TV network to convince the incoming U.S. president that his threat of 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods is a bad idea.
"It's unfortunate," Ford told Fox News in a telephone interview Wednesday after his video feed conked out.
"The real focus should be on China, it should be on Mexico," he added, saying Mexico violates the Canada-United States-Mexico Free Trade Agreement by "slapping" made-in-Mexico stickers on Chinese goods before shipping them north, "costing Americans and Canadians jobs."
The appearance follows Ontario's new U.S. television ad blitz aimed at fighting the tariff plan.
And it's part of a "Team Canada" campaign to get Trump to drop the tariff plan, which would raise consumer prices in the United States, hurt the economy on this side of the border, and likely prompt retaliatory tariffs on American goods, embroiling the continent in a dangerous trade war.
"Canada does $1.3 trillion of trade, more than Japan, U.K. and France combined. Ontario alone does $500 billion in two-way trade" with the United States, Ford said on an afternoon Fox show.
"We're a big auto province here, and parts can go back and forth across the border up to eight times. So the supply chain is very connected."
"We're part of the family," he added, noting Ontario is the top trading partner to 17 states and the number two trading partner to nine others, with nine million Americans going to work every day to make goods for shipment to this province.
Ontario also has the critical minerals needed for electric vehicle batteries and sells surplus electricity to neighbouring U.S. states.
"It's a great friendship that has gone on for many, many generations, and we want to keep it that way," Ford said, repeating arguments regularly used with U.S. officials in Washington, senators, members of Congress and governors to bolster Canada's case.
"We're much stronger when we stick together."
The four-minute interview followed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's dinner with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida on Friday night.
Contrary to the views of some critics, Ford said the sit-down was a good idea.
"I don't think it's a mistake at all ... I'm glad the prime minister went down there, the first leader of the G7 to come and visit president-elect Trump."
Asked by the Fox News host what he makes of Trump's remark to Trudeau that tariffs wouldn't be an issue if Canada became the 51st state, Ford moved to ease tensions over the quip that has infuriated many Canadians.
"The president has a good sense of humour," Ford replied.
"I guess he's upset ... in 1812, we burnt down the White House, and he's holding a grudge after 212 years."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford at an announcement in Ottawa in October. "The real focus should be on China, it should be on Mexico," Ford told Fox News Wednesday about president-elect Donald Trump's threat of 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods.

Document 461 of 500

Doug Ford preparing to override courts, give police and municipalities enhanced legislative powers to 'dismantle' homeless encampments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-preparing-override-courts-give-police/docview/3141077235/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+preparing+to+override+courts%2C+give+police+and+municipalities+enhanced+legislative+powers+to+%27dismantle%27+homeless+encampments&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is prepared to override the courts by giving municipalities and police "enhanced legislative powers" to "dismantle" homeless encampments across Ontario.
"We are acting to put an end to the public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth in encampments," Ford said Thursday.
His comments came in a response to a dozen mayors who appealed to Ford to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
"I am confident that the legislative authorities the province is preparing are common sense, practical and entirely aligned with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, especially for the law-abiding residents who simply want to enjoy the benefits of their local public spaces," the premier told them.
"For that reason, I do not expect the legislation will need to proactively invoke Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," he said.
"However, should the courts interfere with our shared goal of effectively addressing and clearing out encampments using these enhanced tools, with your support, our government is fully prepared to use the notwithstanding clause."
His letter came after the mayors of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor appealed to him two weeks ago for help.
They urged his government to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments" and "strengthen the existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions."
While there are more than 1,000 people living in tents in Toronto parks throughout the city, Mayor Olivia Chow did not sign the letter.
The premier agreed there is "an urgent need to do more and I am thrilled that you have shown considerable leadership in putting forward concrete and actionable solutions."
"Ontario is finalizing the details of new initiatives, including enhanced legislative powers, that will be responsive to your requests," he wrote.
"The new legislation and supporting initiatives will achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Legislation, which is expected before the house rises for the holiday break next Thursday, will include a funding boost "to create more capacity in our shelter systems, supported by the new accountability measures to ensure these funds support dismantling encampments."
As well, it will gives police "new tools and authorities" to further curb the use of illicit drugs in public.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he was "very pleased with the response from the premier."
"We don't permit illicit drugs in our Peel shelters and it's why we have seen encampments grow among those struggling with addictions," said Brown.
"We simply can't have dangerous drug use in our parks and public spaces. The commitment to clearly prohibit it and back it up legally is helpful," he said.
"I just hope the next shoe to fall is compassionate intervention for addictions."
In Waterloo last year, a judge ruled that evicting those living in homeless encampments would be a violation of their Charter rights if no shelter spaces are available. A similar judicial ruling was also handed down in Kingston.
Use of the notwithstanding clause - which Ford has invoked before, being the first Ontario premier to use the Constitution's nuclear option - allows a government to pass legislation overriding certain rights.
On Tuesday, the auditor general criticized him for his decision to close 10 of 17 Ontario's supervised consumption sites in favour of 19 Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment hubs.
Auditor general Shelley Spence said shuttering the facilities near schools and daycares was made "without proper planning."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says the government will put an end to the loss of public space regarding homeless encampments in Ontario.

Document 462 of 500

City eyes changes to program: Toronto considers giving down payment help to higher-income households

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-eyes-changes-program/docview/3140808877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+eyes+changes+to+program%3A+Toronto+considers+giving+down+payment+help+to+higher-income+households&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Toronto is proposing to loosen its affordable home ownership rules to offer assistance to higher-income households - as city hall has repeatedly fallen short of its own targets for getting families into lower-cost homes.
Though the city has aimed to help 400 low-to-mid-income households a year gain a foothold into home ownership since 2015 - a target several non-profits have suggested is "relatively modest" - a new city hall report acknowledges that target "typically" hasn't been met. In 2022, for example, city hall approved 151 affordable ownership units, their data shows.
City staff blame a series of roadblocks in their report, including a smaller number of builders interested in building affordable owned units, limited funds available at city hall and from higher governments, and increased borrowing and construction costs that have raised the overall price-tag of new homes.
Toronto's assistance program today promises to waive fees charged to groups such as Habitat for Humanity and Options for Homes, with the idea the savings be passed on as down payment assistance loans. Those non-profits also rely on federal and provincial programs to offer more down payment help to buyers.
The province recently mandated that all cities provide these kinds of fee waivers to non-profits. Given Toronto was also facing an uptake problem, city staff saw it as a chance to try to offer more aid.
City hall is now proposing several adjustments to their approach. Along with additional fee waivers and new options for non-profits to partner with private developers to build affordable owned homes, the city is proposing to assist households earning up to $160,000 per year, or those in the 80th percentile of earnings. Previously, the program capped eligible incomes at the 60th percentile, around $102,000 per year.
"We're experiencing a deepened crisis in our city, where middle-income earners can't afford housing as well," city hall housing development director Noah Slater said in an interview. "The program had to go through some changes."
One challenge has been the swift deterioration of home affordability, with a widening gulf between what the market demands and what households can actually afford.
Slater pointed to a condo in Toronto selling for around $750,000. For a family at the 80th percentile of earnings, they might be within tens of thousands of dollars of being able to buy their own home, versus the hundreds of thousands of dollars a family within the 60th percentile of earnings would need.
The city considers home ownership affordable when housing bills eat up no more than 30 per cent of a household's earnings. For example, this year, a three-bedroom purchased home would need to cost $320,556 or less to count as affordable to someone in that 60th income percentile, city hall says.
Daniel Ger, CEO of affordable home ownership provider Options for Homes, welcomed the proposal, noting one key challenge they've faced in recent years is that midincome households earning around $90,000 a year or less were simply unable to pass a mortgage stress test, even with down payment help.
"The program called for incomes that would be great to be able to serve, but external factors made it really hard," Ger said.
If approved, the aid offered to higher income earners would be formally counted as "attainable" rather than "affordable" ownership housing.
Focusing on that kind of housing was a requirement of the sweeping deal struck between Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford last year that saw the province assume responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway.
But even with the proposed changes, Slater said Toronto won't likely see "substantial gains" on its home ownership assistance goals unless Ottawa and Queen's Park also offer more down payment support.
Along with the city proposal, staff released an analysis of their current approach conducted by Beam Group and BGM Strategy Group. While highlighting strengths like the 1,242 households assisted from 2009 to 2023, their assessment also flagged problems like restrictive eligibility criteria.
In Alexandra Park, the Star recently reported on an affordable home ownership program aimed at tenants of the area's subsidized and co-op housing. Run by Habitat for Humanity, that program struggled this year to find takers, which some tenants blamed on eligibility rules. While the average income in 2023 among Toronto's subsidized housing tenants was roughly $19,200, the income required to qualify for these units was $63,000 per year.
City staff, by adjusting its overall affordable home ownership rules, hope to have an easier path toward Toronto's overall goal of approving 4,000 affordable owned homes by 2030.
The proposed changes will be considered by Toronto's planning and housing committee on Thursday. If approved, it will go before council this month.
CAPTION: The City of Toronto is proposing to adjust its affordable home ownership program to assist higher-income households, as it has repeatedly failed to meet its own targets for getting families into lower-cost homes.Lance McMillan Toronto Star
CREDIT: Victoria Gibson Toronto Star

Subject: Construction costs; Down payments; Waivers; Cost control; Earnings; Households; Profits; Home ownership
