PCs urged to pay truckers' tolls: Proponents say move could quickly reduce gridlock on highways, roads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-urged-pay-truckers-tolls/docview/3134626944/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+urged+to+pay+truckers%27+tolls%3A+Proponents+say+move+could+quickly+reduce+gridlock+on+highways%2C+roads&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Proponents of diverting truck traffic from packed 400-series highways to private Highway 407 say the Ford government doesn't need to strike a deal with the 407's owners - it can quickly ease gridlock by simply paying truckers' tolls.
Vaughan Coun. Marilyn Iafrate will on Tuesday urge her colleagues to lobby Premier Doug Ford to study the feasibility of buying back Highway 407 which the provincial government built and in 1999 sold to private operators to run as a tolled alternative to public roads.
But Iafrate said that, in the meantime, the province should launch a one-year pilot program to subsidize or eliminate truckers' Highway 407 tolls to lure them off public highways and regional roads.
The province should then, she said, study the move's impact on "traffic congestion and overall traffic efficiency."
In an interview, Iafrate accused Ford of dreaming up new roads to combat gridlock, including planned Highway 413 that would impact her ward and which she opposes, and a possible tunnel under a swath of Highway 401, when a quicker, more cost-effective and underutilized solution sits in plain sight.
"If I'm on Highway 7 stuck at Bathurst Street going toward Bayview Avenue, I look south and see Highway 407 running free and that's what irritates people - they're very angry because we're stuck while traffic is running free over there on a highway that we paid for," she said.
"It's a no-brainer - they've got a piece of road that could be kick-started tomorrow and provide immediate relief to residents of Vaughan," and beyond.
Her push comes days after a spokesperson for Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said the government has "been in conversations with the 407," adding: "We will continue to find ways to make life more affordable for Ontarians and ensure they can get where they need to go every day. This includes looking at all options and building the critical infrastructure we need to keep up with our growing population."
The spokesperson for the Ministry of Transportation, Dakota Brasier, declined to say if talks are about buying back the highway or subsidizing truck tolls, or something else.
In an email Highway 407 ETR spokesperson Christina Basil said: "We continue to have regular meetings with the Government and explore opportunities to alleviate congestion across the region. We're always open to constructive dialogue that benefits both commercial users and the greater transportation network, though the Ontario Government hasn't engaged in any discussions with us regarding a potential buyback of Highway 407 ETR."
Basil declined to say if talks have included the possibility of Ontario subsidizing truck tolls.
Iafrate, whose ward includes Maple and Kleinburg, argued that Highway 407 majority owners, the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, should not charge Ontario for a pilot project to shift trucks to the tollway given that, in 2021, the Ford government opted to not pursue about $1 billion in penalties when 407 traffic levels fell below a contractual target during the pandemic.
The province could strengthen its bargaining position by buying out the current 407 shareholders, she said, including the 6.76 per cent AtkinsRéalis Group Inc., formerly SNC Lavalin Group Inc., which plans to sell by the end of 2027. But even if no deal can be reached, the province could simply start a process to fully or partially rebate truckers any Highway 407 tolls, Iafrate said, predicting an immediate decrease in gridlock on Highway 401 and other roads at a cost affordable to taxpayers.
A September 2023 report commissioned by Environmental Defence argued that fully subsidizing Highway 407 truck tolls for 30 years would cost substantially less than building the 52-kilometre Highway 413 through York, Peel and Halton while sparing agricultural land from the steamroller.
Phil Pothen, the group's Ontario Environment Program Manager, said in an interview: "Subsidizing tolls on the 407 could be implemented easily and ramped down as measures to actually reduce traffic on Highway 401 kick in.
"The actual solution to traffic problems is, in concert with shifting traffic to 407, moving other trips to transit and active transportation while densifying urban neighbourhoods and suburbs," so people have shorter trips to work, shops, hockey arenas and other vital trips, he said.
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Subject: Transportation planning; Tolls; Pilot projects; Roads & highways

Document 496 of 500

Judge slashes sentence over jail conditions: Ruling comes amid push to keep more people in custody ahead of their trials

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/judge-slashes-sentence-over-jail-conditions/docview/3134626534/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Judge+slashes+sentence+over+jail+conditions%3A+Ruling+comes+amid+push+to+keep+more+people+in+custody+ahead+of+their+trials&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto judge has joined a growing number of her judicial colleagues by condemning "harsh conditions" at the Toronto South Detention Centre while sentencing a man who "embarked on a night of terror" across the GTA.
Superior Court Justice Susan Himel recently sentenced Niko Gokool-Clark to six years in prison after he pleaded guilty to five counts of robbery and to possession of a loaded firearm stemming from a series of robberies at GTA convenience stores in the span of four hours.
But the 25-year-old will have just one year and eight months left to serve after the judge granted him an extra five months of sentencing credit due to the conditions at Toronto South, where he had been detained since his arrest. (That's on top of the one-and-a-half days of credit for each day spent in pretrial custody that's typically granted to all offenders, regardless of conditions.)
Himel's Oct. 24 decision comes amid calls by the Ford government - which oversees the jails - to deny bail more often and keep more people in jail ahead of their trials.
But experts warn that doing so without addressing long-standing concerns about jail conditions will simply result in more people getting extra credit once they're sentenced - reducing the amount of time they actually spend in prison, while also violating inmates' human rights.
Gokool-Clark had been in jail since his arrest on April 1, 2022. In an affidavit filed with the court, he complained he had been subjected to frequent lockdowns, usually due to staffing shortages, which led to limited access to showers and to the phone, making it difficult for him to speak with his two-year-old son. He was also squeezed into a triple-bunked cell, served improperly cooked food and wrote of witnessing violence among frustrated inmates due to the lockdowns.
"My time at TSDC has been horrific," he wrote. "Filthy, underfed and caged for prolonged periods. I feel like I am being treated worse than an animal."
His experience mirrors that of numerous people who have been held in the South over the years, and whose cases were criticized by Toronto judges, including findings that the conditions are "inhumane" and the result of "deliberate state misconduct."
In her October decision, Himel wrote "it seems that the circumstances of incarceration have not changed significantly despite the judicial comments that have been critical of government for allowing such harsh conditions to exist in the jail."
She continued: "I only hope that those conditions have caused him to reflect and to not want to commit further offences and end up back in jail."
Gokool-Clark's lawyer expressed doubt in an interview with the Star that anything would significantly change, given that Himel's comments echo statements made by other judges that have led to very little action.
"I don't think people necessarily care to make the conditions of jails better for those that are in them because I assume many people are of the opinion that they probably deserve to be in jail," said Victoria Strugurescu. "The problem is only going to get worse," she said, adding that, while extra sentencing credit helps the individual offender, "it doesn't address the root of the problem."
Gokool-Clark and an accomplice who can't be identified under the provisions of the Youth Criminal Justice Act held up 16 locations, primarily convenience stores and gas stations, late at night over the course of just four hours in Halton, Peel and York regions, as well as Toronto. They wore masks, often simply demanding money from the employee behind the counter, and in other instances making threats.
Although no weapon was used in the robberies, a discarded firearm with Gokool-Clark's fingerprint was found following a foot chase by police after the last one. (While he pleaded guilty to five counts of robbery and possession of the gun, he accepted the facts regarding all 16 robberies.)
Court heard that Gokool-Clark, who was 23 at the time, had been abusing drugs and alcohol, and was in a state of mania.
He is now on medication, and apologized in court to the victims and said he wants to be a productive member of society.
Crown attorney Alice Bradstreet asked for seven years in prison, while Strugurescu asked for five.
In granting Gokool-Clark extra credit, Himel accepted that the harsh jail conditions had "a significant physical and mental impact on him," as detailed in his affidavit, where he mentioned being admitted to hospital in May 2022 for an anxiety attack.
"Witnessing all of the violence in the jail has significantly affected my mental health and well-being," he wrote.
The vast majority of people held in Ontario's overcrowded and under-resourced jails are legally innocent; just over 80 per cent of inmates are awaiting trial or sentencing.
The provincial government has long called for even more people to be jailed after their arrests; Premier Doug Ford stated earlier this year he wants to appoint "like-minded judges" who will deny more people bail, while the Tories recently demanded the federal government amend the Criminal Code so that people charged with certain offences are automatically barred from getting bail. (The federal justice minister has expressed doubt that such a proposal would be compliant with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which guarantees the right "not to be denied reasonable bail without just cause.")
In Gokool-Clark's case, there was no bail hearing following his arrest; he had a fairly lengthy criminal record of property offences and breaching court orders, and was already on a release with a curfew for a different offence when he committed the robberies.
But the conditions he faced in jail will undoubtedly come up again when an offender housed at the South is being sentenced, observers say.
"It really begs the question: At the point where judges are actively calling out the Ministry of the Solicitor General over the years, what is it that is stopping them from addressing this issue? Why is it that they feel no need to actually address those conditions?" said Shakir Rahim, director of the criminal justice program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association.
He said it also raises a concern from a public safety perspective: If the conditions are so poor in the jails - where inmates have little access to basic hygiene, social supports and meaningful programming - how will they become rehabilitated?
"I think one of the main concerns is how those types of conditions undermine rehabilitation, and can actually increase the prospect of future offending," he said.
A spokesperson for Solicitor General Michael Kerzner maintained the province has seen an "unprecedented rise in violent crimes" and said the government will "not hesitate to put anyone who contributes to that activity behind bars."
When lockdowns happen, "every effort is made to maintain the regular schedule of visits, showers, telephone calls, programming and other services, regardless of whether a lockdown is partial or full," said spokesperson Chelsea McGee.
The reality is the jails are severely overcrowded and understaffed, and the government has been told that for years, said the chair of the corrections division of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
The combination of those two factors "creates a powder keg leading to high-stress, violent and often traumatic working conditions for correctional staff and unsafe living conditions for inmates," said Chad Oldfield.
"The only way the Ministry of the Solicitor General can fix conditions inside correctional institutions is by making real investments in permanent correctional staff, and by building the infrastructure we need for bed space, health care and programming," Oldfield said.
Toronto criminal defence lawyer Tonya Kent questioned whether the conditions have been allowed to persist for so long because no one, including jail superintendents, have ever been held accountable.
"Someone has to answer in the sense of who is operating ineffectively and why is it ineffective," she said.
"These cases are going to continue to happen and the credit people are getting is going to keep going up because the conditions are going to get worse and worse," Kent said.
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Subject: Criminal sentences; Court hearings & proceedings; Correctional personnel; Convenience stores; Robbery; Prison overcrowding; Prison reform; Living conditions; Prison administration

Document 497 of 500

One fed up Albertan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/one-fed-up-albertan/docview/3134626530/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+fed+up+Albertan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The U.S. is the primary destination for our crude oil, receiving approximately 97 per cent of Canada's crude oil exports in 2023. The Trans Mountain pipeline expansion has done little to change that - it is not near full and much of the additional volumes, transported at pipeline tolls three or four times higher than for the old pipeline, goes to refineries in Washington state and California.
This Albertan is fed up with his province selling crude oil at a discounted price because it has just one market. Fed up with high pipeline tolls. Fed up with exporting away most of the retail value of a barrel of oil by not upgrading domestically. Fed up with a lack of access to world markets and world prices and fed up with Donald Trump's threat of a 25 per cent tariff on products imported from Canada.
It is long past time that a two million barrel/day oil pipeline is built in an energy corridor to Prince Rupert, B.C., to export our crude oil and refined products, as well as LPG, gas liquids, and natural gas as LNG worldwide at world prices - not at discounted, un-upgraded, and potentially tariffed prices to just one buyer.
Mike Priaro, Calgary, AB
Just how will businesses remove the GST?Trudeau government announces $250 cheques for some Canadians, plus GST cuts on food, beer, children's clothes, Nov. 21
No one is mentioning how the removal of tax will occur. Most vendors will not have time to update systems so that clients are not charged GST. Will we be saving our receipts to bundle up and send to Revenue Canada?
Andree Shore, Toronto
Charities are suffering with Canada Post strikeFederal government will not send Canada Post strike to arbitration, minister says, Nov. 27
Charities in Canada receive almost one third of their annual donations during the month of December. Those direct mail packages we receive in the mail spur both mail and on-line donations. Let's get the negotiators back to the table and get this thing settled.
Tom Scanlan, Toronto
Ford should take responsibility not create distractionsDoug Ford wants you to focus on bike lanes and beer - and pay no attention to this, his biggest failure, Nov. 26
Lately, Ontario Premier Doug Ford is creating distractions from his own follies and failures, by weighing in on municipal and federal issues. As we are well aware, he is not the mayor of Toronto, nor the prime minister of Canada. Bike lanes are a municipal issue. They reduce pollution from commuting and save lives. Public transit, if fully functioning, would do the same, and Ford should shoulder responsibility for some of the delays instead of using bike lanes as his congestion excuse. His attempt to play elder statesman on U.S. tariffs is laughable when you look at his ambitions and accomplishments: selling out Ontario Place, furtively closing the Ontario Science Centre, compromising the Greenbelt, but hey, you can buy beer at convenience stores. His limitations are evident . He has a job and should concentrate on doing it effectively.
Joan McGoey, Toronto
Government funding for Ontario post-secondary education has never been lowerSheridan College announces major program cuts and layoffs amid international student cap, Nov. 27
This piece on the struggles faced by Sheridan (and a number of other Ontario colleges) cites a Ministry of Colleges and Universities (MCU) statement that asserts "the provincial government invested $1.3 billion to stabilize the post-secondary education sector earlier this year" with the additional claim that "funding for colleges and universities is higher than it's ever been."
This statement from the MCU merits scrutiny, as the first part needs context, and the second part is patently absurd. The MCU investment of $1.3 billion is a drop in the bucket that has to be divided among all the universities and colleges in the province and falls far short of the recommended actions of the provincial government's own Blue-Ribbon Panel on post-secondary education. The second part is ridiculous in that it can only be based on 2024 dollars being inflated compared to real dollars50-plus years ago. The fact is that, in terms of the percentage of operation budgets, government funding for Ontario post-secondary education has never been lower. Ontario is dead last in the country when it comes to post-secondary funding.
Yes, the federal government's recent changes to international student permit policies are causing uncertainty and disruption in the sector, but these changes can only have the impact they do because of the provincial government's ongoing failure to properly fund Ontario post-secondary education. This is an issue that is the result of neglect by successive Ontario governments, with the problem reaching a crisis level because of the policies of the current Ford government. The sky is not falling. The challenges facing the Ontario post-secondary sector can be navigated, but that will require the provincial government owning up to its responsibility - and it will require institutions in the sector, and indeed all concerned Ontarians, to demand it of them.
Jeff Brown, professor at George Brown College, Toronto
Why National Day of Truth and Reconciliation should not be a holiday Ontario won't support making Truth and Reconciliation Day a holiday for now: minister, Nov. 28
As a retired public school teacher I strongly disagree with the Ontario NDP's motion to make the National Day of Truth and Reconciliation a statutory holiday - at least not in schools. When I was teaching, Remembrance Day was always a day full of learning and solemn ceremony preceded by at least a week's worth of student preparation for that day. As a child, when Remembrance Day was a holiday, I hung out with friends, watched TV, and learned nothing about the meaning of Nov. 11th or previous wars.
This past "Orange Shirt Day," I drove around my neighbourhood at recess time and was delighted to view a sea of children dressed in orange shirts playing in the playground. Canada's legacy of the residential schools is foundational to this country's history and must continue to be taught in our classrooms and our schools now and for decades to come.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto, ON
Share your favourite holiday memories
The Star invites readers to tell us about their favourite holiday memories, traditions, adventures or misadventures. Do you have a special family tradition passed down for generations, a funny childhood experience you'd like to share or certain event that has become family lore?We'd love to hear your favourite story. Please send your submissions - try to keep them to less than 350 words - to lettertoed@thestar.ca. If you have a photograph to accompany it, please send it as a jpeg file, no smaller than one mb. We'll run a selection of these letters later in December.
CAPTION: TransCanada's Keystone pipeline facilities are seen in Hardisty, Alta., on Friday, Nov. 6, 2015.

Document 498 of 500

'Nobody can even get out here': Intense snowstorms in cottage country strand motorists, drop more than a metre of snow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobody-can-even-get-out-here/docview/3134626519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Nobody+can+even+get+out+here%27%3A+Intense+snowstorms+in+cottage+country+strand+motorists%2C+drop+more+than+a+metre+of+snow&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dan Vucic was driving on Highway 11 Saturday afternoon when he, along with so many other motorists in the Muskoka region, got caught up in an intense snow squall, causing traffic to grind to a halt. Then the snowmobiles came carrying the bad news.
"OPP police on snowmobiles passed by and they said be ready for a long wait," the Hamilton resident told the Star from the highway in Gravenhurst.
After more than 24 hours, there were no signs his plight would soon come to an end.
Communities in cottage country north of Toronto continued to dig out Sunday after intense snowstorms buried some parts of Ontario under more than a metre of snow. The squalls stranded motorists on Highway 11, left thousands in the dark and caused Gravenhurst to declare a state of emergency.
But Vucic was well prepared with a bed and plenty of food in his truck. "I've been stuck before, I never leave unprepared," Vucic said.
Buried in two and a half feet of snow, Vucic believes he may be stranded on the highway until Monday.
"There's nobody getting out of the trucks," Vucic said. "There's no movement at all."
Gravenhurst was one of the hardest-hit communities from the multi-day "lake-effect snow squall event" that Environment Canada has been warning about since Friday.
Mayor Heidi Lorenz says she has never seen this much snow in her 27 years as a resident.
"The snow banks outside my home, mind you I'm not very tall, but they're as tall as I am. In two days we got that much snow," said Lorenz.
Lorenz decided on the emergency declaration around 2 a.m. Sunday after realizing Highway 11 was hit hard by the storm and littered with abandoned vehicles.
An impassable, snow-covered stretch of the highway was inundated by collisions and had separated parts of the town from plows and emergency services on Saturday.
"It was a perfect disaster."
Plow reinforcements were called in and skies started to clear later Sunday after more than two days of nearly relentless snowfall.
"That highway intersects with our entire community," Lorenz said, adding that "it really shut off our access to (the southern) portion of our community."
It was unclear how many people were still stranded as of Sunday. Police and city officials have not reported any serious injuries due to the storm.
People who requested help had been pulled from the highway by Sunday morning as work continued into the afternoon to tow the last abandoned cars, said Brookyln Harker, OPP Central Region's media relations co-ordinator.
People living around Highway 11 have helped shelter or provided hot meals to the stranded drivers.
Kim Tamwichai, the owner of Thong Tai Cuisine, said her restaurant off Highway 11 south of Gravenhurst lost power Friday night, but it came back on Saturday night.
After she reopened the door Sunday morning, dozens of drivers and nearby residents dropped by her store to warm up, use the washroom and get some food.
"They have been stranded on the highway for 18 hours. Everyone seemed so tired," Tamwichai said.
Another restaurant in Gravenhurst downtown, Essence of Spice, has been offering free meals and drink to anyone struck in the area.
"No questions asked," said Akansha Singh, the owner's daughter, adding that many of the people stranded are youth hockey players and their families.
Rural residents snowed in by the storm have turned to social media to plead for help.
Penny Clark and her partner in his 70s have been without hydro in their trailer about 20 kilometres east of Gravenhurst for three days.
Clark turned to Facebook on Sunday after they ran out of gas for the generator - but help had not arrived by the evening.
Clark said she also called the OPP who said they will get to her as soon as they can.
"Nobody can even get out here, and it's over five feet deep," said Clark, adding that she couldn't seek help on foot due to a degenerative disorder.
"We're just going with some candles for tonight and hunkering down under some blankets and coats," said Clark, her voice shaking.
"We're just praying that somebody can get to us sooner than later."
Lorenz said it "sheer chaos" right now but thinks the town will be back to normal by Tuesday.
About nine groups took refuge at the town hall. The mayor recalled "Come from Away," the hit musical based on the experience of a Newfoundland town taking in thousands of airline passengers diverted by the 9/11 attacks.
Another local restaurant brought food, while movies were shown on council chamber screens to help entertain kids until hotel rooms were arranged for those who took shelter at the town hall. Firefighters helped act as "sort of chauffeurs" to help get people and supplies where they needed to go, Lorenz said.
Muskoka Lakes Mayor Peter Kelley spent Sunday working from a community centre after his house lost power.
Police in Muskoka and Bracebridge teamed up to "help through the night with the rescue of anyone that was stranded in a vehicle that couldn't get off Highway 11," he said.
"It's a small village, we have 8,000 permanent residents. There's a lot of people who are related to people in other townships. It's quite natural that we lean on each other when we need to," Kelley said, noting the town had to cancel its annual Santa Claus Parade because of the storm.
More than 25,000 homes and businesses from Orillia north to Parry Sound and east to Haliburton were still without power Sunday night, with some only expected to get it back on Tuesday, according to Hydro One.
Highway 11 was still closed Sunday night in both directions at Highway 60 in Huntsville and at West Street in Orillia, the OPP said.
Premier Doug Ford said the province was working closely with local authorities to help them respond to the storm.
Snow squall warnings extended into Sunday for many parts of Ontario, including Gravenhurst, which saw a total of 140 centimetres of snow.
Environment Canada's forecast expected the storm to taper off later in the evening before Ontario is hit with another broad brush of snow mid-week and a potential blast of lake-effect snow again next weekend.
With files from
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Emergency services came to the aid of stranded motorists in Muskoka on Saturday evening after intense snowstorms stranded motorists, knocked out power and caused Gravenhurst to declare a state of emergency. Thousands of homes and businesses in the region were still without power as of Sunday night.Muskoka Paramedic Services
CREDIT: Kristjan Lautens and Estella Ren Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

Full text: One fed up Alberta oil owner
The U.S. is the primary destination for our crude oil, receiving approximately 97 per cent of Canada's crude oil exports in 2023. The Trans Mountain pipeline expansion has done little to change that - it is not near full and much of the additional volumes, transported at pipeline tolls three or four times higher than for the old pipeline, goes to refineries in Washington state and California.
This Albertan is fed up with his province selling crude oil at a discounted price because it has just one market. Fed up with high pipeline tolls. Fed up with exporting away most of the retail value of a barrel of oil by not upgrading domestically. Fed up with a lack of access to world markets and world prices and fed up with Donald Trump's threat of a 25 per cent tariff on products imported from Canada.
It is long past time that a two-million-barrel-a-day oil pipeline is built in an energy corridor to Prince Rupert, B.C., to export our crude oil and refined products, as well as LPG, gas liquids and natural gas as LNG worldwide at world prices.
Mike Priaro, Calgary
Post-secondary funding too low
Sheridan College announces major program cuts and layoffs amid international student cap, Nov. 27
This piece on the struggles faced by Sheridan (and a number of other Ontario colleges) cites a Ministry of Colleges and Universities statement that asserts "the provincial government invested $1.3 billion to stabilize the post-secondary education sector earlier this year" with the additional claim that "funding for colleges and universities is higher than it's ever been."
This statement from the ministry merits scrutiny, as the first part needs context and the second part is patently absurd. The ministry's investment of $1.3 billion is a drop in the bucket that has to be divided among all the universities and colleges in the province and falls far short of the recommended actions of the provincial government's own Blue-Ribbon Panel on post-secondary education. The second part is ridiculous in that it can only be based on 2024 dollars being inflated compared to real dollars50-plus years ago. The fact is that, in terms of the percentage of operation budgets, government funding for Ontario post-secondary education has never been lower. Ontario is dead last in the country when it comes to post-secondary funding.
Yes, the federal government's recent changes to international student permit policies are causing uncertainty and disruption in the sector, but these changes can only have the impact they do because of the provincial government's ongoing failure to properly fund Ontario post-secondary education. This is an issue that is the result of neglect by successive Ontario governments, with the problem reaching a crisis level because of the policies of the current Ford government. The sky is not falling. The challenges facing the Ontario post-secondary sector can be navigated, but that will require the provincial government owning up to its responsibility.
Jeff Brown, professor at George Brown College, Toronto
Can businesses remove GST?
Trudeau government announces $250 cheques for some Canadians, plus GST cuts on food, beer, children's clothes, Nov. 21
No one is mentioning how the removal of tax will occur. Most vendors will not have time to update systems so that clients are not charged GST. Will we be saving our receipts to bundle up and send to Revenue Canada?
Andree Shore, Toronto

Federal government will not send Canada Post strike to arbitration, minister says,
Nov. 27
Charities in Canada receive almost one third of their annual donations during the month of December. Those direct mail packages we receive in the mail spur both mail and online donations. Let's get the negotiators back to the table and get this thing settled.

Ford should take responsibility
Doug Ford wants you to focus on bike lanes and beer - and pay no attention to this, his biggest failure, Nov. 26
Premier Doug Ford is creating distractions from his own follies and failures, by weighing in on municipal and federal issues. As we are well aware, he is not the mayor of Toronto, nor the prime minister of Canada. Bike lanes are a municipal issue. They reduce pollution from commuting and save lives. Public transit, if fully functioning, would do the same, and Ford should shoulder responsibility for some of the delays instead of using bike lanes as his congestion excuse. His attempt to play elder statesman on U.S. tariffs is laughable when you look at his ambitions and accomplishments: selling out Ontario Place, furtively closing the Science Centre, compromising the Greenbelt, but hey, you can buy beer at convenience stores. He has a job and should concentrate on doing it effectively.
Joan McGoey, Toronto

Ontario won't support making Truth and Reconciliation Day

As a retired public school teacher I strongly disagree with the Ontario NDP's motion to make the National Day of Truth and Reconciliation a statutory holiday - at least not in schools. When I was teaching, Remembrance Day was always a day full of learning and solemn ceremony preceded by at least a week's worth of student preparation for that day.
This past "Orange Shirt Day," I drove around my neighbourhood at recess time and was delighted to view a sea of children dressed in orange shirts playing in the playground. Canada's legacy of the residential schools is foundational to this country's history and must continue to be taught in our classrooms and our schools now and for decades to come.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto

Document 500 of 500

'Filthy, underfed and caged': Citing Toronto jail conditions, judge slashes sentence of man who 'embarked on night of terror'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/filthy-underfed-caged-citing-toronto-jail/docview/3134626294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Filthy%2C+underfed+and+caged%27%3A+Citing+Toronto+jail+conditions%2C+judge+slashes+sentence+of+man+who+%27embarked+on+night+of+terror%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-12-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Toronto judge has joined a growing number of her judicial colleagues by condemning "harsh conditions" at the Toronto South Detention Centre while sentencing a man who "embarked on a night of terror" across the GTA.Superior Court Justice Susan Himel recently sentenced Niko Gokool-Clark to six years in prison after he pleaded guilty to five counts of robbery and to possession of a loaded firearm stemming from a series of robberies at GTA convenience stores in the span of four hours. But the 25-year-old will have just one year and eight months left to serve after the judge granted him an extra five months of sentencing credit due to the conditions at Toronto South, where he had been detained since his arrest. (That's on top of the standard one-and-a-half days of credit for each day spent in pre-trial custody that's typically granted to all offenders, regardless of jail conditions.)Himel's Oct. 24 decision comes amid calls by the Ford government - which oversees the jails - to deny bail more often and keep more people in jail ahead of their trials. But experts warn that doing so without addressing long-standing concerns about jail conditions will simply result in more people getting extra credit once they're sentenced - reducing the amount of time they actually spend in prison, while also violating inmates' human rights. 'My time at TSDC has been horrific'Gokool-Clark had been in jail since his arrest on April 1, 2022. In an affidavit filed with the court, he complained that he had been subjected to frequent lockdowns, usually due to staffing shortages, which led to limited access to showers and to the phone, making it difficult for him to speak with his two-year-old son. He was also squeezed into a triple-bunked cell, served improperly cooked food, and wrote of witnessing violence among frustrated inmates due to the lockdowns."My time at TSDC has been horrific," he wrote. "Filthy, underfed and caged for prolonged periods. I feel like I am being treated worse than an animal."His experience mirrors that of numerous people who have been held in the South over the years, and whose cases were criticized by Toronto judges, including findings that the conditions are "inhumane" and the result of "deliberate state misconduct." In her October decision, Himel wrote that "it seems that the circumstances of incarceration have not changed significantly despite the judicial comments that have been critical of government for allowing such harsh conditions to exist in the jail."She continued: "I only hope that those conditions have caused him to reflect and to not want to commit further offences and end up back in jail."Gokool-Clark's lawyer expressed doubt in an interview with the Star that anything would significantly change, given that Himel's comments echo statements made by other judges which have led to very little action. "I don't think people necessarily care to make the conditions of jails better for those that are in them because I assume many people are of the opinion that they probably deserve to be in jail," said Victoria Strugurescu."The problem is only going to get worse," she said, adding that while extra sentencing credit helps the individual offender, "it doesn't address the root of the problem."Gokool-Clark and an accomplice who can't be identified under the provisions of the Youth Criminal Justice Act held up 16 different locations, primarily convenience stores and gas stations, late at night over the course of just four hours in Halton, Peel, and York regions, as well as Toronto. They wore masks, often simply demanding money from the employee behind the counter, and in other instances making threats. Although no weapon was used in the robberies, a discarded firearm with Gokool-Clark's fingerprint was found following a foot chase by police after the last one. (While he pleaded guilty to five counts of robbery and possession of the gun, he accepted the facts regarding all 16 robberies.)Court heard that Gokool-Clark, who was 23 at the time, had been abusing drugs and alcohol, and was in a state of mania. He is now on medication, and apologized in court to the victims and said he wants to be a productive member of society. Crown attorney Alice Bradstreet asked for seven years in prison, while Strugurescu asked for five.In granting Gokool-Clark extra credit, Himel accepted that the harsh jail conditions had "a significant physical and mental impact on him," as detailed in his affidavit, where he mentioned being admitted to hospital in May 2022 for an anxiety attack. "Witnessing all of the violence in the jail has significantly affected my mental health and well-being," he wrote. An overcrowded 'powder keg'The vast majority of people held in Ontario's overcrowded and under-resourced jails are legally innocent; just over 80 per cent of inmates are awaiting trial or sentencing.The provincial government has long called for even more people to be jailed after their arrests; Premier Doug Ford stated earlier this year he wants to appoint "like-minded judges" who will deny more people bail, while the Tories recently demanded that the federal government amend the Criminal Code so that people charged with certain offences are automatically barred from getting bail. (The federal justice minister has expressed doubt that such a proposal would be compliant with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which guarantees the right "not to be denied reasonable bail without just cause.")In Gokool-Clark's case, there was no bail hearing following his arrest; he had a fairly lengthy criminal record of property offences and breaching court orders, and was already on a release with a curfew for a different offence when he committed the robberies. But the conditions he faced in jail will undoubtedly come up again when an offender housed at the South is being sentenced, observers say. "It really begs the question: At the point where judges are actively calling out the Ministry of the Solicitor General over the years, what is it that is stopping them from addressing this issue? Why is it that they feel no need to actually address those conditions?" said Shakir Rahim, director of the criminal justice program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association. He said it also raises a concern from a public safety perspective: if the conditions are so poor in the jails - where inmates have little access to basic hygiene, social supports, and meaningful programming - how will they become rehabilitated?"I think one of the main concerns is how those types of conditions undermine rehabilitation, and can actually increase the prospect of future offending," he said. A spokesperson for Solicitor General Michael Kerzner maintained that the province has seen an "unprecedented rise in violent crimes" and said the government will "not hesitate to put anyone who contributes to that activity behind bars."When lockdowns happen, "every effort is made to maintain the regular schedule of visits, showers, telephone calls, programming, and other services, regardless of whether a lockdown is partial or full," said spokesperson Chelsea McGee. The reality is that the jails are severely overcrowded and understaffed, and the government has been told that for years, said the chair of the corrections division of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union. The combination of those two factors "creates a powder keg leading to high-stress, violent and often traumatic working conditions for correctional staff, and unsafe living conditions for inmates," said Chad Oldfield. "The only way the Ministry of the Solicitor General can fix conditions inside correctional institutions is by making real investments in permanent correctional staff, and by building the infrastructure we need for bed space, health care, and programming."Toronto criminal defence lawyer Tonya Kent questioned whether the conditions have been allowed to persist for so long because no one, including jail superintendents, have ever been held accountable. "Someone has to answer in the sense of who is operating ineffectively and why is it ineffective," she said. "These cases are going to continue to happen and the credit people are getting is going to keep going up because the conditions are going to get worse and worse."
CAPTION: The Toronto South Detention Centre, seen here in a file photo.
